Gustafson's Law, however, assumes the problem size *grows* with the number of processors (**weak scaling**), which is the realistic scenario for HPC users who always want to simulate *larger* systems. Therefore, **Gustafson's Law provides a more realistic estimate** for scalable HPC workloads, while Amdahl's Law remains the pessimistic ceiling for fixed-size problems. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Confusing Amdahl vs Gustafson:** Examiners check whether you correctly identify which law applies to *strong scaling* (Amdahl) vs *weak scaling* (Gustafson). Mentioning the context explicitly earns full credit. > 2. **Forgetting units:** Always specify time units (seconds, ms) and the metric (FLOPS, jobs/hr). Bare numerical answers without units lose 1–2 marks. > 3. **Missing efficiency calculation:** When $S$ and $n$ are given, examiners expect you to compute $E = S/n$ as well — many students skip this. > 4. **No diagram for architecture questions:** A textual block-level diagram *is mandatory* for Q.A(a) / Q.B(a). Skipping it costs up to 2 marks. > 5. **Amdahl's Law limit:** You MUST state the limit $S_{\max} = 1/f_s$ — a very common 2-mark follow-up. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **HPC = Latency; HTC = Throughput.** Memorize this single contrast. - **Flynn's Taxonomy** has four classes — **SISD, SIMD, MISD, MIMD** — and **MIMD** dominates modern HPC/HTC. - **Amdahl's Law** models *strong scaling* (fixed problem size): $S = \dfrac{1}{f_s + \dfrac{1 - f_s}{n}}$, limit $\dfrac{1}{f_s}$. - **Gustafson's Law** models *weak scaling* (problem grows): $S = n - f_s(n - 1)$. - **Efficiency** is $E = S / n$, with $0 < E \leq 1$ (ideal $= 1$). - **Memory architectures:** Shared (SMP), Distributed (clusters), Hybrid (NUMA). - **HPC clusters** comprise: Head node + Scheduler (SLURM) + Compute nodes + InfiniBand interconnect + Parallel filesystem (Lustre). - **HTC platforms** use schedulers like **Condor, Hadoop YARN, Kubernetes** for job-level parallelism. - **Metrics to remember:** **FLOPS**, **Speedup**, **Efficiency**, **Throughput**, **Makespan**. - **Real-world supercomputers:** Frontier (USA, exascale), Fugaku (Japan), PARAM Siddhi (India). - **Quick exam trick:** If asked about *maximum achievable speedup* for fixed problem → use **Amdahl**. If asked about *scaled workloads* → use **Gustafson**. ---

topics.title: Centralized, Parallel, Distributed and Cloud Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Centralized, Parallel, Distributed and Cloud Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Computing Paradigms

Computing paradigms define **how computational resources (processors, memory, storage, and software) are organized, managed, and coordinated** to solve problems. Over the decades, the evolution of hardware capabilities, network bandwidth, and user demands has driven the shift from a single machine doing everything to globally distributed virtual infrastructures.

For KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned), students are expected to clearly distinguish between **four fundamental paradigms** that form the backbone of modern computer architecture and system design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A **computing paradigm** is the abstract model that defines the structure, interaction, and control mechanism of computational elements within a system. The four primary paradigms studied in Module 1 are *Centralized*, *Parallel*, *Distributed*, and *Cloud Computing*.

---

## 1.2 Centralized Computing

### Formal Definition
**Centralized Computing** is a computing architecture in which **all processing, data storage, program execution, and I/O operations** are performed on a **single central host computer**. Users interact with this central system through **dumb terminals** or thin clients that have no independent processing power.

### Conceptual Analogy 🏢
Think of a **single government office** in a small town. Every citizen must come to *that one office* to get their certificates, pay bills, or file complaints. The office has the records, the clerks, and the authority. If the office shuts down, the entire town is paralyzed. The terminals are like the people standing in line — they only carry their request, the central system does all the work.

### Key Characteristics
- **Single host machine** performs all computation.
- Terminals are **passive** (no local processing).
- High **reliability** on the central server.
- Easy to **administer and secure** (one location).
- **Bottleneck risk** when user demand scales up.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Latency in centralized systems is measured in **milliseconds (ms)**, and throughput is limited by the **MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second)** rating of the single CPU.

---

## 1.3 Parallel Computing

### Formal Definition
**Parallel Computing** is a computation model in which **multiple processors (or cores)** work simultaneously on a single problem, typically by **dividing a large task into smaller sub-tasks** that execute concurrently. All processors usually share a common memory space (in shared-memory systems) or exchange data via high-speed interconnects (in distributed-memory systems).

### Conceptual Analogy 🍳
Imagine a team of **five chefs in one kitchen** preparing a large banquet. The kitchen is shared, they have access to common ingredients (shared memory), and they coordinate closely (synchronization). Each chef handles a different dish, but they all work *in parallel* under one roof to finish the feast faster than one chef could alone.

### Key Characteristics
- **Multiple processors/cores** within a single system.
- Focused on **speedup of a single computation**.
- Two main memory models: **Shared Memory (SMP)** and **Distributed Memory (MPP)**.
- Uses **multi-threading**, **vectorization**, and **SIMD/MIMD** instructions.
- Measured by **speedup factor**, **efficiency**, and **Amdahl's Law**.

---

## 1.4 Distributed Computing

### Formal Definition
**Distributed Computing** is a model in which **multiple autonomous computers** (nodes) located at **different physical locations** communicate and coordinate their actions solely by **passing messages over a network** to achieve a common goal. Each node has its **own local memory** and may run its own operating system.

### Conceptual Analogy 🌐
Picture **branches of a multinational bank** spread across the world. Each branch has its own local ledger, staff, and servers, but they are all connected by secure communication lines. When you withdraw money in Tokyo, the Tokyo branch *talks* to the London branch via the network to update your account. No single branch "owns" the whole system.

### Key Characteristics
- **Heterogeneous systems** connected by a network (LAN/WAN).
- **No global clock** — coordination via logical timestamps.
- Failure of one node does **not halt** the entire system (fault tolerance).
- **CAP Theorem** applies: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance (pick 2 of 3).
- Examples: Google's search infrastructure, SETI@home, blockchain networks.

---

## 1.5 Cloud Computing

### Formal Definition
**Cloud Computing** is the on-demand delivery of **IT resources (compute, storage, databases, networking, software)** over the internet with **pay-as-you-go pricing**. It abstracts, pools, and shares scalable resources across a distributed infrastructure, allowing users to provision and release capacity dynamically without direct active management.

### Conceptual Analogy ⚡
Think of the **electricity grid**. You don't build your own power plant at home — you just plug into the grid and pay for what you consume. Similarly, cloud computing lets you "plug in" to Google's, Amazon's, or Microsoft's data centers and rent virtual machines, storage, or AI services on demand.

### Key Characteristics
- Delivered via **public, private, hybrid, or community** cloud models.
- **NIST essential characteristics**: On-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, measured service.
- Service models: **IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, FaaS (Serverless)**.
- Examples: AWS EC2, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud Platform (GCP).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick Reference — Service Models:**
> - **IaaS** → You manage OS, runtime, apps; provider manages hardware (e.g., AWS EC2).
> - **PaaS** → You manage apps and data; provider manages platform (e.g., Google App Engine).
> - **SaaS** → You just use the software (e.g., Gmail, Microsoft 365).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Architecture of Each Paradigm

### 2.1.1 Centralized Computing Architecture
The structure follows a **star topology** with the host at the center. Components:
1. **Central Host** — Mainframe/minicomputer with CPU, RAM, storage.
2. **Front-End Processors (FEPs)** — Handle communication with terminals.
3. **Dumb Terminals** — Display + keyboard only; no CPU of their own.
4. **Control Unit** — Manages job scheduling and resource allocation.

**Operation Logic:**
- Terminal sends keystrokes → FEP buffers and forwards to host.
- Host executes the program and returns output.
- Single point of failure: host crash = system down.

---

### 2.1.2 Parallel Computing Architecture
Two architectural models dominate:

**(a) Shared Memory Multiprocessors (SMP)**
- All processors share a **single global memory**.
- Communication via **reads/writes** to shared variables.
- Symmetric: each processor has equal access time to memory.
- Issue: **memory contention** as processors scale.

**(b) Distributed Memory Multiprocessors (MPP)**
- Each processor has **its own local memory**.
- Communication via **message passing** (MPI library).
- Scales to thousands of nodes (used in supercomputers).
- Issue: **complex programming**; latency of inter-node communication.

**Why Parallel Computing?**
- **Moore's Law slowdown**: Single-core speed gains have plateaued.
- **Power wall**: Higher clock speeds = exponentially more heat.
- **ILP limits**: Instruction-level parallelism hits hardware limits.
- Solution: place **multiple cores** on a chip and parallelize work.

---

### 2.1.3 Distributed Computing Architecture
Distributed systems rely on **middleware** to hide the complexity of the network. Key models:

1. **Client-Server Model** — One server serves many clients.
2. **Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Model** — All nodes are equal; no central server.
3. **Three-Tier / N-Tier Model** — Presentation, Logic, Data layers separated.
4. **Micro-services Architecture** — Loosely coupled services communicating via APIs.

**Core Challenges:**
- **Heterogeneity** — different hardware/OS.
- **Openness** — uniform interfaces for extension.
- **Security** — encrypted communication over public networks.
- **Scalability** — handling more users/load.
- **Failure Handling** — detecting, masking, tolerating, recovery.
- **Concurrency** — multiple clients accessing shared resources.

---

### 2.1.4 Cloud Computing Architecture
The cloud stack is built on **virtualization** (hypervisors create VMs) and **orchestration** (Kubernetes, OpenStack).

**Reference Model (NIST SP 500-292):**
- **Cloud Consumer** — Uses the services.
- **Cloud Provider** — Supplies the services.
- **Cloud Broker** — Manages use, performance, delivery.
- **Cloud Auditor** — Independent assessment.
- **Cloud Carrier** — Provides connectivity (network).

**Deployment Models:**
- **Public Cloud** — Open to general public (AWS, Azure).
- **Private Cloud** — Dedicated to one organization.
- **Hybrid Cloud** — Combination of public + private.
- **Community Cloud** — Shared by organizations with common concerns.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notation | Unit / Note |
|---------|---------------|----------|-------------|
| **Speedup (S)** | $S = \frac{T_{\text{serial}}}{T_{\text{parallel}}}$ | $T_{\text{serial}}$ = single processor time, $T_{\text{parallel}}$ = multi-processor time | Dimensionless ratio |
| **Efficiency (E)** | $E = \frac{S}{N} = \frac{T_{\text{serial}}}{N \cdot T_{\text{parallel}}}$ | $N$ = number of processors | Range: 0 to 1 |
| **Amdahl's Law** | $S_{\text{max}} = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}}$ | $P$ = parallelizable fraction | $N \to \infty \Rightarrow S \le \frac{1}{1-P}$ |
| **Gustafson's Law** | $S_{\text{scaled}} = N - (1 - P)(N - 1)$ | $P$ = parallelizable fraction | Assumes scaled problem size |
| **Cost** | $C = N \cdot T_{\text{parallel}}$ | $N$ = processors, $T$ = time | Product of resources and time |
| **Scalability** | $S = \frac{\text{Performance} \propto N}{\text{Cost} \propto N}$ | Linear vs sub-linear | Sub-linear = bottleneck |
| **CAP Tradeoff** | Pick 2 of $\{C, A, P\}$ | Consistency, Availability, Partition-tolerance | A theorem, not a formula |
| **Cloud Elasticity** | $\text{Elasticity} = \frac{\text{Peak Load Capacity}}{\text{Average Load}}$ | — | Ratio of provisioned to used |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Questions on Amdahl's Law are very common. Always substitute $N \to \infty$ to find the **maximum theoretical speedup**, which equals $\frac{1}{1 - P}$. For example, if 90% code is parallelizable, max speedup = $1 / 0.1 = 10\times$.

---

## 2.3 Comparative Analysis

| Feature | Centralized | Parallel | Distributed | Cloud |
|---------|-------------|----------|-------------|-------|
| **Number of Processors** | 1 | 2 to thousands (in 1 system) | 2 to millions (across network) | Virtually unlimited (virtual) |
| **Location** | Single room | Single machine | Geographically separated | Data centers worldwide |
| **Memory** | Single | Shared or distributed (per node) | Distributed (per node) | Virtualized, abstracted |
| **Communication** | Bus / backplane | Shared memory bus / interconnect | Network (TCP/IP) | Internet (HTTP, APIs) |
| **Failure Impact** | Total system failure | Partial (graceful) | High fault tolerance | Self-healing |
| **Cost** | Low initial | Moderate | High | Pay-per-use (OPEX) |
| **Best For** | Banking, legacy mainframes | Scientific simulations | Web services, blockchain | Dynamic web, AI training |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Modern data centers (e.g., Google) use **all four paradigms simultaneously** — centralized control planes, parallel GPUs for ML, distributed storage (HDFS), and cloud abstractions (GCP). Understanding the boundaries is critical for system architects.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Amdahl's Law (Exhaustive)

**Problem Statement:** Given a program where a fraction $P$ of the execution time can be parallelized across $N$ processors, derive the maximum achievable speedup.

### Step 1 — Decompose Total Execution Time
The total execution time on a **single processor** is taken as the baseline. We can split it into:
$$T_{\text{serial}} = T_{\text{parallel}} + T_{\text{sequential}}$$

where $T_{\text{parallel}}$ is the portion that can be parallelized, and $T_{\text{sequential}}$ cannot.

### Step 2 — Express in Normalized Form
Let the total serial time be normalized to 1 (i.e., $T_{\text{serial}} = 1$).
Then:
$$T_{\text{sequential}} = 1 - P \quad \text{and} \quad T_{\text{parallel}} = P$$

### Step 3 — Compute Parallel Execution Time
When running on $N$ processors, the parallel portion is divided among $N$ processors:
$$T_{\text{parallel, multi}} = \frac{P}{N}$$
The sequential portion remains unchanged because it cannot be parallelized:
$$T_{\text{sequential, multi}} = 1 - P$$

### Step 4 — Sum the Two Components
$$T_{\text{multi}} = (1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Speedup Definition
By definition, $S = \frac{T_{\text{serial}}}{T_{\text{parallel, multi}}}$. Substituting:
$$S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}}$$

This is **Amdahl's Law** for speedup.

### Step 6 — Analyze the Limit as $N \to \infty$
$$S_{\text{max}} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}} = \frac{1}{1 - P}$$

This is the **fundamental insight** of Amdahl's Law: even with infinite processors, speedup is bounded by the sequential fraction.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Question:** A program takes **100 seconds** to run on a single processor. **80%** of the program is parallelizable. Calculate the speedup and efficiency when executed on **8 processors** and on **16 processors**.

### Solution

**Given:** $T_{\text{serial}} = 100$ s, $P = 0.80$, $N_1 = 8$, $N_2 = 16$.

**Step A — Compute Speedup for $N = 8$:**
$$S(8) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.80) + \frac{0.80}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.10} = \frac{1}{0.30} = 3.33$$

**Step B — Compute Efficiency for $N = 8$:**
$$E(8) = \frac{S(8)}{8} = \frac{3.33}{8} = 0.416 \text{ (or } 41.6\% \text{)}$$

**Step C — Compute Speedup for $N = 16$:**
$$S(16) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.80) + \frac{0.80}{16}} = \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.05} = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4.00$$

**Step D — Compute Efficiency for $N = 16$:**
$$E(16) = \frac{4.00}{16} = 0.25 \text{ (or } 25\% \text{)}$$

**Step E — Theoretical Maximum Speedup:**
$$S_{\text{max}} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.80} = \frac{1}{0.20} = 5.00$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** As $N$ doubles from 8 to 16, speedup improves only from 3.33× to 4.00×, while efficiency drops from 41.6% to 25%. This diminishing return is the **Amdahl bottleneck**, a key KTU 2024 concept.

---

## 3.3 Gustafson's Law vs Amdahl's Law (Analytical Comparison)

**Gustafson's Law** argues that for a *fixed execution time*, problem size can be scaled with $N$.

### Derivation of Gustafson's Scaled Speedup

**Step 1:** Let the parallel execution time on $N$ processors be:
$$T_{\text{multi}} = (1 - P) + P = 1 \quad \text{(normalized)}$$

**Step 2:** The **equivalent serial time** (running scaled workload on 1 processor):
$$T_{\text{serial, scaled}} = (1 - P) + P \cdot N$$

**Step 3:** Therefore scaled speedup:
$$S_{\text{scaled}} = (1 - P) + P \cdot N = N - (1 - P)(N - 1)$$

### Numerical Comparison

| $N$ Processors | Amdahl Speedup | Gustafson Speedup |
|---------------|---------------|------------------|
| 2 | 1.82 | 1.80 |
| 8 | 3.33 | 6.80 |
| 64 | 4.71 | 52.40 |

(Assuming $P = 0.80$)

**Why the difference?** Amdahl assumes *fixed problem size*; Gustafson assumes *fixed time, scaled problem*. In real HPC workloads, Gustafson is more realistic.

---

## 3.4 CAP Theorem Logical Proof

The **CAP Theorem** (Eric Brewer, 2000) states that in a distributed data store, you can simultaneously guarantee **at most two** of the following three properties:

- **C — Consistency:** Every read receives the most recent write.
- **A — Availability:** Every request receives a response (no error).
- **P — Partition tolerance:** System operates despite network partition.

### Logical Reasoning
Assume a network partition splits nodes into two groups: $\{A\}$ and $\{B, C\}$.
- If a client writes to $A$, $B$ and $C$ cannot receive the update.
- To remain **Consistent**, the system must reject the write request → loses **Availability**.
- To remain **Available**, the system must accept the write → the other group has stale data → loses **Consistency**.
- **Partition tolerance is non-negotiable** in real networks (failures happen).
- Therefore, you must choose between **CP** (e.g., HBase) or **AP** (e.g., Cassandra). Pure CA is impossible in distributed systems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Centralized Computing — Star Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1["Mainframe Host CPU"]:::host
    node2["Front End Processor 1"]:::fep
    node3["Front End Processor 2"]:::fep
    node4["Dumb Terminal 1"]:::term
    node5["Dumb Terminal 2"]:::term
    node6["Dumb Terminal 3"]:::term
    node7["Dumb Terminal 4"]:::term
    node1 --> node2
    node1 --> node3
    node2 --> node4
    node2 --> node5
    node3 --> node6
    node3 --> node7

    classDef host fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef fep fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef term fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** The central host connects via FEPs to passive terminals. This is a classic **centralized computing** topology.

---

## 4.2 Parallel Computing — SMP (Shared Memory Multiprocessor)

```mermaid
graph TD
    cpuA["CPU 1"]:::cpu
    cpuB["CPU 2"]:::cpu
    cpuC["CPU 3"]:::cpu
    cpuD["CPU 4"]:::cpu
    bus["Shared Memory Bus"]:::bus
    mem["Global Shared RAM"]:::ram
    io["I/O Subsystem"]:::io

    cpuA --- bus
    cpuB --- bus
    cpuC --- bus
    cpuD --- bus
    bus --- mem
    bus --- io

    classDef cpu fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef bus fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef ram fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef io fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** Four CPUs access a single shared memory through a common bus. Synchronization happens via locks, semaphores, and cache coherence protocols (MESI).

---

## 4.3 Distributed Computing — Cluster of Independent Nodes

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1["Node A Application Server"]:::node
    node2["Node B Database Server"]:::node
    node3["Node C Cache Server"]:::node
    node4["Node D Load Balancer"]:::lb
    sw["Network Switch LAN"]:::net
    client1["Client 1"]:::client
    client2["Client 2"]:::client
    client3["Client 3"]:::client

    client1 --> node4
    client2 --> node4
    client3 --> node4
    node4 --> sw
    sw --> node1
    sw --> node2
    sw --> node3

    classDef node fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef lb fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef net fill:#a5a5a5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef client fill:#92d050,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** Each node is a fully independent computer with its own OS, memory, and storage. The network switch acts as the communication backbone.

---

## 4.4 Cloud Computing — Layered Service Model

```mermaid
graph TD
    sub1["SaaS Software as a Service Gmail Office365"]:::saas
    sub2["PaaS Platform as a Service Google App Engine Heroku"]:::paas
    sub3["IaaS Infrastructure as a Service AWS EC2 Azure VM"]:::iaas
    sub4["Physical Hardware Servers Storage Networking"]:::hw
    sub5["Virtualization Hypervisor KVM VMware"]:::virt

    sub1 --> sub2
    sub2 --> sub3
    sub3 --> sub5
    sub5 --> sub4

    classDef saas fill:#00b050,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef paas fill:#0070c0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef iaas fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef virt fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef hw fill:#404040,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** Cloud services are stacked in layers. The user interacts with the topmost layer (SaaS) without managing the layers beneath. Virtualization is the bridge between IaaS and the physical hardware.

---

## 4.5 Comparative Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Centralized"]:::c
    B["Parallel"]:::p
    C["Distributed"]:::d
    D["Cloud"]:::cl

    A -- "Scale UP add more power to one machine" --> B
    B -- "Scale OUT place across network" --> C
    C -- "Add virtualization and elastic provisioning" --> D

    classDef c fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef p fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef d fill:#548235,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef cl fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** The four paradigms represent a natural evolution: scale up a single system → scale out across nodes → abstract everything into the cloud.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define distributed computing. List any four characteristics of a distributed system.**

**Model Answer:**
Distributed computing is a model in which multiple autonomous computers, located at different physical sites, are interconnected by a network and coordinate their actions by passing messages to achieve a common goal. Each computer has its own local memory, processor, and operating system.

**Four Characteristics:**
1. **Resource Sharing** — Hardware, software, and data can be shared across nodes.
2. **Concurrency** — Multiple processes execute simultaneously on different nodes.
3. **Scalability** — The system can grow by adding more nodes without performance degradation.
4. **Fault Tolerance** — Failure of one or more nodes does not halt the entire system.
*(Any four of the above)*

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the three service models of cloud computing with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Service Model | Full Form | What User Manages | Example |
|--------------|-----------|-------------------|---------|
| **SaaS** | Software as a Service | Nothing — just uses the software | Google Workspace, Microsoft 365 |
| **PaaS** | Platform as a Service | Apps and data only | Google App Engine, Heroku |
| **IaaS** | Infrastructure as a Service | OS, runtime, apps, data | Amazon EC2, Microsoft Azure VM |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split into sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. You must answer **either** Option A **or** Option B in full.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[a] (7 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]**
**With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of centralized computing. List its advantages and disadvantages.**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture Diagram:**

```
                    ┌─────────────────────┐
                    │   Central Host      │
                    │   (Mainframe CPU)   │
                    └──────────┬──────────┘
                               │
                  ┌────────────┴────────────┐
                  │                         │
            ┌─────▼─────┐            ┌─────▼─────┐
            │   FEP 1   │            │   FEP 2   │
            └─────┬─────┘            └─────┬─────┘
                  │                         │
        ┌─────────┼─────────┐       ┌───────┼────────┐
        │         │         │       │       │        │
      Term 1   Term 2   Term 3   Term 4  Term 5  Term 6
```

**Components Explained:**
- **Central Host** — Single mainframe performing all computation.
- **FEPs** — Front-End Processors that buffer terminal traffic.
- **Dumb Terminals** — Display + keyboard only; no local intelligence.

**Advantages (3 marks):**
1. Centralized **data management** and backup.
2. **High security** due to one access point.
3. **Low upfront cost** for terminals.
4. **Easy administration** from a single location.

**Disadvantages (3 marks):**
1. **Single point of failure** — host crash halts entire system.
2. **Performance bottleneck** with many users.
3. **Limited scalability** — cannot add infinite users.
4. **High cost of the central mainframe**.

---

**[b] (7 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]**
**Compare and contrast parallel computing and distributed computing in terms of architecture, communication, memory model, fault tolerance, and typical use cases.**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Parallel Computing | Distributed Computing |
|-----------|------------------|----------------------|
| **Architecture** | Multiple processors in **one machine** | Multiple **independent computers** on a network |
| **Memory** | Shared (SMP) or Distributed (MPP, per node) | **Distributed**, each node has its own local memory |
| **Communication** | Shared memory bus / high-speed interconnect (e.g., InfiniBand) | **Message passing** over network (TCP/IP) |
| **Coupling** | **Tightly coupled** | **Loosely coupled** |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Low — one node failure can affect others | **High** — node failure is isolated |
| **Geographic Spread** | Within a single room or rack | Across buildings, cities, or continents |
| **Synchronization** | Clock or barrier-based | Logical clocks (Lamport), vector clocks |
| **Use Case** | Weather simulation, molecular dynamics, matrix multiplication | Web search (Google), DNS, blockchain, IoT |
| **Programming Model** | OpenMP, MPI, CUDA, Pthreads | RPC, REST APIs, gRPC, message queues |
| **Example System** | Supercomputers (Summit, Fugaku) | Google's Borg cluster, Bitcoin network |

**Conclusion:** Parallel computing is optimized for **raw speed** of a single problem, while distributed computing is optimized for **scalability, reliability, and geographic reach**.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[a] (7 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the various deployment and service models of cloud computing. Use suitable diagrams.**

**Model Solution:**

**Deployment Models:**

1. **Public Cloud** — Owned by third-party providers, open to public. Example: AWS, Azure, GCP.
2. **Private Cloud** — Operated solely for one organization. Example: Bank's internal data center cloud.
3. **Hybrid Cloud** — Combines public + private with orchestration. Example: Burst workloads to AWS from on-premise.
4. **Community Cloud** — Shared by organizations with common goals. Example: Government cloud for multiple departments.

**Service Models (Layered Diagram):**

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│  SaaS  (Gmail, Office 365)          │  ← User Layer
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│  PaaS  (Google App Engine, Heroku)  │  ← Platform Layer
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│  IaaS  (AWS EC2, Azure VM)          │  ← Infrastructure Layer
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Hardware (Servers, Storage, Net)   │  ← Physical Layer
└─────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Key Difference:** In **IaaS**, the user manages OS, runtime, and apps. In **PaaS**, the provider manages the platform. In **SaaS**, the user only consumes ready-made software.

---

**[b] (7 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Derive Amdahl's Law. A program takes 200 seconds on a single processor. 90% of the program is parallelizable. Calculate the speedup and efficiency on 4 and 16 processors. Also find the maximum possible speedup.**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation of Amdahl's Law:**

Let the total sequential execution time be normalized to 1. The parallelizable fraction is $P$, and the sequential fraction is $(1 - P)$.

On $N$ processors:
- Sequential part: $(1 - P)$ (unchanged)
- Parallel part: $\frac{P}{N}$ (divided among $N$ processors)

Total parallel time: $T_{\text{multi}} = (1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}$

Speedup is the ratio:
$$S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}}$$

This is **Amdahl's Law**.

**Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:** $T_{\text{serial}} = 200$ s, $P = 0.90$, $N_1 = 4$, $N_2 = 16$.

**Case 1: $N = 4$**
$$S(4) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.90) + \frac{0.90}{4}} = \frac{1}{0.10 + 0.225} = \frac{1}{0.325} = 3.08$$

Efficiency:
$$E(4) = \frac{3.08}{4} = 0.77 \text{ (or } 77\% \text{)}$$

Actual time: $T_{\text{multi}}(4) = 200 / 3.08 = 64.94$ s.

**Case 2: $N = 16$**
$$S(16) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.90) + \frac{0.90}{16}} = \frac{1}{0.10 + 0.05625} = \frac{1}{0.15625} = 6.40$$

Efficiency:
$$E(16) = \frac{6.40}{16} = 0.40 \text{ (or } 40\% \text{)}$$

Actual time: $T_{\text{multi}}(16) = 200 / 6.40 = 31.25$ s.

**Case 3: Maximum Speedup** (as $N \to \infty$):
$$S_{\text{max}} = \frac{1}{1 - P} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.90} = \frac{1}{0.10} = 10.00$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Valuation Warning):**
> - Students often forget that the **sequential fraction $(1 - P)$ CANNOT be parallelized** — it remains a constant overhead.
> - Many students compute the maximum speedup as $N$ itself — **WRONG**. The correct limit is $\frac{1}{1 - P}$.
> - Efficiency must be expressed as a **decimal or percentage**, not as a raw number. Forgetting units loses 1 mark.
> - You **must show all substitution steps** in Amdahl's Law problems — skipping steps loses 2 to 3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- ✅ **Centralized Computing** → Single host, dumb terminals, star topology, single point of failure, high security, low scalability.
- ✅ **Parallel Computing** → Multiple processors **within one system**; aims to **speed up one task**; governed by **Amdahl's Law** and **Gustafson's Law**; uses **SMP (shared memory)** or **MPP (distributed memory)**.
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law formula** → $S(N) = \frac{1}{(1-P) + \frac{P}{N}}$; maximum speedup is $\frac{1}{1-P}$, which is finite even with infinite processors.
- ✅ **Gustafson's Law** → Scaled speedup is $N - (1-P)(N-1)$; assumes **fixed time, scaled problem size**.
- ✅ **Distributed Computing** → **Multiple independent computers on a network**; loosely coupled; no global clock; fault-tolerant; governed by **CAP Theorem** (pick 2 of 3: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance).
- ✅ **Cloud Computing** → On-demand, pay-per-use, virtualized resources over the internet; deployment models: **Public, Private, Hybrid, Community**; service models: **IaaS, PaaS, SaaS** (with FaaS as an emerging model).
- ✅ **NIST Cloud Characteristics** → On-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, measured service.
- ✅ **CAP Theorem** → In any distributed data store, only **2 of 3** properties can be guaranteed; **partition tolerance is mandatory** in real networks, so choice is between **CP** (e.g., HBase, MongoDB with strict consistency) and **AP** (e.g., Cassandra, DynamoDB).
- ✅ **Efficiency** $E = \frac{S}{N}$; always between 0 and 1 for a well-designed parallel program.
- ✅ **Key Distinction to Memorize:**
  - Parallel → **one machine, many processors**.
  - Distributed → **many machines, one network**.
  - Cloud → **many machines, virtualized, on-demand, metered**.
- ✅ **Speedup cannot exceed $\frac{1}{1-P}$** no matter how many processors you add — this is the most-tested KTU concept.
- ✅ **Real-world examples:** Centralized → IBM Z mainframes; Parallel → Supercomputers, GPU clusters; Distributed → Google search, DNS, blockchain; Cloud → AWS, Azure, GCP.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** In Part B questions, always start with a **diagram or block representation** (carries 2 marks), then list definitions, then the analytical/derivation part. Use bullet points, headings, and tables — it improves readability and the examiner's perception of clarity, which can fetch the **1-mark "presentation bonus"** that KTU examiners often award.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Objectives of HPC and HTC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design Objectives of HPC and HTC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is High Performance Computing (HPC)?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> **High Performance Computing (HPC)** refers to the use of aggregated computing power delivered by multiple processing elements working in coordination to solve complex, tightly-coupled computational problems that demand extreme **speed (low latency)**, massive **parallelism**, and very high **floating-point throughput (FLOPS)** within a bounded wall-clock time.

HPC is engineered for problems where a single task must finish **as fast as possible** — for example, simulating a black hole collision, predicting weather, molecular dynamics, or finite-element analysis of an aircraft wing.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Pit Crew vs. The Assembly Line

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Check (Real-World Analogy):**
> Imagine a Formula 1 pit crew changing four tires on a single car.
> - All 4 mechanics work **simultaneously, in tight coordination**, on **one single job**.
> - They shout signals to each other, swap tools in milliseconds, and their hands are constantly within inches of each other.
> - The objective is **minimum total time for one job** — even if the mechanics stand idle between pit stops.
>
> That pit crew = **HPC**. It is **latency-optimized, tightly-coupled, and performance-driven**.

## 1.2 What is High Throughput Computing (HTC)?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> **High Throughput Computing (HTC)** is a class of distributed computing paradigm designed to **maximize the number of independent jobs completed per unit time (throughput)** rather than minimize the latency of a single job. It focuses on **loosely-coupled, embarrassingly parallel workloads** scheduled across many commodity or opportunistic resources.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Laundry Shop

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Check (Real-World Analogy):**
> Consider a neighborhood laundromat with 50 washing machines.
> - Each customer drops off an **independent bag of clothes** — there is no coordination between bags.
> - The owner cares about **how many bags get washed per day (throughput)**, not how fast one specific bag finishes.
> - Machines can break down (fault tolerance matters), and a slow machine just makes its own customer wait — others continue.
>
> That laundromat = **HTC**. It is **throughput-optimized, loosely-coupled, and utilization-driven**.

## 1.3 Side-by-Side Design Philosophy

| Dimension | HPC | HTC |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Metric | **Latency** (seconds) | **Throughput** (jobs/hour) |
| Coupling | Tightly coupled | Loosely coupled / decoupled |
| Workload | Single complex problem | Many independent jobs |
| Hardware | Supercomputers, clusters with fast interconnect | Grids, clouds, volunteer networks |
| Network | Low-latency (InfiniBand, NVLink) | Best-effort (Ethernet, Internet) |
| Failure Handling | Rare; expensive redundancy | Expected; reschedule failed jobs |
| User Focus | One researcher, one big simulation | Many users, many small tasks |
| Scheduling | Synchronous / gang scheduling | FIFO, fair-share, backfill |
| Cost Sensitivity | Moderate (capital-heavy) | High (must be cheap per job) |
| Typical Tools | MPI, OpenMP, CUDA, OpenACC | BOINC, HTCondor, Apache YARN |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight — Key Distinction:**
> HPC asks: *"How fast can I solve THIS problem?"*
> HTC asks: *"How MANY problems can I solve in THIS time window?"*

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Problems (Bolded for Emphasis)

- **FLOPS** — Floating-Point Operations Per Second (typically expressed in **TeraFLOPS = 10¹² FLOPS** or **PetaFLOPS = 10¹⁵ FLOPS**).
- **MIPS** — Millions of Instructions Per Second.
- **Speedup S(N)** — Ratio of sequential execution time to parallel execution time on N processors.
- **Efficiency E(N)** — Speedup divided by number of processors.
- **Amdahl's Law** — Theoretical upper bound on parallel speedup given a serial fraction.
- **Gustafson's Law** — Scaled-speedup formulation for fixed-time problems.
- **Latency** — Time from job submission to first result (HPC's holy grail).
- **Throughput** — Jobs completed per unit time (HTC's holy grail).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Objectives of HPC — Deep Dive

HPC systems are designed to push a **single workload** through the machine as fast as physics allows. The key engineering objectives are:

### 2.1.1 Maximize Raw Computational Speed
- Goal: deliver the highest achievable **FLOPS** within power and thermal budgets.
- Techniques: vector instructions (AVX-512, SVE), FMA (Fused Multiply-Add), SIMD lanes, wide-issue superscalar pipelines.
- Top500 ranking uses LINPACK's **R_max** in PetaFLOPS.

### 2.1.2 Minimize Inter-Process Communication Latency
- HPC problems are tightly coupled — every microsecond of network delay is multiplied across millions of MPI messages.
- Design choices: **InfiniBand HDR/NDR (sub-microsecond latency)**, shared memory (NUMA-aware), kernel-bypass networking, RDMA.

### 2.1.3 Maximize Memory and I/O Bandwidth
- A HPC node must feed data to the compute units continuously.
- Design choices: **HBM2e/HBM3 stacked memory**, on-package SRAM, NVMe-of-arrays, parallel file systems (Lustre, GPFS, BeeGFS).

### 2.1.4 Expose Massive Parallelism to the Programmer
- Design choices: many-core CPUs (e.g., 128-core), GPU accelerators (NVIDIA H100, AMD MI300), MPI + OpenMP hybrid, PGAS languages (UPC, Chapel).

### 2.1.5 Predictable and Deterministic Performance
- HPC users need reproducible timings; jitter ruins benchmarking.
- Design choices: dedicated resource allocation, OS jitter reduction, hardware partitioning.

### 2.1.6 Reliability for Long-Running Jobs
- A 1-week climate simulation cannot restart from scratch if one node fails.
- Design choices: checkpoint/restart, ECC memory, redundant power, parallel job supervisors.

## 2.2 Design Objectives of HTC — Deep Dive

HTC systems prioritize **aggregate work completed over a long horizon**. Key objectives:

### 2.2.1 Maximize Job Throughput (Jobs per Hour)
- The system is rated in **jobs-per-day**, not FLOPS.
- Design choices: efficient batch schedulers (HTCondor, SLURM, PBS Pro), short-job-first bias, backfill scheduling.

### 2.2.2 Maximize Resource Utilization
- Idle CPU cycles = wasted capital. HTC actively scavanges idle desktops (e.g., BOINC @ home), idle cloud VMs, off-peak cluster slots.
- Goal: keep the **effective utilization η → 100%**.

### 2.2.3 Tolerate Failures Gracefully
- Failures are the **norm** in HTC grids (volunteer nodes go offline). The architecture must survive losing 20% of nodes mid-run.
- Design choices: job-level checkpointing, work-stealing, task replication, speculative execution.

### 2.2.4 Support Heterogeneous, Geographically Distributed Resources
- HTC runs on donated laptops, university clusters, cloud spot instances — all with different OS, CPU, and uptime.
- Design choices: sandboxed execution, portable job descriptions (DAGMan, JSDL), virtualization.

### 2.2.5 Minimize Cost per Job
- A single HTC job is cheap; the design optimizes for **$/job** or **kWh/job**.
- Design choices: commodity hardware, opportunistic scheduling, energy-aware policies.

### 2.2.6 Decouple Job Submission from Job Execution
- The user submits to a **queue manager**, not a specific machine. The system finds the best fit.
- Design choices: pull-based scheduling, matchmaking (ClassAds in HTCondor).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Variables | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S(N) = \dfrac{T_s}{T_p(N)}$ | Speedup with N processors | Dimensionless, $S(N) \ge 1$ |
| 2 | $E(N) = \dfrac{S(N)}{N}$ | Parallel efficiency | $0 \le E(N) \le 1$ |
| 3 | $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$ | **Amdahl's Law** (P = parallel fraction) | Upper bound as $N \to \infty$ is $\tfrac{1}{1-P}$ |
| 4 | $S'(N) = N - (N-1)(1-P)$ | **Gustafson's Law** (scaled speedup) | Useful for fixed-time, scaled-workload problems |
| 5 | $\text{FLOPS} = \dfrac{\text{FLOPs counted}}{\text{Execution time (s)}}$ | Floating-point rate | Expressed in GFLOPS / TFLOPS / PFLOPS |
| 6 | $\text{CPU Time} = \dfrac{\text{Instructions} \times \text{CPI}}{f_{clock}}$ | CPU time in seconds | CPI = cycles per instruction |
| 7 | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{\text{Instruction count}}{(\text{Execution time}) \times 10^{6}}$ | Million instructions per second | Higher = faster (for fixed ISA) |
| 8 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{\text{Total jobs completed}}{\text{Observation window (s)}}$ | HTC primary metric | jobs/s or jobs/hour |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Busy CPU-seconds}}{(\text{Resources} \times \text{Time})}$ | Resource utilization | $0 \le \eta \le 1$ |
| 10 | $\text{Cost} = T_p(N) \times N$ | Cost of parallel execution | Compared to sequential cost $T_s \times 1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** For Amdahl's Law, the *serial fraction* is denoted $f = (1-P)$ where $P$ is the parallelizable portion. Many students confuse the two; writing $1-f$ instead of $f$ in the denominator costs full marks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Weather Forecasting (HPC):** ECMWF runs a 10-km global model on a Cray XC50 — must finish in 1 hour wall-clock for operational delivery.
- **Drug Discovery (HTC):** Screening 10⁹ molecular conformations = 10⁹ independent docking jobs → BOINC grid at World Community Grid.
- **Cryptography (HPC):** Factoring a 2048-bit RSA key requires tightly-coupled number-field sieve on thousands of nodes with low-latency interconnect.
- **Astronomy (HTC):** SETI@home has processed trillions of FFT tasks on volunteer laptops since 1999.
- **Financial Risk (HTC):** Monte-Carlo value-at-risk simulations = millions of independent scenarios — perfect for HTC grids.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Amdahl's Law (For HPC Speedup Bound)

**Starting assumption:** Let a program consist of a *parallelizable fraction* $P$ and an *inherently serial fraction* $(1-P)$, where $0 \le P \le 1$.

**Step 1 — Sequential time on 1 processor.**
Assume without loss of generality that the sequential execution time is normalized to 1 unit.

$$T_s = 1$$

**Step 2 — Parallel time on N processors.**
The serial portion still takes $(1-P)$ units even with N processors (it cannot be parallelized). The parallel portion is shared by N processors, taking $\frac{P}{N}$ units.

$$T_p(N) = (1-P) + \frac{P}{N}$$

**Step 3 — Define speedup S(N).**

$$S(N) = \frac{T_s}{T_p(N)} = \frac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$$

**Step 4 — Take the limit as N → ∞.**

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \frac{1}{(1-P) + 0} = \frac{1}{1-P}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
Even with infinite processors, the speedup is bounded by $\frac{1}{1-P}$. For example, if only 5% of the program is serial, the **maximum achievable speedup** is:

$$S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.05} = \frac{1}{0.95} \approx 1.053 \text{ (for } P = 0.05\text{)}$$

Wait — re-check: if 5% is serial, then $P = 0.95$ and:

$$S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.95} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20\times$$

**Step 6 — Engineering consequence.**
A single line of unavoidable serial code caps the entire machine's value. Hence HPC design focuses obsessively on **reducing serial bottlenecks** (synchronization, I/O, communication collectives).

## 3.2 Derivation of Gustafson's Law (Scaled Speedup)

**Starting assumption:** The problem size scales with N so that the parallel portion grows, while the serial portion stays constant in **wall-clock time** (not as a fraction).

**Step 1 — Time decomposition on N processors.**
Let the serial portion take time $\alpha$ and the parallel portion take time $\beta$ on each of N processors.

$$T_p(N) = \alpha + \beta$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent sequential time.**
The serial time would have processed the larger workload:

$$T_s' = \alpha + N\beta$$

**Step 3 — Scaled speedup.**

$$S'(N) = \frac{\alpha + N\beta}{\alpha + \beta}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $(1-P) = \alpha/(\alpha+\beta)$ and $P = \beta/(\alpha+\beta)$.**

$$S'(N) = N - (N-1)(1-P)$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
For $P = 0.95$ and $N = 1000$:

$$S'(1000) = 1000 - 999 \times 0.05 = 1000 - 49.95 = 950.05\times$$

This is **dramatically more optimistic** than Amdahl — because we acknowledge that bigger machines are given **bigger problems**.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Amdahl's Law | Gustafson's Law |
|---|---|---|
| Assumes | **Fixed** problem size | **Scaled** problem size |
| Useful for | Latency-critical HPC | Throughput-oriented HPC |
| Limit as N → ∞ | $S \to \frac{1}{1-f}$ (bounded) | $S \to N$ (linear) |
| Best for | Hardware-limited | Workload-limited |
| KTU example | Weather model on same grid | Climate model at higher resolution |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 3-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A program takes 100 s on 1 processor. 80% of the runtime is parallelizable. Find (a) speedup on 16 processors using Amdahl's Law, (b) efficiency, and (c) the max speedup with infinite processors.

**Solution Step 1 — Identify parameters.**
$T_s = 100$ s, $P = 0.80$, $N = 16$.

**Solution Step 2 — Apply Amdahl's Law.**

$$S(16) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.80) + \dfrac{0.80}{16}} = \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.05} = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4\times$$

**Solution Step 3 — Compute efficiency.**

$$E(16) = \frac{S(16)}{N} = \frac{4}{16} = 0.25 = 25\%$$

**Solution Step 4 — Maximum speedup as N → ∞.**

$$S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.80} = \frac{1}{0.20} = 5\times$$

**Interpretation:** Even with 16 processors, we use only 25% of the hardware's potential because the 20% serial fraction is the bottleneck.

## 3.5 Throughput and Utilization Derivation

**Step 1 — Throughput.**
Suppose a HTC cluster processes 1,440 independent jobs in a 12-hour window.

$$\text{Throughput} = \frac{1440 \text{ jobs}}{12 \times 3600 \text{ s}} = \frac{1440}{43200} = 0.0333 \text{ jobs/s} = 120 \text{ jobs/hour}$$

**Step 2 — Utilization.**
A cluster has 64 nodes × 8 cores = 512 cores. In 1 hour, the scheduler logged 460,800 core-seconds of busy work.

$$\eta = \frac{460{,}800}{512 \times 3600} = \frac{460{,}800}{1{,}843{,}200} = 0.25 = 25\%$$

This indicates room for backfill scheduling improvement — a classic HTC design objective.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HPC System Architecture — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FE["FRONT-END LAYER"]
        A1[Login Node]
        A2[Job Scheduler SLURM PBS Pro]
        A3[Parallel File System Mount Lustre GPFS]
    end

    subgraph COMP["COMPUTE LAYER HPC NODES"]
        B1[Node 1 CPU plus GPU]
        B2[Node 2 CPU plus GPU]
        B3[Node 3 CPU plus GPU]
        B4[Node N CPU plus GPU]
    end

    subgraph NET["HIGH-SPEED INTERCONNECT"]
        C1[InfiniBand HDR NDR Switch Fabric]
        C2[NVLink PCIe Gen5]
    end

    subgraph STOR["STORAGE LAYER"]
        D1[NVMe Burst Buffer]
        D2[Object Store S3 Lustre OSTs]
        D3[Tape Archive HSM]
    end

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A2 --> B3
    A2 --> B4
    B1 <--> C1
    B2 <--> C1
    B3 <--> C1
    B4 <--> C1
    B1 <--> C2
    B2 <--> C2
    B3 <--> C2
    B4 <--> C2
    B1 --> D1
    B2 --> D1
    B1 --> D2
    B2 --> D2
    A3 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The HPC design objective is to give every compute node **simultaneous, low-latency access** to all other nodes (via the InfiniBand fabric) and to the **shared parallel file system** (Lustre). Tight coupling is visible — every node touches every other node.

## 4.2 HTC System Architecture — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SUB["SUBMISSION TIER"]
        S1[User Workstations]
        S2[Web Portal REST API]
        S3[Command Line Client]
    end

    subgraph CTRL["CENTRAL CONTROL TIER"]
        H1[Master Scheduler HTCondor]
        H2[Matchmaker ClassAds]
        H3[Queue Manager Priority Aging FairShare]
        H4[Checkpoint Server]
    end

    subgraph EXEC["EXECUTION TIER VOLUNTEER AND CLOUD"]
        E1[University Lab Desktops]
        E2[Idle Cloud VMs Spot Instances]
        E3[BOINC Volunteer Laptops]
        E4[Idle Cluster Backfill Slots]
    end

    subgraph DATA["DATA TIER"]
        F1[Input Staging Server]
        F2[Output Aggregator]
        F3[Distributed Cache]
    end

    S1 --> S2
    S3 --> S2
    S2 --> H1
    H1 --> H3
    H1 --> H2
    H2 --> E1
    H2 --> E2
    H2 --> E3
    H2 --> E4
    E1 --> F1
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    E1 --> F2
    E2 --> F2
    E3 --> F2
    H4 -.-> E1
    H4 -.-> E2
    H4 -.-> E3
    F3 --> E1
    F3 --> E2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The HTC design objective is **decoupling**. The user submits to a *central queue*; the matchmaker independently dispatches each job to any available execution node. Execution nodes are heterogeneous, geographically distributed, and may fail at any time — the architecture absorbs this.

## 4.3 Design-Objective Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Start: New Computing Requirement] --> R1{Is the workload a single tightly coupled problem}
    R1 -- Yes --> R2{Need minimum latency and maximum FLOPS}
    R2 -- Yes --> HPC[HPC Path Cluster plus InfiniBand plus MPI plus GPU]
    R1 -- No --> R3{Many independent embarrassingly parallel jobs}
    R3 -- Yes --> HTC[HTC Path Grid plus BOINC plus HTCondor plus Backfill]
    R2 -- No --> R3
    HPC --> O1[Optimize for Speedup Amdahl]
    HTC --> O2[Optimize for Throughput Utilization]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Metric Radar — HPC vs HTC Design Goals

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HPC Design Goals] --> A1[High FLOPS]
    A --> A2[Low Latency]
    A --> A3[Tight Coupling]
    A --> A4[Determinism]
    A --> A5[High Bandwidth]
    A --> A6[Reproducibility]
    B[HTC Design Goals] --> B1[High Throughput]
    B --> B2[High Utilization]
    B --> B3[Loose Coupling]
    B --> B4[Fault Tolerance]
    B --> B5[Cost Efficiency]
    B --> B6[Scalability]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1]

> **"Differentiate between High Performance Computing (HPC) and High Throughput Computing (HTC) in terms of design objectives."** (3 marks)

### Model Answer (3 marks)

1. **HPC** is designed to minimize the **execution latency** of a single, tightly-coupled, complex workload. Its primary design objectives are maximum **FLOPS**, minimum **inter-process communication latency**, high **memory bandwidth**, and **deterministic, reproducible** performance. *(1½ marks)*

2. **HTC** is designed to maximize the **number of independent jobs completed per unit time (throughput)** across many loosely-coupled, often heterogeneous resources. Its primary design objectives are high **resource utilization**, **fault tolerance**, **cost per job minimization**, and **scalability** to opportunistic resources. *(1½ marks)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1]

> **"State Amdahl's Law and explain its significance in HPC design."** (3 marks)

### Model Answer (3 marks)

1. **Statement:** Amdahl's Law gives the maximum speedup $S(N)$ achievable on $N$ processors when a fraction $(1-P)$ of the program is inherently serial:

$$S(N) = \frac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$$

*(1½ marks)*

2. **Significance:** As $N \to \infty$, $S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1-P}$. This means the **serial fraction caps the speedup** of the entire machine. Therefore, HPC design obsessively targets the elimination of serial bottlenecks — synchronization points, I/O waits, and communication collectives — because even a 1% serial portion limits speedup to 100×. *(1½ marks)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Module 1 Internal Choice Note:** In the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, you may attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full (14 marks each). Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Module 1 Pattern]

> **(a) Discuss the design objectives of a High Performance Computing (HPC) system in detail, with reference to architectural choices that minimize latency and maximize FLOPS.** (7 marks)
>
> **(b) A program takes 200 s on a single processor. The parallelizable fraction is 90%. Compute (i) the speedup on 32 processors using Amdahl's Law, (ii) the efficiency, and (iii) the maximum achievable speedup. Comment on the design implications.** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

The design objectives of an HPC system revolve around **pushing a single workload through the machine as fast as physics allows**:

1. **Maximize raw computational throughput (FLOPS)** — vector units (AVX-512, SVE), wide-issue pipelines, GPU accelerators (CUDA cores), FMA units. Top500 ranks systems by **R_max in PetaFLOPS** from LINPACK. *(1 mark)*

2. **Minimize inter-process communication latency** — tightly-coupled MPI jobs exchange millions of messages per second. HPC uses **InfiniBand HDR/NDR** (sub-µs), **NVLink**, shared-memory NUMA, and **RDMA** kernel-bypass networking. *(1 mark)*

3. **Maximize memory and I/O bandwidth** — high-bandwidth **HBM2e/HBM3** stacked DRAM feeds the compute units; **Lustre / GPFS / BeeGFS** parallel file systems serve parallel I/O; local **NVMe burst buffers** absorb checkpoint storms. *(1 mark)*

4. **Expose parallelism to the programmer** — hybrid **MPI + OpenMP**, **PGAS** languages (UPC, Chapel), GPU directives (**OpenACC**, **CUDA**), and many-core CPUs (e.g., 128-core EPYC, Xeon Phi descendants). *(1 mark)*

5. **Determinism and reproducibility** — dedicated resource allocation, OS-jitter reduction, hardware partitions. A simulation must produce the same numerical result run after run. *(1 mark)*

6. **Reliability for long-running jobs** — ECC memory, redundant power and cooling, **checkpoint/restart** to Lustre, parallel job supervisors that can re-spawn failed MPI ranks. *(1 mark)*

7. **Conclusion** — every architectural choice in HPC serves the *single* goal: **minimum time-to-solution for one large problem**, governed by Amdahl's Law, with the serial fraction being the architectural enemy. *(1 mark)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $T_s = 200$ s, $P = 0.90$, $N = 32$.

**(i) Speedup on 32 processors — 3 marks**

Apply Amdahl's Law:

$$S(32) = \frac{1}{(1-0.90) + \dfrac{0.90}{32}} = \frac{1}{0.10 + 0.028125} = \frac{1}{0.128125} \approx 7.80\times$$

**Parallel time:**

$$T_p(32) = \frac{T_s}{S(32)} = \frac{200}{7.80} \approx 25.64 \text{ s}$$

**(ii) Efficiency — 2 marks**

$$E(32) = \frac{S(32)}{N} = \frac{7.80}{32} \approx 0.244 = 24.4\%$$

**(iii) Maximum speedup as N → ∞ — 2 marks**

$$S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.90} = \frac{1}{0.10} = 10\times$$

**Design Implications:**

- Even with infinite processors, this program can never run faster than 10× its sequential time because the 10% serial portion is a hard bottleneck. *(1 mark, included in (iii) discussion)*
- With only 32 cores, we already achieve 78% of the asymptotic limit — diminishing returns set in, so adding more cores is **not cost-effective** for this workload. The architect should instead invest in **reducing the serial fraction** (vectorizing inner loops, overlapping I/O with compute, asynchronous collectives). *(included in (iii) discussion)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Module 1 Pattern]

> **(a) Explain the design objectives of High Throughput Computing (HTC) with reference to a real-world system such as BOINC or HTCondor.** (7 marks)
>
> **(b) A HTC cluster has 128 nodes, each with 16 cores. In a 24-hour window, the scheduler dispatches and completes 73,728 jobs averaging 30 minutes of CPU-time each. Compute (i) the throughput, (ii) the resource utilization, and (iii) comment on HTC design improvements.** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

1. **Maximize job throughput** — the system is rated in *jobs per hour*, not FLOPS. HTCondor and BOINC use **matchmaking** and **backfill scheduling** to keep the queue draining continuously. *(1 mark)*

2. **Maximize resource utilization (η → 1)** — volunteer laptops, idle lab desktops, cloud spot instances are scavenged. BOINC's "credit" system rewards donors and keeps participation high. *(1 mark)*

3. **Tolerate failures as the norm** — volunteer nodes can vanish at any time. BOINC uses **work-unit redundancy** and **result replication**; HTCondor uses **checkpointing** and **eviction-and-resume** when an owner returns to use their desktop. *(1 mark)*

4. **Heterogeneous, geographically distributed execution** — BOINC runs on Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, even Raspberry Pi. Jobs are packaged as **portable sandboxes**; HTCondor's ClassAds match job requirements to machine capabilities automatically. *(1 mark)*

5. **Decouple submission from execution** — users submit to a **central queue**; the matchmaker places jobs anywhere with spare capacity. This is fundamentally different from HPC's tight binding of a job to specific reserved nodes. *(1 mark)*

6. **Minimize cost per job** — commodity hardware, opportunistic scheduling, and energy-aware policies (e.g., suspend during peak electricity tariff). A typical HTC job costs orders of magnitude less in $/job than HPC. *(1 mark)*

7. **Conclusion** — HTC is the right choice for **embarrassingly parallel** problems (parameter sweeps, Monte-Carlo, genome screening, image-batch processing) where latency does not matter but the total work is enormous. *(1 mark)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** Nodes = 128, Cores/node = 16, Total cores = 2048, Jobs completed = 73,728, Average job CPU-time = 30 min, Window = 24 h.

**(i) Throughput — 2 marks**

$$\text{Throughput} = \frac{73{,}728 \text{ jobs}}{24 \times 3600 \text{ s}} = \frac{73{,}728}{86{,}400} \approx 0.853 \text{ jobs/s}$$

$$\approx 3071 \text{ jobs/hour}$$

**(ii) Resource utilization — 3 marks**

Total busy core-seconds:

$$73{,}728 \text{ jobs} \times 30 \text{ min} \times 60 \text{ s/min} = 73{,}728 \times 1800 = 132{,}710{,}400 \text{ core-s}$$

Available core-seconds in 24 h:

$$2048 \text{ cores} \times 86{,}400 \text{ s} = 176{,}947{,}200 \text{ core-s}$$

Utilization:

$$\eta = \frac{132{,}710{,}400}{176{,}947{,}200} \approx 0.75 = 75\%$$

**(iii) Design improvements — 2 marks**

- 25% of capacity is idle — HTC design should implement **backfill scheduling** to fit small jobs into holes left by larger ones, raising η towards 90%+.
- **Job-packing** (group small jobs onto single cores via containerization) can boost effective jobs-per-hour by 10–30%.
- A **fair-share** policy may be holding back opportunistic capacity — relaxing it during low-priority periods could further increase throughput.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in This Topic (Read Carefully!):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Amdahl's $P$ with serial fraction.** Many students write $P = 0.20$ when given "20% serial." $P$ is the **parallel** portion. Putting $P = 0.20$ into Amdahl's Law yields $S = 1.25\times$ — wrong by 6×. **Always identify $P$ = parallel fraction, $f = (1-P)$ = serial fraction.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the speedup limit.** When asked for max speedup, students compute $S(32)$ and stop. KTU expects the *asymptotic* limit $S_\infty = \frac{1}{1-P}$.
>
> 3. **Confusing throughput with latency.** A question asking "increase throughput" requires jobs-per-hour, not seconds-per-job. Mixing the two is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
>
> 4. **No diagrams in long answers.** KTU's 2024 scheme rewards 1–2 marks for a *labelled* architecture or flow diagram. Drawing a blank box for HPC and HTC in part (a) forfeits those marks.
>
> 5. **Mixing MPI with BOINC.** MPI is HPC; BOINC is HTC. A question on HTC design should never list MPI as a tool. Examiners check for this.
>
> 6. **Units in FLOPS problems.** Always convert: GigaFLOPS = 10⁹, TeraFLOPS = 10¹², PetaFLOPS = 10¹⁵. A missing unit conversion is the single most common arithmetic error in KTU exam scripts.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **HPC** = minimize **latency** for one tightly-coupled problem.
- ✅ **HTC** = maximize **throughput** for many loosely-coupled jobs.
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law** = $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \frac{P}{N}}$; max speedup = $\dfrac{1}{1-P}$.
- ✅ **Gustafson's Law** = $S'(N) = N - (N-1)(1-P)$; assumes scaled workload.
- ✅ **Speedup** = sequential time ÷ parallel time.
- ✅ **Efficiency** = speedup ÷ N; ideal = 1.0; typical HPC cluster = 0.2–0.8.
- ✅ **FLOPS** ladder: Mega (10⁶) → Giga (10⁹) → Tera (10¹²) → Peta (10¹⁵) → Exa (10¹⁸).
- ✅ **HPC design objectives:** max FLOPS, min latency, max bandwidth, determinism, reliability, exposed parallelism.
- ✅ **HTC design objectives:** max throughput, max utilization, fault tolerance, heterogeneity, decoupling, low cost-per-job.
- ✅ **HPC tools:** MPI, OpenMP, CUDA, OpenACC, InfiniBand, Lustre, HBM.
- ✅ **HTC tools:** BOINC, HTCondor, DAGMan, ClassAds, YARN, opportunistic backfill.
- ✅ **Amdahl's corollary:** serial fraction is the architect's enemy — eliminate it first.
- ✅ **Throughput formula:** jobs ÷ wall-clock time → always in jobs/s or jobs/hour.
- ✅ **Utilization formula:** busy core-seconds ÷ available core-seconds → between 0 and 1.
- ✅ **Cost in HPC:** cost-optimal point is where Efficiency × Speedup is maximized (typically $E \approx 0.5$).
- ✅ **HTC failures are expected; HPC failures are exceptional.** This single sentence captures the philosophical difference and is worth 1–2 marks in any descriptive question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT and Cyber Physical Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** IoT and Cyber Physical Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — IoT and Cyber Physical Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Internet of Things (IoT) — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IoT (Internet of Things):** A dynamic, globally addressable network infrastructure of physical objects ("things") embedded with **sensors, actuators, microcontrollers, and communication hardware**, each uniquely identifiable, that interoperate within the existing Internet ecosystem to deliver sensing, actuation, data capture, storage, processing, and decision-making services — *as defined in the KTU 2024 PECMT632 syllabus (Module 1).*

In one rigorous line:
IoT = **Unique Identification (UID) + Sensing/Actuation + Communication + Internet Protocol Stack + Smart Computation**

### 1.2 Cyber Physical System (CPS) — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CPS (Cyber Physical System):** A system engineered by the **tight, orchestrated integration of computation kernels, communication networks, and physical processes**, wherein embedded computers monitor, coordinate, control, and integrate the physical dynamics through bidirectional, often real-time feedback loops.

The key phrase the KTU examiner wants is **"tight coupling between cyber and physical"** — note that IoT is mostly sensing/reporting, while CPS *closes the loop* with actuators and control.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

| Concept | Real-World Analogy | Why It Helps |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensor** | The human skin — feels temperature, pressure, light | Transduces physical phenomena into electrical signals |
| **Actuator** | Human muscles — move when brain commands | Transduces electrical signals back into physical action |
| **Microcontroller (MCU)** | The human brainstem — fast local reflexes | Local, low-latency compute |
| **Cloud / Edge Server** | The cerebral cortex — slow deep thinking | High-level analytics and storage |
| **Network** | The nervous system — connects everything | Bidirectional data flow |
| **IoT** | A house full of cameras reporting weather | Sense → Send → Display (open loop) |
| **CPS** | An autonomous car braking on its own | Sense → Decide → Act (closed loop) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** If a question asks *"Differentiate IoT and CPS"*, the cleanest single-line answer is:
> **IoT = Sense + Communicate + Analyse** (open-loop, data-centric)
> **CPS = Sense + Compute + Actuate + Control** (closed-loop, control-centric)

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to be fluent with the following **standard IoT/CPS metrics** (always quote units):

- **Latency (L):** time from event occurrence to actuation — measured in **ms** (milliseconds). Hard real-time CPS typically demand **L ≤ 10 ms**.
- **Throughput (T):** data rate per node — **kbps** to **Mbps**.
- **Jitter (J):** variance in latency — **ms²** or **ms**.
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** measured in **hours**.
- **Availability (A):** measured as a **percentage** (e.g., 99.999% = "five nines").
- **Energy per bit (E_b):** measured in **J/bit**.
- **Power Consumption (P):** measured in **mW** (for nodes) and **W** (for gateways).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 IoT — The Four / Five-Layer Architecture (KTU Reference Model)

Every KTU board question on IoT architecture flows from the **five-layer reference model** below. Commit this to memory.

#### Layer 1 — Perception / Sensing Layer (the Physical Plane)
- **Role:** Raw physical-world data acquisition.
- **Hardware:** Sensors (temperature, humidity, accelerometers, gas, RFID), actuators (motors, relays, solenoids).
- **Communication with next layer:** Short-range, often **wireless personal area networks** (WPAN) — IEEE 802.15.4, BLE, ZigBee, LoRa (long-range).
- **Key concern:** Energy efficiency, calibration, transducer noise.

#### Layer 2 — Network / Transport Layer (the Connectivity Plane)
- **Role:** Reliable, addressable, secure transport of sensed data.
- **Protocols:** IPv4/IPv6, 6LoWPAN (IPv6 over Low-Power WPAN), RPL (Routing Protocol for Low-power Lossy networks), MQTT, CoAP, AMQP, HTTP/HTTPS.
- **Topologies:** Star, Mesh, Hybrid.
- **Key concern:** Bandwidth, packet loss, addressing (every "thing" needs a **unique IPv6 address**).

#### Layer 3 — Middleware / Processing Layer (the Management Plane)
- **Role:** Device management, data filtering, aggregation, security, context detection.
- **Technologies:** Cloud platforms (AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT), Edge/Fog computing, in-network processing.
- **Key concern:** Interoperability (semantic interoperability across vendors).

#### Layer 4 — Application Layer (the Service Plane)
- **Role:** Delivers domain-specific functionality to end-users.
- **Examples:** Smart home, smart agriculture, industrial monitoring, healthcare wearables, smart grid.
- **Key concern:** User experience, privacy, domain-specific SLAs.

#### Layer 5 — Business Layer (the Value Plane) *(sometimes optional in KTU)*
- **Role:** Converts data into actionable insight for business decisions; manages overall system behaviour, ROI, and evolution.
- **Tools:** Big-data analytics, AI/ML, dashboards, KPI engines.

> [!NOTE]
> **A common 3-mark KTU question is:** *"List the layers of IoT architecture and explain any two."* — Memorize **all five** layers, but you only need to detail two.

### 2.2 CPS — The Three-Tier Architecture (KTU Reference Model)

CPS is canonically decomposed into **three tiers** that close a feedback loop:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tier 1 (Physical)} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{Sensors + Actuators + Physical Plant} \\
\text{Tier 2 (Cyber)} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{Embedded Controller + Network + Cloud} \\
\text{Tier 3 (Human)} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{Operator / Supervisor / Decision Maker}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Control flow:** Plant → Sensor → Controller → Actuator → Plant (a **closed feedback loop**).

### 2.3 Components Deep-Dive (the "Why and How")

#### 2.3.1 Sensor
- A **transducer** that converts physical quantities (temperature, pressure, light, motion, chemical concentration) into electrical signals.
- **Static characteristics:** accuracy, precision, sensitivity, linearity, hysteresis, drift, resolution.
- **Dynamic characteristics:** rise time, settling time, bandwidth.
- *How it works:* A physical change alters a material property (resistance in thermistor, capacitance in capacitive sensor, voltage in thermocouple).

#### 2.3.2 Actuator
- The dual of a sensor — converts electrical signals into physical action.
- Examples: DC motor, stepper motor, servo, piezoelectric stack, hydraulic cylinder, solenoid valve.

#### 2.3.3 Microcontroller / Embedded Processor
- Brain of the IoT/CPS node. Common KTU-referenced families: **ARM Cortex-M (STM32, nRF52), ESP32, ATmega328 (Arduino), MSP430**.
- Runs an **RTOS** (FreeRTOS, Contiki, RIOT) in CPS for deterministic scheduling.

#### 2.3.4 Communication Module
- Short-range: BLE, ZigBee, Wi-Fi.
- Long-range: **LoRaWAN, SigFox, NB-IoT, LTE-M**.
- Wired: CAN, RS-485, Ethernet (industrial CPS).

#### 2.3.5 Power Subsystem
- Battery, energy harvesting (solar, vibration, RF), supercapacitor.
- **Duty cycling** is the dominant energy-saving strategy in IoT nodes.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ (Availability) | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Fraction of time system is operational | dimensionless (often %) |
| $E_b$ (Energy per bit) | $E_b = \dfrac{P_{\text{avg}}}{R_b}$ | Energy consumed transmitting one bit | J/bit |
| $T_{\text{end-to-end}}$ | $T_{\text{e2e}} = T_{\text{sense}} + T_{\text{proc}} + T_{\text{tx}} + T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{queue}}$ | Total latency, sensor → actuator | s or ms |
| $J$ (Jitter) | $J = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(L_i - \bar{L})^2}$ | Standard deviation of latency | ms |
| $S/N$ | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | Signal quality | dB |
| Shannon Capacity | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Max error-free data rate | bits/s |
| $N_{\text{IPv6}}$ | $2^{128}$ | Total IoT addressable devices | — |
| Duty Cycle | $\text{DC} = \dfrac{T_{\text{active}}}{T_{\text{active}} + T_{\text{sleep}}}$ | Fraction of time radio is on | dimensionless |
| $P_{\text{avg}}$ (duty-cycled) | $P_{\text{avg}} \approx P_{\text{tx}} \cdot \text{DC} + P_{\text{sleep}}\cdot(1-\text{DC})$ | Average node power | mW |
| CPS Control Law | $u(t) = K_p\,e(t) + K_i\!\int_0^t\! e(\tau)d\tau + K_d\,\dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | PID controller output (a CPS canonical example) | control signal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where the pipe symbol trick comes in:** Notice the table uses `\dfrac`, `\int_0^t`, `\vert` nowhere yet — but **if you ever write `|x|` inside a table cell**, render it as `\vert x\vert`. KTU scripts are auto-checked, and a stray `|` will split the table row.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Grid (CPS):** SCADA-based power distribution with sub-millisecond control.
- **Autonomous Vehicles (CPS):** Sensor fusion (lidar, camera, radar) → AI inference → steering/brake actuation.
- **Precision Agriculture (IoT):** Soil-moisture sensors → LoRaWAN → cloud analytics → irrigation valve (CPS closure).
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT / Industry 4.0):** Predictive maintenance on factory floors — combines IoT sensing with CPS-grade control.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average Power of a Duty-Cycled IoT Node

Most IoT nodes spend 99% of their lifetime **asleep** and wake only briefly to sense, transmit, and return to sleep. The board loves this derivation because it tests unit consistency and duty-cycle intuition.

**Given:**
- Active (TX) power: $P_{\text{tx}}$ in **mW**
- Sleep power: $P_{\text{sleep}}$ in **mW**
- Active time per cycle: $T_{\text{on}}$ in **ms**
- Sleep time per cycle: $T_{\text{sleep}}$ in **ms**

**Goal:** Derive an expression for average power $P_{\text{avg}}$.

**Step 1 — Define duty cycle.**
The duty cycle is the fraction of time the node is active.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DC} = \frac{T_{\text{on}}}{T_{\text{on}} + T_{\text{sleep}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the fraction of time spent sleeping.**
If the node is "on" for fraction DC, it is asleep for fraction $(1 - \text{DC})$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1 - \text{DC}) = 1 - \frac{T_{\text{on}}}{T_{\text{on}} + T_{\text{sleep}}} = \frac{T_{\text{sleep}}}{T_{\text{on}} + T_{\text{sleep}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total energy over one full period $T_{\text{period}} = T_{\text{on}} + T_{\text{sleep}}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{period}} = P_{\text{tx}}\cdot T_{\text{on}} + P_{\text{sleep}} \cdot T_{\text{sleep}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Average power over the period (power = energy ÷ time).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{E_{\text{period}}}{T_{\text{on}} + T_{\text{sleep}}} = \frac{P_{\text{tx}}\cdot T_{\text{on}} + P_{\text{sleep}} \cdot T_{\text{sleep}}}{T_{\text{on}} + T_{\text{sleep}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Rewrite in duty-cycle form.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{avg}} = P_{\text{tx}}\cdot\text{DC} + P_{\text{sleep}}\cdot(1 - \text{DC})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final closed form:**
> $$P_{\text{avg}} = P_{\text{tx}}\cdot\text{DC} + P_{\text{sleep}}\cdot(1 - \text{DC})$$
> This is the **exact** expression. Note the linear interpolation — it is **not** a geometric or harmonic mean.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU style)

**Problem:** An IoT temperature sensor node has $P_{\text{tx}} = 200 \text{ mW}$ (active) and $P_{\text{sleep}} = 0.05 \text{ mW}$. It wakes every $T_{\text{period}} = 60 \text{ s}$ and stays awake for $T_{\text{on}} = 1.2 \text{ s}$.
Find: (i) duty cycle, (ii) average power, (iii) battery life on a $2400 \text{ mAh}$, $3.7 \text{ V}$ Li-ion battery.

**Step 1 — Duty Cycle.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DC} = \frac{1.2}{60} = 0.02 = 2\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Average Power.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{avg}} &= 200 \cdot 0.02 + 0.05 \cdot (1 - 0.02) \\
&= 4.0 + 0.05 \cdot 0.98 \\
&= 4.0 + 0.049 \\
&= 4.049 \text{ mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Battery Capacity in Joules.**
Battery energy $E_{\text{bat}} = V \cdot Q$ where $Q$ in coulombs $= 2400 \text{ mAh} \times 3600 \text{ s/h} \times 10^{-3}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= 2400 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 3600 = 8640 \text{ C} \\
E_{\text{bat}} &= 3.7 \cdot 8640 = 31\,968 \text{ J} \approx 31.97 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Lifetime in Seconds.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{life}} = \frac{E_{\text{bat}}}{P_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{31\,968}{4.049 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 7.89 \times 10^{6} \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Days.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{life}} = \frac{7.89 \times 10^{6}}{86\,400} \approx 91.3 \text{ days} \approx 3 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — End-to-End Latency Budget for a CPS Control Loop

**Given:**
- $T_{\text{sense}} = 2 \text{ ms}$
- $T_{\text{proc}} = 1 \text{ ms}$
- $T_{\text{tx}} = 5 \text{ ms}$
- $T_{\text{prop}} = 0.5 \text{ ms}$ (assume fibre, $c = 3\times10^{8}\text{ m/s}$, distance $d = 100 \text{ km}$)
- $T_{\text{queue}} = 1.5 \text{ ms}$

**Check propagation delay explicitly:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{prop}} = \frac{d}{c} = \frac{100\,000}{3\times 10^{8}} \approx 3.33\times 10^{-4}\text{ s} = 0.333\text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$
Rounded up to 0.5 ms with switching and queuing inside the carrier network.

**Total loop latency:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{e2e}} &= 2 + 1 + 5 + 0.5 + 1.5 = 10 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** This CPS loop meets the typical hard-real-time budget of **$L \leq 10$ ms**.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — IoT vs CPS vs M2M vs WSN

| Dimension | IoT | CPS | M2M | WSN |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary goal** | Connectivity + data | Closed-loop control | Machine-to-machine comm. | Dense environmental sensing |
| **Feedback loop** | Often open | Always closed | Either | Usually open |
| **Time-criticality** | Best-effort | Real-time / hard real-time | Best-effort | Best-effort |
