| **Computation** | Edge + Cloud | Embedded + Edge | Minimal | On-node only |
| **Typical network** | Internet (TCP/IP) | Industrial bus + IP | Cellular/SMS | 802.15.4 mesh |
| **Example** | Smart watch | Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) | Vending machine restock alert | Forest fire detection sensor grid |
| **KTU buzzword** | "Internet connectivity" | "Tight cyber-physical coupling" | "M2M SIM" | "Self-organizing mesh" |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Five-Layer IoT Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1[SensorNode1 TempHumidity] --> node2[GatewayAggregator BLE ZigBee]
    node3[SensorNode2 MotionPIR] --> node2
    node4[SensorNode3 SoilMoisture] --> node2
    node2 --> node5[EdgeFogLayer LocalPreprocessing]
    node5 --> node6[CloudPlatform AWSIoTAzureIoT]
    node6 --> node7[AnalyticsML AIInference]
    node7 --> node8[ApplicationLayer SmartHomeApp]
    node8 --> node9[BusinessLayer KPIDashboard]
    node6 -.control.-> node2
    node2 -.actuate.-> node10[ActuatorValvePump]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows show upstream data flow (sensor → cloud → app). The dotted `-.control.->` arrow shows the **CPS-style feedback closure** — the cloud sends a control command back through the gateway to an actuator. This single diagram is sufficient for any "draw and explain the IoT architecture" question.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — CPS Three-Tier Closed-Loop Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PhysicalPlant MotorVehicle] --> B[SensorTemperaturePressureSpeed]
    B --> C[MicrocontrollerRTOS EmbeddedController]
    C --> D[CommunicationBus CAN Ethernet]
    D --> E[CloudDecisionLayer MPCOptimizer]
    E --> D
    D --> C
    C --> F[ActuatorMotorValveBrake]
    F --> A
    G[HumanOperator SupervisorHMI] -.supervise.-> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The loop `A → B → C → D → E → D → C → F → A` is the **physical control feedback loop** (cyber → physical → cyber). The dotted arrow `G -.supervise.-> E` represents the **human-in-the-loop / supervisory tier**, completing the three-tier CPS model.

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Energy Model of an IoT Node

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    n1[BatteryCell 3p7V LiIon] --> n2[VoltageRegulator LDO Buck]
    n2 --> n3[Microcontroller MCU ARM Cortex]
    n3 --> n4[RadioTransceiver BLE LoRa]
    n4 --> n5[Antenna50ohm]
    n6[SensorSubsystem I2C SPI] --> n3
    n7[DutyCycleController WakeupTimer] -.gate.-> n4
    n7 -.gate.-> n3
```

**Explanation for KTU answer sheets:** The block diagram above maps the **energy flow path** of an IoT node. Each block draws current from the battery; the duty-cycle controller gates the MCU and radio, collapsing their average current draw to the equation derived in §3.1.

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — IoT vs CPS Information Flow Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IoT_OpenLoop
        s1[Sensor] --> s2[CloudAnalytics]
        s2 --> s3[DashboardReport]
    end
    subgraph CPS_ClosedLoop
        c1[Sensor] --> c2[Controller]
        c2 --> c3[Actuator]
        c3 --> c1
    end
```

**Engineering takeaway:** The IoT sub-diagram has **no arrow returning to the sensor** — it is an *open* information flow. The CPS sub-diagram has the arrow `c3 → c1` closing the loop, which is the defining structural property of any CPS.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### **Q1.** Define the Internet of Things (IoT). List any four enabling technologies of IoT. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> The **Internet of Things (IoT)** is a network of physical objects — devices, vehicles, appliances and other items — embedded with **sensors, software, electronics, actuators and connectivity**, which enables these objects to **collect, exchange and act on data** over the Internet without requiring human-to-human or human-to-computer interaction.

**Four enabling technologies:**
1. **RFID (Radio-Frequency Identification)** — for unique identification of "things".
2. **WSN (Wireless Sensor Networks)** — for distributed sensing.
3. **Cloud Computing** — for storage, analytics and elasticity.
4. **Embedded Systems / Microcontrollers** — for on-node intelligence and actuation.

*(Acceptable alternatives: IPv6/6LoWPAN, Big-data analytics, AI/ML, Edge/Fog computing, NFC, BLE, ZigBee, LoRaWAN.)*

---

#### **Q2.** Differentiate between IoT and CPS with suitable examples. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | IoT | CPS |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Sense and communicate | Sense, compute and **control** |
| **Loop** | Mostly open-loop | Strictly closed-loop with feedback |
| **Time-critical** | Generally best-effort | Often real-time / hard real-time |
| **Primary focus** | Data and connectivity | Tight cyber-physical integration |
| **Example** | Smart thermostat reporting room temperature to a phone | ABS (anti-lock braking) — wheel-speed sensor → ECU → brake-actuator pressure modulation, all in <10 ms |

**One-line crisp form:** *IoT observes the world; CPS **changes** the world in response.*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Architecture Deep-Dive** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **five-layer IoT architecture** with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of each layer. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The five-layer IoT architecture is the standard reference model adopted in KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 1. Each layer has a clearly defined role:

1. **Perception / Sensing Layer (Layer 1):**
   - The physical layer where sensors and actuators interface with the environment.
   - Converts physical phenomena (temperature, humidity, pressure, motion, light) into electrical signals.
   - Identifies "things" uniquely — via RFID tags, QR codes, or MAC/IP addresses.
   - Uses short-range radios: **ZigBee, BLE, NFC**.

2. **Network / Transport Layer (Layer 2):**
   - Transfers sensed data from perception layer to processing layer.
   - Protocols: **Wi-Fi, 6LoWPAN, LoRaWAN, MQTT, CoAP, AMQP, HTTP**.
   - Handles addressing (IPv6), routing (RPL), and quality-of-service.

3. **Middleware / Processing Layer (Layer 3):**
   - Performs data filtering, aggregation, semantic tagging, context detection.
   - Stores data in cloud or fog nodes.
   - Manages devices, authentication, access control.

4. **Application Layer (Layer 4):**
   - Domain-specific services: smart home, smart agriculture, industrial monitoring, healthcare.
   - Provides user interfaces, dashboards, alerts.

5. **Business Layer (Layer 5):**
   - Converts processed data into business intelligence.
   - Manages applications, business models, ROI, future evolution.

*(Use the Mermaid diagram from §4.1 directly in your answer sheet — replace with a hand-drawn equivalent.)*

**(b)** Describe the **architecture of a Cyber Physical System (CPS)** with a block diagram and explain any **two real-time applications**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

A CPS is built from **three tiers** interconnected by bidirectional data paths that form a **closed control loop**:

- **Tier 1 — Physical / Plant Layer:** The physical process being controlled (e.g., a robotic arm, a power grid, a vehicle).
- **Tier 2 — Cyber Layer:** Embedded controllers, communication networks, edge/cloud compute. Implements the control law (e.g., PID, MPC).
- **Tier 3 — Human / Supervisory Layer:** Operators, dashboards, supervisory control systems.

**Control loop:** Plant → Sensor → Controller → Actuator → Plant.

**Two real-time applications:**

1. **Autonomous Vehicles:** Multiple sensors (camera, lidar, radar, GPS, IMU) feed into an AI inference engine; the controller commands steering, throttle, and brake actuators in real time (latency < 50 ms for collision avoidance).
2. **Smart Grid / SCADA:** Power-generation and distribution are monitored and controlled by networked embedded systems. Demand-response loops operate on minute-scale, while frequency-control loops operate on sub-second scale.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Energy & Latency Engineering** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** An IoT sensor node transmits data at $P_{\text{tx}} = 150 \text{ mW}$ when active and consumes $P_{\text{sleep}} = 0.08 \text{ mW}$ when sleeping. It wakes once every $T_{\text{period}} = 30 \text{ s}$ for $T_{\text{on}} = 0.9 \text{ s}$.
**(i)** Compute the duty cycle. **(ii)** Compute the average power consumption. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Duty Cycle:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DC} = \frac{T_{\text{on}}}{T_{\text{period}}} = \frac{0.9}{30} = 0.03 = 3\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Average Power:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{avg}} &= P_{\text{tx}} \cdot \text{DC} + P_{\text{sleep}} \cdot (1 - \text{DC}) \\
&= 150 \cdot 0.03 + 0.08 \cdot 0.97 \\
&= 4.50 + 0.0776 \\
&= 4.5776 \text{ mW} \approx 4.58 \text{ mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Inference:** Because the node sleeps 97% of the time, the average power is dominated by the active bursts — confirming why duty-cycling is the **single most effective** energy-saving strategy in IoT nodes.

**(b)** With reference to part (a), estimate the **battery life in days** on a $2500 \text{ mAh}$, $3.7 \text{ V}$ battery. List **three duty-cycling strategies** used to extend IoT node lifetime. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Battery capacity in joules:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= 2500 \text{ mAh} = 2.5 \text{ Ah} = 2.5 \cdot 3600 = 9000 \text{ C} \\
E_{\text{bat}} &= V \cdot Q = 3.7 \cdot 9000 = 33\,300 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Lifetime:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{life}} &= \frac{E_{\text{bat}}}{P_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{33\,300}{4.5776 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 7.275 \times 10^{6} \text{ s} \\
t_{\text{life}} &= \frac{7.275 \times 10^{6}}{86\,400} \approx 84.2 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Three duty-cycling strategies:**

1. **Scheduled Sleep / Wake-up:** Node sleeps according to a fixed or learned schedule; optimal when traffic is predictable.
2. **Event-Triggered Wake-up:** Node sleeps until an external interrupt (motion sensor, button, threshold) wakes it; optimal for bursty traffic.
3. **On-Demand Polling:** Gateway queries the node only when data is needed; optimal for sparse telemetry.

*(Acceptable fourth: **TDMA-synchronized wake-up**, **asynchronous wake-up radios**, **energy-harvesting-aware adaptive duty cycling**.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (where students lose marks)**
>
> 1. **Confusing IoT and CPS in definitions.** Saying *"IoT is a closed-loop system"* costs you the definition mark. Always clarify: IoT = data-centric (often open-loop); CPS = control-centric (always closed-loop).
> 2. **Skipping the battery-conversion step.** Examiners expect you to convert **mAh → C → J** explicitly using $E = VQ$. Skipping units = full-mark loss on part (b) of energy questions.
> 3. **Forgetting the units.** "$P_{\text{avg}} = 4.05$" without **mW** or **W** loses a mark. KTU 2024 strictly enforces unit notation.
> 4. **Drawing a layer diagram without naming the protocols** in each layer. Examiners award partial credit only when protocol names (MQTT, CoAP, RPL, 6LoWPAN) are listed inside the diagram.
> 5. **Misapplying duty-cycle equation.** The formula is **linear**, not geometric. Some students mistakenly write $P_{\text{avg}} = \sqrt{P_{\text{tx}} \cdot P_{\text{sleep}}}$, which is the geometric mean — wrong.
> 6. **In CPS answers, omitting the feedback arrow.** A diagram without the arrow returning from actuator to plant is graded as IoT, not CPS.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision before the KTU board exam — go through this list once.**

- ☐ IoT = **Sense + Connect + Analyse**; CPS = **Sense + Decide + Actuate** (closed loop).
- ☐ The **five layers of IoT**: Perception, Network, Middleware, Application, Business.
- ☐ The **three tiers of CPS**: Physical, Cyber, Human-Supervisory.
- ☐ **Availability formula:** $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR})$.
- ☐ **Duty cycle:** $\text{DC} = T_{\text{on}}/(T_{\text{on}}+T_{\text{sleep}})$.
- ☐ **Average IoT node power:** $P_{\text{avg}} = P_{\text{tx}}\cdot\text{DC} + P_{\text{sleep}}\cdot(1-\text{DC})$.
- ☐ **Battery energy:** $E_{\text{bat}} = V \cdot Q$ with $Q$ in coulombs.
- ☐ **Latency budget** for hard real-time CPS: ≤ **10 ms** end-to-end.
- ☐ **IPv6** provides $2^{128}$ addresses — sufficient for global IoT addressing.
- ☐ **Key protocols** to memorize: 6LoWPAN, RPL, MQTT, CoAP, ZigBee, BLE, LoRaWAN.
- ☐ **Key sensors** to memorize: temperature (thermistor/thermocouple), humidity (DHT22), motion (PIR), gas (MQ-2), pressure (piezoresistive).
- ☐ **Key actuators** to memorize: DC motor, stepper, servo, solenoid, relay, piezoelectric stack.
- ☐ **CPS canonical control law:** PID — write it from memory: $u(t) = K_p e + K_i\!\int e\,dt + K_d\,\dot e$.
- ☐ **Common real-world CPS:** ABS, autonomous vehicles, drones, smart grid, robotics, medical infusion pumps.
- ☐ **Common real-world IoT:** smart homes, wearables, smart agriculture, smart cities, supply-chain tracking.
- ☐ Always **state the units** (mW, ms, kbps, %) and **name the protocols** in your answers — these are cheap marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Technologies for Network-Based Systems: Multicore CPUs and Multithreading Technologies, GPU Computing, Virtual Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Technologies for Network-Based Systems: Multicore CPUs and Multithreading Technologies, GPU Computing, Virtual Machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Multicore CPUs

**Formal Definition:**
A *Multicore CPU* is a single computing component (a single physical integrated circuit, i.e., a single die) that contains **two or more independent processing units** called *cores*, each capable of reading and executing program instructions independently of the others.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The focus is on *architectural* technologies enabling network-based systems, not microelectronics fabrication. Treat cores as independent execution engines sharing memory hierarchy resources.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a single chef (single-core CPU) in a kitchen. He can only cook one dish at a time. Now hire four chefs and put them in the same kitchen (shared refrigerator, shared pantry, shared oven). Each can cook a *different* dish *concurrently*, communicating by yelling across the kitchen (shared memory). This is precisely a multicore CPU — multiple execution engines sharing memory and I/O resources on one chip.

Key metrics (standard):
- **Core Count (n)**: typically 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64.
- **Clock Frequency (f)**: measured in **GHz (Gigahertz)**.
- **Die**: the silicon substrate containing all cores.

---

## 1.2 Multithreading Technologies

**Formal Definition:**
*Multithreading* is the ability of a CPU (or a single core) to manage multiple *threads of execution* within a single process by interleaving instruction streams, either *coarsely-grained* (context switch on stalls) or *simultaneously* (fine-grained / simultaneous multithreading — SMT).

Three classical approaches (per KTU syllabus lineage, Hwang):
1. **Coarse-Grain Multithreading (CGMT)**: switches thread only on long-latency stalls (cache miss).
2. **Fine-Grain Multithreading (FGMT)**: switches threads every cycle (round-robin).
3. **Simultaneous Multithreading (SMT)**: issues instructions from multiple threads *in the same cycle* to different execution units.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hyper-Threading Technology (HTT)** as used by Intel is the commercial branding of SMT. From an OS perspective, one physical SMT core exposes itself as *two* logical processors.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A single chef working on a stew (thread A) discovers he's waiting for water to boil. Instead of staring at the pot, he slices vegetables for a salad (thread B), then switches back. Coarse-grain multithreading. Fine-grain would be chopping one slice of onion, then one slice of cucumber, alternating rapidly. SMT is having two *different* cooks inside the same brain, simultaneously plating two dishes to use *all four burners* (ALUs) at once.

---

## 1.3 GPU Computing (General-Purpose GPU — GPGPU)

**Formal Definition:**
A *Graphics Processing Unit (GPU)* is a massively parallel processor originally designed for rendering graphics, now widely used as a *throughput-oriented* coprocessor. Modern GPUs employ **SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data)** or more precisely **SIMT (Single Instruction, Multiple Threads)** execution, where thousands of lightweight threads execute the same kernel in lockstep across thousands of data items.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A CPU is a single fast delivery scooter — flexible, agile, can deliver any item anywhere. A GPU is a fleet of **5000 identical scooters** moving in **convoy formation**. They all leave at the same time, follow the same route, deliver to neighboring houses. Individually each is slow, but together they cover a neighborhood in minutes. This is *data parallelism*: same operation, many data items.

Key standard metric: **CUDA cores** (NVIDIA terminology), **Stream Processors** (AMD), or **Shader cores**.

---

## 1.4 Virtual Machines (VMs)

**Formal Definition:**
A *Virtual Machine* is a software abstraction that presents to a *guest* operating system or application an illusion of a complete, dedicated hardware environment, while sharing physical hardware with other VMs under the control of a *Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM)* or *hypervisor*.

Two canonical categories:
- **System VM (Hardware-level VM)**: hosts an entire OS; managed by a hypervisor. Examples: VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM.
- **Process VM (Application-level VM)**: provides an abstract instruction set executed by a runtime engine. Examples: JVM (Java Virtual Machine), CLR (.NET).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a high-rise apartment building. The land, water pipes, electrical wiring, and elevator shafts are *physical hardware*. Each apartment is a *virtual machine*. The building manager (hypervisor) gives each tenant the illusion that they own the whole building. Tenant A can paint his walls red; tenant B can knock down a non-load-bearing wall — they never affect each other because the manager isolates them. Yet they share the same foundation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three classical properties of a VM** (Popek & Goldberg criteria, often tested):
> 1. **Equivalence / Fidelity** — guest behavior identical to native.
> 2. **Efficiency / Performance** — most guest instructions execute directly on hardware.
> 3. **Resource Control / Safety** — guest cannot alter host resources it does not own.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Multicore CPU — Architectural Anatomy

A multicore chip is structured as follows:

- **n Cores**: each core is essentially a complete, independent processor (with its own L1 instruction cache, L1 data cache, integer/FP pipeline, register file, branch predictor).
- **Shared L2 or L3 Cache** (some architectures — Intel "Ring", AMD "Infinity Fabric" — share LLC).
- **Shared Memory Controller** (DDR interface).
- **Shared I/O Hub** (PCIe, USB, SATA).
- **Interconnect**: bus, crossbar, or NoC (Network-on-Chip) — e.g., **Intel QuickPath Interconnect (QPI)**, **AMD Infinity Fabric**.

**Why "Multi" instead of "Mega-fast single"?**
Three physical walls halt single-core scaling (the *Power Wall*, the *Memory Wall*, the *ILP Wall*). Multicore is the engineering response: instead of one core running at **5 GHz** (heat bound), use **8 cores** at **3 GHz** (thermally feasible, comparable performance at lower power).

### The "Why" — Amdahl's Law Insight (KTU must-know)

When parallelizing a workload, the *serial fraction* **f** limits speedup regardless of how many cores **n** are used. This is Amdhal's Law.

## 2.2 Multithreading Technologies — Operational Theory

**Coarse-Grain Multithreading (CGMT)**:
- Single thread executes until a long-latency event (cache miss, branch misprediction).
- Pipeline is *switched* (flushed of pending instructions) to a new thread.
- **Throughput benefit**: hidden miss latency.
- **Cost**: pipeline refill penalty (15–25 cycles), switch is infrequent.

**Fine-Grain Multithreading (FGMT)**:
- Every cycle, the issue logic rotates to a new thread.
- Switch cost amortized to zero because pipeline is never drained.
- Used historically in Tera MTA / Horizon supercomputers.
- **Drawback**: per-thread latency *increases* because each thread gets 1/n of cycles.

**Simultaneous Multithreading (SMT)**:
- Multiple threads issue *in the same cycle* to *different* functional units of a wide-issue superscalar pipeline.
- Most efficient: dynamically balances resource utilization.
- Used in Intel Core i-series (since 2002, Banias), IBM POWER8/9, Oracle SPARC.

## 2.3 GPU Computing — SIMT Architecture

A modern GPU contains:
- **Many SMs (Streaming Multiprocessors)** — e.g., 80+ in NVIDIA A100.
- Each SM contains: integer ALU, FP ALU, special function units, register file, warp scheduler, L1/shared memory.
- A **warp = 32 threads** executed in lockstep (SIMT model).
- **Thread block (CUDA)** / **Compute unit work-group (OpenCL)**: group of warps that can synchronize via `__syncthreads()`.

The host (CPU) launches a **kernel** (function). The GPU's hardware scheduler (HWS) dispatches thread blocks to SMs; each SM executes its warps in a round-robin to hide memory latency.

**Memory hierarchy** (must know for exam):
| Level | Location | Typical Size | Latency (cycles) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Registers | Inside SM | 64 KB per SM | 0 |
| Shared Memory / L1 | Inside SM | 128 KB configurable | ~30 |
| L2 Cache | Whole GPU | 4–40 MB | ~200 |
| Global Memory (HBM/GDDR) | Off-chip | 16–80 GB | ~400–800 |

## 2.4 Virtual Machines — Theory of Operation

A **Hypervisor (VMM)** runs in a more privileged mode than the guest OS. For x86, this requires *hardware-assisted virtualization*: **Intel VT-x** (with a new "non-root" mode and VMCS — Virtual Machine Control Structure) and **AMD-V**.

The hypervisor performs:
1. **Translating** guest virtual addresses → host physical addresses (EPT — Extended Page Tables).
2. **Intercepting** privileged instructions (e.g., `MOV CR3`, `IN/OUT`, `CPUID`).
3. **Emulating** I/O devices (network, disk) via para-virtualized or fully emulated drivers.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they recur in numerical problems.**

| Symbol / Term | Meaning / Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $n$ | Number of cores / processors | dimensionless |
| $f$ | Serial fraction of a program (not parallelizable) | dimensionless $\in [0,1]$ |
| $1-f$ | Parallel fraction | dimensionless |
| $S(n)$ | Speedup with $n$ processors | dimensionless |
| $E(n)$ | Parallel efficiency | dimensionless $\in [0,1]$ |
| $T_s$ | Sequential execution time | seconds (s) |
| $T_p$ | Parallel execution time on $n$ cores | seconds (s) |
| $P$ | Throughput (transactions per second) | TPS |
| $R$ | Memory bandwidth | GB/s |
| $L$ | Memory latency | cycles or ns |

### Performance & Speedup Equations

| Equation | Description |
|---|---|
| $S(n) = \dfrac{T_s}{T_p(n)}$ | General definition of speedup |
| $S(n) = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}$ | **Amdhal's Law** (fixed workload) |
| $S_{\max} = \lim_{n\to\infty} S(n) = \dfrac{1}{1-f}$ | Maximum speedup under Amdhal |
| $E(n) = \dfrac{S(n)}{n} = \dfrac{1}{n\left[(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}\right]}$ | Efficiency |
| Gustafson: $S(n) = n - f(n-1)$ | Scaled workload speedup |

### Multicore / SMT Formulas

| Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Ideal throughput (SMT) | $ILP_{max} \times n_{threads}$ | Approximate; degraded by shared resources |
| Pipeline CPI | $CPI = CPI_{base} + \text{miss penalty} \times \text{miss rate}$ | Per-thread |

### GPU Throughput

| Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|
| Arithmetic Intensity | $AI = \dfrac{\text{FLOPs}}{\text{Bytes loaded/stored}}$ |
| Execution Time | $T = \max\left(\dfrac{\text{FLOPs}}{R_{compute}},\; \dfrac{\text{Bytes}}{R_{bandwidth}}\right)$ |

> **Note:** In all table cells above, the absolute value or division symbol is rendered using LaTeX `\dfrac` / `\vert`-style notation to avoid breaking markdown tables.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Multicore CPUs** power laptops, servers, and embedded SoCs (mobile phones use **ARM big.LITTLE** — 8 cores in heterogeneous arrangement).
- **Multithreading** powers web servers handling **10,000+ concurrent connections** per process (e.g., Node.js, NGINX worker pools).
- **GPUs** drive deep learning: training GPT-scale models needs **petaFLOPS** of FP16 compute — only achievable on GPU clusters.
- **Virtual Machines** enable cloud computing (AWS EC2, Azure VMs) — a single physical server hosts 30–100 VMs, achieving **70–80% utilization** vs 10–20% for non-virtualized servers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Amdhal's Law (Full Working)

### Statement

> Given a workload with serial fraction $f$, the maximum speedup achievable using $n$ processors is constrained by the *unparallelizable* portion.

### Step 1 — Partition the workload

Consider a program whose total sequential execution time on one core is $T_s$. Partition it:

- The **serial part** has time $(1-f)\cdot T_s$.
- The **parallel part** has time $f \cdot T_s$.

Wait — note the convention differs across textbooks. We adopt the convention used in KTU/Hwang:

- $f$ = fraction of program that is **parallelizable**.
- $(1-f)$ = fraction that is **serial**.

### Step 2 — Time on a single processor

On one processor:

$$T(1) = (1-f)\cdot T_s + f \cdot T_s = T_s$$

### Step 3 — Time on $n$ processors

The serial part cannot be sped up:

$$T_{serial} = (1-f)\cdot T_s$$

The parallel part runs on $n$ cores:

$$T_{parallel}(n) = \dfrac{f \cdot T_s}{n}$$

So total parallel execution time:

$$T(n) = (1-f)\cdot T_s + \dfrac{f \cdot T_s}{n}$$

### Step 4 — Speedup

$$S(n) = \dfrac{T(1)}{T(n)} = \dfrac{T_s}{(1-f)\cdot T_s + \dfrac{f\cdot T_s}{n}}$$

Cancel $T_s$ (assuming $T_s > 0$):

$$S(n) = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}$$

This is **Amdhal's Law** in canonical KTU form.

### Step 5 — Limiting behavior

Take the limit as $n \to \infty$:

$$S_{\max} = \lim_{n\to\infty} \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}} = \dfrac{1}{1-f + 0} = \dfrac{1}{1-f}$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** If just **5%** of your program is serial, i.e., $f = 0.95$ and $(1-f) = 0.05$, then

$$S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{1-0.95} = \dfrac{1}{0.05} = 20\times$$

Even adding 1000 cores caps you at 20×. This is why parallel programmers obsess over eliminating serial regions.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-style)

> **Problem:** A program requires $T_s = 100$ seconds on a single-core CPU. **90%** of the program is parallelizable. The system is upgraded to an **8-core** CPU. Compute (a) speedup $S(8)$, (b) efficiency $E(8)$, (c) maximum possible speedup with infinite cores.

### Given
- $T_s = 100$ s, $n = 8$, $f = 0.90$, $(1-f) = 0.10$.

### (a) Speedup $S(8)$

Apply Amdhal's Law:

$$S(8) = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}} = \dfrac{1}{0.10 + \dfrac{0.90}{8}}$$

Compute the parallel portion's contribution:

$$\dfrac{0.90}{8} = 0.1125$$

Sum:

$$0.10 + 0.1125 = 0.2125$$

Take the reciprocal:

$$S(8) = \dfrac{1}{0.2125} \approx 4.706 \times$$

### (b) Efficiency $E(8)$

$$E(8) = \dfrac{S(8)}{n} = \dfrac{4.706}{8} \approx 0.5883 = 58.83\%$$

### (c) Maximum possible speedup

$$S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{1-f} = \dfrac{1}{1-0.90} = \dfrac{1}{0.10} = 10\times$$

**Conclusion:** Even with 1,000,000 cores the speedup cannot exceed **10×** because the 10% serial portion is the bottleneck.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Multithreading Trade-offs

| Technique | Issue Rate | Latency Hiding | Pipeline Drain Cost | Resource Use per Thread |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Superscalar (no MT) | 1 thread | None | None | Full |
| CGMT | 1 thread, switched on stall | Stalls hidden | High (one switch ~ 15–25 cycles) | Full |
| FGMT | 1 thread/cycle | All stalls hidden | Zero (round-robin) | Full |
| SMT | Multiple threads/cycle | All stalls hidden | Zero | Sub-divided |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — CPU vs GPU

| Property | CPU (Multicore) | GPU |
|---|---|---|
| Optimization target | Latency | Throughput |
| Core count | 4–64 heavy cores | 1000s of lightweight lanes |
| Per-core cache | Large (MBs) | Small (KBs) |
| Clock frequency | 2–5 GHz | 1–2.5 GHz |
| Memory type | DDR4/DDR5 | HBM2e/HBM3 |
| Bandwidth | ~50 GB/s | ~2000 GB/s |
| Best for | Sequential, branchy | Data-parallel, regular |
| Threading model | MIMD / SMT | SIMT |
| Programming model | OpenMP, pthreads | CUDA, OpenCL, ROCm |

---

## 3.5 Derived Identity — Gustafson's Law (For Completeness)

**Problem context:** Amdhal fixes the *workload*. In practice, when given $n$ cores, users solve a *bigger* problem in the same wall-clock time. Gustafson's Law formalizes this:

$$S(n) = n - f \cdot (n - 1) = n(1-f) + f$$

**Derivation sketch:**

Let scaled workload on $n$ cores have the same total time $T$ as on 1 core. Serial portion stays at $(1-f)\cdot T$; parallel portion scales to $f \cdot n \cdot T$.

Total time on 1 core would be:

$$T_s = (1-f)\cdot T + f\cdot n \cdot T$$

Speedup:

$$S(n) = \dfrac{T_s}{T} = (1-f) + f \cdot n = n - f(n-1)$$

> **Why this matters:** Gustafson's Law is why we *do* see linear speedup in HPC — users scale up the problem size. Amdhal is the pessimist, Gustafson is the optimist, both are correct in their respective contexts.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style]
**Differentiate between coarse-grain multithreading and simultaneous multithreading (SMT). Mention the key advantage of SMT.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Coarse-Grain MT | Simultaneous MT |
|---|---|---|
| Threads issued per cycle | 1 | Multiple |
| Thread switch trigger | Long-latency stall (cache miss) | Every cycle, opportunistically |
| Pipeline drain | Yes (15–25 cycle penalty) | No |
| Resource utilization | Underutilized between stalls | Maximally utilized |

**Key advantage of SMT:** SMT issues instructions from *multiple* threads in the same cycle to different functional units of a wide-issue pipeline, achieving higher throughput than CGMT or even single-threaded superscalar execution, especially when one thread stalls on memory while another is compute-ready. (Awarded **3 marks** for correct table + advantage statement.)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Style]
**Explain the difference between a System Virtual Machine and a Process Virtual Machine with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **System VM** (hardware-level): Provides a complete system environment — boots an entire guest OS. The hypervisor manages physical resources and multiplexes them among multiple OS instances. *Example:* VMware ESXi, KVM, Hyper-V.
- **Process VM** (application-level): Runs as a normal process inside an existing OS, providing an abstract instruction set (bytecode) that is interpreted/JIT-compiled by a runtime engine. *Example:* JVM for Java, CLR for .NET languages.

System VM is used for server consolidation and cloud computing; Process VM is used for cross-platform application portability. (**3 marks** for correct distinction + one valid example per type.)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Explain the working of **Amdhal's Law**. A program takes **T = 120 seconds** on a single-core system. **92%** of the program is parallelizable. Compute the **speedup** when executed on a system with **16 cores**, the **efficiency**, and the **maximum theoretical speedup** achievable. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the **three classical multithreading approaches** (coarse-grain, fine-grain, and simultaneous) with diagrams and state **two advantages** and **one disadvantage** of each. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 — Identify given values:**

- $T_s = 120$ s, $f = 0.92$, $(1-f) = 0.08$, $n = 16$.

**Step 2 — Apply Amdhal's Law:**

$$S(n) = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}} = \dfrac{1}{0.08 + \dfrac{0.92}{16}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the parallel portion term:**

$$\dfrac{0.92}{16} = 0.0575$$

**Step 4 — Sum the denominator:**

$$0.08 + 0.0575 = 0.1375$$

**Step 5 — Invert:**

$$S(16) = \dfrac{1}{0.1375} \approx 7.27 \times$$

**Step 6 — Efficiency:**

$$E(16) = \dfrac{S(16)}{n} = \dfrac{7.27}{16} \approx 0.4544 = 45.44\%$$

**Step 7 — Maximum speedup:**

$$S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{1-f} = \dfrac{1}{1-0.92} = \dfrac{1}{0.08} = 12.5 \times$$

**Final parallel execution time:**

$$T_p = \dfrac{T_s}{S(16)} = \dfrac{120}{7.27} \approx 16.5 \text{ seconds}$$

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Coarse-Grain MT (CGMT):**
- Thread executes until a long-latency stall; then processor switches to a new thread.
- *Advantages:* Simple hardware; minimal cross-thread resource contention.
- *Disadvantage:* Pipeline must be drained/refilled on switch (~20 cycle penalty).

**Fine-Grain MT (FGMT):**
- Switches thread every cycle in round-robin order.
- *Advantages:* No pipeline drain; long latencies naturally hidden.
- *Disadvantage:* Per-thread latency increases; single-thread performance suffers.

**Simultaneous MT (SMT):**
- Multiple threads issue simultaneously to different functional units in same cycle.
- *Advantages:* Highest throughput; full utilization of wide-issue resources.
- *Disadvantage:* Complex hardware; shared cache and TLB contention.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "SMT = two cores." This is wrong. SMT is a *single physical core* presenting itself as multiple *logical* processors. Do not equate SMT with multicore.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]

**(a)** Explain the **SIMT execution model** used in modern GPUs. How does it differ from SIMD? Describe the **memory hierarchy** of a typical GPU with approximate latencies. (7 marks)

**(b)** With the aid of a layered diagram, explain how **system virtual machines** are implemented using a **hypervisor**. Discuss the **Popek–Goldberg virtualization criteria** and their significance. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a)

**SIMT (Single Instruction, Multiple Threads) Model:**
- The GPU executes *warps* (groups of 32 threads). All 32 threads in a warp execute the **same instruction** in lockstep but operate on **different data items** from their own registers.
- SIMT differs from classical SIMD in two ways:
  1. SIMT threads have **independent control flow** (via predicated execution and warp divergence).
  2. SIMT threads have **independent register state** and can synchronize via shared memory.

**GPU Memory Hierarchy:**

| Level | Size (typical) | Latency |
|---|---|---|
| Registers | 64 KB per SM | 0 cycles |
| Shared Memory / L1 | 128 KB per SM | ~20–30 cycles |
| L2 Cache | 4–40 MB (whole GPU) | ~200 cycles |
| Global HBM | 16–80 GB | ~400–800 cycles |

Throughput is maximized when kernels have **high arithmetic intensity** (FLOPs/byte ratio) so that the GPU stays compute-bound rather than memory-bound.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**System VM Layered Architecture** (from bottom to top):
1. **Physical Hardware**: CPU, RAM, disk, NIC.
2. **Hypervisor (VMM)**: software layer (Type-1 bare-metal like ESXi, or Type-2 hosted like VirtualBox) that controls all hardware.
3. **Guest Operating Systems**: Windows, Linux, etc., each believing it owns the hardware.
4. **Guest Applications**: run inside each guest OS.

**Popek–Goldberg Criteria for a "real" VMM:**
1. **Fidelity**: A program run inside the VM produces the same results as if run natively.
2. **Efficiency**: Most guest instructions execute *directly* on the CPU without hypervisor intervention (only privileged operations are trapped).
3. **Resource Control / Safety**: The VMM has *complete* control over all system resources; a guest cannot access or modify resources not allocated to it.

**Significance:** These criteria define the *minimum* properties that justify calling a system a true virtual machine monitor rather than a mere emulator or simulator.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse *Type-1* (bare-metal, runs directly on hardware — ESXi, Xen) and *Type-2* (hosted, runs as application on host OS — VirtualBox, VMware Workstation). Examiners *will* deduct marks for misclassification.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick revision checklist — read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- **Multicore CPU**: Multiple independent cores on one die sharing memory hierarchy. Solves the power/ILP/memory walls.
- **Three types of multithreading**: CGMT (switch on stall), FGMT (switch per cycle), SMT (issue from many threads per cycle). SMT = Hyper-Threading. Single physical core ≠ multiple cores.
- **Amdhal's Law**: $S(n) = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + f/n}$; max speedup = $1/(1-f)$. The serial fraction *f* caps performance no matter how many cores.
- **Gustafson's Law**: $S(n) = n - f(n-1)$; applies when problem size scales with $n$.
- **GPU SIMT**: 32 threads per warp, lockstep, thousands of warps in flight. Maximize arithmetic intensity.
- **GPU memory**: Registers → Shared/L1 → L2 → HBM. Latency grows ~10× per level.
- **System VM**: Hypervisor multiplexes hardware among guest OSes. Type-1 (bare-metal) vs Type-2 (hosted).
- **Process VM**: Runs as a regular program; examples: JVM, CLR. Used for portability, not consolidation.
- **Popek–Goldberg**: Fidelity + Efficiency + Resource Control. Required to qualify as a VMM.
- **Hardware-assisted virtualization** (Intel VT-x, AMD-V) needed because x86 was not classically virtualizable (sensitive-but-not-privileged instructions existed).
- **Standard metric to memorize**: **Bandwidth** measured in **GB/s**, **Latency** in **ns/cycles**, **Throughput** in **FLOPS / TPS**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Models for Distributed and Cloud Computing: Clusters, Grids, P2P Systems, Clouds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** System Models for Distributed and Cloud Computing: Clusters, Grids, P2P Systems, Clouds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Four Pillars of Modern Distributed System Models

A **Distributed Computing System** is a collection of independent, geographically dispersed, interconnected computers that collaborate to achieve a common objective by coordinating through message passing over a communication network. KTU 2024 scheme classifies these systems into four major architectural models based on their topology, ownership, coupling, and resource-sharing semantics.

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **System Model** in distributed and cloud computing is an abstract representation that specifies the architectural, interactional, and failure-handling assumptions of a distributed system. The four canonical models are:

| # | Model | One-Line Formal Definition |
|---|-------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **Cluster** | A collection of homogeneous, tightly-coupled compute nodes interconnected by a high-speed LAN, treated as a single unified resource. |
| 2 | **Grid** | A collection of heterogeneous, geographically distributed compute resources, dynamically provisioned across multiple administrative domains via virtual organizations. |
| 3 | **P2P System** | A fully decentralized network of peer nodes where each node simultaneously acts as a supplier and consumer of resources and services. |
| 4 | **Cloud** | A virtualized, elastic, on-demand, pay-per-use pool of configurable computing resources accessible over the public Internet. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The four models are **NOT** mutually exclusive. A modern data center may simultaneously exhibit **Cluster** behavior at the rack level, **Grid** behavior across data centers, **P2P** behavior in its content-distribution layer, and **Cloud** behavior toward its end users.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The Power Grid Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a modern city as a *Distributed Computing System*.
> - A **Cluster** is like the **generators inside one power station** — identical turbines wired to the same switchboard, churning out electricity cooperatively for a single neighborhood.
> - A **Grid** is the **inter-state power grid** — different power plants (solar, hydro, nuclear) in different states pool their output, and electricity flows wherever demand is highest, regardless of who owns the plant.
> - A **P2P system** is like a **village microgrid** — every house has solar panels, and every house can both *draw* power from its neighbors and *inject* surplus power back to them; there is no central authority.
> - A **Cloud** is the **electrical socket in your wall** — you don't care which generator, turbine, or state produced the electricity. You plug in your device, consume exactly the watts you need, and get billed only for what you used.

---

## 1.3 Why These Four Models? (The Engineering Motivation)

The evolution from Clusters → Grids → P2P → Clouds mirrors the historical quest to solve four engineering problems:

1. **Scalability Beyond a Single Box** — Clusters solved this first (1990s, Beowulf).
2. **Resource Sharing Across Organizations** — Grids solved this (early 2000s, Globus, SETI@home).
3. **Elimination of Central Coordination** — P2P solved this (2000s, Napster, Gnutella, BitTorrent).
4. **Abstracting Hardware Entirely** — Clouds solved this (2006+, AWS EC2, Azure, GCP).

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Always map any real-world system to *which* of these four models it most closely resembles. Examiners reward correct classification, not just rote memorization.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

The following **bold** metrics are universally used in the KTU 2024 scheme to evaluate these four models:

- **Speedup (S)** — ratio of sequential execution time to parallel execution time. Unit: dimensionless.
- **Efficiency (E)** — Speedup divided by the number of processors. Unit: dimensionless, range [0, 1].
- **Throughput (λ)** — number of completed tasks per unit time. Unit: tasks/second.
- **Latency (L)** — end-to-end delay to complete a single task. Unit: milliseconds.
- **Scalability** — ability of the system to maintain performance as N (number of nodes) grows.
- **Availability (A)** — fraction of time the system is operational. Computed as $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$, where **MTBF** = Mean Time Between Failures and **MTTR** = Mean Time To Repair.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cluster Computing — Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 Architecture

A cluster is built from **commodity-off-the-shelf (COTS)** hardware — standard x86/ARM servers, Ethernet or InfiniBand switches, and a middleware layer (e.g., **OpenHPC**, **Kubernetes** for container orchestration, **Slurm** for job scheduling, **MOSIX** for single-system-image clustering).

### 2.1.2 Taxonomy of Clusters

| Cluster Type | Primary Purpose | Typical Workload |
|--------------|-----------------|------------------|
| **High-Performance Computing (HPC)** Cluster | Maximum floating-point throughput | Scientific simulation, CFD, genomics |
| **High-Availability (HA) Cluster | Eliminate single points of failure | Database replication, banking backends |
| **Load-Balancing Cluster | Distribute client requests | Web-tier, e-commerce frontends |
| **Storage Cluster | Aggregate disk I/O bandwidth | Hadoop HDFS, Ceph, MinIO |

### 2.1.3 Operating Principles

- **Homogeneity** — all nodes run the same OS, CPU architecture, and middleware.
- **Dedicated high-speed LAN** — typical interconnect: **10 GbE, 40 GbE, 100 GbE, or InfiniBand HDR/NDR**.
- **Centralized job scheduler** — a head node (master) arbitrates work distribution to compute nodes (workers).
- **Single System Image (SSI)** — the cluster appears to users as a single, powerful machine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a cluster is *not* a grid:** A cluster assumes a *single administrative domain*, a *homogeneous* hardware profile, and a *trust boundary* that includes all nodes. Grids relax all three assumptions simultaneously.

---

## 2.2 Grid Computing — Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 The Virtual Organization (VO) Concept

The defining abstraction of a grid is the **Virtual Organization** — a dynamic, cross-institutional coalition of users and resources bound by a common goal but governed by *different* local policies. Example: the **Worldwide LHC Computing Grid (WLCG)** federates **170+ data centers across 40+ countries** to process petabytes of CERN particle-collision data.

### 2.2.2 Layered Grid Architecture (Open Grid Services Architecture — OGSA)

The grid stack is conventionally divided into **five logical layers**, each abstracting the one below:

1. **Fabric Layer** — physical resources (CPUs, disks, sensors, telescopes).
2. **Connectivity Layer** — communication and security protocols (TCP/IP, TLS, GSI).
3. **Resource Layer** — information about individual resources (GRAM, GridFTP).
4. **Collective Layer** — directory services, co-allocation, replica management.
5. **Application Layer** — scientific portals, problem-solving environments.

### 2.2.3 Key Properties

- **Heterogeneity** — nodes may differ in CPU, OS, storage, network.
- **Geographic distribution** — across cities, countries, continents.
- **Multi-administrative** — different security policies, AUPs, accounting.
- **Standard interfaces** — historically **OGSI/WSRF**, now evolving to **RESTful OGC APIs**.

---

## 2.3 Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Systems — Deep Dive

### 2.3.1 Taxonomy by Overlay Structure

| Topology | Description | Examples |
|----------|-------------|----------|
| **Centralized P2P (Hybrid)** | A central index server helps peers discover each other | Napster |
| **Unstructured Decentralized** | Peers flood queries through random neighbors | Gnutella 0.4, Freenet |
| **Structured Decentralized (DHT)** | A distributed hash table maps keys to peers deterministically | Chord, Pastry, Kademlia, Cassandra |
| **Hierarchical / Super-peer** | Powerful peers act as regional hubs for weaker peers | Skype (early), KaZaA |

### 2.3.2 The Distributed Hash Table (DHT) — Conceptual Walkthrough

In a DHT, every peer is assigned a **160-bit identifier** (e.g., SHA-1 hash of its IP:port). Every data item is hashed to a key. The DHT routing protocol guarantees that **lookup(key)** reaches the peer responsible for that key in **O(log N)** overlay hops, where N is the total number of peers.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU favourite:** Be ready to compare the **three classic DHT algorithms** — Chord (ring-based, O(log N) hops, O(log N) state), Pastry (prefix-based, O(log N) hops via numeric ID matching), and Kademlia (XOR-distance metric, O(log N) hops, highly resilient to churn).

### 2.3.3 The CAP Theorem Lens

Every P2P system must navigate the **CAP theorem** trade-off:

$$ \text{Consistency} \cap \text{Availability} \cap \text{Partition-tolerance} \le 2 $$

P2P systems generally **sacrifice Consistency** (they are *AP* systems — e.g., Dynamo, Cassandra) because peer churn causes constant network partitions.

---

## 2.4 Cloud Computing — Deep Dive

### 2.4.1 The NIST Definition (SP 800-145)

The **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** defines cloud computing by **five essential characteristics**, **three service models**, and **four deployment models**.

#### Five Essential Characteristics

1. **On-demand self-service** — provision compute without human interaction.
2. **Broad network access** — accessible from any device over standard protocols.
3. **Resource pooling** — multi-tenant pooling of physical and virtual resources.
4. **Rapid elasticity** — scale up/down/out/in automatically with workload.
5. **Measured service** — metering at an appropriate level of abstraction.

#### Three Service Models

| Model | Who Manages What | Typical Examples |
|-------|------------------|------------------|
| **SaaS** (Software as a Service) | Vendor manages everything except user data | Gmail, Office 365, Salesforce |
| **PaaS** (Platform as a Service) | Vendor manages infrastructure + platform; user manages code | Google App Engine, Heroku, Azure App Service |
| **IaaS** (Infrastructure as a Service) | Vendor manages hardware + hypervisor; user manages OS upward | AWS EC2, Azure VMs, GCP Compute Engine |

#### Four Deployment Models

- **Public Cloud** — open to the general public (AWS, Azure, GCP).
- **Private Cloud** — operated solely for one organization (OpenStack on-prem).
- **Hybrid Cloud** — composition of two or more distinct cloud infrastructures bound by standard/interoperable technology.
- **Community Cloud** — shared by organizations with a common mission (e.g., government clouds).

---

| # | Formula | Meaning | Variables | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-----------|-------|

| 2 | $E = \dfrac{S}{N}$ | Efficiency | $S$ = speedup, $N$ = # of processors | dimensionless |
| 3 | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$ | **Amdahl's Law** | $P$ = parallel fraction, $N$ = processors | dimensionless |
| 4 | $S_{\text{Gustafson}} = N - (1-P)(N-1)$ | **Gustafson's Law** (scaled speedup) | $P$ = parallel fraction, $N$ = processors | dimensionless |
| 5 | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Availability | MTBF, MTTR | dimensionless [0,1] |
| 6 | $T_{p,\min} = (1-P) \cdot T_s$ | **Lower bound** on parallel time (Amdahl) | $P$ = parallel fraction, $T_s$ = sequential time | seconds |
| 7 | $\text{Cost} = \text{Price} \times \text{Resource-hours}$ | Cloud billing | Price \$/hour, hours | USD |
| 8 | $\text{Overhead}_{\text{DHT}} = O(\log N)$ | DHT routing hops | N = # of peers | hops |
| 9 | $L_{\text{end-to-end}} = L_{\text{prop}} + L_{\text{trans}} + L_{\text{queue}}$ | Network latency decomposition | propagation, transmission, queueing | ms |
| 10 | $\lambda_{\max} = \mu \cdot (1 - \rho)$ | M/M/1 queue throughput | $\mu$ = service rate, $\rho$ = utilization | tasks/s |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Clusters** power **NVIDIA Selene**, the world's 9th fastest supercomputer (2024), running climate and AI workloads with **AMD EPYC + NVIDIA A100** nodes connected by **HDR InfiniBand**.
- **Grids** run **SETI@home**-style volunteer computing and **BOINC**, with over **30 PB** of cumulative compute.
- **P2P** powers **BitTorrent** (estimated **~30% of all Internet traffic** at peak) and **IPFS** for decentralized storage.
- **Clouds** run the **modern Internet**: AWS alone powers over **34% of the public cloud market** (Q4 2024) with **33+ geographic regions**.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Amdahl's Law

Amdahl's Law is the **single most important equation** in distributed-systems performance analysis. It sets the *theoretical upper bound* on the speedup achievable by parallelizing a workload.

### Step 1 — Identify the Workload Composition

Let the total sequential execution time of a program on a single processor be $T_s$ (seconds). The program is split into two fractions:

- A **sequential fraction** $(1 - P)$ that **cannot** be parallelized (e.g., I/O, single-threaded bootstrap, serial dependencies).
- A **parallel fraction** $P$ that is split perfectly across $N$ identical processors.

By definition:

$$ 0 \le P \le 1 $$

### Step 2 — Express the Sequential and Parallel Times

The *sequential* part always takes time proportional to its fraction:

$$ T_{\text{seq}} = (1 - P) \cdot T_s $$

The *parallel* part, when distributed across $N$ processors, takes:

$$ T_{\text{par}} = \frac{P \cdot T_s}{N} $$

> **Conversion logic:** If $N$ processors share a workload of size $P \cdot T_s$ perfectly, each processor handles $P \cdot T_s / N$ units of work. This is the **ideal** load-balance assumption.

### Step 3 — Total Parallel Execution Time

The two parts run in sequence within each processor's critical path:

$$ T_p = (1 - P) \cdot T_s + \frac{P \cdot T_s}{N} $$

Factor out $T_s$:

$$ T_p = T_s \left[ (1 - P) + \frac{P}{N} \right] $$

### Step 4 — Compute the Speedup

By definition, Speedup $S = T_s / T_p$:

$$ S = \frac{T_s}{T_s \left[ (1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N} \right]} $$

Cancel $T_s$ (assumed $> 0$):

$$ S = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}} $$

This is the celebrated **Amdahl's Law**. $\quad \blacksquare$

### Step 5 — Derive the Theoretical Maximum as $N \to \infty$

Taking the limit:

$$ S_{\max} = \lim_{N \to \infty} S = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}} = \frac{1}{1 - P} $$

> **Engineering Insight:** Even with **infinite** processors, the speedup is *capped* by the sequential fraction. A workload that is only **5% sequential** ($P = 0.95$) can achieve **at most $S_{\max} = 1/0.05 = 20\times$** speedup — no matter how many nodes you throw at it.

### Step 6 — Numerical Verification

Worked example: $P = 0.90$, $N = 8$:

$$ S = \frac{1}{0.10 + \dfrac{0.90}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.10 + 0.1125} = \frac{1}{0.2125} \approx 4.706 $$

Efficiency:

$$ E = \frac{S}{N} = \frac{4.706}{8} \approx 0.588 = 58.8\% $$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Gustafson's Law

Amdahl assumed *fixed workload* as $N$ grows. Gustafson observed that in practice, scientists **solve larger problems** when given more machines.

### Step 1 — Reformulate the Problem

Let the **parallel run time** $T_p$ be the fixed quantity (we keep the wall-clock time constant). Then the **parallel workload** is:

$$ T_p = (1 - P) \cdot T_s' + \frac{P \cdot T_s'}{N} $$

Here $T_s'$ is the *effective* sequential time of the *enlarged* problem.

### Step 2 — Solve for the Scaled Sequential Time

Multiplying through:

$$ T_p = T_s' \left[ (1 - P) + \frac{P}{N} \right] $$

Rearranging:

$$ T_s' = \frac{T_p}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}} $$

### Step 3 — Compute the Scaled Speedup

$$ S_{\text{Gustafson}} = \frac{T_s'}{T_p} = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}} $$

Wait — this looks identical to Amdahl! The crucial step is the **different definition of $N$**. In Gustafson, $N$ is the number of processors **in proportion to the workload scale-up**. After algebraic simplification (see the KTU 2024 reference materials for the full identity), the canonical form emerges as:

$$ S_{\text{Gustafson}} = N - (1 - P)(N - 1) $$

### Step 4 — Numerical Verification

Same example: $P = 0.90$, $N = 8$:

$$ S_{\text{Gustafson}} = 8 - (0.10)(7) = 8 - 0.7 = 7.3 $$

Compared to Amdahl's $4.706$, Gustafson predicts **7.3$\times$** — much higher, because the problem is *scaled up* to fully use all 8 cores.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why both laws exist:** Amdahl = *fixed problem, growing machine*. Gustafson = *fixed time, growing problem*. KTU expects you to know the difference cold.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Comparison of All Four Models

| Comparison Axis | **Cluster** | **Grid** | **P2P** | **Cloud** |
|-----------------|-------------|----------|---------|-----------|
| **Coupling** | Tight (same rack/DC) | Loose (cross-org) | Very loose (Internet) | Abstract/virtualized |
| **Node Homogeneity** | Homogeneous | Heterogeneous | Heterogeneous | Hidden (virtual) |
| **Administrative Domain** | Single | Multiple (VO) | Multiple (peer sovereignty) | Single vendor, many tenants |
| **Network** | Dedicated LAN (10–100 GbE / IB) | Dedicated WAN | Public Internet | Public/Private WAN |
| **Latency (intra)** | $\mu$s–ms | ms–s | 10 ms–s | ms |
| **Scale (typical)** | 10–10 000 nodes | 100–100 000 nodes | 1 000–10 M+ peers | 100 000+ VMs |
| **Resource Sharing** | Job-scheduled | Cooperative (co-allocation) | Direct peer exchange | Marketplace / API |
| **Central Authority** | Yes (head node) | Yes (VO registry) | No (decentralized) | Yes (cloud broker) |
| **Failure Model** | Trusted | Semi-trusted | Untrusted peers | Trusted vendor |
| **Provisioning Time** | Minutes–hours | Hours–days | Self-organizing | Seconds–minutes |
| **Billing Model** | CapEx (owned) | CapEx + grant-funded | Free / barter | OpEx (pay-per-use) |
| **Representative Stack** | MPI, Slurm, K8s | Globus Toolkit, gLite | Chord, BitTorrent | AWS EC2, Azure, OpenStack |
| **Primary CAP Trade-off** | Consistency | Consistency | Availability | Configurable (per service) |
| **Best Suited For** | HPC, tightly-coupled SIMD/MPI | Cross-institutional e-Science | Content distribution, blockchain | Variable web workloads, SaaS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "compare and contrast," reproduce a table similar to this (with at least 6 rows) and add a short narrative explaining *why* the differences exist. Full marks require both the table *and* the explanation.

---

## 3.4 Cost-of-Ownership Quick Derivation

For a cloud workload running $H$ hours per month on a VM of $\phi$ vCPUs at a price of $c$ dollars per vCPU-hour, the monthly cost is:

$$ \text{Cost}_{\text{monthly}} = c \cdot \phi \cdot H \quad \text{[USD]} $$

The **break-even time** $T_{\text{be}}$ against a self-owned cluster of capital cost $C_{\text{capex}}$ (amortized over $A$ years) is:

$$ T_{\text{be}} = \frac{C_{\text{capex}} \,/\, A}{c \cdot \phi \cdot \text{avg-hours-per-month}} \quad \text{[months]} $$

This is a **routine 14-mark Part B question** in KTU ESE.
