# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
*Distinguish between a Cluster and a Grid in distributed computing. Mention at least three points of difference. (3 marks)*

#### Model Answer (Mappable to 3-Mark Rubric)

A **Cluster** is a collection of homogeneous compute nodes interconnected by a high-speed LAN and administered under a single organizational domain, typically used for tightly-coupled parallel jobs (HPC, MPI workloads). A **Grid**, in contrast, is a federation of heterogeneous, geographically distributed resources spanning multiple administrative domains and bound together through a Virtual Organization.

**Three precise points of difference:**

1. **Heterogeneity** — Clusters use identical hardware/OS; Grids deliberately integrate heterogeneous resources across institutions.
2. **Administrative boundary** — Clusters have one owner; Grids have many, each with its own security and accounting policy.
3. **Scale and topology** — Clusters fit in one data center (rack-scale); Grids span cities/countries (WAN-scale).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Award 1 mark per crisp point. Vague answers like "Cluster is small, Grid is large" earn partial credit only.

---

### Question A.2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**
*List the five essential characteristics of cloud computing as defined by NIST. (3 marks)*

#### Model Answer

The **NIST SP 800-145** definition specifies exactly five essential characteristics:

1. **On-demand self-service** — automated provisioning without human intervention.
2. **Broad network access** — access over standard protocols from heterogeneous client platforms.
3. **Resource pooling** — multi-tenant pooling of physical/virtual resources with location independence.
4. **Rapid elasticity** — capabilities scaled up or down, ideally automatically, with workload.
5. **Measured service** — metering of storage, processing, bandwidth, etc., for transparency and billing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Listing all five correctly awards full 3 marks. Naming only three earns 2 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Module 1, Sample Paper Pattern]**
*Answer the following:*
**(a)** Derive Amdahl's Law from first principles. State clearly all assumptions. (7 marks)
**(b)** A program takes **$T_s = 100$ seconds** on a single processor. **95%** of the program is parallelizable. Calculate the maximum speedup achievable with **$N = 16$** and **$N = 64$** processors. What is the *theoretical upper limit* of speedup as $N \to \infty$? Comment on the practical implications. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Define the fractions:**
Let $P$ = parallel fraction of the program, $(1 - P)$ = sequential fraction.

**Step 2 — Sequential time:** $T_s$.
**Step 3 — Parallel time on N processors:**

$$ T_p = (1 - P) \cdot T_s + \frac{P \cdot T_s}{N} $$

**Step 4 — Apply the speedup definition $S = T_s / T_p$:**

$$ S = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}} $$

**Assumptions to state explicitly (each worth 1 mark):**
1. Workload is split into one *purely sequential* and one *purely parallel* part.
2. The parallel part divides *perfectly* across $N$ identical processors (no load imbalance, no communication).
3. Negligible parallelization overhead.
4. All processors have equal performance.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Given: $P = 0.95$, $T_s = 100$ s.

**Case 1: N = 16**

$$ S_{16} = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.95) + \dfrac{0.95}{16}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.059375} = \frac{1}{0.109375} \approx 9.143 $$

**Case 2: N = 64**

$$ S_{64} = \frac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{64}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.014844} = \frac{1}{0.064844} \approx 15.42 $$

**Theoretical Limit as N → ∞:**

$$ S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - P} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20 $$

**Practical Implication (1 mark):**
Diminishing returns set in rapidly. Even with **$N = 16$**, we already capture ~46% of the available speedup. Going from 16 → 64 processors (4× more hardware) yields only ~69% more speedup. Beyond ~$N = 100$, the curve flattens. This justifies the **cluster-sizing rule of thumb**: never buy more than $\sim 10 / (1 - P)$ processors for a given workload.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert the percentage to a decimal (writing $P = 95$ instead of $P = 0.95$) — gives nonsensical answer.
> 2. Computing $S_{\max}$ as just $1/P$ (forgetting the $1 - P$ in the denominator) — loses 2 marks.
> 3. Not stating the assumptions in part (a) — loses up to 2 marks.
> 4. Missing the "practical implication" sentence in part (b) — loses 1 mark.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Module 1, Sample Paper Pattern]**
*Answer the following:*
**(a)** Explain the four deployment models of cloud computing with suitable real-world examples. (7 marks)
**(b)** Compare and contrast **P2P** and **Client-Server** architectures across at least six dimensions, with one real-world example for each. Discuss how **DHT-based** P2P systems overcome the **single-point-of-failure** weakness of centralized P2P. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **NIST SP 800-145** specifies four deployment models:

1. **Public Cloud** — Infrastructure is open for use by the general public. Owned and operated by a third-party provider. *Example:* **Amazon Web Services (AWS) EC2**, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud Platform.
2. **Private Cloud** — Provisioned for exclusive use by a single organization. May be on-premises or vendor-hosted. *Example:* A bank running **OpenStack** within its own data center for compliance reasons.
3. **Hybrid Cloud** — Composition of two or more cloud infrastructures (private + public) that remain unique entities but are bound by standardized or proprietary technology enabling data/application portability. *Example:* Netflix running burst capacity on AWS public cloud while keeping its content library in a private S3-compatible store.
4. **Community Cloud** — Shared by several organizations with a common concern (mission, security, policy, compliance). *Example:* **GovCloud** for federal/state governments, or the **European Helix Nebula** scientific cloud.

> **Allocation hint:** ~1.5 marks per deployment model with a correct example.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Comparison Table (6 dimensions):**

| Dimension | Client-Server | P2P |
|-----------|---------------|-----|
| **Architecture** | Centralized server, thin clients | Symmetric, fully decentralized |
| **Single Point of Failure** | Yes (server) | No (every peer is a node) |
| **Scalability** | Limited by server capacity | Grows organically with peer count |
| **Resource Contribution** | Server provides everything | Peers both consume *and* contribute |
| **Discovery** | Server's directory / DNS | DHT lookup, flooding, or tracker |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Requires replication/redundancy | Inherent (no central node to fail) |
| **Example** | Web browser ↔ Nginx server | BitTorrent swarm, IPFS |

**DHT-Based P2P and SPOF Mitigation:**

Centralized P2P (e.g., **Napster**) relied on a single index server — when the server went down, the entire system died. **Distributed Hash Tables (Chord, Pastry, Kademlia)** eliminate this SPOF by:
1. Distributing the key→peer mapping across **all N peers**.
2. Each peer stores only an O(log N) routing table.
3. **Lookup(key)** is resolved via iterative or recursive XOR-distance routing in O(log N) hops.
4. When a peer leaves or fails, its keys are **re-replicated** to its *k* successors (typically $k = 20$) within seconds, ensuring no data loss.
5. There is **no privileged node** — any peer can initiate or serve a lookup.

Result: the system tolerates **arbitrary peer churn** with graceful degradation rather than catastrophic failure.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. Listing deployment models without *real-world* examples → lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. Confusing "Hybrid" with "Community" cloud → lose 1 mark.
> 3. In part (b), failing to use a *table* or *numbered list* for the comparison → lose 1 mark for poor presentation.
> 4. Describing DHT routing as "fast" without giving the **O(log N)** complexity → lose 1 mark.
> 5. Forgetting the *re-replication on peer failure* mechanism in SPOF discussion → lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Module 1 Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 reasons students lose marks on "System Models" questions in KTU ESE:**
> 1. **Conflating Cluster and Grid.** If a question explicitly contrasts them, do NOT mention "cloud" or "P2P" — stay on-topic.
> 2. **Omitting the five/six standard lists** (NIST characteristics, CAP theorem, OGSA layers). Memorize them verbatim.
> 3. **Muddling CAP trade-offs.** Clusters and Grids typically prioritize **Consistency + Partition-tolerance (CP)**; pure P2P and Dynamo-style clouds choose **Availability + Partition-tolerance (AP)**. Get this wrong and you lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram.** A 14-mark question in Module 1 *always* expects a labelled system architecture diagram. Draw it in pencil before writing prose.
> 5. **Ignoring units and decimal conversion** in Amdahl's-law problems. 95% means $P = 0.95$, not $P = 95$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print this and glance 30 minutes before the exam:**

- [ ] **Cluster** = homogeneous, single-owner, LAN, tightly-coupled, MPI/HPC, single system image.
- [ ] **Grid** = heterogeneous, multi-owner (VO), WAN, loosely-coupled, Globus/gLite, co-allocation.
- [ ] **P2P** = decentralized, peers are both clients and servers, DHT (Chord/Pastry/Kademlia) for O(log N) lookup.
- [ ] **Cloud** = virtualized, elastic, pay-per-use, NIST's 5-4-3 model (5 characteristics, 4 deployment, 3 service).
- [ ] **Amdahl's Law:** $S = 1 / [(1 - P) + (P / N)]$; limit is $1 / (1 - P)$.
- [ ] **Gustafson's Law:** $S = N - (1 - P)(N - 1)$; assumes scaled workload, not fixed.
- [ ] **CAP Theorem:** A distributed system can guarantee at most **two** of {Consistency, Availability, Partition-tolerance}.
- [ ] **NIST 5 Characteristics:** on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, measured service.
- [ ] **NIST 3 Service Models:** SaaS, PaaS, IaaS — manage layers from "everything" (SaaS) to "just hardware" (IaaS).
- [ ] **NIST 4 Deployment Models:** Public, Private, Hybrid, Community.
- [ ] **DHT properties:** O(log N) routing, O(log N) state per node, replicated successor list handles churn.
- [ ] **Availability formula:** $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$.
- [ ] **Speedup efficiency** $E = S / N$, $0 \le E \le 1$.
- [ ] **Diagrams matter:** Always draw a labelled block diagram for any 14-mark question in Module 1.
- [ ] **Real-world touchstones:** Beowulf (cluster), WLCG/EGEE (grid), BitTorrent (P2P), AWS EC2 (cloud).

> **End of Module 1 Notes — System Models for Distributed and Cloud Computing.**
> *Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol for the 2024 Scheme.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computer Clusters: Clustering for Massive Parallelism - Design Objectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Computer Clusters: Clustering for Massive Parallelism - Design Objectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Computer Cluster

A **Computer Cluster** is a collection of loosely or tightly connected standalone computers (nodes) that work together closely so that, in many respects, they may be viewed as a single unified computing resource. The interconnected nodes coordinate their activities through high-speed network interconnects and a layer of middleware that presents a **Single System Image (SSI)** to the user and application layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A cluster is a type of parallel/distributed system that consists of a collection of interconnected stand-alone computers working together as a single, integrated computing resource. The goal of clustering is to achieve **Massive Parallelism** — executing many concurrent tasks across hundreds or thousands of commodity nodes to solve a single, large computational problem.

The three primary architectural classifications of clusters recognized in the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

- **High Performance Clusters (HPC)** — engineered for compute-intensive, tightly-coupled scientific workloads (e.g., weather modeling, molecular dynamics).
- **High Availability (HA) Clusters** — engineered to provide continuous service availability through redundancy and failover mechanisms.
- **Load Balancing Clusters** — engineered to distribute incoming requests across multiple nodes to maximize throughput and minimize response time.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Factory Assembly Line"**
> Imagine a massive automotive factory. Instead of one giant, super-complex machine trying to build an entire car (which is slow and breaks down often), the factory uses **hundreds of smaller, identical workstations**. Each workstation performs a small, specialized step. The stations are connected by a conveyor belt (the network), and a floor manager (the middleware) dispatches work to whichever station is free. If one station breaks, another takes over its job — the factory keeps producing cars.
>
> **A computer cluster works exactly the same way:** instead of buying one ultra-expensive supercomputer, you connect many affordable commodity servers via a fast network and have middleware coordinate them. This gives you massive parallel processing power at a fraction of the cost, with built-in fault tolerance.

## 1.3 Why Clustering for Massive Parallelism?

The fundamental motivation for clustering stems from four engineering realities:

- **Physical limits of single processors** — Clock speeds have stagnated due to power, heat, and quantum-tunneling barriers. The path to more performance is now **horizontal scaling** (more nodes) rather than **vertical scaling** (faster CPUs).
- **Cost economics** — A cluster of 1000 commodity servers at $2000 each costs less than a single supercomputer of equivalent FLOPS.
- **Scalability** — Capacity can be grown incrementally by adding nodes, matching workload growth.
- **Fault tolerance** — Redundancy is built into the architecture; the failure of one node does not collapse the system.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Standard Metric — FLOPS:** Cluster performance is measured in **FLOPS (Floating-Point Operations Per Second)**. Modern top-tier clusters reach the **ExaFLOPS** scale ($10^{18}$ FLOPS). For example, **Frontier** (ORNL) sustains ~1.194 ExaFLOPS. The KTU exam will expect you to know this benchmark metric and its SI prefixes.

## 1.4 The Five Foundational Design Objectives

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are the **core design objectives** the KTU 2024 module directly tests. Every cluster design is evaluated against these five pillars:

| # | Design Objective | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Scalability** | Ability to add more nodes and proportionally increase performance. |
| 2 | **High Availability (HA)** | Continuous service uptime even when individual nodes fail. |
| 3 | **Single System Image (SSI)** | Hides distributed nature; user sees one machine. |
| 4 | **Load Balancing** | Even distribution of work to prevent bottlenecks. |
| 5 | **Middleware-driven Programmability** | Hides complexity of parallelism from the application developer. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Design Objectives — Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 Scalability

**Definition:** A cluster is scalable if its performance improves linearly (or near-linearly) with the addition of nodes.

**Mechanisms enabling scalability:**
- **Decentralized architecture** — no single point of coordination.
- **Hierarchical interconnection** — fat-tree, Clos, dragonfly topologies.
- **Distributed state management** — shared-nothing design philosophy.

**Theoretical limits captured by Amdahl's Law** dictate the maximum achievable speedup:

$$S(N) = \frac{1}{f_s + \frac{(1 - f_s)}{N}}$$

where $f_s$ is the **serial fraction** of the program (proportion that cannot be parallelized) and $N$ is the number of processing nodes. Even with infinite nodes, speedup is bounded by:

$$S_{\infty} = \frac{1}{f_s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This formula appears in KTU 2019 and 2023 university papers. It tells designers the *parallel ceiling* of their application and guides decisions on whether adding nodes will yield diminishing returns.

### 2.1.2 High Availability (HA)

**Definition:** The cluster remains accessible and operational despite hardware or software failures.

**Quantitative metric — Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):**

$$\text{Availability} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

where **MTTR** is *Mean Time To Repair*. A "five-nines" availability system (99.999%) tolerates only ~5.26 minutes of downtime per year.

**HA mechanisms:**
- Active-passive failover pairs
- Active-active redundant nodes
- Distributed consensus algorithms (Paxos, Raft)
- Heartbeat monitoring and automatic service migration

### 2.1.3 Single System Image (SSI)

**Definition:** SSI provides a unified view of the cluster — files, memory, processes, and network — hiding the fact that resources are physically distributed.

**SSI is achieved at four layers:**

1. **Hardware SSI** — identical components, shared interconnect.
2. **Operating System SSI** — distributed OS like MOSIX, Kerrighed, or Linux + cluster extensions.
3. **Middleware SSI** — cluster managers (SLURM, Torque) and resource brokers.
4. **Application SSI** — parallel programming frameworks (MPI, OpenMP distributed).

### 2.1.4 Load Balancing

**Definition:** Distribution of computational work across cluster nodes to prevent any single node from becoming a bottleneck.

**Two primary strategies:**

- **Static load balancing** — Decisions made at compile time or job submission. Work is partitioned based on predicted execution time.
- **Dynamic load balancing** — Work is migrated at runtime based on monitored node load. Algorithms include:
  - Round-robin
  - Least-loaded-first
  - Sender-initiated / receiver-initiated diffusion
  - Work-stealing schedulers

**Quality metric — Load Imbalance Factor (LIF):**

$$\text{LIF} = \frac{T_{\max} - T_{\text{avg}}}{T_{\text{avg}}}$$

where $T_{\max}$ is the maximum per-node execution time and $T_{\text{avg}}$ is the mean. An LIF approaching 0 indicates an ideal balance.

### 2.1.5 Programmability and Middleware

**Definition:** Middleware is the software layer that bridges applications and the distributed cluster infrastructure, exposing a usable API to programmers.

**Major middleware components:**

- **Resource managers** — SLURM, PBS Pro, LSF, Torque.
- **Communication libraries** — MPI (Message Passing Interface), PVM.
- **Parallel file systems** — Lustre, GPFS, BeeGFS, HDFS.
- **Job schedulers** — FIFO, fair-share, gang scheduling.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Unit | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Amdahl's Speedup | $S(N) = \frac{1}{f_s + \frac{(1-f_s)}{N}}$ | Dimensionless | Maximum parallel speedup with N nodes |
| 2 | Amdahl's Limit | $S_\infty = \frac{1}{f_s}$ | Dimensionless | Upper bound of speedup |
| 3 | Availability | $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Fraction (0–1) | Quantifying HA |
| 4 | Load Imbalance Factor | $\text{LIF} = \frac{T_{\max} - T_{\text{avg}}}{T_{\text{avg}}}$ | Dimensionless | Load balance quality |
| 5 | Parallel Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{S(N)}{N} = \frac{T_1}{N \cdot T_N}$ | Fraction | Fraction of ideal scaling achieved |
| 6 | Throughput | $\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Total Jobs}}{\text{Unit Time}}$ | Jobs/sec | HPC cluster metric |
| 7 | Network Bisection Bandwidth | $B_{\text{bisect}}$ | GB/s | Interconnect performance |
| 8 | Cluster Memory Bandwidth | $B_{\text{agg}} = N \cdot B_{\text{node}}$ | GB/s | Aggregate memory throughput |
| 9 | Power Efficiency | $\frac{\text{FLOPS}}{\text{Watt}}$ | GFLOPS/W | Green computing metric |
| 10 | Gustafson's Law | $S(N) = N - f_s(N-1)$ | Dimensionless | Scaled-speedup for larger problems |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization tip:** KTU exam questions on this module typically offer 2-4 marks for *defining* the design objective and 6-8 marks for *explaining the mechanisms* (SSI layers, HA failover, load-balancing algorithms). Always include an example (e.g., a real cluster) to score full marks.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Weather forecasting** (ECMWF, NOAA) — clusters run atmospheric models across **thousands of cores**.
- **Genomics** — DNA sequence alignment using MPI-based clusters.
- **Cryptocurrency mining** — large-scale load-balanced mining clusters.
- **E-commerce** — HA clusters behind every major web service (Google, Amazon).
- **Scientific simulation** — molecular dynamics (GROMACS, NAMD), CFD (OpenFOAM).

The five design objectives form a **trade-off triangle**: pushing for more scalability often increases complexity, reducing SSI; pursuing higher availability requires redundant hardware (lower efficiency); aggressive load balancing introduces synchronization overhead.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Amdahl's Law and Its Parallel Ceiling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequent Theme:** Amdahl's Law is the most-tested formula in this module. You must be able to derive it and apply it to numerical problems.

### Step 1 — Set Up the Execution Time Model

Consider a program whose total execution time on a single node is $T_1$. Partition the program into two parts:

- A **serial fraction** $f_s$ (cannot be parallelized) — runs on only one node.
- A **parallel fraction** $(1 - f_s)$ — can be split across $N$ nodes.

Therefore:

$$T_1 = T_s + T_p = f_s \cdot T_1 + (1 - f_s) \cdot T_1$$

### Step 2 — Compute Parallel Execution Time on N Nodes

The serial part still takes the full $f_s \cdot T_1$ time. The parallel part is divided among $N$ nodes, so it takes $\frac{(1 - f_s) \cdot T_1}{N}$. Adding both:

$$T_N = f_s \cdot T_1 + \frac{(1 - f_s) \cdot T_1}{N}$$

Factor out $T_1$:

$$T_N = T_1 \left[ f_s + \frac{(1 - f_s)}{N} \right]$$

### Step 3 — Define Speedup

Speedup $S(N)$ is the ratio of single-node time to multi-node time:

$$S(N) = \frac{T_1}{T_N} = \frac{T_1}{T_1 \left[ f_s + \frac{(1 - f_s)}{N} \right]}$$

Cancel $T_1$:

$$S(N) = \frac{1}{f_s + \frac{(1 - f_s)}{N}}$$

This is **Amdahl's Law**.

### Step 4 — Take the Limit as N → ∞

As we add infinitely many nodes:

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \frac{1}{f_s + 0} = \frac{1}{f_s}$$

This shows that the maximum speedup is **bounded by the inverse of the serial fraction** — a hard wall that no amount of hardware can break.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A program spends 20% of its time in serial code and 80% in parallel code. Find the speedup when run on 8 nodes. What is the absolute maximum speedup no matter how many nodes you add?

**Solution:**

Given: $f_s = 0.20$, $1 - f_s = 0.80$, $N = 8$.

Apply Amdahl's Law:

$$S(8) = \frac{1}{0.20 + \frac{0.80}{8}}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$\frac{0.80}{8} = 0.10$$

$$0.20 + 0.10 = 0.30$$

$$S(8) = \frac{1}{0.30} = 3.33$$

**Maximum speedup (N → ∞):**

$$S_\infty = \frac{1}{0.20} = 5.00$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Even with infinite processors, this program can never be faster than 5×. This is the practical lesson: *optimize the serial code first* before scaling hardware.

## 3.2 Derivation: Parallel Efficiency

Parallel efficiency $\eta$ measures how well the cluster utilizes added nodes. It is the speedup divided by the number of nodes:

$$\eta = \frac{S(N)}{N}$$

Substituting Amdahl's $S(N)$:

$$\eta = \frac{1}{N \left[ f_s + \frac{(1 - f_s)}{N} \right]}$$

Simplify:

$$\eta = \frac{1}{N f_s + (1 - f_s)} = \frac{1}{1 + f_s (N - 1)}$$

This form makes the cost of the serial fraction explicit: as $N$ grows, efficiency drops unless $f_s \to 0$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Availability from MTBF and MTTR

A system is **up** for an average of MTBF and **down** for an average of MTTR. The fraction of time it is up is its availability:

$$A = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime} + \text{Downtime}} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

**Worked example:** A node has MTBF = 5000 hours and MTTR = 5 hours:

$$A = \frac{5000}{5000 + 5} = \frac{5000}{5005} = 0.99900 \approx 99.9\%$$

**For "five-nines" (99.999%):** If MTTR is fixed at 1 hour, MTBF must be:

$$0.99999 = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + 1} \Rightarrow 0.99999 \cdot \text{MTBF} + 0.99999 = \text{MTBF}$$

$$0.99999 = 0.00001 \cdot \text{MTBF} \Rightarrow \text{MTBF} = 99{,}999 \text{ hours} \approx 11.4 \text{ years}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the Three Cluster Types

| Design Dimension | HPC Cluster | HA Cluster | Load-Balancing Cluster |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Objective | Maximum compute throughput | Maximum uptime | Maximum request handling |
| Typical Interconnect | InfiniBand, Omni-Path (low latency) | Ethernet + Heartbeat LAN | Ethernet + L4/L7 switches |
| Workload | Tightly-coupled MPI jobs | Service daemons (DB, Web) | Independent user requests |
| Node Interdependence | Very high (sync via MPI) | Medium (state replication) | Low (independent tasks) |
| Failover Strategy | Checkpoint-restart | Active-passive / active-active | Drop node from pool |
| SSI Requirement | Strong (process group) | Strong (IP takeover) | Weak (URL only) |
| Example | Frontier, Fugaku | AWS EC2 HA pairs | LAMP web farms, CDNs |

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Cluster Sizing Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A university lab needs a cluster to run a CFD code. The serial fraction is 5%, and they want at least 10× speedup. How many nodes are needed?

**Given:** $f_s = 0.05$, desired $S(N) \geq 10$.

**Step 1:** Apply Amdahl's Law and solve for N:

$$S(N) = \frac{1}{0.05 + \frac{0.95}{N}} \geq 10$$

**Step 2:** Invert both sides:

$$0.05 + \frac{0.95}{N} \leq 0.1$$

**Step 3:** Isolate the fraction:

$$\frac{0.95}{N} \leq 0.05$$

**Step 4:** Solve for N:

$$N \geq \frac{0.95}{0.05} = 19$$

**Answer:** The lab needs **at least 19 nodes** to achieve a 10× speedup. With 19 nodes, the actual speedup is:

$$S(19) = \frac{1}{0.05 + \frac{0.95}{19}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.05} = \frac{1}{0.10} = 10$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cluster Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph clientLayer["CLIENT ACCESS LAYER"]
        clientA[CLIENT NODE 1]
        clientB[CLIENT NODE 2]
        clientC[CLIENT NODE 3]
    end

    subgraph frontendLayer["FRONTEND AND SCHEDULING LAYER"]
        master1[MASTER HEAD NODE]
        sched1[JOB SCHEDULER SLURM PBS]
        monit1[MONITORING GANGLIA NAGIOS]
    end

    subgraph computeLayer["COMPUTE NODE POOL"]
        node1[COMPUTE NODE 01]
        node2[COMPUTE NODE 02]
        node3[COMPUTE NODE 03]
        node4[COMPUTE NODE NN]
    end

    subgraph storageLayer["SHARED STORAGE LAYER"]
        file1[PARALLEL FS LUSTRE GPFS]
        meta1[METADATA SERVER MGS]
    end

    subgraph networkLayer["HIGH SPEED INTERCONNECT"]
        sw1[FAT TREE SWITCH]
        sw2[LEAF SPINE SWITCH]
    end

    clientA --> master1
    clientB --> master1
    clientC --> master1
    master1 --> sched1
    master1 --> monit1
    sched1 --> node1
    sched1 --> node2
    sched1 --> node3
    sched1 --> node4
    monit1 --> node1
    monit1 --> node2
    monit1 --> node3
    monit1 --> node4
    node1 --> sw1
    node2 --> sw1
    node3 --> sw2
    node4 --> sw2
    node1 --> file1
    node2 --> file1
    node3 --> file1
    node4 --> file1
    file1 --> meta1
    sw1 <--> sw2
```

## 4.2 Design Objective Relationship Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    obj1[SCALABILITY]
    obj2[HIGH AVAILABILITY]
    obj3[SINGLE SYSTEM IMAGE]
    obj4[LOAD BALANCING]
    obj5[MIDDLEWARE PROGRAMMABILITY]

    obj1 --> trade1[TRADE OFF: COST VS COMPLEXITY]
    obj2 --> trade2[TRADE OFF: REDUNDANCY VS EFFICIENCY]
    obj3 --> trade3[TRADE OFF: TRANSPARENCY VS OVERHEAD]
    obj4 --> trade4[TRADE OFF: SYNC OVERHEAD VS LATENCY]
    obj5 --> trade5[TRADE OFF: API ABSTRACTION VS PERFORMANCE]

    obj1 --> cluster[UNIFIED CLUSTER DESIGN]
    obj2 --> cluster
    obj3 --> cluster
    obj4 --> cluster
    obj5 --> cluster

    cluster --> outcome[MASSIVE PARALLELISM]
    outcome --> app1[WEATHER SIMULATION]
    outcome --> app2[GENOMICS]
    outcome --> app3[AI TRAINING]
    outcome --> app4[FINANCIAL MODELING]
```

## 4.3 Single System Image (SSI) Layered Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layer1[LEVEL 1: APPLICATION SSI]
    layer2[LEVEL 2: MIDDLEWARE SSI]
    layer3[LEVEL 3: OS SSI]
    layer4[LEVEL 4: HARDWARE SSI]

    layer1 --> layer2
    layer2 --> layer3
    layer3 --> layer4

    layer1 -.examples.-> ex1[MPI RUNTIME OPENMP CLUSTER API]
    layer2 -.examples.-> ex2[SLURM PBS TORQUE RESOURCE MANAGER]
    layer3 -.examples.-> ex3[KERRIGHED MOSIX DISTRIBUTED LINUX]
    layer4 -.examples.-> ex4[INFINIBAND ETHERNET HOMOGENEOUS NODES]
```

## 4.4 HA Failover Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant App as USER APPLICATION
    participant LB as LOAD BALANCER
    participant P1 as PRIMARY NODE
    participant P2 as STANDBY NODE
    participant Mon as HEARTBEAT MONITOR

    Note over P1,P2: NORMAL OPERATION
    App->>LB: Send Request
    LB->>P1: Route to Primary
    P1-->>App: Response
    Mon->>P1: Heartbeat Check
    P1-->>Mon: ACK Alive

    Note over P1,P2: FAILURE DETECTED
    Mon->>P1: Heartbeat Check
    P1-->>Mon: NO RESPONSE
    Mon->>LB: Signal Failure Detected
    LB->>P2: Trigger Failover Activation
    P2->>P1: Assume IP and State
    P2-->>App: Response from Standby
    Note over P2: SERVICE CONTINUES
```

## 4.5 Load Balancing Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start[NEW TASK ARRIVES]
    check{ALL NODES BUSY?}
    query1[QUERY LOAD METRICS FROM ALL NODES]
    sort[SORT NODES BY CURRENT LOAD ASC]
    select[SELECT NODE WITH MIN LOAD]
    assign[ASSIGN TASK TO SELECTED NODE]
    update[UPDATE NODE LOAD METRICS]
    queue[ENQUEUE TASK IN WAIT QUEUE]
    monitor[CONTINUOUS LOAD MONITORING]
    migrate{TASK LONGER THAN THRESHOLD?}
    steal[WORK STEALING FROM BUSY NODE]

    start --> check
    check -->|NO| query1
    check -->|YES| queue
    query1 --> sort
    sort --> select
    select --> assign
    assign --> update
    update --> monitor
    monitor --> migrate
    migrate -->|YES| steal
    migrate -->|NO| monitor
    steal --> update
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Computer Cluster. State its two key design objectives.
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
A **computer cluster** is a collection of interconnected stand-alone computers (nodes) that collaborate through high-speed networking and middleware to function as a single, integrated computing resource, enabling massive parallelism.

**Two key design objectives:**

1. **Scalability** — The cluster's performance must increase linearly (or near-linearly) with the addition of nodes, allowing capacity to grow incrementally.
2. **High Availability (HA)** — The cluster must remain operational even when individual nodes fail, ensuring uninterrupted service through redundancy and failover mechanisms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Mentioning the term **Single System Image (SSI)** or a real example (e.g., Google's search cluster) earns a half-mark bonus.

---

### Question 2: What is Single System Image (SSI)? List its four layers.
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
**Single System Image (SSI)** is the property of a cluster that hides its distributed physical nature and presents itself as a single, unified computer to the user and application.

**The four SSI layers are:**

1. **Hardware SSI** — Homogeneous components and unified interconnect.
2. **Operating System SSI** — Distributed OS providing global process and memory view.
3. **Middleware SSI** — Resource managers, schedulers, and parallel file systems.
4. **Application SSI** — Parallel programming frameworks (MPI, OpenMP cluster API).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - PECMT632 Module 2]**
> 
> **(a)** Explain the five major design objectives of computer clustering for massive parallelism. Discuss how each objective is achieved in a real-world cluster. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A parallel program has a serial fraction of 8%. Calculate the speedup achieved on 16 nodes using Amdahl's Law. Comment on the observed parallel efficiency. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Five Design Objectives:**

1. **Scalability** — The cluster must support horizontal scaling. Achieved via:
   - Modular, decentralized architecture.
   - High-bandwidth, low-latency interconnects (InfiniBand, fat-tree topology).
   - Shared-nothing design that minimizes contention.
   - *Example:* The Frontier supercomputer at ORNL scales to ~9,408 nodes using AMD EPYC CPUs and NVIDIA GPUs.

2. **High Availability (HA)** — The cluster must tolerate faults. Achieved via:
   - Redundant nodes and failover pairs.
   - Heartbeat monitoring with automatic service migration.
   - Distributed consensus protocols (Paxos, Raft).
   - *Example:* Amazon's EC2 deploys HA pairs with sub-second failover.

3. **Single System Image (SSI)** — Hide distributed nature. Achieved via:
   - Distributed filesystems (Lustre, GPFS).
   - Cluster resource managers (SLURM, PBS).
   - Distributed OS layers (Kerrighed, MOSIX).
   - *Example:* SLURM provides a unified job-submission interface across thousands of nodes.

4. **Load Balancing** — Distribute work evenly. Achieved via:
   - Static partitioning at job submission.
   - Dynamic scheduling with work-stealing.
   - Real-time monitoring of node load.
   - *Example:* Google's Borg/Kubernetes scheduler balances millions of containers.

5. **Middleware-driven Programmability** — Simplify parallel development. Achieved via:
   - MPI for message-passing.
   - Parallel I/O libraries (HDF5, MPI-IO).
   - Job schedulers exposing a simple API.
   - *Example:* A researcher submits a job to SLURM and receives transparent parallel execution.

**(b) Amdahl's Law Calculation:**

**Given:** $f_s = 0.08$, $1 - f_s = 0.92$, $N = 16$.

**Step 1:** Apply the formula:

$$S(16) = \frac{1}{f_s + \frac{1 - f_s}{N}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$S(16) = \frac{1}{0.08 + \frac{0.92}{16}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the parallel fraction term:

$$\frac{0.92}{16} = 0.0575$$

**Step 4:** Add:

$$0.08 + 0.0575 = 0.1375$$

**Step 5:** Invert:

$$S(16) = \frac{1}{0.1375} = 7.27$$

**Step 6:** Compute Parallel Efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{S(16)}{16} = \frac{7.27}{16} = 0.4543 \approx 45.43\%$$

**Comment:** The cluster achieves a 7.27× speedup on 16 nodes, but efficiency has dropped to ~45%. This is because the 8% serial fraction has become a relative bottleneck. To improve efficiency, the developer must refactor the serial portion of the code. With 8% serial fraction, the *theoretical maximum* speedup (as $N \to \infty$) is:

$$S_\infty = \frac{1}{0.08} = 12.5$$

So even infinite nodes cannot break the 12.5× barrier for this program.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023 - PECMT632 Module 2]**
> 
> **(a)** Describe the three architectural classifications of computer clusters. Compare their design priorities with a clear table. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the layered architecture of Single System Image (SSI). How does middleware contribute to SSI in a High-Performance Cluster? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Three Architectural Classifications:**

1. **High Performance Clusters (HPC):**
   - Designed for compute-intensive, tightly-coupled parallel workloads.
   - Use low-latency interconnects like InfiniBand or Omni-Path.
   - Rely on MPI for fine-grained inter-process communication.
   - *Examples:* Frontier, Fugaku, Sunway TaihuLight.

2. **High Availability (HA) Clusters:**
   - Designed to provide continuous service despite node failures.
   - Use redundant nodes with automatic failover.
   - Rely on heartbeat monitoring and shared state replication.
   - *Examples:* Telecom core networks, banking transaction systems.

3. **Load Balancing Clusters:**
   - Designed to maximize throughput by evenly distributing requests.
   - Use a front-end dispatcher (L4/L7 switch or software load balancer).
   - Backends are largely independent.
   - *Examples:* Web server farms, content delivery networks (CDNs), e-commerce sites.

**Comparison Table:**

| Dimension | HPC Cluster | HA Cluster | Load Balancing Cluster |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Goal | Compute throughput | Uptime | Request throughput |
| Interconnect | InfiniBand / Omni-Path | Ethernet + heartbeat | Ethernet + load balancer |
| Workload Type | Tightly coupled MPI | Long-running services | Independent requests |
| Failover | Checkpoint-restart | Active-passive / active-active | Drop node from pool |
| SSI Strength | Very strong | Strong (IP takeover) | Weak (URL only) |
| Example | Frontier | AWS HA pair | Google web farm |

**(b) Layered SSI Architecture:**

The SSI architecture is realized in **four hierarchical layers** (refer to Section 4.3 diagram):

- **Level 1 – Application SSI:** Provides the application-facing API. Examples: MPI runtime, OpenMP cluster extensions, parallel I/O libraries like HDF5. The application sees a unified job environment.
- **Level 2 – Middleware SSI:** Includes resource managers (SLURM, PBS Pro, LSF), job schedulers, and parallel file systems. The user submits a job; middleware handles placement, scheduling, and I/O.
- **Level 3 – Operating System SSI:** Implemented by distributed OS kernels (Kerrighed, MOSIX, Sprite). Provides global process management, distributed shared memory, and unified process groups.
- **Level 4 – Hardware SSI:** Homogeneous compute nodes, uniform interconnect fabric (InfiniBand, Ethernet), shared power and cooling infrastructure.

**Role of Middleware in HPC SSI:**

Middleware is the **glue** of a High-Performance Cluster. Specifically:

- **Resource Management** — SLURM and PBS Pro allocate nodes, CPUs, and memory to jobs.
- **Job Scheduling** — FIFO, fair-share, and backfill policies ensure efficient utilization.
- **Parallel File System Access** — Lustre and GPFS provide a unified namespace across all nodes; a process on any node can read/write the same files transparently.
- **Communication Layer** — MPI implementations (OpenMPI, MPICH) leverage middleware to route messages over InfiniBand.
- **Monitoring and Accounting** — Ganglia, Prometheus, and Nagios provide cluster-wide observability.

Without middleware, the user would need to manually log into each node, copy data, start processes, and synchronize them — a task that defeats the purpose of clustering. Middleware thus **enables the Single System Image** that the cluster advertises to the outside world.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on This Topic:**
> 
> 1. **Amdahl's Law Mis-application** — Students often confuse the parallel fraction $(1 - f_s)$ with the serial fraction $f_s$. Always label which is which.
> 2. **Forgetting Units** — MTBF and MTTR must carry units (hours, days). Forgetting them costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **SSI Layer Confusion** — Students frequently mix up OS SSI and Middleware SSI. Remember: **OS SSI** = kernel-level (process/memory view), **Middleware SSI** = user-level (jobs/files).
> 4. **Skipping the "Why"** — A bare definition of "load balancing" without an example or algorithm loses 1–2 marks. Always add: *e.g., round-robin, work-stealing, LIF metric.*
> 5. **Ignoring Trade-offs** — The five design objectives are *not* independent. Stating "scalability ↔ cost" and "HA ↔ efficiency" trade-offs shows the examiner you understand real engineering.
> 6. **No Diagram in 14-mark Answers** — A cluster-block or SSI-layer diagram is **mandatory** for full marks in Part B.
> 7. **Confusing Cluster Types** — HPC ≠ Load Balancing. Don't say "Google's HPC runs web queries." Google uses **load-balancing** clusters for web serving; HPC is for science.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Computer Cluster** = a collection of independent nodes coordinated via middleware to behave as one machine, enabling **massive parallelism**.
- The **five design objectives** are: **Scalability, High Availability, Single System Image, Load Balancing, Middleware-driven Programmability.**
- **Amdahl's Law** $S(N) = \frac{1}{f_s + \frac{(1-f_s)}{N}}$ is the central formula. Its limit $S_\infty = \frac{1}{f_s}$ is the hard ceiling.
- **Parallel Efficiency** $\eta = \frac{S(N)}{N}$ measures how well added nodes are utilized.
- **Availability** $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ — five nines = 99.999% uptime.
- **Three cluster types**: HPC (compute), HA (uptime), Load-Balancing (throughput).
- **SSI has 4 layers**: Hardware → OS → Middleware → Application.
- **Cluster performance metric**: **FLOPS** — modern top clusters reach **ExaFLOPS** ($10^{18}$).
- **Interconnects**: InfiniBand, Omni-Path, Ethernet (10/25/100 GbE), and emerging silicon photonics.
- **Middleware** includes SLURM, PBS, Lustre, GPFS, MPI, and OpenMP runtime.
- **Static load balancing** = compile time; **Dynamic load balancing** = runtime, includes work-stealing.
- **Load Imbalance Factor (LIF)** = $\frac{T_{\max} - T_{\text{avg}}}{T_{\text{avg}}}$ — lower is better.
- **Real-world examples**: Frontier (HPC), AWS HA pairs, Google Borg/Kubernetes (load balancing), Lustre (parallel file system).
- **Trade-off triangle**: Scalability, Availability, and Efficiency cannot all be maximized simultaneously — engineering must balance them.
- **Gustafson's Law** (advanced): $S(N) = N - f_s(N-1)$ — relaxes Amdahl's pessimism by assuming the problem size scales with N.
- **Key exam buzzwords**: SSI, MTBF, MTTR, MPI, SLURM, fat-tree, ExaFLOPS, Amdahl, Gustafson, work-stealing, failover.
- **Always** include a diagram in 14-mark answers; **always** show the algebraic steps in numerical problems; **always** mention a real-world example for design questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Issues: Ensuring High Availability, Cluster Families
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design Issues: Ensuring High Availability, Cluster Families

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**High Availability (HA)** in distributed computing systems refers to a system design approach and associated service implementation that ensures a prearranged level of operational performance — typically **uptime** — for a higher than normal period. It is formally defined as the proportion of time a system remains in a functional and accessible state, expressed as a percentage of total scheduled operational time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECMT632 / Module 2):**
> *High Availability* is the quality of a system to continuously perform its functions without failure for a prolonged duration. It is achieved by eliminating single points of failure (SPOF) and providing rapid failover capability through redundant hardware, software, and network components organized into *clusters*.

A **Cluster**, in this context, is a loosely coupled collection of independent computers (nodes) that work together cooperatively as a single, unified computing resource, coordinated by specialized middleware and interconnects. A **Cluster Family** is the taxonomic classification of such clusters based on their primary design intent: performance, load balancing, availability, or storage provisioning.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Restaurant Chain"**
>
> Imagine a single restaurant. If the chef gets sick, the whole restaurant shuts down — this is a **single point of failure**. Now, imagine a *chain of restaurants* (a cluster). If one branch's chef falls ill, customers are seamlessly redirected to the nearest branch. The chain might also have a backup chef on standby (passive node). Even if a branch is busy, the manager (load balancer) shifts orders to less busy branches. The **chain never closes** — that is High Availability. Different chains organize this differently: some prioritize *speed* (high-performance clusters), some prioritize *serving more customers* (load-balancing clusters), some prioritize *never closing* (HA clusters), and some specialize in *storing ingredients* (storage clusters) — these are the **Cluster Families**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in HA Design

The following **standard industry metrics** are the core vocabulary of HA design:

- **MTBF — Mean Time Between Failures**: The average operational time between two consecutive system failures.
- **MTTR — Mean Time To Repair**: The average time required to restore a failed system back to operational state.
- **MTTF — Mean Time To Failure**: For non-repairable components, the average expected lifetime before failure.
- **Availability (A)**: Expressed as a ratio or percentage.
- **RTO — Recovery Time Objective**: Maximum tolerable downtime after a failure.
- **RPO — Recovery Point Objective**: Maximum tolerable data loss measured in time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **"Nines"** notation is universally used in the HA industry:
> - **99.9%** ("three nines") → ≤ 8.77 hours downtime/year
> - **99.99%** ("four nines") → ≤ 52.6 minutes downtime/year
> - **99.999%** ("five nines") → ≤ 5.26 minutes downtime/year

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Design Issues for Ensuring High Availability

Designing for high availability requires a systematic elimination of failure propagation paths. The KTU Module-2 syllabus emphasizes the following core design issues:

### 2.1.1 Elimination of Single Points of Failure (SPOF)
A SPOF is any component whose failure causes the entire system to fail. Redundancy is applied at every tier:
- **Hardware redundancy**: dual power supplies, RAID disks, redundant NICs.
- **Software redundancy**: process pairs, watchdog timers, heartbeat monitors.
- **Network redundancy**: bonded links, multi-path routing, mesh topologies.

### 2.1.2 Fault Detection and Heartbeating
Nodes must rapidly detect peer failure. This is done through:
- **Heartbeat messages**: periodic UDP/TCP pings exchanged between cluster nodes.
- **Quorum algorithms**: prevent split-brain scenarios.
- **Watchdog timers**: hardware-level failure detection that reset unresponsive nodes.

### 2.1.3 Failover and Failback Strategies
- **Active-Passive (Hot Standby)**: One node actively serves; a passive node takes over on failure.
- **Active-Active**: All nodes actively serve; load is redistributed upon node loss.
- **N+1 / N+M Redundancy**: One or M spare nodes back up N active nodes.

### 2.1.4 Data Replication and Consistency
- **Synchronous replication**: Zero data loss, higher latency.
- **Asynchronous replication**: Lower latency, potential data loss.
- **Quorum-based writes**: Paxos, Raft consensus.

### 2.1.5 Stateful vs Stateless Service Design
Stateless services are trivially HA-friendly; stateful services require shared session stores or session replication.

## 2.2 Cluster Families — Taxonomic Classification

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, cluster families are categorized by their **primary design intent**:

| Cluster Family | Primary Purpose | Typical Use Case | Key Characteristic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High-Performance Computing (HPC) Cluster** | Maximum compute throughput | Scientific simulation, weather modeling | Tightly coupled, low-latency interconnect (InfiniBand, Myrinet) |
| **Load-Balancing Cluster** | Distribute workload across nodes | Web servers, application farms | Request dispatcher front-end |
| **High-Availability (HA) Cluster** | Continuous service uptime | Databases, ERP, mission-critical apps | Redundant nodes with automatic failover |
| **Storage Cluster** | Reliable, scalable data storage | File servers, archival, big-data | Distributed file system (HDFS, Lustre, Ceph) |
| **Beowulf Cluster** | Cost-effective HPC using commodity hardware | Research labs, universities | Built from COTS PCs, Linux, MPI |

## 2.3 The Beowulf Cluster Family — A Detailed Sub-Classification

The **Beowulf family** is a KTU-favorite topic and is itself subdivided:

| Beowulf Sub-Class | Description |
|---|---|
| **Class I Beowulf** | Identical commodity hardware, identical OS, dedicated LAN interconnect |
| **Class II Beowulf** | Heterogeneous hardware/OS, dedicated high-speed network |
| **Follow-the-Leader Cluster** | Master node feeds tasks to worker nodes sequentially |
| **Pipelined Cluster** | Workstations arranged in a pipeline; each performs a stage |
| **Split-Server Cluster** | Workstations partition into sub-clusters serving different purposes |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas required to solve KTU exam problems on this topic:**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Steady-state **Availability** | Ratio in $[0, 1]$; multiply by 100 for % |
| 2 | $A = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR}$ | Availability using MTTF for non-repairable systems | Used when component is replaced on failure |
| 3 | $Downtime_{year} = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$ | Annual downtime in **minutes** | For $A$ expressed as a ratio |
| 4 | $A_{system} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | **Series** (non-redundant) availability | Product of component availabilities |
| 5 | $A_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - A_i)$ | **Parallel** (redundant) availability | All components must fail for system failure |
| 6 | $MTTF_{parallel} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{i}$ | MTTF of $n$ parallel identical components | $\lambda$ = failure rate |
| 7 | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | Failure rate | Failures per hour |
| 8 | $A_{k-out-of-n} = \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} A^i (1-A)^{n-i}$ | Availability of $k$-of-$n$ redundancy | At least $k$ of $n$ must work |
| 9 | $RTO + RPO \leq SLA_{max}$ | Service Level Agreement bound | KTU often asks RTO/RPO trade-offs |

**Critical Rule:** In table cells, use `\vert` (NOT `|`) for absolute value or conditional — this prevents Markdown table breakage in the rendered KTU portal.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications**: Five-nines availability mandated for carrier-grade switches (5ESS, Ericsson MSC).
- **Financial Trading**: Stock exchanges require sub-second RTO and zero RPO — uses synchronous replication across geographically distributed HA clusters.
- **Cloud Providers (AWS, Azure, GCP)**: Multi-AZ HA clusters with active-active load balancing; S3 storage clusters achieve 99.999999999% (eleven nines) durability.
- **Healthcare / Aviation**: Life-critical and flight-control systems use active-passive hot-standby with hardware-triple-modular-redundancy (TMR).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Availability $A$

We begin with the formal definition of availability as the expected fraction of time a system is operational within a long observation window $T$.

**Step 1 — Partition the observation window into two disjoint intervals.**
During time $T$, the system is in one of two states: operational (up) or failed (down). The total operational time is the sum of all uptime intervals, and the total downtime is the sum of all repair intervals:

$$T = T_{up} + T_{down}$$

**Step 2 — Express uptime and downtime via MTBF and MTTR.**
By definition, MTBF is the *average* time between failures (i.e., average uptime), and MTTR is the *average* time to repair (i.e., average downtime). So in the long run:

$$T_{up} = MTBF \cdot N, \quad T_{down} = MTTR \cdot N$$

where $N$ is the number of failure-repair cycles in window $T$.

**Step 3 — Substitute into the availability definition.**
Availability $A$ is the ratio of uptime to total time:

$$A = \frac{T_{up}}{T_{up} + T_{down}}$$

**Step 4 — Insert MTBF and MTTR.**
Replacing $T_{up}$ and $T_{down}$ with their averaged counterparts:

$$A = \frac{MTBF \cdot N}{MTBF \cdot N + MTTR \cdot N}$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $N$.**
The factor $N$ cancels cleanly from numerator and denominator:

$$\boxed{A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}}$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for steady-state availability. $A \in [0, 1]$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Series System Availability

A series system functions *only if* every component functions. Let $A_i$ be the availability of component $i$, and assume component failures are independent.

**Step 1 — Failure of the system.**
The system fails if *at least one* component fails. The probability that component $i$ is down is $(1 - A_i)$.

**Step 2 — Probability all components are up simultaneously.**
For independent events, multiply the probabilities:

$$P(\text{all up}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$$

**Step 3 — Since "all up" = "system up":**

$$\boxed{A_{series} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Parallel (Redundant) System Availability

A parallel system functions if *at least one* component functions. Equivalent: the system fails only if **all** components fail.

**Step 1 — System-down event.**
All components must be in the down state. Probability of component $i$ being down is $(1 - A_i)$.

**Step 2 — Joint probability of all being down (independent):**

$$P(\text{system down}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - A_i)$$

**Step 3 — System-up probability is the complement:**

$$\boxed{A_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - A_i)}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard Style)

> **Problem:** A web server has MTBF = 1000 hours and MTTR = 10 hours. Calculate:
> (i) Availability as a percentage.
> (ii) Annual downtime in minutes.
> (iii) Availability if **two such servers** are deployed in active-passive redundancy.

**Solution:**

**Part (i):**

$$A = \frac{1000}{1000 + 10} = \frac{1000}{1010} = 0.990099...$$

$$A_{\%} = 99.01\%$$

**Part (ii):** Use the downtime formula with $A = 0.990099$:

$$Downtime_{year} = (1 - 0.990099) \times 525600$$

$$= 0.009901 \times 525600 = 5203.6 \text{ minutes/year} \approx 86.7 \text{ hours/year}$$

**Part (iii) — Active-Passive Redundancy:**
The system is up if *server 1 is up* OR *server 2 is up*. Apply the parallel formula with $A_1 = A_2 = 0.990099$:

$$A_{parallel} = 1 - (1 - 0.990099)^2 = 1 - (0.009901)^2$$

$$= 1 - 0.00009803 = 0.99990197$$

$$A_{\%} \approx 99.9902\% \quad (\text{nearly four nines from two nines!})$$

This dramatic improvement is precisely why **redundancy is the cornerstone of HA design**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Trade-offs in HA Design

| Design Choice | Pro | Con | KTU Trade-off Comment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Active-Passive cluster | Simple failover logic, no split-brain | 50% hardware idle | Cheaper; used in legacy ERP |
| Active-Active cluster | Higher utilization, load sharing | Complex quorum management | Preferred for web-tier |
| Synchronous replication | RPO = 0 (no data loss) | Higher write latency | Used in banking |
| Asynchronous replication | Low latency | Possible data loss on failover | Used in social media feeds |
| Geographic distribution | Survives site disasters | WAN latency, CAP trade-off | DR sites use this |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: High-Availability Cluster — Active-Passive Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    ClientA[Client 1] --> LB[Load Balancer / Virtual IP]
    ClientB[Client 2] --> LB
    ClientC[Client 3] --> LB
    LB --> NodeA[Active Node A - Primary]
    NodeA -.heartbeat.-> NodeB[Passive Node B - Standby]
    NodeB -.heartbeat.-> NodeA
    NodeA --> SharedFS[Shared Storage - SAN / NAS]
    NodeB --> SharedFS
    NodeA --> Monitor[Cluster Manager / Heartbeat Daemon]
    NodeB --> Monitor
    Monitor -.failover signal.-> NodeB
    NodeA -.failure detected.-> Monitor
```

**Reading the diagram:** Clients send requests to a Virtual IP fronted by a load balancer. The Active Node serves all traffic while sending periodic heartbeats to the Passive Node. A Cluster Manager monitors both. Upon Active-Node failure, the Manager triggers failover, the Passive Node promotes itself, takes over the Virtual IP, and resumes service. The shared storage (SAN/NAS) ensures data consistency.

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Active-Active Cluster with Load Distribution

```mermaid
graph TD
    User1[Users] --> EdgeLB[Edge Load Balancer]
    User2[Users] --> EdgeLB
    User3[Users] --> EdgeLB
    EdgeLB --> Node1[Active Node 1]
    EdgeLB --> Node2[Active Node 2]
    EdgeLB --> Node3[Active Node 3]
    Node1 -.sync heartbeat.-> Node2
    Node2 -.sync heartbeat.-> Node3
    Node3 -.sync heartbeat.-> Node1
    Node1 --> ReplStore[Replicated Storage Cluster]
    Node2 --> ReplStore
    Node3 --> ReplStore
    ReplStore --> CephFS[(Ceph / Distributed FS)]
```

**Reading the diagram:** All three nodes actively serve requests in parallel. Continuous heartbeats ensure rapid mutual awareness. A replicated distributed filesystem (Ceph) provides data durability. If one node fails, the load balancer redistributes its traffic to the remaining two, achieving both scalability and availability in one design.

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of an HA System

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph S1 [Application Layer]
        App1[Web Server]
        App2[App Server]
    end
    subgraph S2 [Middleware Layer - Clustering Framework]
        LM[Load Manager]
        FM[Failover Manager]
        QM[Quorum Manager]
        HR[Heartbeat Receiver]
    end
    subgraph S3 [Data Layer]
        DB1[DB Primary]
        DB2[DB Replica]
    end
    subgraph S4 [Hardware Layer - Redundancy]
        H1[Power Supply A]
        H2[Power Supply B]
        Net1[NIC 1]
        Net2[NIC 2]
    end
    App1 --> LM
    App2 --> FM
    LM --> HR
    FM --> QM
    HR -.pulse.-> HR
    App1 --> DB1
    App2 --> DB2
    DB1 -.replication.-> DB2
    S1 --> S4
    S2 --> S4
    S3 --> S4
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is a four-tier functional decomposition. The *Application Layer* runs user-facing services. The *Middleware Layer* houses the clustering intelligence (load manager, failover manager, quorum manager, heartbeat receiver). The *Data Layer* maintains replicated databases. The *Hardware Layer* provides power and network redundancy. Every layer has a built-in redundancy mechanism — this layered defense-in-depth is the engineering essence of HA.

## 4.4 Diagram 4: HA Decision Flowchart — Failover Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start([System Boot]) --> CheckQuorum{Quorum Reached?}
    CheckQuorum -- No --> HaltNode[Node Halts - Avoids Split Brain]
    CheckQuorum -- Yes --> ElectLeader[Elect Cluster Leader]
    ElectLeader --> ActiveRole{Designated Active?}
    ActiveRole -- Yes --> ServeTraffic[Serve Client Traffic]
    ActiveRole -- No --> Standby[Enter Standby Mode]
    ServeTraffic --> MonitorHB[Monitor Heartbeat from Leader]
    Standby --> MonitorHB
    MonitorHB --> HBAlive{Heartbeat Alive?}
    HBAlive -- Yes --> ServeTraffic
    HBAlive -- No --> WaitGrace[Wait Grace Period]
    WaitGrace --> VerifyFail{Confirmed Failure?}
    VerifyFail -- No --> ServeTraffic
    VerifyFail -- Yes --> PromoteSelf[Promote Self to Active]
    PromoteSelf --> ServeTraffic
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define High Availability. List any two techniques used to ensure high availability in distributed systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *Definition (1.5 marks):* High Availability (HA) is a system design attribute that ensures a system remains operational and accessible for a high percentage of scheduled time, typically expressed as a percentage such as 99.99% ("four nines").
- *Two techniques (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):*
  1. **Redundancy through clustering**: Deploying multiple nodes such that failure of one is masked by another (active-passive or active-active failover).
  2. **Data replication**: Maintaining multiple copies of data across geographically distributed nodes (synchronous or asynchronous) to tolerate storage failures.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between MTBF and MTTR. How are they related to system availability?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *MTBF (1 mark):* Mean Time Between Failures — the average operational duration between two consecutive system failures. Indicates system reliability.
- *MTTR (1 mark):* Mean Time To Repair — the average time taken to restore a failed system to operational state. Indicates maintainability.
- *Relationship to Availability (1 mark):* System availability is computed as $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$. Higher MTBF and lower MTTR both increase availability.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students often **forget to convert** the availability ratio to a percentage — losing 0.5 marks.
> - For **parallel redundancy**, students frequently **square the availability** instead of using $1 - (1-A)^2$ — losing 1 full mark.
> - In **cluster-family** questions, students fail to mention **which class** (Beowulf sub-family) and lose half the mark allocated to classification.
> - **No diagram** in a "Design Issues" question = guaranteed loss of 2 marks (KTU examiners expect at least a block diagram).
> - Students often confuse **MTTF** with **MTBF** in single-component formulas — read the question carefully.

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**a)** Explain the various design issues that must be addressed to ensure high availability in distributed systems. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Describe the different cluster families with their salient features. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Design Issues for High Availability (7 marks):**

The following design issues must be systematically addressed (each sub-issue ≈ 1.4 marks):

1. **Elimination of Single Points of Failure (SPOF):** Every tier — hardware, software, network, power — must be duplicated. Redundant NICs, RAID storage, dual power supplies, and bonded network links are standard practices. **No single component failure should bring the system down.**

2. **Fault Detection and Heartbeating:** Cluster nodes exchange periodic heartbeat signals (typically UDP packets on a dedicated link). Absence of a heartbeat within a timeout window triggers a fault suspicion, followed by a confirmation phase to avoid false positives.

3. **Failover and Recovery (Active-Passive / Active-Active):** Upon confirmed failure, the standby node is promoted and assigned the virtual IP. Recovery mechanisms (failback) restore the original configuration once the failed node returns. RTO (Recovery Time Objective) is the key metric.

4. **Data Replication and Consistency:** Synchronous replication guarantees RPO = 0 (no data loss) at the cost of latency; asynchronous replication trades data loss for speed. Quorum-based consensus (Paxos, Raft) prevents split-brain.

5. **Quorum Management:** In clusters with an even number of nodes, split-brain can occur. Quorum algorithms (e.g., majority voting, witness nodes) ensure only one partition continues serving.

6. **State Management:** Stateful services require session replication or shared session stores (e.g., Redis Cluster). Stateless services are inherently HA-friendly.

7. **Disaster Recovery and Geographic Redundancy:** HA within a data center is necessary but insufficient. Multi-site replication and DNS failover handle site-level disasters.

**Part (b) — Cluster Families (7 marks):**

| S.No | Cluster Family | Salient Features |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **High-Performance (HPC) Cluster** | Designed for compute-intensive parallel jobs. Uses MPI, InfiniBand. Example: LINUX Beowulf supercomputers. |
| 2 | **Load-Balancing Cluster** | Distributes incoming client requests across multiple nodes via a dispatcher. Used in web farms. |
| 3 | **High-Availability (HA) Cluster** | Provides continuous service through redundancy and automatic failover. Used for databases, ERP, email. |
| 4 | **Storage Cluster** | Provides unified, scalable, fault-tolerant storage. Uses distributed file systems (Ceph, HDFS, Lustre). |
| 5 | **Beowulf Cluster Family** | Sub-classified into Class I (homogeneous) and Class II (heterogeneous). Built from COTS hardware running Linux + MPI. Inexpensive HPC. |
| 6 | **Follow-the-Leader Cluster** | Master dispatches tasks sequentially to workers. |
| 7 | **Pipelined Cluster** | Each node performs one stage of a pipeline; data flows through. |

Each family addresses a different design intent — performance, scalability, availability, or storage — but modern systems often **combine families** (e.g., a load-balancing HA cluster with replicated storage).

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper — Alternative Choice]

**a)** A distributed system consists of two independent servers each with MTBF = 2000 hours and MTTR = 8 hours. Calculate: (i) availability of a single server; (ii) annual downtime in hours; (iii) availability when the two servers operate in active-passive redundancy. **(7 marks)**

**b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an Active-Active high-availability cluster. Discuss the role of heartbeats and quorum in preventing split-brain. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Single-server availability (2 marks):**
$$A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{2000}{2000 + 8} = \frac{2000}{2008} = 0.996016$$

$$A_{\%} = 99.6016\% \approx 99.60\%$$

**(ii) Annual downtime in hours (2 marks):**
$$Downtime_{year} = (1 - 0.996016) \times 365 \times 24$$

$$= 0.003984 \times 8760 = 34.90 \text{ hours/year}$$

**(iii) Active-Passive redundant availability (3 marks):**
The system is up if at least one server is up. Using the parallel-availability formula:

$$A_{parallel} = 1 - (1 - A_1)(1 - A_2)$$

With $A_1 = A_2 = 0.996016$:

$$A_{parallel} = 1 - (0.003984)^2 = 1 - 0.00001587 = 0.99998413$$

$$\boxed{A_{\%} \approx 99.998\% \quad (\text{nearly five nines})}$$

**Part (b) — Architecture and Split-Brain (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
        ┌──────────────┐
Clients→│ Virtual IP / │→┬─→ Node 1 (Active) ─┐
        │ Load Balancer│  │                   │
        └──────────────┘  ├─→ Node 2 (Active) ─┼─→ Replicated DB
                          │                   │
                          └─→ Node 3 (Active) ┘
                              ↕ ↕ ↕ (heartbeats)
                          Quorum Service
```

**Explanation (3 marks):**
- All three nodes actively serve client requests simultaneously. The load balancer (or DNS round-robin) distributes traffic. Each node is a peer; no dedicated standby.
- All nodes run the same application stack and access the replicated database. Failures are masked by the load balancer redistributing traffic from the failed node to survivors.
- **Heartbeat mechanism (0.5 mark):** Each node sends a heartbeat (typically every 1–2 seconds) to all peers. If a node misses three consecutive heartbeats from a peer, that peer is declared failed.
- **Quorum and Split-Brain (0.5 mark):** A quorum is the minimum number of nodes that must agree on cluster state for a partition to remain active. With 3 nodes, quorum = 2. If the cluster splits into 1+2, the partition with 2 nodes retains quorum and continues; the lone node halts — preventing **split-brain** (both partitions writing conflicting data).

**Advantages:** Full hardware utilization, horizontal scalability.
**Disadvantages:** Complex state synchronization; more expensive.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Availability** = $MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$ — the single most tested formula on this topic.
- **Series (no redundancy)** → multiply availabilities. **Parallel (redundancy)** → use $1 - \prod(1 - A_i)$.
- **$k$-of-$n$ systems** require the binomial-sum formula — useful for telecom and disk-array problems.
- The **"nines"** table (99.9% = 8.77 h/yr, 99.99% = 52.6 min/yr, 99.999% = 5.26 min/yr) is **must-memorize** for KTU short-answer questions.
- **Five cluster families** in KTU syllabus: HPC, Load-Balancing, High-Availability, Storage, and Beowulf (with Class I / Class II subdivisions).
- **Active-Passive** = simple, half hardware idle. **Active-Active** = utilized, complex, split-brain risk.
- **Synchronous replication** → RPO = 0, high latency. **Asynchronous replication** → low latency, possible data loss.
- **Quorum** = minimum vote count to prevent split-brain. With 2 nodes, a **witness node** is needed to break ties.
- Always include a **block diagram** in design questions — it carries 2–3 marks independently of the prose.
- For numerical problems: convert to **percentage** only at the final step; keep intermediate calculations as ratios.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cluster Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cluster Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — CLUSTER ARCHITECTURE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cluster Architecture** is a parallel and distributed computing architecture in which a collection of loosely coupled, independently operational computers (called **nodes**) are tightly integrated through high-speed interconnects and specialized **middleware** to act as a single, unified computing resource. Each node typically runs its own instance of an operating system, possesses its own local memory, local I/O, and local storage, but cooperatively executes workloads, shares state, and presents a consolidated system image to the end user.

According to the **IEEE Task Force on Cluster Computing (TFCC)** and standard textbook references (Buyya, *High Performance Cluster Computing*), a cluster is formally defined as a type of parallel system that comprises:

1. A collection of **commodity-off-the-shelf (COTS)** stand-alone computers (workstations, PCs, or servers).
2. A **high-performance network interconnect** (LAN/SAN fabric).
3. A **cluster middleware layer** that provides a single-system-image (SSI) illusion and offers services such as resource management, job scheduling, failover, and parallel programming support.
4. **Application-layer software** that exploits the aggregated compute, memory, and I/O capacity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Multiprocessors:** Clusters use **loose coupling** (message passing over a network) rather than **tight coupling** (shared memory bus), making them highly scalable but requiring explicit communication between nodes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen Brigade:**
> Imagine a single chef in a small kitchen. He can prepare only a few dishes per hour. Now imagine a *brigade de cuisine* (a professional kitchen team) where each station chef (saucier, pâtissier, rôtisseur, etc.) specializes in a specific task, all communicating through standardized *call-outs* (the network/middleware) and coordinated by an *aboyeur* (the head scheduler). They are individually competent, but together they serve hundreds of customers in parallel.
>
> - **Each chef** = a *node* (a complete, autonomous computer).
> - **The shared call-out system and ticket rail** = the *interconnect and middleware*.
> - **The head chef scheduling orders** = the *cluster job scheduler / resource manager*.
> - **Customers seeing one unified menu** = the *Single System Image (SSI)* illusion.
>
> If one chef falls sick (node failure), the others absorb the orders — this is **high availability through redundancy**.

### 1.3 Key Metrics and Standard Parameters

| **Parameter** | **Standard Value / Range** | **Significance** |
|---|---|---|
| Interconnect Bandwidth | **1 Gbps to 200 Gbps** | Determines communication throughput |
| Interconnect Latency | **< 1 µs (InfiniBand) to 100 µs (Ethernet)** | Critical for parallel performance |
| Node Count (typical HPC) | **8 to 100,000+** | Scalability metric |
| Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) | **10,000 to 1,000,000 hours** | Reliability metric |
| Cluster Availability | **99.9% to 99.999% ("five nines")** | Uptime target for HA clusters |
| Parallel Efficiency | **η = Speedup / N** | Fraction of ideal performance achieved |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Architectural Layers of a Cluster

A cluster is conventionally decomposed into **four logical layers** stacked from bottom (hardware) to top (application):

### Layer 1 — **Physical / Hardware Layer**
- **Compute nodes**: Each node is a complete computer (CPU + RAM + local disk + NIC).
- **Network Interface Cards (NICs)**: Multiple NICs per node for redundancy and bandwidth aggregation (NIC bonding/teaming).
- **Storage devices**: Local disks, shared SAN/NAS volumes.
- **Interconnect hardware**: Switches, routers, fiber-optic cabling, host channel adapters (HCAs).

### Layer 2 — **Operating System Layer**
- Each node runs an independent OS (typically Linux: CentOS, Rocky, Ubuntu, RHEL, or specialized HPC OS like SUSE Linux Enterprise Server).
- The OS provides:
  - Process scheduling on the local node.
  - Device drivers for the NIC and storage.
  - TCP/IP stack (or RDMA-capable kernel bypass).
  - Optionally, OS-bypass mechanisms for low-latency messaging (e.g., Mellanox VMA, OFED).

### Layer 3 — **Middleware / Single System Image (SSI) Layer**
This is the *defining* layer of a cluster. It provides:
- **Resource Management & Scheduling** (e.g., Slurm, Torque/PBS Pro, LSF, Kubernetes).
- **Parallel Programming Runtime** (MPI: OpenMPI, MPICH, Intel MPI; PVM; OpenMP).
- **Cluster File System** (Lustre, GPFS/Spectrum Scale, BeeGFS, GlusterFS, CephFS).
- **Cluster Monitoring & Management** (Ganglia, Nagios, Zabbix, Prometheus).
- **Availability Infrastructure** (Pacemaker + Corosync, Keepalived, Heartbeat for HA failover).
- **Load Balancers** (HAProxy, LVS/IPVS, NGINX).

### Layer 4 — **Application Layer**
- Scientific applications (CFD, FEA, molecular dynamics — GROMACS, NAMD, LAMMPS).
- Big Data frameworks (Hadoop MapReduce, Apache Spark, Kafka).
- Web/transactional stacks (LAMP on a load-balanced web farm).
- AI/ML training (TensorFlow, PyTorch distributed).

## 2.2 Classification of Clusters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test the **classification of clusters**. Memorize the four primary categories below:

### A. High-Performance Computing (HPC) Clusters
- **Goal**: Maximize aggregate compute throughput for *compute-bound* workloads.
- **Workload type**: Scientific/engineering simulations, tightly-coupled parallel jobs requiring frequent inter-node communication.
- **Example**: **Beowulf clusters** (the most cited textbook example — built from COTS Linux PCs and Ethernet, often Myricom Myrinet, or InfiniBand).
- **Programming model**: **MPI (Message Passing Interface)** is the de-facto standard.
- **Bottleneck**: Inter-node communication latency.

### B. High-Availability (HA) Clusters
- **Goal**: Provide *continuous service* by eliminating single points of failure.
- **Workload type**: Mission-critical transactional services (DBs, ERP, banking).
- **Mechanism**: **Active-Passive** (one node hot-stands-by) or **Active-Active** (both serve; load balances and fails over to survivor).
- **Heartbeat messaging**: Periodic liveness signals over a dedicated serial link or NIC; if missed beyond threshold → failover triggered.
- **Example**: Pacemaker + Corosync on Linux.

### C. Load-Balancing (LB) Clusters
- **Goal**: Distribute client requests across a *farm* of front-end nodes to maximize throughput and minimize response time.
- **Workload type**: Stateless request-response (web servers, API gateways).
- **Technique**: Round-robin DNS, L4/L7 load balancers (LVS, NGINX, HAProxy, F5 BIG-IP).
- **Backend sharing**: All nodes typically mount a shared filesystem (NFS) or replicate state.

### D. Storage Clusters
- **Goal**: Provide *highly available, scalable, and performant* block or object storage.
- **Examples**: Ceph, GlusterFS, MinIO, Hadoop HDFS.
- **Access patterns**: Block (iSCSI), File (NFS/SMB), Object (S3-compatible).

## 2.3 Cluster Topologies — Shared Memory Models

| **Topology** | **Memory Model** | **Communication Mechanism** | **Example System** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shared Nothing (SN)** | Each node has private memory & disk | **Message Passing** (MPI, PVM) | Beowulf, Hadoop, typical HPC |
| **Shared Disk (SD)** | Private memory, shared disk array | DSM cache coherencing; locking over interconnect | Oracle RAC, GPFS |
| **Shared Memory (SM)** | Single global address space | Memory load/store (cache-coherent NUMA) | SGI UV, classical SMPs — *not pure clusters* |

> [!NOTE]
> **Distributed Shared Memory (DSM)** software (e.g., TreadMarks, IVY) can *emulate* shared memory on top of a shared-nothing cluster, but with high software overhead.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are essential for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE and continuous-assessment problems.

| **Concept** | **Formula / Definition** | **Units** | **Engineering Utility** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Amdahl's Law (Speedup Limit)** | $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}$ | dimensionless | Predicts max speedup for $N$ nodes given serial fraction $f$ |
| **Parallel Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{S(N)}{N} = \dfrac{1}{N(1-f) + f}$ | dimensionless | Measures scalability efficiency |
| **Gustafson's Law (Scaled Speedup)** | $S_{\text{scaled}}(N) = N - f(N - 1)$ | dimensionless | Realistic speedup when problem size scales with $N$ |
| **Communication-to-Computation Ratio** | $r_c = \dfrac{T_{\text{comm}}}{T_{\text{comp}}}$ | dimensionless | Determines if workload is communication-bound |
| **Cluster Availability** | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | fraction / "nines" | HA design target |
| **Aggregate Bandwidth** | $B_{\text{agg}} = N \times B_{\text{link}}$ | bits/sec or bytes/sec | Total network throughput capacity |
| **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** of cluster | $\dfrac{1}{\text{MTTF}_{\text{cluster}}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{1}{\text{MTTF}_{i}}$ | hours | Reliability of N parallel components |
| **Isoefficiency** | $\Theta(P) = \dfrac{T_{o}(P)}{T_{p}(P)}$ | work units | Workload needed to keep efficiency constant |
| **Network Diameter** | $D = \max_{u,v} d(u,v)$ | hops | Worst-case communication latency in topology |
