| **Bisection Bandwidth** | $B_b = \min(\text{cut sum})$ | bits/sec | Lower bound on all-to-all communication |
| **Cost-Optimality** | $T_{\text{parallel}} = \Theta(T_{\text{serial}} / P)$ | time | Optimal use of $P$ processors |
| **Speedup Definition** | $S = \dfrac{T_{\text{serial}}}{T_{\text{parallel}}}$ | dimensionless | Baseline performance metric |

> **Syllabus highlight:** $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}$ is the single most-tested formula in cluster/parallel computing questions.

## 2.5 Why and How — The Design Philosophy

- **Why clusters?** Mainframes and supercomputers are expensive and proprietary. Clusters offer *price/performance* advantage by leveraging commodity hardware and open-source software.
- **How does it work?** A *resource manager* (Slurm) accepts jobs, allocates nodes, and the *runtime* (MPI) provides a uniform communication API. The OS on each node handles local execution; the middleware stitches them into one logical system.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Amdahl's Law and Parallel Efficiency

### Derivation 1: Amdahl's Law (Fixed-Workload Model)

Let a program be decomposed into a **serial fraction** $f$ (must run on a single processor) and a **parallelizable fraction** $(1 - f)$. With $N$ processors:

- Time taken by serial fraction: $T_{\text{serial\_part}} = f \cdot T$
- Time taken by parallel fraction on one processor: $T_{\text{parallel\_part}} = (1 - f) \cdot T$
- Time taken by parallel fraction on $N$ processors: $\dfrac{(1 - f) \cdot T}{N}$

Therefore, the **total parallel execution time** is:

$$
T_{\text{parallel}}(N) = f \cdot T + \dfrac{(1 - f) \cdot T}{N}
$$

**Speedup** $S(N)$ is defined as the ratio of serial to parallel time:

$$
S(N) = \dfrac{T}{T_{\text{parallel}}(N)} = \dfrac{T}{f \cdot T + \dfrac{(1 - f) \cdot T}{N}}
$$

Factor $T$ from the denominator:

$$
S(N) = \dfrac{1}{f + \dfrac{1 - f}{N}}
$$

This is the canonical Amdahl's Law. Re-written by splitting the denominator:

$$
S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) \cdot \dfrac{1}{N} + f}
$$

**Limiting case** as $N \to \infty$:

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \dfrac{1}{f}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Even with infinite processors, the speedup is *bounded* by the reciprocal of the serial fraction. If 5% of the code is serial ($f = 0.05$), the maximum achievable speedup is **20×**, no matter how many processors are used.

### Derivation 2: Parallel Efficiency

Efficiency is the fraction of *useful* work per processor. It is defined as:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{S(N)}{N}
$$

Substituting the expression for $S(N)$:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{1}{N \left( f + \dfrac{1-f}{N} \right)} = \dfrac{1}{N f + (1 - f)}
$$

Or, expanded:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{1}{N f - f + 1}
$$

This shows efficiency approaches zero as $N \to \infty$ for any $f > 0$.

### Derivation 3: Gustafson's Law (Scaled Workload Model)

Gustafson observed that Amdahl's law is *pessimistic* because real users **increase problem size** with more processors. Let the parallel runtime on $N$ processors be the *fixed* baseline $T'$. The serial equivalent time is:

$$
T_{\text{serial}} = f \cdot T' + (1 - f) \cdot N \cdot T'
$$

Scaled speedup:

$$
S_{\text{scaled}}(N) = \dfrac{f \cdot T' + (1 - f) \cdot N \cdot T'}{T'} = f + N(1 - f)
$$

Which can also be written as:

$$
S_{\text{scaled}}(N) = N - f(N - 1)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Gustafson's Law yields *linear* or *super-linear* speedup in practice because problem size grows with $N$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A cluster application spends 15% of its execution time in inherently serial code. Compute the speedup and efficiency on 8 processors and on 64 processors.

**Given:** $f = 0.15$, $N_1 = 8$, $N_2 = 64$.

**For $N = 8$:**

$$
S(8) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.15) \cdot \dfrac{1}{8} + 0.15} = \dfrac{1}{0.85 \cdot 0.125 + 0.15}
$$

$$
S(8) = \dfrac{1}{0.10625 + 0.15} = \dfrac{1}{0.25625} \approx 3.90
$$

$$
\eta(8) = \dfrac{3.90}{8} \approx 0.488 \text{ or } 48.8\%
$$

**For $N = 64$:**

$$
S(64) = \dfrac{1}{0.85 \cdot \dfrac{1}{64} + 0.15} = \dfrac{1}{0.01328 + 0.15} = \dfrac{1}{0.16328} \approx 6.12
$$

$$
\eta(64) = \dfrac{6.12}{64} \approx 0.0956 \text{ or } 9.56\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Doubling $N$ from 8 to 64 only increased speedup from 3.90× to 6.12× due to the serial bottleneck. The cluster becomes increasingly inefficient — typical of a **compute-bound** regime.

## 3.3 Cluster Availability Derivation

For an HA cluster of $N$ *independent* nodes, each with MTBF $\mu_i$ and MTTR $\tau_i$:

$$
A_i = \dfrac{\mu_i}{\mu_i + \tau_i}
$$

System availability assuming parallel redundancy:

$$
A_{\text{system}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{N} (1 - A_i)
$$

If all $N$ nodes are identical with availability $A$:

$$
A_{\text{system}} = 1 - (1 - A)^{N}
$$

**Numerical example:** Single node availability $A = 0.99$ (i.e., 99%). With $N = 2$ active-passive nodes:

$$
A_{\text{system}} = 1 - (0.01)^2 = 1 - 0.0001 = 0.9999 \text{ (four nines)}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This formula explains the economic rationale of HA clusters: redundancy dramatically improves availability.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cluster Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userLayer[User and Client Applications]
    appLayer[Application Layer: MPI, Spark, Web Stack, AI Frameworks]
    ssiLayer[Middleware and SSI Layer: Slurm Scheduler, Pacemaker, LVS Load Balancer, Ganglia Monitor]
    fsLayer[Cluster File System: Lustre, GPFS, CephFS, NFS]
    netLayer[Interconnect Fabric: InfiniBand HDR, 100GbE, Myrinet]
    nodeA[Compute Node 1: CPU RAM NIC Disk OS]
    nodeB[Compute Node 2: CPU RAM NIC Disk OS]
    nodeC[Compute Node 3: CPU RAM NIC Disk OS]
    nodeD[Compute Node N: CPU RAM NIC Disk OS]
    storageA[Shared Storage: SAN or NAS or Object Store]
    mgmtNode[Management Node: Head Login Master]
    userLayer --> appLayer
    appLayer --> ssiLayer
    ssiLayer --> fsLayer
    ssiLayer --> netLayer
    netLayer --> nodeA
    netLayer --> nodeB
    netLayer --> nodeC
    netLayer --> nodeD
    nodeA --> netLayer
    nodeB --> netLayer
    nodeC --> netLayer
    nodeD --> netLayer
    netLayer --> storageA
    mgmtNode --> netLayer
```

## 4.2 Cluster Classification — Topology Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    clusterStart[Cluster Architecture]
    clusterStart --> hpc[High Performance Computing HPC Cluster]
    clusterStart --> ha[High Availability HA Cluster]
    clusterStart --> lb[Load Balancing Cluster]
    clusterStart --> st[Storage Cluster]
    hpc --> hpcEx[Example: Beowulf, MPI, InfiniBand]
    hpc --> hpcUse[Use: Scientific Simulation CFD, Genomics]
    ha --> haEx[Example: Pacemaker Corosync, Active Passive]
    ha --> haUse[Use: Banking, ERP, Mission Critical DB]
    lb --> lbEx[Example: LVS, NGINX, HAProxy]
    lb --> lbUse[Use: Web Farms, API Gateways]
    st --> stEx[Example: Ceph, GlusterFS, HDFS]
    st --> stUse[Use: Big Data, Backup, Object Stores]
```

## 4.3 Single System Image (SSI) — Service Stack View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ssiTop[Single System Image View to User]
    ssiTop --> s1[Single File System across nodes]
    ssiTop --> s2[Single Network Identity and IP]
    ssiTop --> s3[Single Job Queue and Scheduler]
    ssiTop --> s4[Single Process Space optional]
    ssiTop --> s5[Single Checkpoint and Migration Service]
    ssiMid[SSI Provided by Middleware]
    ssiMid --> m1[Resource Manager Slurm]
    ssiMid --> m2[Cluster File System Lustre]
    ssiMid --> m3[HA Framework Pacemaker]
    ssiMid --> m4[Cluster Shell and Tools clush, pdsh]
    ssiBase[Underlying Independent Operating Systems]
    ssiBase --> o1[Linux Node 1]
    ssiBase --> o2[Linux Node 2]
    ssiBase --> o3[Linux Node N]
```

## 4.4 Beowulf Cluster — Logical Layout (Classic Reference)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    frontEnd[Front End Node: Master Login Job Submission]
    switch1[Gigabit Ethernet Switch]
    node1[Compute Node 1: COTS PC]
    node2[Compute Node 2: COTS PC]
    node3[Compute Node 3: COTS PC]
    nodeN[Compute Node N: COTS PC]
    privateNet[Private Interconnect Subnet: 192.168.x.x]
    pubNet[Public Network: Internet Access]
    frontEnd --> pubNet
    frontEnd --> privateNet
    privateNet --> switch1
    switch1 --> node1
    switch1 --> node2
    switch1 --> node3
    switch1 --> nodeN
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Beowulf** design (Sterling et al., 1999) is the canonical textbook example: commodity PCs + Linux + Ethernet + MPI. Modern descendants (Top500 supercomputers) use InfiniBand or proprietary fabrics but the architectural principles are identical.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a cluster. Differentiate between tightly coupled and loosely coupled multiprocessor systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *cluster* is a collection of independent, stand-alone computers interconnected through a high-speed network and coordinated by middleware to function as a single unified computing resource.

- **Tightly coupled systems (e.g., SMP)**: Share a *single physical memory* and a common system bus; communication happens via shared variables with low latency (nanoseconds); typically limited to a few dozen cores.
- **Loosely coupled systems (clusters)**: Each node has *private memory*; communication happens via explicit *message passing* over a network (microseconds); scalability extends to thousands of nodes.

| **Aspect** | **Tightly Coupled (SMP)** | **Loosely Coupled (Cluster)** |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | Shared | Distributed (private) |
| Communication | Shared variables | Message passing (MPI) |
| Latency | ~ns | ~µs |
| Scalability | Poor (bus contention) | Excellent |
| Cost | Very high | Low (commodity) |

*(1 mark definition + 2 marks comparison table.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List the four main types of cluster architectures and state the purpose of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **High-Performance Computing (HPC) cluster** — Maximize aggregate compute power for scientific/engineering parallel jobs. *Example: Beowulf.*
2. **High-Availability (HA) cluster** — Provide continuous service via redundancy and failover. *Example: Pacemaker-based DB cluster.*
3. **Load-Balancing (LB) cluster** — Distribute client requests across a server farm for scalability. *Example: LVS web farm.*
4. **Storage cluster** — Deliver scalable, redundant, distributed storage. *Example: Ceph, GlusterFS.*

*(1 mark for naming + 2 marks for correctly identifying their distinct goals with examples.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **🔹 Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a Beowulf cluster**. Discuss the role of the master node, compute nodes, and the private interconnect. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A parallel program spends **20% of its execution in serial mode** and the rest in parallel mode. Compute: **(i)** the maximum achievable speedup using Amdahl's Law; **(ii)** the speedup on 16 and 64 processors; **(iii)** the parallel efficiency on each. Comment on the results. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Beowulf Cluster Architecture (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** A Beowulf cluster is a high-performance computing system built entirely from **commodity-off-the-shelf (COTS)** hardware (standard PCs/workstations), connected by a **private Ethernet network**, running **open-source software** (typically Linux), and using **message-passing libraries** (MPI/PVM) for parallel computation.

**Block diagram description (3 marks):**

```
   [Public Internet]
         |
   [Master/Front-End Node]
   - Job submission (qsub, srun)
   - NFS server for /home
   - Scheduler daemon (Slurm/PBS)
         |
   [Private Gigabit Switch]  ← 192.168.x.x subnet
    /        |        \
 [Node1] [Node2] ... [NodeN]   (diskless or local-disk compute slaves)
```

**Roles (3 marks):**

| **Component** | **Role** |
|---|---|
| **Master / Front-End Node** | Sole node exposed to the public network; handles user login, job submission, NFS serving of /home, hosts the scheduler and shared services. Compute jobs are *not* run here. |
| **Compute Nodes** | Worker nodes that execute parallel tasks allocated by the scheduler. They boot via PXE, mount /home from the master via NFS, and run MPI processes. |
| **Private Interconnect** | Dedicated Ethernet switch (often a second NIC per node for a service network) carrying MPI traffic; isolated from public traffic to reduce latency and jitter. |
| **Middleware** | Slurm/Torque (scheduling), OpenMPI/MPICH (parallel communication), Ganglia (monitoring), NFS (file sharing). |

---

#### **Part (b) — Amdahl's Law Computation (7 marks)**

**Given:** $f = 0.20$ (serial fraction), $1 - f = 0.80$ (parallel fraction).

**(i) Maximum speedup as $N \to \infty$ (1 mark):**

$$
S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{0.20} = 5
$$

**(ii) Speedup on $N = 16$ (2 marks):**

$$
S(16) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) \cdot \dfrac{1}{N} + f} = \dfrac{1}{0.80 \cdot \dfrac{1}{16} + 0.20}
$$

$$
S(16) = \dfrac{1}{0.05 + 0.20} = \dfrac{1}{0.25} = 4.0
$$

**Speedup on $N = 64$ (2 marks):**

$$
S(64) = \dfrac{1}{0.80 \cdot \dfrac{1}{64} + 0.20} = \dfrac{1}{0.0125 + 0.20} = \dfrac{1}{0.2125} \approx 4.71
$$

**(iii) Parallel efficiency (2 marks):**

$$
\eta(16) = \dfrac{4.0}{16} = 0.25 \text{ (25%)}
$$

$$
\eta(64) = \dfrac{4.71}{64} \approx 0.0736 \text{ (7.36%)}
$$

**Comment (1 mark):** Although we quadrupled the processors (16 → 64), the speedup only improved from 4.0× to 4.71×. The 20% serial bottleneck caps the maximum achievable speedup at 5×. Efficiency drops sharply, demonstrating poor scalability — the application is **strongly serial-bound**.

---

### **🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **HPC clusters, HA clusters, and Load-Balancing clusters** in terms of objective, workload type, programming model/coordination mechanism, and a representative real-world example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **availability formula for a cluster of $N$ identical nodes** each with individual availability $A$. Compute the system availability of a 2-node HA cluster if each node has MTBF = 5000 hours and MTTR = 10 hours. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Comparison of Three Cluster Types (7 marks)**

| **Criterion** | **HPC Cluster** | **HA Cluster** | **Load-Balancing Cluster** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Objective** | Maximize aggregate FLOPS / throughput | Maximize uptime; eliminate single-point-of-failure | Maximize request throughput; minimize response time |
| **Workload Type** | Tightly-coupled parallel jobs (MPI) | Transactional, stateful services | Stateless request-response (web, API) |
| **Coordination** | Message Passing (MPI/PVM), scheduler (Slurm) | Heartbeat + failover (Pacemaker/Corosync) | L4/L7 load balancer (LVS/NGINX) |
| **Node Redundancy** | Optional (for resilience, not for performance) | **Mandatory** (active-passive or active-active) | All nodes actively serve |
| **Data Sharing** | Often shared-nothing; cluster FS for I/O | Shared disk (SAN); replicated state | Stateless: shared DB or shared nothing |
| **Failure Impact** | Job restarts; tolerated | Transparent failover | Node removed from LB pool |
| **Example System** | Beowulf, Top500 supercomputers | Linux HA, Veritas Cluster Server, MSFC | LVS, NGINX, F5 BIG-IP, HAProxy |
| **Application Domain** | CFD, weather, genomics, AI training | Banking, ERP, telecom core, databases | E-commerce, search engines, SaaS APIs |

*(2 marks for criterion identification + 3 marks for accurate distinctions + 2 marks for valid examples.)*

---

#### **Part (b) — Cluster Availability Derivation (7 marks)**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

For a single node, availability is the probability it is operational:

$$
A_{\text{node}} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = A
$$

The unavailability (downtime probability) is:

$$
U_{\text{node}} = 1 - A
$$

For an **HA cluster of $N$ identical nodes** running in parallel redundancy, the cluster fails *only* if **all $N$ nodes fail simultaneously**. Assuming independent failures:

$$
U_{\text{cluster}} = \prod_{i=1}^{N} U_{\text{node}} = (1 - A)^{N}
$$

Therefore:

$$
A_{\text{cluster}} = 1 - (1 - A)^{N}
$$

**Numerical computation (3 marks):**

Given MTBF = 5000 h, MTTR = 10 h, $N = 2$:

**Step 1:** Per-node availability

$$
A = \dfrac{5000}{5000 + 10} = \dfrac{5000}{5010} \approx 0.998004
$$

**Step 2:** Per-node unavailability

$$
U = 1 - 0.998004 = 0.001996
$$

**Step 3:** Cluster availability

$$
A_{\text{cluster}} = 1 - (0.001996)^{2} = 1 - 0.000003984 = 0.999996
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to "nines":

$$
A_{\text{cluster}} \approx 99.9996\% \text{ (approximately "five nines")}
$$

**Interpretation (included in 3 marks):** The 2-node HA cluster achieves ~99.9996% availability — a 250× improvement over a single node. Downtime reduces from ~87.6 hours/year (single node) to ~0.35 hours/year.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common marks-losing mistakes in Cluster Architecture questions:**
> 1. **Confusing cluster types** — HA and Load-Balancing are *not* the same. HA targets *uptime* via redundancy; LB targets *throughput* via distribution. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting to compute efficiency separately** — Many students compute $S(N)$ but skip $\eta(N) = S(N)/N$. This forfeits 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Plugging wrong values into Amdahl's Law** — The serial fraction $f$ goes in the *un-shared* term; the parallel fraction $(1 - f)$ is divided by $N$. Reversing them yields nonsense.
> 4. **Writing "Speedup = 5" without showing the formula** — Always show the symbolic substitution: $S = 1 / ((1-f)/N + f)$ → substitute values → compute.
> 5. **Treating availability as simply $1 - \text{MTTR}/\text{MTBF}$** — For a *cluster*, the formula is $1 - (1 - A)^N$, which is a *product*, not a sum.
> 6. **Omitting the block diagram** — In Beowulf / HA questions, the block diagram is worth 2–3 marks *by itself*. Always include one, even if hand-drawn.
> 7. **Stating "MPI is for shared memory"** — Wrong. MPI is *message passing* for distributed memory clusters. OpenMP (or MPI-3 shared memory windows) is for shared memory. This single error can cost full marks on a "programming model" sub-question.
> 8. **Confusing $f$ and $1-f$** — If a problem says "20% serial," then $f = 0.20$, NOT 0.80. Read carefully.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cluster Architecture — Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

- ✅ A **cluster** is a collection of COTS computers integrated via network and middleware to act as one system.
- ✅ Clusters are **loosely coupled, distributed-memory, message-passing** systems — distinct from SMPs and MPPs.
- ✅ **Four cluster types**: HPC, HA, Load-Balancing, Storage — each with a distinct goal and design.
- ✅ **Beowulf** is the canonical HPC cluster example: COTS + Linux + Ethernet + MPI.
- ✅ The **Single System Image (SSI)** illusion is delivered by **middleware** (Slurm, Pacemaker, LVS, Lustre, etc.).
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law**: $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-f)/N + f}$ is the *single most important formula* — practice numerical problems.
- ✅ **Limiting speedup** as $N \to \infty$ is $1/f$ — the serial fraction is the *bottleneck*.
- ✅ **Parallel efficiency** $\eta = S(N)/N$ must be reported alongside speedup in every problem.
- ✅ **Gustafson's Law** ($S = N - f(N-1)$) is the realistic scaled-workload counterpart to Amdahl — both are syllabus-relevant.
- ✅ **Cluster availability** for $N$ identical nodes: $A = 1 - (1 - A_{\text{node}})^N$ — the redundancy multiplier effect.
- ✅ **Interconnects**: Ethernet (cheap, ubiquitous), InfiniBand (high bandwidth, low latency — HPC standard), Myrinet (legacy HPC), Fibre Channel (SAN storage).
- ✅ **Programming models**: MPI (cluster standard), PVM (legacy), OpenMP (shared-memory hybrid with MPI), MapReduce / Spark (data-parallel).
- ✅ **Cluster file systems**: Lustre, GPFS, BeeGFS, GlusterFS, CephFS — distinguish from local FS.
- ✅ **Key middleware tools to remember**: Slurm (scheduler), Pacemaker (HA), LVS / NGINX / HAProxy (LB), Ganglia / Nagios (monitor), NFS (file sharing).
- ✅ **Be able to draw** a Beowulf architecture, an HA active-passive pair, and an LVS load-balancing cluster — diagrams fetch 2–3 marks each.
- ✅ **Differentiate**: tightly coupled (SMP, shared bus) vs loosely coupled (cluster, message-passing) — a perennial KTU favorite.
- ✅ **Keywords to use in answers**: *scalability, fault tolerance, single point of failure, single system image, high availability, message passing, parallel efficiency, isoefficiency, bisection bandwidth.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GPU Clusters - Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** GPU Clusters - Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GPU Cluster:** A **GPU Cluster** is a distributed heterogeneous parallel computing architecture that integrates multiple Graphics Processing Units (GPUs) across several compute nodes interconnected via a high-bandwidth, low-latency network fabric. Each node typically pairs one or more multi-core CPUs (acting as the *host*) with one or more GPUs (acting as the *device*), orchestrated through a unified software stack for massively parallel workloads such as deep learning, scientific simulation, and large-scale data analytics.

The cluster is fundamentally a **heterogeneous Many Integrated Core (MIC) / GPU hybrid system** that exploits two levels of parallelism:

1. **Intra-node (Device-level) parallelism:** Exploited within a single GPU through thousands of fine-grained SIMT (Single Instruction, Multiple Thread) cores.
2. **Inter-node (Cluster-level) parallelism:** Exploited across multiple GPU-equipped nodes communicating through Message Passing Interface (MPI) or NCCL.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from CPU Clusters:** A traditional CPU cluster relies on coarse-grained MIMD parallelism (a few powerful cores per node). A GPU cluster combines *coarse-grained MIMD across nodes* with *fine-grained SIMT within each GPU*, yielding a two-tier parallel hierarchy unmatched by CPU-only systems for throughput-oriented workloads.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Bakery Production Line vs. The Pizza Factory":**
>
> - Imagine a **CPU cluster** as a fleet of small artisan bakeries, each with 8–32 highly skilled chefs who can individually prepare any complex dish from scratch. They are great at varied, decision-heavy tasks but each dish takes a while.
> - A **GPU cluster** is like a **massive pizza factory**: at each node you have a few managers (the CPUs) who plan the workflow and supply raw materials, but the actual dough-stretching, topping, and baking is performed by thousands of simple, synchronized workers (the GPU cores) all performing the *same step* on *different pizzas simultaneously*.
> - Connecting many such pizza factories via high-speed highways (InfiniBand / NVLink network) creates a **GPU cluster** — a continental-scale production network for massive orders (think: training a Large Language Model on billions of tokens).

---

### 1.3 Why GPU Clusters? — The Driving Metrics

| Metric | CPU Cluster | GPU Cluster |
|---|---|---|
| **Peak FLOPS (FP32)** | ~1–3 TFLOPS/node | ~30–150 TFLOPS/node |
| **Memory Bandwidth** | ~50–100 GB/s | ~900–3,000 GB/s |
| **Energy per FLOP** | ~10–30 pJ | ~1–5 pJ |
| **Core Count / Node** | 16–128 cores | 10,000+ CUDA cores |

> The **performance-per-watt** and **memory-bandwidth-per-dollar** superiority of GPUs is precisely why they have become the *de facto* accelerator in modern HPC and AI infrastructure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Component Breakdown

A GPU cluster is **not just GPUs bolted together**. It is a layered system. Let us walk through each component layer, top-down, from the user-facing job submission all the way down to the silicon.

---

#### **Layer 1 — Front-End / Scheduling Layer**

This is the user-facing component. No GPU ever runs a user's job unless the scheduler allows it.

- **Job Scheduler (Resource Manager):** Software that queues user jobs and allocates cluster resources. Examples: **SLURM** (Simple Linux Utility for Resource Management), PBS Pro, LSF, Kubernetes (for cloud-native clusters).
- **Login Node:** The interactive entry node where users compile code, edit scripts, and submit jobs. It does **not** execute compute jobs.
- **Head Node / Controller:** Runs the scheduler daemon and orchestrates job placement across compute nodes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept — "Head Node vs. Compute Node"**: The head node is the *brain* (makes decisions); compute nodes are the *muscle* (do the work). Mixing these is a common architectural anti-pattern.

---

#### **Layer 2 — Compute Node Layer (The Heterogeneous Box)**

Each compute node is a self-contained heterogeneous computer with:

- **Host CPU(s):** Typically 1–2 server-grade multi-core processors (Intel Xeon, AMD EPYC). Used for control flow, I/O orchestration, MPI ranks, and CUDA kernel launches.
- **System Memory (Host RAM):** DDR4/DDR5 memory, typically **256 GB – 2 TB per node**, used for staging data, MPI buffers, and CPU code.
- **GPU(s):** The accelerators. Typical configurations:
  - 4× NVIDIA H100 (94 GB HBM3 each) — frontier-class AI node
  - 8× NVIDIA A100 (40/80 GB HBM2e) — common research cluster
  - Consumer: 1–2× RTX 4090 (24 GB GDDR6X) — academic lab scale
- **Local NVMe SSD:** Fast scratch storage on the node (often 1–10 TB) for staging large datasets before training, avoiding network bottlenecks.

---

#### **Layer 3 — GPU Internal Architecture (The Device)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The GPU is itself a cluster in miniature.** Understanding its components is essential to understanding the cluster as a whole.

The GPU consists of:

- **Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs):** The GPU's fundamental compute units. An H100 GPU has **132 SMs**; each SM contains:
  - **CUDA Cores** (FP32 ALUs)
  - **Tensor Cores** (specialized matrix-multiply units)
  - **Special Function Units (SFUs)** for transcendentals
  - **Warp Schedulers** that dispatch **warps** (groups of 32 threads) to execution units.
- **Memory Hierarchy (from fastest to slowest):**
  1. **Registers** — Per-thread, ~256 KB total per SM.
  2. **Shared Memory / L1 Cache** — ~128 KB per SM, software-managed, latency ~20 cycles.
  3. **L2 Cache** — Shared across all SMs, ~50 MB on H100, latency ~200 cycles.
  4. **Global Memory (HBM3/VRAM)** — 40–94 GB, latency ~400–800 cycles, bandwidth ~3 TB/s.
- **Memory Controller & HBM Stacks:** High-Bandwidth Memory uses stacked DRAM dies connected via a silicon interposer (2.5D packaging) using **TSV (Through-Silicon Vias)**.

---

#### **Layer 4 — Intra-Node Interconnect (CPU ↔ GPU Communication)**

How does data get from the host (CPU) RAM to the device (GPU) VRAM? Through the **PCI Express bus** or, in modern systems, **NVLink**.

- **PCIe Gen5:** 16 lanes → **~64 GB/s** bidirectional, but typically **~28 GB/s** practical.
- **NVLink 4.0 (H100):** Up to **900 GB/s** bidirectional GPU-to-GPU; ~**450 GB/s** CPU↔GPU when combined with NVSwitch.
- **NVSwitch:** A silicon chip that creates a non-blocking fabric connecting up to 8 GPUs at full NVLink bandwidth.

> [!TIP]
> **NVLink vs PCIe Analogy:** PCIe is a *two-lane country road*; NVLink is a *12-lane superhighway*. For multi-GPU training, NVLink can yield **2–5× speedups** over PCIe.

---

#### **Layer 5 — Inter-Node Network Fabric**

This is what makes a cluster a *cluster* — the network connecting nodes.

| Network | Bandwidth | Latency | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gigabit Ethernet** | 1 Gbps (~0.125 GB/s) | ~100 µs | Budget clusters |
| **10/100 GbE** | 10–100 Gbps | ~50 µs | Mid-tier, cloud |
| **InfiniBand HDR** | 200 Gbps (~25 GB/s) | ~2–5 µs | HPC, AI clusters |
| **InfiniBand NDR** | 400 Gbps (~50 GB/s) | ~1–3 µs | Frontier systems |
| **Slingshot (HPE)** | 200–400 Gbps | <1 µs | Exascale (Frontier) |

The topology is usually **fat-tree** or **Dragonfly+**, designed to give **bisection bandwidth** ≈ aggregate bandwidth (so any node can talk to any other node at full speed).

---

#### **Layer 6 — Storage Layer**

Compute is useless without data. GPU clusters need storage that can feed the GPUs at full bandwidth.

- **Parallel File Systems:** Lustre, GPFS (IBM Spectrum Scale), BeeGFS. They stripe data across hundreds of OSS (Object Storage Servers).
- **Burst Buffers:** Node-local NVMe or SSD arrays that absorb I/O spikes, then drain to the parallel filesystem in the background.
- **Metadata Servers (MDS):** Track file locations, permissions, striping information.

> **Typical Cluster Storage Target:** A petascale GPU cluster must sustain **>1 TB/s aggregate read bandwidth** to keep the GPUs fed.

---

#### **Layer 7 — Software / Programming Stack**

The glue that makes everything usable:

- **CUDA / ROCm:** Vendor-specific GPU programming models.
- **OpenCL / SYCL:** Vendor-neutral standards.
- **MPI (OpenMPI, MVAPICH2):** Inter-node communication.
- **NCCL (NVIDIA Collective Communications Library):** Highly optimized multi-GPU primitives (AllReduce, AllGather) that saturate NVLink and InfiniBand.
- **Math Libraries:** cuBLAS, cuFFT, cuDNN, CUTLASS.
- **Frameworks:** PyTorch, TensorFlow, JAX, Megatron-LM, DeepSpeed.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are *tested concepts* in KTU ESE and are routinely asked as 3-mark or 14-mark questions.

**Table 2.1 — Essential GPU Cluster Formulas**

| Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CUDA Occupancy** | $O = \dfrac{\text{Active Warps per SM}}{\text{Max Warps per SM}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le O \le 1$ | Lower occupancy ⇒ lower latency hiding |
| **Theoretical Peak FLOPs** | $P_{\text{peak}} = 2 \cdot N_{\text{SM}} \cdot F \cdot C$ | FLOPs/s | $N_{\text{SM}}$ = SM count, $F$ = clock (Hz), $C$ = FMA units per SM |
| **Effective Bandwidth** | $B_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{D}{T}$ | GB/s | $D$ = data transferred, $T$ = elapsed time |
| **Compute-to-Bandwidth Ratio** | $r = \dfrac{P_{\text{peak}}}{B_{\text{eff}}}$ | FLOPs/byte | The **Arithmetic Intensity (AI)** inverse metric |
| **Roofline Bound** | $P \le \min(P_{\text{peak}},\; B_{\text{eff}} \cdot AI)$ | FLOPs/s | If $AI \ge r$: compute-bound. Else: memory-bound |
| **Amdahl's Law** | $S_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{N}}$ | Dimensionless | $p$ = parallel fraction, $N$ = number of accelerators |
| **Gustafson's Law** | $S_{\text{scaled}} = (1-p) + p \cdot N$ | Dimensionless | Scaled (weak) speedup |
| **Energy-Delay Product** | $EDP = E \cdot T = P \cdot T^2$ | Joules · seconds | Lower is better; GPU clusters optimize this |
| **Communication Lower Bound** | $T_{\text{comm}} \ge \alpha + \dfrac{\beta}{B}$ | seconds | $\alpha$ = latency (s), $\beta$ = message size (bits), $B$ = bandwidth (bps) |
| **HBM Bandwidth** | $B_{\text{HBM}} = N_{\text{stacks}} \cdot 2 \cdot F_{\text{DDR}} \cdot W_{\text{IO}}$ | GB/s | $N_{\text{stacks}}$ = memory stacks, $F_{\text{DDR}}$ = data rate (GT/s), $W_{\text{IO}}$ = I/O width |
| **Cluster Aggregate FLOPS** | $P_{\text{cluster}} = P_{\text{node}} \cdot N_{\text{nodes}} \cdot \eta$ | FLOPS | $\eta$ = parallel efficiency, $0 \le \eta \le 1$ |

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

GPU clusters are not academic curiosities — they are the **backbone of modern AI infrastructure**:

1. **Large Language Model Training:** GPT-4-scale models require **~25,000 A100 GPUs** running for ~3 months. Without a properly engineered cluster (InfiniBand + NVLink + parallel filesystem), this is infeasible.
2. **Climate & Weather Simulation:** The ECMWF IFS weather model runs on GPU clusters to produce 10-day forecasts in under 1 hour.
3. **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD):** ANSYS Fluent and OpenFOAM GPU ports enable 10–50× speedups for turbine and aerodynamics simulation.
4. **Drug Discovery:** AlphaFold 2 inference and molecular dynamics (GROMACS) exploit GPU clusters to fold proteins in hours instead of months.
5. **Autonomous Vehicle Training:** Self-driving companies (Waymo, Tesla) train perception models on **10,000+ GPU clusters**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The derivations below are exhaustive — each algebraic step is justified. This is the level of rigor the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE expects for full 7-mark sub-questions.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Roofline Model Bound for a GPU Kernel

**Problem Statement:**
A GPU kernel performs $N_{\text{FLOP}}$ floating-point operations and requires $N_{\text{byte}}$ bytes of DRAM traffic on a GPU with peak compute $P_{\text{peak}} = 150$ TFLOPS and peak HBM bandwidth $B_{\text{HBM}} = 3$ TB/s. Derive the achievable performance and determine the bottleneck regime.

**Step 1 — Define Arithmetic Intensity (AI).**
The arithmetic intensity $AI$ of a kernel is the number of FLOPs performed per byte of memory traffic.

$$
AI = \dfrac{N_{\text{FLOP}}}{N_{\text{byte}}} \quad \text{[FLOPs/byte]}
$$

**Step 2 — Memory-Bound Performance Upper Limit.**
The GPU cannot run faster than the time it takes to fetch the data. If the kernel performs $AI$ FLOPs per byte, and the bandwidth is $B_{\text{HBM}}$, then the achievable performance $P_{\text{mem}}$ is bounded by:

$$
P_{\text{mem}} = B_{\text{HBM}} \cdot AI
$$

**Step 3 — Compute-Bound Performance Upper Limit.**
The GPU also cannot exceed its peak hardware throughput $P_{\text{peak}}$.

$$
P_{\text{comp}} = P_{\text{peak}}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine via the Min-Operation (Roofline).**
The actual achievable performance is the minimum of the two ceilings:

$$
P_{\text{achievable}} = \min(P_{\text{peak}},\; B_{\text{HBM}} \cdot AI)
$$

This piecewise-linear function is the **Roofline**.

**Step 5 — Find the Crossover Point (Machine Balance).**
Set the two expressions equal to find the ridge point $AI^*$:

$$
P_{\text{peak}} = B_{\text{HBM}} \cdot AI^*
$$

$$
AI^* = \dfrac{P_{\text{peak}}}{B_{\text{HBM}}} = \dfrac{150 \times 10^{12}\; \text{FLOPS}}{3 \times 10^{12}\; \text{B/s}} = 50 \;\; \text{FLOPs/byte}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Example.**
Suppose our kernel has $AI = 8$ FLOPs/byte (typical of a memory-bound element-wise op like ReLU).

$$
P_{\text{mem}} = (3 \times 10^{12}) \cdot 8 = 24 \times 10^{12}\; \text{FLOPS} = 24\; \text{TFLOPS}
$$

Since $24 < 150$:

$$
P_{\text{achievable}} = 24\; \text{TFLOPS} \quad \text{(memory-bound)}
$$

The kernel achieves only **16% of peak**. **Engineering lesson:** optimize memory access patterns before adding more compute.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cluster Parallel Efficiency & Amdahl's Limit

**Problem Statement:**
A GPU cluster has $N = 64$ nodes. Each node has a sequential boot, data loading, and result-saving phase taking $T_s = 5$ s per node, plus a parallel compute phase of $T_p = 100$ s distributed across nodes. Derive (a) the strong-scaling speedup, (b) the parallel efficiency $\eta$, and (c) the maximum useful $N$ if $T_p$ must remain at least 4× $T_s$.

**Step 1 — Identify the Serial and Parallel Fractions.**
Total wall-clock time per node (single-node baseline):

$$
T_1 = T_s + T_p = 5 + 100 = 105\; \text{s}
$$

Serial fraction (common across all $N$ nodes, executed once globally on the slowest node):

$$
f_s = \dfrac{T_s}{T_1} = \dfrac{5}{105} \approx 0.0476
$$

Parallel fraction:

$$
f_p = 1 - f_s = \dfrac{T_p}{T_1} = \dfrac{100}{105} \approx 0.9524
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Amdahl's Law (Strong Scaling).**

$$
S(N) = \dfrac{T_1}{T_s + \dfrac{T_p}{N}}
$$

Plugging in for $N = 64$:

$$
S(64) = \dfrac{105}{5 + \dfrac{100}{64}} = \dfrac{105}{5 + 1.5625} = \dfrac{105}{6.5625} = 16.0
$$

**Step 3 — Parallel Efficiency.**

$$
\eta(N) = \dfrac{S(N)}{N} = \dfrac{16.0}{64} = 0.25 = 25\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall:** A naïve student might claim "64 nodes ⇒ 64× speedup." The serial $T_s$ dominates once $T_p$ shrinks below it. Here, even adding nodes beyond $N \approx 20$ gives almost no benefit.

**Step 4 — Maximum Useful $N$ Constraint.**
We require $T_p / N \ge 4 \cdot T_s$ (parallel phase dominates):

$$
\dfrac{100}{N} \ge 20 \implies N \le 5
$$

> Surprisingly, beyond **5 nodes**, the serial boot phase starts to dominate. **Engineering lesson:** optimize $T_s$ (use parallel filesystem preload, container pre-warming) before scaling out.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — NVLink vs PCIe Bandwidth Utilization in Multi-GPU Training

**Problem Statement:**
A 4-GPU node must synchronize gradients during AllReduce. Each GPU holds a gradient tensor of size $D = 1$ GB. PCIe Gen5 practical bandwidth is $B_{\text{PCIe}} = 28$ GB/s; NVLink via NVSwitch achieves $B_{\text{NVL}} = 450$ GB/s per GPU. Compute the AllReduce time for each case, assuming bandwidth-limited communication.

**Step 1 — AllReduce Communication Volume.**
For $K$ GPUs, the AllReduce algorithm (ring or tree) requires each GPU to send and receive approximately:

$$
V_{\text{AR}} = \dfrac{2(K-1)}{K} \cdot D \;\; \text{bytes per GPU}
$$

For $K = 4$, $D = 1$ GB:

$$
V_{\text{AR}} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 3}{4} \cdot 1 = 1.5\; \text{GB per GPU}
$$

**Step 2 — Time over PCIe.**

$$
T_{\text{PCIe}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{AR}}}{B_{\text{PCIe}}} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^9}{28 \times 10^9} \approx 0.0536\; \text{s} = 53.6\; \text{ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Time over NVLink.**

$$
T_{\text{NVL}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{AR}}}{B_{\text{NVL}}} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^9}{450 \times 10^9} \approx 3.33 \times 10^{-3}\; \text{s} = 3.33\; \text{ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Speedup Factor.**

$$
\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{T_{\text{PCIe}}}{T_{\text{NVL}}} = \dfrac{53.6}{3.33} \approx 16.1\times
$$

> A **16× speedup** in communication alone explains why modern AI training infrastructure (DGX H100, HGX H100) refuses to ship without NVSwitch.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Component | CPU Cluster Counterpart | GPU Cluster Component | Performance Lever |
|---|---|---|---|
| Compute Unit | Multi-core CPU (16–128) | Many-core GPU (10,000+) | SIMT parallelism |
| Memory | DDR4/DDR5 RAM | HBM3 / GDDR6X | 10–30× bandwidth |
| Node Interconnect | Ethernet / 10 GbE | InfiniBand HDR/NDR | 100× lower latency |
| Intra-Node Interconnect | QPI / Infinity Fabric | NVLink + NVSwitch | 15× bandwidth vs PCIe |
| File System | NFS | Lustre / GPFS + Burst Buffers | 1000× throughput |
| Scheduler | SLURM | SLURM + GPU-aware plugins | GPU job packing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams below render the **functional architecture** and **component interaction flows** of a GPU cluster. All node IDs follow the alphanumeric-prefix rule; all labels use clean uppercase text.

---

### 4.1 Top-Level GPU Cluster Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    user1[USER / SCIENTIST]
    login1[LOGIN NODE]
    head1[HEAD NODE SLURM CONTROLLER]
    sched1[SCHEDULER DAEMON]
    rackA1[COMPUTE RACK A]
    rackB1[COMPUTE RACK B]
    rackC1[COMPUTE RACK C]
    stor1[PARALLEL FILE SYSTEM LUSTRE]
    mds1[METADATA SERVERS]
    oss1[OBJECT STORAGE SERVERS]
    bb1[BURST BUFFER NVME POOL]

    user1 --> login1
    login1 --> head1
    head1 --> sched1
    sched1 --> rackA1
    sched1 --> rackB1
    sched1 --> rackC1
    rackA1 -. reads/writes .-> stor1
    rackB1 -. reads/writes .-> stor1
    rackC1 -. reads/writes .-> stor1
    stor1 --> mds1
    stor1 --> oss1
    stor1 --> bb1
```

---

### 4.2 Internal Compute Node Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cpu1[CPU 1 XEON HOST]
    cpu2[CPU 2 XEON HOST]
    mem1[HOST RAM DDR5 1 TB]
    pcie1[PCIe Gen5 SWITCH]
    nvsw1[NVSWITCH CHIP]
    gpu1[GPU 0 H100 80 GB]
    gpu2[GPU 1 H100 80 GB]
    gpu3[GPU 2 H100 80 GB]
    gpu4[GPU 3 H100 80 GB]
    nvme1[LOCAL NVMe SSD 4 TB]
    ib1[INFINIBAND HCA ADAPTER]

    cpu1 --- mem1
    cpu2 --- mem1
    cpu1 --> pcie1
    cpu2 --> pcie1
    pcie1 --> nvsw1
    nvsw1 --> gpu1
    nvsw1 --> gpu2
    nvsw1 --> gpu3
    nvsw1 --> gpu4
    nvsw1 --> ib1
    pcie1 --> nvme1
```

---

### 4.3 GPU Internal Microarchitecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    hostIf[HOST INTERFACE PCIe NVLINK]
    l2c1[L2 CACHE 50 MB]
    mc1[MEMORY CONTROLLER HBM3]
    hbm1[HBM3 STACK 80 GB]
    sm1[SM 0]
    sm2[SM 1]
    sm3[SM N]
    warp1[WARP SCHEDULER]
    cuda1[CUDA CORES FP32]
    tens1[TENSOR CORES FP16 BF16]
    sfu1[SFU SPECIAL FUNCTIONS]
    shm1[SHARED MEMORY 128 KB]
    reg1[REGISTER FILE 256 KB]

    hostIf --> l2c1
    l2c1 --> mc1
    mc1 --> hbm1
    l2c1 --> sm1
    l2c1 --> sm2
    l2c1 --> sm3
    sm1 --> warp1
    warp1 --> cuda1
    warp1 --> tens1
    warp1 --> sfu1
    sm1 --> shm1
    sm1 --> reg1
```

---

### 4.4 Software Stack Flow (From User Job to Silicon)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    job1[USER PYTHON SCRIPT PYTORCH]
    mgr1[SLURM SBATCH SCRIPT]
    q1[JOB QUEUE]
    alloc1[RESOURCE ALLOCATION 4 NODES 16 GPUS]
    mpi1[MPI LAUNCHER MPMD or SPMD]
    nccl1[NCCL COLLECTIVE LIBRARY]
    cuda1[CUDA RUNTIME API]
    drv1[NVIDIA KERNEL DRIVER]
    gpu1[GPU HARDWARE SMs and HBM]

    job1 --> mgr1
    mgr1 --> q1
    q1 --> alloc1
    alloc1 --> mpi1
    mpi1 --> nccl1
    nccl1 --> cuda1
    cuda1 --> drv1
    drv1 --> gpu1
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]**
*List and briefly describe the four major hardware components of a GPU cluster.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The four major hardware components of a GPU cluster are:

1. **Compute Nodes:** Each node contains one or more multi-core CPUs (host processors) coupled with one or more GPUs (device accelerators). The CPU orchestrates control flow, MPI communication, and I/O, while the GPU executes data-parallel kernels.
2. **High-Speed Interconnect:** A low-latency, high-bandwidth network fabric — typically **InfiniBand HDR/NDR** or **100 GbE** — connects all nodes using a fat-tree or Dragonfly+ topology to provide non-blocking bisection bandwidth.
3. **GPU Device Architecture:** Each GPU contains multiple Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs), a hierarchy of memories (registers, shared memory, L1/L2 cache, HBM global memory), and specialized units such as Tensor Cores for matrix operations.
4. **Parallel Storage Subsystem:** A distributed parallel filesystem (e.g., **Lustre** or **GPFS**) with metadata servers, object storage servers, and burst buffers that sustain aggregate read/write bandwidth of TB/s to feed the GPUs.

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark per component; 0.5 mark for naming, 0.5 mark for a one-line description. Award 3 only if all four are correctly explained.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
*Explain the role of NVLink and NVSwitch in a multi-GPU node.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **NVLink** is a high-bandwidth, point-to-point interconnect developed by NVIDIA that directly links GPUs (and GPUs to CPUs) at speeds far exceeding PCIe. Each NVLink 4.0 link offers up to **~50 GB/s** per direction, and a single H100 GPU exposes **18 NVLink links** for an aggregate of **~900 GB/s** bidirectional bandwidth.
- **NVSwitch** is a silicon chip that acts as a non-blocking crossbar, aggregating multiple NVLink ports to create a fully connected **all-to-all GPU fabric** within a node (and across nodes in NVLink Switch System topologies).
- Together, NVLink + NVSwitch eliminate PCIe as the bottleneck for collective operations such as **AllReduce**, which dominates the communication time in distributed deep learning. They enable direct GPU-to-GPU memory accesses (e.g., via CUDA `cudaMemcpyPeer` or NCCL primitives) without staging through host RAM, drastically reducing communication latency and enabling near-linear scaling across 8 GPUs per node.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark NVLink definition + bandwidth, 1 mark NVSwitch role, 1 mark for the engineering impact on AllReduce/scaling.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme allows internal choice between two questions in each module. Both **Question A** and **Question B** below are designed to be independently selected.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Components and Interconnect Deep Dive**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a GPU cluster and explain the role of each component. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A GPU cluster is a hierarchical, heterogeneous parallel system. The architecture is layered as follows:

**1. Front-End Layer (Login & Head Nodes):** Users log into lightweight **login nodes** to compile code and submit jobs. The **head node** runs the SLURM/PBS scheduler, manages the job queue, and dispatches jobs to compute nodes. It does **not** execute compute workloads.

**2. Compute Node Layer:** Each compute node contains:
- **Host CPU(s):** Multi-core Intel Xeon or AMD EPYC processors that run MPI ranks, CUDA kernel launches, and I/O.
- **Host RAM:** DDR4/DDR5 memory (256 GB – 2 TB) for data staging and MPI buffers.
- **GPU(s):** 4–8 accelerators per node, connected via **NVLink/NVSwitch** internally and PCIe Gen5 to the CPUs.
- **Local NVMe:** Fast scratch storage for staging large datasets.

**3. Inter-Node Network:** A high-bandwidth, low-latency fabric — **InfiniBand HDR/NDR** — using fat-tree topology to provide non-blocking communication. Typical bandwidth: 200–400 Gbps per port.

**4. Parallel Storage:** A **Lustre / GPFS** filesystem distributes data across hundreds of object storage servers (OSS), with metadata servers (MDS) tracking file layouts. **Burst buffers** (NVMe arrays) absorb I/O spikes.

**5. Software Stack:** CUDA / ROCm programming models, MPI for inter-node communication, NCCL for GPU collectives, and frameworks like PyTorch or TensorFlow.

**Role Summary:** The front-end *orchestrates*, the compute nodes *execute*, the network *couples*, and the storage *feeds*. Each layer must be engineered so the GPUs are never idle.

*(Mark split: 2 marks for diagram, 5 marks for component roles.)*

---

**(b)** Compare PCIe Gen5 and NVLink interconnects with respect to bandwidth, latency, and use case in GPU clusters. Compute the AllReduce time for 4 GPUs each holding a 2 GB gradient tensor under each interconnect. PCIe effective bandwidth = **28 GB/s**; NVLink effective bandwidth = **450 GB/s**; AllReduce communication volume per GPU = $\frac{2(K-1)}{K} \cdot D$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | PCIe Gen5 | NVLink 4.0 (with NVSwitch) |
|---|---|---|
| Peak Bandwidth (bidir) | ~64 GB/s | ~900 GB/s |
| Effective Bandwidth | ~28 GB/s | ~450 GB/s |
| Latency | ~1–2 µs | ~1 µs (peer access) |
| Topology | CPU-rooted tree | Non-blocking all-to-all |
| Primary Use | CPU ↔ GPU transfers | GPU ↔ GPU, multi-GPU collectives |

NVLink is purpose-built for **GPU-to-GPU** peer traffic and is the only interconnect capable of saturating modern multi-GPU training workloads.

**Computation (4 Marks):**

For $K = 4$ GPUs, $D = 2$ GB:

$$
V_{\text{AR}} = \dfrac{2(4-1)}{4} \cdot 2 = \dfrac{6}{4} \cdot 2 = 3.0\; \text{GB per GPU}
$$

**Over PCIe:**

$$
T_{\text{PCIe}} = \dfrac{3.0\; \text{GB}}{28\; \text{GB/s}} = 0.1071\; \text{s} \approx 107.1\; \text{ms}
$$

**Over NVLink:**

$$
T_{\text{NVL}} = \dfrac{3.0\; \text{GB}}{450\; \text{GB/s}} = 6.67 \times 10^{-3}\; \text{s} \approx 6.67\; \text{ms}
$$

**Speedup:**

$$
\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{107.1}{6.67} \approx 16.1\times
$$

> **Engineering Takeaway:** In a training step that does AllReduce every iteration, switching from PCIe to NVLink can yield a 10–20% reduction in total training time — a multi-million-dollar saving at frontier scale.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Performance Modeling and Roofline**

**(a)** Derive the Roofline model bound for a GPU kernel. A GPU has peak compute $P_{\text{peak}} = 100$ TFLOPS (FP32) and HBM bandwidth $B = 1.5$ TB/s. A kernel has arithmetic intensity $AI = 20$ FLOPs/byte. Determine whether the kernel is compute-bound or memory-bound and compute its achievable performance. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (4 Marks):**

The achievable performance $P$ of a GPU kernel is bounded by both the peak compute throughput and the memory bandwidth:

$$
P \le \min(P_{\text{peak}},\; B \cdot AI)
$$

- **Memory-bound regime:** $P \le B \cdot AI$ when the kernel cannot fetch data fast enough.
- **Compute-bound regime:** $P \le P_{\text{peak}}$ when the kernel does more FLOPs per byte than the machine can deliver.

The **ridge point** (crossover) is:

$$
AI^* = \dfrac{P_{\text{peak}}}{B}
$$

**Numerical Computation (3 Marks):**

Ridge point:

$$
AI^* = \dfrac{100 \times 10^{12}}{1.5 \times 10^{12}} = 66.67\; \text{FLOPs/byte}
$$

Compare with given $AI = 20$ FLOPs/byte:

$$
20 < 66.67 \implies \text{memory-bound}
$$

Achievable performance:

$$
P_{\text{achievable}} = B \cdot AI = (1.5 \times 10^{12}) \cdot 20 = 30 \times 10^{12}\; \text{FLOPS} = 30\; \text{TFLOPS}
$$

Utilization:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{achievable}}}{P_{\text{peak}}} = \dfrac{30}{100} = 30\%
$$

> **Optimization Guidance:** Since the kernel is memory-bound, the student should recommend (a) increasing data reuse (e.g., tiling for matrix multiplication), (b) using shared memory to reduce HBM traffic, or (c) increasing batch size to amortize memory cost — *not* adding more FLOPs.

---

**(b)** Apply Amdahl's Law to a GPU cluster. A job has serial fraction $f_s = 0.05$ and parallel fraction $f_p = 0.95$. Compute the speedup for $N = 8, 64, 512$ GPUs and comment on scalability. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Amdahl's Law (3 Marks):**

$$
S(N) = \dfrac{1}{f_s + \dfrac{f_p}{N}} = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{N}}
$$

with $p = 0.95$, $1 - p = 0.05$.

**Numerical Computation (2 Marks):**

For $N = 8$:

$$
S(8) = \dfrac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{8}} = \dfrac{1}{0.05 + 0.11875} = \dfrac{1}{0.16875} \approx 5.93
$$

For $N = 64$:

$$
S(64) = \dfrac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{64}} = \dfrac{1}{0.05 + 0.01484} = \dfrac{1}{0.06484} \approx 15.42
$$

For $N = 512$:

$$
S(512) = \dfrac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{512}} = \dfrac{1}{0.05 + 0.001855} = \dfrac{1}{0.051855} \approx 19.28
$$

**Parallel Efficiency Table (2 Marks):**

| $N$ | $S(N)$ | $\eta = S(N)/N$ |
|---|---|---|
| 8 | 5.93 | 74.1% |
| 64 | 15.42 | 24.1% |
| 512 | 19.28 | 3.8% |

**Comment:** Beyond $N \approx 64$, doubling the cluster size yields less than a 2× speedup — the **serial 5%** caps the theoretical maximum speedup at $\dfrac{1}{0.05} = 20\times$. The cluster is **Amdahl-bound**: scaling out without reducing the serial fraction yields diminishing returns. To unlock further speedup, the serial portion (data loading, I/O orchestration, model initialization) must be re-engineered — this is the motivation behind **Gustafson's Law** (weak scaling), where the problem size is grown proportionally to $N$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently *compute Amdahl's Law correctly but forget to convert units*. Speedup is **dimensionless** — do not write "5.93 TFLOPS." Also, always state the **theoretical maximum speedup** ($1/f_s = 20\times$) explicitly — this is worth 1 mark and many students skip it.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for GPU Clusters — Components**

- ✅ A GPU cluster is a **two-tier heterogeneous parallel system**: MIMD across nodes, SIMT within GPUs.
- ✅ **Compute Node** = multi-core CPU + system RAM + GPU(s) + local NVMe SSD + InfiniBand HCA.
- ✅ **GPU Device** = Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs) + CUDA/Tensor Cores + Register File + Shared Memory + L2 Cache + HBM Global Memory.
- ✅ **Intra-Node Interconnect**: PCIe Gen5 (~28 GB/s effective) vs. **NVLink + NVSwitch** (~450 GB/s effective per GPU) — NVLink yields ~16× speedup for AllReduce.
- ✅ **Inter-Node Interconnect**: **InfiniBand HDR/NDR** dominates; latency 1–5 µs, bandwidth 200–400 Gbps; topology usually **fat-tree**.
- ✅ **Storage**: **Lustre / GPFS** parallel filesystems + **Burst Buffers** (NVMe) are essential to avoid GPU starvation.
- ✅ **Software Stack**: SLURM scheduler → MPI (inter-node) → NCCL (GPU collectives) → CUDA (kernel execution) → Driver → Hardware.
- ✅ **Roofline Model**: $P \le \min(P_{\text{peak}}, B \cdot AI)$; the ridge point is $AI^* = P_{\text{peak}}/B$.
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law**: Maximum speedup $= 1/f_s$; serial I/O and boot phases cap scalability.
- ✅ **GPU clusters win** on memory-bandwidth-bound and compute-throughput-bound workloads (matrix math, convolutions, FFTs, Monte Carlo).
- ✅ **Always state units**: bandwidth in **GB/s**, latency in **µs**, throughput in **FLOPS** (or TFLOPS/GFLOPS).
- ✅ KTU-typical keywords to use verbatim: *Streaming Multiprocessor, NVLink, InfiniBand, HBM, Roofline, Amdahl's Law, NCCL, fat-tree, burst buffer, Lustre, SLURM, PCIe Gen5, Tensor Core, CUDA Core, SIMT.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computer Clusters - Design Principles - Single System Image Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Computer Clusters - Design Principles - Single System Image Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Computer Cluster?

A **Computer Cluster** is a collection of standalone, interconnected computers (called **nodes**) that cooperate with each other and work together as a single, unified computing resource. Each node runs its own instance of an operating system, possesses its own local memory, local I/O devices, and is interconnected through a high-speed network fabric (LAN/SAN/Infiniband). From a programmer's or user's perspective, the entire set of nodes behaves as one logical computer.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A cluster is a type of parallel/distributed system that consists of a collection of interconnected stand-alone computers working together as a single, integrated computing resource. Clusters are characterized by multiple computers, multiple OS instances, a single system image (optional but desirable), and a unified job management system.

## 1.2 Cluster Design Principles — The Foundational Pillars

The design of any high-performance cluster is governed by four fundamental design principles. KTU examiners heavily test these as 3-mark short-answer items.

| Design Principle | Definition | Real-World Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Scalability** | Ability to increase throughput by adding more nodes. | HPC (Weather forecasting, Computational Fluid Dynamics). |
| **Availability** | System remains operational despite node/hardware failures. | Banking transaction servers, Google Search backends. |
| **Flexibility** | Accommodates heterogeneous hardware and software. | Research grids, mixed-architecture clouds. |
| **Performance** | Higher throughput & faster response time per unit cost. | Scientific simulations (CERN LHC data analysis). |

## 1.3 Single System Image (SSI) — Intuitive Definition

**Single System Image (SSI)** is a cluster design property that provides the illusion to the user that the entire collection of independent nodes is **one single, integrated computing system**, despite being physically composed of many autonomous computers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SSI is the "magic glue" of clustering.** Without SSI, every user would have to manually log into 100 different servers, run jobs on each one, copy files between them, and monitor 100 separate processes. SSI hides all this complexity.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen" 🍳

Imagine a giant restaurant kitchen:
- **Without SSI:** There are 10 separate mini-kitchens. To get your meal, you must walk to each kitchen, place an order separately, check if each is open, and combine your dishes yourself.
- **With SSI:** There is one grand, single kitchen. You place one order at one counter. Behind the scenes, 10 chefs (nodes) work in parallel, but to you, it is just **one kitchen**.

The restaurant's single counter = **Single Entry Point (SSI)**. The shared pantry accessible to all chefs = **Single File Hierarchy (SSI)**. One head chef coordinating all = **Single Control Point (SSI)**.

## 1.5 Why SSI is the Soul of Cluster Computing

A cluster without SSI is merely a "pile of connected computers." SSI transforms it into a true coherent computing engine. It delivers:

- **Transparency** — Hides the complexity of distribution from end-users.
- **Simplicity** — Single set of commands manages thousands of nodes.
- **Resource Aggregation** — CPUs, memory, and disks of all nodes pool together logically.
- **Fault Tolerance** — Failures become transparent via transparent failover.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard SSI Metric:** The degree of SSI is often quantified using **availability** (target ≥ **99.999% — the "five nines"**) and **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** measured in hours.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Layers of a Cluster

A cluster is built in **four logical layers**, and SSI is provided through middleware sitting between the OS and the application.

**Layer 1 — Hardware Layer:**
Commodity PCs/workstations (nodes), Network Interface Cards (NICs), switches, and interconnect (Ethernet/Myrinet/InfiniBand).

**Layer 2 — Operating System Layer:**
Each node runs its own OS (Linux, Windows Server). The cluster middleware layer hides this from users.

**Layer 3 — Middleware / SSI Layer:**
This is the heart of SSI. It provides services like single file hierarchy, process migration, load balancing, and checkpointing.

**Layer 4 — Application & User Layer:**
Parallel/distributed applications (MPI programs, Hadoop jobs) run here, unaware they are executing on multiple nodes.

## 2.2 The Nine Pillars of Single System Image (SSI Features)

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists **SSI features** as a key topic. Below are the **nine canonical SSI features** — each one is a frequent exam question.

### Pillar 1 — Single Entry Point
A single login node (head node) allows the user to log in once and gain access to the entire cluster. The user does **not** need to individually `ssh` into every compute node.

### Pillar 2 — Single User Interface
A unified command-line or graphical interface (e.g., **SLURM** `srun`, `sbatch`, or **PBS** `qsub`) treats the entire cluster as one machine. The user issues commands to "the cluster," not to individual nodes.

### Pillar 3 — Single File Hierarchy (SFH)
All local and remote file systems appear as **one rooted file tree** under `/`. A user can read `/home/user/data.txt` without knowing which physical node holds the disk. Implemented via **NFS, Lustre, GlusterFS, or Hadoop HDFS**.

### Pillar 4 — Single Control Point
A master node (called the **head node**, **front-end node**, or **cluster manager**) manages job scheduling, resource allocation, and node health monitoring. Tools: **SLURM, Torque, Kubernetes**.

### Pillar 5 — Single Job Management System (SJMS)
A global job scheduler queues, prioritizes, dispatches, monitors, and re-runs jobs across the cluster. The user submits one job; the scheduler decides which nodes to use.

### Pillar 6 — Single Memory Space (Distributed Shared Memory — DSM)
Processors of different nodes see a **logically shared address space**, even though physical RAM is distributed. Implemented via software DSM (e.g., TreadMarks) or hardware cache coherence (NUMA).

### Pillar 7 — Single Process Space
Processes can be created, scheduled, and migrated across nodes using global PIDs. A process running on node 3 can be checkpointed, killed, and resumed on node 17 transparently.

### Pillar 8 — Single I/O Space
Any node can perform I/O on any disk attached to any other node, appearing as a local operation. Implemented via clustered file systems and SAN.

### Pillar 9 — Checkpoint and Restart (C/R)
The state of a long-running parallel job is periodically saved (checkpointed) to shared storage. If a node fails, the job is **automatically restarted** from the last checkpoint — failure becomes invisible to the user.

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Tip for KTU:** Memorize these 9 features. Examiners love asking: *"Explain any FIVE features of Single System Image"* (7 marks) or *"Differentiate between SSI and non-SSI clusters"* (7 marks).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Cluster Performance Metrics

These equations govern cluster scalability and are frequently tested.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $S(n) = \dfrac{T(1)}{T(n)}$ | **Speedup** of *n* processors. | $T(1)$ = sequential time, $T(n)$ = parallel time. |
| 2 | $E(n) = \dfrac{S(n)}{n}$ | **Efficiency** (0 ≤ E ≤ 1). | n = number of nodes. |
| 3 | $S_{\text{Amdahl}} = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{n}}$ | **Amdahl's Law** for fixed problem. | $p$ = parallel fraction. |
| 4 | $f = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{S} - \dfrac{1}{n}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{n}}$ | **Karp-Flatt Metric** (serial fraction). | $f$ = inherently sequential fraction. |
| 5 | $S_{\text{Gustafson}} = n - (1-p)(n-1)$ | **Gustafson's Law** for scaled problems. | $p$ = parallel fraction. |
| 6 | $\text{Availability} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | **System Availability** (target ≈ 0.99999). | MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures, MTTR = Mean Time To Repair. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipeline Bubble Note:** Always convert percentages carefully. If 90% of code is parallel, then $p = 0.9$, NOT 90. Plugging $p = 90$ into Amdahl's law will cost you 7 marks on the exam.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

SSI-based clusters power:
- **Weather Forecasting (NCAR, ECMWF):** Tens of thousands of nodes deliver 7-day global forecasts.
- **Genetic Sequencing (BLAST):** Search across petabytes of genomic data.
- **Cryptocurrency Mining:** Single system image for thousands of GPUs.
- **Big Data Analytics (Apache Spark on YARN):** SSI-style resource pooling.
- **Defense Simulations (DRDO):** Real-time radar signature processing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Speedup and Efficiency from First Principles

**Problem Statement:** A cluster solves a workload in $T(1) = 200$ seconds on a single node. When run on $n = 8$ nodes, it takes $T(8) = 40$ seconds. Compute Speedup, Efficiency, and Karp-Flatt serial fraction.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Apply the Speedup formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(n) &= \frac{T(1)}{T(n)} \\
S(8) &= \frac{200}{40} \\
S(8) &= 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical interpretation:** The 8-node cluster runs the workload **5 times faster** than a single node. (Note: $S(8) < 8$ because of communication overhead.)

**Step 2:** Compute Efficiency.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(n) &= \frac{S(n)}{n} \\
E(8) &= \frac{5}{8} \\
E(8) &= 0.625 \quad \text{(or 62.5%)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical interpretation:** Each of the 8 nodes is being utilized only 62.5% of its capacity — the remaining 37.5% is wasted on overhead (network latency, synchronization, idle waiting).

**Step 3:** Derive Karp-Flatt serial fraction $f$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{\frac{1}{S} - \frac{1}{n}}{1 - \frac{1}{n}} \\
f &= \frac{\frac{1}{5} - \frac{1}{8}}{1 - \frac{1}{8}} \\
f &= \frac{0.2000 - 0.1250}{0.8750} \\
f &= \frac{0.0750}{0.8750} \\
f &= 0.0857
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical interpretation:** About **8.57%** of the program is inherently sequential (cannot be parallelized). This is a powerful diagnostic — it tells engineers *why* their cluster underperforms.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Amdahl's Law Asymptotic Speedup Limit

**Problem Statement:** 95% of a cluster program is parallelizable. Find the maximum achievable speedup as $n \to \infty$.

**Step 1:** Set $p = 0.95$, take the limit.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty} S(n) &= \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{(1-p) + \frac{p}{n}} \\
\lim_{n \to \infty} S(n) &= \frac{1}{(1-p) + 0} \\
\lim_{n \to \infty} S(n) &= \frac{1}{1-p} \\
\lim_{n \to \infty} S(n) &= \frac{1}{1 - 0.95} \\
\lim_{n \to \infty} S(n) &= \frac{1}{0.05} \\
\lim_{n \to \infty} S(n) &= 20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** No matter how many nodes are added, the speedup is **capped at 20×** because the sequential 5% becomes the bottleneck. This is the central message of Amdahl's Law for cluster design.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Karp-Flatt Diagnostic for Communication Overhead

**Problem Statement:** A cluster reports $S(64) = 15$. Compute $f$ and interpret.

**Step 1:** Apply Karp-Flatt formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{\frac{1}{15} - \frac{1}{64}}{1 - \frac{1}{64}} \\
f &= \frac{0.06667 - 0.01563}{0.98438} \\
f &= \frac{0.05104}{0.98438} \\
f &= 0.0519
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Interpretation matrix:

| Value of $f$ | Cluster Health Status | Engineering Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $f < 0.05$ | Excellent — code is highly parallelizable. | Scale up further. |
| $0.05 \leq f \leq 0.20$ | Moderate — some sequential bottleneck. | Profile for hotspots, optimize sync points. |
| $f > 0.20$ | Poor — heavy sequential or communication overhead. | Redesign algorithm; consider faster interconnect. |

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SSI vs Non-SSI Clusters

| Feature | Non-SSI Cluster (Bare Pile) | SSI Cluster (True Cluster) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| User Login | Must SSH to each node separately. | **Single login** at head node. |
| File Access | Each node has its own `/home`. | **Single file hierarchy** visible across cluster. |
| Process Management | `ps` shows only local processes. | **Single process space** across all nodes. |
| Job Submission | Manual execution on each node. | **Single job scheduler** (SLURM/PBS). |
| Memory View | Local RAM only. | **DSM** presents shared logical memory. |
| Failure Handling | User must restart jobs manually. | **Checkpoint/Restart** is automatic. |
| Ease of Use | Low — requires expert knowledge. | High — feels like one machine. |

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Availability for SSI Fault Tolerance

**Problem Statement:** A cluster node has MTBF = 5,000 hours, MTTR = 1 hour. Compute availability.

**Step 1:** Plug into availability formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \\
A &= \frac{5000}{5000 + 1} \\
A &= \frac{5000}{5001} \\
A &= 0.99980 \\
A &\approx 99.98\% \text{ uptime}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** For a 100-node SSI cluster (assuming independent failures), overall availability drops:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{cluster}} &= A^{100} = (0.99980)^{100} \\
A_{\text{cluster}} &\approx 0.98020 \approx 98.02\%
\end{aligned}
$$

This is why SSI's **Checkpoint/Restart** feature is critical — it preserves the user's job even as individual nodes fail.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SSI Cluster — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

This diagram maps the four-layer architecture and how SSI middleware stitches the cluster into a single logical machine.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    user[User Application MPI or Spark or Hadoop]
    middleware[Cluster Middleware SSI Layer]
    sched[Single Job Scheduler SLURM or PBS]
    fs[Single File Hierarchy NFS or Lustre or HDFS]
    proc[Single Process Space Global PID]
    io[Single I/O Space SAN or Clustered FS]
    chk[Checkpoint and Restart Service]
    head[Head Node Front End]
    n1[Compute Node 01]
    n2[Compute Node 02]
    n3[Compute Node 03]
    n4[Compute Node N]
    nicA[Network Interconnect Ethernet or InfiniBand]

    user -->|submit job| sched
    user -->|read write file| fs
    sched -->|dispatch| head
    fs --> proc
    proc --> io
    io --> chk
    head -->|ssh RPC| n1
    head -->|ssh RPC| n2
    head -->|ssh RPC| n3
    head -->|ssh RPC| n4
    n1 --> nicA
    n2 --> nicA
    n3 --> nicA
    n4 --> nicA
    nicA -->|heartbeat metrics| sched
    middleware -.wraps.-> sched
    middleware -.wraps.-> fs
    middleware -.wraps.-> proc
```

## 4.2 SSI Feature Hierarchy — Mapping the Nine Pillars

```mermaid

    ssi[Single System Image]

    p6[P6 Single Memory Space DSM]
    p7[P7 Single Process Space]
    p8[P8 Single I/O Space]
    p9[P9 Checkpoint Restart]

    ssi --> p1
    ssi --> p2
    ssi --> p3
    ssi --> p4
    ssi --> p5
    ssi --> p6
    ssi --> p7
    ssi --> p8
    ssi --> p9

    p3 --> nfs[NFS or GlusterFS]
    p5 --> slurm[SLURM or Torque]
    p6 --> dsm[TreadMarks or NUMA]
    p9 --> blcr[BLCR or DMTCP]
```

## 4.3 Sequential vs Parallel Execution Timeline (Amdahl's Law)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Sequential vs Parallel Execution on 4 Nodes
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s
    section Sequential Baseline
    Task A serial      :a1, 0, 10
    Task B parallel    :a2, 10, 18
    section Parallel Cluster
    Task A serial      :b1, 0, 4
    Task B part 1      :b2, 4, 7
    Task B part 2      :b3, 4, 7
    Task B part 3      :b4, 4, 7
    Task B part 4      :b5, 4, 7
```

**Interpretation:** The parallel cluster speeds up only the parallel portion. The sequential portion becomes the dominant cost — this is Amdahl's bottleneck visualized.

## 4.4 Cluster Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cluster[Computer Cluster]
    lo[Loosely Coupled Cluster Beowulf]
    ti[Tightly Coupled Cluster SMP Based]
    classic[Classic Cluster SSI via Middleware]
    specialized[Specialized Cluster HPC or HA or Load Balancing]

    cluster --> lo
    cluster --> ti
    cluster --> classic
    cluster --> specialized
    lo --> beo[Commodity PCs over Ethernet]
    ti --> smp[Shared Memory Hardware]
    classic --> ssi[Single System Image]
    specialized --> hpc[Scientific Computing]
    specialized --> ha[High Availability]
    specialized --> lb[Load Balancing]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`

**Q: Define a Computer Cluster. List any FOUR design principles of a cluster.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Computer Cluster** is a collection of interconnected stand-alone computers (nodes) working cooperatively as a single integrated computing resource. The nodes communicate through a high-speed network and present a unified interface to the user.

**Four Design Principles:**
1. **Scalability** — Ability to scale performance by adding nodes.
2. **Availability** — Continued operation despite component failures.
3. **Flexibility** — Support for heterogeneous hardware/software.
4. **Performance** — High throughput and reduced execution time.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary]`

**Q: What is Single System Image (SSI)? Mention any THREE features.**

**Model Answer:**

**SSI** is a property of a cluster that makes a collection of independent nodes appear as a **single, unified computing system** to the user.

**Three SSI Features:**
1. **Single Entry Point** — One login point for the entire cluster.
2. **Single File Hierarchy** — All files accessible via one unified directory tree.
3. **Checkpoint and Restart** — Transparent recovery from node failures.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Slot A]`

**Q: Explain the design principles of computer clusters in detail. Discuss Amdahl's Law with its significance in cluster scalability. Compute the maximum speedup for $p = 0.90$ when the number of processors $n$ approaches infinity.**

#### Part (a) — Design Principles of Clusters (7 Marks)

A computer cluster's design revolves around four key principles:

**1. Scalability:**
The cluster must allow incremental addition of nodes to linearly or near-linearly increase throughput. **Scalability** is measured using Speedup $S(n) = T(1)/T(n)$ and Efficiency $E(n) = S(n)/n$. A perfectly scalable cluster has $E \to 1$.

**2. Availability:**
A cluster must continue functioning despite individual node failures. This is measured by **Availability $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$**, with the industry target being **99.999% ("five nines")** for mission-critical clusters.

**3. Flexibility:**
The cluster should support heterogeneous hardware (different CPU architectures) and allow mixing of OS versions. This is essential in research environments.

**4. Performance:**
Clusters must deliver higher performance-per-cost than monolithic SMP (Symmetric Multiprocessor) systems. They achieve this by using commodity hardware and standard interconnects.

#### Part (b) — Amdahl's Law Derivation and Computation (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** State the law. The total execution time on a single processor is $T(1)$. A fraction $p$ can be parallelized across $n$ nodes, and the remaining $(1-p)$ is sequential.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= (1-p) \cdot T(1) + \frac{p \cdot T(1)}{n} \\
S(n) &= \frac{T(1)}{T(n)} = \frac{1}{(1-p) + \frac{p}{n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Take the limit as $n \to \infty$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty} S(n) &= \frac{1}{(1-p) + 0} = \frac{1}{1-p}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $p = 0.90$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty} S(n) &= \frac{1}{1 - 0.90} = \frac{1}{0.10} = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Even with infinite nodes, the speedup cannot exceed **10×** because 10% of the workload remains sequential. This proves that **node count alone does not guarantee better performance** — algorithmic efficiency and serial fraction minimization matter.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Slot B - Alternative Choice]`

**Q: What is Single System Image (SSI)? Explain ANY SEVEN features of SSI in detail with examples.**

#### Part (a) — Definition and Concept of SSI (7 Marks)

**Definition:** SSI is a critical middleware layer in a cluster that **hides the distributed nature** of the underlying hardware and presents a **single, coherent, unified computing environment** to the user and application programs.

Without SSI, the user must manually manage 100s of nodes. With SSI, the cluster behaves as one computer.

**Architectural Position:** SSI resides in the **middleware layer** between the local operating systems and the user applications, providing transparency across distributed resources.

#### Part (b) — Seven SSI Features Explained (7 Marks — 1 mark each)

**1. Single Entry Point:** Users log in once at the head node and gain transparent access to all cluster nodes. Example: Logging into `cluster.university.edu`.

**2. Single User Interface:** A unified command set (e.g., `srun`, `sbatch` in SLURM) treats the cluster as one machine. Example: `sbatch job.sh` submits to the entire cluster.

**3. Single File Hierarchy (SFH):** Files on any node appear under one rooted tree. Example: `/home/user/data.txt` is accessible regardless of which physical node stores it. Implemented via **NFS, Lustre, HDFS**.

**4. Single Control Point:** One master node (head node) administers the cluster — scheduling, monitoring, and failure handling. Example: SLURM controller.

**5. Single Job Management System (SJMS):** A central scheduler queues and dispatches jobs. Example: Torque, PBS Pro, SLURM.

**6. Single Process Space:** Global process IDs (PIDs) span all nodes. A user can view `ps -ef` across all nodes from one command. Example: Distributed process management.

**7. Checkpoint and Restart (C/R):** Long-running jobs are periodically saved; on node failure, they resume from the last checkpoint. Example: BLCR (Berkeley Lab Checkpoint/Restart) library.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Loss of 2 marks** is common when students confuse **Single Process Space** with **Single Memory Space (DSM)**. They are different — process space is about PIDs; memory space is about RAM addressability.
> - **Loss of 3 marks** occurs when students list SSI features but fail to give an **example or implementation** (e.g., naming NFS for SFH). Always cite a real tool/system.
> - **Loss of 2 marks** for not drawing a block diagram of the cluster showing SSI's position in the middleware layer. Always include one — even a simple one earns 2 free marks.
> - Amdahl's Law problems: forgetting to write $p$ as a decimal (e.g., using $p = 90$ instead of $p = 0.90$ in $1/(1-p)$) leads to a wildly wrong answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **Cluster** = many independent nodes + middleware = one logical supercomputer.
- ✅ Four **design principles**: Scalability, Availability, Flexibility, Performance.
- ✅ **SSI** = cluster appears as a **single machine** to users and applications.
- ✅ **Nine SSI features** (must remember for 7-mark questions): Single Entry Point, Single User Interface, Single File Hierarchy, Single Control Point, Single Job Management System, Single Memory Space (DSM), Single Process Space, Single I/O Space, Checkpoint & Restart.
- ✅ **Speedup** $S(n) = T(1)/T(n)$. **Efficiency** $E(n) = S(n)/n$.
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law** $S = 1/[(1-p) + p/n]$. Upper bound as $n \to \infty$ is $1/(1-p)$.
- ✅ **Karp-Flatt metric** $f = (1/S - 1/n)/(1 - 1/n)$ diagnoses whether performance loss is from sequential code or communication overhead.
- ✅ **Gustafson's Law** $S = n - (1-p)(n-1)$ — for scaled problems where data grows with $n$.
- ✅ **Availability target** for HA clusters: **99.999% (five nines)**.
- ✅ **Middleware tools** to know: SLURM, Torque, PBS Pro (schedulers); NFS, Lustre, HDFS (file hierarchy); BLCR, DMTCP (checkpointing); TreadMarks (DSM).
- ✅ Cluster types: **Beowulf** (commodity Linux PCs), **SMP-based**, **SSI-enhanced**, **HPC/HA/Load-Balancing specialized**.
- ✅ Always convert percentages to decimals in formulas: $p = 90\% \Rightarrow p = 0.90$.
- ✅ Always provide a **diagram** of the cluster architecture in long-answer questions — it earns easy marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: High Availability through Redundancy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** High Availability through Redundancy # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **High Availability (HA)** is a quality-of-service discipline in computing systems engineering that ensures a system, service, or component remains operationally accessible and functional for a pre-defined, statistically near-continuous percentage of time, typically measured against scheduled and unscheduled downtime.
