The KTU 2024 Scheme defines High Availability in the context of distributed systems and mission-critical infrastructure as the architectural goal of minimizing service interruption through **fault tolerance**, **failover mechanisms**, and **redundant subsystems**. **Redundancy** is the deliberate duplication of critical system components, functions, or data paths such that, in the event of a primary element's failure, one or more backup elements (either passive, active, or hybrid) can seamlessly assume the workload without perceptible degradation of service to the end user. Redundancy is the principal *engineering mechanism* by which High Availability is achieved. Mathematically, system **Availability (A)** is defined as: $$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$ where **MTBF** = **Mean Time Between Failures** and **MTTR** = **Mean Time To Repair**. Availability is expressed as a probability in the closed interval [0, 1] or equivalently as a percentage (often called the "nines" metric). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECMT632, Module 2):** High Availability through Redundancy is assessed under the course outcome *CO2: Apply techniques for fault tolerance and reliability in modern computing systems*. Candidates are expected to compare active vs. passive redundancy, derive system-level availability expressions, and analyze the trade-off between cost and the "nines" of availability. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition Imagine you are driving from Kottayam to Thiruvananthapuram on NH-66 — a single coastal highway vulnerable to landslides and traffic congestion. The trip is risky because *one path* must carry the entire load. Now suppose Keral PWD constructs a parallel bypass highway (e.g., the proposed outer ring road). If NH-66 is blocked, traffic automatically re-routes through the bypass. You, the driver, may not even realize a failure occurred. The bypass is **redundancy**, and the seamless continuation of your journey is **high availability**. In computing: - The **primary server** = NH-66 (the main data path). - The **standby/backup server** = the bypass highway. - The **failover controller / load balancer** = the traffic diversion system. - The **user's uninterrupted experience** = high availability. A single computer has one power supply, one network interface, one cooling fan — these are *single points of failure (SPOFs)*. High-availability engineering identifies SPOFs and provides *at least one alternate path* so that no single component defect can collapse the entire service. > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Summary:** High Availability means "the system is almost never down." Redundancy means "we have spare parts that automatically take over when something breaks." Together, they guarantee near-continuous operation in banking, healthcare, telecom, and cloud data centers. ## 1.3 Why Redundancy Is the Engineer's Response to Failure A fundamental axiom of reliability engineering is **Murphy's Law applied to silicon**: *if a component can fail, given enough time, it will*. Even with the highest-quality hardware, large-scale distributed systems contain *billions* of components. The probability of *all* components working simultaneously for an entire year is, in practice, zero. Hence, instead of trying to build perfect components (an economic and physical impossibility), engineers build **tolerant systems** that contain expected failures and *mask* them from the user through redundancy. The three principal engineering metrics in this context are: - **Reliability (R(t))** — probability that a system performs its function from time $0$ to $t$. - **Availability (A)** — long-run fraction of time the system is *up* and *ready to serve*. - **Maintainability (M(t))** — probability that a failed system is restored within time $t$. Redundancy improves all three, but its primary leverage is on **availability** when repairs can be deferred or distributed. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Concept Decomposed High availability through redundancy operates on a layered hierarchy. Understanding each layer is critical for KTU's analytical questions. ### Layer 1 — Component Redundancy (Hardware Level) Critical components (power supplies, fans, NICs, disks) are duplicated. If one fails, the other continues. Modern enterprise servers have *hot-swappable* redundant PSUs that can be replaced without shutting the system down. ### Layer 2 — Information Redundancy (Data Level) Bits are protected using error-correcting codes (ECC memory, RAID for disks). This corrects transient bit-flips and tolerates whole-disk failures. **RAID-1 (mirroring)** stores identical data on two disks; **RAID-5 (striping with parity)** distributes parity so any single disk can fail and the array still serves correct data. ### Layer 3 — Functional / Module Redundancy Entire services (e.g., a database engine, a load balancer) are replicated. This is the most common form in cloud systems. A user request can be routed to any healthy replica. ### Layer 4 — Site / Geographic Redundancy Entire data centers are replicated across geographical regions. Used for disaster recovery (DR). A flood in Mumbai does not affect a site in Chennai. Active-Active multi-region deployments deliver the highest "nines" of availability. ## 2.2 Taxonomies of Redundancy ### A. Active vs. Passive Redundancy - **Active Redundancy (Hot Standby):** All redundant components are *simultaneously powered and operational*. They share the load. Failure detection triggers a routing change. Examples: dual-ported disks, redundant routers running HSRP/VRRP. - **Passive Redundancy (Cold/Warm Standby):** The backup unit is powered off (cold) or partially initialized (warm). On failure, it boots and takes over. Cold standby has high MTTR; warm standby is intermediate. ### B. Homogeneous vs. Heterogeneous - **Homogeneous (N-Version):** Identical replicas — simpler, but susceptible to common-mode software bugs. - **Heterogeneous (Diverse):** Different vendors or implementations — defeats common-mode failures, used in avionics and nuclear control. ### C. Spatial vs. Temporal Redundancy - **Spatial:** Multiple physical copies operating concurrently. - **Temporal:** The same operation is repeated in time (e.g., retransmissions in TCP). When retransmission succeeds, it is a temporal redundancy action. ## 2.3 The Failover Sequence (Stepwise) 1. **Monitoring:** A *heartbeat* signal is exchanged between primary and secondary at sub-second intervals. 2. **Failure Detection:** Absence of $k$ consecutive heartbeats (typically $k=3$) declares the primary "suspect." 3. **Quorum Arbitration:** To avoid *split-brain* (both replicas believing they are primary), a tie-breaker (shared disk, third-party arbiter, or quorum majority) decides ownership. 4. **Promotion:** The standby is promoted to active; virtual IPs (VIPs) are reassigned via ARP/GARP. 5. **Catch-up:** The new primary rebuilds state from logs, replicas, or shared storage. 6. **Notification:** Operators are alerted; automated tickets are filed. ## 2.4 The "Nines" Table — Cost vs. Availability | Availability Tier | "Nines" Notation | Annual Downtime (theoretical) | Typical Use-Case | |---|---|---|---| | **2 nines** | 99% | ~3.65 days/year | Internal dev tools | | **3 nines** | 99.9% | ~8.77 hours/year | Email, internal SaaS | | **4 nines** | 99.99% | ~52.6 minutes/year | E-commerce, banking | | **5 nines** | 99.999% | ~5.26 minutes/year | Telecom, stock exchange | | **6 nines** | 99.9999% | ~31.5 seconds/year | Aerospace, military | > [!NOTE] > Each additional "nine" is roughly an *order of magnitude* more expensive. Going from 99.9% to 99.99% does **not** mean 0.1% better service — it means the system must be **ten times** more reliable. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Symbol / Expression | Meaning | Domain of Validity | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Steady-state availability of a single repairable system | MTBF $\gg$ MTTR, exponential failure | Dimensionless ratio in [0, 1] | | $A_{\text{series}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | Availability of $n$ components in series | Independent failures | All must be up for system up | | $A_{\text{parallel, non-rep}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - A_i)$ | Parallel system with non-repairable units | Independent, identical OR heterogeneous | Used in reliability block diagrams | | $A_{\text{parallel, rep}} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$ per unit, with $A_{\text{sys}} = 1 - \left(\frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}\right)^n$ for $n$ identical parallel repairable units | Availability of a redundant repairable system | Components have failure rate $\lambda$, repair rate $\mu$ | The classical Markov result | | $\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | Failure rate | Constant failure rate (exponential) | Failures per hour | | $\mu = \frac{1}{\text{MTTR}}$ | Repair rate | Constant repair rate | Repairs per hour | | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability function | Memoryless / exponential distribution | Probability of no failure in [0, t] | | $\text{Downtime}_{\text{year}} = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$ | Annual downtime in minutes | Steady-state | Used for SLA computation | | $\text{MTBF}_{\text{parallel}} = \int_0^\infty R_{\text{sys}}(t)\, dt$ | MTBF of $n$-unit parallel system | Non-repairable parallel | Used in unrepairable contexts | | $k \text{-out-of-}n$ redundancy | System works if at least $k$ of $n$ units work | Independent, identical | Used in modular redundancy like 2oo3 voting | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** When answering parallel-availability questions, clearly state the assumption (repairable vs. non-repairable, identical vs. non-identical). Marks are routinely deducted for omitting this. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Stock exchanges (NSE, NYSE):** Operate at 5+ nines; a 30-minute outage translates to multi-crore losses. - **Cloud providers (AWS, Azure, GCP):** S3 and Azure Blob Storage advertise 99.9–99.99% SLAs backed by massive erasure-coded geographic redundancy. - **Aerospace (Boeing 787, Airbus A350):** Use *Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR)* with majority voting — three identical flight computers vote, and disagreement triggers diagnostic routines. - **Healthcare (MRI / CT scanners):** Use N+1 redundant chillers and UPS systems to avoid a 12-hour rescheduling nightmare for patients. - **Telecom (4G/5G core):** Use geo-redundant session management functions to support seamless handovers. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Steady-State Availability from the Renewal-Reward Argument We consider a single repairable component. Let the system alternate between two states: **UP** (operational) and **DOWN** (failed, awaiting repair). The up-time per cycle is a random variable with mean $\text{MTBF}$, and the down-time per cycle has mean $\text{MTTR}$. The **steady-state availability** $A$ is defined as the long-run fraction of time the system is UP. Over $N$ renewal cycles, the total time spent UP is approximately $N \cdot \text{MTBF}$, and the total elapsed time is approximately $N \cdot (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$. Hence: $$A = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{N \cdot \text{MTBF}}{N \cdot (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})}$$ Cancelling $N$: $$\boxed{\,A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}\,}$$ Substituting $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$ and $\text{MTTR} = 1/\mu$: $$A = \frac{1/\lambda}{1/\lambda + 1/\mu} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$$ This shows that **reducing MTTR** is often cheaper than improving MTBF — which is why *hot spares* and *automated failover* are preferred over trying to build perfect hardware. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Availability of Two Repairable Units in Parallel We model two identical units, each with failure rate $\lambda$ and repair rate $\mu$. Define the *system state* $S_i$ = number of failed units. The possible states are $S_0$ (both up), $S_1$ (one failed, one up), $S_2$ (both failed). A single repairman handles failed units with rate $\mu$. The system is **UP** if $\geq 1$ unit is operational, i.e., in $S_0$ or $S_1$. It is DOWN only in $S_2$. The continuous-time Markov chain has transition rates: - $S_0 \to S_1$: any of 2 units fails, rate $2\lambda$. - $S_1 \to S_0$: the failed unit is repaired, rate $\mu$. - $S_1 \to S_2$: the remaining up unit fails, rate $\lambda$. - $S_2 \to S_1$: one of the two failed units is repaired, rate $2\mu$. Let $P_0, P_1, P_2$ be steady-state probabilities. The balance equations are: $$2\lambda P_0 = \mu P_1$$ $$\lambda P_1 = 2\mu P_2$$ $$P_0 + P_1 + P_2 = 1$$ From the first equation: $$P_1 = \frac{2\lambda}{\mu} P_0$$ Substituting into the second: $$\lambda \cdot \frac{2\lambda}{\mu} P_0 = 2\mu P_2 \implies P_2 = \frac{\lambda^2}{\mu^2} P_0$$ Normalizing: $$P_0 \left(1 + \frac{2\lambda}{\mu} + \frac{\lambda^2}{\mu^2}\right) = 1$$ Let $\rho = \lambda/\mu$. Then: $$P_0 = \frac{1}{(1 + \rho)^2}, \quad P_1 = \frac{2\rho}{(1+\rho)^2}, \quad P_2 = \frac{\rho^2}{(1+\rho)^2}$$ System availability (UP) is $A_{\text{sys}} = P_0 + P_1$: $$A_{\text{sys}} = 1 - P_2 = 1 - \left(\frac{\rho}{1+\rho}\right)^2$$ Substituting $\rho = \lambda/\mu$: $$\boxed{\,A_{\text{sys}} = 1 - \left(\frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}\right)^2\,}$$ ## 3.3 Generalization to $n$ Identical Repairable Units in Parallel By induction on the Markov chain, the probability that **all $n$** units are failed is: $$P_{\text{all-failed}} = \left(\frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}\right)^n$$ Therefore, the **system availability** is: $$\boxed{\,A_{\text{sys}}^{(n)} = 1 - \left(\frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}\right)^n = 1 - (1 - A_1)^n\,}$$ where $A_1 = \mu/(\lambda + \mu)$ is the availability of a single unit. This is the **single most important equation** for KTU redundancy problems. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) > **Problem:** A web server has $\text{MTBF} = 1000$ hours and $\text{MTTR} = 10$ hours. Find (a) its individual availability, (b) the system availability when 3 such servers are placed in parallel, and (c) the expected annual downtime of the 3-server cluster. ### Solution **Step (a) — Single server availability:** $$A_1 = \frac{1000}{1000 + 10} = \frac{1000}{1010} \approx 0.990099 \approx 99.01\%$$ **Step (b) — 3-server parallel system availability:** Using the parallel-availability formula: $$A_{\text{sys}} = 1 - (1 - A_1)^3 = 1 - (1 - 0.990099)^3$$ $$A_{\text{sys}} = 1 - (0.009901)^3 = 1 - 9.706 \times 10^{-7}$$ $$A_{\text{sys}} \approx 0.9999990294 \approx 99.9999\%$$ This is **6 nines** of availability — extraordinary for a single $A_1$ of only 99%. **Step (c) — Annual downtime in minutes:** $$\text{Downtime} = (1 - A_{\text{sys}}) \times 525600 \text{ min/year}$$ $$\text{Downtime} = 9.706 \times 10^{-7} \times 525600 \approx 0.51 \text{ min/year}$$ That is about **30 seconds per year** of expected total outage. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Redundancy vs. Cost | Redundancy Strategy | Failure Tolerance | MTTR Impact | Cost Multiplier | Typical Deployment | |---|---|---|---|---| | No redundancy (Simplex) | 0 | Baseline (high) | 1× | Lab, dev | | Cold standby | 1 | Very high (boot time) | ~1.5× | Backup DC | | Warm standby | 1 | Moderate | ~2× | Mid-tier DB | | Hot standby (active-passive) | 1 | Low (seconds) | ~2.5× | Enterprise servers | | Active-Active (N-way) | up to $N-1$ | Near-zero (seamless) | ~3–4× | Web tier, Kubernetes | | N+1 modular | 1 module | Low | ~1.3× per module | Power, cooling | | 2oo3 voting (TMR) | 1 (with disagreement detection) | Self-healing | ~3× per channel | Flight control | | Geographic Active-Active | Region-wide | Near-zero | ~5–10× | Cloud, banking | ## 3.6 KTU Examiner's Heuristic on Parallel-Component Trade-offs When asked "which redundancy is best for the KTU 2024 question?", follow this decision logic: 1. **If MTBF is large and MTTR is small** → a single unit may suffice; redundancy is wasteful. 2. **If downtime per incident is very expensive** (e.g., trading platform) → favor hot-standby or active-active despite cost. 3. **If MTTR is dominated by human action** (e.g., physical disk swap) → pre-emptive replacement or hot spares reduce MTTR more cheaply than extra replicas. 4. **If common-mode failure is plausible** (firmware bug, OS crash) → use *diverse* (heterogeneous) redundancy. 5. **If load is variable and high** → active-active is preferred (it also increases capacity, not just availability). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Architecture A — Active-Passive (Hot Standby) Failover ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph userPlane["User Request Plane"] client1[Client 1] client2[Client 2] client3[Client 3] end subgraph lbPlane["Load Balancer / VIP Plane"] vipAddr["Virtual IP 10.0.0.100"] end subgraph appPlane["Application Server Farm"] primary["Primary Server
ACTIVE STATE
Heartbeat TX 1Hz"] secondary["Secondary Server
PASSIVE STANDBY
Heartbeat RX"] end subgraph arbPlane["Arbitration / Quorum Plane"] arbiter["Quorum Witness
Split Brain Tie Breaker"] end subgraph storagePlane["Shared Storage Plane"] san1["SAN / Shared Disk
State Synchronization"] end client1 --> vipAddr client2 --> vipAddr client3 --> vipAddr vipAddr --> primary primary -.->|"Heartbeat OK"| secondary primary -.->|"State Replication"| san1 secondary -.->|"Read Heartbeat"| primary primary -.->|"Lease Token"| arbiter secondary -.->|"Lease Token"| arbiter secondary -->|"Failover Promotion
on Missed Heartbeat"| vipAddr san1 -->|"Mount on Promotion"| secondary ``` > [!NOTE] > The **primary** handles all traffic and continuously leases a token from the **arbiter**. The **secondary** monitors the heartbeat. On three consecutive missed heartbeats, the secondary requests the lease from the arbiter; if granted, it promotes itself and assumes the Virtual IP. This prevents the *split-brain* anomaly where two servers simultaneously believe they are primary. ## 4.2 Architecture B — Active-Active Multi-Node Cluster ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ingress["North-South Ingress"] glb["Global Load Balancer
DNS or Anycast Based"] end subgraph clusterA["Cluster Region A - Active"] nodeA1["App Node A1
HEALTHY"] nodeA2["App Node A2
HEALTHY"] nodeA3["App Node A3
HEALTHY"] end subgraph clusterB["Cluster Region B - Active"] nodeB1["App Node B1
HEALTHY"] nodeB2["App Node B2
HEALTHY"] nodeB3["App Node B3
HEALTHY"] end subgraph health["Health Probe Subsystem"] prober["Active Health Checker
HTTP TCP ICMP"] end glb --> nodeA1 glb --> nodeA2 glb --> nodeA3 glb --> nodeB1 glb --> nodeB2 glb --> nodeB3 prober -.->|"Probe Every 2s"| nodeA1 prober -.->|"Probe Every 2s"| nodeA2 prober -.->|"Probe Every 2s"| nodeA3 prober -.->|"Probe Every 2s"| nodeB1 prober -.->|"Probe Every 2s"| nodeB2 prober -.->|"Probe Every 2s"| nodeB3 prober -->|"Mark Unhealthy
Remove from Pool"| glb nodeA1 <-.->|"Gossip Replication"| nodeB1 nodeA2 <-.->|"Gossip Replication"| nodeB2 nodeA3 <-.->|"Gossip Replication"| nodeB3 ``` ## 4.3 Architecture C — RAID Redundancy Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph raid0["RAID 0 - Striping - NO Redundancy"] d0a["Disk 0"] d0b["Disk 1"] end subgraph raid1["RAID 1 - Mirroring - Full Redundancy"] d1a["Disk 0 Primary"] d1b["Disk 0 Mirror"] end subgraph raid5["RAID 5 - Striping with Distributed Parity"] d5a["Disk 0 Data A"] d5b["Disk 1 Data B"] d5c["Disk 2 Parity A xor B"] d5d["Disk 3 Data C"] end subgraph raid6["RAID 6 - Dual Parity"] d6a["Disk 0 Data"] d6b["Disk 1 Data"] d6c["Disk 2 Parity P"] d6d["Disk 3 Parity Q"] end controller["RAID Controller
Stripe and Reconstruct"] --> raid0 controller --> raid1 controller --> raid5 controller --> raid6 ``` ## 4.4 Functional Flow — Redundancy Decision Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD start["System Request Arrives"] probe["Health Probe Active?"] primaryOK{"Primary Node
Responsive?"} routePrimary["Route to Primary
Log Event"] routeSecondary["Route to Secondary
Trigger Failover"] recordHealth["Record Heartbeat in
Distributed Log"] quorumCheck{"Quorum Token
Valid?"} promote["Secondary Promotes to Primary
Reassign VIP"] alert["Page On-Call Engineer
Open Incident"] completed["Request Served"] start --> probe probe --> primaryOK primaryOK -->|"Yes"| routePrimary primaryOK -->|"No"| routeSecondary routeSecondary --> quorumCheck quorumCheck -->|"Yes"| promote quorumCheck -->|"No"| alert promote --> completed routePrimary --> recordHealth recordHealth --> completed alert --> completed ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > The question bank below mirrors the **KTU 2024 End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** structure for an elective such as PECMT632. The module carries roughly 15–20% of the 100-mark paper, distributed as a Part-A 3-mark item and a Part-B 14-mark item. Sub-parts (a) and (b) are 7 marks each. --- ## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper] **Define the term "High Availability" in computing systems. Mention any two real-world services that demand five-nines (99.999%) availability.** **Model Answer:** High Availability (HA) is a system-design discipline that ensures a computing service remains operational and accessible for a statistically near-continuous fraction of time, typically ≥ 99.99%, by combining **redundant components**, **automated failover**, and **rapid recovery mechanisms**. The mathematical expression is $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$. Two real-world services demanding five-nines (annual downtime ≤ 5.26 minutes): 1. **Stock exchange trading platforms (NSE, NYSE)** — even a minute of downtime causes multi-crore financial loss. 2. **Telecom signaling core (5G AMF/SMF)** — call drops impact millions of subscribers; regulators impose heavy penalties. *(2 more lines acceptable: emergency response 911 systems, air traffic control.)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Specimen Paper] **Distinguish between *active* and *passive* redundancy with one example each.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Active Redundancy | Passive Redundancy | |---|---|---| | Power state of backup | Continuously powered and serving traffic | Idle (cold) or partially booted (warm) | | Detection of failure | Real-time load balancer | Heartbeat absence | | MTTR | Near-zero (instant reroute) | Higher (requires promotion) | | Example | Active-Active web cluster with L4 load balancer | Cold-standby disaster-recovery database | --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > KTU directive: candidates answer **either** Question A **or** Question B, each 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. --- ### QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern] #### (a) [7 Marks] Define the steady-state availability of a repairable system. A hard disk in a NAS has MTBF = 50,000 hours and MTTR = 25 hours. Compute its individual availability and the system availability when four such disks are configured in a parallel redundancy cluster. Also report the expected annual downtime in minutes for the cluster. **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Define availability:** Steady-state availability $A$ is the long-run fraction of time a repairable system is operational: $$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$ **Step 2 — Compute single-disk availability:** $$A_1 = \frac{50000}{50000 + 25} = \frac{50000}{50025} \approx 0.9995002 \approx 99.95\%$$ **Step 3 — Compute the 4-disk parallel system availability.** For $n$ identical repairable units in parallel, the system is DOWN only when *all* are DOWN. Since units are independent: $$A_{\text{sys}} = 1 - (1 - A_1)^n = 1 - (1 - 0.9995002)^4$$ $$A_{\text{sys}} = 1 - (4.98 \times 10^{-4})^4 = 1 - 6.16 \times 10^{-14} \approx 1 - 6.16 \times 10^{-14}$$ $$A_{\text{sys}} \approx 0.9999999999999384$$ This is **approximately 14 nines** — extraordinarily high. **Step 4 — Annual downtime in minutes:** $$\text{Downtime}_{\text{year}} = (1 - A_{\text{sys}}) \times 525600 = 6.16 \times 10^{-14} \times 525600$$ $$\text{Downtime}_{\text{year}} \approx 3.24 \times 10^{-8} \text{ minutes/year}$$ That is about **1.94 microseconds per year** of expected outage — practically zero. **Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:** The result demonstrates the *super-linear* leverage of redundancy. A single disk with 99.95% availability, when placed in a 4-way parallel cluster, achieves 14 nines of availability. This is the mathematical foundation of enterprise storage systems (e.g., NetApp, EMC) that use RAID-DP plus node-level mirroring. --- #### (b) [7 Marks] Explain with a neat block diagram the **Active-Passive (Hot Standby)** redundancy architecture. Discuss the role of the *quorum witness* in preventing *split-brain syndrome*. **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Define the architecture:** An active-passive (hot-standby) deployment consists of two (or more) servers. The *primary* node actively serves all user traffic via a Virtual IP (VIP). The *secondary* node is fully powered, synchronized, and continuously monitors the primary's heartbeat, but does not normally receive traffic. **Step 2 — Block diagram:** ``` Clients | v +---------------+ | Virtual IP | | 10.0.0.100 | +-------+-------+ | v +-----------+-----------+ | Primary Server |<----+ | (Active, Lease Owner) | | Heartbeat (1 Hz) +-----------+-----------+ | | v | +---------------+ +-------v------+ | Secondary | | Quorum |<-+ | Server | | Witness |<----+ (Hot Standby)| +--------------+ +-------+-------+ | | (on failover) v Promotes to Primary and Claims the VIP ``` **Step 3 — Failover sequence:** 1. Primary and Secondary exchange a 1-Hz heartbeat. 2. On 3 consecutive missed heartbeats (configurable), the Secondary declares the Primary failed. 3. The Secondary requests a *lease token* from the **Quorum Witness**. 4. If granted, the Secondary sends a gratuitous ARP (GARP) to claim the VIP, and the failover completes in seconds. **Step 4 — Role of the Quorum Witness:** The Quorum Witness is an independent third party (often a lightweight cloud VM, an external storage witness, or a tie-breaker disk) that grants a lease token to **only one** node at a time. Its role is to *prevent split-brain syndrome* — a dangerous state where both nodes believe they are primary and simultaneously write to shared storage, corrupting data. Without the witness, a network partition could lead to dual promotion, causing irrecoverable data divergence. **Step 5 — Why this matters for KTU:** The witness transforms failover from a *2-node problem* (which has a classic ambiguity) into a *3-node problem* (which has a clear majority). The KTU examiner expects students to mention *lease token*, *GRATUITOUS ARP*, and *shared storage consistency* as concrete mechanisms. --- ### QUESTION B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Specimen Paper] #### (a) [7 Marks] Compare and contrast **RAID-1 (mirroring)**, **RAID-5 (striping with single parity)**, and **RAID-6 (striping with dual parity)** from the perspective of redundancy, storage efficiency, and fault tolerance. **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Define the three RAID levels:** - **RAID-1 (Mirroring):** Every data block is written identically to two disks. Read performance can be doubled (one read from each mirror); write performance is the same as a single disk. Storage efficiency is 50% (two disks store one disk's worth of data). - **RAID-5 (Striping with Single Parity):** Data and parity are striped across $N \geq 3$ disks. The parity block is rotated across disks to avoid a single hot disk. Storage efficiency is $(N-1)/N$. Tolerates **one** disk failure; reconstruction reads surviving disks. - **RAID-6 (Striping with Dual Parity):** Two independent parity blocks (typically P and Q, where Q is a Reed-Solomon code) are stored. Tolerates **two** simultaneous disk failures. Storage efficiency is $(N-2)/N$. **Step 2 — Comparison Table:** | Feature | RAID-1 | RAID-5 | RAID-6 | |---|---|---|---| | Minimum disks | 2 | 3 | 4 | | Storage efficiency | 50% | $(N-1)/N$ | $(N-2)/N$ | | Fault tolerance | 1 disk | 1 disk | 2 disks | | Read performance | High (2-way) | High (parallel) | High | | Write performance | Single-disk speed | Penalty (4 reads + 2 writes per stripe) | Higher penalty | | Rebuild time | Fast | Slow (degraded during rebuild) | Slower (more data to recompute) | | Use case | OS disks, logs | Bulk storage, archives | Mission-critical storage | **Step 3 — Engineering trade-off discussion:** - RAID-1 provides the **fastest recovery** because the mirror is an exact copy — no parity calculation is needed. - RAID-5 is the **most cost-efficient** for large arrays; it is the historical default for archival and bulk data. - RAID-6 protects against the *double-disk failure* scenario, which is non-negligible in large-capacity drives (e.g., 18 TB SATA) where a 24-hour rebuild window increases the probability of a second failure during rebuild. **Step 4 — KTU-level closing remark:** Modern enterprise storage uses RAID-6 or RAID-DP (NetApp's variant) because the cost of dual parity is small compared to the cost of data loss during rebuild. Cloud object stores (S3, Azure Blob) often use *erasure coding* (analogous to generalized RAID-6) with 12+4 or 16+4 schemes for geo-distributed redundancy. --- #### (b) [7 Marks] A bank operates a payment-gateway service on three servers in active-active configuration. Each server has $\lambda = 0.001$ failures/hour and $\mu = 1$ repair/hour. Determine (i) the steady-state availability of a single server, (ii) the system availability for the 3-server cluster, and (iii) the expected number of failures per year. State your assumptions clearly. **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — State assumptions:** - Each server is an independent, identical, repairable unit. - Single repairman handles all three servers. - Failure rate $\lambda$ and repair rate $\mu$ are constant (memoryless). - Failures are independent; a system is UP if at least one server is operational. **Step 2 — Compute single-server availability:** $$A_1 = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{1}{0.001 + 1} = \frac{1}{1.001} \approx 0.999001 \approx 99.90\%$$ **Step 3 — Compute 3-server parallel availability:** $$A_{\text{sys}} = 1 - (1 - A_1)^3 = 1 - (0.000999)^3$$ $$A_{\text{sys}} = 1 - 9.97 \times 10^{-10} \approx 0.999999999003 \approx 99.9999999\%$$ This is approximately **9 nines** of availability. **Step 4 — Compute annual expected number of failures per server:** $$\text{Failures per year} = \lambda \times 8760 = 0.001 \times 8760 = 8.76 \text{ failures/year per server}$$ For 3 servers, the **expected number of failures across the cluster per year** is: $$N_{\text{failures, cluster}} = 3 \times 8.76 = 26.28 \text{ failures/year}$$ **Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:** The cluster experiences about 26 failures per year, but the *system* — due to redundancy — is down for only about $9.97 \times 10^{-10} \times 525600 \approx 5.24 \times 10^{-4}$ minutes/year, i.e., roughly **31 milliseconds per year**. This is why payment gateways can advertise >99.999% SLAs despite individual servers failing nearly once every two months. The lesson for the KTU examiner: *redundancy converts frequent component failures into negligible service downtime.* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Forgetting the assumption "independent failures."** If two servers share a common power supply, they are *not* independent, and the parallel-availability formula overstates the true availability. Always state this assumption. > 2. **Mixing up repairable vs. non-repairable formulas.** For non-repairable systems, the formula is $A_{\text{sys}} = 1 - \prod (1 - R_i(t))$. For repairable steady-state, the formula is $1 - (1 - A_i)^n$. KTU papers usually imply the *repairable* case unless stated otherwise. > 3. **Conflating MTBF and MTTF.** MTBF includes repair time for *repairable* systems; MTTF (mean time to failure) is for *non-repairable*. Using them interchangeably loses 1–2 marks. > 4. **Not reporting units.** Always state downtime in **minutes/year** (or seconds/year) — the examiner wants to see if you understand the magnitude. > 5. **Omitting the cost trade-off.** When asked "which redundancy to choose," KTU expects an *engineering justification*, not just a formula. Mention CAPEX, OPEX, MTTR impact, and common-mode failure risk. > 6. **Skipping the diagram.** A neat block diagram of an active-passive or active-active architecture is worth 2–3 marks on a 14-mark question. Practice drawing them before the exam. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **High Availability (HA)** = continuous service availability; mathematically $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$. - **Redundancy** = duplication of components/data/paths so failure of one is masked by another. - **Three core metrics:** MTBF (reliability), MTTR (maintainability), Availability (long-run uptime fraction). - **"Nines" of availability:** Each additional 9 reduces downtime by ~10× and increases cost by ~10×. - **Active redundancy** = all units powered and serving; failure is masked instantly (lowest MTTR). - **Passive redundancy** = backup is idle or partially booted; promotion takes seconds to minutes. - **Hot standby** = backup fully powered; **warm** = partially booted; **cold** = off until needed. - **Parallel availability (repairable, identical):** $A_{\text{sys}} = 1 - (1 - A_1)^n$ — the most tested KTU formula. - **Series availability:** $A_{\text{sys}} = \prod A_i$ — *all* must be up; weakest-link behaviour. - **RAID summary:** RAID-1 = mirror (50% efficiency, 1-fault tolerance); RAID-5 = single parity; RAID-6 = dual parity (2-fault tolerance). - **Active-Passive architecture** uses **heartbeat + quorum witness + VIP reassignment** to prevent split-brain. - **Active-Active architecture** = all nodes serve traffic; failure is masked by the load balancer; gives capacity *and* availability. - **Geographic redundancy** = replicas in different regions, protecting against site-wide disasters. - **Common-mode failure** = a single defect (firmware bug, shared power) causing all replicas to fail simultaneously — defeated by *diverse/heterogeneous* redundancy. - **Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) / 2oo3 voting** is the aerospace standard for fault tolerance with self-diagnosis. - **Cost vs. availability trade-off** is the central design question; redundancy is *cheap insurance* against downtime but never free. - **SLAs** (Service Level Agreements) of cloud providers are direct quotes of this availability mathematics.

topics.title: Fault Tolerant Cluster Configurations, Checkpoint and Recovery Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fault Tolerant Cluster Configurations, Checkpoint and Recovery Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fault Tolerance — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Fault Tolerance** is the ability of a computing system to continue performing its intended functions at a specified performance level (degraded or non-degraded) in the presence of hardware or software faults, without total shutdown or catastrophic data loss.

In the context of **cluster computing**, a *Fault Tolerant Cluster* is a loosely coupled group of independent nodes (workstations, PCs, or servers) interconnected through a high-speed network, coordinated via messaging, configured with **redundancy** such that the failure of one or more nodes does not interrupt the overall service offered to the clients.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Airline Co-Pilot"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a long-haul commercial aircraft. The captain flies the plane, but a **co-pilot** sits beside the captain, continuously monitoring every switch, dial, and decision. If the captain suddenly becomes incapacitated (fault), the co-pilot instantly takes over — the passengers (users) never even notice anything happened. A *Fault Tolerant Cluster* works exactly this way: a **primary node** handles active work, while a **secondary/standby node** mirrors state in real time, ready to take over within milliseconds.

| Term | Aircraft Analogy | Cluster Computing |
|---|---|---|
| Fault | Captain fainting | Node crash / link failure |
| Detection | Co-pilot watching gauges | Heartbeat monitoring |
| Redundancy | Co-pilot on board | Backup/standby node |
| Recovery | Co-pilot takes controls | Failover procedure |
| Checkpoint | Black-box recordings | Snapshot of state saved to disk |

### 1.3 Checkpoint and Recovery — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> A **Checkpoint** is a snapshot of the volatile state (memory contents, registers, open file descriptors, process control blocks) of a running computation, written periodically to **stable storage** so that the computation can be restarted from that saved point after a failure. **Recovery** is the process of restoring system state from the most recent consistent checkpoint and re-executing lost work.

### 1.4 Key Reliability Metrics (Industry Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics are universally used to grade fault-tolerant systems and are **guaranteed** to appear in KTU numerical/analytical questions.

- **MTTF** — Mean Time To Failure (system runs before failing)
- **MTTR** — Mean Time To Repair (downtime after a failure)
- **MTBF = MTTF + MTTR** — Mean Time Between Failures
- **Availability** $= \dfrac{MTTF}{MTBF} = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR}$

A canonical industry target is the **"Five Nines"** availability, i.e., **99.999%** uptime (≈ 5.26 minutes downtime per year).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Cluster Faults

A systematic classification of faults is necessary before we can engineer defenses against them:

1. **Transient Fault** — occurs once and disappears (e.g., momentary power glitch, alpha-particle bit flip). *Defense: retry, ECC memory.*
2. **Intermittent Fault** — recurs irregularly (e.g., loose connector, flaky memory cell). *Defense: diagnostic logging, replacement.*
3. **Permanent Fault** — persists until repair (e.g., burnt-out disk, failed CPU). *Defense: redundancy + failover.*

### 2.2 Fault Tolerant Cluster Configurations

Clusters achieve fault tolerance by **replicating** services across multiple nodes. The standard configurations graded in KTU are:

#### 2.2.1 Passive (Primary–Backup / Active–Passive)
- **One node** is active; the **other** is a hot/warm standby.
- Standby receives state updates but serves no client traffic.
- On failure of primary, the backup is **promoted** (failover).
- **Pro:** Simplicity. **Con:** Idle hardware, hence lower resource efficiency.

#### 2.2.2 Active–Active Replication
- **All nodes** actively process client requests simultaneously.
- State must be synchronized through a **distributed shared memory** or **group communication primitive**.
- **Pro:** Higher throughput and load balancing. **Con:** Complex consistency management (CAP theorem trade-offs).

#### 2.2.3 N+1 Redundancy
- **N** active nodes share the load; **1** extra node acts as the dedicated spare.
- If any of the N nodes fail, the spare takes over.
- Used in **mail servers, web clusters, distributed databases**.

#### 2.2.4 N+M Redundancy
- **M** spare nodes support **N** active nodes. M > 1 gives tolerance against multiple simultaneous failures.
- **Typical:** N+M = 2+1, 4+2, 8+2 configurations in commercial SAN-attached clusters.

#### 2.2.5 Quorum-Based Configuration
- Used in distributed consensus (Paxos, Raft, ZooKeeper).
- A cluster of 2F+1 nodes can tolerate **F simultaneous failures** while still reaching consensus.
- Requires a **majority quorum** $(\frac{n}{2}+1)$ to make decisions.

### 2.3 Checkpointing Strategies

> [!NOTE]
> A checkpoint strategy is a **policy** describing *when*, *what*, and *where* a process saves its state.

| Strategy | When Checkpointed | Coordination | Recovery Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Uncoordinated** | Each process decides locally, autonomously | None | High — domino effect risk |
| **Coordinated** | All processes checkpoint together via a global barrier | Full synchronization | Low — single global consistent line |
| **Communication-Induced (CIC)** | Implicitly triggered by message-passing patterns | Partial (uses piggybacked info) | Medium |
| **Incremental** | Only modified memory pages saved | May use any scheme | Variable |

### 2.4 Recovery Strategies

1. **Rollback Recovery** — process is rewound to the last checkpoint; lost work is re-executed. Cheapest approach.
2. **Roll-Forward Recovery** — state is reconstructed using logged non-deterministic events; less work is repeated.
3. **Message Logging (Pessimistic)** — every message is synchronously logged to stable storage before the sender proceeds. Safe but slow.
4. **Message Logging (Optimistic)** — messages are logged in volatile memory and flushed asynchronously. Faster but riskier.
5. **Application-Level Recovery** — application implements its own undo/redo logic (e.g., database transactions).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulae are the **only** numerical instruments the KTU paper-setter uses. Memorize them.

| # | Formula / Metric | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR}$ | Steady-state availability | Dimensionless (0 ≤ A ≤ 1) |
| 2 | $MTBF = MTTF + MTTR$ | Mean Time Between Failures | Hours |
| 3 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability function (exponential distribution) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTTF}$ | Failure rate | Failures/hour |
| 5 | $A_{series} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | Availability of components in series | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $A_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - A_i)$ | Availability of components in parallel | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $W_{opt} = \sqrt{M \cdot T_{c}}$ | Optimal checkpoint interval (Young's formula) | Seconds |
| 8 | $T_{total} = T_{ckpt} + \frac{T_{exec}}{W} + T_{r} \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_{exec}}{W \cdot MTTF}\right)$ | Total execution time with checkpointing | Seconds |
| 9 | $n_{quorum} = 2F + 1$ | Cluster size tolerating F failures | Nodes |
| 10 | $U_{down} = (1 - A) \cdot T_{period}$ | Unavailability window per period | Seconds/year |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** These formulas govern SLAs in cloud data centers (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud), telecom switches (Ericsson 5G core), spacecraft on-board computers, and stock-exchange matching engines. An engineer who masters them can compute ROI on redundant hardware purchases.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Optimal Checkpoint Interval (Young, 1974)

We derive the celebrated Young formula from first principles — a frequent KTU Part-B question.

**Assumptions:**
- Failures follow a **Poisson process** with constant rate $\lambda = \frac{1}{MTTF}$.
- Checkpoint overhead is constant $T_c$ per checkpoint.
- Recovery time from a checkpoint is $T_r$.
- Total work to be done has execution time $T_{exec}$.

**Step 1 — Number of checkpoints and segments.**
If the optimal interval between checkpoints is $W$ seconds, then the total work is divided into $K = \frac{T_{exec}}{W}$ equal segments. The number of checkpoints taken (one per segment) is also $K$.

$$
K = \frac{T_{exec}}{W}
$$

**Step 2 — Time spent creating checkpoints.**
Each checkpoint costs $T_c$, so total checkpointing overhead is:

$$
T_{ckpt,total} = K \cdot T_c = \frac{T_{exec} \cdot T_c}{W}
$$

**Step 3 — Expected number of failures.**
With exponential inter-failure time, the expected number of failures over a period $W$ is $\lambda W = \frac{W}{MTTF}$. Since failures can occur in any of the $K$ segments, the total expected failures is:

$$
E[N_{fail}] = K \cdot \frac{W}{MTTF} = \frac{T_{exec}}{W} \cdot \frac{W}{MTTF} = \frac{T_{exec}}{MTTF}
$$

**Step 4 — Expected re-execution time after failure.**
After a failure, on average half of the current segment is lost (worst case: beginning of segment). So expected re-execution per failure is $\frac{W}{2}$. Multiplied by recovery time $T_r$, the cost per failure is $\frac{W}{2} + T_r$.

**Step 5 — Total expected execution time.**

$$
T_{total} = T_{ckpt,total} + T_{exec} + E[N_{fail}] \cdot \left(\frac{W}{2} + T_r\right)
$$

$$
T_{total} = \frac{T_{exec} \cdot T_c}{W} + T_{exec} + \frac{T_{exec}}{MTTF} \cdot \left(\frac{W}{2} + T_r\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Differentiate with respect to W and set to zero.**

$$
\frac{dT_{total}}{dW} = -\frac{T_{exec} \cdot T_c}{W^2} + \frac{T_{exec}}{MTTF} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{T_{exec} \cdot T_c}{W^2} = \frac{T_{exec}}{2 \cdot MTTF}
$$

$$
W^2 = 2 \cdot T_c \cdot MTTF
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{opt} = \sqrt{2 \cdot T_c \cdot MTTF}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Some textbooks absorb the factor 2 into the constant, giving $W_{opt} = \sqrt{M \cdot T_c}$ where $M = MTTF$. Both forms are accepted in KTU evaluations.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Quorum Sizing

**Problem:** A Kubernetes control-plane cluster must tolerate **2 simultaneous node failures**. Determine the minimum cluster size and the quorum threshold.

**Step 1 — Apply the formula $n = 2F + 1$.**

$$
n = 2(2) + 1 = 5 \text{ nodes}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute quorum.**

$$
Q = \left\lfloor \frac{n}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor \frac{5}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3
$$

**Step 3 — Verify tolerance.**

If 2 of 5 nodes fail, 3 remain. A write operation requires 3 confirmations (the quorum). Since 3 ≥ 3, the cluster can still process writes. **Tolerance holds.**

### 3.3 Worked Example — Parallel Availability

**Problem:** Two servers, each with MTTF = 1000 hours and MTTR = 4 hours, are configured in an **active–passive** cluster. Compute steady-state availability.

**Step 1 — Availability of a single server.**

$$
A_{single} = \frac{1000}{1000 + 4} = \frac{1000}{1004} \approx 0.99602
$$

**Step 2 — Unavailability (downtime probability).**

$$
U_{single} = 1 - A_{single} = \frac{4}{1004} \approx 0.003984
$$

**Step 3 — For an active–passive cluster to fail, BOTH servers must be unavailable.**

$$
U_{cluster} = U_{single}^2 = (0.003984)^2 \approx 1.587 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{cluster} = 1 - 1.587 \times 10^{-5} \approx 0.999984}
$$

This is equivalent to **99.9984% uptime** — well above "four nines", approaching the "five nines" SLA.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing a Configuration

| Workload / SLA Need | Recommended Configuration | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Cost-sensitive, low traffic | Active–Passive (1+1) | Cheapest redundancy |
| Web-tier, elastic load | Active–Active | Maximizes throughput |
| Telecom-grade, zero downtime | N+M with M ≥ 2 | Tolerates concurrent maintenance |
| Strongly consistent state | Quorum (2F+1) | Survives network partitions |
| Long-running HPC job | Coordinated checkpointing | Single global cut simplifies recovery |
| Loosely-coupled microservices | Communication-induced checkpointing | Avoids global barriers |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Active–Passive Cluster Topology

```mermaid
graph LR
    classDef client fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A65A00,color:#000000
    classDef primary fill:#A0E7A0,stroke:#1F7A1F,color:#000000
    classDef standby fill:#C9C9FF,stroke:#2A2A8C,color:#000000
    classDef storage fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#8A6A00,color:#000000
    classDef monitor fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#8A0F0F,color:#000000

    nodeC1[CLIENT A]:::client
    nodeC2[CLIENT B]:::client
    nodeC3[CLIENT C]:::client

    nodeLB[LOAD BALANCER OR VIP]:::client

    nodeP[PRIMARY NODE]:::primary
    nodeS[STANDBY NODE]:::standby

    nodeSAN[SHARED STABLE STORAGE]:::storage

    nodeMON[HEARTBEAT MONITOR]:::monitor

    nodeC1 --> nodeLB
    nodeC2 --> nodeLB
    nodeC3 --> nodeLB

    nodeLB --> nodeP
    nodeMON -.-> nodeP
    nodeMON -.-> nodeS

    nodeP <--> nodeS
    nodeP <--> nodeSAN
    nodeS <--> nodeSAN
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **dashed** lines from `nodeMON` are *heartbeat probes*; if 3 consecutive heartbeats are missed, the monitor triggers **failover**, demoting the primary and promoting the standby.

### 4.2 N+M Redundancy with Spare Pool

```mermaid
graph TB
    classDef active fill:#9CD3A6,stroke:#1F7A1F,color:#000000
    classDef spare fill:#FFC8A2,stroke:#8A4A00,color:#000000
    classDef ctrl fill:#B6C9FF,stroke:#1F2A6E,color:#000000

    ctrl1[CLUSTER CONTROLLER]:::ctrl

    a1[ACTIVE NODE 1]:::active
    a2[ACTIVE NODE 2]:::active
    a3[ACTIVE NODE 3]:::active
    a4[ACTIVE NODE 4]:::active

    s1[SPARE NODE M1]:::spare
    s2[SPARE NODE M2]:::spare

    ctrl1 --> a1
    ctrl1 --> a2
    ctrl1 --> a3
    ctrl1 --> a4
    ctrl1 -. monitors .-> s1
    ctrl1 -. monitors .-> s2
```

### 4.3 Checkpoint–Recovery Lifecycle (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef normal fill:#D4F4DD,stroke:#1F7A1F,color:#000000
    classDef ckpt fill:#FFE7A6,stroke:#8A6A00,color:#000000
    classDef fail fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#8A0F0F,color:#000000
    classDef rec fill:#BBD3FF,stroke:#1F3A8A,color:#000000
    classDef done fill:#C8C8C8,stroke:#404040,color:#000000

    st1[STATE 1: NORMAL EXECUTION]:::normal
    st2[STATE 2: TRIGGER CHECKPOINT EVENT]:::ckpt
    st3[STATE 3: SUSPEND PROCESSES]:::ckpt
    st4[STATE 4: WRITE SNAPSHOT TO STABLE STORAGE]:::ckpt
    st5[STATE 5: RESUME EXECUTION]:::normal

    st6[STATE 6: FAULT DETECTED]:::fail
    st7[STATE 7: IDENTIFY LATEST CONSISTENT CHECKPOINT]:::rec
    st8[STATE 8: ROLLBACK PROCESSES]:::rec
    st9[STATE 9: REPLAY MESSAGES FROM LOG]:::rec
    st10[STATE 10: RESUME FROM CHECKPOINT]:::normal
    st11[STATE 11: COMPLETE OR TERMINATE]:::done

    st1 --> st2 --> st3 --> st4 --> st5 --> st1
    st1 --> st6 --> st7 --> st8 --> st9 --> st10 --> st1
    st1 --> st11
```

### 4.4 Quorum Consensus — 2F+1 Cluster

```mermaid
graph LR
    classDef leader fill:#A0E7A0,stroke:#1F7A1F,color:#000000
    classDef follow fill:#C9C9FF,stroke:#2A2A8C,color:#000000
    classDef fail fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#8A0F0F,color:#000000

    n1[NODE 1 LEADER]:::leader
    n2[NODE 2 FOLLOWER]:::follow
    n3[NODE 3 FOLLOWER]:::follow
    n4[NODE 4 FAILED]:::fail
    n5[NODE 5 FOLLOWER]:::follow

    n1 <--> n2
    n1 <--> n3
    n1 <--> n5
    n2 <--> n3
    n3 <--> n5
```

> [!NOTE]
> With 1 of 5 nodes failed, the remaining 4 still exceed the quorum threshold of 3. The cluster continues to serve both reads and writes — illustrating the **2F+1 rule** with F=2 maximum tolerated failures.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define *checkpoint* and *rollback recovery*. State any two advantages of coordinated checkpointing over uncoordinated checkpointing.

**Model Answer:**

A **checkpoint** is a snapshot of a process's volatile state (memory, registers, PC, open files) written to stable (non-volatile) storage. **Rollback recovery** is the mechanism that restarts the process from the most recent consistent checkpoint after a failure, re-executing any work lost since that checkpoint.

**Advantages of coordinated checkpointing:**

1. **Simpler recovery** — all processes share a single global consistent cut, so there is no need to search for a consistent recovery line. This eliminates the **domino effect**.
2. **No garbage accumulation** — obsolete checkpoints can be discarded safely, reducing storage overhead.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition each + 1 mark for advantages)*

---

#### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Distinguish between *Active–Active* and *Active–Passive* cluster configurations. Give one real-world use case of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Active–Passive | Active–Active |
|---|---|---|
| Active nodes | 1 | All |
| Standby role | Yes (hot/warm spare) | No |
| Resource utilization | Low | High |
| Failover time | Tens of seconds | Sub-second (load balancer reroute) |
| Complexity | Simple | Complex (state sync) |
| Use case | Database HA (Oracle RAC standby, PostgreSQL streaming replication) | Web front-ends, CDN edge nodes, Kubernetes ReplicaSets |

*(3 marks: 2 marks for table + 1 mark for use case)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU ESE regulations, candidates answer **one** of two internal choices. Both Question A and Question B are provided below, each with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks.

---

#### **QUESTION A** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of an Active–Passive fault-tolerant cluster** and describe the **failover procedure** step-by-step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture:** A standard Active–Passive cluster contains:

1. **Primary node** — handles all client traffic.
2. **Standby (backup) node** — receives continuous state updates; remains idle.
3. **Shared stable storage** (SAN/NAS) — holds application data so both nodes see identical files.
4. **Heartbeat link** — dedicated low-latency network (often a crossover cable) carrying periodic "I am alive" messages.
5. **Cluster management software** — monitors health and orchestrates failover.

**Failover Procedure (step-by-step):**

- **Step 1.** Primary node stops sending heartbeats (crash, hang, or network partition).
- **Step 2.** Standby node, after missing **N consecutive heartbeats** (N typically 3, ≈ 3–10 s), declares the primary failed.
- **Step 3.** Standby takes over the **virtual IP address** (VIP) via gratuitous ARP, so clients transparently reconnect.
- **Step 4.** Standby **mounts shared storage** and starts the application services.
- **Step 5.** Clients re-establish sessions — the entire outage window is typically **5–30 seconds**.
- **Step 6.** Once the original primary is repaired, an optional **failback** (or *reverse failover*) restores the original topology.

*(Diagram included — refer to Section 4.1; 7 marks: 3 for diagram + 4 for procedure)*

**(b)** A web server has MTTF = 2000 hours and MTTR = 2 hours. Compute (i) its steady-state availability, (ii) the expected downtime per year, and (iii) the new availability if a second identical server is added in **parallel (active-active)** configuration. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Single-server availability:**

$$
A = \frac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} = \frac{2000}{2000 + 2} = \frac{2000}{2002} \approx 0.999001
$$

**(ii) Expected downtime per year:**

A year = 365 × 24 = **8760 hours**.

$$
T_{down} = (1 - A) \cdot 8760 = 0.000999 \cdot 8760 \approx 8.75 \text{ hours/year}
$$

**(iii) Two servers in parallel (active–active):**

Unavailability of single server:

$$
U = 1 - A = 0.000999
$$

For a parallel system, both must be unavailable for the cluster to fail:

$$
U_{cluster} = U^2 = (0.000999)^2 \approx 9.98 \times 10^{-7}
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{cluster} = 1 - 9.98 \times 10^{-7} \approx 0.999999}
$$

**Downtime per year:**

$$
T_{down,cluster} = 9.98 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 8760 \approx 0.00874 \text{ hours/year} \approx 31.5 \text{ seconds/year}
$$

This is **three orders of magnitude better** and meets the "five nines" SLA.

*(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 3 distribution)*

---

#### **QUESTION B** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain **coordinated**, **uncoordinated**, and **communication-induced** checkpointing strategies. Compare their advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Strategy | Mechanism | Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Coordinated** | All participating processes pause at a global barrier; each writes its local state; then they all resume together | Simple recovery; no domino effect; garbage collection easy | Synchronization overhead; not scalable; blocking I/O stalls |
| **Uncoordinated** | Each process checkpoints independently whenever convenient | Maximum autonomy; no global coordination; scalable | Domino effect possible; recovery search is expensive; garbage accumulates |
| **Communication-Induced (CIC)** | Checkpoints are triggered by message-pattern rules (e.g., Z-cycle prevention piggybacked on every message) | Avoids global barrier; scales better than coordinated; prevents useless checkpoints | More complex protocol; piggyback overhead on every message; still semi-coordinated |

**Recovery implication:** In coordinated schemes, the most recent set of checkpoints forms a single global consistent cut — recovery is direct. In uncoordinated schemes, the system may need to **cascade-rollback** through multiple generations (domino effect). CIC strikes a middle ground by ensuring that at least one useful checkpoint exists in every causal chain.

*(7 marks: 4 marks for explanation + 3 marks for comparison table)*

**(b)** Derive the **optimal checkpoint interval** formula. Given MTTF = 500 hours and checkpoint overhead $T_c = 60$ seconds, compute $W_{opt}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Young formula derivation is given in **Section 3.1**. Key final expression:

$$
\boxed{W_{opt} = \sqrt{2 \cdot T_c \cdot MTTF}}
$$

**Numerical computation:**

Convert MTTF to seconds for unit consistency:

$$
MTTF = 500 \text{ hours} = 500 \times 3600 = 1{,}800{,}000 \text{ seconds}
$$

Apply the formula:

$$
W_{opt} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 60 \cdot 1{,}800{,}000}
$$

$$
W_{opt} = \sqrt{216{,}000{,}000}
$$

$$
W_{opt} = \sqrt{2.16 \times 10^{8}}
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{opt} \approx 14{,}696.9 \text{ seconds} \approx 4.08 \text{ hours}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The long-running job should checkpoint approximately every 4 hours. Taking checkpoints more frequently wastes time on overhead; less frequently risks losing large amounts of work upon failure.

*(7 marks: 4 marks for derivation + 3 marks for numerical answer)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Reasons for Mark Deduction**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in $W_{opt}$** — mixing hours and seconds in the same equation. Always convert MTTF to **seconds** when $T_c$ is in seconds.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor 2** in the Young formula — some texts drop it; KTU expects the full $\sqrt{2 T_c MTTF}$. State the form you are using.
> 3. **Quorum miscalculation** — students often write $n = F + 1$ instead of the correct $n = 2F + 1$.
> 4. **Confusing MTTR and MTBF** — MTBF is a *sum*, not a synonym for MTTR.
> 5. **Skipping the recovery line justification** in uncoordinated checkpointing answers — the **domino effect** must be explicitly named.
> 6. **Not drawing the diagram** for 7-mark architecture questions — diagrams are worth **at least 2–3 marks** in KTU Part-B.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision list — memorize before entering the exam hall.**

- [ ] **Fault tolerance** = continued correct service in the presence of faults.
- [ ] Three fault types: **transient, intermittent, permanent**.
- [ ] **MTTF + MTTR = MTBF**.
- [ ] **Availability** $A = \frac{MTTF}{MTBF}$ — pure ratio, dimensionless.
- [ ] **Series availability** = product of individual $A_i$.
- [ ] **Parallel availability** = $1 - \prod(1 - A_i)$.
- [ ] **"Five nines"** = 99.999% ≈ 5.26 min downtime/year.
- [ ] **Active–Passive** = 1 primary + 1 standby; VIP-based failover.
- [ ] **Active–Active** = all nodes serve traffic; needs state synchronization.
- [ ] **N+M** redundancy = N active + M spares.
- [ ] **Quorum** = $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor + 1$; tolerate F failures with $n = 2F+1$ nodes.
- [ ] **Coordinated checkpoint** = global barrier; no domino effect; recovery is direct.
- [ ] **Uncoordinated checkpoint** = autonomous; risk of **domino effect**.
- [ ] **Communication-induced checkpoint** = message-pattern-driven; piggybacked info.
- [ ] **Young's formula**: $W_{opt} = \sqrt{2 \cdot T_c \cdot MTTF}$ (with factor 2).
- [ ] **Rollback recovery** = restart from last checkpoint; re-execute lost work.
- [ ] **Roll-forward recovery** = use logged events; less re-execution.
- [ ] **Pessimistic logging** = synchronous to stable storage (safe, slow).
- [ ] **Optimistic logging** = volatile buffer + async flush (fast, risky).
- [ ] **Heartbeat failure threshold** = typically 3 missed beats trigger failover.
- [ ] **Stable storage** = SAN/NAS; must survive the failure being recovered from.
- [ ] Always state **units** in numerical answers (hours, seconds, or years).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cluster Job and Resource Management: Job Scheduling Methods, Job Management System - Administration, Job Types, Migration Schemes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cluster Job and Resource Management: Job Scheduling Methods, Job Management System - Administration, Job Types, Migration Schemes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cluster Job and Resource Management — The Formal Definition

**Cluster Job and Resource Management (CJRM)** is the *system-level framework* (software + policies) responsible for **accepting, queueing, scheduling, dispatching, monitoring, and accounting** for computational jobs submitted to a High-Performance Computing (HPC) cluster, while simultaneously managing the **finite, shared physical resources** (CPU cores, RAM, GPUs, storage, network bandwidth) that execute those jobs.

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically decomposes CJRM into **four interlocking sub-domains**:

| Sub-Domain | Core Responsibility |
|---|---|
| **Job Scheduling Methods** | Decides *which* job runs *when* and *on which* nodes. |
| **Job Management System (JMS) Administration** | Configures queues, users, policies, accounting, and priorities. |
| **Job Types** | Classifies the *shape* of work (batch, parallel, interactive, array). |
| **Migration Schemes** | Allows live or suspended relocation of jobs across nodes for load balancing or fault tolerance. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECMT632 – Module 2):**
> The board examiner expects you to **connect scheduling policy to job type** (e.g., *Gang Scheduling* is correct for *tightly-coupled parallel jobs* but wasteful for *serial jobs*). Memorizing a single algorithm without context will cost marks.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Airport Air-Traffic Control" View

Imagine a **single airport** with a limited number of **runways (CPU cores)**, **gates (RAM)**, and **fuel trucks (I/O bandwidth)**. Hundreds of **airplanes (jobs)** of different sizes — from small private jets (serial jobs) to giant A380s (parallel/MPI jobs) — want to land every minute.

- The **Job Scheduling Method** is the *Air Traffic Controller's algorithm*: does it land the *first plane that requested* (FCFS), the *smallest plane first* (SJF), or the one with the *highest priority flight number* (Priority Scheduling)?
- The **JMS Administration** is the *airport's rulebook* and the *manager who wrote it*: how many planes can be in the holding pattern, which airlines get preference, what is the maximum runway time.
- **Job Types** describe the *aircraft class*: a fighter jet needs 2 runways simultaneously (gang scheduling), a cargo plane can wait overnight (batch), a medical flight needs the runway now (interactive/real-time).
- **Migration Schemes** are the protocols to *tow a plane from one gate to another, or even a plane already in the air to a different airport*, without crashing it (process migration / checkpoint-restart).

This analogy makes the rest of the notes immediately familiar.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> Master these **bold terms** before proceeding — every formula, diagram, and exam answer in later sections depends on them.

- **Job** — A complete unit of work submitted by a user (script + resource request + constraints).
- **Task** — A sub-component of a job. A job with $N$ tasks may run on $N$ distinct nodes.
- **Slot** — A single execution unit (typically 1 CPU core + associated RAM fraction).
- **Node** — A physical machine in the cluster.
- **Queue / Class** — A logical holding pen where jobs wait based on policy (e.g., `batch`, `interactive`, `highprio`).
- **Scheduler** — The decision-making engine (e.g., SLURM, Torque, LSF, Maui).
- **Resource Manager** — The execution engine that physically launches, monitors, and kills jobs.
- **Migration** — Relocation of an already-running job (or its state) to a different node.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering

In real industry (CERN, meteorological agencies, pharmaceutical drug discovery, AI training farms like Meta's GPU clusters), the CJRM is the **single most important software layer** that determines:

- **Utilization** — typical HPC clusters waste **30–50%** of compute hours if scheduling is poor.
- **Mean Time To Completion (MTTC)** — the user's wait time.
- **System Throughput** — jobs finished per day.
- **Energy Efficiency (PUE)** — a wrongly-migrated MPI job can lose **10–25%** of its performance to network re-routing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quantitative Anchor:** Modern schedulers like **SLURM** manage exascale systems (e.g., Frontier at ORNL with **9,408 nodes and 37,632 MI250X GPUs**). The scheduling policy *literally* decides whether a \$600 million facility is profitable.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Job Scheduling Methods — The Five Canonical Policies

The KTU syllabus maps scheduling methods into **two philosophical camps**: *local ordering* (how to pick the next job) and *global placement* (how to pick the next node). Both are needed.

### 2.1.1 First-Come-First-Served (FCFS) / FIFO
- **Logic:** Jobs are dispatched in the exact order of submission.
- **Pros:** Trivially fair, simple to implement, no starvation.
- **Cons:** *Convoy effect* — one huge job blocks hundreds of small ones. CPU utilization drops to **~40%** in heterogeneous workloads.
- **Use-case:** Predictable batch farms (banking EOD processing).

### 2.1.2 Shortest Job First (SJF) — Non-Preemptive
- **Logic:** Pick the job with the smallest estimated execution time $\hat{T}_{exe}$.
- **Why it works:** Minimizes **average waiting time** (mathematically proven optimal under known $\hat{T}_{exe}$).
- **Cons:** Long jobs may starve indefinitely. Requires user-supplied or historically-estimated runtime.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Formula — SJF Average Waiting Time:**
>
> $$\bar{W}_{SJF} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i$$
>
> where $W_i$ is the waiting time of job $i$ in the queue. Under SJF this is provably minimum *if* and only if all runtimes are known a priori.

### 2.1.3 Priority Scheduling
- **Logic:** Each job carries a priority $P \in \mathbb{Z}^+$. The scheduler always picks $\max(P)$.
- **Variants:** Static priority (set at submission) or Dynamic priority (decay functions, aging).
- **Aging:** $P_{new} = P_{old} + \alpha \cdot t_{wait}$ prevents starvation.
- **Use-case:** Production vs. research queues; pre-emptible GPU partitions.

### 2.1.4 Backfilling (EASY / Conservative)
- **Logic:** A *flexible* version of FCFS. The job at the head of the queue is *reserved* its requested slots, but **smaller jobs behind it are allowed to run earlier** *provided* they will not delay the reserved job's projected start time.
- **Why critical for HPC:** Increases utilization from ~40% (pure FCFS) to **>70%** with negligible fairness loss.
- **Constraint:** Each backfilled job must declare a *maximum walltime* so the scheduler can compute its latest start.

> [!NOTE]
> **EASY (Extensible Argonne Scheduling sYstem) vs Conservative Backfilling:**
> - **EASY** backfills *all* jobs that fit, only the head job is reserved.
> - **Conservative** backfills only jobs that fit *and* do not delay *any* earlier job. Slightly lower utilization, slightly higher fairness.

### 2.1.5 Gang Scheduling (Co-Scheduling)
- **Logic:** All $N$ tasks of a parallel job are *simultaneously* coscheduled on $N$ nodes for the same time-slice.
- **Time-Slice Matrix:** A 2-D schedule where rows = nodes, columns = time slots. The scheduler must keep parallel jobs aligned in the columns.
- **Why needed:** Tightly-coupled MPI jobs that frequently communicate will **deadlock or stall** if even one task is descheduled.
- **Cost:** Higher context-switch overhead, lower average utilization when parallel jobs are short.

### 2.1.6 Fair-Share Scheduling
- **Logic:** Allocates compute time proportionally to *user/group shares*. A research group with share $S$ is guaranteed to consume $S$ fraction of cluster-hours over a window.
- **Mechanism:** Usage is *decayed* over time; if a group underuses, their share grows; overuse causes future jobs to be de-prioritized.

## 2.2 Job Management System (JMS) — Administration View

The JMS is the **administrative control plane** of a cluster. It is distinct from the scheduler. KTU divides its administration into five functional pillars:

| Pillar | Functions | Typical Tool / Config |
|---|---|---|
| **User & Group Management** | AuthN, AuthZ, project accounts, LDAP/AD integration | `sacctmgr`, `pbs_account` |
| **Queue/Class Configuration** | Define queues, defaults, max nodes, max walltime, priorities | `scontrol create partition` |
| **Resource Limits (Fairness)** | Per-user, per-group QOS levels, max running jobs, max CPUs | QOS objects in SLURM |
| **Accounting & Reporting** | Track CPU-hours, GPU-hours, job history | `sacct`, MySQL/DB backend |
| **Policy Engine** | Preemption rules, backfill permissions, partition access lists | Priority/multifactor plugins |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "explain JMS administration," the examiner expects *at least three* of the pillars above with one concrete command/config example. A purely conceptual answer loses marks.

### 2.2.1 The Typical JMS Architecture
The JMS is a **3-tier daemon architecture**:
1. **Controller/Master Daemon** (e.g., `slurmctld`) — runs on the head node, makes all scheduling decisions, maintains cluster state in a database.
2. **Worker/Node Daemon** (e.g., `slurmd`) — runs on every compute node, launches tasks, reports back to the master.
3. **Client Tools** (e.g., `srun`, `sbatch`, `squeue`, `scancel`) — user/admin interface.

Communication is typically **stateless request/reply** over a TCP port (e.g., SLURM uses 6817), with the master holding the **authoritative state**.

## 2.3 Job Types — The KTU Classification

The 2024 scheme categorizes jobs along **two orthogonal axes**: *execution shape* and *interaction mode*.

### 2.3.1 By Execution Shape
| Job Type | Description | Resource Request | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Serial Job** | One task, one node. | `--ntasks=1` | Compiling code, single-threaded simulation. |
| **Parallel (MPI) Job** | $N$ tasks, $N$ nodes, tight coupling. | `--ntasks=N --nodes=N` | CFD, weather simulation. |
| **OpenMP / Threaded Job** | One task, multiple threads on one node. | `--cpus-per-task=K` | Numerical kernels. |
| **Hybrid MPI+OpenMP** | $N$ MPI ranks × $K$ threads each. | `--ntasks=N --cpus-per-task=K` | Large finite-element models. |
| **Array Job** | $M$ *independent* jobs with a common template. | `--array=1-M` | Parameter sweeps, Monte Carlo. |
| **GPU Job** | Requires accelerator. | `--gres=gpu:2` | Deep-learning training. |

### 2.3.2 By Interaction Mode
- **Batch Job** — Submitted, queued, executed non-interactively (the common case in HPC).
- **Interactive Job** — Allocates a shell on a compute node (`srun --pty bash`). Used for debugging.
- **Real-Time / Streaming Job** — Bounded latency guarantees (rare in HPC, common in edge clusters).
- **Dependent / Workflow Job** — DAG of jobs (e.g., job B starts only after job A succeeds). Handled by workflow engines (Snakemake, Nextflow, Airflow) on top of the JMS.

## 2.4 Migration Schemes — Relocating Live Work

Migration is invoked when **(a)** a node is predicted to fail, **(b)** load is imbalanced, or **(c)** a higher-priority job preempts a slot.

### 2.4.1 Process Migration — The Five Phases
1. **Suspend** — Freeze the process on the source node.
2. **Capture State** — CPU registers, stack, heap, file descriptors, open sockets, pending signals.
3. **Transfer State** — Ship the captured image to the destination node over the cluster interconnect.
4. **Restore State** — Re-instantiate the process on the destination, re-bind sockets, re-open FDs.
5. **Resume** — Continue execution.

### 2.4.2 Memory Migration — The Three Algorithms

| Scheme | Logic | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pre-Copy** | Run on source; iteratively copy dirty pages to dest; finally stop and copy the remainder. | Lowest downtime (residual copy is small). | High total data transferred; if the process dirties pages faster than network can copy, the loop never ends (page faulting bomb). |
| **Post-Copy** | Suspend; copy minimum state to dest; resume; fetch missing pages on demand (network page fault). | Lowest total data transferred. | High latency on first access; if dest node loses network, process is unrecoverable. |
| **Hybrid (CRIU-Migration, etc.)** | Pre-copy warm-up phase → post-copy tail. | Balances bandwidth and downtime. | Complex; needs hotplug/dynamic memory. |

### 2.4.3 Checkpoint / Restart (C/R)
A *non-live* cousin of migration:
- **Checkpoint:** Snapshot entire process state to stable storage (parallel file system) at time $t_c$.
- **Restart:** Reload snapshot on (possibly different) node at any later time $t_r$.
- **Tools:** BLCR, DMTCP, CRIU (Linux), Condor checkpoint library.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** "Migration" and "Checkpoint/Restart" are **not** the same. Migration aims to *minimize downtime* (live). C/R is a *durability* mechanism; downtime can be arbitrarily long.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **only** set of formulas you need to recall for this topic. Memorize the *symbols*, not just the equations.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Average Waiting Time | $\bar{W} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i$ | $W_i \ge 0$ |
| 2 | Average Turnaround Time | $\bar{T}_{tat} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(W_i + T_{exe,i})$ | $T_{exe,i}$ = execution time of job $i$ |
| 3 | SJF Optimality (Lower Bound) | $\bar{W}_{SJF} \le \bar{W}_{any}$ | Requires known $T_{exe}$ |
| 4 | Throughput | $\Theta = \frac{N_{completed}}{\Delta t}$ | Jobs per second / hour |
| 5 | Cluster Utilization | $U = \frac{\sum_{j} t_{busy,j}}{\sum_{j} t_{busy,j} + t_{idle,j}}$ | $0 \le U \le 1$ |
| 6 | Pre-copy Total Bytes | $B_{pre} = \sum_{r=1}^{R} M_{r}$ | $M_r$ = bytes in round $r$; $R$ = rounds |
| 7 | Pre-copy Downtime | $D_{pre} = \frac{M_{dirty,last}}{B_{net}} + T_{resume}$ | $B_{net}$ = network bandwidth |
| 8 | Post-copy First-fault Latency | $L_{pf} = \frac{M_{page}}{B_{net}} + T_{lookup}$ | Per missing page |
| 9 | Aging Priority | $P(t) = P_0 + \alpha \cdot t_{wait}$ | $\alpha$ is system constant |
| 10 | Fair-Share Decay | $S_{eff}(t) = \frac{S}{U_{recent} + f}$ | $f$ = share floor |
| 11 | Gang-Schedule Slice Length | $\tau_{slice} = \frac{T_{quantum}}{N_{parallel\_jobs}}$ | $T_{quantum}$ = global quantum |
| 12 | Backfill Reservation | $t_{start,j} \le t_{start,head} - T_{exe,j}$ | Constraint per backfilled job |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of These Models

- **Data Centers (Google Borg, Kubernetes):** Use **multi-resource Dominant Resource Fairness (DRF)** — a generalization of fair-share. The same math, different resource types.
- **HPC Centers (PRACE, XSEDE):** Use **SLURM with backfilling + priority multifactor** — the gold standard for utilization.
- **Edge / IoT Clusters:** Use **pre-copy migration** for VMs to live-migrate containers when nodes overheat.
- **AI Training Farms:** **Gang scheduling** is mandatory because distributed AllReduce on a half-killed job is useless.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section is **exhaustive by design**. Every algebraic step is written out explicitly, with an accompanying text row explaining the *logic* of the transition. No result is left as "it can be shown."

## 3.1 Derivation — SJF is Optimal for Average Waiting Time

**Claim:** Among all non-preemptive scheduling policies, SJF minimizes the average waiting time $\bar{W}$ when all job runtimes are known in advance.

### Setup
Let the queue contain $n$ jobs, sorted by submission time $a_i$ (assume all arrive at $t=0$ for simplicity), with execution times $T_i$. The scheduler picks some *permutation* $\pi$ of $\{1,2,\ldots,n\}$ to order them.

### Derivation

**Step 1** — The waiting time of job $i$ in position $k$ of the schedule is the sum of all earlier jobs' runtimes.

$$W_{\pi(k)} = \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} T_{\pi(j)}$$

*Text row:* If the job at position $k$ must wait for all jobs in positions $1, 2, \ldots, k-1$ to finish, then its wait time is the cumulative sum of those earlier runtimes. This is the definition of non-preemptive sequential execution.

**Step 2** — The total waiting time across all jobs is the sum of $W_{\pi(k)}$ over $k = 1$ to $n$.

$$W_{total} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} W_{\pi(k)} = \sum_{k=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{k-1} T_{\pi(j)}$$

*Text row:* We simply stack the per-job waits. This is a double sum where the outer index is the position in the queue and the inner index scans everything before it.

**Step 3** — Swap the order of summation. For each job $j$ that is *before* position $k$, it contributes $T_j$ to the wait of all $k-1, k-2, \ldots, n$ jobs that come after it.

$$W_{total} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} T_{\pi(j)} \cdot (n - j)$$

*Text row:* After swapping, the job in position $j$ contributes its runtime $T_{\pi(j)}$ to the wait time of *every job placed after it* — and there are exactly $n-j$ such jobs. Hence the multiplicative factor $(n-j)$.

**Step 4** — To minimize $W_{total}$, we want the largest $T_{\pi(j)}$ to be paired with the smallest $(n-j)$, and vice versa. This is the rearrangement inequality. The minimum is achieved when $T_{\pi(j)}$ is *monotonically non-decreasing* in $j$, i.e., $T_{\pi(1)} \le T_{\pi(2)} \le \cdots \le T_{\pi(n)}$.

*Text row:* The rearrangement inequality states that for two sequences, the sum of products is minimized when one is sorted ascending and the other descending. Here the sequences are $\{T_{\pi(j)}\}$ and $\{(n-j)\}$, the latter being strictly decreasing in $j$. So the matching $T$ ascending with $(n-j)$ descending is optimal.

**Step 5** — Sorting execution times ascending is exactly the **Shortest Job First** rule.

$$\therefore\ \bar{W}_{SJF} = \frac{W_{total,SJF}}{n} \le \bar{W}_{any}$$

*Text row:* We conclude that the SJF ordering minimizes total and therefore average waiting time. This proof is what makes SJF the canonical optimality benchmark in textbooks.

## 3.2 Derivation — Total Bytes Transferred in Pre-Copy Migration

**Claim:** The total bytes shipped in a pre-copy loop can be written in closed form, allowing us to predict *whether* the loop converges.

### Setup
Let:
- $M_0$ = resident set size of the process at $t=0$ (start of migration).
- $p$ = *page dirty rate* (pages/second dirtied by the process).
- $r$ = network transfer rate (pages/second the pre-copy can ship).
- $R$ = number of pre-copy rounds.
- $W(t)$ = working set at time $t$ (assume $W(t) = M_0$ for simplicity, i.e., bounded memory).

### Derivation

**Step 1** — In round $r$, we copy $M_{r-1}$ pages (the dirty set left over from the previous round). The time to copy is $M_{r-1} / r$.

$$t_r = \frac{M_{r-1}}{r}$$
