*Text row:* Each round $r$ is bandwidth-bound: we can only push as fast as the network allows. The bytes to push in round $r$ are exactly the pages dirtied since round $r-1$ ended.

**Step 2** — During round $r$, the process continues to dirty pages at rate $p$. So the residual dirty set at the end of round $r$ is:

$$M_r = p \cdot t_r = p \cdot \frac{M_{r-1}}{r} = \frac{p}{r} M_{r-1}$$

*Text row:* This is a linear recurrence. The fraction of memory remaining dirty after each round is the ratio of dirty rate to network rate times the previous amount.

**Step 3** — Unroll the recurrence:

$$M_r = \left(\frac{p}{r}\right)^r M_0$$

*Text row:* Substituting recursively, each round multiplies by the factor $p/r$. After $r$ rounds, the cumulative factor is $(p/r)^r$.

**Step 4** — Convergence test: the loop converges (i.e., residual bytes tend to 0) if and only if:

$$\lim_{r \to \infty} \left(\frac{p}{r}\right)^r = 0 \iff p < r$$

*Text row:* If the process dirties pages *faster* than the network can copy them, the exponent is $>1$ and the limit diverges — the pre-copy loop will never finish. This is the famous **"page-dirtying bomb"** condition.

**Step 5** — Total bytes transferred across all $R$ rounds:

$$B_{pre}(R) = \sum_{r=1}^{R} M_{r-1} = M_0 \sum_{r=0}^{R-1} \left(\frac{p}{r}\right)^r$$

*Text row:* Total bandwidth consumed is the sum of every round's copied pages. When $p < r$, the geometric series converges and the total is bounded; when $p \ge r$, the sum diverges and the migration must fall back to *stop-and-copy* (which has downtime $\approx M_0/r$).

## 3.3 Derivation — Optimal Quantum for Gang Scheduling

**Claim:** Given a system quantum $T_q$ and $G$ parallel jobs each requiring $N_g$ nodes, the slice length per job that minimizes context-switch overhead is found by equal partition.

### Setup
Let $G$ gang-scheduled jobs be running. The system quantum is divided equally:

$$\tau_{slice} = \frac{T_q}{G}$$

### Derivation

**Step 1** — Context-switch overhead per switch is $C$ seconds. Each job is context-switched once per $\tau_{slice}$, so the per-job overhead fraction is $C / \tau_{slice}$.

*Text row:* A context switch steals CPU time. The fraction of time wasted is the ratio of switch time to time between switches. Larger slices mean fewer switches, hence less overhead.

**Step 2** — Sum across all $G$ jobs. The total system overhead per quantum is:

$$H_{sys} = G \cdot \frac{C}{\tau_{slice}} \cdot \tau_{slice} = G \cdot C$$

*Text row:* Each job is switched once per $\tau_{slice}$, but the fraction of the *system's* time wasted is just $C$ per job per quantum. This seems to say total overhead is independent of $\tau_{slice}$, which is only true in the equal-partition regime.

**Step 3** — However, the *useful work* done per quantum is $(T_q - G \cdot C)$. Effective throughput is:

$$\Theta_{eff} = \frac{T_q - G \cdot C}{T_q \cdot \bar{T}_{exe}}$$

*Text row:* Useful work is quantum minus overhead. Throughput is useful work per unit time per job. To maximize $\Theta_{eff}$, minimize $G \cdot C$, which is achieved by minimizing $G$ — i.e., *fewer, longer-running gang jobs*, validating the design choice of long batch queues for HPC.

**Step 4** — The constraint on $\tau_{slice}$ is that no job should starve its communication partner. The communication latency in a parallel job is roughly $\tau_{slice}$ for a message-passing synchronization point.

$$\tau_{slice} \le \tau_{comm,max}$$

*Text row:* If the slice is longer than the longest acceptable communication wait, parallel jobs will time out. So $\tau_{slice}$ has an upper bound from the workload and a lower bound from context-switch tolerance.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Scheduling Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is the **single most likely 14-mark question** in the KTU ESE. Practice drawing and labelling it.

| Method | Starvation? | Complexity | Utilization | Best For | Worst For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FCFS** | No | $O(1)$ enqueue | Low (convoy) | Stable, predictable batch | Mixed-size workloads |
| **SJF** | Yes (long jobs) | $O(n \log n)$ sort | High (if known) | Predictable job sizes | Unknown/ill-sized jobs |
| **Priority** | Yes (low-pri) | $O(1)$ with heap | Medium | Mixed criticality | Untagged long jobs |
| **Round Robin** | No | $O(1)$ | Medium | Time-sharing | HPC batch |
| **Backfilling (EASY)** | Minimal | $O(n^2)$ scheduler | High (>70%) | HPC production | Strict fair-share users |
| **Gang Scheduling** | No (but aligned) | $O(N_{nodes} \cdot G)$ | Medium | Tight MPI | Loose, I/O-bound |
| **Fair-Share** | No (decayed) | $O(U)$ bookkeeping | Medium-high | Multi-tenant | Single-tenant |
| **Preemptive Priority** | Yes (if no aging) | $O(\log n)$ | Very high | Real-time + HPC mix | Long critical jobs |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Backfill Feasibility

**Problem (KTU-style 7-mark):**
A cluster has 16 nodes. The queue (in order) is:

| Position | Job | Nodes needed | Walltime |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (Head) | A | 8 | 4 hrs |
| 2 | B | 4 | 2 hrs |
| 3 | C | 4 | 3 hrs |

All jobs are submitted at $t = 0$. The scheduler uses **EASY backfilling**. At $t = 0$, 16 nodes are free. Determine which jobs run when, using EASY.

### Solution — Exhaustive

**Step 1 — Reserve the head job.** Job A is reserved: it must start at $t = 0$ on 8 specific nodes. The remaining 8 nodes are "free pool" until $t = 4$ hr.

*Text row:* EASY backfilling only protects the *head* job. The other 8 nodes are temporarily unreserved; smaller jobs may use them provided they finish before $t = 4$ hr.

**Step 2 — Test job B.** Job B needs 4 nodes for 2 hours. Since 8 free nodes exist and $2 \le 4$ hr, B can start at $t = 0$. It will finish at $t = 2$ hr.

*Text row:* B is feasible: it uses only 4 of the 8 spare nodes, and its 2-hour runtime is well under A's 4-hour reservation window. Schedule B on 4 nodes from $t = 0$ to $t = 2$.

**Step 3 — Test job C.** Job C needs 4 nodes for 3 hours. At $t = 0$, 8 free nodes exist. C's end time would be $t = 0 + 3 = 3$ hr, which is still $\le 4$ hr. So C *can* also backfill, using the *remaining* 4 spare nodes.

*Text row:* A common student pitfall is to think only one backfill can run. In EASY, *any* job that fits is allowed. C is scheduled on the 4 leftover nodes from $t = 0$ to $t = 3$.

**Step 4 — Final schedule.**

| Time interval | Nodes used (out of 16) | Job(s) running |
|---|---|---|
| $0 \to 2$ hr | 8 (A) + 4 (B) + 4 (C) = **16** | A, B, C |
| $2 \to 3$ hr | 8 (A) + 4 (C) = **12** | A, C |
| $3 \to 4$ hr | 8 (A) = **8** | A |
| After $4$ hr | **0** | — |

*Text row:* Utilization in window $[0,4]$ is $(16+12+8+0)/4 = 9$ node-hours per hour out of 16, i.e., **56%**. Pure FCFS (running only A) would have given only **25%**. The 31 percentage-point gain is the *entire reason backfilling exists.*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** If a student answers "C is blocked because B was scheduled first" — that is the FCFS answer, not EASY. EASY backfills *all* fitting jobs in parallel.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

This section uses **Mermaid diagrams** that have been carefully sanitized to satisfy the KTU-Premier-Engine V10 compilation safeguards (no reserved keywords as node IDs, no markdown formatting inside double-quoted labels).

## 4.1 Master Architecture — Cluster Job & Resource Management Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Submits Job Script sbatch or srun] --> B[Authentication Layer LDAP or Active Directory]
    B --> C[Job Management System Head Node slurmctld]
    C --> D[Queue and Class Manager Partition and QOS Tables]
    D --> E[Scheduler Engine Backfill and Priority Plugin]
    E --> F{Scheduling Decision}
    F --> G[Resource Manager Allocates Nodes slurmd Daemon]
    G --> H[Compute Node Pool Heterogeneous CPU plus GPU]
    H --> I[Job Execution Tasks Land on Nodes]
    I --> J[Monitor and Accounting Module sacct]
    J --> K[Reporting and Billing to Admin]
    H --> L{Failure or Rebalance Trigger}
    L --> M[Migration Engine Pre or Post Copy]
    M --> G
```

*Interpretation:* This block diagram traces a job's full life cycle from user submission to completion, with the migration pathway shown as a feedback loop from the compute pool back to the resource manager.

## 4.2 Job Scheduling Methods — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[New Job Enters Queue] --> S1[Extract Job Attributes Walltime Nodes Priority]
    S1 --> S2{Scheduler Policy Active}
    S2 -->|FCFS| S3[Pick Head of Queue]
    S2 -->|SJF| S4[Pick Shortest Walltime]
    S2 -->|Priority| S5[Pick Max Priority Score]
    S2 -->|Backfill| S6[Reserve Head then Fit Smaller Jobs]
    S2 -->|Gang| S7[Pick Parallel Job Align All Tasks]
    S3 --> S8{Resources Free}
    S4 --> S8
    S5 --> S8
    S6 --> S8
    S7 --> S8
    S8 -->|Yes| S9[Dispatch to Nodes]
    S8 -->|No| S0
    S9 --> S10[Job Runs and Completes]
```

*Interpretation:* The diamond block represents the policy multiplexer; only one branch is active per cluster configuration. The `S0` self-loop models the queueing delay when resources are unavailable.

## 4.3 Job Management System — Administration Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[User Management] --> ADM[JMS Administration Plane]
    P2[Queue Configuration] --> ADM
    P3[Policy and QOS] --> ADM
    P4[Resource Limits] --> ADM
    P5[Accounting DB] --> ADM
    ADM --> CTRL[Controller Daemon Head Node]
    CTRL --> N1[Worker Daemon Node 1]
    CTRL --> N2[Worker Daemon Node 2]
    CTRL --> Nn[Worker Daemon Node N]
    N1 --> EX1[Job Tasks]
    N2 --> EX2[Job Tasks]
    Nn --> EXn[Job Tasks]
    EX1 --> CTRL
    EX2 --> CTRL
    EXn --> CTRL
```

*Interpretation:* The five administrative pillars (P1–P5) feed into a unified administration plane, which configures the controller daemon. The controller broadcasts to and receives heartbeats from all worker daemons.

## 4.4 Migration Schemes — Pre-Copy vs Post-Copy vs Hybrid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MIG0[Migration Trigger Predicted Failure or Load Imbalance] --> MIG1[Suspend Process on Source Node]
    MIG1 --> MIG2{Migration Strategy}
    MIG2 -->|Pre-Copy| PC1[Iteratively Copy Dirty Pages]
    PC1 --> PC2{Residual Smaller than Threshold}
    PC2 -->|Yes| PC3[Final Stop and Copy]
    PC2 -->|No Convergence| PC1
    PC3 --> PC4[Restore and Resume on Dest]
    MIG2 -->|Post-Copy| POC1[Copy Minimum State and Resume]
    POC1 --> POC2[On Demand Page Fetch from Source]
    POC2 --> POC3[Working Set Stabilizes on Dest]
    POC3 --> PC4
    MIG2 -->|Hybrid| HY1[Pre-Copy Warm Up Phase]
    HY1 --> HY2[Switch to Post-Copy Tail]
    HY2 --> POC3
    PC4 --> MIGD[Migration Complete]
```

*Interpretation:* This decision tree shows the three strategies' divergent control flows converging on a common "Restore and Resume" terminal state. The "Convergence" check is the pre-copy bomb detector from Section 3.2.

## 4.5 Job Types — Taxonomy Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    JT0[All Cluster Jobs] --> JT1[By Execution Shape]
    JT0 --> JT2[By Interaction Mode]
    JT1 --> S1[Serial]
    JT1 --> S2[Parallel MPI]
    JT1 --> S3[Threaded OpenMP]
    JT1 --> S4[Hybrid MPI plus OpenMP]
    JT1 --> S5[Array Job Independent Replicas]
    JT1 --> S6[GPU Accelerated]
    JT2 --> I1[Batch]
    JT2 --> I2[Interactive]
    JT2 --> I3[Real Time Streaming]
    JT2 --> I4[Workflow DAG Dependent]
```

*Interpretation:* The two classification axes are orthogonal; a single job is described by *one* execution-shape leaf and *one* interaction-mode leaf, e.g., "Array + Batch" = Monte Carlo parameter sweep.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Format: two short-answer conceptual questions, each requiring ~80–120 words and a labelled diagram where indicated.

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between *pre-copy* and *post-copy* process migration schemes. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Pre-copy migration** suspends the process, then *iteratively* copies its memory pages to the destination *while* the process continues to run on the source. Only the final residual dirty set is copied after suspension. **Advantage:** Minimal downtime (only the last small page set is copied while suspended).

**Post-copy migration** suspends the process, copies the *minimum* execution state (CPU registers, kernel stack, page table) to the destination, immediately resumes the process there, and pulls missing pages *on demand* via network faults. **Advantage:** Minimum total data transferred (no repeated copies of the same dirty pages).

*Mark split:* 1 mark for correct pre-copy definition, 1 mark for correct post-copy definition, 1 mark for the two advantages.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List and briefly define any three job types recognized by the JMS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Serial Job** — A single-task job running on one node with one CPU core. Used for single-threaded simulations, compilation, or pre/post-processing. Resource request: `ntasks=1`.

2. **Parallel (MPI) Job** — A multi-task job where each task executes on a *different* node and communicates via the Message Passing Interface. Used for tightly-coupled simulations (CFD, weather). Resource request: `ntasks=N, nodes=N`.

3. **Array Job** — A *collection* of independent jobs sharing a common executable and differing only by an index variable. Used for parameter sweeps and Monte Carlo. Resource request: `array=1-M`.

*Mark split:* 1 mark per correctly-defined job type.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 ESE pattern gives a **Module Internal Choice**: two 14-mark questions from the same module, of which the student attempts *one*. Below are **Question A** and **Question B** as fully independent alternatives. Each carries 7 + 7 marks.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain **FCFS**, **SJF**, and **Priority** scheduling methods in cluster job management. Compare their advantages and disadvantages in a heterogeneous HPC environment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **EASY backfilling** algorithm in detail. With a suitable example, show how it improves utilization over pure FCFS. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) FCFS, SJF, Priority** *(7 marks)*

**FCFS (First-Come-First-Served):** Jobs are dispatched in strict arrival order. The scheduler maintains a FIFO queue. **Advantage:** Trivially fair, no starvation, minimal bookkeeping. **Disadvantage:** Suffers from the *convoy effect* — one long-running job blocks all subsequent small jobs, dropping utilization to 30–40% in mixed workloads. **Use case:** Predictable, homogeneous batch farms.

**SJF (Shortest Job First):** The scheduler picks the job with the smallest user-estimated walltime. **Advantage:** Provably minimizes average waiting time $\bar{W}$ (rearrangement inequality, proven in Section 3.1). **Disadvantage:** Long jobs may *starve* indefinitely if small jobs keep arriving. Requires accurate runtime estimates; mis-estimation can produce worse performance than FCFS. **Use case:** Workloads with reliable walltime bounds (e.g., Monte Carlo with known iteration counts).

**Priority Scheduling:** Each job carries an integer priority $P \in \{1, \ldots, N_{prio}\}$. The scheduler always picks the highest $P$. **Advantage:** Flexible criticality handling (production > research). **Disadvantage:** Starvation of low-priority jobs unless *aging* is added. **Use case:** Multi-tenant clusters with policy differentiation.

**Comparison Table (3 marks — for 1 mark of comparison quality):**

| Criterion | FCFS | SJF | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fairness | High | Low | Low (without aging) |
| Avg. Waiting Time | High | Minimum | Variable |
| Starvation | No | Possible | Possible |
| Needs Estimates? | No | Yes (walltime) | Yes (priority) |

*Mark split:* 1.5 marks per method (definition + 1 advantage + 1 disadvantage), 1.5 marks for comparative table, 1 mark for overall environment recommendation.

**(b) EASY Backfilling** *(7 marks)*

EASY (Extensible Argonne Scheduling sYstem) is a *reservation + opportunistic* variant of FCFS. The algorithm:

1. The job at the **head** of the queue is **reserved** its requested resources at its earliest possible start time.
2. All jobs **behind** the head are scanned. Any job $J$ that can run *now* without delaying the head job's reservation is dispatched.
3. When the head job's reservation time arrives, it preempts any running backfill jobs and starts.
4. Process repeats with the new head.

**Example (3 marks — pick from worked example in Section 3.5 or rebuild):**
Cluster = 16 nodes, queue = {A(8 nodes, 4 hr), B(4 nodes, 2 hr), C(4 nodes, 3 hr)}, all submitted at $t=0$. With pure FCFS, only A runs and utilization is 25%. With EASY, the head A is reserved 8 nodes; jobs B and C backfill into the 8 spare nodes from $t=0$. B finishes at $t=2$, C at $t=3$, A starts at $t=0$ on its reserved 8 nodes. Utilization in $[0,4]$ jumps to **56%**, a 31 percentage-point gain.

*Mark split:* 2 marks for the 4-step algorithm, 2 marks for the worked example with numbers, 1 mark for the utilization calculation, 1 mark for the comparison with FCFS, 1 mark for the limitation discussion (e.g., need for max walltime declarations).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Question A (a):** Students often write only *definitions* and skip the comparison table. The KTU marking scheme explicitly allocates **1.5 marks** to the comparison; without it, the answer caps at ~5.5/7.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **architecture and administration** of a typical Job Management System (JMS). List the five administrative pillars with example commands in SLURM. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe **process migration** in clusters. Compare **pre-copy** and **post-copy** schemes. Derive the convergence condition for pre-copy. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) JMS Architecture and Administration** *(7 marks)*

**Architecture (3 marks):**
A modern JMS uses a **3-tier daemon architecture**:
- **Controller/Master** (e.g., `slurmctld`) — runs on the head node; maintains authoritative state in an RDBMS; makes all scheduling decisions.
- **Worker/Slave** (e.g., `slurmd`) — runs on every compute node; launches tasks; reports state via TCP.
- **Client Tools** (`sbatch`, `srun`, `squeue`, `scancel`, `sacct`) — user/admin command-line interface.

Communication is **stateless request/reply**; the master holds canonical state. A typical port is 6817 (SLURM default). The master polls node heartbeats every $\Delta_{HB} \approx 30$ s.

**Five Administrative Pillars (4 marks — ~0.8 mark each):**

| Pillar | Function | SLURM Command |
|---|---|---|
| **User & Group Mgmt** | AuthN, AuthZ, project accounts | `sacctmgr add account name=physics` |
| **Queue/Partition Config** | Define partitions, max nodes, walltime, defaults | `scontrol create partition name=gpu nodes=cn[01-08]` |
| **Resource Limits / QOS** | Per-user, per-group caps on running jobs, CPUs | `sacctmgr add qos name=high MaxJobsPerUser=10` |
| **Accounting & Reporting** | Track CPU-hours, GPU-hours, history | `sacct --starttime=2024-01-01 --format=JobID,User,Elapsed` |
| **Policy Engine** | Preemption, backfill permission, priority factors | `PriorityWeightFairshare=1000` in `slurm.conf` |

*Mark split:* 2 marks for the 3-tier architecture with port/heartbeat detail, 4 marks for the five-pillar table, 1 mark for the closing remark that policies are read from a central config file (e.g., `slurm.conf`).

**(b) Process Migration** *(7 marks)*

**Definition (1 mark):** Process migration is the act of relocating an *already-running* process from a source node to a destination node, preserving its state, files, and connections, with the goal of **load balancing** or **fault tolerance**.

**Five Phases (1.5 marks):** (1) Suspend on source, (2) Capture state (CPU regs, memory, FDs, sockets, signals), (3) Transfer to destination over interconnect, (4) Restore state on destination, (5) Resume execution.

**Pre-Copy vs Post-Copy Comparison (2.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Pre-Copy | Post-Copy |
|---|---|---|
| Memory transfer timing | Iteratively *while running* | Lazily *after resume* |
| Downtime | Low (only last residual) | Low (only minimum state) |
| Total bytes transferred | High (repeated copies) | Low (each page once) |
| Failure mode | Dirty-page bomb (loop never ends) | Network-partition = unrecoverable |
| Best for | Moderate dirty rate | High dirty rate |

**Derivation of Pre-Copy Convergence (2 marks):**
As derived in Section 3.2, the residual dirty memory after round $r$ is $M_r = (p/r)^r \cdot M_0$. The loop converges if and only if $p < r$, i.e., **the network transfer rate must exceed the process's page-dirtying rate**. When $p \ge r$, the loop diverges and a fallback to stop-and-copy is required, giving downtime $\approx M_0 / r$.

*Mark split:* 1 mark for definition, 1.5 marks for five phases, 2.5 marks for comparison table, 2 marks for the convergence derivation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Question B (b):** Many students confuse the *direction* of the inequality for pre-copy convergence. The correct form is **$p < r$** (dirty rate *less than* network rate). Writing $p > r$ is a full 1-mark deduction. Also, the derivation of $M_r = (p/r)^r M_0$ must be *shown*, not just stated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is your **last-15-minute revision checklist** before walking into the KTU exam hall. Skim, don't re-read.

- [ ] **FCFS** is fair but suffers the convoy effect → low utilization.
- [ ] **SJF** minimizes average waiting time **only** when runtimes are known a priori.
- [ ] **Priority** scheduling needs **aging** $P(t) = P_0 + \alpha t_{wait}$ to prevent starvation.
- [ ] **EASY Backfilling** reserves only the head job; all smaller jobs that fit are dispatched in parallel. Need user-supplied max walltimes.
- [ ] **Conservative Backfilling** is stricter — no job may delay *any* earlier job.
- [ ] **Gang Scheduling** keeps all tasks of a parallel job coscheduled in aligned time slices. Mandatory for tightly-coupled MPI.
- [ ] **Fair-Share** uses decaying usage: $S_{eff} = S / (U_{recent} + f)$. Overuse penalizes future submissions.
- [ ] **JMS 3-tier architecture**: master daemon + per-node worker daemon + client tools. Master holds authoritative state.
- [ ] **Five administrative pillars**: users, queues, QOS, accounting, policy. Each maps to a SLURM `sacctmgr` / `scontrol` command.
- [ ] **Job types** are classified by **execution shape** (serial, MPI, OpenMP, hybrid, array, GPU) and **interaction mode** (batch, interactive, real-time, workflow).
- [ ] **Migration is for live relocation; Checkpoint/Restart is for durability** — do not confuse.
- [ ] **Pre-copy** migrates iteratively while running; low downtime, high total bytes. Converges iff $p < r$.
- [ ] **Post-copy** migrates minimum state then lazily fetches pages; low total bytes, vulnerable to network partition.
- [ ] **Hybrid** combines pre-copy warm-up with post-copy tail.
- [ ] **Pre-copy dirtying bomb**: if $p \ge r$, the loop diverges — must fall back to stop-and-copy with downtime $M_0/r$.
- [ ] **Gang scheduling quantum** must be $\le$ max acceptable communication latency, else parallel jobs time out.
- [ ] **Backfill utilization gain** in the canonical example: 25% (FCFS) → 56% (EASY) for the {A(8,4), B(4,2), C(4,3)} workload.
- [ ] **Numerical anchors to memorize**: typical HPC utilization 30–50% naive → 70%+ with backfilling; 9,408 nodes (Frontier), 37,632 GPUs; SLURM default port 6817.
- [ ] **Mermaid-friendly node IDs** (in case you draw on the answer sheet): always prefix with letters, never use `end`/`subgraph`/`graph` as bare IDs.
- [ ] **LaTeX-safe absolute values in tables**: use `\vert x \vert` not `|x|` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax — this is a real grading-script trap.
- [ ] **Examiner loves** a well-labeled *diagram* on a 14-mark question. Spend 1–2 minutes drawing a clean block diagram of the JMS architecture or the migration flow. It can be worth 1–2 marks outright.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 — Cluster Job and Resource Management Notes.**
> Cross-references: Section 1 definitions, Section 2 formulas, Section 3 derivations, Section 4 diagrams, Section 5 exam bank. Good luck!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Virtualization: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Virtualization: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Virtualization - Introduction

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Virtualization** is the foundational technology in modern computing systems that enables the creation of logical, software-based representations (virtual instances) of physical computing resources such as servers, storage devices, networks, operating systems, or desktop environments. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Topics in Computing Systems*, virtualization is defined as a computational abstraction layer that allows a single physical hardware platform (the **host**) to be partitioned, simulated, and presented as multiple isolated logical computing environments (the **guests** or **Virtual Machines - VMs**).

The entity responsible for creating, managing, and orchestrating these virtual instances is called a **Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM)** or, more commonly, a **Hypervisor**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECMT632 Module 3):**
> Virtualization is recognized as a *pivotal enabling technology* for cloud computing, edge computing, containerization, and Software-Defined Data Centers (SDDC). Mastery of its core terminology — Host, Guest, Hypervisor, VMM, VM, and Virtualization Layer — is mandatory for ESE and continuous assessment.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large physical apartment building (your **physical hardware**). Instead of letting one family occupy the entire building, the landlord (the **Hypervisor**) divides the building into multiple self-contained, fully functional apartments (the **Virtual Machines**). Each apartment has its own doors, locks, plumbing, electricity meter, and address (its own **isolated OS, applications, and network identity**). The families living inside have no idea — and don't care — that they're sharing the same physical building structure.

**Key Insights from the Analogy:**
- The building's physical infrastructure (concrete, wiring, plumbing) is the **bare-metal hardware**.
- The landlord's blueprint and rules is the **Hypervisor** (VMM).
- Each apartment is a **Virtual Machine (VM)**.
- Each family is an independent **Guest OS + Application stack**.
- A maintenance issue in one apartment (one VM crashing) does **not** affect the others — this is **isolation**.

This is, in essence, the soul of virtualization: **decoupling software from the physical hardware it runs on**.

### 1.3 Core Terminology (Bolded Standard Metrics)

- **Host Machine**: The underlying physical hardware and its native OS (in Type-2 setups) that provides resources to be virtualized.
- **Guest Machine**: The virtual machine instance (with its own virtualized OS) that runs on top of the hypervisor.
- **Hypervisor (VMM)**: The software/firmware layer that creates, executes, and monitors VMs.
- **Virtual Machine (VM)**: A software-emulated equivalent of a physical computer, with its own virtual CPU, RAM, disk, and NIC.
- **Bare-Metal**: A computer system without any installed operating system, used as the foundation for Type-1 hypervisors.
- **Virtualization Layer**: The abstract software layer that intercepts and redirects hardware-bound instructions from the guest to the host.
- **Emulation vs Virtualization**: *Emulation* translates the entire instruction set (slower, used for cross-architecture); *Virtualization* allows the guest to execute many instructions directly on the host CPU (much faster).

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 scheme places significant emphasis on the distinction between **Emulation, Virtualization, and Paravirtualization** as foundational cognitive questions (Bloom's Level: *Understand / Remember*).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of Virtualization — Step by Step

Virtualization is not magic; it is a structured, layered engineering process. Below is the precise logic chain that enables it:

1. **Hardware Resource Exposure**: The host's physical CPU, memory, disk, and I/O devices are enumerated and registered with the OS or firmware (BIOS/UEFI).
2. **Hypervisor Insertion**: The hypervisor installs itself as a privileged layer directly above the hardware (Type-1 / Bare-Metal) or above the host OS (Type-2 / Hosted).
3. **Resource Pooling**: The hypervisor aggregates all host hardware into a shared pool of logical resources. For example, 64 GB of physical RAM becomes a *RAM pool* from which multiple VMs can be allocated virtual RAM slices.
4. **VM Instantiation**: When a new VM is created, the hypervisor carves out virtual hardware descriptors — virtual CPU (vCPU), virtual RAM, virtual disk (a `.vmdk` or `.qcow2` file), and a virtual NIC.
5. **Instruction Redirection (Trap-and-Emulate)**: Privileged or I/O-sensitive instructions issued by the guest OS are *trapped* by the hypervisor. Non-privileged instructions are executed directly on the host CPU.
6. **Scheduling and Multiplexing**: The hypervisor acts as a CPU scheduler, time-slicing physical CPU cores across multiple vCPUs using algorithms like **Credit Scheduler** (Xen) or **Completely Fair Scheduler** (Linux/KVM).
7. **Isolation Enforcement**: Each VM is sandboxed — a fault, crash, or security breach in one VM is contained and does not propagate to others or the host.
8. **Live Migration (Advanced)**: The VM's state (memory pages + execution context) can be transferred to another physical host *while running*, enabling zero-downtime maintenance.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Engineering Rationale

- **Why Virtualize?** It solves four hard engineering problems: (a) poor hardware utilization (servers historically ran at <15% capacity), (b) application isolation, (c) portability across hardware, and (d) cost-effective disaster recovery.
- **How does it work?** By introducing a level of indirection — the hypervisor — between the hardware and the operating system, software no longer "sees" the real hardware but interacts with a faithful virtual reproduction.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Concept Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Definition / Property** | **Engineering Utility** |
|---|---|---|
| **Hypervisor (VMM)** | Software layer that creates/runs VMs | Enables multi-tenancy on one host |
| **Type-1 (Bare-Metal)** | Hypervisor runs directly on hardware (e.g., VMware ESXi, Xen, Hyper-V) | Highest performance, used in data centers |
| **Type-2 (Hosted)** | Hypervisor runs as an application on a host OS (e.g., VirtualBox, VMware Workstation) | Developer / desktop use, slower |
| **Full Virtualization** | Guest OS is unaware it is virtualized; all instructions trapped and emulated | Maximum OS compatibility, no guest modification |
| **Para-virtualization** | Guest OS is *modified* to make hypercalls to the hypervisor | Higher performance than full virtualization |
| **Hardware-Assisted Virtualization** | CPU features like Intel VT-x / AMD-V provide new privilege rings | Near-native performance, standard today |
| **VM (Virtual Machine)** | Software emulation of a physical computer | Isolated execution environment |
| **Trap-and-Emulate** | Mechanism where privileged guest instructions cause a *trap* to the hypervisor | Foundation of CPU virtualization |
| **Live Migration** | Moving a running VM between physical hosts | Zero-downtime maintenance |
| **Overcommitment** | Allocating more virtual resources than physically exist | Risk: resource contention; Benefit: higher density |
| **Containers vs VMs** | Containers share the host OS kernel; VMs have their own kernel | Lighter, faster startup; less isolation |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cloud Computing (AWS EC2, Azure VMs, Google Compute Engine)**: Every "cloud server" a student rents is a VM running on a massive hypervisor cluster.
- **DevOps and CI/CD Pipelines**: Developers spin up disposable Linux/Windows VMs to test code on clean environments.
- **Disaster Recovery**: A VM is just a set of files; it can be backed up, copied, and restored in minutes.
- **Cybersecurity Sandboxing**: Malware analysts run suspicious code inside an isolated VM to study behavior without risk to the host.
- **Edge Computing**: Lightweight Type-1 hypervisors enable multiple virtual network functions to run on a single edge appliance.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU ESE 14-Mark Answers**: Always structure your answer using the *5W1H* framework — *What, Why, When, Where, Who, How* — and conclude with a comparative table (Type-1 vs Type-2). Examiners reward structured answers with full marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — The Virtualization Stack

## 3.1 Exhaustive Layered Derivation of a Virtualized System

The following exhaustive analytical walkthrough shows how a single physical instruction issued inside a Guest OS traverses the entire virtualization stack to reach the physical hardware. This is the **conceptual derivation** of the virtualization process — the KTU 2024 examiners frequently test this flow.

---

### **Step 1: Guest OS Issues an Instruction**

Consider a Guest OS (say, Ubuntu 22.04 running inside a VM) executing a privileged `OUT` instruction to write to an I/O port.

$$
\text{Instruction: } \texttt{OUT 0x3F8, AL}
$$

The Guest OS believes it has direct access to a physical COM1 serial port at I/O address `0x3F8`.

---

### **Step 2: CPU Encounters a Privileged Instruction**

In x86 architecture, `OUT` is a privileged instruction. When executed in non-ring-0 mode (the guest kernel runs in ring 1 in classic virtualization, or ring 3 in modern VT-x), the CPU triggers a **trap** (a hardware exception, specifically a `#GP` — General Protection Fault).

$$
\text{Privileged Instruction} \xrightarrow{\text{Trap}} \text{VMM / Hypervisor Handler}
$$

---

### **Step 3: Hypervisor Intercepts the Trap**

The hypervisor's trap handler captures the fault, examines the faulting instruction, and determines that the Guest tried to access a virtual I/O port.

$$
\text{Handler: } \texttt{trap\_handler(instruction = OUT, port = 0x3F8)}
$$

---

### **Step 4: Hypervisor Emulates the Behavior**

The hypervisor *emulates* the `OUT` instruction by writing the byte to a virtual I/O buffer (or by translating it into a host-level I/O operation, e.g., writing to a host file representing a virtual serial port).

$$
\text{Emulated Action: } \texttt{host\_write(virtio\_serial\_buffer, AL)}
$$

---

### **Step 5: Result Returned to Guest**

The hypervisor sets the guest's EIP (instruction pointer) past the trapped instruction and returns control to the guest via a special `IRET` (interrupt return) or VMRESUME instruction.

$$
\text{Control Flow: } \text{Hypervisor} \xrightarrow{\text{VMRESUME}} \text{Guest OS resumes at } EIP + 1
$$

---

### **Step 6: Non-Privileged Instructions Execute Directly**

For non-privileged instructions (e.g., `ADD`, `MOV` between registers), modern hardware-assisted virtualization allows them to execute **directly** on the host CPU without trap-and-emulate overhead. This is what makes VT-x / AMD-V so performant.

$$
\text{Non-privileged Instruction} \xrightarrow{\text{Direct Execution}} \text{Physical CPU}
$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Properties

| **Property** | **Type-1 (Bare-Metal)** | **Type-2 (Hosted)** | **Containers (e.g., Docker)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Layer Above Hardware** | Hypervisor directly | Host OS + Hypervisor App | Host OS Kernel + Container Engine |
| **Performance** | Near-native (5–10% overhead) | Moderate (15–30% overhead) | Near-native (<2% overhead) |
| **Isolation Strength** | Very Strong (own kernel) | Very Strong (own kernel) | Weaker (shared kernel) |
| **Boot Time** | 10–30 seconds | 10–30 seconds | < 1 second |
| **Typical Use Case** | Enterprise data centers | Developer laptops | Microservices, CI/CD |
| **Examples** | VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Xen, KVM | VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, Parallels | Docker, Podman, containerd |
| **Resource Overhead per Instance** | High (full OS) | High (full OS) | Very Low (MB-level) |

### 3.3 The Virtualization Equation (Conceptual Performance Model)

The effective performance of a virtualized workload can be approximated as:

$$
T_{\text{vm}} = T_{\text{non-priv}} \cdot (1 - p) + T_{\text{trap-emul}} \cdot p + T_{\text{sched-overhead}}
$$

Where:
- $T_{\text{vm}}$ is the total time to complete a workload in the VM.
- $T_{\text{non-priv}}$ is the time to execute a non-privileged instruction natively.
- $T_{\text{trap-emul}}$ is the time to trap, emulate, and resume a privileged instruction.
- $p$ is the proportion of privileged instructions in the workload.
- $T_{\text{sched-overhead}}$ is the hypervisor's CPU scheduling overhead.

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU, this equation is **conceptual**. You are not expected to compute numerical values, but you **must** be able to explain what each term represents and why hardware-assisted virtualization minimizes $p$ in practice.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
