<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define Virtualization. List any four benefits of server virtualization in modern data centers.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Virtualization is the technique of creating logical, software-based instances of physical computing resources using a hypervisor (VMM), enabling multiple isolated virtual machines to share a single physical hardware platform.
- **Benefits (1.5 Marks — any four):**
  1. Improved hardware utilization (servers run at 60–80% instead of <15%).
  2. Reduced physical footprint, power, and cooling costs.
  3. Faster provisioning of new servers (minutes vs days).
  4. Simplified disaster recovery via VM snapshots and replication.
  5. Application isolation and improved security.
  6. Live migration enables zero-downtime maintenance.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Differentiate between a Type-1 hypervisor and a Type-2 hypervisor with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Type-1 Hypervisor (1.5 Marks):** Runs *directly* on the bare-metal hardware without a host OS. Higher performance, lower overhead, used in enterprise data centers. *Examples: VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Citrix XenServer, KVM.*
- **Type-2 Hypervisor (1.5 Marks):** Runs as an *application on top of an existing host OS*. Easier to set up, used by developers and end-users. *Examples: Oracle VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, Parallels Desktop.*
- **Key Difference:** Type-1 has no host OS layer → better performance; Type-2 has a host OS layer → easier to use but slower.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern (Module 3 — Internal Choice Provided)**

### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a) Explain in detail the architecture and working of a Type-1 hypervisor-based virtualization system. Include a neat diagram. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):** A Type-1 hypervisor, also known as a bare-metal hypervisor, is virtualization software that installs and runs directly on the host's hardware without an intervening host operating system. This eliminates one layer of software indirection, making it the preferred choice for production data centers and cloud infrastructure.

**Architecture (3 Marks):**
1. The hypervisor boots directly from the UEFI/BIOS firmware and assumes full control of the hardware.
2. It includes built-in *resource management subsystems*: a CPU scheduler, a memory manager, an I/O multiplexer, and a network bridge.
3. It exposes Application Programming Interfaces (APIs) to management consoles (e.g., vSphere Client, Hyper-V Manager) for VM lifecycle control.
4. Guest VMs are isolated sandboxes, each with its own virtual BIOS, virtual hardware descriptors, and guest OS kernel.

**Working (2 Marks):**
- The hypervisor intercepts privileged instructions from guest VMs using **trap-and-emulate** or **hardware-assisted virtualization** (Intel VT-x / AMD-V).
- Non-privileged guest instructions execute directly on the physical CPU.
- The hypervisor's **CPU scheduler** time-slices physical cores across multiple vCPUs.
- The **memory manager** maintains shadow page tables or Extended Page Tables (EPT) for memory isolation.
- **I/O virtualization** is performed via emulated devices, paravirtualized drivers (virtio), or direct device assignment (SR-IOV / VT-d).

**Diagram (1 Mark):** *A neat labelled block diagram showing: Physical Hardware → Type-1 Hypervisor → Guest VMs (Linux, Windows, Unix) → Guest Apps.*

**Examples (Optional 0.5–1 Mark):** VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Citrix XenServer, KVM.

---

**(b) Describe the key benefits and challenges of virtualization. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Benefits (3.5 Marks):**
1. **Server Consolidation:** Multiple physical servers can be replaced by VMs on one machine, reducing CapEx and OpEx.
2. **Improved Utilization:** Idle hardware is reclaimed; studies show 60–80% utilization after virtualization.
3. **Isolation & Security:** Each VM is independent; a crash or breach is contained.
4. **Portability:** VMs are hardware-agnostic files; they can be moved between servers with different physical hardware.
5. **Disaster Recovery:** VM snapshots and replication enable near-instant recovery.
6. **DevOps Enablement:** Disposable test environments accelerate software development.
7. **Live Migration:** Workloads can be moved between hosts with zero downtime.

**Challenges (3.5 Marks):**
1. **Performance Overhead:** Although modern hardware-assisted virtualization reduces it to 2–10%, certain I/O-heavy workloads still suffer.
2. **Resource Overcommitment Risks:** Overcommitting RAM or vCPUs can cause contention and unpredictable slowdowns.
3. **Single Point of Failure:** If the hypervisor crashes, all VMs on that host go down.
4. **Licensing Complexity:** Per-VM OS licensing can be expensive (e.g., Windows Server).
5. **Security Hyperjumping:** A compromised VM might (in rare cases) escape to the host — known as *VM escape*.
6. **Storage I/O Bottleneck:** Multiple VMs sharing the same physical disk can create contention.
7. **Management Complexity:** Large VM fleets require sophisticated orchestration tools (vCenter, System Center, OpenStack).

**Conclusion:** Virtualization is the cornerstone of modern computing infrastructure, but its benefits must be carefully weighed against its operational and security challenges.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks [Alternative Choice — KTU University Exam Model]**

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the difference between full virtualization, para-virtualization, and hardware-assisted virtualization. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| **Parameter** | **Full Virtualization** | **Para-virtualization** | **Hardware-Assisted Virtualization** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Guest OS Modification** | None required | Required (uses hypercalls) | None required |
| **Performance** | Lower (binary translation) | Higher (direct hypercalls) | Highest (direct execution) |
| **CPU Support** | Any x86 | Any x86 | Requires Intel VT-x / AMD-V |
| **Examples** | Early VMware Workstation, IBM CP/CMS | Xen (in PV mode), early Denali | Modern KVM, Hyper-V, VMware ESXi |
| **Mechanism** | Binary translation of privileged instructions | Guest makes hypercalls to hypervisor | CPU provides new VMX root/non-root modes |

**Explanation:**
- **Full Virtualization (2 Marks):** The guest OS is *not* modified. The hypervisor translates binary privileged instructions on the fly. Compatible with any OS, but translation overhead reduces performance.
- **Para-virtualization (2 Marks):** The guest OS is *modified* to replace privileged operations with *hypercalls* that communicate directly with the hypervisor. Faster than full virtualization, but requires OS source-code access.
- **Hardware-Assisted Virtualization (2 Marks):** Modern CPUs (Intel VT-x, AMD-V) introduce new execution modes (VMX root and non-root) that allow guest privileged instructions to be trapped natively by the hardware, eliminating the need for binary translation. Best performance, today's industry standard.
- **Diagram (1 Mark):** A side-by-side block diagram comparing the three stacks.

---

**(b) Explain the concept of containers and compare them with virtual machines. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):** Containers are a lightweight alternative to virtual machines. They package an application along with its libraries and dependencies into a single portable unit, while sharing the host OS kernel.

**How Containers Work (2 Marks):**
- A *container engine* (Docker, containerd, Podman) runs on the host OS.
- The engine uses Linux kernel features — **namespaces** (for process, network, and filesystem isolation) and **cgroups** (for resource limits) — to create isolated execution environments.
- All containers on a host *share the same host kernel*; they do not boot a full OS.
- Container images are layered, read-only filesystems with a thin writable top layer.

**Comparison with Virtual Machines (3 Marks):**
- **Architecture:** VMs run a full guest OS on a hypervisor; containers share the host OS kernel.
- **Startup Time:** VMs take 10–30 seconds; containers start in <1 second.
- **Footprint:** VMs are gigabytes in size; containers are typically megabytes.
- **Isolation:** VMs provide stronger isolation (separate kernel); containers provide process-level isolation (weaker).
- **Performance:** Containers have near-native performance; VMs have 2–10% overhead.
- **Use Case:** VMs are best for running different OSes or full servers; containers are best for microservices and cloud-native apps.

**Engineering Relevance (1 Mark):** Modern systems often use *containers-in-VMs* — VMs provide hardware isolation, while containers provide application agility. Kubernetes orchestrates thousands of containers across VM clusters in cloud platforms.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Emulation with Virtualization (–2 Marks):** Emulation translates *all* instructions; virtualization allows direct execution. Students frequently use these terms interchangeably — *don't*.
> 2. **Calling Type-1 a "host OS" (–1 Mark):** Type-1 has *no* host OS. It runs directly on bare metal. Misclassifying this is a top-reason for losing marks.
> 3. **Skipping Diagrams in 7-mark sub-questions (–1 to –2 Marks):** KTU 2024 scheme explicitly awards diagram marks. Always include a labelled block diagram for full credit.
> 4. **Ignoring "real-world example" (–1 Mark):** Examiners reward the inclusion of industry examples (VMware ESXi, KVM, Docker, etc.).
> 5. **Writing unstructured answers:** Use headings, sub-bullets, and tables. Wall-of-text answers are penalized for poor presentation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Virtualization = Abstraction of physical hardware into multiple logical instances.**
- **Hypervisor (VMM)** is the core software that creates and runs VMs.
- **Type-1 (Bare-Metal)**: Runs directly on hardware — used in data centers (ESXi, Hyper-V, Xen, KVM).
- **Type-2 (Hosted)**: Runs on a host OS — used on desktops (VirtualBox, VMware Workstation).
- **Full Virtualization**: Guest unmodified; binary translation of privileged instructions.
- **Para-virtualization**: Guest modified; uses hypercalls for better performance.
- **Hardware-Assisted Virtualization**: Uses Intel VT-x / AMD-V for near-native speed; modern standard.
- **Trap-and-Emulate**: Core mechanism where privileged guest instructions are intercepted and emulated.
- **Live Migration**: Move a running VM between physical hosts with zero downtime.
- **Snapshots**: Point-in-time saved states of a VM — used for backup and rollback.
- **Overcommitment**: Allocating more virtual resources than physically available — increases density but risks contention.
- **Containers vs VMs**: Containers share the host kernel (lightweight, fast); VMs have their own kernel (heavier, stronger isolation).
- **Real-world examples**: AWS EC2 (Xen/Nitro), Azure VMs (Hyper-V), GCP (KVM), Docker, Kubernetes.
- **Key Use Cases**: Cloud computing, server consolidation, DevOps/CI-CD, disaster recovery, security sandboxing, edge computing.
- **Performance Equation**: $T_{\text{vm}} = T_{\text{non-priv}} \cdot (1 - p) + T_{\text{trap-emul}} \cdot p + T_{\text{sched-overhead}}$
- **Isolation**: A hallmark of virtualization — failure in one VM does not affect others.
- **Always draw diagrams** in 7-mark and 14-mark answers for KTU 2024 ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Virtualization at Different Levels and Their Comparison
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Virtualization at Different Levels and Their Comparison

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Virtualization** is the process of creating a virtual (rather than actual) version of something — including virtual computer hardware platforms, storage devices, and computer network resources. It is a framework or methodology that divides the resources of a computer into multiple execution environments, by applying one or more concepts or technologies such as **hardware partitioning, software partitioning, emulation, time-sharing, and quality of service (QoS)**.

In the context of computing systems, virtualization refers to the abstraction of computer resources. It hides the physical characteristics of a computing platform from the users, presenting instead an abstract computing platform. This is achieved by assigning a logical name to a physical storage device and providing a pointer to that physical resource on demand.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Real Estate Analogy:** Imagine a large empty plot of land (the physical hardware). Instead of building one large house for one family, we partition the land into multiple apartments (virtual machines). Each apartment looks, feels, and operates as an independent house — with its own door, electricity meter, water supply, and address — yet they all share the same physical foundation, structural pillars, and utility lines.

To the outside world, each apartment is a self-contained home. But internally, they are all sharing the same physical infrastructure (CPU cycles, memory, storage, network bandwidth). This is exactly what **virtualization** does to a physical computer server — it makes one server *appear* to be many, and makes many smaller resources *appear* to be one large unified resource.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Virtualization

1. **Virtualization Infrastructure** – The underlying physical hardware (Host) that hosts virtual machines.
2. **Virtualization Layer / Hypervisor** – The software layer responsible for creating, running, and managing virtual machines (Guests).
3. **Virtual Machine (VM)** – A software emulation of a physical computer that runs an operating system and applications.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Bold Definitions)

- **Host Machine**: The actual physical machine on which the virtualization takes place.
- **Guest Machine**: The virtual machine that is emulated by the host.
- **Hypervisor (VMM - Virtual Machine Monitor)**: The software/firmware that creates, runs, and manages virtual machines.
- **Emulation**: Imitating the behavior of one system using another system.
- **Abstraction**: Hiding implementation details and exposing only essential features.
- **Isolation**: Ensuring that VMs are independent of one another; failure in one does not affect others.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Virtualization can be performed at **different levels** of a computer system, ranging from the hardware (instruction set) level up to the application (high-level language) level. Each level offers different trade-offs between **performance, flexibility, isolation, and implementation complexity**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Levels of Virtualization — The Taxonomy

Virtualization is performed at different abstraction layers of a computer system. From bottom (closest to hardware) to top (closest to the user), these levels are:

### Level 1: Instruction Set Architecture (ISA) Level
- The entire machine (CPU) is emulated at the **instruction set level**.
- A translation is performed between the **source ISA** (guest) and the **target ISA** (host).
- Example: Transmeta Crusoe CPU translating x86 instructions to VLIW.
- **Use Case:** Running code compiled for a different processor architecture (e.g., ARM apps on x86).

### Level 2: Hardware Abstraction Level (HAL)
- Virtualization at the hardware level — typically using a **Type-1 (bare-metal) or Type-2 (hosted) hypervisor**.
- The hypervisor presents an abstract hardware interface (virtual CPU, virtual RAM, virtual disk) to multiple guest operating systems.
- Example: VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM, Xen.

### Level 3: Operating System (OS) Level
- Virtualization occurs **within a single operating system kernel** — no separate hypervisor.
- The OS kernel itself allows multiple isolated user-space instances called **containers** or **virtual private servers**.
- All guests share the same OS kernel, but each has its own filesystem, users, and processes.
- Example: Solaris Zones/Containers, Linux OpenVZ, Docker (uses OS-level primitives like cgroups & namespaces), FreeBSD Jails.

### Level 4: Library / API Level
- Virtualization intercepts and redirects **API calls** made by an application to the underlying OS.
- Different application environments are created by providing **virtual libraries/API interfaces** that mimic the original.
- Example: WINE (Windows API on Linux), Cygwin (POSIX API on Windows), Java Runtime providing a virtual platform on top of the OS.

### Level 5: User / Application / High-Level Language Level
- Applications are written in a **high-level language** and compiled to an **intermediate representation (bytecode)**.
- A **virtual machine** (language runtime) interprets or JIT-compiles this bytecode on the host.
- The application's behavior is hidden from the underlying OS.
- Example: **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)**, Microsoft Common Language Runtime (CLR), Python Interpreter.

## 2.2 KTU Comparison Cheat Sheet (Level-by-Level)

| Level of Virtualization | What is Abstracted | Performance | Isolation | Flexibility | Implementation Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ISA Level** | Instruction set of the CPU | Moderate to Low | High | Very High | Very High |
| **Hardware Abstraction Level** | Physical hardware (CPU, RAM, Disk, NIC) | Near Native | Very High | Very High | High |
| **Operating System Level** | User-space processes & filesystem | Near Native | Moderate | Moderate | Low |
| **Library / API Level** | System calls and library API calls | Native | Low | Low | Moderate |
| **Application / Language Level** | Application binary / bytecode | Interpreted: Low, JIT: Near Native | Low | Low | Moderate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exams:** Remember the order — **"ISA → HAL → OS → API → App"** (from bottom to top). A common exam question asks to **arrange in increasing order of abstraction** or **decreasing order of performance overhead**.

## 2.3 Detailed Pros and Cons of Each Level

### ISA-Level Virtualization
- ✅ **Pros:** Allows cross-architecture execution; can run software for any CPU on any host.
- ❌ **Cons:** Extremely complex; requires translating every instruction; high performance overhead.
- **Real-world engineering use:** Legacy software preservation; cross-platform emulation (e.g., QEMU).

### Hardware Abstraction Level Virtualization
- ✅ **Pros:** Strong isolation; heterogeneous OS support (Windows + Linux on one server); mature ecosystem.
- ❌ **Cons:** Hypervisor overhead; resource contention; requires hardware-assisted virtualization (Intel VT-x, AMD-V).
- **Real-world engineering use:** Cloud computing (AWS EC2, Azure VMs), data center consolidation.

### OS-Level Virtualization
- ✅ **Pros:** Lightweight; near-native performance; minimal overhead; fast startup.
- ❌ **Cons:** All guests must use the **same OS kernel**; weaker isolation than VMs.
- **Real-world engineering use:** Microservices, container orchestration (Docker + Kubernetes).

### Library-Level Virtualization
- ✅ **Pros:** Allows apps written for one OS to run on another; no need for full VM.
- ❌ **Cons:** API mismatches; incomplete compatibility; maintenance burden.
- **Real-world engineering use:** Cross-platform desktop apps, enterprise middleware.

### Application-Level Virtualization
- ✅ **Pros:** Platform independence ("write once, run anywhere"); safety via sandboxing.
- ❌ **Cons:** Cold-start and interpretation overhead; dependent on VM implementation.
- **Real-world engineering use:** Android Runtime (ART), enterprise Java backends, web browsers (JavaScript engine).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Comparative Derivation

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough — How to Determine Virtualization Level

When a KTU question asks to identify the "level of virtualization" in a given scenario, follow this analytical decision tree:

### Step 1 — Identify the boundary of abstraction
- **Is the CPU instruction set being translated?** → **ISA Level**
- **Is the entire hardware (CPU + RAM + Disk) being abstracted via a hypervisor?** → **HAL Level**
- **Is the OS kernel creating multiple isolated user spaces without a separate hypervisor?** → **OS Level**
- **Are system library calls (POSIX/Win32) being intercepted?** → **Library / API Level**
- **Is a high-level language bytecode being interpreted by a language VM?** → **Application Level**

### Step 2 — Cross-check with the following analytical matrix

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Virtualization Level} &=
\begin{cases}
\text{ISA}, & \text{if Instruction translation is performed} \\
\text{HAL}, & \text{if Hypervisor mediates hardware access} \\
\text{OS}, & \text{if Kernel shares resources via namespaces/cgroups} \\
\text{API}, & \text{if System library calls are remapped} \\
\text{App}, & \text{if Language runtime interprets bytecode} \\
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Verify by performance heuristic

$$
\text{Overhead}_{\text{relative}} : \; \text{OS} \; < \; \text{HAL} \; \approx \; \text{API} \; < \; \text{App (interpreted)} \; < \; \text{ISA}
$$

This ordering is **derived from the abstraction depth**: the deeper the abstraction (closer to hardware = ISA), the larger the translation overhead; the shallower the abstraction (closer to the host OS = OS-level), the smaller the overhead.

### Step 4 — Engineering Decision Logic

For a given problem statement, the choice of virtualization level can be modeled as an optimization:

$$
\text{Choose Level} = \arg\min_{L \in \{\text{ISA, HAL, OS, API, App}\}} \left( \alpha \cdot \text{Overhead}_L + \beta \cdot \text{Complexity}_L - \gamma \cdot \text{Isolation}_L \right)
$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ is the weight given to **performance** (overhead).
- $\beta$ is the weight given to **implementation difficulty** (complexity).
- $\gamma$ is the weight given to **security/isolation**.

This is a weighted-cost decision model often used in **cloud architecture selection**.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Comparison Table (KTU Board-Ready Format)

| Criterion | ISA Level | HAL Level | OS Level | Library Level | App Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Granularity** | Instruction | Hardware device | Process / Container | Function call | Bytecode |
| **Mediator** | Emulator (Binary Translator) | Hypervisor / VMM | Host OS Kernel | Library shim / Wrapper | Language VM (JVM/CLR) |
| **OS Independence** | Yes | Yes | No (Same kernel) | Partial | Yes |
| **Performance Overhead** | High | Moderate | Negligible | Low | Low–Moderate |
| **Isolation Strength** | Strong | Very Strong | Medium | Weak | Weak–Medium |
| **Startup Time** | Slow | Slow | Fast | Fast | Fast |
| **Resource Efficiency** | Poor | Moderate | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Typical Use Case** | Cross-arch emulation | Server consolidation, Cloud | Containers, Microservices | Cross-platform compat | Cross-platform apps |
| **Example Software** | QEMU, Crusoe | VMware, KVM, Hyper-V | Docker, LXC, Solaris Zones | WINE, Cygwin | JVM, .NET CLR |

## 3.3 KTU-Style Numerical / Analytical Problem Walkthrough

> **Problem:** A cloud provider wants to host 500 customer virtual machines on a single physical server. Each customer demands **complete hardware isolation** and may run a different OS (Windows, Linux, BSD). Performance should be **near-native**. Identify the most appropriate virtualization level and justify with two reasons.

### Solution Steps:

1. **Identify requirements:**
   - Need strong isolation → eliminates Library and App level.
   - Different OS per customer → eliminates OS Level (same kernel required).
   - Near-native performance → eliminates pure ISA level.
   - Result: **Hardware Abstraction Level (HAL)** is the correct choice.

2. **Justification:**
   - HAL uses a Type-1 hypervisor (e.g., VMware ESXi) that gives each guest a **virtualized CPU, virtual RAM, virtual disk, and virtual NIC** with strong isolation.
   - Hardware-assisted virtualization (Intel VT-x / AMD-V) reduces the **guest-to-host transition overhead**, providing near-native speed.
   - Examples: AWS EC2, Azure VMs all rely on HAL virtualization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key takeaway for KTU exams:** Always map the *requirement* (isolation, performance, heterogeneity) to the *correct level* (ISA / HAL / OS / API / App). The examiner expects this mapping to be **explicit and justified**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Layered View of Virtualization Levels

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L5 ["Level 5: Application / Language Level"]
        nodeA1["Java Application"]
        nodeA2["Python Application"]
        nodeA3[".NET Application"]
        nodeLVM["Language Virtual Machine - JVM / CLR / Interpreter"]
    end

    subgraph L4 ["Level 4: Library / API Level"]
        nodeL1["User Application"]
        nodeL2["Virtual API Layer - WINE / Cygwin"]
        nodeL3["Host OS Native Calls"]
    end

    subgraph L3 ["Level 3: Operating System Level"]
        nodeO1["Container 1 - Isolated FS / Net / PID"]
        nodeO2["Container 2 - Isolated FS / Net / PID"]
        nodeO3["Container 3 - Isolated FS / Net / PID"]
        nodeOK["Host OS Kernel - Shared"]
    end

    subgraph L2 ["Level 2: Hardware Abstraction Level"]
        nodeV1["Guest VM 1 - OS A"]
        nodeV2["Guest VM 2 - OS B"]
        nodeV3["Guest VM 3 - OS C"]
        nodeHV["Hypervisor - VMM - Type 1 or Type 2"]
    end

    subgraph L1 ["Level 1: Instruction Set Level"]
        nodeIS1["Guest Binary - Source ISA"]
        nodeIS2["Binary Translator"]
        nodeIS3["Host CPU - Target ISA"]
    end

    subgraph L0 ["Physical Hardware Layer"]
        nodeHW["CPU - RAM - Disk - NIC"]
    end

    nodeA1 --> nodeLVM
    nodeA2 --> nodeLVM
    nodeA3 --> nodeLVM
    nodeL1 --> nodeL2
    nodeL2 --> nodeL3
    nodeO1 --> nodeOK
    nodeO2 --> nodeOK
    nodeO3 --> nodeOK
    nodeV1 --> nodeHV
    nodeV2 --> nodeHV
    nodeV3 --> nodeHV
    nodeIS1 --> nodeIS2
    nodeIS2 --> nodeIS3
    nodeLVM --> nodeHW
    nodeL3 --> nodeHW
    nodeOK --> nodeHW
    nodeHV --> nodeHW
    nodeIS3 --> nodeHW
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Logic for Choosing a Virtualization Level

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([System Requirement]) --> q1{Need to run different OS kernels?}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Need strong isolation?}
    q1 -- No --> q3{Sharing same OS kernel acceptable?}
    q3 -- Yes --> q4{Need lightweight deployment?}
    q4 -- Yes --> os[Choose OS Level Virtualization - Docker / LXC]
    q4 -- No --> api{Intercepting system calls?}
    api -- Yes --> lib[Choose Library / API Level - WINE / Cygwin]
    q2 -- Yes --> hal[Choose Hardware Abstraction Level - VMware / KVM]
    q2 -- No --> isa[Choose ISA Level - QEMU / Binary Translation]
    api -- No --> app[Choose Application Level - JVM / .NET CLR]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Block — Trade-offs Summary

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Performance] --> B[ISA Level - Lowest]
    A --> C[OS Level - Highest]
    D[Isolation] --> E[Library Level - Weakest]
    D --> F[HAL Level - Strongest]
    G[Flexibility] --> H[OS Level - Limited to Same Kernel]
    G --> I[HAL Level - Cross OS Support]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Board Exams:** When asked to *draw a block diagram*, examiners expect a clear layering from **Hardware (bottom)** to **User Application (top)**, with the virtualization layer clearly labeled between them.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define virtualization. List any two advantages of server virtualization. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Virtualization** is the creation of a virtual (rather than actual) version of a computing resource, such as a server, desktop, storage device, operating system, or network resources. It uses software to abstract and partition the underlying physical hardware so that multiple operating systems and applications can run concurrently on a single physical machine.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Server Consolidation & Cost Reduction:** Multiple workloads can run on a single physical server, reducing hardware cost, power consumption, and physical space requirements.
2. **Improved Disaster Recovery:** Virtual machines can be snapshotted, cloned, and migrated between hosts easily, enabling faster backup and recovery than physical systems.

*(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for listing any two relevant advantages)*

---

### **Q2.** Differentiate between Type-1 and Type-2 hypervisors with one example each. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Type-1 Hypervisor (Bare-Metal) | Type-2 Hypervisor (Hosted) |
|---|---|---|
| **Position** | Installed directly on physical hardware | Runs as an application on a host OS |
| **Performance** | Better, direct hardware access | Slower, goes through host OS |
| **Use Case** | Enterprise data centers, cloud | Desktop testing, development |
| **Example** | VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Xen | Oracle VirtualBox, VMware Workstation |

Type-1 is used in **enterprise-grade server virtualization**, while Type-2 is used for **development and learning purposes**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** Explain the different levels at which virtualization can be performed in a computer system. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Compare these levels with respect to performance overhead, isolation, and flexibility. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks**

Virtualization can be performed at five different abstraction levels in a computer system:

**1. Instruction Set Architecture (ISA) Level:** *(1.5 marks)*
At this level, the entire CPU instruction set is emulated. Every instruction of the guest (source ISA) is translated into one or more instructions of the host (target ISA). This is used when running software compiled for a different processor architecture (e.g., x86 code on ARM). *Example: QEMU, Transmeta Crusoe.*

**2. Hardware Abstraction Level (HAL):** *(1.5 marks)*
The hypervisor creates a virtual replica of the underlying hardware (virtual CPU, virtual memory, virtual disk, virtual network adapter) and presents it to each guest VM. Multiple guest OS instances run in parallel, unaware of each other. *Example: VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM.*

**3. Operating System Level:** *(1.5 marks)*
The host OS kernel itself partitions the user-space environment into multiple isolated containers, each with its own filesystem, process tree, and network namespace, but sharing the same kernel. *Example: Docker, LXC, Solaris Zones.*

**4. Library / API Level:** *(1 mark)*
A shim layer intercepts application calls to system libraries (e.g., Win32 API) and redirects them to native calls of the host OS. *Example: WINE, Cygwin.*

**5. Application / Language Level:** *(1.5 marks)*
A language runtime (virtual machine) interprets or JIT-compiles application bytecode, abstracting the underlying hardware and OS. *Example: JVM, .NET CLR, Python interpreter.*

### **Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks**

| Level | Performance Overhead | Isolation | Flexibility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ISA** | Highest (every instruction translated) | Very High | Highest (cross-architecture) |
| **HAL** | Moderate (hardware-assisted VT-x/AMD-V) | Very High | High (any OS) |
| **OS Level** | Lowest (kernel-level sharing) | Moderate | Limited (same OS kernel) |
| **Library** | Low | Weak | Low (specific API matching) |
| **Application** | Low–Moderate (JIT helps) | Weak–Medium | High (write once, run anywhere) |

**Analytical Conclusion:** *(2 marks)*
- For **maximum performance** with minor isolation needs → use **OS-level virtualization** (containers).
- For **maximum isolation** with cross-OS needs → use **HAL virtualization** (VMs).
- For **maximum portability** of application code → use **Application-level virtualization** (JVM/CLR).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a Hardware Abstraction Level (HAL) virtualization using a hypervisor. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Discuss why OS-level virtualization is preferred for microservices architecture over HAL-level virtualization. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Diagram:**
*(Refer to the Layer 2 / HAL block from Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

**Working:** *(5 marks)*
1. A **Type-1 (bare-metal) hypervisor** is installed directly on the physical server's hardware.
2. The hypervisor creates multiple **virtual machines (VMs)**, each with a virtual CPU, virtual RAM, virtual disk, and virtual NIC.
3. Each VM runs its **own guest operating system** independently — Windows, Linux, BSD, etc.
4. The hypervisor **schedules CPU cycles, partitions RAM, and arbitrates I/O** requests among all VMs.
5. **Hardware-assisted virtualization extensions** (Intel VT-x, AMD-V) reduce the overhead of VM-Exit and VM-Entry operations.

**Advantages:** *(2 marks)*
- Strong isolation between VMs.
- Heterogeneous OS support.
- Live migration (vMotion) of running VMs across physical hosts.

### **Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks**

OS-level virtualization (containers) is preferred for microservices for the following reasons:

1. **Lightweight Footprint:** *(2 marks)* Containers share the host OS kernel, so they don't need a full guest OS per service. Image size is in MBs vs. GBs for VMs.

2. **Fast Startup & Scaling:** *(2 marks)* Containers start in milliseconds, enabling rapid horizontal scaling of microservices on demand. VMs typically take tens of seconds.

3. **Higher Resource Density:** *(1.5 marks)* Hundreds of containers can run on a single host, while VMs are limited by RAM and CPU overhead.

4. **DevOps & CI/CD Friendly:** *(1.5 marks)* Container images are immutable, version-controlled, and integrate natively with CI/CD pipelines, supporting microservices deployment workflows.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> - ❌ Do NOT confuse **HAL virtualization** with **Type-1 / Type-2 hypervisor classification** — they are related but different concepts.
> - ❌ Do NOT list OS-level and container virtualization as the same as VM virtualization — they differ in **kernel sharing**.
> - ❌ Do NOT forget to mention **hardware-assisted virtualization** (Intel VT-x / AMD-V) when discussing HAL-level performance.
> - ❌ Avoid one-line answers in Part B; KTU expects **diagram + bullet justification** for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Things to Remember Before Entering the Exam Hall:**

- ✅ Virtualization = **abstraction of physical computing resources** to create logical/virtual versions.
- ✅ There are **5 levels of virtualization**: **ISA → HAL → OS → Library/API → Application/Language**.
- ✅ **ISA Level** uses binary translation; highest flexibility but lowest performance.
- ✅ **HAL Level** uses a **hypervisor (VMM)**; gives strong isolation and runs multiple OSes.
- ✅ **Type-1 Hypervisor** = Bare-metal (VMware ESXi); **Type-2 Hypervisor** = Hosted (VirtualBox).
- ✅ **OS Level** uses containers (Docker/LXC); shares kernel, lightweight, fastest.
- ✅ **Library Level** uses API shims (WINE/Cygwin) to remap system calls.
- ✅ **Application Level** uses language VMs (JVM, CLR) to run bytecode portably.
- ✅ **Performance order (best → worst):** OS > API ≈ HAL (HW-assisted) > App (JIT) > ISA.
- ✅ **Isolation order (strongest → weakest):** HAL > ISA > OS > App > Library.
- ✅ Cloud (AWS, Azure) → **HAL virtualization**. Microservices → **OS-level (containers)**.
- ✅ KTU frequently asks **"Compare levels based on ___ "** — always use a **tabular format**.
- ✅ **Mnemonic to remember order:** **"ISA H-A-L O-S A-P-I A-P-P"** → going up the abstraction ladder.
- ✅ Hardware-assisted virtualization (Intel **VT-x**, AMD **AMD-V**) reduces HAL overhead significantly.
- ✅ Always pair **isolation requirement + performance requirement + OS heterogeneity** to pick the correct level.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: VMM Design Requirements, OS Level Virtualization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** VMM Design Requirements, OS Level Virtualization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition: Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM)

A **Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM)** — also commonly called a **Hypervisor** — is a thin, privileged software layer that creates and manages multiple **virtual machines (VMs)** on a single physical host, by multiplexing and isolating the underlying physical hardware resources (CPU, memory, I/O, storage) among them. 

Formally, in the foundational Popek–Goldberg framework (1974), a VMM is defined as:

> *"A VMM is a piece of system software that efficiently virtualizes the underlying machine and provides an environment that is functionally identical to the original bare machine, such that any program running within that environment is unaware that it is being virtualized."*

The VMM retains **exclusive control** over the real hardware, while the guest operating systems inside each VM operate as if they were running directly on bare metal.

## 1.2 Three Classical Properties of a VMM (Popek & Goldberg)

For a VMM to be considered correct, it must satisfy three essential properties:

| Property | Formal Statement | Engineering Meaning |
|----------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Fidelity / Equivalence** | A program running inside a VM must behave **identically** to one running on the equivalent physical machine (modulo timing dependencies). | The guest must "feel" it has real hardware. |
| **Efficiency** | Most guest instructions must execute **directly on the CPU** without software translation/trapping by the VMM. | Minimal virtualization overhead; close to native speed. |
| **Isolation / Safety (Resource Control)** | The VMM must retain **complete control** over all physical resources; no guest can directly alter system resources. | One buggy/malicious VM cannot crash or compromise another. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** The examiner expects you to *state and briefly explain* all three properties. Simply listing them is worth only partial marks; illustrate with examples (e.g., a misbehaving driver in VM-A must not crash VM-B).

## 1.3 What is OS-Level Virtualization?

**OS-Level Virtualization** (also called *containerization* or *operating-system virtualization*) is a lightweight virtualization paradigm in which the **host operating system's kernel** itself is virtualized to allow multiple **isolated user-space instances** (called **containers**, **zones**, **jails**, or **VE — Virtual Environments**) to run concurrently on a single OS instance.

Unlike a VMM-based VM, each container does **not** carry its own kernel. Instead, all containers share the **same host kernel**, and isolation is achieved through OS primitives such as **namespaces** (for resource view isolation) and **cgroups** (for resource usage control).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Apartment Building vs. The Hotel"**
>
> - **Traditional VMs (VMM-based)** are like **independent houses built on a single plot of land**. Each house has its own foundation, walls, plumbing, and roof (i.e., its own guest OS, drivers, kernel). The construction supervisor is the VMM (hypervisor).
>
> - **OS-level virtualization (Containers)** is like a **high-rise apartment building on a single foundation**. There is one shared foundation (host kernel), but each apartment (container) has its own walls, locks, and utilities — appearing fully independent to the resident, but actually sharing the building's underlying infrastructure.
>
> Containers boot in **seconds** and consume only **megabytes**, whereas full VMs take **minutes** to boot and consume **gigabytes** because each VM must boot its own kernel.

## 1.4 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Terms

These are the standard engineering metrics used when discussing virtualization design:

- **Memory Overhead of VMM:** typically **0.5% – 3%** of total host RAM.
- **CPU Performance Overhead:** **2% – 10%** with hardware-assisted virtualization (Intel VT-x / AMD-V); up to **20–30%** for pure software emulation (e.g., QEMU without KVM).
- **Container Boot Time:** **< 1 second** (vs. 30–60 s for a full VM).
- **Container Image Size:** typically **5 – 500 MB** (vs. **1 – 20 GB** for VM images).
- **Sensitive Instruction:** an instruction whose behavior or result depends on the value of the privilege mode or on the contents of relocation/limit registers.
- **Privileged Instruction:** an instruction that traps (causes an exception) if executed in user mode.
- **Popek–Goldberg Theorem (1974):** the foundational mathematical criterion that determines whether a given CPU architecture can be fully virtualized.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of CPU Instructions (Foundation of VMM Design)

To design a VMM, the architect must first **classify every CPU instruction** into one of three buckets. The Popek–Goldberg theorem is built on this taxonomy:

### 2.1.1 The Three Instruction Classes

**Class 1 — Privileged Instructions**
An instruction is *privileged* if attempting to execute it in user (unprivileged) mode generates a **trap** to the supervisor/kernel. Examples on x86: `LGDT`, `LIDT`, `LTR`, `HLT`, `MOV CR0,…`, `IN`, `OUT`, `CLI`, `STI`, `IRET` (in some configurations).

**Class 2 — Control-Sensitive Instructions**
An instruction whose behavior or result **depends on the contents of the privilege-sensitive registers** (e.g., the processor's current privilege level, the code-segment descriptor's DPL field, the contents of control registers CR0/CR2/CR3/CR4, or the interrupt-enable flag IF).

*Example:* `PUSH CS` (on x86) behaves differently in user mode vs. kernel mode — it pushes a different value of CS.

**Class 3 — Behaviour-Sensitive Instructions**
An instruction whose behavior or result depends on the **mode** (kernel vs. user) **OR** on the values in relocation/base registers, limit registers, or other virtualization-relevant state. On x86, the classic example is `POPF` — in user mode it silently ignores attempts to set the IF (interrupt flag) bit, but in kernel mode it actually flips it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensitive Instructions = Control-Sensitive ∪ Behaviour-Sensitive.** These are the *dangerous* ones. Privileged instructions are *easy* to virtualize (they trap automatically). The hard problem is virtualizing **non-privileged sensitive** instructions — those that exhibit different behavior in user mode but do **not** trap. The x86 architecture has 17 such instructions (e.g., `SMSW`, `SGDT`, `SIDT`, `SLDT`, `STR`, `LAR`, `LSL`, `VERR`, `VERW`, `POPF`, `PUSHF`, etc.).

### 2.1.2 The Popek–Goldberg Theorem (1974)

For a given CPU architecture, let:

- $U$ = set of all user-mode instructions (unprivileged ISA subset).
- $P$ = set of privileged instructions ($P \subset U$).
- $S$ = set of sensitive instructions.

**Theorem Statement (Two-Part Criterion):**

A CPU architecture is **virtualizable** (i.e., a VMM can be constructed satisfying all three classical properties) **if and only if**:

$$
S \subseteq P
$$

In plain words: **every sensitive instruction must also be a privileged instruction.** Equivalently, the set of *non-privileged sensitive* instructions must be **empty**.

### 2.1.3 Why x86 Failed Classic Virtualization (and How It Was Fixed)

The x86 (IA-32) instruction set does **NOT** satisfy $S \subseteq P$. There exist at least **17 ring-3 executable instructions** that read or modify privileged state without trapping (e.g., `SMSW`, `SGDT`, `SIDT`, `SLDT`, `STR`, `POPF`, `PUSHF`, `VERR`, `VERW`, `LAR`, `LSL`, …).

Two historical engineering solutions:

1. **Full Virtualization via Binary Translation (Software Solution)** — Pioneered by VMWare ESX. The VMM **dynamically translates** guest binary at run time, replacing every non-privileged sensitive instruction with safe trap-inducing equivalents. Costly: code-cache overhead, high engineering complexity.

2. **Paravirtualization** — Pioneered by Xen (Cambridge, 2003). Modify the guest kernel to **replace** all non-virtualizable instructions with **hypercalls** (explicit synchronous calls into the VMM). Cost: requires guest OS source-code access (open source).

3. **Hardware-Assisted Virtualization (HAV)** — Modern solution. Intel VT-x (IVT, 2005) and AMD-V (Pacifica, 2006) introduce a **new privilege mode** (ring -1) and **shadow control structures** (VMCS on Intel, VMCB on AMD). Non-privileged sensitive instructions now trap automatically, making $S \subseteq P$ hold in practice.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Every cloud VM you have ever used (AWS EC2, Azure, GCP Compute Engine, Oracle Cloud) relies on either Intel VT-x or AMD-V. Without hardware-assisted virtualization, public cloud at today's scale would be economically infeasible.

## 2.2 Design Requirements of a VMM (KTU Exam Focus)

For the KTU 2024 scheme, the examiner expects you to enumerate and explain **seven** core design requirements. These are derived from the Popek–Goldberg framework and operational practice:

### 2.2.1 The Seven VMM Design Requirements

| # | Requirement | Engineering Sub-Task |
|---|-------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **CPU Virtualization** | Schedule and emulate vCPUs; trap-and-emulate sensitive instructions; maintain per-VM register state. |
| 2 | **Memory Virtualization** | Use a **shadow page table** or **EPT/NPT (Extended Page Tables / Nested Page Tables)** to translate GVA → GPA → HPA. |
| 3 | **I/O Virtualization** | Emulate devices (NIC, disk, console); can use direct assignment (PCI passthrough / SR-IOV) for high performance. |
| 4 | **Device-Model Consistency** | Guest-visible hardware should match a real architecture (e.g., Intel e1000 NIC, LSI 53C895 SCSI) so that guest drivers run unmodified. |
| 5 | **Isolation & Security** | Enforce per-VM resource quotas; prevent covert channels (e.g., cache-based timing attacks like Flush+Reload). |
| 6 | **Performance & Efficiency** | Avoid excessive VM-exits; use **VT-d, APICv, posted interrupts**; batched MMIO. Goal: < 5% overhead for CPU-bound workloads. |
| 7 | **Live Migration / Save-Restore** | Transfer running VM between hosts with sub-second downtime (e.g., KVM live migration uses pre-copy iterative dirty-page transfer). |

### 2.2.2 Hypervisor Classification: Type 1 vs Type 2

| Attribute | **Type 1 (Bare-Metal)** | **Type 2 (Hosted)** |
|-----------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Runs as | OS itself (on bare metal) | Application atop host OS |
| Performance | **Higher** (no host OS overhead) | Lower (host syscall overhead) |
| Use case | Data-center / cloud | Desktop / dev / testing |
| Examples | VMware ESXi, Xen, Microsoft Hyper-V Server, KVM (when configured as host) | VMware Workstation, VirtualBox, Parallels, QEMU (user mode) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** KVM is technically a kernel module of Linux, but in KTU 2024 scheme answers, classify KVM as **Type 1** because the Linux kernel it extends is *itself* the VMM in production deployments. State your assumption clearly.

## 2.3 OS-Level Virtualization — Deep Dive

### 2.3.1 Core OS Primitives Used

Two Linux kernel features (introduced circa 2006–2008) make modern containerization possible:

**(a) Namespaces** — *What a container can SEE*
A namespace wraps a global system resource in an abstraction layer so that processes inside the namespace appear to have their own isolated instance. Linux supports **8 namespace types**:

| Namespace | Flag | Isolates |
|-----------|------|----------|
| Mount | `CLONE_NEWNS` | Filesystem mount points |
| UTS | `CLONE_NEWUTS` | Hostname, NIS domain name |
| IPC | `CLONE_NEWIPC` | System V IPC, POSIX message queues |
| PID | `CLONE_NEWPID` | Process IDs (PIDs) |
| Network | `CLONE_NEWNET` | Network devices, sockets, routes |
| User | `CLONE_NEWUSER` | User and group IDs |
| Cgroup | `CLONE_NEWCGROUP` | cgroup root directory |
| Time | `CLONE_NEWTIME` | Boot time, monotonic clock |

**(b) Control Groups (cgroups)** — *What a container can USE*
cgroups limit, account for, and isolate resource usage (CPU, memory, disk I/O, network bandwidth, device access) of a collection of processes.

### 2.3.2 Container Lifecycle on Linux

```
Container start  →  clone(3) with CLONE_NEW* flags  →  setrlimit() / cgroup attach  →  chroot() or pivot_root()  →  execve() user command
```

### 2.3.3 Comparison Matrix: VM vs Container

| Dimension | **Full VM (VMM-based)** | **Container (OS-level)** |
|-----------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| Boot time | 30–60 s | < 1 s |
| Disk footprint | 1–20 GB | 5–500 MB |
| Own kernel? | **Yes** | **No (shares host kernel)** |
| Isolation strength | **Strong** (hardware boundary) | Weaker (kernel shared) |
| Max density (per host) | ~50–100 | ~1000+ |
| Cross-OS support | Yes (Linux on Windows, etc.) | **No** (kernel-bound) |
| Performance overhead | 2–10 % (HAV) | < 1 % |
| Security boundary | Hypervisor | Linux kernel + LSM (AppArmor/SELinux) |
| Examples | VMware, KVM, Hyper-V | Docker, LXC, Podman, runC |

## 2.4 KTU Formula & Concept Sheet (High-Yield Cheat-Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Although virtualization is conceptually heavy, a few compact formulas appear repeatedly in KTU numericals (especially memory-virtualization overhead and migration downtime).

| Concept | Equation | Variables / Units | Notes |
|---------|----------|--------------------|-------|
| Popek–Goldberg virtualizability condition | $S \subseteq P$ | $S$ = sensitive set, $P$ = privileged set | Both sets are subsets of the ISA. |
| EPT/NPT TLB miss cost | $T_{miss} = T_{L2} + T_{phys} \approx 25\text{–}40 \text{ cycles}$ | cycles per nested walk | Compare to native 4-level walk $\approx 12$ cycles. |
| Memory overhead (shadow page table) | $M_{overhead} = \dfrac{N_{VM} \times S_{PT}}{M_{host}} \times 100\%$ | $N_{VM}$ = VMs, $S_{PT}$ = per-VM PT size | Used in shadow-PT scheme (legacy). |
| Live-migration downtime bound | $T_{down} \approx \dfrac{S_{dirty}}{B_{link}}$ | $S_{dirty}$ = dirty-page bytes during stop-and-copy, $B_{link}$ = link bandwidth (bytes/s) | Bound the stop-and-copy phase. |
| CPU virtualization overhead (pure emulation) | $O_{CPU} = \dfrac{N_{trap} \times T_{trap}}{T_{prog}} \times 100\%$ | $N_{trap}$ = traps emitted, $T_{trap}$ = trap cost, $T_{prog}$ = total program time | Why HAV dominates classical software emulation. |
| Container density gain | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{container}}{N_{VM}}$ | dimensionless ratio | Typically $\eta \in [5, 50]$ on the same hardware. |
| Address translation (virtualized) | $GVA \xrightarrow{\text{guest PT}} GPA \xrightarrow{\text{EPT}} HPA$ | 3-tuple virtual → guest-physical → host-physical | EPT/NPT is the modern mechanism. |

**Boundary conditions and rules of thumb:**

- If $S \subseteq P$ holds for the ISA, **hardware-assisted virtualization is unnecessary** in principle (RISC-V, MIPS, ARM in early versions all qualify).
- The memory overhead of EPT itself is $\approx 1\text{ MB}$ per VM in worst-case page-walk working set.
- A container can never safely host a kernel-mode attack against the host kernel — security boundary must be enforced by **seccomp-bpf**, **AppArmor/SELinux**, or **gVisor / Kata Containers** (a hybrid bringing VM-level isolation to containers).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Popek–Goldberg Theorem (The Equivalence Lemma)

We derive the necessity of $S \subseteq P$ step by step.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Definitions

Let $M$ be the original (real) machine with instruction set $I$, and let $V$ be a virtual machine. We want $V$ to be **behaviorally identical** to $M$ for every user program.

**Definition 3.1** (Sensitive Instruction): An instruction $i \in I$ is *sensitive* if its behavior or its result depends on either (a) the current **privilege mode** of the CPU, or (b) the contents of **relocation/limit/control registers** that govern addressing or protection.

Let:
- $P$ = set of all privileged instructions (those that trap in user mode).
- $S$ = set of all sensitive instructions.
- $U$ = set of all user-allowed (unprivileged) instructions; $P \subseteq U$.

### 3.1.2 Proof Sketch (Necessity): $S \subseteq P$

We prove by **contradiction**. Assume there exists a sensitive instruction $s \in S$ such that $s \notin P$.

**Step 1.** Since $s \notin P$, executing $s$ in user mode does **not** trap. So the guest can issue $s$ without VMM interception.

**Step 2.** Because $s$ is sensitive, the *result* of $s$ in user mode differs from the *result* of $s$ in supervisor mode. (Otherwise, by Definition 3.1, $s$ would not be sensitive.)

**Step 3.** Suppose the guest issues $s$ while running unprivileged code. The real hardware executes $s$ in user mode, producing **Result A**.

**Step 4.** But the *correct* behavior (what the guest expects, since it believes it has full hardware control) is **Result B** — the value $s$ would produce in supervisor mode.

**Step 5.** Without trapping, the VMM **cannot intervene** to substitute Result B for Result A. Therefore the guest sees Result A.

**Step 6.** A guest program that branches on the result of $s$ will therefore take a different branch than it would on the real machine. **This violates the Equivalence (Fidelity) property.**

Contradiction. Therefore $S \subseteq P$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.1.3 Sufficiency Sketch: If $S \subseteq P$, VMM Exists

Assume $S \subseteq P$. Then every sensitive instruction is privileged, so any guest execution of a sensitive instruction in user mode **traps** to the VMM. The VMM can:

1. Inspect the trapped instruction and the guest's privileged state.
2. Emulate the instruction's supervisor-mode behavior using the real hardware.
3. Resume the guest with the correct state.

Non-sensitive instructions are by definition behavior-invariant under mode change, so they can execute natively without intervention. **Efficiency is preserved.**

### 3.1.4 Worked Example: Verifying $S \subseteq P$ on RISC-V RV64

On RISC-V RV64 (privileged spec 1.13), examine all instructions that read/write the `sstatus`, `sepc`, `stvec`, `scause`, `satp` registers and all CSR instructions.

**Step A.** Every CSR access in user mode (U-mode) to an `s*` or `m*` CSR triggers an **illegal-instruction exception** — these are privileged.

**Step B.** The only "sensitive-looking" behavior in RISC-V is virtual-memory translation, fully controlled by `satp` (CSR 0x180). Reading `satp` from U-mode is illegal → traps.

**Step C.** No instruction in the base RV64I/M/A/F/D/C ISA produces a different *result* in U-mode vs M/S-mode without trapping.

**Conclusion:** $S = \varnothing$ (for the privileged-relevant subset) and trivially $S \subseteq P$. Hence **RISC-V is fully virtualizable** — the reason it is the architecture of choice for embedded/edge-cloud workloads.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical: EPT Page-Table Memory Overhead

A host runs 32 VMs simultaneously. Each guest uses a 4-level page table with 512 entries per level (4 KB pages, 8-byte PTE). Estimate the worst-case shadow-page-table memory overhead for the **guest PTEs only** (ignore EPT layer for now).

**Given:**
- $N_{VM} = 32$ VMs.
- Page size $= 4 \text{ KB} = 4096 \text{ bytes}$.
- PTE size $= 8 \text{ bytes}$.
- Entries per page-table page $= 4096 / 8 = 512$.

**Step 1.** Compute guest-page-table depth.

$$
\text{Address bits to translate} = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{2^{48} \text{ virtual bytes}}{4096}\right) = 48 - 12 = 36 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
\text{Levels needed} = \left\lceil \dfrac{36}{9} \right\rceil = 4 \text{ levels (each level indexes 9 bits)}
$$

**Step 2.** Per-VM maximum PT size (assuming full virtual-address space is mapped):

$$
S_{PT} = 4 \text{ levels} \times 1 \text{ page} = 4 \times 4 \text{ KB} = 16 \text{ KB}
$$

**Step 3.** Total shadow-PT memory:

$$
M_{total} = N_{VM} \times S_{PT} = 32 \times 16 \text{ KB} = 512 \text{ KB}
$$

**Step 4.** Host RAM available for guests, say $M_{host} = 64 \text{ GB}$:

$$
\% M_{overhead} = \dfrac{512 \text{ KB}}{64 \text{ GB}} \times 100 \approx 7.6 \times 10^{-4}\,\% \approx 0.00076\%
$$

This is **negligible** — explaining why EPT/NPT is preferred over shadow page tables in modern KVM/Hyper-V.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In early VMware ESX (without EPT), the shadow page-table memory overhead could reach **0.5–2 %** of host RAM because the VMM had to maintain a *separate, completely populated* shadow PT for every guest, eagerly translating on every guest page-table write. Modern hardware-assisted nested paging eliminated this entire class of overhead.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Sensitivity Classification of Selected x86 Instructions

The KTU examiner loves a table that classifies x86 instructions. Here is an authoritative classification:

| Instruction | Privileged? | Sensitive? | Reason | VMM Action |
|-------------|-------------|------------|--------|------------|
| `HLT` | **Yes** | Yes | Halt is supervisor-only | Trap-and-emulate (idle the vCPU) |
| `IN` / `OUT` | Yes | Yes | I/O port access | Emulate PIO or use VT-d passthrough |
| `MOV CR0, reg` | **Yes** | Yes | Writes control register | Trap, validate, reflect |
| `LGDT` / `LIDT` / `LLDT` / `LTR` | **Yes** | Yes | Load descriptor tables | Trap-and-emulate (inject shadow values) |
| `CPUID` | No | **Yes** | Returns different family/model in VM | Trap (force CPUID), emulate; bit-mask features |
| `RDTSC` | No | **Yes** | Reads time-stamp counter (different in VM) | Trap, offset, return virtual TSC |
| `SMSW` (Store Machine Status Word) | No | **Yes** | Reads CR0 bits without trapping | Classic problem; VMM must trap via PATCH or HAV |
| `SGDT` / `SIDT` / `SLDT` / `STR` | No | **Yes** | Leak hidden base of descriptor tables | Trap on HAV (since 2006); emulated pre-HAV |
| `POPF` | No | **Yes** | IF flag ignored in user mode but set in kernel | Trap-and-emulate on legacy x86 |
| `PUSHF` | No | **Yes** | Reads VM flag (bit 17) only visible at CPL=0 | Trap-and-emulate (mask bit 17) |
| `INTO` / `INT 3` / `INT n` | No | Yes | Software interrupt | Trap-and-emulate |
| `MOV DRx, reg` (Debug Registers) | **Yes** | Yes | Hardware debug only allowed in ring 0 | Trap |

The **non-privileged sensitive** instructions (`SMSW`, `SGDT`, `SIDT`, `SLDT`, `STR`, `POPF`, `PUSHF`, `CPUID`, `RDTSC`, `RDPMC`, `VERR`, `VERW`, `LAR`, `LSL`, …) collectively are the **17 problematic instructions** that prevent naive VMM design on pre-HAV x86.


**Reading the diagram:** Every time the guest issues a sensitive/privileged instruction, the CPU does an automatic **VM Exit** (typically 500–1000 cycles). The VMM emulates, then a **VM Entry** (also ~500 cycles) returns control. With Intel VT-x, the non-privileged sensitive instructions now also trap, so the loop in Section 3.1 no longer bites.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    GPT["Guest Page Table 4 levels CR3 rooted guest physical"]:::b
    GPA["GPA Guest Physical Address 48 bit"]:::c

    HPA["HPA Host Physical Address 52 bit"]:::e
    Phys["Actual DRAM cell"]:::f

```

The **nested walk** shown above is performed entirely by hardware (MMU) on modern Intel/AMD CPUs — no VMM intervention needed. This is the single most important reason hardware-assisted virtualization reclaimed the performance lost to shadow page tables.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**Differentiate between full virtualization and paravirtualization. Which approach requires modification of the guest operating system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Full Virtualization | Paravirtualization |
|--------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Guest modification | **No** modification needed | Guest OS **kernel is modified** |
| Mechanism | Trap-and-emulate; binary translation; HAV | Replaces non-virtualizable instructions with **hypercalls** to VMM |
| Performance | Good with HAV (2–10% overhead) | Better in some workloads (~5%) |
| Portability | Works with closed-source OS (Windows) | Only for open-source OS (Linux, NetBSD) |
| Example | VMware ESXi, KVM, Hyper-V | Classic Xen (pre-HAV) |

**Conclusion:** Paravirtualization requires guest OS modification. Full virtualization does not.

*(1 mark each for: definition + one key difference, guest-modification clarification, example.)*

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**List any three design requirements of a Virtual Machine Monitor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per requirement):**

1. **Fidelity** — Guest program behavior identical to that on real hardware.
2. **Efficiency** — Most guest instructions must execute natively without VMM intervention.
3. **Isolation / Resource Control** — VMM retains complete control; guest cannot directly affect physical resources.

*(Examiner's note: also accept CPU virtualization, memory virtualization, I/O virtualization, security, and live-migration as valid 3 of 7 design requirements.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B-A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Module 3 Internal Choice]

**(a) [7 marks]** *State and explain the Popek–Goldberg virtualization theorem. With the help of a Venn diagram representation, classify the instruction sets P (privileged) and S (sensitive), and justify why the IA-32 (x86) architecture is **not** classically virtualizable. List at least four x86 instructions that fail the condition.*

**(b) [7 marks]** *Explain in detail how **hardware-assisted virtualization** (Intel VT-x) overcomes the limitations of classical x86 virtualization. Describe the roles of **VMCS** and **VMX** transitions. Also, briefly compare Type 1 and Type 2 hypervisors with two examples each.*

---

### Model Solution to Question B-A (a) — [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Theorem Statement [2 marks]:**

Popek and Goldberg (1974) proved that for any given CPU architecture, a Virtual Machine Monitor can be constructed to satisfy all three classical properties (Fidelity, Efficiency, Isolation) **if and only if** the set of sensitive instructions is a subset of the set of privileged instructions:

$$
S \subseteq P
$$

where
- $S$ = set of sensitive instructions (those whose behavior depends on privilege mode or control-register state),
- $P$ = set of privileged instructions (those that trap in user mode).

**Step 2 — Venn Diagram Description [1 mark]:**

[Draw two overlapping circles inside a rectangle representing the universe $U$ of all user-mode-allowed instructions. The intersection $P \cap S$ is the *trap-and-emulatable* zone; the crescent $S \setminus P$ is the *danger zone* containing non-privileged sensitive instructions. When $S \setminus P = \varnothing$, the entire sensitive set is inside the privileged set, and the architecture is virtualizable. See Diagram 1 in Section 4 for the Mermaid version.]

**Step 3 — Why x86 Fails [3 marks]:**

The x86 (IA-32) instruction set defines **17 non-privileged sensitive instructions** that read or modify privileged state without trapping when executed in ring 3. This makes $S \setminus P \neq \varnothing$, violating the Popek–Goldberg condition.

Examples (list any four):

1. `SGDT` / `SIDT` / `SLDT` / `STR` — leak the base address of the GDT, IDT, LDT, or TSS register respectively, without trapping in ring 3. A guest can therefore inspect real-machine hidden state.
2. `SMSW` — stores the low 16 bits of `CR0` (PE, MP, EM, TS, ET bits) to a general-purpose register, allowing the guest to detect whether it is running on a real machine or inside a VMM (the classic "red-pill" anti-VM trick).
3. `POPF` — in ring 3, attempts to set the Interrupt Flag (IF) bit in EFLAGS are silently ignored; but in ring 0, the IF bit is actually flipped. Hence result differs by privilege mode.
4. `CPUID` — returns different CPU family/model/stepping values and feature bits depending on whether the processor is "real" or virtualized. (Historically used for VMWare detection.)
5. *(Additional, for completeness)* `PUSHF`, `RDTSC`, `RDPMC`, `VERR`, `VERW`, `LAR`, `LSL`, `IN`, `OUT` (in some configurations).

**Step 4 — Consequence [1 mark]:**

Because these instructions do not trap, the VMM cannot intercept and emulate them. The guest's view of hardware state will diverge from that of a real machine, violating the **Fidelity** property. Hence x86 in its pre-VT-x form is **not classically (Popek–Goldberg) virtualizable**.

---

### Model Solution to Question B-A (b) — [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Hardware-Assisted Virtualization Introduction [1 mark]:**

Intel introduced **VT-x** (Virtualization Technology, codename *Vanderpool*) in 2005; AMD introduced **AMD-V** (codename *Pacifica*) in 2006. Both define a **new CPU execution mode**, the **VMX (Virtual Machine Extensions)** mode, that introduces a fourth privilege level below ring 0, conventionally called **"ring -1"**. This new ring hosts the VMM directly with full hardware privilege.

**Step 2 — VMCS (Virtual Machine Control Structure) [3 marks]:**

The **VMCS** is a 4 KB-aligned data structure in host memory that holds **all** guest and host CPU state. It is the formal interface between hardware and VMM.

The VMCS is logically partitioned into six groups:

| Group | Contents | Use |
|-------|----------|-----|
| Guest-state area | Guest RIP, RSP, RFLAGS, segment regs, CR0/CR3/CR4, etc. | Loaded on VM Entry |
| Host-state area | Host RIP, RSP, segment regs, CR0/CR3/CR4, etc. | Loaded on VM Exit |
| VM-execution controls | Pin-based, processor-based, exception bitmap, I/O bitmap, etc. | What causes a VM Exit |
| VM-exit controls | Define which state is saved on exit | Configuration |
| VM-entry controls | Define state to be loaded on entry | Configuration |
| VM-exit information | Reason for exit, exit qualification, I/O details | Diagnose why VMM got control |

**Step 3 — VMX Transitions [2 marks]:**

- **VM Entry:** `VMLAUNCH` (first time) or `VMRESUME` (subsequent). The CPU atomically loads the guest-state area from the VMCS into the real CPU registers and begins guest execution in VMX non-root mode.
- **VM Exit:** Triggered automatically by hardware on any of the configured events (I/O, interrupt, exception, CR access, MSR access, INVD, HLT, etc.). The CPU atomically saves guest state into the guest-area and loads the host-state area into real registers, then transfers control to the host VMM entry point.

**Performance gain:** Because non-privileged sensitive instructions (e.g., `SGDT`, `SIDT`, `SMSW`) can now be configured to cause VM Exits, the x86 architecture effectively satisfies $S \subseteq P$ in hardware.

**Step 4 — Type 1 vs Type 2 Hypervisors [1 mark]:**

| | Type 1 (Bare-Metal) | Type 2 (Hosted) |
|--|---------------------|------------------|
| Runs on | Bare hardware directly | As an application over host OS |
| Examples | VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Xen | Oracle VirtualBox, VMware Workstation |

---

### Question B-B. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model — Alternative Internal Choice]

**(a) [7 marks]** *Explain **OS-level virtualization** in detail. With a neat diagram, describe how **Linux namespaces** and **cgroups** together provide process isolation and resource control. List the eight Linux namespace types with their isolation roles.*

**(b) [7 marks]** *Compare and contrast **OS-level virtualization (containers)** with **hardware-assisted VM virtualization** along the dimensions of: (i) kernel sharing, (ii) boot time, (iii) memory overhead, (iv) isolation strength, (v) cross-OS portability, and (vi) maximum container/VM density per host. Why are containers not considered suitable for hosting a different operating system kernel?*

---

### Model Solution to Question B-B (a) — [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Definition [1 mark]:**

OS-level virtualization is a server-virtualization method in which the **kernel of the host operating system** is itself virtualized to allow multiple **isolated user-space instances (containers)** to run on a single host OS. All containers **share the host kernel**; isolation is enforced by OS-level primitives (namespaces + cgroups + LSMs).

**Step 2 — Two Pillars [1 mark]:**

- **Namespaces** (what a container can **see**).
- **Control Groups / cgroups** (what a container can **use**).

**Step 3 — Namespaces Deep Dive [3 marks]:**

A namespace wraps a global system resource so that processes inside the namespace see an *isolated instance* of that resource. The eight namespace types and their isolation role:

| # | Namespace | Clone Flag | Isolates |
|---|-----------|------------|----------|
| 1 | Mount | `CLONE_NEWNS` | Filesystem mount points |
| 2 | UTS | `CLONE_NEWUTS` | Hostname & NIS domain name |
| 3 | IPC | `CLONE_NEWIPC` | System V IPC, POSIX message queues |
| 4 | PID | `CLONE_NEWPID` | Process IDs (PIDs) |
| 5 | Network | `CLONE_NEWNET` | Network devices, sockets, IP routing tables |
| 6 | User | `CLONE_NEWUSER` | User IDs and Group IDs |
| 7 | Cgroup | `CLONE_NEWCGROUP` | Cgroup root directory |
| 8 | Time | `CLONE_NEWTIME` | `CLOCK_BOOTTIME`, `CLOCK_MONOTONIC` |

Implementation note: A process acquires a namespace view by passing the corresponding `CLONE_NEW*` flag to the `clone(2)` system call. The first PID in a `CLONE_NEWPID` namespace sees PID 1 (its init), and the host sees this container's processes under different PIDs in the parent namespace — this is the foundation of Docker/Podman.

**Step 4 — cgroups Deep Dive [1.5 marks]:**

cgroups (introduced by Paul Menage, 2006) limit, account for, and isolate resource usage. cgroup v2 (unified hierarchy) exposes controllers:

- `cpu`, `cpuacct` — CPU shares and accounting.
- `memory` — RSS, cache, swap limits; OOM-killer policy.
- `blkio` / `io` — block I/O bandwidth/IOPS.
- `pids` — number of processes/threads.
- `devices` — whitelist/blacklist of device nodes.
- `net_cls`, `net_prio` — network class ID and priority.
- `freezer` — suspend/resume process group (used in live container migration).
- `rdma`, `misc` — RDMA and miscellaneous controller.

**Step 5 — Security Layer [0.5 mark]:**

Even with namespaces and cgroups, a kernel-exploiting container can compromise the host kernel. Hence, production deployments layer **LSMs (Linux Security Modules)** such as **AppArmor** or **SELinux**, and **seccomp-bpf** syscall filters to reduce the kernel attack surface. Newer runtimes such as **gVisor** and **Kata Containers** further narrow the surface by intercepting syscalls in user space or running each container in a lightweight VM.

---

### Model Solution to Question B-B (b) — [7 marks]

**Six-dimension comparison table [4 marks — distribute 0.5 to 0.75 per dimension]:**

| Dimension | Container (OS-level) | Full VM (HAV) |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------|
| (i) Kernel sharing | **Shared host kernel** | Each VM has its **own guest kernel** |
| (ii) Boot time | **< 1 second** (process spawn) | **30 – 60 seconds** (firmware + bootloader + kernel + services) |
| (iii) Memory overhead | ~10–50 MB per container (no separate kernel) | 256 MB – 4 GB per VM (kernel + drivers + services) |
| (iv) Isolation strength | **Weaker** — kernel-level breach = host compromise | **Stronger** — separate kernels; hardware boundary at VT-x/EPT |
| (v) Cross-OS portability | **No** — host-kernel bound (Linux on Linux, Windows on Windows) | **Yes** — Linux VM on Windows host, FreeBSD on macOS, etc. |
| (vi) Max density per host | **1000+ containers** on commodity server | **50–100 VMs** on same hardware |

**Numerical illustration [1.5 marks]:**

On a 64 GB / 32-vCPU host:
- VMs: $\approx \lfloor 32 / 2 \rfloor = 16$ VMs (assuming 2 vCPU each) with realistic overhead.
- Containers: $\approx 32 \times 8 = 256$ containers at 1 GB each — **density gain of 16×** at much faster startup.

**Why containers cannot host a different OS kernel [1.5 marks]:**

Containers share the host kernel and execute system calls *directly on it*. If you tried to run a Windows container on a Linux host (or vice-versa), the container's applications would issue Windows NT system calls, but the Linux kernel would **not recognize** them (different ABI, different data structures, different semantics). Therefore, a container can only run workloads whose system calls match the host kernel's ABI. To run a different OS, you must use a **VM** (with its own kernel) or a user-mode emulation layer (Wine, WSL2's hidden Hyper-V VM, etc.).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls Students Lose Marks On:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "sensitive" with "privileged".** These are *different* sets. Sensitive depends on *behaviour* (mode/state); privileged depends on *trapping*. Most students write "sensitive = privileged" — that is exactly the wrong direction of understanding.
> 2. **Writing $S = P$ instead of $S \subseteq P$.** $S \subseteq P$ allows $P$ to be a *strict superset* (containing non-sensitive privileged instructions too). Marks lost for using equality.
> 3. **Listing only ring-3 vs ring-0 for x86.** The "17 problematic instructions" must be enumerated (or at least 4 named) for full marks in question (a) of the 14-mark choice.
> 4. **Calling KVM a Type-2 hypervisor.** Wrong in production deployment; state assumption clearly.
> 5. **Stating that containers are "more secure" than VMs.** False by default. VMs offer stronger isolation. Containers are smaller attack surface per service but share the kernel, which is the *single point of compromise*. Mention **LSM/seccomp/gVisor** when discussing container security.
> 6. **Omitting the 8 namespace types in Q-B (a).** Examiners expect all eight — leaving out cgroup and time namespaces loses marks.
> 7. **Skipping the diagram.** A diagram in Q-B (a) of namespaces + cgroups carries 1–2 marks on its own. Skipping it caps you at ~5/7.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final-Exam Night Revision — Print This Section and Read Twice.**

- [ ] **VMM** = Hypervisor = software layer that creates and manages VMs. Must satisfy **Fidelity + Efficiency + Isolation** (Popek & Goldberg, 1974).
- [ ] **Popek–Goldberg Theorem:** $S \subseteq P$ is necessary and sufficient for a CPU architecture to be classically virtualizable.
- [ ] **Sensitive Instruction:** behaviour/result depends on privilege mode OR on control-register state.
- [ ] **Privileged Instruction:** traps in user mode.
- [ ] **x86 problem:** 17 *non-privileged sensitive* instructions (`SGDT`, `SIDT`, `SLDT`, `STR`, `SMSW`, `POPF`, `PUSHF`, `CPUID`, `RDTSC`, `RDPMC`, `VERR`, `VERW`, `LAR`, `LSL`, `INTO`, …) — violate $S \subseteq P$.
- [ ] **Three engineering fixes:** Binary Translation (VMWare), Paravirtualization (Xen), Hardware-Assisted Virtualization (Intel VT-x, AMD-V).
- [ ] **Intel VT-x:** introduces **VMX mode** (root / non-root), **VMCS** data structure, **`VMLAUNCH` / `VMRESUME`** for VM Entry, automatic VM Exit on configured events.
- [ ] **AMD-V:** equivalent; uses **VMCB** instead of VMCS, **`VMRUN`** for entry.
- [ ] **EPT (Intel) / NPT (AMD):** hardware-accelerated nested paging; translates GVA → GPA → HPA in one MMU walk. Eliminates shadow page-table memory overhead.
- [ ] **Type 1 (Bare-Metal) hypervisors:** VMware ESXi, Xen, Hyper-V, KVM. Run on bare hardware; used in data centers.
- [ ] **Type 2 (Hosted) hypervisors:** VMware Workstation, VirtualBox, Parallels. Run as host-OS applications; used on desktops.
- [ ] **OS-Level Virtualization:** containers share the host kernel; isolation via **namespaces** (visibility) and **cgroups** (quotas); security via **AppArmor/SELinux/seccomp-bpf**.
- [ ] **8 Linux namespaces:** Mount, UTS, IPC, PID, Network, User, Cgroup, Time.
- [ ] **cgroups v2 controllers:** cpu, memory, io, pids, devices, rdma, freezer, etc.
- [ ] **Container vs VM:** containers boot in < 1 s vs 30–60 s; 5–500 MB image vs 1–20 GB; share kernel vs own kernel; weaker isolation vs stronger; density 1000+ vs 50–100 per host.
- [ ] **Container cannot host a different OS kernel** — system-call ABI is host-bound.
- [ ] **Co-tenant security models:** gVisor (user-space kernel interception), Kata Containers (container-in-VM), Firecracker (microVM).
- [ ] **Live migration downtime:** $\approx S_{dirty} / B_{link}$ during the stop-and-copy phase.
- [ ] **Memory-virtualization overhead:** shadow-PT (0.5–2%) > EPT/NPT (~0.001%) > native (0%).

> **End of Module 3 Notes — VMM Design Requirements & OS-Level Virtualization.**
> **All the best for the KTU 2024 Scheme Examinations!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Virtualization Structures and Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Virtualization Structures and Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Virtualization** is the process of creating a software-based (virtual) representation of physical computing resources — including CPUs, memory, storage, and I/O devices — that abstracts the underlying hardware into multiple independent, isolated execution environments called **Virtual Machines (VMs)**. Each VM runs its own complete operating system (Guest OS) and applications, while the actual hardware is managed by a thin software layer known as the **Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM)** or **Hypervisor**.

The **Mechanisms of Virtualization** are the hardware and software primitives that enable this abstraction:

- **CPU Virtualization**: Time-multiplexing of physical processor cores across multiple VMs.
- **Memory Virtualization**: Logical-to-physical memory translation using nested page tables.
- **I/O Virtualization**: Decoupling guest device access from actual physical devices via emulated or para-virtual drivers.
- **Privileged Instruction Handling**: Safe execution of control-sensitive operations through traps and emulation.

The formal correctness criterion for a VMM was given by **Popek and Goldberg (1974)**:

> A VMM is correct if it satisfies three properties:
> 1. **Fidelity** — The program behavior inside a VM must be identical to native execution.
> 2. **Efficiency** — Most guest instructions execute directly on the CPU without VMM intervention.
> 3. **Resource Control** — The VMM retains full control over all physical resources.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Apartment Building** 🏢
>
