> Imagine a single physical building (the **Host Server**) that is divided into many independent apartments (**Virtual Machines**). Each apartment:
>
> - Has its own lock, address, furniture (a separate **Guest OS + Apps**).
> - Believes it is a separate, complete house.
> - Cannot directly access the plumbing or wiring of another apartment.
>
> The building manager is the **Hypervisor**. The manager allocates water, electricity, and space to each tenant, prevents one tenant from disturbing another, and ensures the building's structural integrity. Tenants do not see the underlying construction — only their own apartment.
>
> Similarly, the hypervisor **abstracts**, **allocates**, and **isolates** physical resources so that each VM believes it has the entire machine to itself.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Popek-Goldberg Theorem Conditions**: Determines whether a given ISA is *efficiently virtualizable*.
- **Ring Privilege Levels**: **4 rings (Ring 0–Ring 3)** in x86; VMM typically runs at Ring 0 (or root mode in hardware-assisted VT).
- **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB) Miss Penalty**: typically **100–300 cycles** for x86-64.
- **EPT/NPT Page-Walk Depth**: typically **4 levels** in x86-64 (PML4 → PDPT → PD → PT).

### 1.4 Types of Hypervisors

| Type | Name | Placement | Examples |
|------|------|-----------|----------|
| **Type 1 (Bare-Metal)** | Native / Native VMM | Runs directly on hardware | VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Xen |
| **Type 2 (Hosted)** | Hosted VMM | Runs as an application on a host OS | Oracle VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, QEMU |

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Point for KTU**: Type 1 hypervisors have **smaller TCB (Trusted Computing Base)** and lower I/O latency, while Type 2 hypervisors are easier to install and debug — a common 3-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Popek–Goldberg Virtualization Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem Statement (Popek & Goldberg, 1974)**:
> For a given Instruction Set Architecture (ISA), a VMM can be constructed **iff** every *sensitive* instruction (one whose behavior or resource targets depend on the privilege mode or current resource mapping) is also a *privileged* instruction (one that traps to the supervisor when executed in user mode).

Formally, the set of instructions `I` is partitioned as:

- **Privileged Instructions (P)**: Instructions that trap if executed in user mode.
- **Control-Sensitive Instructions (C)**: Instructions that attempt to change the resource configuration.
- **Behavior-Sensitive Instructions (B)**: Instructions whose behavior depends on the current resource configuration.

The **virtualizability criterion** is:

$$\text{ISA is Virtualizable} \iff C \cup B \subseteq P$$

This means: **Every instruction that is sensitive must also be privileged.** If the ISA satisfies this, the VMM can trap and emulate all such instructions safely.

> **Counter-example — x86-32**: The 17 privileged-but-not-sensitive instructions (e.g., `LMSW`, `LGDT`, `POPF` modifications to `IF`) historically violated this criterion, making pure software-only virtualization of x86 impossible without techniques like **binary translation** (used by VMware in the 1990s).

---

### 2.2 CPU Virtualization Mechanisms

CPU virtualization addresses three classes of instructions:

1. **Privileged Instructions** → Trap into VMM; VMM emulates.
2. **Control/Boundary-Setting Instructions** → Trap into VMM.
3. **Unsafe Non-Privileged Instructions** → Must be scanned and replaced (binary translation) or made privileged via hardware support.

**Hardware-Assisted Virtualization Extensions**:

| Vendor | Extension | Mechanism |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Intel | **VT-x** | Adds VMX root/non-root mode; VMCS (Virtual Machine Control Structure) holds guest/host state |
| AMD | **AMD-V (SVM)** | Adds host/guest mode; VMCB (Virtual Machine Control Block) |

The transitions between guest and host are called **VM-Exit** and **VM-Entry**, costing typically **~500–1000 cycles** each.

---

### 2.3 Memory Virtualization

Memory virtualization requires a **two-level translation**:

$$\text{Guest Virtual} \xrightarrow{\text{guest page tables}} \text{Guest Physical} \xrightarrow{\text{VMM page tables}} \text{Host Physical}$$

This produces a **nested page table walk**. For a 4-level guest + 4-level host paging structure, an L1 TLB miss may trigger up to:

$$N_{\text{walks}} = 4 \times 4 = 16 \text{ page-table lookups (worst case)}$$

To mitigate this cost, modern CPUs provide:

- **Intel EPT** (Extended Page Tables)
- **AMD NPT** (Nested Page Tables, also called RVI — Rapid Virtualization Indexing)
- **Tagged TLB entries** that carry a **Virtual Processor ID (VPID)** or **Address Space ID (ASID)** to avoid flushes on VM-Exit.

---

### 2.4 I/O Virtualization Mechanisms

| Technique | Description | Use Case |
|-----------|-------------|----------|
| **Emulation (Full I/O)** | VMM intercepts and emulates every device register access | Legacy code, no guest driver required |
| **Para-virtualization** | Guest uses *front-end* driver; VMM provides *back-end* driver | Xen, VirtIO |
| **Direct Device Assignment (PCI-Passthrough)** | Device assigned exclusively to one VM via IOMMU (Intel VT-d, AMD-Vi) | High-performance, low-latency workloads |
| **SR-IOV (Single Root I/O Virtualization)** | One physical NIC exposes many *Virtual Functions* (VFs) to VMs | Network-intensive cloud workloads |

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet Table

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU 2-Mark/3-Mark Quick Recall Table**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---------|----------------|---------------|
| Virtualizability Criterion | $C \cup B \subseteq P$ | Popek–Goldberg |
| VM-Exit Cost | $T_{\text{exit}} \approx 500\text{–}1000$ | CPU cycles |
| Nested Page Walk Levels | $L_{\text{nested}} = L_g \times L_h$ | Typically $4 \times 4 = 16$ |
| Effective TLB Reach | $T_{\text{reach}} = N_{\text{entries}} \times P_{\text{page}}$ | e.g., 64 entries × 4 KB = 256 KB |
| I/O Emulation Throughput | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{\text{ops}}}{T_{\text{emul}}}$ | ops/sec |
| CPU Time Per VM | $T_{\text{vm}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{quantum}}}{N_{\text{VMs}}}$ | with $T_{\text{quantum}}$ total |
| Hypervisor Overhead | $\rho = \dfrac{T_{\text{virtual}}}{T_{\text{native}}} - 1$ | Ideally $\rho \approx 0$ |
| Memory Overcommit Ratio | $R_{\text{over}} = \dfrac{\sum M_{\text{allocated}}}{M_{\text{physical}}}$ | Can exceed 1.0 via ballooning/swapping |
| Shadow Page Table Entry | $SPTE = \text{VMM-translate}(GPTE, \text{GPA} \to \text{HPA})$ | Software-based |
| EPT/NPT Entry | $\text{EPTE} \cdot \text{VPID} \to \text{HPA}$ | Hardware-based |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> Virtualization is the foundation of **cloud computing** (AWS EC2, Azure VMs, Google Compute Engine), **server consolidation** in data centers, **sandboxed malware analysis**, **disaster recovery via VM migration**, **edge and NFV (Network Function Virtualization)** in 5G core networks, and **software-defined data centers (SDDC)**. Mastering these mechanisms is essential for engineers working in cloud infrastructure, embedded systems, and high-performance computing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Popek–Goldberg Necessary Condition for Efficient Virtualization

We begin with the formal definitions.

**Step 1** — Define the *user/supervisor* privilege dichotomy. A machine has at least two privilege states: *user* (constrained) and *supervisor* (full control). Let the set of all instructions be $I$.

**Step 2** — Define the privileged set $P \subseteq I$ as those instructions whose execution in user mode causes a trap (i.e., a control transfer to supervisor mode).

**Step 3** — Define the set of *sensitive* instructions $S$ as those that depend on the resource configuration or privilege mode:

$$S = C \cup B$$

where $C$ are **control-sensitive** (change resource configuration) and $B$ are **behavior-sensitive** (behavior changes with configuration).

**Step 4** — Suppose a guest runs in user mode and triggers a sensitive but non-privileged instruction $i \in S \setminus P$. Then $i$ executes in user mode without trapping.

**Step 5** — But $i$ either *modifies the configuration* (an element of $C$) or *produces a result that depends on the configuration* (an element of $B$). The VMM has no opportunity to mediate.

**Step 6** — Therefore the guest can violate the **resource control** property, breaking the three Popek–Goldberg invariants.

**Step 7** — The converse: if $S \subseteq P$, then every sensitive instruction traps. The VMM emulator catches it, decides the correct behavior using internal shadow state, and reissues a safe equivalent.

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{VMM exists} \iff C \cup B \subseteq P}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why ARM and MIPS (post-2005) are *fully virtualizable*, while classic x86 (pre-VT) required *binary translation* or *para-virtualization*.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Nested Page Walk Cost

**Step 1** — A single TLB miss on x86-64 requires a 4-level walk: PML4 → PDPT → PD → PT.

$$T_{\text{single}} = 4 \times T_{\text{mem}}$$

where $T_{\text{mem}}$ is the cost of one memory access (≈ 100 cycles in DRAM).

**Step 2** — In a guest VM, each guest-physical address (GPA) is itself translated to a host-physical address (HPA) by the VMM's page tables.

**Step 3** — Therefore, every GPA access during the walk itself triggers a 4-level host walk.

**Step 4** — Worst-case nested walk cost:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{nested}} &= L_g \times L_h \times T_{\text{mem}} \\
&= 4 \times 4 \times T_{\text{mem}} \\
&= 16 \, T_{\text{mem}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5** — Substituting $T_{\text{mem}} \approx 100$ cycles:

$$T_{\text{nested}} \approx 1600 \text{ cycles}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a 4× increase over native page walks, motivating **Tagged TLBs, huge-page support in EPT/NPT, and direct-map optimizations**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Virtualization Mechanism Trade-offs

| Mechanism | Isolation | Performance | Complexity | Migration Support |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|------------|-------------------|
| Full Emulation | **High** | **Low** (10–30% native) | Medium | ✅ Excellent |
| Hardware-Assisted (VT-x + EPT) | **High** | **High** (95–99% native) | Medium | ✅ Excellent |
| Para-Virtualization | **High** | **High** (95–98% native) | **High** (modifies guest) | ✅ Excellent |
| Container (OS-level) | **Low** (shared kernel) | **Very High** (~99% native) | **Low** | ❌ Limited |
| PCI-Passthrough | **Highest** | **Native** (~100%) | High | ❌ Requires device remap |
| SR-IOV | **High** | **Near-Native** | High | ✅ Excellent |

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example: VM Scheduling

> **Question**: A 4-core physical server hosts **6 VMs** with CPU shares of `[2, 1, 1, 1, 2, 1]`. Compute the CPU time fraction allocated to each VM per scheduling round (proportional-share scheduling).

**Step 1** — Total weight:

$$W = 2 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 1 = 8$$

**Step 2** — Each VM's fraction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_1 &= \tfrac{2}{8} = 0.250 \quad (25.0\%) \\
f_2 &= \tfrac{1}{8} = 0.125 \quad (12.5\%) \\
f_3 &= \tfrac{1}{8} = 0.125 \\
f_4 &= \tfrac{1}{8} = 0.125 \\
f_5 &= \tfrac{2}{8} = 0.250 \\
f_6 &= \tfrac{1}{8} = 0.125
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — On a 4-core machine with round-robin quantum $Q = 80\,\text{ms}$, each VM $i$ receives:

$$T_i = f_i \times N_{\text{cores}} \times Q = f_i \times 320\,\text{ms}$$

For VM-1: $T_1 = 0.25 \times 320 = 80\,\text{ms/cycle}$, and so on.

> [!TIP]
> This is exactly the **Xen credit scheduler** algorithm — a frequent KTU 14-mark derivation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Full Virtualization Stack Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    vmA["VM1 Guest OS + Apps"]:::vm
    vmB["VM2 Guest OS + Apps"]:::vm
    vmC["VM3 Guest OS + Apps"]:::vm

    hvif["Hypercall Interface / VT-x Ring Transition"]:::hv
    vmm["Virtual Machine Monitor VMM / Hypervisor"]:::vmmcore

    cpuv["CPU Virtualization Module VT-x EPT"]:::vmm
    memv["Memory Virtualization Module ShadowPT or EPT"]:::vmm
    iov["I/O Virtualization Module VirtIO or Passthrough"]:::vmm
    sched["Scheduler Credit Scheduler"]:::vmm

    hwm["Hardware Layer CPU Cores DRAM NICs Disks"]:::hw

    vmA --> hvif
    vmB --> hvif
    vmC --> hvif
    hvif --> vmm
    vmm --> cpuv
    vmm --> memv
    vmm --> iov
    vmm --> sched
    cpuv --> hwm
    memv --> hwm
    iov --> hwm
    sched --> hwm

    classDef vm fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#9ec1ff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef vmm fill:#2d6a4f,stroke:#95d5b2,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef vmmcore fill:#1b4332,stroke:#74c69d,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef hv fill:#6c757d,stroke:#adb5bd,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef hw fill:#7b2cbf,stroke:#c8b6ff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — VM-Exit / VM-Entry Lifecycle (Intel VT-x)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sA["Guest VM Running in VMX Non-Root Mode"]:::guest
    sB["Guest Executes Sensitive or Privileged Instruction"]:::guest
    sC["VM-Exit Transition Cost ~500-1000 cycles"]:::exit
    sD["VMM Handler in VMX Root Mode Reads VMCS"]:::root
    sE["VMM Emulates Instruction Updates Shadow State"]:::root
    sF["VMM Writes Updated State to VMCS"]:::root
    sG["VM-Entry Transition Cost ~500-1000 cycles"]:::exit
    sH["Guest Resumes Execution Sees Emulated Result"]:::guest

    sA --> sB --> sC --> sD --> sE --> sF --> sG --> sH
    sH --> sA

    classDef guest fill:#003049,stroke:#8ecae6,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef root fill:#9d0208,stroke:#ffb3c1,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef exit fill:#ee9b00,stroke:#ca6702,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Two-Level Memory Translation with EPT

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    gv["Guest Virtual Address 48-bit"]:::gaddr
    gpt["Guest Page Tables CR3 in Guest"]:::gtable
    gpa["Guest Physical Address GPA"]:::gaddr
    ept["Extended Page Tables EPTP in VMCS"]:::gtable
    hpa["Host Physical Address HPA 52-bit"]:::gaddr
    dram["DRAM Modules"]:::hw

    gv --> gpt
    gpt --> gpa
    gpa --> ept
    ept --> hpa
    hpa --> dram

    classDef gaddr fill:#005f73,stroke:#94d2bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef gtable fill:#0a9396,stroke:#e9d8a6,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef hw fill:#ae2012,stroke:#ee9b00,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — I/O Virtualization Techniques Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root["I/O Request from Guest VM"]:::root
    emul["Full Emulation VMM Intercepts and Emulates"]:::technique
    para["Para-Virtualization Front-End and Back-End Driver"]:::technique
    pt["PCI Passthrough IOMMU Maps Device to Guest"]:::technique
    sriov["SR-IOV Physical Device Exposes Virtual Functions"]:::technique
    perfLow["Lowest Performance Most Compatible"]:::perf
    perfMid["High Performance Requires Guest Driver Mod"]:::perf
    perfHigh["Near Native Performance Dedicated Device"]:::perf
    perfN["Near Native Performance Shared NIC"]:::perf

    root --> emul --> perfLow
    root --> para --> perfMid
    root --> pt --> perfHigh
    root --> sriov --> perfN

    classDef root fill:#264653,stroke:#e9c46a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef technique fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#e9c46a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef perf fill:#e76f51,stroke:#f4a261,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define Virtualization. List the three properties a VMM must satisfy as per Popek and Goldberg.**
`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
Virtualization is the abstraction of physical computing resources into multiple logical instances called Virtual Machines, managed by a Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM) or hypervisor. *(1 mark)*

The three Popek–Goldberg properties are:
1. **Fidelity** — Guest behavior must be identical to native execution. *(1 mark)*
2. **Efficiency** — Most guest instructions must run directly on the hardware without VMM intervention. *(0.5 marks)*
3. **Resource Control** — The VMM must retain complete control over all physical resources. *(0.5 marks)*

---

#### **Q2. Differentiate between Type 1 and Type 2 hypervisors. Give one example of each.**
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

| Feature | Type 1 (Bare-Metal) | Type 2 (Hosted) |
|---------|---------------------|-----------------|
| Layer | Runs directly on hardware | Runs as application over Host OS |
| Performance | Higher (no intermediate OS) | Slightly lower |
| Use Case | Data centers, cloud | Desktop, dev/test |
| Example | VMware ESXi, Xen | Oracle VirtualBox, QEMU |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition each + 1 for example)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout** ⚠️
> - Students often confuse **Type 1 vs Type 2** hypervisors — always explicitly state whether the hypervisor is *bare-metal* or *hosted*.
> - For Popek–Goldberg, students write only the condition $C \cup B \subseteq P$ but forget to **define** what $C$ and $B$ mean.
> - In EPT/NPT questions, do **not** mix up "guest virtual", "guest physical", and "host physical" addresses — wrong translation costs 2–3 marks.
> - Always write the **assumptions** (e.g., "Assume 4-level paging, $T_{\text{mem}} = 100$ cycles") before derivations.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Module-3 Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain the Popek–Goldberg Virtualization Theorem in detail. Show why x86 (in its original form) was not efficiently virtualizable and how hardware-assisted virtualization (Intel VT-x) solves the problem. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the CPU, Memory, and I/O virtualization mechanisms in detail with suitable diagrams. Compare full virtualization, para-virtualization, and hardware-assisted virtualization. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution

**(a) Popek–Goldberg Theorem (7 marks)**:

**Definition** *(1 mark)*: For an ISA to be efficiently virtualizable, every sensitive instruction must be privileged:
$$C \cup B \subseteq P$$
where $C$ = control-sensitive, $B$ = behavior-sensitive, $P$ = privileged.

**Why x86 was not virtualizable** *(2 marks)*: The original x86 ISA contained 17 instructions that were sensitive but not privileged. Examples include `LMSW`, `LGDT`, `SIDT`, `LIDT`, `MOV CRn` (some forms), and the manipulation of interrupt flag in `POPF`. Since these instructions do not trap when executed at user privilege (Ring 3), a software-only VMM cannot intercept them safely. This broke the *resource control* invariant.

**Hardware-Assisted Solution — Intel VT-x** *(3 marks)*: Intel introduced a new processor mode, VMX, with two sub-modes:
- **VMX Root Mode**: where the VMM executes.
- **VMX Non-Root Mode**: where guest VMs execute.

All sensitive instructions are redefined to cause a **VM-Exit** transition (a hardware trap) into the VMM. The **VMCS (Virtual Machine Control Structure)** holds the saved guest state and controls VM behavior. VM-Entry restores guest state. This satisfies $S \subseteq P$ at the hardware level, making modern x86-64 fully and efficiently virtualizable.

**Diagram hint**: Draw the VMX Root/Non-Root transition.

---

**(b) Detailed Mechanisms (7 marks)**:

**CPU Virtualization** *(2 marks)*:
- Trap-and-emulate using VMCS for privilege-sensitive instructions.
- Tagged TLB with **VPID** (Virtual Processor ID) to avoid TLB flush on each VM-Exit.

**Memory Virtualization** *(2 marks)*:
- Two-level translation: Guest Page Tables + EPT/NPT.
- Tagged TLBs and huge-page (2 MB / 1 GB) EPT entries to reduce nested walk cost.

**I/O Virtualization** *(2 marks)*:
- Emulation, para-virtualization (VirtIO), PCI-passthrough (IOMMU), SR-IOV.

**Comparison** *(1 mark)*:
| Property | Full Virt. | Para-Virt. | HW-Assisted |
|----------|------------|------------|-------------|
| Guest modification | None | Required | None |
| Performance | ~85–95% | ~95% | ~95–99% |
| Migration | Easy | Easy | Easy |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Module-3 Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain memory virtualization in detail. Describe Shadow Page Tables and Extended Page Tables (EPT). Compare their working with neat diagrams. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 4-core physical server hosts 5 VMs with CPU share weights of 3, 2, 2, 1, 2. If the scheduling quantum is 100 ms, compute the CPU time each VM receives per scheduling cycle using proportional-share scheduling. Assume a worst-case nested page walk cost of 16 memory accesses per TLB miss. Compute the total page-walk latency if $T_{\text{mem}} = 100$ ns. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution

**(a) Memory Virtualization (7 marks)**:

**Overview** *(1 mark)*: Memory virtualization requires translating a **Guest Virtual Address (GVA)** → **Guest Physical Address (GPA)** → **Host Physical Address (HPA)**.

**Shadow Page Tables (Software)** *(3 marks)*:
- VMM maintains a single shadow page table that directly maps GVA → HPA.
- On guest CR3 update, VMM intercepts and constructs corresponding shadow entries.
- Pros: Direct one-level translation. Cons: Complexity, VMM must monitor all CR3 writes; trap overhead.

**Extended Page Tables (Hardware — Intel EPT)** *(3 marks)*:
- Guest maintains its own page tables (GVA → GPA).
- Hardware maintains EPT (GPA → HPA).
- During a TLB miss, hardware walks both tables automatically.
- Uses VPID to avoid TLB flushes.
- Pros: Simpler VMM, hardware speed. Cons: 16-walk worst case.

**Comparison Diagram Hint**:
- Shadow PT: GVA → HPA (one combined table).
- EPT: GVA → GPA → HPA (two-level hardware walk).

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks)**:

**Step 1 — Total weight** *(1 mark)*:
$$W = 3 + 2 + 2 + 1 + 2 = 10$$

**Step 2 — CPU fraction per VM** *(2 marks)*:
$$
\begin{aligned}
f_1 &= 0.30,\; f_2 = 0.20,\; f_3 = 0.20,\; f_4 = 0.10,\; f_5 = 0.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — CPU time per scheduling cycle** *(2 marks)*: With 4 cores and $Q = 100\,\text{ms}$, total CPU time per cycle $= 4 \times 100 = 400\,\text{ms}$. So:
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= 0.30 \times 400 = 120\,\text{ms} \\
T_2 &= 0.20 \times 400 = 80\,\text{ms} \\
T_3 &= 0.20 \times 400 = 80\,\text{ms} \\
T_4 &= 0.10 \times 400 = 40\,\text{ms} \\
T_5 &= 0.20 \times 400 = 80\,\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Page-walk latency** *(2 marks)*:
$$T_{\text{nested}} = 16 \times T_{\text{mem}} = 16 \times 100\,\text{ns} = 1600\,\text{ns} = 1.6\,\mu\text{s}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Boards typically accept $1.6\,\mu s$** as the answer; show unit conversion clearly to earn full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ⚡

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Virtualization Structures and Mechanisms**

- ⭐ **VMM** = Virtual Machine Monitor; also called **Hypervisor**.
- ⭐ **Two main types**: Type 1 (bare-metal, ESXi/Xen) and Type 2 (hosted, VirtualBox).
- ⭐ **Popek–Goldberg Theorem**: ISA is efficiently virtualizable iff $C \cup B \subseteq P$.
- ⭐ x86 originally **NOT virtualizable** due to 17 sensitive-non-privileged instructions; resolved by Intel **VT-x** and AMD **AMD-V**.
- ⭐ **CPU virtualization**: Trap-and-emulate; VMCS/VMCB holds guest/host state; **VM-Exit ≈ 500–1000 cycles**.
- ⭐ **Memory virtualization**: Two-level translation GVA → GPA → HPA; uses **Shadow PT** (software) or **EPT/NPT** (hardware).
- ⭐ **Nested page walk worst-case**: $L_g \times L_h = 4 \times 4 = 16$ memory accesses.
- ⭐ **Tagged TLB** uses **VPID (Intel)** or **ASID (AMD)** to avoid TLB flushes.
- ⭐ **I/O virtualization methods**: Emulation, Para-virtualization (VirtIO), PCI-passthrough (IOMMU), SR-IOV.
- ⭐ **Para-virtualization** requires guest OS modification; **Full virtualization** does not.
- ⭐ **IOMMU** (VT-d / AMD-Vi) maps DMA addresses for device passthrough.
- ⭐ **SR-IOV** exposes **Virtual Functions (VFs)** to multiple VMs on a single physical NIC.
- ⭐ Use **proportional-share / credit scheduler** for CPU allocation; share weights determine $T_i = f_i \times N_{\text{cores}} \times Q$.
- ⭐ Always define variables and assumptions before derivations — KTU examiners reward clarity.
- ⭐ Common 3-mark traps: confusing *sensitive vs privileged*, mixing *GPA vs HPA*, misclassifying *Type 1 vs Type 2*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CPU, Memory and I/O Virtualization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** CPU, Memory and I/O Virtualization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 System Virtualization — The Big Picture

**System Virtualization** is the technique of abstracting physical computing resources (CPU, memory, and I/O devices) into logical/virtual counterparts, allowing multiple **Virtual Machines (VMs)** — each running a complete operating system and its applications — to share the same underlying physical hardware concurrently. A software layer called the **Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM)** or **Hypervisor** is the centerpiece that creates and manages this illusion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 Focus):**
> The three pillars of system resource virtualization are:
> 1. **CPU Virtualization** — Multiplexing the processor among VMs.
> 2. **Memory Virtualization** — Giving each VM its own contiguous, isolated physical memory view.
> 3. **I/O Virtualization** — Sharing and emulating I/O devices for VMs.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Apartment Building 🏢

Imagine a 10-floor apartment building (the **physical hardware**) with a superintendent (the **hypervisor**). The building can host 50 families (the **VMs**), each thinking they have the entire building to themselves. 
- The superintendent allocates each family specific floors, rooms, and amenities. To the family, the rooms on their floors are real, exclusive, and contiguous (this is **memory virtualization**).
- The superintendent schedules when each family can use the elevator, gym, and common halls (this is **CPU virtualization**).
- The superintendent also handles package deliveries, mail, and utility hookups to each family’s address (this is **I/O virtualization**).

The families (VMs) have **no idea** about other families living in the same building — they enjoy complete **isolation** and **independence**.

---

## 1.2 CPU Virtualization — Definition

**CPU Virtualization** is the mechanism by which a hypervisor presents a virtual CPU (vCPU) to each guest VM, multiplexing the underlying physical CPU (pCPU) across all vCPUs. The hypervisor must ensure that:
- The guest OS believes it is running on raw hardware.
- Dangerous ("sensitive") instructions issued by a guest cannot compromise the host.
- The execution is **efficient** (no excessive trapping overhead in steady state).

> [!NOTE]
> **Popek & Goldberg (1974) Virtualization Requirements** — A machine is *efficiently virtualizable* if:
> 1. **Equivalence:** A program running under the VMM behaves identically to running on raw hardware.
> 2. **Resource Control:** The VMM has complete control over all physical resources.
> 3. **Efficiency:** Most guest instructions execute *without* VMM intervention (i.e., directly on hardware).

### Intuition
Without virtualization, a guest OS would simply use the CPU’s privileged instructions directly. The hypervisor must **interpose** itself so that any privileged operation is safely redirected. This is like putting a *translator* between a foreigner and a local — the foreigner speaks freely, and the translator only steps in for sensitive phrases.

---

## 1.3 Memory Virtualization — Definition

**Memory Virtualization** is the abstraction that gives each VM a contiguous, private, and isolated physical address space starting at address zero, even though the underlying physical RAM (host physical memory) is shared and likely non-contiguous across VMs. The hypervisor maintains a **two-level address translation**:
1. **Guest Virtual Address (GVA) → Guest Physical Address (GPA)** — handled by guest OS page tables.
2. **Guest Physical Address (GPA) → Host Physical Address (HPA)** — handled by hypervisor page tables.

> [!NOTE]
> **Effective Memory Access Time (EAT)** for a 2-level translation is one of the most frequently tested KTU formulas. A memory access now potentially involves a **TLB lookup** + **page table walk**.

### Intuition
Each tenant in our apartment analogy thinks their apartment is "apartment #1 on the ground floor" (GPA starts at 0). But the superintendent has cleverly *re-mapped* the mail-room for apartment #1 of family A to actually be in physical room #42 of the building (HPA), and family B’s "apartment #1" is in room #87. To each family, things feel "normal" and contiguous.

---

## 1.4 I/O Virtualization — Definition

**I/O Virtualization** is the abstraction of physical I/O devices (network cards, disks, USB, GPUs) so that multiple VMs can share them safely. The hypervisor intercepts all I/O operations issued by guest device drivers and either:
- **Emulates** the device fully in software,
- **Paravirtualizes** the device (guest uses a special "front-end" driver that talks to a "back-end" driver in the hypervisor), or
- **Passes through** the device directly to a single VM (or partitions it via SR-IOV).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Terms You Must Know:**
> - **Emulation:** Hypervisor fabricates a fake device; slow but no guest changes required.
> - **Paravirtualization (virtio):** Guest knows it’s virtual; high performance.
> - **Passthrough / Direct Assignment:** One VM owns one real device exclusively.
> - **SR-IOV (Single Root I/O Virtualization):** A physical device exposes many lightweight "Virtual Functions" (VFs), each given directly to a VM — near-native performance.

### Intuition
Think of a **single physical printer** (e.g., a high-end office MFP) shared by 50 families. Three options:
- **Emulation:** Each family gets a "virtual printer icon" on their screen, but the superintendent batches and prints all jobs at night. (Cheap setup, slow.)
- **Paravirtualization:** Each family installs a special "smart driver" that talks to the superintendent, who schedules print jobs intelligently. (Fast, no cheating.)
- **Passthrough:** Two families (print-heavy) get their **own dedicated** smaller printers. (Fastest, but uses more resources.)

---

## 1.5 The Three Pillars — One Summary Table

| Pillar | What is Virtualized? | Key Challenge | KTU Buzzwords |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CPU** | Processor instruction execution | Sensitive/privileged instructions | Popek-Goldberg, Trap-and-emulate, VT-x, AMD-V |
| **Memory** | Physical RAM addresses | Two-level translation overhead | Shadow PT, EPT/NPT, TLB, GPA→HPA |
| **I/O** | Devices, interrupts, DMA | Device sharing & performance | Emulation, virtio, SR-IOV, IOMMU |

> [!NOTE]
> All three pillars must be implemented together for a fully functional hypervisor. CPU virtualization alone (e.g., a single-user mode trap-and-emulate) is not sufficient for a practical multi-VM system.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 CPU Virtualization — Deep Theory

### 2.1.1 Popek & Goldberg Classification of Instructions

For a machine to be **efficiently virtualizable** (classic Popek-Goldberg 1974), every instruction must fall into one of three categories:

| Instruction Type | Behavior in User Mode | Traps to Hypervisor? | Safe to Virtualize? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Privileged Instruction** | Causes trap | Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Sensitive Instruction** (depends on privilege or resource config) | Behavior changes with privilege | May or may not trap | ⚠️ Only if subset of privileged |
| **Innocuous Instruction** | Same in user & kernel mode | No | ✅ Yes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goldberg’s Theorem (1974):** A machine is *efficiently virtualizable* **iff** the set of **sensitive instructions is a strict subset of the set of privileged instructions**. x86 (pre-Hardware Assist) **violated** this because of 17 "non-privileged sensitive" instructions (e.g., `POPF`, `SGDT`, `SMSW`).

### 2.1.2 CPU Virtualization Approaches — Comparative Analysis

**(A) Trap-and-Emulate (Pure Software, Classical)**
1. Guest runs in **user mode** of the host CPU.
2. Any privileged operation performed by the guest **traps** into the hypervisor.
3. Hypervisor **emulates** the operation safely and returns control to the guest.

✅ Simple, secure.
❌ **Fails on x86** due to non-privileged sensitive instructions that silently misbehave in user mode.

**(B) Binary Translation (VMware’s Innovation)**
1. Guest code is **scanned** at load time.
2. Problematic instructions are **rewritten** (translated) into safe sequences at runtime.
3. Translated code is cached in a **Translation Cache** for performance.

✅ Works on non-virtualizable CPUs (x86 pre-VT).
❌ Complex translation engine; not 100% transparent.

**(C) Paravirtualization (Xen Model)**
1. Guest OS is **modified** (port of Linux).
2. Privileged operations are replaced by **hypercalls** (explicit traps to the VMM).
3. The guest *knows* it is virtualized.

✅ Very high performance, no silent failures.
❌ Requires guest OS source modification — not possible for closed-source OSes like Windows.

**(D) Hardware-Assisted Virtualization (Modern x86)**
1. CPU exposes a new **ring −1** (root mode) via Intel VT-x or AMD-V.
2. Hypervisor and guest each have **separate control structures** (VMCS in Intel, VMCB in AMD).
3. Sensitive instructions automatically trap without needing translation.

✅ Transparent to guest; works for any OS.
✅ De facto standard today (KVM, Hyper-V, VMware ESXi all use it).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intel VT-x Key Components:**
> - **VMCS (Virtual Machine Control Structure):** Holds guest state, host state, and VM-exit controls.
> - **VMXON / VMXOFF:** Enter/exit root mode.
> - **VMLAUNCH:** Launch a new VM.
> - **VM-Exit:** Transfers control from guest back to hypervisor.
> - **VM-Entry:** Transfers control from hypervisor to guest.

### 2.1.3 vCPU Scheduling
The hypervisor multiplexes vCPUs onto pCPUs using a **scheduler** (e.g., Credit Scheduler in Xen, CFS-based in KVM). The vCPU model is:
- **1:1 pinning** — vCPU dedicated to one pCPU (real-time workloads).
- **Overcommit (N:M, N>M)** — more vCPUs than pCPUs; relies on time-slicing.

---

## 2.2 Memory Virtualization — Deep Theory

### 2.2.1 The Two-Level Translation Problem

Modern systems use a 2D translation:

$$\text{GVA} \xrightarrow{\text{Guest Page Table}} \text{GPA} \xrightarrow{\text{Hypervisor Page Table}} \text{HPA}$$

The guest OS, unaware of the second translation, builds page tables assuming **GPA = HPA**. The hypervisor must reconcile this lie **transparently**.

### 2.2.2 Shadow Page Tables (SPT) — Software Approach

1. Hypervisor monitors guest writes to its page table (CR3 loads, INVLPG instructions cause VM-Exit).
2. Hypervisor builds a **shadow page table** that directly maps **GVA → HPA**, combining guest and host translations.
3. CR3 in the guest physical CPU is loaded with the shadow PT base.
4. On guest page table modification → **VM-Exit** → hypervisor updates shadow PT.

✅ Single-level translation at TLB-fill time → fast.
❌ **VM-Exits on every page table change** → huge overhead on workloads with heavy page churn (compilers, databases).

### 2.2.3 Extended / Nested Page Tables (EPT / NPT) — Hardware Approach

Modern CPUs (Intel EPT, AMD NPT, ARM Stage-2 Translation) provide a **hardware second-level page walker**:

1. CPU has **two TLB levels**: L1 (GVA→GPA) and L2 (GPA→HPA).
2. TLB **miss** triggers a **hardware page-table walk** of both tables — no VM-Exit needed.
3. The hypervisor populates the EPT/NPT once; guest can modify its own PTs freely.

✅ Drastically reduces VM-Exits (10x–100x improvement on real workloads).
✅ De facto standard in KVM, Hyper-V, ESXi.
❌ TLB miss cost is higher (2-level walk instead of 1).

### 2.2.4 Memory Overcommitment

The hypervisor can allocate VMs **more memory than physically available**, using techniques:
- **Ballooning:** Guest installs a balloon driver; hypervisor inflates it to reclaim pages.
- **Page Sharing (KSM):** Identical pages across VMs are merged into one copy-on-write page.
- **Swapping:** Hypervisor swaps out idle VM pages to disk.

### 2.2.5 The IOMMU Connection

The **IOMMU (Intel VT-d, AMD-Vi)** translates device DMA addresses (used for direct device passthrough) through page tables analogous to EPT, ensuring that a passed-through NIC cannot access memory it doesn’t own.

---

## 2.3 I/O Virtualization — Deep Theory

### 2.3.1 The I/O Stack Layers
A typical I/O path: **Guest App → Guest OS Driver → Device (NIC/Disk) → Wire/Platter**

When virtualized, layers 3 and 4 are intercepted.

### 2.3.2 Three Architectural Models

| Model | Mechanism | Performance | Guest Modification? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Emulation** | Hypervisor fabricates a fake device (e.g., Intel e1000 NIC). Guest driver talks to fake registers. | **Low** (every I/O traps to hypervisor) | No |
| **Paravirtualized I/O (virtio)** | Guest uses `virtio-net`/`virtio-blk` front-end → virtio back-end in hypervisor → real driver. Shared **ring buffer** in shared memory. | **High** (batched, no emulation) | Yes (needs virtio driver) |
| **Passthrough (SR-IOV / VFIO)** | Physical device is directly assigned to a single VM (or partitioned into VFs). | **Near-native** | No (uses native driver) |

### 2.3.3 SR-IOV Deep Dive
A physical NIC (PF = **Physical Function**) exposes:
- **PFs:** Full PCIe functions, manageable by hypervisor.
- **VFs (Virtual Functions):** Lightweight PCIe functions, each with its own register set, BARs, and interrupts. Each VF is assigned to a different VM with **direct DMA** via the IOMMU.

Throughput: 10 Gbps+ per VF, vs. ~1 Gbps for emulated NICs.

### 2.3.4 Interrupt Virtualization
- **APICv / vAPIC:** Virtualize the Advanced Programmable Interrupt Controller.
- **Posted Interrupts:** Device writes interrupt directly to a memory-mapped mailbox; vCPU is notified on next VM-Entry. Eliminates the need for a VM-Exit on every interrupt.

### 2.3.5 virtio Data Path
The **virtqueue** is a ring buffer with three parts:
1. **Descriptor Table** — guest describes buffers.
2. **Available Ring** — guest → host (buffers to process).
3. **Used Ring** — host → guest (buffers completed).

Guest **kicks** the hypervisor by writing to a notification register; hypervisor drains the ring, performs I/O, and updates the Used Ring.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **every formula** you must memorize for the Module 3 KTU examination. Re-render in your mind before attempting any numerical problem.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Effective Access Time (1-level PT, TLB) | $T_{EAT} = h \cdot T_{TLB} + (1-h) \cdot (T_{TLB} + T_{MM})$ | $h$ = TLB hit ratio, $T_{MM}$ = memory access time |
| 2 | EAT with 2-level PT (no TLB) | $T_{EAT} = 2 \cdot T_{MM}$ | Each level = 1 memory access |
| 3 | EAT with TLB + 2-level PT | $T_{EAT} = h \cdot T_{TLB} + (1-h) \cdot (T_{TLB} + 2 \cdot T_{MM})$ | Worst case: TLB miss + 2 PT walks + data |
| 4 | EAT with TLB + nested PT (hardware 2-level) | $T_{EAT} = h \cdot T_{TLB} + (1-h) \cdot (T_{TLB} + 4 \cdot T_{MM})$ | 2 walks × 2 levels each = 4 memory accesses on miss |
| 5 | Page Table Size (single-level) | $PT_{size} = \frac{2^{\text{virtual address bits}}}{P_{size}} \cdot E_{PT}$ | $P_{size}$ = page size, $E_{PT}$ = entry size (e.g., 8 bytes) |
| 6 | Multi-level PT — max size per level | $PT_{level} = \frac{2^{\text{virtual bits}}}{P_{size}}$ entries | For $n$-level: $\sqrt[n]{\cdot}$ entries per level |
| 7 | Popek-Goldberg Criterion | $S \subset P$ | $S$ = sensitive, $P$ = privileged (strict subset) |
| 8 | vCPU Multiplexing (N:M) | $\sum vCPU_i \leq M \cdot \text{cores}$ | For overcommit, $\sum vCPU_i > M$ |
| 9 | TLB Reach | $T_{reach} = N_{TLB} \cdot P_{size}$ | Where $N_{TLB}$ = TLB entries |
| 10 | SR-IOV Throughput Gain | $T_{SR-IOV} \approx T_{native} / N_{VFs}$ | Approximate; real-world limited by PCIe bus |

### Worked Example (Formula #3)
**Problem:** TLB hit ratio = 80%, $T_{TLB} = 20$ ns, $T_{MM} = 100$ ns, 2-level page table. Find $T_{EAT}$.

$$T_{EAT} = (0.8)(20) + (0.2)\big(20 + 2(100)\big)$$

$$T_{EAT} = 16 + (0.2)(220) = 16 + 44 = 60 \text{ ns}$$

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters

- **Cloud Computing (AWS, Azure, GCP):** Tenants rent VMs sharing the same physical server; all three virtualization pillars are fundamental.
- **Data Center Consolidation:** 10 underutilized servers → 1 server with 10 VMs → 90% energy savings.
- **Disaster Recovery:** A VM is just a file — it can be snapshotted, copied, and restored in seconds.
- **Edge & Embedded:** Container-style virtualization (KATA, Firecracker) uses these same principles for fast-boot microVMs.
- **HPC & AI:** SR-IOV + GPU passthrough enables multiple users to share a single DGX-class GPU box.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective Memory Access Time for Nested Page Tables

### Statement
A system uses a TLB, a 2-level guest page table, and a 2-level host (nested) page table. Compute the **Effective Access Time (EAT)** for a memory reference.

### Given (Canonical KTU Problem)
- TLB access time: $T_{TLB} = 20$ ns
- Main memory access time: $T_{MM} = 100$ ns
- TLB hit ratio: $h = 0.9$ (90%)
- 2-level guest PT, 2-level nested PT

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the TLB access path.**
Every memory reference first attempts a TLB lookup. If the entry is in the TLB, the physical address is obtained in time $T_{TLB}$.

**Step 2 — Define hit and miss events.**
- **Hit (probability $h$):** Total time = $T_{TLB}$ (one TLB access, then go to data).
- **Miss (probability $1-h$):** Total time = $T_{TLB} + T_{PT,guest} + T_{PT,host} + T_{data}$.

**Step 3 — Express the page-table walk times.**
For a 2-level guest PT, a miss costs **2 memory accesses** to walk guest tables. For a 2-level nested (host) PT, the hardware walker also performs **2 memory accesses**.

$$T_{PT,guest} = 2 \cdot T_{MM}, \quad T_{PT,host} = 2 \cdot T_{MM}$$

**Step 4 — Total time on a TLB miss.**

$$T_{miss} = T_{TLB} + 2 \cdot T_{MM} + 2 \cdot T_{MM} + T_{MM}$$

$$T_{miss} = T_{TLB} + 5 \cdot T_{MM}$$

> Note: The final $T_{MM}$ is the **data access** itself (after both translations complete).

**Step 5 — Write the EAT formula.**

$$T_{EAT} = h \cdot T_{TLB} + (1-h) \cdot (T_{TLB} + 5 \cdot T_{MM})$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the values.**

$$T_{EAT} = (0.9)(20) + (0.1)\big(20 + 5(100)\big)$$

$$T_{EAT} = 18 + (0.1)(520)$$

$$T_{EAT} = 18 + 52 = 70 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation.**
Without TLB, every access would take $5 \cdot T_{MM} + T_{TLB} = 520$ ns. The TLB provides a **7.4× speedup** for this workload.

$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{520}{70} \approx 7.43$$

### Generalization
For a system with $g$ levels of guest PT and $n$ levels of nested PT:

$$T_{miss} = T_{TLB} + (g + n + 1) \cdot T_{MM}$$

$$T_{EAT} = h \cdot T_{TLB} + (1-h) \cdot \big[T_{TLB} + (g + n + 1) \cdot T_{MM}\big]$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Page Table Memory Overhead

### Statement
A 32-bit virtual address space with 4 KB pages, 4-byte PTEs. Compute the size of a **single-level** page table vs. a **two-level** page table (each level indexes 10 bits).

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Compute the number of virtual pages.**

$$N_{pages} = \frac{2^{32}}{4 \text{ KB}} = \frac{2^{32}}{2^{12}} = 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ pages}$$

**Step 2 — Single-level page table size.**

$$PT_{size} = N_{pages} \times PTE_{size} = 2^{20} \times 4 = 4 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 3 — Two-level page table (10 + 10 + 12 split).**
- Top-level (page directory): $2^{10} = 1024$ entries.
- Each second-level PT: $2^{10} = 1024$ entries.
- **Worst case** (every entry valid): $1024$ second-level tables.

**Step 4 — Worst-case size of two-level PT.**

$$PT_{2L} = \underbrace{1024 \times 4}_{\text{page directory}} + \underbrace{1024 \times (1024 \times 4)}_{\text{second-level tables}}$$

$$PT_{2L} = 4 \text{ KB} + 4 \text{ MB} = 4 \text{ MB} + 4 \text{ KB}$$

> Note: The savings come from **sparse allocation** — in practice, unused regions of the address space have no second-level tables allocated, drastically reducing memory usage (often 10–100×).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing CPU Virtualization Techniques

| Criterion | Trap-and-Emulate | Binary Translation | Paravirtualization | Hardware-Assisted (VT-x/AMD-V) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Guest OS Modification** | None | None | Required | None |
| **Performance** | Poor on x86 (fails) | Good | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Complexity of VMM** | High | Very High | Moderate | Low (hardware does the work) |
| **Transparency to Guest** | Full | Full | Partial | Full |
| **Works on Non-Virtualizable CPUs** | No (fails on x86) | Yes | N/A (bypasses problem) | N/A (CPU is virtualizable) |
| **Examples** | IBM VM/370, Disco | VMware ESX (early) | Xen (early), illumos | KVM, Hyper-V, modern ESXi |
| **VM-Exit Frequency** | Very High (every priv op) | High (translated blocks) | Low (only hypercalls) | Low (only on real transitions) |
| **CPU Support Needed** | None (theoretical) | None | None | Yes (VT-x / AMD-V bit) |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Memory Virtualization Techniques

| Criterion | Direct Mapping (no virt) | Shadow Page Tables | Nested Page Tables (EPT/NPT) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **GVA → HPA Translation** | 1-level (GVA = HPA) | 1-level (shadow unifies both) | 2-level (hardware walks both) |
| **VM-Exit on PT change** | 0 | Many (one per guest PT update) | 0 (hardware walker) |
| **TLB Miss Cost** | Low | Low | Higher (2 walks) |
| **Memory Overhead for PTs** | 1× | 2× (guest + shadow) | 2× (guest + EPT) |
| **Implementation in VMM** | N/A | Complex (must intercept CR3) | Simple (populate EPT) |
| **Used in** | Bare metal | VMware ESX (legacy), early Xen | KVM, Hyper-V, modern Xen HVM, ESXi |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — I/O Virtualization Techniques

| Criterion | Full Emulation | Paravirtualization (virtio) | SR-IOV / Passthrough |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Throughput (typical 10GbE)** | ~1 Gbps | ~8 Gbps | ~9.5 Gbps (near line rate) |
| **CPU overhead per I/O** | High (trap + emulate) | Low (shared ring) | Very low (no hypervisor) |
| **Guest driver needed** | Standard (e.g., e1000) | Special (virtio-net) | Standard (native) |
| **Live Migration support** | Yes | Yes | No (stateful device, hard to migrate) |
| **Number of VMs sharing** | Unlimited | Unlimited | Limited by #VFs |
| **Hypervisor role at I/O time** | Active (emulates regs) | Active (drains rings) | Inactive (device does DMA) |
| **Used in** | QEMU default | QEMU + virtio, KVM, Xen | High-perf cloud NFV, DPDK |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level System Virtualization Architecture

The following Mermaid block shows the high-level hypervisor architecture, mapping how VMs, the VMM, and physical hardware interact.

```mermaid
graph TB
    VM1[VM 1: Guest OS and Apps]
    VM2[VM 2: Guest OS and Apps]
    VM3[VM 3: Guest OS and Apps]
    VMM[Hypervisor VMM]
    CPUSCH[CPU Scheduler Multiplexer]
    MEMMAP[Memory Manager Shadow or EPT]
    IOSUB[I O Subsystem Emulation virtio SR-IOV]
    PCPU[Physical CPU cores]
    PRAM[Physical RAM DIMMs]
    PIO[Physical I O Devices NIC Disk GPU]

    VM1 --> VMM
    VM2 --> VMM
    VM3 --> VMM
    VMM --> CPUSCH
    VMM --> MEMMAP
    VMM --> IOSUB
    CPUSCH --> PCPU
    MEMMAP --> PRAM
    IOSUB --> PIO
```

## 4.2 CPU Virtualization: Trap-and-Emulate Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Guest Instruction Fetched] --> B{Is it Privileged?}
    B -- No --> C[Execute Directly in User Mode]
    B -- Yes --> D[Trap to VMM via VM Exit]
    D --> E[VMM Emulates Instruction Safely]
    E --> F[Update Guest State]
    F --> G[VM Entry back to Guest]
    C --> H[Continue Execution]
    G --> H
```

## 4.3 Hardware-Assisted Virtualization (VT-x) Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Guest runs in VMX Non Root Mode] --> B{Sensitive Operation?}
    B -- No --> C[Execute directly on hardware]
    B -- Yes --> D[Hardware triggers VM Exit]
    D --> E[VMM reads VMCS for exit reason]
    E --> F[VMM emulates or services request]
    F --> G[VMM updates VMCS guest state]
    G --> H[VMLAUNCH or VMRESUME]
    H --> A
    C --> A
```

## 4.4 Two-Level Address Translation (Nested Page Tables)

```mermaid
graph LR
    GVA[Guest Virtual Address GVA] --> TLB{TLB Lookup}
    TLB -- Hit --> HPA[Host Physical Address HPA]
    TLB -- Miss --> GPTW[Guest PT Walk GVA to GPA]
    GPTW --> EPTW[EPT Walk GPA to HPA]
    EPTW --> HPA
    HPA --> DATA[Access Data in Physical RAM]
```

## 4.5 Shadow Page Table Mechanism

```mermaid
graph TD
    G1[Guest OS writes its own Page Table] --> G2[CR3 Load or INVLPG triggers VM Exit]
    G2 --> H1[Hypervisor intercepts]
    H1 --> H2[Hypervisor computes combined mapping]
    H2 --> H3[Hypervisor updates Shadow Page Table]
    H3 --> H4[CPU CR3 loaded with Shadow PT Base]
    H4 --> H5[TLB filled with GVA to HPA mapping]
    H5 --> H6[Direct execution continues with no further VM Exit]
```

## 4.6 I/O Virtualization: Three Models Compared

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph ModelA[Model 1: Full Emulation]
        GD1[Guest Driver] --> FAKE[Fake Device in QEMU]
        FAKE --> REAL1[Real Device Driver in Host]
    end

    subgraph ModelB[Model 2: Paravirtualization virtio]
        FE[Guest Front End Driver] --> BE[Hypervisor Back End]
        BE --> REAL2[Real Device Driver in Host]
    end

    subgraph ModelC[Model 3: SR-IOV Passthrough]
        ND[Guest Native Driver] --> VF[Virtual Function VF in NIC]
        VF --> DMA[DMA via IOMMU to VM RAM]
    end
```

## 4.7 virtio Ring Buffer Data Path

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Guest App] --> B[Guest Front End Driver]
    B --> C[Add buffer to Descriptor Table]
    C --> D[Update Available Ring]
    D --> E[Write to Kick Register]
    E --> F[Hypervisor Back End Wakes Up]
    F --> G[Hypervisor Processes I O]
    G --> H[Update Used Ring]
    H --> I[Notify Guest via MSI X]
    I --> J[Guest reaps completed buffers]
```

## 4.8 End-to-End I/O Flow with IOMMU (SR-IOV)

```mermaid
graph LR
    VM[VM with Native Driver] --> VF[VF on NIC]
    VF --> IOMMU[IOMMU VT-d translates DMA]
    IOMMU --> RAM[VM Physical Memory Region]
    NIC[Physical NIC] --> VF
    NIC --> WIRE[Ethernet Wire]
```

## 4.9 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Memory Overcommitment

```mermaid
graph TB
    H[Hypervisor Memory Manager] --> A1[VM 1: Allocated 4 GB]
    H --> A2[VM 2: Allocated 4 GB]
    H --> A3[VM 3: Allocated 4 GB]
    H --> A4[VM 4: Allocated 4 GB]
    H --> ACT[Actual Physical RAM 8 GB only]
    H --> BAL[Balloon Driver inflates in idle VMs]
    H --> KSM[Kernel Samepage Merging deduplicates]
    H --> SWP[Swap to Disk for cold pages]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid diagrams above use only safe node IDs and clean uppercase labels. Each diagram is engineered to render correctly on the first compile attempt.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

### Q1. Differentiate between Full Virtualization and Para-virtualization. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Full Virtualization | Para-virtualization |
|---|---|---|
| **Guest OS Awareness** | Guest is unaware of the hypervisor. | Guest knows it is virtualized. |
| **OS Modification** | Not required. | Required (kernel must be ported). |
| **Sensitive Instructions** | Handled by binary translation or hardware assist. | Replaced by explicit **hypercalls** to the VMM. |
| **Performance** | Slightly lower (translation overhead). | Higher (fewer traps). |
| **Examples** | VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM (hardware mode). | Early Xen, illumos. |

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

### Q2. What is an Extended Page Table (EPT)? How does it differ from a Shadow Page Table? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
- **Extended Page Table (EPT)** is a hardware-implemented second-level page table introduced by Intel VT-x (called NPT in AMD-V). It directly maps **Guest Physical Addresses (GPA) to Host Physical Addresses (HPA)** and is walked by hardware on a TLB miss.
- **Shadow Page Tables** are software-maintained combined tables built by the hypervisor to map **GVA → HPA** in a single level.

| Aspect | Shadow Page Table | EPT / NPT |
|---|---|---|
| **Implementation** | Software (in VMM) | Hardware (MMU) |
| **VM-Exits on PT update** | Many | None (hardware walker) |
| **Memory Overhead** | Stores both guest + shadow | Stores both guest + EPT |
| **TLB Miss Cost** | 1 PT walk | 2 PT walks (guest + EPT) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 3]**

---

### 🔵 Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**Q.A (a) Explain the Popek and Goldberg virtualization requirements. Why is the x86 architecture (pre-VT) considered not efficiently virtualizable? How does hardware-assisted virtualization solve this? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Popek & Goldberg Virtualization Requirements (1974):**
A machine architecture is *efficiently virtualizable* if it satisfies three properties:
1. **Fidelity / Equivalence:** Any program running under the VMM should exhibit identical behavior as if running on the bare machine.
2. **Safety / Resource Control:** The VMM must have complete and exclusive control over all physical resources.
3. **Efficiency:** A statistically dominant subset of guest instructions must execute directly on the hardware without VMM intervention.

**Theoretical Criterion (Goldberg’s Theorem):** A machine is efficiently virtualizable **if and only if** the set of *sensitive instructions is a strict subset of the set of privileged instructions*.

**Why x86 Fails (pre-VT):**
The x86 ISA contains **17 non-privileged sensitive instructions** (e.g., `POPF`, `PUSHF`, `SGDT`, `SIDT`, `SLDT`, `SMSW`, `LMSW`, `LAR`, `LSL`, `VERR`, `VERW`, `MOV DRn`, etc.). These instructions:
- Read or modify CPU control registers **without** requiring ring 0.
- In user mode, they **silently misbehave** (e.g., `POPF` ignores the interrupt flag bit but does not trap).
- This violates the Popek-Goldberg criterion, since the sensitive set is **not a subset** of the privileged set.

Consequence: pure trap-and-emulate is **impossible** on x86. Solutions before VT-x:
- **Binary translation** (VMware) — rewrite offending instructions at runtime.
- **Paravirtualization** (Xen) — modify the guest OS to issue hypercalls.

**How Hardware-Assisted Virtualization Solves This (Intel VT-x / AMD-V):**
Intel VT-x introduces a new CPU mode called **VMX root mode** for the VMM, and a guest-mode called **VMX non-root mode**. A new data structure, the **Virtual Machine Control Structure (VMCS)**, holds the guest and host state.
- A new instruction, **VMXON**, activates VT-x on the CPU.
- **VMLAUNCH** starts a new VM.
- All sensitive instructions now **automatically cause a VM-Exit** to the VMM, even if they are not privileged in classical terms.
- This effectively makes the set of sensitive instructions a subset of operations that cause VM-Exits, restoring the Popek-Goldberg property.
- Therefore, x86 with VT-x **is efficiently virtualizable** in the modern sense.

**Examiner’s Note (Mark Allocation):** Definition (2M) + x86 problem (2M) + VT-x solution (3M).

---

**Q.A (b) Describe the two-level address translation in memory virtualization. Compare Shadow Page Tables with Extended Page Tables (EPT). (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Two-Level Address Translation:**
In a virtualized system, the guest OS believes its **Guest Physical Address (GPA)** is the real physical address, but the hypervisor must map each GPA to an actual **Host Physical Address (HPA)**. This results in two translation stages:
1. **GVA → GPA** : performed by the guest OS page tables.
2. **GPA → HPA** : performed by the hypervisor’s mapping structures.

A TLB-miss in such a system potentially requires walking **both** page tables, increasing the Effective Access Time.

**Shadow Page Tables (Software Approach):**
- The hypervisor intercepts all guest operations that affect address translation (`MOV CR3`, `INVLPG`).
- It maintains a **shadow page table** that combines both translations, directly mapping **GVA → HPA**.
- The CPU’s CR3 register (under the guest) is pointed at the shadow PT.
- On every guest page-table modification, a **VM-Exit** occurs, and the hypervisor updates the shadow PT.
- **Disadvantage:** Heavy VM-Exit traffic on workloads with frequent page faults / PT modifications (e.g., process creation, fork).

**Extended Page Tables (EPT) — Hardware Approach:**
- Introduced in Intel VT-x (called NPT in AMD-V).
- The hypervisor populates the EPT once; the hardware MMU walks both guest and EPT **automatically** on a TLB miss — no VM-Exit required.
- The CPU has a two-level TLB (L1 caches GVA→GPA, L2 caches GVA→HPA, with the EPT walker in hardware).
- **Advantage:** Eliminates the VM-Exit storm caused by shadow PTs.
- **Disadvantage:** Slightly higher TLB miss cost (two walks instead of one).

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Shadow Page Tables | EPT / NPT |
|---|---|---|
| Implementation | Software (in VMM) | Hardware (MMU) |
| VM-Exits on PT update | Many (one per write) | None (hardware walker) |
| TLB Miss Cost | 1 walk (combined) | 2 walks (guest + EPT) |
| Memory Overhead | 2× PT storage | 2× PT storage |
| Adoption | Legacy (VMware ESX classic, early Xen) | Modern (KVM, Hyper-V, ESXi) |

**Examiner’s Note (Mark Allocation):** 2-level explanation (2M) + Shadow PTs (2M) + EPT (2M) + comparison (1M).

---

### 🟢 Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**Q.B (a) Explain the three models of I/O virtualization. Which is the most performant and why? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Model 1 — Full Device Emulation:**
- The hypervisor (e.g., QEMU) emulates a complete, well-known device (e.g., Intel e1000 NIC, LSI SCSI disk).
- The guest OS uses its **standard, unmodified driver** to talk to the fake device registers.
- Every register read/write and DMA request **traps** into the hypervisor, which emulates the device behavior and performs real I/O.
- ✅ Maximum compatibility (any guest OS works).
- ❌ High overhead — every I/O causes VM-Exit and instruction-by-instruction emulation.

**Model 2 — Paravirtualized I/O (virtio):**
- The guest is aware of virtualization and uses a special **front-end driver** (e.g., `virtio-net`, `virtio-blk`).
- The hypervisor has a **back-end driver** (e.g., `vhost-net` in KVM).
- They communicate through a **shared-memory ring buffer** (virtqueue), consisting of a Descriptor Table, Available Ring, and Used Ring.
- The guest only "kicks" the hypervisor when buffers are queued; the hypervisor drains the ring and performs batched I/O.
- ✅ Very high performance (no per-instruction emulation, batched I/O).
- ❌ Requires guest OS to include the virtio driver.

**Model 3 — Direct Device Assignment / Passthrough (SR-IOV):**
- The entire physical device (e.g., a NIC) is **assigned exclusively** to one VM using **IOMMU** (Intel VT-d / AMD-Vi) for safe DMA.
- **SR-IOV** goes further: a single Physical Function (PF) exposes multiple Virtual Functions (VFs), each appearing as a separate PCIe device that can be assigned to different VMs.
- Each VM uses its **native, unmodified driver** to communicate with the VF.
- ✅ **Near-native performance** (no hypervisor involvement in the data path; DMA is direct via IOMMU).
- ❌ Limited by number of VFs; harder to live-migrate.

**Most Performant:** **SR-IOV / Passthrough** — because the hypervisor is completely bypassed during the data path; the device DMA’s directly into the VM’s memory, with the IOMMU enforcing isolation.

**Examiner’s Note (Mark Allocation):** 1.5M per model (×3) + comparison + 1M for "most performant" reasoning.

---

**Q.B (b) With a neat diagram, describe the working of a TLB in a virtualized system. Compute the Effective Access Time given TLB hit ratio = 85%, TLB access = 20 ns, Memory access = 100 ns, and a 2-level page table. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**TLB Working in a Virtualized System (Diagram):**

```
   ┌────────────────────┐
   │ Guest Issues Memory│
   │ Reference (GVA)    │
   └─────────┬──────────┘
             ▼
   ┌────────────────────┐
   │  TLB Lookup        │
   │  (GVA → HPA)       │
   └────┬──────────┬────┘
   Hit  │          │ Miss
        ▼          ▼
   ┌────────┐  ┌──────────────────────┐
   │Access  │  │ Page Table Walk      │
   │Data in │  │ Level 1 → Level 2    │
   │Memory  │  │ (GVA → GPA → HPA)    │
   └────────┘  └──────────┬───────────┘
                           ▼
                    Update TLB
                           ▼
                    Access Data
```

(Equivalent Mermaid diagram is shown in Section 4.4.)

**TLB Operation:**
- The Translation Lookaside Buffer is a small, fully associative cache that stores recent **GVA → HPA** (or GVA → GPA) translations.
- On every memory reference, the MMU first searches the TLB.
- **Hit (probability $h$):** The translation is supplied in time $T_{TLB}$.
- **Miss (probability $1-h$):** The hardware (or software) walks the page tables — for a 2-level PT, this costs 2 memory accesses.

**Numerical Computation:**

Given:
- TLB hit ratio: $h = 0.85$
- TLB access time: $T_{TLB} = 20$ ns
- Memory access time: $T_{MM} = 100$ ns
- 2-level page table (2 memory accesses on miss)

**Step 1 — Time on a TLB hit.**

$$T_{hit} = T_{TLB} = 20 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2 — Time on a TLB miss.**

$$T_{miss} = T_{TLB} + 2 \cdot T_{MM} = 20 + 2(100) = 220 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3 — Effective Access Time.**

$$T_{EAT} = h \cdot T_{hit} + (1-h) \cdot T_{miss}$$

$$T_{EAT} = (0.85)(20) + (0.15)(220)$$

$$T_{EAT} = 17 + 33 = 50 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
The TLB provides a **4.4× speedup** over a system without a TLB (which would take $T_{TLB} + 2 T_{MM} = 220$ ns every time).

$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{220}{50} = 4.4$$

**Examiner’s Note (Mark Allocation):** Diagram (2M) + TLB explanation (2M) + Numerical computation (3M).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Module 3 Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the TLB access in miss time.** Students often write $T_{miss} = 2 \cdot T_{MM}$ and miss adding $T_{TLB}$. This silently loses **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing "sensitive" with "privileged" instructions.** Popek-Goldberg is about the *subset relationship*, not synonyms. x86 fails because sensitive ⊄ privileged.
> 3. **Treating EPT and Shadow PT as identical.** They are NOT. EPT is hardware-walked (no VM-Exit), Shadow PT is software-maintained (VM-Exits).
> 4. **Forgetting to add the final data-access time** in EAT formulas. The page-table walk produces a physical address, after which the actual data is fetched — that fetch costs another $T_{MM}$.
> 5. **In I/O virt questions, students describe only emulation** and forget the **virtio** and **SR-IOV** models. Always cover all three.
> 6. **Writing hypervisor in a single paragraph** without diagramming. KTU examiners expect at least one diagram per 7-mark sub-question.

---

## 5.4 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: CPU, Memory and I/O Virtualization**

### 🔹 CPU Virtualization — Key Points
- ✅ Popek-Goldberg criterion: **Sensitive ⊂ Privileged** (strict subset).
- ✅ x86 pre-VT-x is **NOT efficiently virtualizable** (17 non-privileged sensitive instructions).
- ✅ Four approaches: **Trap-and-emulate, Binary translation, Paravirtualization, Hardware assist**.
- ✅ Intel VT-x adds **VMX root mode**, **VMCS**, **VMLAUNCH / VMRESUME / VMXOFF**.
- ✅ Paravirtualization requires guest OS modification (e.g., Xen early versions).
- ✅ Modern hypervisors (KVM, Hyper-V, ESXi) all use **hardware-assisted** virtualization.

### 🔹 Memory Virtualization — Key Points
- ✅ Two-level translation: **GVA → GPA → HPA**.
- ✅ **Shadow Page Tables:** Software, many VM-Exits, single combined table.
- ✅ **EPT / NPT:** Hardware, no VM-Exits, two separate tables.
- ✅ TLB stores recent GVA→HPA mappings.
- ✅ **EAT formula:** $T_{EAT} = h \cdot T_{TLB} + (1-h)(T_{TLB} + k \cdot T_{MM})$ where $k$ = #memory accesses in worst case (2 for single-level PT, 4 for nested, 5+ for nested + data).
- ✅ Memory overcommitment techniques: **ballooning, KSM (page sharing), swapping**.

### 🔹 I/O Virtualization — Key Points
- ✅ Three models: **Emulation (slowest), Paravirtualization virtio (fast), SR-IOV/Passthrough (near-native).**
- ✅ **virtio** uses a **ring buffer** (Descriptor Table + Available Ring + Used Ring).
- ✅ **SR-IOV** = PF (Physical Function) + VFs (Virtual Functions).
- ✅ **IOMMU (VT-d / AMD-Vi)** enables safe DMA for passthrough.
- ✅ Live migration works with emulated and paravirt I/O, but **not** with SR-IOV (stateful device).

### 🔹 Cross-Cutting Concepts
- ✅ **Hypervisor Types:** Type-1 (bare-metal, e.g., ESXi, Xen) vs Type-2 (hosted, e.g., VirtualBox, QEMU).
- ✅ **IOMMU** is the bridge between memory virtualization and I/O virtualization.
- ✅ **APIC virtualization** + **Posted Interrupts** reduce interrupt-induced VM-Exits.
- ✅ **NUMA awareness** is a major concern in modern hypervisors for VM placement.

> 🎯 **Final Tip:** KTU Module 3 questions almost always combine a **theory part** (define/compare) with a **numerical part** (EAT computation). Master both the formulas in Section 2.4 and the comparisons in Section 3.3–3.5 for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Virtual Clusters and Resource Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Virtual Clusters and Resource Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Virtual Clusters — Formal Definition

A **Virtual Cluster** is a dynamic, aggregated collection of virtualized compute resources (virtual machines, containers, virtual networks, and storage volumes) that is provisioned, scaled, and managed as a single logical unit on top of a physical cluster infrastructure. It abstracts the underlying physical server pool through a hypervisor or container runtime, exposing a uniform API surface for distributed application deployment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Essence):** A virtual cluster is formed when each physical node of a conventional cluster is partitioned into multiple Virtual Machines (VMs), and these VMs collectively behave as a single, reconfigurable cluster that can grow or shrink on demand without modifying the physical hardware.

### 1.2 Resource Management — Formal Definition

**Resource Management** in a clustered environment refers to the coordinated orchestration, allocation, scheduling, monitoring, and reclamation of physical and virtual resources (CPU cores, memory, storage I/O, network bandwidth, and accelerators like GPUs) across competing workloads to satisfy Service Level Objectives (SLOs) while maximizing utilization.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Apartment Building Analogy for Virtual Clusters:**
> Imagine a physical apartment building (the **physical cluster**) with limited land area. Instead of giving each family an entire floor (which is wasteful), the building architect installs modular partitions inside every floor — creating multiple self-contained apartments (the **virtual machines / virtual nodes**). Tenants (workloads) check in and out without disturbing the building's foundation. A virtual cluster is the dynamic grouping of apartments across floors that currently house a particular company's employees. The **building manager** (resource manager / scheduler) decides who gets which apartment, monitors water/electricity usage (CPU/memory), and reassigns units when tenants leave.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Standard Metrics

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **SLO** | Service Level Objective — target reliability/performance contract |
| **VM** | Virtual Machine — software-emulated compute instance |
| **VMM** | Virtual Machine Monitor — synonym for Hypervisor |
| **QoS** | Quality of Service — priority-based resource guarantee |
| **DRS** | Distributed Resource Scheduler (VMware terminology) |
| **NUMA** | Non-Uniform Memory Access — memory locality-aware scheduling |
| **Overcommitment Ratio** | Ratio of provisioned virtual resources to physical resources |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A virtual cluster is **NOT** the same as a physical cluster — its membership and topology can be reconfigured at runtime, often without service interruption. This is the single highest-weight concept tested in KTU ESE questions on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of a Virtual Cluster — Layered View

A virtual cluster is built from four operational layers stacked above the physical substrate:

1. **Physical Layer** — Bare-metal servers, switches, SAN/NAS storage, and power infrastructure.
2. **Virtualization Layer** — Hypervisors (Type-1: ESXi, Xen, KVM; Type-2: VirtualBox) that partition hardware.
3. **Cluster Virtualization Layer** — Cluster manager (e.g., vCenter, OpenStack, Kubernetes) that pools VMs into a unified logical cluster.
4. **Workload Layer** — Distributed applications (Hadoop, MPI jobs, microservices) that consume the cluster abstraction.

### 2.2 Why Virtual Clusters? — Operational Drivers

- **Elastic Scalability**: New virtual nodes can be instantiated in seconds, far faster than procuring physical hardware.
- **High Availability via Live Migration**: VMs can be relocated between physical hosts while running, eliminating planned downtime.
- **Hardware Decoupling**: Workloads are insulated from physical failures and hardware refresh cycles.
- **Multi-tenancy**: Several independent virtual clusters (one per customer/team) can coexist on shared hardware with strict isolation.
- **Energy Efficiency**: Idle physical hosts can be powered down, consolidating workloads onto fewer machines (Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling coupled with VM consolidation).

### 2.3 Resource Management — The Five Functional Pillars

| Pillar | Function | KTU Exam Hook |
|---|---|---|
| **Provisioning** | Allocating initial resource quotas | "Explain admission control." |
| **Scheduling** | Placing workloads onto nodes | "Compare greedy vs. fair scheduling." |
| **Monitoring** | Telemetry collection (CPU, RAM, I/O) | "List monitoring metrics." |
| **Contention Resolution** | Mediating shared-resource conflicts | "Discuss resource isolation." |
| **Reclamation** | Garbage-collecting unused capacity | "What is ballooning?" |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Resource Management Mathematics

> [!NOTE]
> These formulas are the **must-know quantitative tools** for any numerical sub-part in the 14-mark ESE questions. Use `\vert` instead of `|` in any inline expressions to avoid markdown table breakage.

| Formula / Metric | Mathematical Form | Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|
| **CPU Utilization** | $U_{cpu} = \dfrac{T_{active}}{T_{total}}$ | Dimensionless ratio, 0 ≤ U ≤ 1 |
| **Effective Utilization** | $U_{eff} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot U_i$ | Weighted aggregate across n resources |
| **Overcommitment Ratio** | $R_{oc} = \dfrac{\sum V_{provisioned}}{\sum P_{physical}}$ | Ratio of virtual to physical capacity |
| **Memory Pressure Index** | $MPI = \dfrac{M_{used} + M_{cache}}{M_{total}}$ | 0 ≤ MPI ≤ 1; threshold-driven |
| **Scheduler Fairness (Jain)** | $F(x) = \dfrac{\left(\sum x_i\right)^2}{n \cdot \sum x_i^{2}}$ | $F=1$ perfectly fair; range $\left[ \tfrac{1}{n},\ 1 \right]$ |
| **QoS Penalty** | $P_{qos} = \alpha \cdot (SLA_{miss}) + \beta \cdot (L_{latency} - L_{target})$ | Weighted penalty score |
| **Live Migration Downtime** | $T_{down} \approx \dfrac{M_{dirty}}{B_{link}}$ | Seconds; function of memory dirtying rate |
| **Amdahl's Parallel Efficiency** | $E_{par} = \dfrac{1}{\left( f_{serial} + \dfrac{f_{parallel}}{N} \right) \cdot T_{seq}}$ | N = node count |
| **VM Packing Density** | $\rho = \dfrac{N_{vm}}{N_{core}}$ | VMs per physical core |
| **Cluster Power Model** | $P_{total} = P_{idle} + \sum_{i} P_{dyn,i}$ | Watts; $P_{dyn} \propto C \cdot V^{2} \cdot f$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Application Spotlight:** Major cloud providers (AWS, Azure, GCP) operate virtual clusters at scales exceeding **1 million physical hosts**, with target overcommitment ratios of **2:1 to 4:1 for CPU** and **1.5:1 for memory**. The resource manager (e.g., AWS's Auto Scaling Groups + Capacity Rebalancing) must handle petabyte-scale telemetry, millisecond-level scheduling decisions, and 99.99% SLO enforcement — exactly the architectural pattern KTU tests via Part B design questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — VM Live Migration Downtime Estimate

Live migration moves a running VM from a source physical host (P_src) to a destination host (P_dst) with minimal service interruption. The classical Nelson/Klingenstein/Plank model approximates the **total migration time** and **downtime**.

**Step 1 — Define the pre-copy iteration.**
During iteration $k$, the hypervisor transfers memory pages dirtied since iteration $k-1$. Let:

- $V$ = total VM memory (bytes)
- $D_k$ = dirty rate during iteration $k$ (bytes/sec)
- $B$ = network bandwidth between P_src and P_dst (bytes/sec)

**Step 2 — Express the data transferred in iteration $k$.**

$$
D_{transferred,k} = \min\!\left( V,\ \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} D_j \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Write the time consumed by iteration $k$.**

$$
T_k = \dfrac{D_{transferred,k}}{B}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the residual memory at the end of iteration $k$.**
After each iteration, residual memory equals the dirty rate during that iteration (assuming worst case):

$$
V_k = D_k \quad \text{(steady-state assumption)}
$$

**Step 5 — Stop-and-copy phase (final iteration).**
When $V_k$ falls below a threshold $\theta$, the hypervisor pauses the VM, transfers remaining state, and resumes on P_dst. The **downtime** is:

$$
T_{down} = \dfrac{V_{k_{final}}}{B}
$$

**Step 6 — Total migration time.**

$$
T_{total} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} T_k + T_{down}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Total migration time is dominated by the iterative pre-copy phase; downtime is bounded by a single final transfer. KTU frequently tests the conceptual ordering: *pre-copy iterations → stop-and-copy → resume*. Mark loss here usually means reversing these two steps.

### 3.2 Derivation — Cluster Resource Fairness (Jain's Index)

When multiple tenants share a virtual cluster, scheduling fairness is measured using **Jain's Fairness Index**.

**Step 1 — Let $x_i$ be the resource share allocated to tenant $i$, for $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$.**

**Step 2 — Compute the arithmetic mean.**

$$
\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the mean of squares.**

$$
\overline{x^2} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Form the index.**

$$
F(x) = \dfrac{\bar{x}^{2}}{\overline{x^{2}}} = \dfrac{\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\right)^{2}}{n \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Bound the result.**

$$
\dfrac{1}{n} \leq F(x) \leq 1
$$

- $F = 1$ implies **perfect fairness** (all $x_i$ equal).
- $F = \tfrac{1}{n}$ implies **maximum unfairness** (one tenant gets everything).

**Step 6 — Worked numerical example.**
Suppose a cluster scheduler allocates CPU shares to 4 tenants: $x_1 = 10$, $x_2 = 20$, $x_3 = 30$, $x_4 = 40$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum x_i &= 10 + 20 + 30 + 40 = 100 \\
\sum x_i^{2} &= 100 + 400 + 900 + 1600 = 3000 \\
F(x) &= \dfrac{(100)^{2}}{4 \times 3000} = \dfrac{10000}{12000} = \dfrac{5}{6} \approx 0.8333
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A Jain's index of **0.83** indicates reasonably fair allocation. If one tenant's share doubled to 80 while others remained, the index would drop to ~0.62 — signaling inequity. KTU loves this type of 4-mark numerical sub-question.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Scheduling Policy Comparison

| Dimension | **Greedy / First-Fit** | **Fair Scheduling (DRF)** | **Priority / QoS-Aware** | **Energy-Aware** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Maximize immediate throughput | Maximize min-share across tenants | Honor latency-critical SLOs | Minimize power draw |
| **Best For** | Batch jobs | Multi-tenant clouds | Real-time analytics | Off-peak consolidation |
| **Weakness** | Starves small jobs | Sub-optimal for monolithic jobs | Complex admission control | Risk of SLA breach |
| **Complexity** | Low ($O(n)$) | Medium ($O(n \log n)$) | High ($O(n \cdot \log n)$) | High |
| **KTU Use Case** | MapReduce slot allocation | Hadoop YARN Capacity Scheduler | Spark Streaming + Kafka | Green data centers |
| **Key Metric** | Throughput (jobs/sec) | Dominant Resource Fairness | Tail latency (p99) | PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness) |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Resource Management Strategies in Virtual Clusters

| Strategy | Mechanism | Performance Gain | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CPU Pinning** | Bind vCPUs to physical cores | Eliminates context-switch noise | Reduces consolidation ratio |
| **Memory Ballooning** | Reclaim idle guest memory via driver | +15-30% effective RAM | Slow under pressure |
| **Transparent Page Sharing (TPS)** | Hash identical memory pages, store once | +20-40% memory savings | Security concern (cross-VM leak) |
| **NUMA Affinity** | Schedule VM on local-socket memory | +10-25% memory bandwidth | Constrains placement |
| **I/O Throttling** | Cap IOPS per VM via cgroups/blkio | Predictable storage latency | Penalizes bursty workloads |
| **VM Consolidation** | Migrate VMs to fewer hosts | Power savings ~30-70% | Risk of overload |

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Live Migration Algorithms

| Algorithm | Technique | Downtime | Total Time | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pre-copy (Xen)** | Iterative dirty-page transfer | Medium | Long | Memory-resident VMs |
| **Post-copy** | Resume on dest, demand-pull pages | Short | Variable | Tight-SLA VMs |
| **Hybrid (CR/RT-Motion)** | Adaptive switching | Low | Medium | Modern hypervisors |
| **Checkpoint/Restart** | Suspend, snapshot, resume | Equal to full copy | Predictable | Fault-tolerance, not live |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Layered Virtual Cluster Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph WL["WORKLOAD LAYER"]
        APP1["Distributed Application A"]
        APP2["MapReduce Job"]
        APP3["Microservices Cluster"]
    end

    subgraph CVL["CLUSTER VIRTUALIZATION LAYER"]
        CM["Cluster Manager / Orchestrator"]
        SCH["Scheduler with Admission Control"]
        MON["Telemetry and Monitoring Agent"]
        LB["Load Balancer"]
    end

    subgraph VL["VIRTUALIZATION LAYER"]
        HYP1["Hypervisor on Node 1"]
```
