```mermaid
        HYP2["Hypervisor on Node 2"]
        HYP3["Hypervisor on Node N"]
    end

    subgraph PL["PHYSICAL LAYER"]
        CPU["CPU and Memory Pools"]
        NET["Network Fabric and SAN"]
        PWR["Power and Cooling"]
    end

    APP1 --> CM
    APP2 --> CM
    APP3 --> CM
    CM --> SCH
    CM --> MON
    CM --> LB
    SCH --> HYP1
    SCH --> HYP2
    SCH --> HYP3
    HYP1 --> CPU
    HYP2 --> CPU
    HYP3 --> CPU
    HYP1 --> NET
    HYP2 --> NET
    HYP3 --> NET
    CPU --> PWR
    NET --> PWR
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Resource Management Lifecycle

```mermaid
graph LR
    REQ["Workload Arrival and QoS Request"] --> ADM["Admission Control"]
    ADM --> DEC1{"Resources Available?"}
    DEC1 -- Yes --> SCHED["Scheduler Placement Decision"]
    DEC1 -- No --> QUEUE["Queue or Reject"]
    SCHED --> ALLO["Allocate CPU, Memory, IO"]
    ALLO --> MON["Continuous Telemetry Monitoring"]
    MON --> DEC2{"SLO Violated?"}
    DEC2 -- Yes --> MIG["Live Migration or Rebalance"]
    DEC2 -- No --> MON
    MIG --> SCHED
    MON --> DONE["Workload Termination and Reclamation"]
    DONE --> REQ
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — VM Live Migration Pre-Copy Sequence

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["VM Running on Source Host P_src"] --> S2["Iteration 1: Transfer All Memory"]
    S2 --> S3["VM Continues Running, Pages Dirtied"]
    S3 --> S4["Iteration k: Transfer Only Dirty Pages"]
    S4 --> S5{"Residual Less Than Threshold?"}
    S5 -- No --> S3
    S5 -- Yes --> S6["Stop and Copy: Freeze VM Briefly"]
    S6 --> S7["Transfer Final CPU and Device State"]
    S7 --> S8["Resume VM on Destination Host P_dst"]
    S8 --> S9["Free Source Resources, Update Cluster Map"]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Multi-Tenant Fair Sharing via DRF

```mermaid
graph TD
    T1["Tenant A: 10 CPU, 100 GB RAM"] --> DRF["DRF Scheduler"]
    T2["Tenant B: 5 CPU, 200 GB RAM"] --> DRF
    T3["Tenant C: 20 CPU, 50 GB RAM"] --> DRF
    DRF --> CALC["Compute Dominant Share Per Tenant"]
    CALC --> RANK["Rank by Minimum Dominant Share"]
    RANK --> ALLOC["Allocate to Tenant with Lowest Share"]
    ALLOC --> FB["Feedback Loop Re-evaluate Every Epoch"]
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram — VM Consolidation for Energy Savings

```mermaid
graph LR
    ORIG["Initial Spread: 20 VMs on 10 Hosts"] --> TRIG["Trigger: Avg Utilization Below 30%"]
    TRIG --> SEL["Select VMs for Migration"]
    SEL --> IDENT["Identify Target Underutilized Hosts"]
    IDENT --> MIG["Live Migrate Selected VMs"]
    MIG --> OFF["Power Off Empty Hosts"]
    OFF --> RESULT["Result: 20 VMs on 4 Hosts, 6 Hosts Powered Down"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**  
*Define a virtual cluster. How does it differ from a physical cluster?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **virtual cluster** is an aggregation of virtual machines distributed across physical hosts, managed by a hypervisor and cluster virtualization layer to behave as a single logical cluster.

| Aspect | Physical Cluster | Virtual Cluster |
|---|---|---|
| Composition | Real servers | Virtual machines on real servers |
| Scalability | Days/weeks | Minutes/seconds |
| Reconfiguration | Hardware change | Software re-allocation |
| Failure Domain | Per-machine | Per-VM with live migration |

*(Approx. 1 mark each: definition + 2 differentiating points)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**  
*List any three resource management strategies in virtual clusters and their purpose.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Admission Control** — prevents resource overcommitment by validating workload requirements before deployment.
2. **Live Migration** — relocates VMs across physical hosts to balance load or avoid failures.
3. **Memory Ballooning** — reclaims idle guest memory dynamically to raise effective cluster memory capacity.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Internal Choice Pattern:** Module 3 ESE questions typically offer two 14-mark alternatives, each split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Both alternatives below are calibrated to that pattern.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme ESE]**

**(a)** Explain the **architecture of a virtual cluster** with a neat layered diagram. Discuss the role of the hypervisor and cluster manager. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe **VM live migration** using the pre-copy technique. Derive an expression for the total migration time and downtime. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Virtual Cluster Architecture (7 marks):**

A virtual cluster has **four operational layers** stacked above the physical substrate:

1. **Physical Layer** *(1 mark)* — Bare-metal servers with CPU, memory, disk, and network. Provides the raw compute, storage, and communication substrate. Power and cooling infrastructure is part of this layer.

2. **Virtualization Layer** *(1.5 marks)* — A **Type-1 hypervisor** (ESXi, Xen, KVM) runs directly on hardware and partitions each physical node into multiple isolated VMs. The hypervisor exposes virtual CPUs, virtual memory, virtual NICs, and virtual disks to each guest OS, and it enforces resource quotas via scheduling.

3. **Cluster Virtualization Layer** *(2 marks)* — A **cluster manager** (vCenter, OpenStack, Kubernetes) pools the VMs from all physical nodes into a unified logical cluster. It exposes APIs for VM lifecycle, distributes workloads, and provides:
   - Centralized scheduling
   - Live migration across hosts
   - High availability (restart on host failure)
   - Distributed resource scheduling (DRS)

4. **Workload Layer** *(1.5 marks)* — Distributed applications (Hadoop, MPI jobs, microservices, web tiers) deploy onto the virtual cluster, oblivious to the underlying physical topology.

**Role of the Hypervisor:** Hardware partitioning, isolation, and VM lifecycle control.  
**Role of the Cluster Manager:** Aggregation, cross-host orchestration, and policy enforcement.

*(Refer to the layered block diagram in Section 4.1 for visual reinforcement — 1 mark for diagram.)*

---

**(b) Live Migration — Pre-Copy (7 marks):**

In **pre-copy live migration**, the VM continues running on the source host while its memory pages are iteratively copied to the destination. The procedure is:

**Step 1** *(1 mark)* — **Initial bulk transfer:** All memory pages are copied from P_src to P_dst over the network.

**Step 2** *(1 mark)* — **Iterative dirty-page transfer:** While the VM runs, certain pages get dirtied. Each subsequent iteration transfers only these dirty pages, shrinking the residual set.

**Step 3** *(1 mark)* — **Stopping condition:** When the residual dirty memory falls below a threshold $\theta$ (typically a few MB) or after a maximum iteration count, the procedure transitions to stop-and-copy.

**Step 4** *(1 mark)* — **Stop-and-copy phase:** The VM is paused on P_src, the residual pages and CPU state are transferred, and the VM resumes on P_dst. The brief pause defines the **downtime**.

**Mathematical Model** *(2.5 marks)*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_k &= \dfrac{D_k}{B} \quad \text{(time for iteration k)} \\[6pt]
V_{k+1} &= D_k \quad \text{(residual at end of iteration k)} \\[6pt]
T_{down} &= \dfrac{V_{n}}{B} \quad \text{(final transfer during pause)} \\[6pt]
T_{total} &= \sum_{k=1}^{n} T_k + T_{down}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $V$ is total VM memory, $D_k$ is the dirtying rate during iteration $k$, and $B$ is the available network bandwidth.

**Advantages:** Zero data loss, transparent to applications.  
**Disadvantages:** Long total migration time for write-intensive workloads; worst-case downtime can grow if dirtying rate exceeds transfer rate (convergence failure).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme ESE]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain **live migration of virtual machines** across physical hosts. List two advantages and one disadvantage. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss **resource management strategies** in virtual clusters. Compare at least four strategies in a tabular form. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Live Migration (7 marks):**

**Definition** *(1 mark)*: Live migration is the process of relocating a running VM from one physical host to another without disrupting the services running inside the VM.

**Procedure** *(4 marks — 1 mark per step)*:

1. **Pre-migration:** Initiator signals target host; resources are reserved.
2. **Reservation:** Target host reserves CPU, memory, and network capacity.
3. **Iterative pre-copy:** Memory pages are transferred iteratively while the VM continues running on the source.
4. **Stop-and-copy:** VM is frozen; residual state + CPU registers are sent.
5. **Activation:** VM resumes on target host; source resources are released.
6. **Post-migration:** Source VM image is deleted; cluster membership tables are updated.

**Advantages** *(1 mark — 0.5 each)*:
- **Zero downtime** for end users (typically < 1 second perceived).
- Enables **load balancing** and **proactive fault avoidance**.

**Disadvantage** *(1 mark)*:
- High network bandwidth consumption during iterative pre-copy; performance degradation if dirtying rate exceeds transfer rate.

*(Refer to the live-migration block diagram in Section 4.3.)*

---

**(b) Resource Management Strategies (7 marks):**

Resource management in virtual clusters refers to coordinated allocation, scheduling, monitoring, and reclamation of CPU, memory, storage, and network resources across competing workloads.

**Comparison Table** *(6 marks — 1.5 per row × 4 strategies)*:

| Strategy | Mechanism | Benefit | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CPU Pinning** | Bind vCPUs to fixed pCPUs | Predictable performance, no scheduler jitter | Reduced consolidation, lower utilization |
| **Memory Ballooning** | Guest driver requests memory back | Reclaims idle memory, higher density | Slow under memory pressure |
| **Transparent Page Sharing** | Dedup identical memory pages across VMs | 20-40% memory savings | Cross-VM information leakage risk |
| **NUMA-Aware Scheduling** | Place VM on local memory node | +10-25% memory bandwidth | Restricts scheduler flexibility |
| **VM Consolidation** | Migrate VMs to fewer hosts | Power savings 30-70% | Risk of overload, longer migration windows |

**Conclusion** *(1 mark)*: Modern virtual cluster managers combine multiple strategies adaptively — for instance, ballooning + page sharing for memory, NUMA affinity + CPU pinning for performance-critical VMs, and consolidation during off-peak hours for energy savings.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most Common Mark Loss:**
> 1. **Confusing pre-copy with post-copy:** In pre-copy, *iterations happen before* the VM is paused; in post-copy, the VM is *resumed on the destination before* all pages arrive. Reversing this order costs full marks on sub-part (b).
> 2. **Omitting the iterative dirtying rate equation:** Writing only "pages are transferred" without $D_k$ and $B$ will deduct 2-3 marks in numerical derivations.
> 3. **Missing the resource reclamation pillar:** When listing "five pillars of resource management," students often forget **reclamation** (ballooning, TPS, idle-VM reclamation) and only list 4. Examiner deducts 1 mark explicitly.
> 4. **Conflating cluster manager with hypervisor:** Cluster manager operates *across hosts*; hypervisor operates *within one host*. Examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Virtual cluster** = aggregation of VMs behaving as a single logical cluster; reconfigurable at runtime.
- **Four-layer architecture:** Physical → Virtualization → Cluster Virtualization → Workload.
- **Five pillars of resource management:** Provisioning, Scheduling, Monitoring, Contention Resolution, **Reclamation**.
- **Key quantitative tools:** Jain's Fairness Index $F(x)$, Overcommitment Ratio $R_{oc}$, Live-migration downtime $T_{down} = V_n / B$.
- **DRF (Dominant Resource Fairness)** is the multi-resource generalization of max-min fairness and is the KTU-preferred answer for multi-tenant scenarios.
- **Pre-copy live migration:** iterative dirty-page transfer → stop-and-copy → resume.
- **Post-copy live migration:** VM resumes on destination, then demand-pulls missing pages from source.
- **Energy efficiency** is achieved via VM consolidation + DVFS; typically **30-70%** power savings.
- **NUMA-aware scheduling** yields **10-25%** memory bandwidth gains.
- **Transparent Page Sharing (TPS)** is **disabled by default in modern hypervisors** (security concern).
- Always draw a **layered diagram** in 7-mark questions — examiners reward visual clarity with at least **1 bonus mark** under CO mapping.
- Recall standard metrics: **PUE**, **Jain's Index**, **Amdahl's Efficiency**, and **Overcommitment Ratio**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Live VM Migration Steps, Migration of Memory, Files and Network Resources
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Live VM Migration Steps, Migration of Memory, Files and Network Resources

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Live VM Migration?

**Live Virtual Machine (VM) Migration** is the process of moving a running virtual machine from one physical host (the **source**) to another physical host (the **destination**) **without disrupting the executing applications, network connections, or services** running inside the guest OS. The VM's downtime is reduced to a few milliseconds, typically invisible to the end user.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Context):**
> Live migration refers to the movement of a running VM from one physical server to another with **near-zero perceived downtime**, preserving the execution state, network identity, and storage connectivity of the guest.

### Core Migration States That Must Be Transferred
A live VM carries **three orthogonal state vectors** between hosts:

1. **Memory State** — The volatile RAM contents (page tables, dirty pages, cache).
2. **Storage State** — The virtual disks (block-level or file-level).
3. **Network State** — Active TCP/UDP sockets, IP & MAC addresses, routing context.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The official downtime budget in production-grade live migration (e.g., VMware vMotion, Xen, KVM) is typically **< 1 second**, with total migration time ranging from **a few seconds to several minutes** depending on workload memory footprint and network bandwidth.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Relocation" Analogy

Imagine a **critically ill patient** in Hospital A who must be shifted to Hospital B **without ever losing vital signs or consciousness**:

| Hospital Relocation Analogy | Live VM Migration Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The **patient's body** (vital organs) | The **VM's memory** (RAM contents) |
| The **patient's medical records & X-rays** | The **VM's disk image** (virtual hard disk) |
| The **patient's identity card & contact info** | The **VM's IP & MAC address** |
| The **ambulance ride itself** | The **migration process / network transfer** |
| The **brief moment of disconnection** | **Downtime** (must be near zero) |

Just as doctors copy records ahead of time, transport the patient, then transfer remaining details after arrival — the hypervisor executes a **multi-phase, pre-copy-based transfer** to keep the patient (VM) "alive" throughout.

---

## 1.3 Why Live Migration Matters — Engineering Motivation

- **Proactive Fault Tolerance**: Evacuate VMs from a host showing thermal or hardware failure signs.
- **Load Balancing**: Redistribute workloads to avoid hot-spots (e.g., CPU > 90%).
- **Power Management**: Consolidate workloads and power down idle servers (Green Computing).
- **Disaster Recovery & Maintenance**: Hardware upgrades, kernel patching, datacenter migrations.
- **Cloud Elasticity**: Move VMs closer to users (geo-distribution) or to better-priced resources.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping:** This topic directly addresses **CO3 — Design and evaluate virtualization and resource management strategies for distributed computing systems.**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Canonical Live Migration Pipeline — 6 Standardized Steps

The live migration process (originally formalized in the seminal **Clark, Fraser, Hand, Hansen, Jul, Limpach, Pratt, Warfield, 2005 — "Live Migration of Virtual Machines"** paper from the University of Cambridge Xen team) follows this established sequence:

### **STEP 1 — Pre-Migration Checks & Reservation**
The source host verifies that the destination host has sufficient resources:
- **CPU**: Enough virtual cores available.
- **Memory**: Free RAM ≥ VM's allocated memory.
- **Storage**: Shared file system or SAN access confirmed.
- **Network**: Layer-2 reachability (or tunneling configured).

The destination host **reserves** the necessary capacity, ensuring the VM will not be admitted to a host that is itself overloaded (preventing cascading migration failure).

### **STEP 2 — Iterative Pre-Copy (Memory Pre-Transfer)**
The source hypervisor iteratively transfers the VM's memory pages to the destination **while the VM is still running**. This is the dominant time-consumer.

- **Round 1 (Worst-case):** Transfer *all* dirty + clean pages.
- **Round k:** Transfer only those pages dirtied *during* Round k-1 (convergence).

This stage uses the standard dirty-page tracking mechanism (page-fault shadow pages or hardware-assisted dirty-bit tracking via **Nested Page Tables / EPT**).

### **STEP 3 — Stop-and-Copy (The Critical Freeze)**
When the iteration count achieves a **convergence threshold** (i.e., the remaining dirty pages are below a threshold, typically **< 256 pages** for safety):

1. The source VM is **suspended** (milliseconds).
2. CPU state, registers, and remaining dirty pages are transferred.
3. Network state is committed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **only measurable downtime** in the entire migration. KTU examiners treat this as the defining moment.

### **STEP 4 — Commitment & Activation**
The destination hypervisor **asserts and confirms** receipt of:
- All memory pages.
- CPU register state (PC, SP, PSW, etc.).
- Device/IO state.

The VM is then **resumed on the destination host**.

### **STEP 5 — Network State Handover**
The VM's IP & MAC addresses are migrated to the destination. This is non-trivial because:
- Network switches have learned the old port → MAC mapping.
- A gratuitous **ARP reply** (broadcast) is sent to update all L2 switches: *"Hey, IP X.X.X.X is now at MAC YY:YY:YY at port Z."*

### **STEP 6 — Source Cleanup**
The source host releases:
- Memory reservations.
- Storage handles (if no longer needed).
- Network bindings.

The VM continues execution on the destination, unaware of the transition.

---

## 2.2 Migration of Memory — Three Paradigms

### **Paradigm A: Pre-Copy Migration**
Memory is transferred in **rounds** before stopping the VM.

- **Pros:** Low downtime; VM keeps running on source.
- **Cons:** Can be slow for write-intensive workloads (W₂L = *Worst-case Working Set Limitation*).

### **Paradigm B: Post-Copy Migration**
Memory pages are transferred **lazily after** the VM resumes on the destination. The destination pulls pages on-demand via **network faults**.

- **Pros:** No iterative loops; faster total migration for write-heavy VMs.
- **Cons:** Higher page-fault penalty during execution; network-fault handler must be efficient.

### **Paradigm C: Hybrid (Pre-Copy → Post-Copy Fallback)**
Used in production systems like **XenMotion** and **KVM Live Migration**:
1. Start with pre-copy.
2. If convergence fails (dirty rate ≥ network bandwidth × ε), switch to post-copy.
3. Best of both worlds.

---

## 2.3 Migration of Files & Block Storage

Live migration requires the **virtual disk** to be accessible from both source and destination. There are two principal architectures:

### **Architecture A: Shared Storage (SAN / NAS)**
- The VM's virtual disk resides on a **shared LUN** (iSCSI, FC) or NFS share.
- Only **pointer-level redirection** is needed — the disk does not move.
- **Gold standard for live migration** (used by vMotion).

### **Architecture B: Local-Storage Migration (Block-Level Copy)**
- The VM's disk is on **local storage** of the source.
- The hypervisor performs a **synchronous block copy** (bit-for-bit) before migration.
- Used when shared storage is unavailable.

---

## 2.4 Migration of Network Resources

Network migration ensures the VM retains its IP, MAC, and active connections:

- **IP & MAC Address Preservation**: The VM moves with its identity intact.
- **ARP Redirection**: Source issues **Gratuitous ARP (GARP)** to update switch tables.
- **Routing Continuity**: If the VM was reachable via a specific gateway/route, the new host must advertise the same prefix (handled by VLAN extension or tunneling).
- **TCP Connection Preservation**: Open sockets survive because the VM's kernel buffer state is migrated along with memory.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Metric** | **Expression** | **Description** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Total Migration Time** | $T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{pre-copy}} + T_{\text{stop-copy}} + T_{\text{resume}}$ | Wall-clock time for full migration | seconds |
| **Downtime** | $T_{\text{down}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{state}} + D_{\text{final}}}{B_{\text{net}}}$ | Time VM is suspended | seconds |
| **Pre-copy Convergence** | $W_{k+1} = D(W_k)$ | $W_k$ = working set in round $k$; $D$ = dirty rate per round | pages |
| **Network Bandwidth Required** | $B_{\text{req}} \geq R_{\text{dirty}} \cdot P_{\text{size}}$ | Must exceed the VM's dirty-page generation rate | MB/s |
| **Iteration Count Bound** | $k_{\max} = \left\lceil \log_{R} \!\left( \dfrac{M_{\text{vm}}}{P_{\text{threshold}}} \right) \right\rceil$ | Max rounds before stop-copy | rounds |
| **Dirty Rate** | $R_{\text{dirty}} = \dfrac{\Delta \text{DirtyPages}}{\Delta t}$ | Pages modified per second | pages/sec |
| **Memory Footprint** | $M_{\text{vm}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | Total resident pages of VM | MB |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where to use which formula in KTU exams:**
> - $T_{\text{down}}$ → Numerical "compute downtime" problems.
> - $k_{\max}$ → Working-set convergence problems.
> - $B_{\text{req}}$ → Bandwidth provisioning design problems.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AWS EC2 Live Migration**: Used during hardware maintenance transparently.
- **OpenStack Nova**: Supports both KVM (libvirt-based) and Xen live migration.
- **VMware vMotion**: Industry-standard proprietary implementation.
- **Microsoft Hyper-V Live Migration**: Uses SMB3 shared storage protocol.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Total Downtime in Pre-Copy Migration

**Problem Context:** Given a VM with $M_{\text{vm}}$ MB of memory, dirty rate $R_{\text{dirty}}$ pages/sec, network bandwidth $B_{\text{net}}$ MB/s, and a stop-copy threshold of $P_{\text{th}}$ pages, derive the total downtime.

### **Step 1 — Identify the Dominant Transfer**

During the *stop-and-copy* phase, the VM is suspended. The data that must cross the network during suspension is:

$$
\text{Data}_{\text{stop}} = V_{\text{state}} + P_{\text{th}}
$$

where $V_{\text{state}}$ is the small fixed volume of CPU/IO state (typically $\leq 16$ MB) and $P_{\text{th}}$ is the residual dirty pages still in flight.

### **Step 2 — Express Downtime as a Transfer Delay**

Downtime equals the time to push this data across the available bandwidth:

$$
T_{\text{down}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{state}} + P_{\text{th}}}{B_{\text{net}}}
$$

### **Step 3 — Convergence Check (Pre-Copy Loop Termination)**

The pre-copy loop converges when the residual working set satisfies:

$$
W_{k+1} \;<\; P_{\text{th}} \quad \text{(termination criterion)}
$$

The recursion $W_{k+1} = f(W_k)$ (where $f$ depends on the workload's dirty-rate function) must satisfy:

$$
\lim_{k \to \infty} W_k \;=\; 0 \;\;\; \text{iff} \;\;\; R_{\text{dirty}} < B_{\text{net}} \cdot \rho
$$

where $\rho$ is the *dirty-page ratio compression factor*. If this condition is violated → pre-copy **does not converge** → switch to post-copy.

### **Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example**

> **Given:** A VM has $M_{\text{vm}} = 4096$ MB of RAM. The dirty rate is $R_{\text{dirty}} = 500$ pages/s. Page size $P_{\text{size}} = 4$ KB. Network bandwidth $B_{\text{net}} = 100$ MB/s. Stop-copy threshold $P_{\text{th}} = 256$ pages. CPU state volume $V_{\text{state}} = 8$ MB.

**Step 4a — Compute dirty bytes per second:**

$$
R_{\text{dirty,bytes}} = 500 \;\frac{\text{pages}}{\text{s}} \times 4096 \;\frac{\text{bytes}}{\text{page}} = 2{,}048{,}000 \;\frac{\text{bytes}}{\text{s}} = 2 \;\text{MB/s}
$$

**Step 4b — Check convergence condition:**

$$
R_{\text{dirty,bytes}} = 2 \;\text{MB/s} \;\ll\; B_{\text{net}} = 100 \;\text{MB/s}
$$

Pre-copy converges easily. The migration is feasible.

**Step 4c — Compute the stop-copy threshold in bytes:**

$$
P_{\text{th,bytes}} = 256 \;\text{pages} \times 4096 \;\frac{\text{bytes}}{\text{page}} = 1{,}048{,}576 \;\text{bytes} \approx 1 \;\text{MB}
$$

**Step 4d — Compute total downtime:**

$$
T_{\text{down}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{state}} + P_{\text{th,bytes}}}{B_{\text{net}}} = \dfrac{8 + 1}{100} = \dfrac{9}{100} = 0.09 \;\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{down}} = 90 \;\text{milliseconds}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A sub-100 ms downtime is considered **near-zero perceived downtime**. Always show units clearly — losing marks for missing "MB/s" or "ms" is the #1 KTU valuation pitfall.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Iteration Count

### **Step 1 — Define the Working-Set Decay Model**

Assume the workload exhibits *geometric dirty reduction*: each round, the remaining working set shrinks by a factor $r \in (0,1)$.

$$
W_k = W_0 \cdot r^k
$$

### **Step 2 — Solve for k when $W_k \leq P_{\text{th}}$**

$$
W_0 \cdot r^k \;\leq\; P_{\text{th}}
$$

$$
r^k \;\leq\; \dfrac{P_{\text{th}}}{W_0}
$$

Applying $\log$ on both sides (note that $\log r < 0$ since $0 < r < 1$, so the inequality flips):

$$
k \;\geq\; \dfrac{\log(P_{\text{th}}/W_0)}{\log r}
$$

Since $k$ must be a positive integer:

$$
k_{\max} \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{\log P_{\text{th}} - \log W_0}{\log r} \right\rceil
$$

### **Step 3 — Numerical Illustration**

Let $W_0 = 1{,}048{,}576$ pages (4 GB VM), $P_{\text{th}} = 256$ pages, $r = 0.5$ (worst-case dirty reduction).

$$
k_{\max} \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{\log 256 - \log 1{,}048{,}576}{\log 0.5} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{8 - 20}{-0.301} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 39.8 \right\rceil = 40
$$

So the pre-copy loop performs **40 rounds** before stopping — total pre-copy time:

$$
T_{\text{pre-copy}} = \sum_{k=0}^{k_{\max}-1} \dfrac{W_k \cdot P_{\text{size}}}{B_{\text{net}}} = \dfrac{W_0 P_{\text{size}}}{B_{\text{net}}} \cdot \dfrac{1 - r^{k_{\max}}}{1 - r}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Parameter** | **Pre-Copy** | **Post-Copy** | **Hybrid** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Total Migration Time** | High (iterative) | Moderate (no iterations) | Optimized |
| **Downtime** | Very low (ms range) | Low but variable | Very low |
| **Memory Overhead on Dest.** | None (reserved upfront) | High (fault buffer) | Moderate |
| **Best Workload Type** | Read-heavy | Write-heavy | Mixed |
| **Failure Recovery** | VM continues on source | VM stuck on dest | VM continues on source |
| **Convergence Required?** | Yes (critical) | No | Yes (then fall back) |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Medium | High | Very High |
| **Used In** | VMware vMotion (classic), Xen pre-copy | Xen post-copy (Nicolaescu et al.) | KVM recent builds |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Live Migration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Source Host : VM Running"] --> B["Step 1 : Pre-Migration Resource Check"]
    B --> C["Step 2a : Round 1 Pre-Copy of Memory"]
    C --> D["Step 2b : Subsequent Rounds of Dirty Pages"]
    D --> E{"Convergence Achieved ?"}
    E -- "Yes" --> F["Step 3 : Stop and Copy VM"]
    E -- "No" --> G["Switch to Post-Copy Fallback"]
    G --> F
    F --> H["Step 4 : Transfer CPU State and Final Pages"]
    H --> I["Step 5a : Resume VM on Destination"]
    I --> J["Step 5b : Send Gratuitous ARP on Network"]
    J --> K["Step 6 : Cleanup Source Host Resources"]
    K --> L["Destination Host : VM Continues Running"]
```

## 4.2 Memory Migration Architecture (Pre-Copy Detail)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC["Source Host"]
        VM1["Guest VM Memory"]
        SHAD["Shadow Page Tables : Dirty Tracking"]
        VM1 -.dirty bit updates.-> SHAD
    end

    subgraph NET["Network Channel : Bandwidth B"]
        XFER["Iterative Page Transfer"]
    end

    subgraph DST["Destination Host"]
        VM2["Allocated Memory Buffer"]
        VM3["Resumed VM Execution"]
        VM2 --> VM3
    end

    SHAD -- "Round k : Dirty Pages" --> XFER
    XFER -- "Page Writes" --> VM2
```

## 4.3 Network Resource Migration Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant VM as Guest VM
    participant SH as Source Host Hypervisor
    participant SW as L2 Network Switch
    participant DH as Destination Host Hypervisor

    Note over VM,DH: Phase 1 : Before Migration
    VM->>SW: Normal Traffic on IP 10.0.0.5 MAC AA-AA-AA
    SW-->>SH: Switch Table : 10.0.0.5 at Port 1

    Note over VM,DH: Phase 2 : Stop and Copy
    SH->>DH: Migrate VM State via Tunnel
    SH->>VM: Suspend Execution

    Note over VM,DH: Phase 3 : Network Identity Transfer
    DH->>SW: Gratuitous ARP : 10.0.0.5 is at AA-AA-AA on Port 5
    SW-->>SH: Update Forwarding Table
    DH->>VM: Resume Execution on Destination

    Note over VM,DH: Phase 4 : After Migration
    VM->>SW: Traffic continues on same IP and MAC
```

## 4.4 Storage Migration Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SSA["Shared Storage Architecture : SAN or NFS"]
        DSK["Virtual Disk Image : vHDD.vmdk"]
    end

    subgraph SRC2["Source Host"]
        VM_S["VM : Read Write via FC iSCSI or NFS"]
    end

    subgraph DST2["Destination Host"]
        VM_D["VM : Same Disk Path via Shared LUN"]
    end

    VM_S -- "Pre Migration Access" --> DSK
    VM_D -- "Post Migration Access" --> DSK
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph LSA["Local Storage Migration : Block Copy"]
        SRC3["Source Local Disk : vmdisk.img"]
        DST3["Destination Local Disk : vmdisk.img Copy"]
    end

    BLK["Background Block Level Synchronization"]
    SRC3 -- "Initial Full Copy" --> BLK
    BLK -- "Incremental Bitmap Sync" --> DST3
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Live VM Migration. List any TWO benefits of performing live migration in a cloud data center.**

**Model Answer:**
Live VM Migration is the process of relocating a running virtual machine from one physical host (source) to another (destination) **without interrupting the executing services**, achieving **near-zero perceived downtime** (typically **< 1 second**).

**Two Benefits:**
1. **Load Balancing** — Distributes workloads to prevent hotspots and improves overall resource utilization.
2. **Proactive Fault Tolerance** — Evacuates VMs from hosts showing signs of hardware failure (overheating, memory errors, SMART disk warnings) before failure occurs.

*(Acceptable alternate benefits: power management, hardware maintenance without downtime, geo-distribution for latency.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the role of a Gratuitous ARP (GARP) in the context of live VM migration? Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer:**
A **Gratuitous ARP** is an unsolicited ARP broadcast packet sent by the destination hypervisor after the VM resumes execution. It contains the mapping:
- **IP Address** → **MAC Address** (unchanged from VM's identity)
- The packet informs all L2 switches in the subnet: *"The host owning IP X.X.X.X with MAC YY:YY:YY is now reachable on this new switch port."*

**Why Necessary:** Layer-2 switches maintain a forwarding table mapping MAC addresses to physical ports. Without the GARP, the switches would continue forwarding frames destined to the VM's MAC out the *source host's* port, breaking connectivity. The GARP updates the switch's CAM table atomically, enabling seamless network continuity.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Pre-Copy approach** of live VM migration with a neat sequence diagram. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A VM has a memory footprint of **$8$ GB** with a page size of **$4$ KB**. The dirty-page generation rate is **$2000$ pages/sec**. The available network bandwidth between source and destination is **$1$ Gbps**. The stop-copy threshold is **$512$ pages**, and the CPU state volume to be transferred during stop-and-copy is **$16$ MB**. Calculate:
   - (i) The **dirty rate in MB/s**.
   - (ii) The **downtime in milliseconds** during the stop-and-copy phase.
   - (iii) Comment on whether pre-copy will **converge** under these conditions. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

The **Pre-Copy approach** migrates memory in **iterative rounds while the VM continues running on the source host**.

**Steps:**
1. **Initial Bulk Transfer:** Round 0 copies the entire memory image to the destination.
2. **Dirty-Page Tracking:** A shadow page table (or EPT dirty bits) tracks pages modified during each round.
3. **Iterative Re-Transfer:** Each subsequent round transfers only those pages dirtied during the previous round.
4. **Convergence:** Loop continues until residual dirty pages ≤ threshold.
5. **Stop-and-Copy:** VM is suspended; final CPU state + residual pages transferred.
6. **Resume on Destination:** VM restarts on the destination host.

**Advantages:**
- Very **low downtime** (only stop-and-copy phase).
- **Failure-safe:** If destination fails mid-migration, VM continues on source.
- **Simple rollback** — no partial state on destination.

**Limitations:**
- **Slow for write-heavy workloads** — dirty rate may exceed transfer bandwidth, preventing convergence.
- **Wasted bandwidth** — pages transferred in round $k$ may be dirtied again in round $k+1$.
- **Not suitable for memory-intensive, write-dominant workloads** like in-memory databases.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given:** $M_{\text{vm}} = 8 \text{ GB}$, $P_{\text{size}} = 4 \text{ KB}$, $R_{\text{dirty}} = 2000 \text{ pages/s}$, $B_{\text{net}} = 1 \text{ Gbps}$, $P_{\text{th}} = 512 \text{ pages}$, $V_{\text{state}} = 16 \text{ MB}$.

**(i) Dirty Rate in MB/s:**

$$
R_{\text{dirty,bytes}} = 2000 \;\frac{\text{pages}}{\text{s}} \times 4 \;\frac{\text{KB}}{\text{page}} = 8000 \;\frac{\text{KB}}{\text{s}} = 8 \;\text{MB/s}
$$

**(ii) Downtime Calculation:**

First, compute the available bandwidth in MB/s:

$$
B_{\text{net}} = 1 \;\text{Gbps} = \dfrac{10^9}{8 \times 10^6} \;\text{MB/s} \approx 125 \;\text{MB/s}
$$

(assuming 1 byte = 8 bits; some texts use $10^9 / 8$ giving $125$ MB/s, or $10^9/8/1.024$ giving $\approx 119$ MB/s — we use the common KTU convention $125$ MB/s).

Threshold in bytes:

$$
P_{\text{th,bytes}} = 512 \times 4 \;\text{KB} = 2048 \;\text{KB} = 2 \;\text{MB}
$$

Downtime:

$$
T_{\text{down}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{state}} + P_{\text{th,bytes}}}{B_{\text{net}}} = \dfrac{16 + 2}{125} = \dfrac{18}{125} \approx 0.144 \;\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{down}} \approx 144 \;\text{milliseconds}}
$$

**(iii) Convergence Check:**

Since $R_{\text{dirty,bytes}} = 8 \;\text{MB/s} \;\ll\; B_{\text{net}} = 125 \;\text{MB/s}$, the network can comfortably absorb the dirty rate.

**Conclusion:** Pre-copy **will converge**. The migration is feasible under the given conditions.

---

### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternate]**

**(a)** Compare **Pre-Copy** and **Post-Copy** memory migration strategies. Discuss the **hybrid approach** and explain when it is preferred. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **migration of network resources** during live VM migration. Specifically discuss:
   - (i) IP and MAC address preservation.
   - (ii) The role of Gratuitous ARP.
   - (iii) How active TCP connections are preserved across migration. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

| **Aspect** | **Pre-Copy** | **Post-Copy** |
|---|---|---|
| Order | Memory transferred **before** VM stop | Memory transferred **after** VM resume |
| VM Running? | Yes during most of migration | Suspended during transfer, resumed on dest. |
| Downtime | Very low | Low but with page-fault penalties |
| Failure Recovery | Easy (VM on source) | Difficult (VM on dest.) |
| Suitable Workload | Read-heavy, low dirty rate | Write-heavy, large memory |

**Hybrid Approach:**
The hybrid strategy **starts with pre-copy** and dynamically **switches to post-copy** if the pre-copy loop fails to converge (i.e., when $R_{\text{dirty}} \geq B_{\text{net}}$).

**When Preferred:**
- Mixed workloads with unpredictable write patterns.
- Production cloud environments where workload behavior varies.
- When pre-copy convergence cannot be guaranteed in advance.
- Used in **KVM (Linux Kernel-based Virtual Machine)** for modern enterprise deployments.

**Advantage:** Combines pre-copy's low downtime with post-copy's guaranteed termination.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**(i) IP and MAC Address Preservation:**
During live migration, the VM retains its **original IP address** and **MAC address**. The destination host binds these to its virtual NIC. From the network's perspective, the VM is the same end-host — only the physical attachment point (switch port) has changed.

**(ii) Role of Gratuitous ARP:**
After the VM resumes on the destination, the hypervisor sends a **GARP packet** as a broadcast on the subnet. This informs all L2 switches: "IP X.X.X.X with MAC YY:YY:YY is now reachable on this port." Switches update their CAM tables instantly, ensuring subsequent frames are forwarded to the new location.

**(iii) TCP Connection Preservation:**
Since the VM's **kernel memory (including socket buffers, sequence numbers, and state variables)** is migrated along with the application memory, **all active TCP connections survive**. The VM continues from the exact kernel state at the moment of suspension. No packets are lost because:
- The TCP retransmission timer handles brief network invisibility.
- Sequence numbers are preserved.
- The application sees no interruption.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget to convert Gbps → MB/s using the factor $1/8$. Always show the conversion explicitly.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Conflating *Total Migration Time* with *Downtime*. They are **different** — total time includes pre-copy rounds; downtime is only the suspension window.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Students write "ARP" instead of "Gratuitous ARP" — these are different. A normal ARP is a request/reply; GARP is an **unsolicited announcement**.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Skipping units in numerical answers. KTU strictly deducts **0.5 mark per missing unit**.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** For pre-copy convergence problems, students often forget to **comment on the condition** ($R_{\text{dirty}} < B_{\text{net}}$) — simply computing the value is not enough.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ Live migration = movement of running VM with **near-zero downtime** (< 1 s).
- ✅ **Three state vectors** to migrate: **Memory**, **Files/Storage**, **Network**.
- ✅ **Six canonical steps:** Pre-check → Pre-copy (iterative) → Stop-copy → Commit → Network handover → Source cleanup.
- ✅ **Pre-copy**: Iterative memory transfer *before* stopping VM. Best for read-heavy workloads.
- ✅ **Post-copy**: Transfer memory *after* resume via demand paging. Best for write-heavy workloads.
- ✅ **Hybrid**: Starts with pre-copy, falls back to post-copy on non-convergence.
- ✅ **Downtime formula:** $T_{\text{down}} = (V_{\text{state}} + P_{\text{th}}) / B_{\text{net}}$
- ✅ **Convergence condition:** $R_{\text{dirty}} < B_{\text{net}}$ (otherwise pre-copy loops forever).
- ✅ **Shared Storage (SAN/NFS)** is the gold standard — only pointers move, not the disk.
- ✅ **Local-storage migration** requires full block-level copy before VM stop.
- ✅ **Gratuitous ARP** is essential to update L2 switch CAM tables after VM moves.
- ✅ **TCP connections survive** because socket state is in VM memory and migrates with it.
- ✅ **Production examples:** VMware vMotion, Xen live migration, KVM live migration, OpenStack Nova.
- ✅ **Use cases:** Load balancing, fault tolerance, power management, hardware maintenance.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Memory Aid (Mnemonic):**
> *"**C**opy, **C**opy, **C**ut, **C**ommit, **C**onnect, **C**lean"* — the six C's of live migration steps.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Computing, Microservices and Containers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cloud Computing, Microservices and Containers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Cloud Computing — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Cloud Computing** is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction. This is the formal definition adopted from the **NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology) Special Publication 800-145**, which is the canonical reference cited in the KTU 2024 PECMT632 syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Five Essential Characteristics of Cloud:**
> 1. **On-demand self-service** — provision compute automatically without human intervention.
> 2. **Broad network access** — accessible over the network via standard mechanisms (HTTP, REST).
> 3. **Resource pooling** — multi-tenant model with dynamic physical/virtual resource assignment.
> 4. **Rapid elasticity** — capabilities can be elastically scaled out or in.
> 5. **Measured service** — metering at a level appropriate to the service (pay-per-use).

### 1.2 Microservices — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Microservices** is an architectural style that structures an application as a collection of small, autonomous, loosely coupled services, each focused on doing one thing well, communicating over lightweight protocols (typically HTTP/REST or message queues), and independently deployable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (Lewis & Fowler, 2014):** "An approach to developing a single application as a suite of small services, each running in its own process and communicating with lightweight mechanisms, often an HTTP resource API."

### 1.3 Containers — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **Container** is a lightweight, standalone, executable package of software that includes everything needed to run an application: code, runtime, system tools, system libraries, and settings. Containers are isolated instances sharing the host OS kernel but running in user space.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Vocabulary Builder:** A container is a **standardized unit of software deployment** — a "shipping container" for code. The keyword in board exams is **OS-level virtualization** (not hardware virtualization like a VM).

### 1.4 The "Big Picture" Intuitive Analogy

| Concept | Real-World Analogy | Intuition |
|---------|-------------------|-----------|
| **Cloud Computing** | The **Electricity Grid** | You don't generate your own power anymore — you plug into a national grid and pay only for what you consume (kilowatt-hours). Similarly, cloud gives you compute, storage, and networking on a metered basis. |
| **Microservices** | A **Restaurant Kitchen Brigade** | A monolithic restaurant has one chef doing everything. A microservices kitchen has a *Pastry Chef*, *Saucier*, *Grill Chef*, and *Pastry Cook* — each specialized, independent, and replaceable. |
| **Containers** | A **Shipping Container** | Before 1956, loading cargo on ships was chaos. Malcom McLean standardized the *intermodal container* — a uniform steel box fitting on any ship, train, or truck. Software containers do the same for code: a uniform package fitting on any infrastructure. |
| **Docker** | The **Container Truck** | A tool/engine that builds, ships, and runs containers. |
| **Kubernetes** | The **Container Port Manager** | A system that decides where to place thousands of containers, scales them up/down, and heals failures — like a port orchestrating ships, cranes, and routes. |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for the Board Exam:** **C³ = C**loud, **C**ontainers, and microservices (a form of **C**omponent-ization). All three share the theme of **C**omposable, elastic, on-demand infrastructure.

### 1.5 Why These Three Concepts Are Grouped Together in Module 4

KTU groups these topics because they form a **synergistic stack**:

$$\text{Cloud Infrastructure} \rightarrow \text{Containers (Packaging)} \rightarrow \text{Microservices (Architecture)}$$

- **Cloud** provides the *underlying elastic infrastructure*.
- **Containers** provide the *portable unit of deployment* on that cloud.
- **Microservices** provide the *application architecture* that naturally maps to many containers, each scaling independently on the cloud.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 2024 Industry Reality (Important for Viva):** Netflix, Amazon, Uber, and Airbnb are the textbook KTU examples of microservices-on-cloud-via-containers. Netflix runs **~700+ microservices** on AWS using **Docker containers orchestrated by Kubernetes**.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Cloud Computing — Service Models (The "Stack")

The cloud service model is often taught as a **layered stack**. Each higher layer abstracts away the lower layers.

| Layer | Acronym | Full Form | What YOU Manage | What Provider Manages | KTU Example |
|-------|---------|-----------|-----------------|----------------------|-------------|
| **Bottom** | **IaaS** | Infrastructure-as-a-Service | OS, Runtime, App, Data | Virtualization, Servers, Storage, Networking | AWS EC2, Google Compute Engine, Azure VM |
| **Middle** | **PaaS** | Platform-as-a-Service | App + Data Only | OS, Runtime, Middleware, Virtualization | AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Google App Engine, Heroku |
| **Top** | **SaaS** | Software-as-a-Service | Only your data/config | Everything else (entire stack) | Gmail, Microsoft 365, Salesforce |
| **Serverless** | **FaaS** | Function-as-a-Service | Only function code | All infrastructure + scaling | AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, Google Cloud Functions |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Frequently Asked:** "Differentiate IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS." Memorize the **responsibility column** — that is the most-likely 7-mark question in Part B.

### 2.2 Cloud Computing — Deployment Models

| Model | Description | Ownership | KTU Example |
|-------|-------------|-----------|-------------|
| **Public Cloud** | Open to general public via internet | Third-party provider (AWS, Azure, GCP) | AWS EC2 |
| **Private Cloud** | Operated solely for one organization | Single organization | OpenStack on-premise |
| **Hybrid Cloud** | Composition of two or more clouds (public+private) | Both — with orchestration between them | AWS Outposts + on-prem |
| **Community Cloud** | Shared by organizations with common concerns (e.g., government, healthcare) | Several organizations | GovCloud |

### 2.3 Microservices — Core Principles (Lewis & Fowler)

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically emphasizes these **9 characteristics** of microservices:

1. **Componentization via Services** — Replaceable units.
2. **Organized around Business Capability** — Not technical layers.
3. **Decentralized Governance** — Each team picks its own stack (polyglot).
4. **Decentralized Data Storage** — Each service owns its database.
5. **Infrastructure Automation** — CI/CD pipelines.
6. **Smart Endpoints and Dumb Pipes** — Logic in services, not in ESB/bus.
7. **Design for Failure** — Expect services to fail; build resilience (circuit breakers).
8. **Evolutionary Design** — Replace services over time.
9. **Product not Project** — DevOps ownership ("You build it, you run it").

### 2.4 Containers vs Virtual Machines — The Crucial Distinction

This is a **very common 7-mark KTU question** ("Compare containers and VMs").

| Feature | Virtual Machine (VM) | Container |
|---------|---------------------|-----------|
| **Virtualization Level** | Hardware-level (Hypervisor) | OS-level (Container Engine) |
| **OS Kernel** | Each VM has its own kernel | All containers share host kernel |
| **Boot Time** | Minutes | Milliseconds to seconds |
| **Image Size** | Gigabytes (5–20 GB) | Megabytes (10–500 MB) |
| **Performance** | Near-native (~95%) | Native (~99%) |
| **Isolation** | Strong (separate kernel) | Weaker (process-level) |
| **Density** | 10–20 VMs per host | Hundreds of containers per host |
| **Startup Cost** | High RAM/CPU per VM | Low overhead per container |
| **Use Case** | Legacy apps, full OS control | Cloud-native, microservices |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Saying "containers are lightweight VMs". This is **WRONG**. They are **not VMs**. VMs virtualize hardware; containers virtualize the OS. The KTU examiner deducts 2 marks for this confusion.

### 2.5 Docker Architecture — The Triad

Docker uses a **client-server architecture**:

- **Docker Client (`docker` CLI)** — Sends commands via REST API.
- **Docker Daemon (`dockerd`)** — The server that builds, runs, and distributes containers.
- **Docker Registry** — Stores Docker images (e.g., Docker Hub).

**Key Docker Objects:**
- **Image** — Read-only template with instructions for creating a container (blueprint).
- **Container** — Runnable instance of an image.
- **Dockerfile** — Text file with build instructions.
- **Volume** — Persistent storage outside the container's writable layer.

### 2.6 Kubernetes (K8s) — Container Orchestration

Kubernetes is the **de-facto orchestrator** for containers. KTU 2024 lists the following core primitives:

| K8s Object | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| **Pod** | Smallest deployable unit; wraps 1+ containers sharing network/storage |
| **Deployment** | Manages a replicated set of pods; handles rollouts/rollbacks |
| **Service** | Stable network endpoint (load balancer) for a set of pods |
| **Namespace** | Virtual cluster for resource isolation |
| **Node** | Worker machine (VM/physical) running pods |
| **Cluster** | Set of nodes managed by a control plane |
| **ConfigMap/Secret** | External configuration injection |

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although cloud-native engineering is mostly architectural (not formula-driven), KTU 2024 expects students to know these **quantitative metrics** and **scaling formulas**. Critical: Use `\vert` instead of `\|` in markdown tables.

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Description |
|---------|------------------|-------------|
| **Container Density** | $D_c = \dfrac{N_{containers}}{N_{hosts}}$ | Number of containers per host |
| **VM Density** | $D_{vm} = \dfrac{N_{vms}}{N_{hosts}}$ | Typically 10–20× lower than $D_c$ |
| **Cost Savings (Containers vs VMs)** | $S_{\%} = \left(1 - \dfrac{C_{container}}{C_{vm}}\right) \times 100$ | Where $C$ is total cost of ownership |
| **Theoretical Max Containers per Host** | $N_{max} \approx \dfrac{RAM_{host}}{RAM_{container}}$ | Limited mainly by RAM |
| **Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) — Replicas** | $\text{Replicas} = \left\lceil \dfrac{\text{CurrentMetric}}{\text{TargetMetric}} \right\rceil$ | Kubernetes HPA decision formula |
| **Availability (Cloud SLA)** | $A = \dfrac{Uptime}{Uptime + Downtime} \times 100\%$ | E.g., 99.99% = "four nines" |
| **Downtime Budget per Year** | $D_{year} = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$ | In minutes/year |
| **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** | Max acceptable downtime after failure | Cloud SLA metric |
| **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** | Max acceptable data loss (in time) | Cloud SLA metric |
| **Resource Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{Used}{Allocated} \times 100\%$ | Kubernetes scheduler metric |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize the 99.99% Downtime Budget Calculation** — this is a high-yield KTU numerical problem.
> $D_{year} = (1 - 0.9999) \times 525600 \approx 52.56$ minutes/year.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cloud + Containers + Microservices** power **Netflix's streaming platform** (serving 250M+ users).
- **NASA** uses AWS cloud for Mars rover image processing.
- **Coca-Cola** migrated from on-premise VMs to Google Cloud containers, reducing infrastructure costs by ~**30%** (industry case study, not a KTU exam fact).
- **Spotify** uses microservices to handle **~10,000+ services** in production.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Container Resource Capacity Planning

This is a **typical KTU numerical problem**: "Given a host with 64 GB RAM, how many containers can it run, given each container needs 256 MB RAM? Account for 10% host OS overhead."

**Step 1 — Identify the given parameters.**

$$RAM_{host} = 64 \text{ GB}, \quad RAM_{container} = 256 \text{ MB}, \quad Overhead = 10\%$$

**Step 2 — Convert all units to a common base (MB).**

$$RAM_{host} = 64 \text{ GB} \times 1024 \text{ MB/GB} = 65536 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the usable RAM after subtracting OS overhead.**

$$RAM_{usable} = RAM_{host} \times (1 - Overhead)$$

$$RAM_{usable} = 65536 \times (1 - 0.10) = 65536 \times 0.90 = 58982.4 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the maximum number of containers using the formula.**

$$N_{max} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{RAM_{usable}}{RAM_{container}} \right\rfloor$$

$$N_{max} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{58982.4}{256} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 230.40 \right\rfloor = 230 \text{ containers}$$

**Step 5 — Practical adjustment (CPU bottleneck consideration).**

Assuming each container also needs 0.1 vCPU and the host has 16 vCPUs:

$$N_{cpu} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{16}{0.1} \right\rfloor = 160 \text{ containers}$$

**Step 6 — Final answer is the minimum of RAM-bound and CPU-bound.**

$$N_{actual} = \min(N_{max}, N_{cpu}) = \min(230, 160) = 160 \text{ containers}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The limiting factor is usually **CPU** for stateless microservices and **RAM** for in-memory caches (e.g., Redis). Always compute both.

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation: HPA Scaling Decision

**Problem:** A Kubernetes deployment has 3 replicas. CPU target = 70%. Current average CPU = 90%. HPA scaling formula:

$$\text{Replicas}_{new} = \left\lceil \text{Replicas}_{current} \times \dfrac{\text{CurrentMetric}}{\text{TargetMetric}} \right\rceil$$

**Step-by-step:**

$$\text{Replicas}_{new} = \left\lceil 3 \times \dfrac{90}{70} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 3 \times 1.2857 \right\rceil = \left\lceil 3.857 \right\rceil = 4 \text{ replicas}$$

So HPA scales the deployment from **3 → 4 pods**.

### 3.3 Analytical Derivation: Cloud Availability Downtime Budget

**Problem:** A cloud provider offers 99.95% SLA. Calculate annual downtime budget.

**Step 1 — Total minutes in a year.**

$$T_{year} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525600 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the availability formula inverted.**

$$D_{year} = T_{year} \times (1 - A)$$

$$D_{year} = 525600 \times (1 - 0.9995) = 525600 \times 0.0005 = 262.8 \text{ minutes/year}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to hours for the answer.**

$$D_{hours} = \dfrac{262.8}{60} = 4.38 \text{ hours/year}$$

### 3.4 KTU 2024 — Microservices vs Monolith Trade-off Matrix

| Criterion | Monolithic | Microservices | KTU Verdict |
|-----------|------------|---------------|-------------|
| **Initial Development Speed** | Fast (single codebase) | Slower (multiple services) | Monolith wins early |
| **Scalability** | Scale entire app | Scale individual services | Microservices win |
| **Deployment Risk** | High (all-or-nothing) | Low (independent deploys) | Microservices win |
| **Tech Stack Flexibility** | Single stack | Polyglot (Java, Go, Python mixed) | Microservices win |
| **Operational Complexity** | Low | High (CI/CD, monitoring, networking) | Monolith wins |
| **Fault Isolation** | Single point of failure | One service crash ≠ total outage | Microservices win |
| **Team Coordination** | Easy (one team) | Hard (cross-team APIs) | Monolith wins |
| **Data Consistency** | Strong (single DB) | Eventual consistency | Monolith wins |
| **Time to Market (Mature)** | Slow (tightly coupled) | Fast (independent teams) | Microservices win |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked "which is better", the **model answer** is: *"Neither is universally better; choose based on team size, scale, and complexity. Start with a modular monolith, then decompose into microservices when scale demands it."* This shows nuanced thinking and earns full marks.

### 3.5 Inter-Service Communication Pattern Analysis

Microservices communicate using:

1. **Synchronous HTTP/REST** — Simple, but tightly coupled in time.
2. **Asynchronous Message Queues (RabbitMQ, Kafka)** — Decoupled, eventual consistency.
3. **gRPC (Google Remote Procedure Call)** — High performance, binary protocol, uses Protocol Buffers.
4. **Service Mesh (Istio, Linkerd)** — Sidecar proxies for traffic management.

The KTU syllabus emphasizes **gRPC** and **message queues** as the modern defaults.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Don't confuse **Service Mesh** with **API Gateway**. API Gateway is a single entry point for external clients. Service Mesh handles internal service-to-service traffic.
