## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define cloud computing. List any FOUR essential characteristics of cloud computing as per NIST.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Cloud computing is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (networks, servers, storage, applications, services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort (NIST SP 800-145).

**Four Essential Characteristics (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **On-demand self-service** — User can provision resources automatically.
2. **Broad network access** — Available over the network via standard protocols.
3. **Resource pooling** — Multi-tenant model with dynamic resource assignment.
4. **Measured service** — Pay-per-use metering.

*(Rapid elasticity may be the 5th; any 4 accepted.)*

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*What is a Docker container? How is it different from a virtual machine?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Docker Container (1 mark):** A container is a lightweight, standalone, executable package that includes application code, runtime, system tools, libraries, and settings — running as an isolated process sharing the host OS kernel.

**Difference from VM (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Container | Virtual Machine |
|--------|-----------|-----------------|
| Virtualization | OS-level | Hardware-level (Hypervisor) |
| OS Kernel | Shared with host | Separate kernel per VM |
| Image Size | Megabytes | Gigabytes |
| Boot Time | Seconds | Minutes |

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Choice: Answer ONE 14-mark question)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the three cloud service models — **IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS** — with a neat diagram. Compare the **responsibility boundaries** between the user and the provider in each model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **microservices architecture** in detail. Explain its **key characteristics** and compare it with **monolithic architecture** using a comparative table. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service) (2 marks):** The user rents IT infrastructure — servers, VMs, storage, networks, and operating systems — on a pay-as-you-go basis. The user is responsible for the OS, runtime, middleware, application, and data. *Example: AWS EC2, Google Compute Engine.*

**PaaS (Platform-as-a-Service) (2 marks):** The provider supplies a platform including OS, middleware, runtime, and development tools. The user focuses only on application code and data. *Example: Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Heroku.*

**SaaS (Software-as-a-Service) (2 marks):** The provider delivers ready-to-use software over the internet. The user simply uses the application and manages only their own data. *Example: Gmail, Microsoft 365, Salesforce.*

**Responsibility Boundary Diagram (1 mark):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph I["IAAS"]
        direction TB
        I1["App + Data + Runtime + Middleware + OS = USER"]:::user
        I2["Virtualization + Servers + Storage + Networking = PROVIDER"]:::prov
    end
    subgraph P["PAAS"]
        direction TB
        P1["App + Data = USER"]:::user
        P2["Everything else = PROVIDER"]:::prov
    end
    subgraph S["SAAS"]
        direction TB
        S1["Data + Config = USER"]:::user
        S2["Everything else = PROVIDER"]:::prov
    end

    classDef user fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef prov fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Microservices Architecture (3 marks):**
Microservices is an architectural style that decomposes a single application into a suite of small, independent services, each:
- Running in its own process.
- Communicating via lightweight protocols (HTTP/REST, gRPC, message queues).
- Independently deployable.
- Owning its own data store.
- Owned by a small, cross-functional team ("you build it, you run it").

**Key Characteristics (2 marks):**
1. Single Responsibility — one service = one business capability.
2. Decentralized Data — each service has its own database.
3. Independent Deployability — releases don't require coordination.
4. Polyglot — services may use different languages/databases.
5. Design for Failure — services must be resilient (circuit breakers, retries).

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Criterion | Monolith | Microservices |
|-----------|----------|---------------|
| Coupling | Tight | Loose |
| Scalability | Whole-app | Per-service |
| Deployment | All-or-nothing | Independent |
| Fault Isolation | Total failure possible | Partial failure possible |
| Tech Stack | Single | Polyglot |
| Data Consistency | Strong ACID | Eventual consistency |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** What are **containers**? Explain **Docker architecture** with a neat diagram. Discuss the differences between **containers and virtual machines** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **Kubernetes**? Explain the **Kubernetes cluster architecture** and the role of its key components — **Pod, Deployment, Service, and Node**. Describe how **horizontal pod autoscaling (HPA)** works. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Containers (1 mark):** A container is a lightweight, standalone, executable software package that includes everything needed to run an application — code, runtime, libraries, dependencies, and configuration. Containers share the host OS kernel but run in isolated user-space processes.

**Docker Architecture (3 marks):**
Docker uses a **client-server architecture**:
- **Docker Client:** Command-line tool (`docker build`, `docker run`) that talks to the daemon via REST API.
- **Docker Daemon (`dockerd`):** The background service that builds images, runs containers, and manages the container lifecycle.
- **Docker Registry:** Storage for Docker images (Docker Hub is the public registry).

**Key objects (1 mark):**
- **Image:** Read-only template.
- **Container:** Runnable instance of an image.
- **Dockerfile:** Build script.

**Container vs VM Differences (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Container | Virtual Machine |
|-----------|-----------|-----------------|
| Virtualization Level | OS-level | Hardware-level |
| OS | Shares host kernel | Has own kernel |
| Boot Time | Seconds | Minutes |
| Size | 10–500 MB | 5–20 GB |
| Performance | Near-native | Slight overhead |
| Density | 100s per host | 10s per host |

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Kubernetes (1 mark):** Kubernetes (K8s) is an open-source container orchestration platform that automates deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications.

**Cluster Architecture (2 marks):** A Kubernetes cluster has a **Control Plane (Master)** and one or more **Worker Nodes**.

- **Control Plane components:** kube-apiserver, etcd, kube-scheduler, controller-manager.
- **Worker Node components:** kubelet, kube-proxy, container runtime.

**Key Objects (2 marks):**
- **Pod:** Smallest unit; one or more co-located containers sharing network namespace and storage.
- **Deployment:** Declarative spec for a replicated set of pods with rolling-update capability.
- **Service:** Stable virtual IP (ClusterIP) load-balancing traffic to a set of pods.
- **Node:** A worker machine (VM/physical) managed by the control plane.

**HPA — Horizontal Pod Autoscaling (2 marks):**
HPA automatically adjusts the number of pod replicas based on observed CPU/memory utilization (or custom metrics). It uses the formula:

$$\text{Replicas} = \left\lceil \text{CurrentReplicas} \times \dfrac{\text{CurrentMetricValue}}{\text{TargetMetricValue}} \right\rceil$$

For example, if current CPU = 90% and target = 70% with 3 replicas, HPA scales to 4 replicas.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. ❌ Writing "containers are lightweight VMs" — **Loses 2 marks**. Containers are **not VMs**; they virtualize the OS, not hardware.
> 2. ❌ Confusing **Service** (K8s internal load balancer) with **SaaS** (cloud service model). These are completely different concepts.
> 3. ❌ Forgetting the **NIST 5 characteristics** of cloud (On-demand self-service, Broad network access, Resource pooling, Rapid elasticity, Measured service). KTU specifically cites NIST.
> 4. ❌ In numerical problems, **unit conversion** errors (GB vs MB) cost 1 mark. Always show your unit conversion step.
> 5. ❌ Saying "Kubernetes is a containerization tool" — it is an **orchestration** tool. **Docker containerizes; Kubernetes orchestrates.**

---

### 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **High-density 1-page summary — read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- ☑ **Cloud = on-demand, metered, elastic computing resources** (NIST definition).
- ☑ **5 Essential Characteristics:** On-demand self-service, Broad network access, Resource pooling, Rapid elasticity, Measured service.
- ☑ **3 Service Models:** IaaS (you manage OS+above), PaaS (you manage app+data), SaaS (you manage only data).
- ☑ **4 Deployment Models:** Public, Private, Hybrid, Community.
- ☑ **Microservices = small, autonomous, independently deployable services** communicating over lightweight protocols.
- ☑ **Microservices principles:** Single responsibility, decentralized data, design for failure, polyglot, smart endpoints + dumb pipes.
- ☑ **Container = OS-level virtualization** — shares host kernel, isolated user-space process.
- ☑ **Container ≠ VM.** Containers are MB-sized, boot in seconds, share kernel. VMs are GB-sized, boot in minutes, have own kernel.
- ☑ **Docker triad:** Client + Daemon + Registry. Key objects: Image, Container, Dockerfile, Volume.
- ☑ **Kubernetes cluster = Control Plane + Worker Nodes.** Objects: Pod, Deployment, Service, Node, Namespace.
- ☑ **HPA formula:** $\text{Replicas} = \lceil \text{CurrentReplicas} \times \dfrac{\text{CurrentMetric}}{\text{TargetMetric}} \rceil$.
- ☑ **Availability SLA:** 99.9% = 8.76 hours/year downtime; 99.99% = 52.6 minutes/year.
- ☑ **Remember the analogy:** Cloud = electricity grid; Microservices = restaurant brigade; Container = shipping container; Kubernetes = port manager.
- ☑ **Key KTU example to cite:** Netflix runs 700+ microservices on AWS using Docker + Kubernetes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Computing and Service Models: Private, Public and Hybrid Clouds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cloud Computing and Service Models: Private, Public and Hybrid Clouds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Cloud Computing

**Cloud Computing** is a paradigm that enables ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (NIST SP 800-145 aligned):**
> "Cloud computing is a model for enabling *ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand* network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be *rapidly provisioned and released* with *minimal management effort or service provider interaction*."

The formal definition rests on **five essential characteristics**, **three service models**, and **four deployment models**, as defined by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)**.

### 1.1.1 The Five Essential Characteristics of Cloud

| # | Characteristic | Meaning |
|---|----------------|---------|
| 1 | On-Demand Self-Service | Consumers provision compute, storage, and networking automatically without human intervention |
| 2 | Broad Network Access | Capabilities are available over the network and reached through standard mechanisms (HTTP, REST APIs) |
| 3 | Resource Pooling | Provider resources are pooled to serve multiple consumers using a *multi-tenant model* |
| 4 | Rapid Elasticity | Capabilities can be scaled out/in rapidly and automatically with demand |
| 5 | Measured Service | Cloud systems automatically control and optimize resource use by leveraging metering at an appropriate level of abstraction |

## 1.2 Deployment Model Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **Deployment Models** answer the question: *"Who owns the infrastructure, and where does it physically live?"*

### 1.2.1 Public Cloud
A **public cloud** is owned and operated by a third-party cloud service provider (CSP) such as AWS, Microsoft Azure, or Google Cloud Platform (GCP), and delivers compute, storage, and application services over the **public Internet**. The infrastructure is provisioned for **open use by the general public or a large industry group**.

### 1.2.2 Private Cloud
A **private cloud** is provisioned for **exclusive use by a single organization** comprising multiple business units. It may be owned, managed, and operated by the organization itself, a third party, or a combination of both, and may exist on-premises or off-premises.

### 1.2.3 Hybrid Cloud
A **hybrid cloud** is a composition of **two or more distinct cloud infrastructures** (private, public, or community) that remain unique entities, but are **bound together by standardized or proprietary technology** that enables data and application portability (e.g., cloud bursting for load balancing between clouds).

### 1.2.4 Community Cloud
A **community cloud** is provisioned for **exclusive use by a specific community of consumers** from organizations that have shared concerns (e.g., mission, security requirements, policy, compliance considerations).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Electricity Grid" Analogy:**
> Think of cloud computing exactly like the **electricity supplied to your home**. You don't build a power plant in your backyard. You simply plug into a socket, pay only for the units (kilowatt-hours) you consume, and the grid automatically scales when you turn on more appliances.
>
> - **Public Cloud** = The municipal power grid. Shared by the whole city, very cheap, instantly available.
> - **Private Cloud** = Your own in-house diesel generator. Dedicated, expensive to maintain, but gives you full control and isolation.
> - **Hybrid Cloud** = A hybrid system: you use grid electricity for normal days but switch on your generator during peak hours (or for sensitive loads like hospital equipment). This is exactly what *cloud bursting* does in IT.
> - **Community Cloud** = A cooperative power plant shared by a group of nearby villages for agricultural irrigation.

This analogy helps us remember that cloud computing is fundamentally an **economic and operational model** — not just a technology.

## 1.4 Key Industry Standards and Metrics

- **Service Level Agreement (SLA):** Contractual uptime commitment, typically expressed as a **percentage** (e.g., 99.9% = "three nines", 99.99% = "four nines").
- **Pay-As-You-Go (PAYG):** Metered billing based on actual consumption.
- **Cloud Service Provider (CSP):** The entity that delivers cloud services.
- **Tenancy:** Multi-tenant (shared) vs. single-tenant (dedicated) infrastructure.
- **Scalability:** Vertical (scale up) vs. Horizontal (scale out).
- **Hypervisor:** Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM) used to abstract physical hardware.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** Always mention the **NIST Cloud Computing Reference Model** and the **five essential characteristics** whenever a definition question is asked. Examiners award full marks only when *all five characteristics* are explicitly listed.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comparative Analysis of Deployment Models

### 2.1.1 Private Cloud — Operational Deep Dive

A private cloud is essentially a **dedicated, isolated, single-tenant environment**. It may physically reside in:
- **On-Premises Private Cloud** — within the organization's own data center.
- **Hosted Private Cloud** — hosted by a third-party, but fully dedicated to one client.

**Why choose Private Cloud?**
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Industries like Banking (RBI), Healthcare (HIPAA), and Defense require strict data residency.
- **Workload Control:** Predictable performance without the "noisy neighbor" effect of shared public clouds.
- **Customization:** Full control over hardware, hypervisor (KVM, VMware ESXi, Hyper-V), network fabric, and security policy.

**How it works operationally (step-by-step):**
1. Organization provisions pooled compute, storage, and network resources internally.
2. A **cloud operating system / management plane** (e.g., OpenStack, VMware vCloud) abstracts physical hardware.
3. Internal business units consume resources via self-service portals and APIs.
4. The infrastructure team still owns the physical plant — capital expenditure (CapEx) remains high, but **operational agility** increases.

**Limitations:**
- **High CapEx:** Requires purchasing servers, switches, storage arrays upfront.
- **Limited Elasticity:** Capacity is bounded by physical footprint.
- **Underutilization Risk:** Average server utilization in private data centers historically hovers around **12% to 18%**.

### 2.1.2 Public Cloud — Operational Deep Dive

A public cloud delivers services across the **open Internet** to any consumer who wishes to purchase them. The CSP owns and operates all infrastructure, exposing it through web consoles, SDKs, and RESTful APIs.

**Why choose Public Cloud?**
- **Zero CapEx:** No upfront hardware purchase — pure operational expenditure (OpEx).
- **Global Footprint:** Providers maintain **60+ geographic regions** (e.g., AWS has 33+ regions, 100+ Availability Zones).
- **Massive Service Catalog:** 200+ managed services (compute, ML, IoT, blockchain, quantum).

**How it works operationally (step-by-step):**
1. Consumer authenticates via IAM (Identity and Access Management) credentials.
2. API/Console call triggers resource instantiation in the nearest region/zone.
3. CSP's orchestration layer (e.g., AWS CloudFormation, Azure Resource Manager) provisions virtualized resources on a shared hypervisor pool.
4. Metering service (e.g., CloudWatch, Azure Monitor) records usage; bill is generated monthly.

**Limitations:**
- **Less Control:** CSP owns the stack below the virtualization layer.
- **Data Sovereignty Concerns:** Data may cross international borders.
- **Vendor Lock-in:** Proprietary services are difficult to migrate.

### 2.1.3 Hybrid Cloud — Operational Deep Dive

The hybrid cloud is the most architecturally interesting of the three. It exists to give organizations the **best of both worlds**: control of private + elasticity of public.

**How it works operationally (step-by-step):**
1. **Connectivity Layer:** A dedicated **interconnect** (AWS Direct Connect, Azure ExpressRoute, Google Cloud Interconnect) or an **IPsec VPN tunnel** securely links the private and public environments.
2. **Unified Identity:** A federated identity system (e.g., Azure AD Connect, Okta) allows Single Sign-On (SSO) across both environments.
3. **Data Tiering:** Sensitive data stays on-premises; less sensitive or burst workloads go to the public cloud.
4. **Cloud Bursting:** When private capacity saturates, automated scripts spin up additional public-cloud instances to absorb the spike.
5. **Orchestration:** A unified management plane (e.g., Azure Arc, AWS Outposts, Google Anthos) treats both environments as a single fabric.

**Architectural Patterns of Hybrid Cloud:**

| Pattern | Use Case | Trigger |
|---------|----------|---------|
| Cloud Bursting | Sudden traffic spike on e-commerce site | CPU > 80% for 5 min |
| Tiered Storage | Hot data in private, cold data in public S3 | Object age > 90 days |
| Disaster Recovery | Public cloud as warm DR site | Heartbeat failure |
| Edge–Core Split | IoT data processed locally, aggregated in public | Bandwidth constraint |

## 2.2 Why and How of Each Model — The KTU Perspective

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for the exam:** The most common KTU question is *"Compare Public, Private, and Hybrid cloud with suitable examples."* Always tabulate the comparison and use a **real-world company example** for each:
> - **Public Cloud Example:** Netflix runs entirely on AWS.
> - **Private Cloud Example:** Walmart uses its own private cloud (OpenStack-based) to handle 1M+ transactions/minute.
> - **Hybrid Cloud Example:** General Electric (GE) uses AWS for analytics, but keeps manufacturing control systems on private infrastructure.

### 2.2.1 The "Why" Behind the Models
- **Cost optimization** — Public cloud converts CapEx to OpEx.
- **Compliance** — Private cloud satisfies regulations (PCI-DSS, GDPR data residency).
- **Innovation speed** — Public cloud offers instant access to AI/ML, quantum, and blockchain services.
- **Risk distribution** — Hybrid avoids single-vendor dependency.

### 2.2.2 The "How" Behind the Models
- All three models rely on **virtualization** (hypervisors), **abstraction**, and **automation** to deliver cloud characteristics.
- The difference lies in **who owns the physical infrastructure** and **who can access it**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains all quantitative relationships commonly tested in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations for this topic.**

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Unit | Engineering Meaning |
|---------------|---------------------|------|---------------------|
| **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $TCO = CapEx + OpEx_{\text{annual}} \times N$ | Currency (INR/USD) | Lifetime cost over $N$ years |
| **Pay-as-you-go (PAYG) Cost** | $C_{\text{PAYG}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i \times U_i)$ | Currency | Sum of (per-unit price $\times$ units consumed) |
| **Service Availability** | $A = \dfrac{U_{\text{time}}}{U_{\text{time}} + D_{\text{time}}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage | Uptime percentage (SLA target) |
| **Annual Downtime** | $D_{\text{annual}} = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600 \text{ min}$ | Minutes/Year | Permissible downtime in minutes |
| **Mean Time Between Failures** | $MTBF = \dfrac{T_{\text{total\_operation}}}{N_{\text{failures}}}$ | Hours | Reliability metric |
| **Mean Time To Repair** | $MTTR = \dfrac{T_{\text{total\_repair}}}{N_{\text{repairs}}}$ | Hours | Serviceability metric |
| **System Availability (Series)** | $A_{\text{sys}} = \prod_{i=1}^{k} A_i$ | Percentage | For k components in series |
| **System Availability (Parallel)** | $A_{\text{sys}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{k} (1 - A_i)$ | Percentage | For k redundant components |
| **Server Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{T_{\text{busy}}}{T_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage | Efficiency of resource use |
| **Storage Capacity Required** | $C_{\text{storage}} = N_{\text{users}} \times S_{\text{per\_user}} \times (1 + R_{\text{replication}})$ | GB / TB | With $R$ replication factor |
| **Bandwidth Requirement** | $B_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{D_{\text{total}}}{T_{\text{window}}} \times 8$ | Mbps / Gbps | Bits per second |
| **Elasticity Ratio** | $E = \dfrac{C_{\text{peak}}}{C_{\text{baseline}}}$ | Dimensionless | Indicates scaling factor |
| **Cost per Compute Hour** | $C_{\text{hour}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{machine}} \times 8760}{L_{\text{years}} \times U_{\text{factor}}}$ | Currency/Hour | Effective hourly cost |
| **Cloud Bursting Trigger** | $T_{\text{burst}} = \text{IF}(L_{\text{current}} > L_{\text{threshold}})$ | Boolean | Load-based autoscaling |
| **ROI of Cloud Migration** | $ROI = \dfrac{(B_{\text{savings}} - C_{\text{migration}})}{C_{\text{migration}}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage | Migration business case |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Parsing Rule:** When writing absolute values inside the formulas above in your answer sheet, always use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ — never `|x|` — to prevent markdown table breakage.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Disaster Recovery Engineering:** Hybrid cloud enables sub-15-minute RTOs (Recovery Time Objectives).
2. **DevOps & CI/CD:** Public cloud provides on-demand build/test environments, reducing CI pipeline time by **60–80%**.
3. **Big Data & AI Workloads:** Public cloud's GPU/TPU pools make training deep-learning models feasible for startups without owning supercomputers.
4. **IoT & Edge Computing:** Hybrid cloud architecture (Edge → Private Core → Public Analytics) is the standard pattern for Industry 4.0 smart factories.
5. **Healthcare Imaging:** Hospitals use private clouds (HIPAA-compliant) for primary PACS storage, but burst to public clouds for AI-based diagnostics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Annual Permissible Downtime from SLA Availability

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation appears frequently in KTU numerical questions. **Memorize the constant 525,600 minutes/year.**

**Given:**
- A cloud service provider offers an SLA of $99.9\%$ (three nines) availability.
- We must find the maximum permitted downtime in a non-leap year.

**Step 1: Recall the availability formula.**

$$
A = \frac{U_{\text{time}}}{U_{\text{time}} + D_{\text{time}}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 2: Rearrange to isolate $D_{\text{time}}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A \times (U_{\text{time}} + D_{\text{time}}) &= 100 \times U_{\text{time}} \\
A \times U_{\text{time}} + A \times D_{\text{time}} &= 100 \times U_{\text{time}} \\
A \times D_{\text{time}} &= (100 - A) \times U_{\text{time}} \\
D_{\text{time}} &= \frac{(100 - A)}{A} \times U_{\text{time}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute total minutes in a year.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{year}} &= 365 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ h/day} \times 60 \text{ min/h} \\
&= 525{,}600 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute $A = 99.9\%$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{year}} &= \frac{(100 - 99.9)}{99.9} \times 525{,}600 \\
&= \frac{0.1}{99.9} \times 525{,}600 \\
&= 0.001001 \times 525{,}600 \\
&\approx 526.13 \text{ minutes per year} \\
&\approx 8.77 \text{ hours per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compare with higher nines.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A = 99.99\% \quad &\Rightarrow \quad D \approx 52.6 \text{ min/year} \\
A = 99.999\% \quad &\Rightarrow \quad D \approx 5.26 \text{ min/year} \\
A = 99.9999\% \quad &\Rightarrow \quad D \approx 31.5 \text{ sec/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** Each additional "nine" reduces downtime by a factor of **10**. This is a quick-validation trick examiners love to see in viva voce.

## 3.2 Derivation: Series and Parallel System Availability

### 3.2.1 Series System (Single Point of Failure Path)

A series cloud architecture has all components in the request path: e.g., Load Balancer $\rightarrow$ Web Server $\rightarrow$ App Server $\rightarrow$ Database.

**Assumption:** The entire system fails if *any* component fails.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{sys}} &= P(\text{all components working}) \\
&= \prod_{i=1}^{k} A_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:** A web app with 4 services, each having $A_i = 99.9\%$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{sys}} &= (0.999)^4 \\
&= 0.99600 \\
&\approx 99.6\% \text{ (worse than any single component)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 Parallel System (Redundant Path)

Components in parallel are redundant — the system survives any single failure.

**Assumption:** The system works as long as *at least one* component works.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{sys}} &= 1 - P(\text{all components fail}) \\
&= 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{k} (1 - A_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:** Two redundant databases, each with $A_i = 99.9\%$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{sys}} &= 1 - (1 - 0.999)^2 \\
&= 1 - (0.001)^2 \\
&= 1 - 0.000001 \\
&= 0.999999 \\
&\approx 99.9999\% \text{ (six nines)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Adding one redundant 99.9% database jumped overall system availability from 99.9% to 99.9999% — that's the engineering justification for **multi-AZ deployments** in AWS and Azure.

## 3.3 Derivation: Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) — Public vs. Private

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **classic 14-mark KTU question** in Module 4. The derivation below shows the break-even point.

**Scenario:** A startup is evaluating public cloud vs. private cloud for 3 years.

**Private Cloud TCO:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCO_{\text{private}} &= \text{CapEx} + (OpEx_{\text{private}} \times 3) \\
\text{CapEx} &= \text{Server Cost} + \text{Storage Cost} + \text{Network Cost} \\
OpEx_{\text{private}} &= \text{Power} + \text{Cooling} + \text{IT Staff} + \text{Software Licenses}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Public Cloud TCO:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCO_{\text{public}} &= \text{CapEx}_{\text{public}} + (OpEx_{\text{public}} \times 3) \\
\text{CapEx}_{\text{public}} &= 0 \text{ (no hardware purchase)} \\
OpEx_{\text{public}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i \times U_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:**

| Component | Private (₹) | Public (₹) |
|-----------|-------------|------------|
| Server Hardware (CapEx) | 20,00,000 | 0 |
| Storage (CapEx) | 5,00,000 | 0 |
| Network (CapEx) | 2,00,000 | 0 |
| Annual Power/Cooling | 3,00,000 | 0 |
| Annual IT Staff (2) | 12,00,000 | 4,00,000 (only 1 admin) |
| Annual Cloud Usage | 0 | 18,00,000 (PAYG) |
| Annual Licenses | 2,00,000 | 0 (CSP-managed) |

**Compute TCO over 3 years:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCO_{\text{private}} &= (20 + 5 + 2) \text{ Lakh} + (3 + 12 + 2) \times 3 \text{ Lakh} \\
&= 27 \text{ Lakh} + 17 \times 3 \text{ Lakh} \\
&= 27 + 51 = 78 \text{ Lakh}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCO_{\text{public}} &= 0 + (4 + 18 + 0) \times 3 \text{ Lakh} \\
&= 22 \times 3 = 66 \text{ Lakh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** Public cloud saves **₹12 Lakh over 3 years**, with the additional benefit of zero upfront capital. However, at much higher scale (10x usage), private cloud may become cheaper due to per-unit economics.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Public vs. Private vs. Hybrid

> [!TIP]
> This is the **single most important table** for KTU 2024 Module 4. Memorize it and reproduce it in any 14-mark comparison question.

| Parameter | Public Cloud | Private Cloud | Hybrid Cloud |
|-----------|--------------|---------------|--------------|
| **Owner of Infrastructure** | Third-party CSP | Single organization | Combination |
| **Location** | CSP's data center | On-prem or hosted | Both |
| **Tenancy** | Multi-tenant | Single-tenant | Mixed |
| **CapEx** | Very low (zero) | Very high | Medium |
| **OpEx** | Pay-per-use (medium) | High ongoing | Medium-high |
| **Elasticity** | Unlimited | Limited by hardware | High |
| **Control** | Low | Full | Medium-high |
| **Security** | Provider-managed | Customer-managed | Shared responsibility |
| **Compliance** | Limited regions | Strong | Strong + flexible |
| **Performance** | Variable (noisy neighbor) | Predictable | Predictable for critical, elastic for rest |
| **Internet Dependency** | High | Low | Medium |
| **Vendor Lock-in** | High | Low | Medium |
| **Setup Time** | Minutes | Months | Weeks to months |
| **Best For** | Startups, web apps, ML | Regulated industries, defense | Enterprises with variable load |
| **Examples** | AWS, Azure, GCP | OpenStack, VMware vSphere | Azure Arc, AWS Outposts, Google Anthos |
| **Cost Model** | PAYG / Subscription | CapEx-heavy | Mixed |
| **Disaster Recovery** | Built-in / multi-region | Customer-built | DRaaS from public side |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: Cloud Bursting Decision

**Problem (KTU Style):**
An e-commerce application runs on a private cloud with a baseline capacity of 100 servers. During a flash sale, demand spikes to 350 concurrent users per server, but the private cloud can only host 200 users per server. The company uses cloud bursting to a public cloud at ₹50 per user-hour. The sale lasts 4 hours. Compute:
(a) The number of users the private cloud can host.
(b) The number of users that must be burst to the public cloud.
(c) The total public-cloud cost.

**Solution:**

**(a) Private cloud capacity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{private}} &= N_{\text{servers}} \times U_{\text{per\_server}} \\
&= 100 \times 200 = 20{,}000 \text{ users}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Burst users:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{\text{burst}} &= U_{\text{peak}} - C_{\text{private}} \\
&= (100 \times 350) - 20{,}000 \\
&= 35{,}000 - 20{,}000 = 15{,}000 \text{ users}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Public cloud cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{public}} &= U_{\text{burst}} \times P_{\text{per\_user}} \times T_{\text{hours}} \\
&= 15{,}000 \times 50 \times 4 \\
&= ₹30{,}00{,}000 \quad (\text{₹30 Lakh})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Cloud bursting converted a capital-intensive "buy 250 more servers" decision (₹2.5 Cr+) into a ₹30 Lakh operational expense — a **88% cost reduction** for a one-time event.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Cloud Deployment Model Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> This block-level functional architecture flow illustrates how Private, Public, and Hybrid clouds are composed and interlinked at the **network, identity, and orchestration** layers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EndUser["END USER LAYER"]
        EU1["Web Browser"]
        EU2["Mobile App"]
        EU3["Enterprise Client"]
    end

    subgraph EdgeNet["EDGE AND NETWORK LAYER"]
        CDN["CDN AND GLOBAL LOAD BALANCER"]
        INet["PUBLIC INTERNET"]
        VPN["IPSEC VPN TUNNEL"]
        DX["DEDICATED INTERCONNECT"]
    end

    subgraph HybridCtrl["HYBRID ORCHESTRATION LAYER"]
        IAM["FEDERATED IDENTITY AND IAM"]
        Orch["UNIFIED ORCHESTRATION PLANE ARC OR ANTHOS"]
        Mon["CENTRAL MONITORING AND LOGGING"]
    end

    subgraph PrivateEnv["PRIVATE CLOUD ENVIRONMENT"]
        Priv1["ON-PREM HYPERVISOR KVM OR ESXI"]
        Priv2["PRIVATE STORAGE TIER"]
        Priv3["COMPLIANT WORKLOADS"]
    end

    subgraph PublicEnv["PUBLIC CLOUD ENVIRONMENT"]
        Pub1["REGION A AVAILABILITY ZONE 1"]
        Pub2["REGION A AVAILABILITY ZONE 2"]
        Pub3["MANAGED SERVICES DATABASE ML QUEUE"]
        Pub4["BURST WORKLOADS"]
    end

    EU1 --> CDN
    EU2 --> CDN
    EU3 --> CDN
    CDN --> INet
    INet --> IAM
    IAM --> Orch
    Orch --> Mon
    Orch --> VPN
    Orch --> DX
    VPN --> Priv1
    VPN --> Priv2
    DX --> Pub1
    DX --> Pub2
    Orch -. CLOUD BURST .-> Pub4
    Priv1 --> Priv3
    Priv2 --> Priv3
    Pub1 --> Pub3
    Pub2 --> Pub3
    Mon -. TELEMETRY .-> Priv1
    Mon -. TELEMETRY .-> Pub1
    Mon -. TELEMETRY .-> Pub2
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Decision Flow for Choosing a Deployment Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["START NEW WORKLOAD DESIGN"] --> Q1{"DATA SUBJECT TO STRICT COMPLIANCE PCI HIPAA GDPR"}
    Q1 -- YES --> Priv["USE PRIVATE CLOUD"]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{"PREDICTABLE STEADY STATE LOAD"}
    Q2 -- YES --> Priv2["PRIVATE CLOUD MAY BE COST OPTIMAL"]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{"OCCASIONAL PEAK BURSTS"]
    Q3 -- YES --> Hybrid["USE HYBRID CLOUD WITH BURSTING"]
    Q3 -- NO --> Q4{"FAST TIME TO MARKET AND LOW CAPEX REQUIRED"}
    Q4 -- YES --> Pub["USE PUBLIC CLOUD"]
    Q4 -- NO --> Review["REVIEW WORKLOAD PROFILE AGAIN"]
    Priv --> Done["DEPLOY"]
    Priv2 --> Done
    Hybrid --> Done
    Pub --> Done
    Review --> Start
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Cloud Bursting Sequence Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client as END USER
    participant LB as LOAD BALANCER
    participant Priv as PRIVATE CLOUD POOL
    participant Mon as MONITORING SERVICE
    participant Orch as ORCHESTRATOR
    participant Pub as PUBLIC CLOUD POOL

    Client->>LB: SEND REQUEST
    LB->>Priv: ROUTE TO BASELINE POOL
    Mon->>Priv: COLLECT CPU AND QUEUE METRICS
    Mon->>Orch: ALERT CPU ABOVE 80 PERCENT
    Orch->>Pub: PROVISION BURST INSTANCES
    Pub-->>Orch: INSTANCES READY
    Orch->>LB: REGISTER PUBLIC INSTANCES IN POOL
    LB->>Pub: ROUTE EXCESS TRAFFIC
    Pub-->>Client: SERVE RESPONSE
    Mon->>Orch: ALERT LOAD BELOW 30 PERCENT
    Orch->>Pub: DEPROVISION BURST INSTANCES
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Cloud Service vs. Deployment Model Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DepModels["DEPLOYMENT MODELS"]
        D1["PUBLIC CLOUD"]
        D2["PRIVATE CLOUD"]
        D3["HYBRID CLOUD"]
    end

    subgraph ServModels["SERVICE MODELS"]
        S1["IaaS INFRASTRUCTURE AS A SERVICE"]
        S2["PaaS PLATFORM AS A SERVICE"]
        S3["SaaS SOFTWARE AS A SERVICE"]
    end

    S1 --> D1
    S1 --> D2
    S1 --> D3
    S2 --> D1
    S2 --> D3
    S2 -. LIMITED .-> D2
    S3 --> D1
    S3 --> D3
    S3 -. LIMITED .-> D2
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Note:** When drawing these diagrams in the exam, use **rectangular boxes with clear labels** and **directional arrows**. Mermaid rendering is not required — but a clean hand-drawn equivalent fetches full marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

**Q: Define cloud computing and list its five essential characteristics as per the NIST reference model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Cloud computing is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction.
>
> **Five Essential Characteristics (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
> 1. **On-Demand Self-Service** — Consumers can provision resources automatically without human interaction.
> 2. **Broad Network Access** — Capabilities are available over the network and accessed through standard mechanisms used by heterogeneous client platforms.
> 3. **Resource Pooling** — Provider resources are pooled in a multi-tenant model to serve multiple consumers.
> 4. **Rapid Elasticity** — Capabilities can be elastically provisioned and released to scale with demand.
> 5. **Measured Service** — Cloud systems automatically control and optimize resource use through metering at a level appropriate to the service.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**Q: Differentiate between scalability and elasticity in cloud computing with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Scalability (1.5 marks):** The ability of a system to **handle increased load by adding resources**. It is a *planned, architectural* property.
> - *Example:* A bank adding 2 more application servers during festival season (planned, known in advance).
>
> **Elasticity (1.5 marks):** The ability of a system to **automatically scale out and scale in** based on real-time demand fluctuations.
> - *Example:* A news website that provisions 50 extra servers when a breaking story trends on Twitter, and deprovisions them once traffic subsides — fully automated.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model] (14 Marks)

**(a) Compare and contrast Public, Private, and Hybrid cloud deployment models based on ownership, scalability, cost, control, security, and suitable use cases. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Public Cloud | Private Cloud | Hybrid Cloud |
|-----------|--------------|---------------|--------------|
| **Ownership** | Third-party CSP (AWS, Azure, GCP) | Single organization | Mix of both |
| **Scalability** | Virtually unlimited | Bounded by physical capacity | High — burst from private to public |
| **Cost Model** | Pure OpEx (PAYG) | Heavy CapEx + OpEx | Mixed CapEx + OpEx |
| **Control** | Low (vendor-managed) | Full (customer-managed) | Medium (selective) |
| **Security & Compliance** | Shared responsibility; limited regions | Customer-controlled; strong compliance | Strong for sensitive + flexible for rest |
| **Internet Dependency** | High | Low | Medium (VPN/Interconnect required) |
| **Best Use Case** | Startups, SaaS, ML workloads | Banking, defense, regulated enterprises | Enterprises with variable load + compliance |
| **Real-World Example** | Netflix on AWS | Walmart's private OpenStack cloud | GE Predix (private) + AWS analytics |
| **Time to Deploy** | Minutes | 6–18 months | 3–6 months |

> **Conclusion (1 mark):** Hybrid is the most prevalent enterprise model in 2024, but the choice depends on workload, compliance, and cost profile. *(Examiner tip: Always end with a justified conclusion.)*

---

**(b) A startup anticipates 10,000 users in year 1, growing to 80,000 by year 3. Compute the 3-year TCO for both private and public cloud options. Private: server cost ₹15 Lakh, storage ₹4 Lakh, network ₹2 Lakh, annual ops ₹8 Lakh. Public: ₹30 per user per year PAYG. State which is more economical. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Private Cloud TCO:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CapEx} &= 15 + 4 + 2 = 21 \text{ Lakh} \\
\text{OpEx over 3 years} &= 8 \times 3 = 24 \text{ Lakh} \\
TCO_{\text{private}} &= 21 + 24 = 45 \text{ Lakh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Public Cloud TCO (PAYG — scaled to user count):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Year 1} &= 10{,}000 \times 30 = ₹3{,}00{,}000 = 3 \text{ Lakh} \\
\text{Year 2} &= 40{,}000 \times 30 = ₹12{,}00{,}000 = 12 \text{ Lakh} \\
\text{Year 3} &= 80{,}000 \times 30 = ₹24{,}00{,}000 = 24 \text{ Lakh} \\
\text{(Assumes linear growth with 40K midpoint in year 2)} \\
TCO_{\text{public}} &= 3 + 12 + 24 = 39 \text{ Lakh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta TCO &= TCO_{\text{private}} - TCO_{\text{public}} \\
&= 45 - 39 = 6 \text{ Lakh}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Public cloud is ₹6 Lakh cheaper over 3 years** and offers zero upfront capital, making it the recommended choice for this rapidly growing startup. Beyond **1.5 Lakh users**, public cloud's per-unit cost begins to make private cloud competitive again, especially if utilization stays above 70%.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model] (14 Marks)

**(a) Explain the architecture of a Hybrid Cloud system with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of cloud bursting in enabling elastic workload distribution. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Hybrid Cloud Architecture (5 marks):**

A hybrid cloud architecture is composed of **two distinct environments** (private + public) bound together by a **secure connectivity layer** and a **unified orchestration layer**.

**Key Architectural Layers:**

1. **Private Cloud Layer:** Hosts mission-critical, sensitive, or compliance-bound workloads. Includes on-premises servers, storage arrays, and virtualization platform (e.g., VMware vSphere, OpenStack).
2. **Public Cloud Layer:** Provides elastic, on-demand resources (compute, storage, services) for variable or non-sensitive workloads (e.g., AWS EC2, Azure VMs).
3. **Connectivity Layer:** Securely bridges the two environments using:
   - **IPsec VPN** over the public Internet (low cost, encrypted).
   - **Dedicated Interconnect** (AWS Direct Connect, Azure ExpressRoute) — private fiber link, consistent latency.
4. **Unified Identity Layer:** Federated identity management (Azure AD Connect, Okta) provides Single Sign-On (SSO) and consistent role-based access control (RBAC).
5. **Orchestration Layer:** A central management plane (e.g., Azure Arc, Google Anthos) allows developers to deploy and manage workloads across both environments through a single pane of glass.
6. **Monitoring & Telemetry Layer:** Aggregated logging and metrics (e.g., Azure Monitor, AWS CloudWatch) across both environments for unified observability.

**Cloud Bursting (2 marks):**

Cloud bursting is a **hybrid cloud pattern** where an application running in a private cloud can **automatically "burst" into a public cloud** when private capacity is saturated. The process:

1. Monitoring agent detects that private-cloud CPU > 80% (configurable threshold).
2. Orchestrator triggers API call to public CSP to provision additional VM instances.
3. New instances register with the internal load balancer and start serving traffic.
4. When load drops below a lower threshold (e.g., 30%), burst instances are gracefully deprovisioned.
5. Any persistent state must be synchronized via shared databases (e.g., replicated SQL) or shared storage (e.g., S3, Azure Blob).

**Use case:** E-commerce flash sales, tax filing portals, ticket booking for concerts — all of which experience massive, unpredictable spikes.

---

**(b) An SLA guarantees 99.95% availability. Compute (i) the maximum annual downtime in minutes, (ii) the maximum monthly downtime in minutes, and (iii) the system availability if three such services are deployed in series and in parallel. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Annual Downtime:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{year}} &= 365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525{,}600 \text{ minutes} \\
D_{\text{annual}} &= (1 - 0.9995) \times 525{,}600 \\
&= 0.0005 \times 525{,}600 \\
&= 262.8 \text{ minutes per year} \\
&\approx 4 \text{ hours } 22 \text{ minutes } 48 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Monthly Downtime:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{monthly}} &= \frac{262.8}{12} = 21.9 \text{ minutes per month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Three Services — Series and Parallel:**

**Series:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{series}} &= (0.9995)^3 \\
&= 0.998500375 \\
&\approx 99.85\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Parallel (redundant):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{parallel}} &= 1 - (1 - 0.9995)^3 \\
&= 1 - (0.0005)^3 \\
&= 1 - 1.25 \times 10^{-10} \\
&\approx 0.999999999875 \\
&\approx 99.9999999875\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Series deployment of three high-availability services **degrades** the SLA to 99.85% (worse than any single component). Parallel (redundant) deployment of the same three services delivers **near-perfect** availability. This is the mathematical justification for **load-balanced multi-AZ** cloud architectures.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Cloud Computing Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state the year length constant (525,600 minutes).** Examiners deduct **1 full mark** if you use 365 days × 24 hours but forget the ×60 conversion.
> 2. **Writing "private cloud = on-premises"** — this is a **common misconception**. Private cloud is defined by *exclusivity of use*, not location. A private cloud can be hosted off-premises by a third party.
> 3. **Confusing "scalability" and "elasticity".** Scalability is *planned capacity increase*; elasticity is *automatic, on-demand adjustment*. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 4. **Not providing real-world examples** in comparison questions. Always cite at least **one company example per deployment model** (e.g., Netflix for public, Walmart for private, GE for hybrid).
> 5. **Skipping the conclusion** in a comparison answer. A 7-mark comparison question without a justified conclusion loses **0.5 to 1 mark**.
> 6. **Using `|` for absolute value in tables.** This breaks markdown and irritates examiners. Use $\vert x \vert$ instead.
> 7. **Conflating IaaS/PaaS/SaaS with deployment models.** IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS are *service* models, while public/private/hybrid are *deployment* models. Examiners frequently swap these to test conceptual clarity.
> 8. **Skipping the assumption block in numerical questions.** Always state "assuming non-leap year, 100% PAYG, no reserved instance discounts" etc.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 4:**

- **Cloud Computing Definition:** Ubiquitous, on-demand, shared pool of configurable resources with minimal management effort (NIST).
- **Five Characteristics:** On-Demand Self-Service, Broad Network Access, Resource Pooling, Rapid Elasticity, Measured Service.
- **Three Service Models:** IaaS, PaaS, SaaS (you manage: infrastructure, platform, or software respectively).
- **Four Deployment Models:** Public, Private, Hybrid, Community.
- **Public Cloud:** Third-party-owned, multi-tenant, low CapEx, high elasticity. Examples: AWS, Azure, GCP.
- **Private Cloud:** Single-tenant, full control, high CapEx, compliance-friendly. Examples: OpenStack, VMware vSphere.
- **Hybrid Cloud:** Combines public + private; supports **cloud bursting**; needs VPN/Interconnect and federated identity.
- **Community Cloud:** Shared by organizations with common concerns (e.g., government, healthcare consortiums).
- **Key Formula:** Annual Downtime = $(1 - A) \times 525{,}600$ minutes.
- **Series Availability:** $A_{\text{sys}} = \prod A_i$ (always *worse* than individual).
- **Parallel Availability:** $A_{\text{sys}} = 1 - \prod (1 - A_i)$ (always *better* — redundant).
- **Cloud Bursting:** Pattern where private cloud overflows to public during peak load; enables elasticity without permanent capacity.
- **TCO Formula:** $TCO = CapEx + (OpEx \times N_{\text{years}})$.
- **PAYG Formula:** $C_{\text{PAYG}} = \sum (P_i \times U_i)$.
- **SLA Reference Values:** 99.9% ≈ 8.77 h/year; 99.99% ≈ 52.6 min/year; 99.999% ≈ 5.26 min/year.
- **Real-World Cloud Bursting Example:** Ticket booking site (IRCTC-style) bursting to AWS during Tatkal hours.
- **Memorize:** 525,600 minutes per year, 86,400 seconds per day.
- **Examiner Triggers:** Always cite NIST, always list all 5 characteristics, always end with a justified conclusion.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Cloud Computing and Service Models.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Design Objectives and Cost Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cloud Design Objectives and Cost Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition: Cloud Design Objectives

**Cloud Design Objectives** are a structured set of non-functional and architectural goals that a cloud system must satisfy to deliver resilient, efficient, and economically viable services. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned), these objectives are the guiding pillars that drive every architectural decision — from virtual machine sizing to multi-region deployment strategies.

The canonical taxonomy of cloud design objectives, as specified in the PECMT632 syllabus, includes the following **eight core pillars**:

1. **Scalability** — The ability to handle increasing load by adding resources.
2. **Elasticity** — The ability to scale up **and** down dynamically with workload.
3. **Availability** — The percentage of time the system remains operational.
4. **Reliability** — The probability of failure-free operation over a period.
5. **Performance** — Throughput, latency, and response time guarantees.
6. **Security** — Confidentiality, integrity, and authentication guarantees.
7. **Manageability** — Ease of monitoring, provisioning, and maintenance.
8. **Cost-Effectiveness** — Optimizing the price-to-performance ratio.

> [!NOTE]
> **Scalability vs. Elasticity — The Classic KTU Confusion**
> Scalability = the **capacity** to grow. Elasticity = the **automated, on-demand execution** of that growth (and shrinkage). A system can be scalable but NOT elastic (e.g., manual provisioning), but an elastic system is always scalable.

## 1.2 Formal Definition: Cloud Cost Model

A **Cloud Cost Model** is a mathematical and business framework used to forecast, calculate, and optimize the financial expenditure of operating workloads in a cloud environment. It converts abstract resource consumption (CPU-hours, GB-months, I/O operations) into monetary units using provider-defined pricing schedules.

The primary cost dimensions are:

- **Compute Cost** — Charged per vCPU-hour, instance-hour, or serverless invocation.
- **Storage Cost** — Charged per GB-month across hot, warm, cold, and archive tiers.
- **Network Cost** — Charged per GB of egress (outbound) traffic; ingress is typically free.
- **Service Cost** — Managed services (databases, queues, AI APIs) charged per request or throughput unit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CAPEX vs OPEX — The Foundational Distinction**
> Traditional IT: **CAPEX** (Capital Expenditure) — buy hardware upfront, depreciate over years.
> Cloud: **OPEX** (Operational Expenditure) — pay-as-you-go, billed monthly, treated as a recurring operating cost. This shift from CAPEX to OPEX is the **single largest economic transformation** that cloud computing introduces.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The Electricity Grid Analogy**
> Imagine your college campus needs electricity. **Traditional IT (CAPEX)** = buying a diesel generator, installing it, fueling it, and maintaining it for 10 years. Even on holidays when nobody needs power, you paid for the generator.
>
> **Cloud Computing (OPEX)** = plugging into the Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) grid. You only pay for the kilowatt-hours you actually consume. If the campus is empty on Sunday, your bill drops to zero.
>
> **Cloud Design Objectives** = the *quality of service* contract: how reliable is the grid (Availability), can it handle exam-day surge loads (Elasticity), how fast does it restore after a power cut (Reliability), and how secure is the line (Security).

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in KTU 2024 Valuation

The following metrics are the **expected vocabulary** in board examinations:

- **SLA (Service Level Agreement)** — typically **99.9% ("three nines")**, **99.99% ("four nines")**, or **99.999% ("five nines")**.
- **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** — Max acceptable downtime after a disaster.
- **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** — Max acceptable data loss measured in time.
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** — Average uptime between failures.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** — Average time to restore service.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme places **special emphasis** on the linkage between design objectives and the cost model. Students who can *quantify* how a design choice (e.g., choosing redundancy) impacts the cost equation score significantly higher marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Cloud Design Objectives — Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Scalability

Scalability is the system's ability to maintain performance as workload increases. There are two dominant patterns:

- **Vertical Scaling (Scale-Up)**: Increase the capacity of a *single* node (e.g., 8 vCPU → 64 vCPU). Bounded by hardware limits.
- **Horizontal Scaling (Scale-Out)**: Add *more* nodes to a pool (e.g., 2 servers → 20 servers). Theoretically unbounded.

**Why it matters:** Horizontal scaling requires **stateless application design** and **load balancing**. Vertical scaling is simpler but has a hard ceiling.

### 2.1.2 Elasticity

Elasticity is *automated* scalability. The system uses monitoring signals (CPU %, queue depth, request rate) to trigger provisioning/de-provisioning actions without human intervention.

**Elasticity Equation (conceptual):**

$$
\Delta R_{instances} = \left\lceil \frac{L_{current} - L_{target}}{C_{per\_instance}} \right\rceil
$$

Where:
- $\Delta R_{instances}$ = Number of new instances to add (positive) or remove (negative).
- $L_{current}$ = Current measured load (e.g., requests/sec).
- $L_{target}$ = Target load per instance.
- $C_{per\_instance}$ = Capacity of a single instance.

### 2.1.3 Availability

Availability is the **uptime ratio** over a measurement window (usually monthly or annually):

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

To express as a percentage:

$$
A_{\%} = \frac{Uptime}{Uptime + Downtime} \times 100
$$

### 2.1.4 Reliability

Reliability is the probability of failure-free operation for time $t$, typically modeled with the **exponential distribution**:

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

Where $\lambda = \frac{1}{MTBF}$ is the failure rate.

### 2.1.5 Performance

Performance is measured using:
- **Throughput** — operations per unit time (req/s, IOPS).
- **Latency** — time to complete one operation (ms).
- **Jitter** — variation in latency.

**Little's Law** (foundational for capacity planning):

$$
L = \lambda \times W
$$

Where $L$ = average number of requests in the system, $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $W$ = average time in system.

### 2.1.6 Security

The **CIA Triad** governs cloud security objectives:
- **C**onfidentiality — Only authorized access.
- **I**ntegrity — Data is not tampered with.
- **A**vailability — Data is accessible when needed.

### 2.1.7 Manageability

Measured by **operational toil** reduction. Key sub-properties:
- **Observability** — Metrics, logs, traces.
- **Automation** — IaC (Infrastructure as Code), CI/CD.
- **Auditability** — Compliance and governance.

### 2.1.8 Cost-Effectiveness

The cost-to-performance ratio:

$$
\text{Cost-Effectiveness} = \frac{\text{Performance Achieved}}{\text{Total Cost Incurred}}
$$

## 2.2 The Cloud Cost Model — Operational Breakdown

### 2.2.1 CAPEX vs OPEX

| Parameter | CAPEX | OPEX |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | One-time, upfront | Recurring, pay-as-you-go |
| Accounting | Asset, depreciated | Expense, deducted immediately |
| Risk | High (over-provisioning) | Low (right-sizing) |
| Cash Flow | Front-loaded | Smooth, predictable |
| Example | Buying a rack server | AWS EC2 hourly billing |

### 2.2.2 Pricing Models (Billing Strategies)

| Model | Discount | Commitment | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **On-Demand** | 0% | None | Dev/test, unpredictable workloads |
| **Reserved Instances** | Up to 72% | 1–3 years | Steady-state production |
| **Spot Instances** | Up to 90% | Interruptible | Batch, fault-tolerant jobs |
| **Savings Plans** | Up to 66% | 1–3 years, flexible | Mixed compute families |

### 2.2.3 Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)

TCO captures the *true* cost of a cloud deployment, including hidden costs:

$$
TCO = C_{compute} + C_{storage} + C_{network} + C_{services} + C_{personnel} + C_{downtime}
$$

### 2.2.4 Pay-Per-Use Cost Formula

$$
C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (R_i \times U_i \times T_i)
$$

Where:
- $R_i$ = Unit rate of resource $i$ (e.g., $\text{\₹}$/vCPU-hour).
- $U_i$ = Number of units consumed (e.g., vCPU count).
- $T_i$ = Duration of consumption in the billing unit's time.
- $n$ = Number of distinct resource types.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Memorization List** — These formulas appear in nearly every KTU Module 4 question paper.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Availability as ratio | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $A_{\%} = \frac{Uptime}{Uptime + Downtime} \times 100$ | Availability percentage | Percent |
| 3 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability function | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $L = \lambda \times W$ | Little's Law | requests, req/s, s |
| 5 | $C_{total} = \sum (R_i \times U_i \times T_i)$ | Pay-per-use cost | Currency |
| 6 | $TCO = \sum C_{components}$ | Total Cost of Ownership | Currency |
| 7 | $ROI = \frac{\text{Benefit} - \text{Cost}}{\text{Cost}} \times 100$ | Return on Investment | Percent |
| 8 | $\Delta R = \left\lceil \frac{L_{current} - L_{target}}{C_{per\_instance}} \right\rceil$ | Elasticity scaling | Instances |
| 9 | $\text{CE} = \frac{\text{Performance}}{\text{Cost}}$ | Cost-Effectiveness | Performance/Currency |
| 10 | $\text{Uplift} = \frac{\text{New Cost} - \text{Old Cost}}{\text{Old Cost}} \times 100$ | Cost change percent | Percent |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

> [!TIP]
> **Why This Matters for KTU Graduates:** As a B.Tech engineer, you will rarely be the one *paying* the cloud bill — but you will be the one *generating* it. Every architectural decision (choosing a redundant DB, picking a multi-AZ deployment, adding a load balancer) has a direct cost line. The KTU 2024 syllabus trains you to make **cost-aware design choices**, which is a core industry skill measured in placement interviews at companies like TCS, Infosys, Wipro, and Accenture (which all run massive cloud practices).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Availability from MTBF and MTTR

We start from the definition that availability is the *fraction of time the system is operational* over a long observation window.

**Step 1 — Identify the time components:**

For a long operating window $T_{total}$, the time spent working is the Mean Time Between Failures ($MTBF$), and the time spent being repaired after each failure is the Mean Time To Repair ($MTTR$).

$$
T_{total} = MTBF + MTTR
$$

This is because every failure cycle consists of exactly one operational period (MTBF) followed by one repair period (MTTR).

**Step 2 — Define availability as a ratio:**

Availability $A$ is the proportion of the cycle that the system is *up*:

$$
A = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

This yields a value between 0 and 1.

**Step 3 — Convert to percentage form (board exam requirement):**

$$
A_{\%} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Verification:**

Suppose $MTBF = 1000$ hours and $MTTR = 1$ hour:

$$
A = \frac{1000}{1000 + 1} = \frac{1000}{1001} \approx 0.999
$$

$$
A_{\%} \approx 99.90\%
$$

This corresponds to the industry-standard **"three nines" SLA** (99.9%).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Downtime Allowed per SLA Tier

**Step 1 — Define downtime per period:**

Given an availability $A_{\%}$, the *unavailability* is:

$$
U_{\%} = 100 - A_{\%}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute allowed downtime for a year (8760 hours):**

$$
D_{year} = U_{\%} \times 8760 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Derive the SLA Tier Table (highly valued in KTU exams):**

| SLA | $A_{\%}$ | $U_{\%}$ | Downtime/Year |
|---|---|---|---|
| Two Nines | 99.0% | 1.0% | **87.6 hours** |
| Three Nines | 99.9% | 0.1% | **8.76 hours** |
| Four Nines | 99.99% | 0.01% | **52.56 minutes** |
| Five Nines | 99.999% | 0.001% | **5.26 minutes** |

**Verification for Four Nines:**

$$
D_{year} = 0.0001 \times 8760 = 0.876 \text{ hours} = 52.56 \text{ minutes} \checkmark
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Elasticity Scaling Decision

**Step 1 — Set up the load model:**

Suppose a web service currently serves $L_{current} = 5000$ requests/second across an existing pool of $N = 5$ instances, each capable of $C_{per\_instance} = 1200$ req/s. The target per-instance load is $L_{target} = 1000$ req/s (leaving 17% headroom).

**Step 2 — Compute total current capacity:**

$$
C_{total} = N \times C_{per\_instance} = 5 \times 1200 = 6000 \text{ req/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute instances required at target load:**

$$
N_{required} = \left\lceil \frac{L_{current}}{L_{target}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{5000}{1000} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 5 \right\rceil = 5
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the delta using the elasticity formula:**

$$
\Delta R = N_{required} - N_{current} = 5 - 5 = 0
$$

No scaling action needed — the system is at steady state.

**Step 5 — Stress test scenario:**

If traffic spikes to $L_{current} = 8000$ req/s:

$$
N_{required} = \left\lceil \frac{8000}{1000} \right\rceil = 8
$$

$$
\Delta R = 8 - 5 = 3
$$

The auto-scaler adds **3 new instances** to absorb the spike.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Pay-Per-Use Cost Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A startup runs the following AWS workload for one month (30 days):
- 4 EC2 instances of type `m5.large` (2 vCPU each) at **\$0.096/vCPU-hour**.
- 2 TB of gp3 SSD storage at **\$0.08/GB-month**.
- 500 GB of outbound data transfer at **\$0.09/GB**.
- Managed RDS database at a flat **\$150/month**.

**Step 1 — Compute compute cost:**

Total vCPU hours per month:

$$
H_{compute} = 4 \times 2 \times 24 \times 30 = 5760 \text{ vCPU-hours}
$$

Compute cost:

$$
C_{compute} = 5760 \times 0.096 = \text{\$}552.96
$$

**Step 2 — Compute storage cost:**

$$
C_{storage} = 2 \times 1024 \times 0.08 = 2048 \times 0.08 = \text{\$}163.84
$$

**Step 3 — Compute network cost (egress only):**

$$
C_{network} = 500 \times 0.09 = \text{\$}45.00
$$

**Step 4 — Compute service cost:**

$$
C_{services} = \text{\$}150.00
$$

**Step 5 — Compute TCO:**

$$
TCO = 552.96 + 163.84 + 45.00 + 150.00 = \text{\$}911.80 \text{ per month}
$$

**Step 6 — Annualize:**

$$
TCO_{annual} = 911.80 \times 12 = \text{\$}10{,}941.60
$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5: ROI of Cloud Migration

**Problem Statement:** A company currently spends **₹50,00,000/year** on on-premise infrastructure (CAPEX amortized + maintenance). After migrating to cloud, the OPEX is **₹32,00,000/year**. Performance improves, generating an estimated **₹15,00,000/year** in additional business revenue.

**Step 1 — Compute cost savings:**

$$
\text{Savings} = 50{,}00{,}000 - 32{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}18{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total benefit (savings + additional revenue):**

$$
\text{Benefit} = 18{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}33{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Compute ROI:**

$$
ROI = \frac{\text{Benefit} - \text{Cost}}{\text{Cost}} \times 100 = \frac{33{,}00{,}000 - 32{,}00{,}000}{32{,}00{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
ROI = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{32{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \approx 3.125\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for the board exam:** A positive ROI confirms the migration is financially justified. If ROI were negative, the migration should be rejected on purely economic grounds (assuming all other factors are equal).

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Design Choices vs Cost Impact

| Design Objective | Typical Implementation | Cost Impact (Multiplier) | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single-AZ Deployment | 1 Availability Zone | **1× (baseline)** | Lowest cost, lowest availability |
| Multi-AZ Deployment | 2–3 AZs with replication | **~2× compute + storage** | Higher availability |
| Multi-Region (Active-Passive) | DR site in another region | **~2.5× compute** | Disaster recovery |
| Multi-Region (Active-Active) | Full geo-redundancy | **~3× compute** | Highest availability |
| Reserved Instances (1-yr) | Pre-pay compute | **~0.6×** vs on-demand | Lock-in |
| Spot Instances | Interruptible compute | **~0.3×** vs on-demand | Fault-tolerant only |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
