<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. Define Cloud Design Objectives and list any six of them.
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Cloud Design Objectives are the non-functional and architectural goals that a cloud system must satisfy to deliver resilient, efficient, and economically viable services. The six primary cloud design objectives are:

1. **Scalability** — Ability to handle increasing load.
2. **Elasticity** — Automated scale-up and scale-down.
3. **Availability** — Percentage of uptime.
4. **Reliability** — Probability of failure-free operation.
5. **Performance** — Throughput and latency.
6. **Security** — Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA).

*(1 mark for definition + ½ mark each for any 4 objectives listed = 3 marks)*

### Question A2. Distinguish between CAPEX and OPEX with a cloud computing example.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | CAPEX | OPEX |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | One-time, upfront | Recurring, pay-per-use |
| Accounting | Capitalized asset | Operating expense |
| Cash flow | Front-loaded | Smooth, monthly |

**Cloud Example:** A company buying a ₹15 lakh server is CAPEX. The same company paying ₹45,000/month for an EC2 instance is OPEX. *(1 mark for distinction + 1 mark for table + 1 mark for example = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B1 (Choice A) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the eight cloud design objectives with a one-line description for each. State the relationship between **Availability** and **Reliability** using a mathematical model. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cloud service has an MTBF of 5000 hours and an MTTR of 2 hours. Compute the availability, express it as an SLA tier (nines), and calculate the maximum allowable downtime per year. If the company upgrades to a redundant architecture that doubles the MTBF to 10000 hours but increases the MTTR to 3 hours, recompute the availability and the yearly downtime. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for B1(a):**

The eight cloud design objectives are:

1. **Scalability** — Capacity to handle load growth via vertical or horizontal scaling.
2. **Elasticity** — Automated, on-demand provisioning and de-provisioning.
3. **Availability** — Ratio of uptime to total time.
4. **Reliability** — Probability of failure-free operation over a time interval.
5. **Performance** — Measured via throughput, latency, and jitter.
6. **Security** — Enforced through the CIA triad.
7. **Manageability** — Operational ease through observability and automation.
8. **Cost-Effectiveness** — Maximizing performance per rupee spent.

*(½ mark each × 8 = 4 marks)*

**Mathematical Relationship between Availability and Reliability:**

Availability is the *long-run* fraction of time the system is up. Reliability is the *instantaneous* probability of surviving up to time $t$. The exponential reliability function is:

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} \quad \text{where} \quad \lambda = \frac{1}{MTBF}
$$

The steady-state availability is then:

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

*(3 marks for the derivation and explanation of the relationship)*

---

**Model Solution for B1(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute initial availability:**

$$
A_1 = \frac{MTBF_1}{MTBF_1 + MTTR_1} = \frac{5000}{5000 + 2} = \frac{5000}{5002} \approx 0.99960
$$

So $A_1 \approx 99.96\%$.

**Step 2 — Determine SLA tier:**

This falls between **three nines (99.9%)** and **four nines (99.99%)**. Since $99.96\% > 99.9\%$ but $< 99.99\%$, it is classified as **three-and-a-half nines (≈99.95%)**. *(2 marks for computation)*

**Step 3 — Compute initial yearly downtime:**

$$
U_1 = 1 - A_1 = 1 - 0.99960 = 0.0004
$$

$$
D_1 = 0.0004 \times 8760 = 3.504 \text{ hours/year} \approx 210.24 \text{ minutes/year}
$$

*(1 mark for downtime)*

**Step 4 — Compute new availability after upgrade:**

$$
A_2 = \frac{10000}{10000 + 3} = \frac{10000}{10003} \approx 0.99970
$$

So $A_2 \approx 99.97\%$. *(2 marks)*

**Step 5 — Compute new yearly downtime:**

$$
D_2 = (1 - 0.99970) \times 8760 = 0.0003 \times 8760 = 2.628 \text{ hours/year} \approx 157.68 \text{ minutes/year}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 6 — Comparative Conclusion:**

The redundancy upgrade reduces yearly downtime from **3.504 hours** to **2.628 hours**, a **~25% improvement** in downtime, but at the cost of doubled infrastructure spend (since MTBF doubling implies duplicate components). This is the classic **availability vs cost trade-off** in cloud design.

*(1 mark for the trade-off conclusion)*

---

### Question B2 (Choice B) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **Cloud Cost Model** in detail. Differentiate between **on-demand, reserved, and spot** pricing strategies. Compute the annual cost for a workload using each strategy and identify which is most economical for a steady-state production system. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A company migrates its on-premise system (annual cost ₹80,00,000) to the cloud (annual cost ₹55,00,000). The migration unlocks new business worth ₹30,00,000/year. Calculate the **annual savings, total benefit, and ROI**. Justify whether the migration is financially sound. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for B2(a):**

The Cloud Cost Model is a structured framework that converts resource consumption into monetary expenditure. Its primary components are:

1. **Compute Cost** — Charged per vCPU-hour or instance-hour.
2. **Storage Cost** — Charged per GB-month.
3. **Network Cost** — Charged per GB of outbound (egress) traffic.
4. **Managed Service Cost** — Charged per API call or throughput unit.

*(2 marks for the cost model explanation)*

**Comparison of Pricing Strategies:**

| Parameter | On-Demand | Reserved (1-Year) | Spot |
|---|---|---|---|
| Discount | 0% | ~40–60% | ~70–90% |
| Commitment | None | 1–3 years | None (interruptible) |
| Best For | Dev/Test, spiky workloads | Steady-state production | Batch, fault-tolerant |
| Risk | Highest cost | Lock-in | Preemption |

*(2 marks for the comparison table)*

**Annual Cost Calculation (assume 2 vCPU × 24 × 365 = 17,520 vCPU-hours/year at \$0.10/vCPU-hour baseline):**

On-demand cost:

$$
C_{OD} = 17520 \times 0.10 = \text{\$}1752
$$

Reserved (50% discount):

$$
C_{RI} = 1752 \times 0.50 = \text{\$}876
$$

Spot (80% discount):

$$
C_{spot} = 1752 \times 0.20 = \text{\$}350.40
$$

*(2 marks for the three computations)*

**Conclusion:** For a **steady-state production system**, **Reserved Instances** are the most economical **reliable** choice. Spot is cheaper but risks preemption, which is unacceptable for production. *(1 mark for conclusion)*

---

**Model Solution for B2(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute Annual Cost Savings:**

$$
\text{Savings} = 80{,}00{,}000 - 55{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}25{,}00{,}000
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Compute Total Annual Benefit:**

$$
\text{Benefit} = \text{Savings} + \text{New Revenue} = 25{,}00{,}000 + 30{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}55{,}00{,}000
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Compute Net Benefit (Benefit − Cloud Cost):**

$$
\text{Net Benefit} = 55{,}00{,}000 - 55{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}0
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Compute ROI:**

Using the standard ROI formula (Net Benefit / Cloud Cost):

$$
ROI = \frac{0}{55{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 0\%
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 5 — Financial Justification:**

An ROI of exactly **0%** means the migration is **financially neutral on net benefit**, but the company still benefits from:
- Elimination of **₹25 lakh** in legacy CAPEX exposure.
- **Scalability and elasticity** (not captured in pure ROI).
- Improved **disaster recovery and security posture**.

**Verdict:** The migration is **justified on qualitative grounds** (agility, scalability, business enablement), even though the pure financial ROI is 0%. The ₹30 lakh new revenue alone equals the cloud cost, making the company **cash-flow neutral** while gaining strategic flexibility. *(2 marks for justification)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing Scalability and Elasticity** — A 2-mark penalty is routinely applied if these are used interchangeably. *Scalability = capacity to grow; Elasticity = automated growth and shrinkage.*
> 2. **Forgetting Units in Cost Calculations** — Always show units (₹/hour, GB/month, ₹/GB). A cost without units is considered incomplete.
> 3. **Computing ROI Incorrectly** — Use **Net Benefit / Cost**, NOT just Revenue/Cost. Students frequently write `ROI = Revenue / Cost`, which is wrong.
> 4. **Missing the SLA Tier Link** — When asked to compute availability, students often forget to **map the result to the SLA tier** (three nines, four nines, etc.). This linkage is a frequently tested KTU 2024 outcome.
> 5. **Ignoring Non-Functional Trade-offs** — Always state the **cost implication** of every design decision. A design without a cost trade-off discussion loses 1–2 marks per question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Cloud Design Objectives and Cost Model**

- ☑ **Eight Design Objectives**: Scalability, Elasticity, Availability, Reliability, Performance, Security, Manageability, Cost-Effectiveness.
- ☑ **Scalability ≠ Elasticity**: Scalability is the *capacity* to grow; Elasticity is the *automated execution* of growth.
- ☑ **Availability Formula**: $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$.
- ☑ **SLA Tiers**: 99.9% (8.76 h/yr), 99.99% (52.56 min/yr), 99.999% (5.26 min/yr).
- ☑ **Reliability Function**: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.
- ☑ **Little's Law**: $L = \lambda \times W$ (for capacity planning).
- ☑ **CAPEX vs OPEX**: CAPEX = upfront, depreciable asset; OPEX = recurring, operating expense.
- ☑ **Pricing Models**: On-Demand (no discount), Reserved (~50% off, commit), Spot (~80% off, interruptible).
- ☑ **TCO Formula**: $TCO = C_{compute} + C_{storage} + C_{network} + C_{services} + C_{personnel} + C_{downtime}$.
- ☑ **Pay-Per-Use**: $C_{total} = \sum R_i \times U_i \times T_i$.
- ☑ **ROI Formula**: $ROI = \frac{\text{Net Benefit}}{\text{Cost}} \times 100$ — always use *net* benefit, not gross revenue.
- ☑ **Cost-Awareness**: Every design decision (redundancy, multi-AZ, multi-region) must be justified with a **cost multiplier** (1×, 2×, 2.5×, 3×).
- ☑ **Egress is Billable**: Inbound (ingress) traffic is typically **free**; outbound (egress) is **charged per GB**.
- ☑ **Storage Tiering**: Hot ($$$) → Warm ($$) → Cold ($) → Archive (¢) — choose based on access frequency.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Cloud Design Objectives and Cost Model**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) — PECMT632 — Topics in Computing Systems*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Infrastructure-as-a-Service, Platform-as-a-Service, Software-as-a-Service
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Infrastructure-as-a-Service, Platform-as-a-Service, Software-as-a-Service

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — The Cloud Service Models

The **NIST SP 800-145** document defines Cloud Computing through three primary **Service Models**. According to KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4, the three canonical deployment service abstractions are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NIST Definition of Cloud Service Models**
> The three standardized service models represent layered abstractions over underlying compute, storage, and networking infrastructure. They differ fundamentally in **who manages what** — i.e., the boundary of the *shared responsibility model*.

### 1.1.1 Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)
**IaaS** is the provisioning of fundamental computing resources — *processing, storage, networks, and other fundamental physical/virtual resources* — where the consumer can deploy arbitrary software (OS, middleware, applications) but **cannot manage or control the underlying cloud infrastructure**.

> **Formal Definition (verbatim NIST-aligned):**
> *"The capability provided to the consumer is to provision processing, storage, networks, and other fundamental computing resources where the consumer is able to deploy and run arbitrary software, which can include operating systems and applications. The consumer does not manage or control the underlying cloud infrastructure but has control over operating systems, storage, deployed applications, and possibly limited control of select networking components (e.g., host firewalls)."*

### 1.1.2 Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS)
**PaaS** allows the consumer to deploy **consumer-created or acquired applications** onto the cloud infrastructure using **programming languages, libraries, services, and tools supported by the provider**. The consumer manages the application and data; the provider manages everything beneath it.

> **Formal Definition (verbatim NIST-aligned):**
> *"The capability provided to the consumer is to deploy onto the cloud infrastructure consumer-created or acquired applications created using programming languages, libraries, services, and tools supported by the provider. The consumer does not manage or control the underlying cloud infrastructure including network, servers, operating systems, or storage, but has control over the deployed applications and possibly configuration settings for the application-hosting environment."*

### 1.1.3 Software-as-a-Service (SaaS)
**SaaS** allows the consumer to use the **provider's applications running on a cloud infrastructure**, accessible via thin client interfaces (typically a web browser). The consumer has **no control over the underlying cloud infrastructure or even the application capabilities** beyond limited user-configuration settings.

> **Formal Definition (verbatim NIST-aligned):**
> *"The capability provided to the consumer is to use the provider's applications running on a cloud infrastructure. The applications are accessible from various client devices through either a thin client interface, such as a web browser (e.g., web-based email), or a program interface. The consumer does not manage or control the underlying cloud infrastructure including network, servers, operating systems, storage, or even individual application capabilities, with the possible exception of limited user-specific application configuration settings."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pizza-as-a-Service" Model

The cleanest pedagogical analogy for the three service models is the **Pizza-as-a-Service** model, originally popularized by **Albert Barron (IBM, 2014)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pizza Analogy Intuition**
> - **IaaS** = Buying a pizza dough base, cheese, and toppings from a store, and baking it at home. You handle the oven, the baking, the slicing, and the plating.
> - **PaaS** = Ordering a take-and-bake pizza: the dough, sauce, and toppings are pre-assembled. You only bake it in your own oven.
> - **SaaS** = Ordering a fully cooked, delivered pizza. You just eat it.

In computing terms:

| Abstraction | Analogy | You Manage | Provider Manages |
|---|---|---|---|
| **On-Premise** | Making pizza from scratch | Everything (ingredients → oven → delivery) | Nothing |
| **IaaS** | Semi-prepared kit | OS, Middleware, Runtime, Apps, Data | Servers, Storage, Networking, Virtualization |
| **PaaS** | Take-and-bake | Apps, Data | OS, Middleware, Runtime, Servers, Storage, Networking |
| **SaaS** | Delivered cooked pizza | Just usage / user config | Everything beneath |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exams:** The further "up the stack" you go (On-Premise → IaaS → PaaS → SaaS), the **less responsibility** you carry and the **more you pay per unit** but with **lower operational complexity**.

---

## 1.3 The Shared Responsibility Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shared Responsibility Model — Core Concept**
> In every cloud service model, the cloud **provider** is always responsible for **Security *of* the Cloud** (the underlying infrastructure), while the cloud **consumer** is responsible for **Security *in* the Cloud** (data, access, application logic). The *boundary* between these two responsibilities shifts depending on the service model.

This boundary shift is the **single most examinable concept** in Module 4 of PECMT632.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Cloud Computing Stack

To understand the three service models, we decompose the IT stack into **seven canonical layers** (per NIST):

| # | Layer | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Application** | End-user software (CRM, ERP, email) |
| 2 | **Data** | Information stored and managed |
| 3 | **Runtime** | Execution environment (JRE, Python interpreter) |
| 4 | **Middleware** | API gateways, message brokers, ESBs |
| 5 | **OS** | Operating system kernel and shell |
| 6 | **Virtualization** | Hypervisor, container engine |
| 7 | **Compute / Storage / Network** | Physical servers, disks, switches |

Each service model draws a **horizontal cut** through this stack. The layers **above** the cut are managed by the **consumer**; the layers **below** the cut are managed by the **provider**.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Layer-by-Layer Control Matrix

| Layer | On-Premise | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Application** | You | You | You | Provider |
| **Data** | You | You | You | Provider (mostly) |
| **Runtime** | You | You | Provider | Provider |
| **Middleware** | You | You | Provider | Provider |
| **OS** | You | You | Provider | Provider |
| **Virtualization** | You | Provider | Provider | Provider |
| **Compute/Storage/Network** | You | Provider | Provider | Provider |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A common 14-mark question asks to *"compare and contrast IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS with respect to the layers managed by the consumer."* Memorize the cut-points:
> - **IaaS cut:** between Virtualization and OS
> - **PaaS cut:** between Runtime and Data
> - **SaaS cut:** between Application and Data (only thin user config)

---

## 2.3 Provider vs Consumer — Explicit Responsibility Allocation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix is the **single most heavily weighted topic** in KTU Module 4 examinations. Memorize both directions.

| Responsibility Component | IaaS (Consumer / Provider) | PaaS (Consumer / Provider) | SaaS (Consumer / Provider) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Facility / Physical Plant | Provider | Provider | Provider |
| Physical Security | Provider | Provider | Provider |
| Power, Cooling, Racks | Provider | Provider | Provider |
| Servers (Hardware) | Provider | Provider | Provider |
| Storage (Hardware) | Provider | Provider | Provider |
| Network Hardware | Provider | Provider | Provider |
| Virtualization / Hypervisor | Provider | Provider | Provider |
| **Operating System** | **Consumer** | Provider | Provider |
| **Middleware** | **Consumer** | Provider | Provider |
| **Runtime / Language Env.** | **Consumer** | Provider | Provider |
| **Application Code** | **Consumer** | **Consumer** | Provider |
| **Data** | **Consumer** | **Consumer** | Provider (mostly) |
| User Access / IAM Policies | **Consumer** | **Consumer** | **Consumer** |
| Network Traffic Protection (in-transit) | **Consumer** | **Consumer** | **Consumer** |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Cloud Service Selection Metrics

Cloud consumers must evaluate services using **standardized metrics**. The following formulas and indicators are high-yield for KTU numerical/analytical questions.

### 2.4.1 Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)

$$
\text{TCO} = \text{CapEx} + \text{OpEx}_{\text{period}} + \text{Migration Cost} + \text{Training Cost} - \text{Salvage Value}
$$

Where:
- $\text{CapEx}$ = Capital expenditure (one-time hardware, facility)
- $\text{OpEx}_{\text{period}}$ = Operational expenditure over the analysis period
- $\text{Migration Cost}$ = Cost to port existing workloads

> For cloud-native deployments, $\text{CapEx} \rightarrow 0$ and $\text{OpEx}$ becomes the dominant term.

### 2.4.2 Pay-as-you-go Billing (IaaS/SaaS Pricing)

$$
\text{Monthly Cost} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \bigl( u_i \cdot p_i \bigr) + S_{\text{storage}} + T_{\text{egress}}
$$

Where:
- $u_i$ = usage units of resource $i$ (e.g., vCPU-hours, GB-months)
- $p_i$ = unit price of resource $i$
- $S_{\text{storage}}$ = persistent storage cost
- $T_{\text{egress}}$ = data transfer-out cost (often the **hidden cost trap**)

### 2.4.3 Utilization Efficiency

$$
\eta_{\text{util}} = \frac{\text{Actual Used Capacity}}{\text{Provisioned Capacity}} \times 100\,\%
$$

In IaaS, $\eta_{\text{util}}$ is often **low (10–30%)** due to over-provisioning for peak loads. Serverless/PaaS improves this via elastic scaling.

### 2.4.4 Availability (Downtime-Equivalent)

$$
\text{Availability (\%)} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100
$$

| Availability | Max Downtime / Year | Tier |
|---|---|---|
| 99% ("two nines") | 3.65 days | Consumer-grade |
| **99.9% ("three nines")** | **8.77 hours** | Standard SLA (IaaS) |
| **99.99% ("four nines")** | **52.6 minutes** | Premium SLA (PaaS/SaaS) |
| 99.999% ("five nines") | 5.26 minutes | Carrier / Mission-critical |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** Always quote the **"nines" of availability** as a self-contained number. Saying *"four nines availability"* without writing $99.99\%$ loses partial marks.

### 2.4.5 Elasticity / Scaling Metric

$$
\text{Scaling Latency} = T_{\text{trigger}} - T_{\text{demand}}
$$

Where $T_{\text{trigger}}$ is when the cloud platform allocates a new resource, and $T_{\text{demand}}$ is when the actual demand spike occurred.

- **Vertical Scaling (Scale-Up/Down):** Add CPU/RAM to an existing VM → IaaS-friendly
- **Horizontal Scaling (Scale-Out/In):** Add more VM instances → native to PaaS

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Service Model | Real-World Use Cases | Industry Adoption |
|---|---|---|
| **IaaS** | Disaster Recovery (DR), HPC workloads, lift-and-shift migrations, dev/test environments, network-bound enterprise apps | AWS EC2, Azure VMs, Google Compute Engine, Oracle Cloud Infrastructure |
| **PaaS** | API development, microservices, ML pipelines, IoT backends, serverless functions | AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Azure App Service, Google App Engine, Heroku, Vercel |
| **SaaS** | Email, CRM, collaboration, ERP, video conferencing, productivity | Microsoft 365, Salesforce, Slack, Zoom, Google Workspace, Dropbox |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight (KTU Project Relevance):**
> For M.Tech dissertation work involving ML/IoT, the optimal choice is **PaaS** (e.g., Heroku, Google App Engine) — you avoid OS hardening, network configuration, and patching, yet retain full control over application logic and data. **IaaS** is appropriate only when your workload requires custom kernels, GPUs, or specialized network topologies.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: TCO Comparison — On-Premise vs IaaS

> **Problem:** A startup needs $N_{\text{vm}} = 4$ virtual machines each requiring $r = 8$ vCPUs and $m = 32$ GB RAM for the next $T = 36$ months. Compare On-Premise TCO against IaaS TCO.

### Step 1: Define Parameters

We define the following terms:

- $N_{\text{vm}} = 4$ virtual machines
- $r = 8$ vCPUs per VM
- $m = 32$ GB RAM per VM
- $T = 36$ months (analysis period)
- $H_{\text{monthly}} = 730$ hours/month (industry standard: $365 \times 24 / 12$)
- $U_{\text{peak}} = 100\%$ utilization target

### Step 2: On-Premise Capital Expenditure (CapEx)

Assume a physical server hosts 2 VMs (overcommitted):

$$
N_{\text{server}} = \left\lceil \frac{N_{\text{vm}}}{2} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{4}{2} \right\rceil = 2 \text{ servers}
$$

Server unit cost: $C_{\text{server}} = \$\,12{,}000$
Annual power and cooling per server: $C_{\text{power}} = \$\,1{,}800$

$$
\text{CapEx} = N_{\text{server}} \cdot C_{\text{server}} = 2 \times 12{,}000 = \$24{,}000
$$

### Step 3: On-Premise Operational Expenditure (OpEx) Over 36 Months

Annual IT admin cost: $A_{\text{admin}} = \$\,15{,}000$ (1 FTE)
Annual power, cooling, and rack costs: $C_{\text{ops}} = N_{\text{server}} \cdot C_{\text{power}}$

$$
C_{\text{ops}} = 2 \times 1{,}800 = \$3{,}600 \text{ per year}
$$

$$
\text{OpEx}_{36} = (A_{\text{admin}} + C_{\text{ops}}) \times 3 = (15{,}000 + 3{,}600) \times 3 = \$55{,}800
$$

### Step 4: On-Premise TCO (36 Months)

We sum the capitalized hardware, operational costs, and software licensing (assume $C_{\text{lic}} = \$\,6{,}000$):

$$
\text{TCO}_{\text{on-prem}} = \text{CapEx} + \text{OpEx}_{36} + C_{\text{lic}} - \text{Salvage}
$$

$$
\text{Salvage} = \frac{N_{\text{server}} \cdot C_{\text{server}}}{5} = \frac{24{,}000}{5} = \$4{,}800
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCO}_{\text{on-prem}} &= 24{,}000 + 55{,}800 + 6{,}000 - 4{,}800 \\
&= 81{,}000 \text{ USD over 36 months}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: IaaS Cost Over 36 Months

IaaS pricing (AWS EC2 m5.2xlarge example):
- $p_{\text{vm-hr}} = \$0.384$ per hour per VM (on-demand)

$$
\text{vCPU-hours} = N_{\text{vm}} \cdot r \cdot H_{\text{monthly}} \cdot T = 4 \cdot 8 \cdot 730 \cdot 36 = 840{,}960 \text{ vCPU-hours}
$$

$$
\text{VM-hours} = N_{\text{vm}} \cdot H_{\text{monthly}} \cdot T = 4 \cdot 730 \cdot 36 = 105{,}120 \text{ VM-hours}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Compute Cost} &= \text{VM-hours} \cdot p_{\text{vm-hr}} \\
&= 105{,}120 \times 0.384 = \$40{,}366.08
\end{aligned}
$$

Storage: $S = 500 \text{ GB/VM}$, $p_{\text{storage}} = \$0.10/\text{GB-month}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Storage Cost} &= S \cdot N_{\text{vm}} \cdot p_{\text{storage}} \cdot T \\
&= 500 \cdot 4 \cdot 0.10 \cdot 36 = \$7{,}200
\end{aligned}
$$

Data egress (20% of storage traffic egressed): $T_{\text{egress}} = 0.20 \cdot S \cdot N_{\text{vm}} \cdot T$, $p_{\text{egress}} = \$0.09/\text{GB}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Egress Cost} &= (0.20 \cdot 500 \cdot 4 \cdot 36) \cdot 0.09 = 14{,}400 \cdot 0.09 = \$1{,}296
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: IaaS TCO (36 Months)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCO}_{\text{iaas}} &= \text{Compute Cost} + \text{Storage Cost} + \text{Egress Cost} \\
&= 40{,}366.08 + 7{,}200 + 1{,}296 \\
&= \$48{,}862.08 \text{ over 36 months}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7: Final Comparative Decision

$$
\Delta \text{TCO} = \text{TCO}_{\text{on-prem}} - \text{TCO}_{\text{iaas}} = 81{,}000 - 48{,}862.08 = \$32{,}137.92
$$

$$
\text{Savings \%} = \frac{\Delta \text{TCO}}{\text{TCO}_{\text{on-prem}}} \times 100 = \frac{32{,}137.92}{81{,}000} \times 100 \approx 39.68\%
$$

> **Verdict:** IaaS provides ~39.7% cost savings over 36 months for this workload, *without* including opportunity cost savings from elastic scaling during low-traffic periods. **Engineering utility:** Demonstrates why KTU-aligned industry projects favor IaaS for variable workloads.

---

> **For 14-mark KTU questions, this matrix is the canonical structure expected in answers.**

| Attribute | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Abstraction Level** | Low | Medium | High |

| **Skill Required** | Sysadmin + DevOps | Developer only | End-user |

| **Examples** | AWS EC2, Azure VM, GCE | Heroku, App Engine, Beanstalk | Gmail, Salesforce, M365 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Note:** When comparing services in a 14-mark answer, structure the response as a **labeled table** (above) followed by a **2–3 paragraph narrative** explaining *why* each characteristic matters. Avoid bare bullet points — examiners in KTU deduct marks for unstructured prose.

## 3.3 Derivation: Elasticity Equation for PaaS Auto-Scaling

PaaS platforms implement **reactive auto-scaling** using threshold-based rules.

Let:
- $N_{\text{inst}}(t)$ = Number of active instances at time $t$
- $N_{\min}$ = Minimum instances (always running)
- $N_{\max}$ = Maximum cap
- $\rho(t)$ = CPU utilization at time $t$
- $\rho_{\text{up}}$ = Scale-out threshold (e.g., 70%)
- $\rho_{\text{down}}$ = Scale-in threshold (e.g., 30%)

The reactive rule is:

$$
N_{\text{inst}}(t + \Delta t) = \begin{cases} \min\bigl(N_{\text{inst}}(t) \cdot 2, \; N_{\max}\bigr), & \text{if } \rho(t) > \rho_{\text{up}} \\ \max\bigl(N_{\text{inst}}(t) / 2, \; N_{\min}\bigr), & \text{if } \rho(t) < \rho_{\text{down}} \\ N_{\text{inst}}(t), & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

> **Interpretation:** PaaS effectively eliminates the elasticity headache because the platform — not the developer — implements this loop. This is precisely why PaaS is recommended for **bursty workloads** (e.g., exam-form submission systems, e-commerce flash sales).

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture — Request Flow Across Service Models

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant U as End User Browser
    participant S as SaaS Provider App
    participant P as PaaS Runtime

    Note over DC: Physical Infrastructure
    DC-->>U: 6. Response Returns Up the Stack
```

> **Engineering Reading:** Notice how the same request traverses **all seven layers** regardless of which service model the user purchased. The model only determines **who configures each layer**, not whether the layer exists.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

---

### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]`
**Q: Define the three cloud service models as per NIST and identify one real-world example for each.** *(3 Marks)*

> **Model Answer:**
>
> According to **NIST SP 800-145**, the three cloud service models are:
>
> 1. **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** — provides fundamental computing resources (processing, storage, networks) on which the consumer can deploy arbitrary software including OS and applications. The consumer controls OS, storage, and applications; the provider controls the underlying infrastructure. **Example:** *Amazon EC2.*
>
> 2. **Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS)** — provides a platform where consumers can deploy their own (or acquired) applications using provider-supported languages, libraries, and tools. The consumer controls the application and data; the provider manages OS, runtime, and middleware. **Example:** *Google App Engine.*
>
> 3. **Software-as-a-Service (SaaS)** — provides access to provider's applications running on cloud infrastructure via thin client interfaces such as browsers. The consumer only configures limited user-specific settings. **Example:** *Microsoft 365.*
>
> *(Each definition ~1 mark; examples bundled)*

---

### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]`
**Q: What is the "shared responsibility model" in cloud computing? Who is responsible for the operating system in each of IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS?** *(3 Marks)*

> **Model Answer:**
>
> The **shared responsibility model** is a security and operational framework in which the cloud **provider** is always responsible for **Security *of* the Cloud** (the underlying physical and virtual infrastructure), while the cloud **consumer** is responsible for **Security *in* the Cloud** (their data, applications, access policies, and configurations). The boundary shifts depending on the service model.
>
> | Service Model | OS Managed By |
> |---|---|
> | **IaaS** | **Consumer** |
> | **PaaS** | **Provider** |
> | **SaaS** | **Provider** |
>
> *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for naming boundary-shift principle, 1 mark for the table)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B (Choice A)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Question]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **cloud service models — IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS** — with reference to the NIST definition, the layers of the IT stack each model exposes to the consumer, and a real-world provider for each. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Compare IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS based on **consumer control, flexibility, time-to-deploy, cost model, vendor lock-in, and typical user**. Provide a structured comparison table. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

The three cloud service models defined by **NIST SP 800-145** are layered abstractions over a common underlying infrastructure. They differ in **how much of the stack the consumer manages**.

**1. Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS):**
- **Definition:** Provides processing, storage, networks, and other fundamental computing resources on which the consumer can deploy arbitrary software including OS and applications.
- **Stack exposure:** Consumer controls OS, runtime, middleware, application, and data. Provider manages virtualization, servers, storage, and networking hardware.
- **Analogy:** Semi-prepared kit — provider supplies raw materials; consumer assembles and bakes.
- **Real-world example:** *Amazon Web Services EC2*, *Microsoft Azure Virtual Machines*, *Google Compute Engine (GCE)*.

**2. Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS):**
- **Definition:** Provides a cloud-hosted platform with runtime, middleware, OS, and development tools. The consumer deploys only the application code and data.
- **Stack exposure:** Consumer controls application code, data, and limited host-environment configuration. Provider manages everything below.
- **Analogy:** Take-and-bake — provider prepares the platform; consumer only brings the application.
- **Real-world example:** *Google App Engine*, *AWS Elastic Beanstalk*, *Heroku*, *Azure App Service*.

**3. Software-as-a-Service (SaaS):**
- **Definition:** Provides access to provider-hosted applications via thin clients (typically browsers).
- **Stack exposure:** Consumer uses only the application; limited user-specific configuration. Provider manages all layers below.
- **Analogy:** Delivered cooked meal — provider cooks, delivers, and maintains; consumer just eats.
- **Real-world example:** *Microsoft 365*, *Google Workspace*, *Salesforce*, *Slack*, *Zoom*.

**Layer-cut summary (crucial 1–2 marks in valuation):**
- IaaS cut is between **Virtualization and OS**.
- PaaS cut is between **Runtime and Data**.
- SaaS cut is between **Application and Data** (only thin config above).

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

| Attribute | **IaaS** | **PaaS** | **SaaS** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Consumer Control** | High (OS, runtime, apps) | Medium (apps + data) | Low (just user config) |
| **Flexibility** | Maximum | Medium | Minimum |
| **Time to Deploy** | Hours to days | Minutes | Immediate |
| **Cost Model** | Pay per provisioned resource | Pay per usage / request | Subscription / per-seat |
| **Vendor Lock-In** | Low (portable VM images) | Medium-High (proprietary APIs) | Very High (proprietary apps) |
| **Skill Required** | Sysadmin + DevOps | Application developer | End-user |
| **Typical User** | IT Operations team | Application developer | Business end-user |

**Narrative synthesis:**
The three models represent a **trade-off triangle** between control, agility, and operational burden. IaaS offers maximum flexibility at the cost of operational complexity; PaaS optimizes developer productivity by abstracting infrastructure concerns; SaaS minimizes user burden entirely at the cost of configurability. KTU industry-aligned projects favor **PaaS** when the goal is rapid prototyping, **IaaS** when custom kernels/GPUs/specialized networking are required, and **SaaS** for non-engineering business functions.

---

### **Question B (Choice B — Alternative Set)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model Question]`

**(a)** With the aid of a **layered architecture diagram**, explain the **shared responsibility model** for IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS. Identify the layers the **consumer** controls in each model. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A startup requires 5 virtual machines, each with 4 vCPUs and 16 GB RAM, for a 24-month period. Compare the **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** between an On-Premise deployment and an IaaS deployment. Use the following assumptions:

- On-premise: server unit cost = ₹8,00,000; one server hosts 2 VMs; annual admin cost = ₹12,00,000; annual power/cooling per server = ₹1,50,000; software licensing = ₹4,00,000; salvage value = 20% of CapEx.
- IaaS: ₹8 per VM-hour; storage = ₹5/GB-month with 200 GB per VM; egress negligible.

Compute both TCOs and determine the savings percentage. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

The shared responsibility model divides operational and security duties between the cloud **provider** and cloud **consumer**. The dividing line shifts upward as we move from IaaS → PaaS → SaaS, transferring more responsibility to the provider.

**Architecture diagram (textual rendition for exam script):**

```
LAYER                   IaaS      PaaS       SaaS
----------------------------------------------------------
Application              C         C          P
Data                     C         C          P
Runtime                  C         P          P
Middleware               C         P          P
Operating System         C         P          P
Virtualization           P         P          P
Physical Servers         P         P          P
Physical Network         P         P          P
Physical Storage         P         P          P
Facility                 P         P          P
----------------------------------------------------------
C = Consumer manages, P = Provider manages
```

**Consumer-controlled layers:**
- **IaaS:** Application, Data, Runtime, Middleware, OS (5 layers)
- **PaaS:** Application, Data (2 layers)
- **SaaS:** Effectively none (only user-level config of an existing app)

**Key conclusion (1 mark):** In IaaS, the consumer has the most flexibility but also carries the largest security and operational burden. In SaaS, the provider takes nearly all responsibility, freeing the consumer to focus on business use of the software.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given Data:**
- $N_{\text{vm}} = 5$ VMs
- $r = 4$ vCPUs per VM
- $m = 16$ GB RAM per VM
- $T = 24$ months
- $H_{\text{monthly}} = 730$ hours/month
- On-premise server capacity: 2 VMs per server
- Server unit cost: $C_{\text{server}} = \text{₹}8{,}00{,}000$
- Annual admin cost: $A_{\text{admin}} = \text{₹}12{,}00{,}000$
- Annual power/cooling: $C_{\text{ops}} = \text{₹}1{,}50{,}000$ per server
- Software licensing: $C_{\text{lic}} = \text{₹}4{,}00{,}000$
- Salvage value: $\text{Salvage} = 0.20 \times \text{CapEx}$
- IaaS VM-hour rate: $p_{\text{vm-hr}} = \text{₹}8$
- Storage: $S = 200$ GB per VM, $p_{\text{storage}} = \text{₹}5/\text{GB-month}$
- Data egress: negligible

---

**Step 1: On-Premise CapEx**

Number of servers required:

$$
N_{\text{server}} = \left\lceil \frac{N_{\text{vm}}}{2} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{5}{2} \right\rceil = 3 \text{ servers}
$$

$$
\text{CapEx} = N_{\text{server}} \cdot C_{\text{server}} = 3 \times 8{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}24{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2: On-Premise OpEx Over 24 Months**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OpEx}_{24} &= (A_{\text{admin}} + N_{\text{server}} \cdot C_{\text{ops}}) \times 2 \\
&= (12{,}00{,}000 + 3 \times 1{,}50{,}000) \times 2 \\
&= (12{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}50{,}000) \times 2 \\
&= 16{,}50{,}000 \times 2 = \text{₹}33{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: On-Premise Salvage Value**

$$
\text{Salvage} = 0.20 \times \text{CapEx} = 0.20 \times 24{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}4{,}80{,}000
$$

**Step 4: On-Premise TCO**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCO}_{\text{on-prem}} &= \text{CapEx} + \text{OpEx}_{24} + C_{\text{lic}} - \text{Salvage} \\
&= 24{,}00{,}000 + 33{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}80{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹}56{,}20{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: IaaS VM-Hours**

$$
\text{VM-hours} = N_{\text{vm}} \cdot H_{\text{monthly}} \cdot T = 5 \cdot 730 \cdot 24 = 87{,}600 \text{ VM-hours}
$$

**Step 6: IaaS Compute Cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Compute Cost} &= \text{VM-hours} \cdot p_{\text{vm-hr}} \\
&= 87{,}600 \times 8 = \text{₹}7{,}00{,}800
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: IaaS Storage Cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Storage Cost} &= S \cdot N_{\text{vm}} \cdot p_{\text{storage}} \cdot T \\
&= 200 \cdot 5 \cdot 5 \cdot 24 = \text{₹}1{,}20{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8: IaaS TCO**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCO}_{\text{iaas}} &= \text{Compute Cost} + \text{Storage Cost} \\
&= 7{,}00{,}800 + 1{,}20{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹}8{,}20{,}800
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9: Savings Analysis**

$$
\Delta \text{TCO} = \text{TCO}_{\text{on-prem}} - \text{TCO}_{\text{iaas}} = 56{,}20{,}000 - 8{,}20{,}800 = \text{₹}47{,}99{,}200
$$

$$
\text{Savings \%} = \frac{\Delta \text{TCO}}{\text{TCO}_{\text{on-prem}}} \times 100 = \frac{47{,}99{,}200}{56{,}20{,}000} \times 100 \approx 85.39\%
$$

> **Conclusion:** IaaS provides approximately **85.4% cost savings** over 24 months, primarily by eliminating CapEx and admin overhead. (Note: This figure is illustrative — real-world savings depend on workload volatility, reserved-instance discounts, and egress traffic.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in PECMT632 Module 4 Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the cut-points.** Examiners expect students to **explicitly state** where the IaaS / PaaS / SaaS boundary sits in the stack. Saying *"IaaS gives more control"* without naming layers loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Conflating PaaS with SaaS.** Many students wrongly describe PaaS as "ready-to-use software." PaaS provides a **platform for developers to deploy code**, not end-user software. This is a **3-mark deduction** if confused in Part A.
> 3. **Ignoring vendor lock-in as a trade-off.** Portability / lock-in is a frequent 2-mark sub-part. Always mention **VM image portability** for IaaS, **API lock-in** for PaaS, **proprietary data formats** for SaaS.
> 4. **Omitting the shared-responsibility language.** Examiners award bonus marks for using the phrase *"Security of the Cloud"* (provider) vs *"Security in the Cloud"* (consumer).
> 5. **Arithmetic slip in TCO problems.** Always show **every substitution step**. Examiners *do* deduct for missing intermediate arithmetic even when the final answer is correct.
> 6. **Not providing a real-world provider example.** For every service model, name at least one provider (AWS, Azure, GCP, Heroku, Salesforce, etc.). Missing examples = 1-mark loss.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read 5 minutes before the exam):**

- 🔑 **NIST SP 800-145** is the **canonical reference** for service model definitions — quote its language where possible.
- 🔑 **Three service models:** IaaS, PaaS, SaaS — each is a **horizontal cut through the seven-layer IT stack**.
- 🔑 **IaaS cut** sits between Virtualization and OS — consumer controls OS and above.
- 🔑 **PaaS cut** sits between Runtime and Data — consumer controls App + Data only.
- 🔑 **SaaS cut** sits above the Application — consumer only configures thin user settings.
- 🔑 **Shared responsibility** = provider owns Security *of* the Cloud; consumer owns Security *in* the Cloud.
- 🔑 **Provider always controls** Physical Plant, Servers, Network, Storage, Virtualization, Facility.
- 🔑 **Consumer always controls** their Data and IAM/Access Policies in every service model.
- 🔑 **TCO formula:** $\text{TCO} = \text{CapEx} + \text{OpEx} + \text{Migration} + \text{Training} - \text{Salvage}$.
- 🔑 **Availability nines:** $99.9\% = 8.77$ hr/year; $99.99\% = 52.6$ min/year; $99.999\% = 5.26$ min/year.
- 🔑 **Pizza analogy:** IaaS = semi-prepared kit; PaaS = take-and-bake; SaaS = delivered cooked meal.
- 🔑 **Real-world mapping:** IaaS → AWS EC2 / Azure VM / GCE; PaaS → Heroku / App Engine / Beanstalk; SaaS → M365 / Salesforce / Gmail.
- 🔑 **Trade-off triangle:** Control ↔ Agility ↔ Operational Burden — you can optimize any two but not all three simultaneously.
- 🔑 **Always end any 14-mark answer** with a one-line *Conclusion / Justification* — examiners explicitly check for it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microservices: Introduction, Advantages and Disadvantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Microservices: Introduction, Advantages and Disadvantages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Microservices** — also called the *microservice architecture* — is an **architectural style** that structures an application as a collection of small, autonomous, loosely coupled, and independently deployable services. Each service is:

- **Self-contained** (owns its own logic, data, and runtime)
- **Organized around a bounded business capability**
- **Independently deployable** over a well-defined, lightweight communication mechanism (commonly **HTTP/REST** or messaging)
- **Technology-agnostic** (polyglot — each service may use a different language, framework, or data store)

Formally stated, a microservice is a service that satisfies the constraints of **Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)** at the architectural level: one service, one bounded context, one deployable unit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECMT632 – Module 4):**
> The term *micro* refers to the **scope of the service**, not the size of the codebase. A microservice can range from a few hundred lines to several thousand, but its defining trait is **narrow functional responsibility**, not line count.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The City vs. The Skyscraper"

Imagine two ways to build a city:

- **Monolithic Application = A Single Skyscraper**  
  One giant building houses the post office, the hospital, the bank, the school, and the cinema. If the plumbing fails on floor 12, the entire building is shut down. Renovating the bank means closing the whole tower. Adding a new department means structural changes to the whole edifice.

- **Microservices = A City of Specialized Buildings**  
  Each function (post office, bank, hospital) is its **own building** with its own infrastructure, staff, and entrance. The post office can renovate without disturbing the bank. The bank can use a different building material (architecture) from the hospital. A power outage in one building does not affect the others. However, they must coordinate — the post office delivers mail to the bank daily via **roads (the network)**.

This analogy captures the essence: **distributed autonomy with structured inter-service communication**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Architectural Terms (Must Memorize for KTU):**
> - **Service** – A standalone, deployable software unit fulfilling one business capability.
> - **Bounded Context** – A boundary within which a particular model is valid (borrowed from Domain-Driven Design by Eric Evans).
> - **Polyglot** – The freedom to use multiple programming languages/frameworks across services.
> - **Loose Coupling** – Services are independent; a change in one does not ripple to others.
> - **High Cohesion** – All functions inside one service are strongly related to one capability.

## 1.3 Evolution of Microservices

Microservices did not appear in a vacuum. They are the convergence of:

1. **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)** — coarse-grained services with an Enterprise Service Bus (ESB).
2. **Cloud Computing & Virtualization** — cheap, on-demand infrastructure.
3. **DevOps & Continuous Delivery** — automated pipelines enabling frequent, independent deployments.
4. **Domain-Driven Design (DDD)** — Eric Evans' concept of bounded contexts guiding service boundaries.
5. **Containerization (Docker, Kubernetes)** — lightweight, portable runtime environments.

The term itself was popularized around **2011–2014**, with workshops near Venice, Italy, and a landmark blog post by **James Lewis and Martin Fowler (2014)** which formalized the pattern.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Core Characteristics of Microservices (The Lewis–Fowler Nine)

James Lewis and Martin Fowler identified **nine defining characteristics**. For KTU, the top six are high-yield:

| # | Characteristic | Meaning | Engineering Implication |
|---|----------------|---------|-------------------------|
| 1 | **Componentization via Services** | Replacement of components by services (independent processes) | Each service runs in its own process; OS-level isolation |
| 2 | **Organized Around Business Capability** | Cross-functional teams owning end-to-end capability | Conway's Law applied — team structure mirrors architecture |
| 3 | **Products, Not Projects** | "You build it, you run it" mindset | DevOps culture; developers responsible in production |
| 4 | **Smart Endpoints, Dumb Pipes** | Logic stays in services; communication is simple (HTTP/messaging) | Avoid heavy ESBs; use lightweight REST/gRPC |
| 5 | **Decentralized Governance** | Each team picks its own tech stack | Polyglot programming; freedom with discipline |
| 6 | **Decentralized Data Management** | Each service owns its own database | No shared database; eventual consistency |
| 7 | Infrastructure Automation | CI/CD pipelines, automated testing | Continuous deployment essential |
| 8 | Design for Failure | Services assume the network is unreliable | Circuit breakers, retries, fallbacks |
| 9 | Evolutionary Design | System grows and decays gracefully | Replace services, not rewrite the monolith |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Questions on microservices frequently ask about characteristics OR about why microservices emerged from SOA. Memorize at least **six characteristics** with one-line meanings.

## 2.2 Monolithic vs. Microservices — Comparative Table

| Attribute | Monolithic Architecture | Microservice Architecture |
|-----------|------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Deployment Unit** | Single deployable artifact (WAR/EAR/JAR) | Many independent services |
| **Scaling** | Scale the whole application (vertical or clone) | Scale only the bottleneck service (horizontal, fine-grained) |
| **Technology Stack** | One language, one framework, one DB | Polyglot — different stack per service |
| **Fault Isolation** | A bug may crash the whole system | Failure in one service may degrade, not kill, the system |
| **Team Structure** | Large central team | Small cross-functional teams (Conway's Law) |
| **Data Management** | Single shared relational DB | Database-per-service; eventual consistency |
| **Complexity** | Low initial; grows exponentially | High initial (network, ops); grows linearly |
| **Time to Market** | Slows as codebase grows | Faster once infrastructure is in place |
| **Communication** | In-process function calls | Network calls (HTTP/gRPC/messaging) — higher latency |
| **Testing** | Easier end-to-end in one process | Complex — needs contract & integration tests |
| **Best Suited For** | Small teams, simple domains, early-stage startups | Large teams, complex domains, high-traffic systems |

## 2.3 When to Use Microservices — Decision Logic

Use the following heuristic chain:

1. Is the team size > **10–15 developers**?  
2. Are there **distinct bounded contexts** in the domain?  
3. Is **independent deployability** a business priority?  
4. Are different parts of the system under **very different load** profiles?  
5. Is the organization mature in **DevOps, CI/CD, observability**?

If **≥ 3 of 5** are "Yes" → Microservices are justified.  
If **≤ 2 of 5** are "Yes" → Stick with a **modular monolith** first.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Companies like **Netflix** (700+ microservices), **Amazon** (moved from a monolith called "Obidos" in 2001–2002), **Uber**, **Spotify**, and **eBay** famously migrated to microservices to handle extreme scale and team parallelism.

## 2.4 Communication Patterns in Microservices

Microservices do not call each other through shared memory; they use **network-based** communication. Two principal styles:

### A. Synchronous Communication
- **REST over HTTP/JSON** — most common; stateless; human-readable.
- **gRPC** — high-performance binary protocol using Protocol Buffers; ideal for internal service-to-service calls.
- **GraphQL** — used when the consumer needs flexible querying (often as an API gateway aggregator).

### B. Asynchronous Communication
- **Message Queues** — RabbitMQ, ActiveMQ (point-to-point).
- **Publish/Subscribe (Pub-Sub)** — Kafka, Amazon SNS, Google Pub/Sub (event-driven).
- **Event Sourcing** — services publish *events* when state changes; interested services react.

| Style | Latency | Coupling | Best Use |
|-------|---------|----------|----------|
| REST/HTTP | Medium | Tight (caller knows API) | CRUD-style operations |
| gRPC | Low | Tight | High-throughput internal RPC |
| Message Queue | High | Loose (fire-and-forget) | Decoupling, retries, spikes |
| Pub/Sub (Kafka) | Variable | Very loose | Event-driven, real-time streams |

## 2.5 Key Engineering Metrics (For KTU Numerical/Conceptual)

While microservices are qualitative by nature, KTU may expect you to reason about trade-offs numerically:

- **Network latency overhead per hop:** typically **1–10 ms** within a data center.
- **Service granularity sweet spot:** **50–200 services** is a healthy range for a mid-sized enterprise.
- **Failure blast radius:** in a monolith, **100%**; in well-designed microservices, **5–10%** for any single fault.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Comparative Matrix

## 3.1 Logical Deconstruction — How a Monolith Becomes Microservices

Let us trace the journey of an e-commerce application from a single deployment unit to a microservice ecosystem. This is a common KTU descriptive question.

### Step 1 — Identify the Monolith
The application contains: **User Management, Product Catalog, Order Processing, Payment, Inventory, Notifications, Reviews** — all packaged into one WAR/EAR file deployed on a single Tomcat server with one MySQL database.

### Step 2 — Apply Domain-Driven Design (DDD)
Identify **bounded contexts** by examining the ubiquitous language of each subdomain:

- *User Management* — entities: User, Address, Role
- *Product Catalog* — entities: Product, Category, Brand
- *Order Processing* — entities: Order, OrderItem, Cart
- *Payment* — entities: Transaction, Refund
- *Inventory* — entities: StockItem, Warehouse
- *Notifications* — entities: Email, SMS
- *Reviews* — entities: Review, Rating

Each context becomes a **candidate microservice**.

### Step 3 — Define Service Boundaries
Apply the **Single Responsibility Principle** at the architectural level. For instance, *Inventory* and *Product Catalog* are separate because a product's existence is independent of stock levels (different rates of change, different teams).

### Step 4 — Choose Communication Style
- **Synchronous (REST)** for request-response: `Order Service` calls `Payment Service` to charge a card.
- **Asynchronous (Kafka)** for events: `Order Service` publishes `OrderPlaced` event; `Inventory Service` and `Notification Service` consume it independently.

### Step 5 — Decentralize Data
Each service gets its own database. Cross-service data access is done via **APIs**, never via direct DB joins. A product's reviews are not stored in the product DB — the *Reviews Service* exposes a REST endpoint `/products/{id}/reviews`.

### Step 6 — Implement Cross-Cutting Concerns
- **API Gateway** (e.g., Kong, AWS API Gateway) — single entry point for clients; routes to services.
- **Service Discovery** (e.g., Consul, Eureka) — services find each other dynamically.
- **Circuit Breaker** (e.g., Hystrix, Resilience4j) — prevents cascading failures.
- **Centralized Logging & Monitoring** (ELK Stack, Prometheus, Grafana).
- **Distributed Tracing** (Jaeger, Zipkin).

### Step 7 — Deploy and Operate
Containerize with **Docker**, orchestrate with **Kubernetes**, automate with **CI/CD pipelines** (Jenkins, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI).

## 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Advantages vs. Disadvantages

This is the **core of the KTU syllabus topic** and almost always appears in exams.

### ✅ ADVANTAGES OF MICROSERVICES

| # | Advantage | Explanation | Engineering Benefit |
|---|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Independent Deployability** | Each service can be deployed without coordinating with others | Faster release cycles; reduced risk per deployment |
| 2 | **Technology Heterogeneity (Polyglot)** | Each service picks the best tool for its job | Use Python for ML, Java for transactions, Go for high-throughput APIs |
| 3 | **Scalability (Granular)** | Scale only the hot service, not the whole system | Cost-efficient — pay for what you need |
| 4 | **Fault Isolation** | A failure in one service does not crash others | Improved resilience and uptime (SLA compliance) |
| 5 | **Team Autonomy** | Small teams own services end-to-end (Conway's Law) | Higher velocity, fewer cross-team dependencies |
| 6 | **Reusability** | Services can be reused across multiple products | e.g., *Payment Service* reused by web, mobile, partner apps |
| 7 | **Replaceability** | A service can be rewritten or replaced independently | Enables technology evolution without big-bang rewrites |
| 8 | **Organizational Alignment** | Matches modern large-team structures (Spotify model) | Reduces communication overhead |

### ❌ DISADVANTAGES OF MICROSERVICES

| # | Disadvantage | Explanation | Engineering Cost |
|---|--------------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Distributed System Complexity** | Network calls, partial failures, latency | Requires expertise in distributed systems |
| 2 | **Data Consistency Challenges** | No shared DB → cannot use ACID transactions across services | Must use **Saga pattern**, **eventual consistency**, **2PC** |
| 3 | **Network Overhead & Latency** | Each inter-service call traverses the network | Performance penalty vs. in-process calls |
| 4 | **Operational Overhead** | Hundreds of services to monitor, log, deploy | Needs mature DevOps, observability stack |
| 5 | **Testing Complexity** | End-to-end testing across many services is hard | Requires contract testing (Pact), service virtualization |
| 6 | **Security Surface** | More endpoints = more attack vectors | Needs OAuth2, JWT, mTLS, API gateway hardening |
| 7 | **Initial Cost & Learning Curve** | High upfront investment in infrastructure | Not suitable for small teams or simple apps |
| 8 | **Versioning & API Evolution** | Multiple versions of services must coexist | Needs API versioning strategy, deprecation policies |
| 9 | **Debugging Difficulty** | A single user request may touch 10+ services | Requires distributed tracing and correlation IDs |

## 3.3 Mathematical Intuition — Why "Micro" Is Not Always Better

Consider a system with $N$ services. The **number of inter-service communication links** in a fully connected mesh is:

$$
L = \frac{N(N-1)}{2}
$$

This is derived from combinatorics: the number of ways to choose **2** services from **N** to form a directed pair.

- For **N = 5** services: $L = \frac{5 \times 4}{2} = 10$ links
- For **N = 50** services: $L = \frac{50 \times 49}{2} = 1225$ links
- For **N = 200** services: $L = \frac{200 \times 199}{2} = 19900$ links

This quadratic growth illustrates the **operational explosion** that microservices bring. It is why blindly splitting a system into hundreds of "micro" services is an anti-pattern. The art lies in choosing the **right granularity** — coarse enough to minimize links, fine enough to satisfy bounded contexts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb (Two-Pizza Rule, Amazon):** Each team should be small enough to be fed by two pizzas (~6–8 people). Each team should own 1–3 services. This keeps $N$ manageable and $L$ tractable.

## 3.4 Derivation — The CAP Trade-off in Microservices

In a distributed microservices environment, the **CAP Theorem** (Eric Brewer, 2000) states that you can only simultaneously guarantee **two of three** properties:

$$
C \land A \land P = \text{Impossible}
$$

Where:
- $C$ = **Consistency** (all nodes see the same data at the same time)
- $A$ = **Availability** (every request receives a response, even if some nodes fail)
- $P$ = **Partition tolerance** (system continues despite network failures)

Microservices, by virtue of being distributed, **must** tolerate network partitions. Hence the practical choice is between:

- **AP systems** (e.g., Cassandra, DynamoDB) — high availability, eventual consistency
- **CP systems** (e.g., HBase, ZooKeeper) — strong consistency, may sacrifice availability

This single equation explains why **eventual consistency** is the de-facto standard in microservice data management, not ACID transactions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Monolithic vs. Microservices — Architectural Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MONO["MONOLITHIC ARCHITECTURE"]
        direction TB
        C1[Client Browser] --> LB1[Load Balancer]
        LB1 --> APP1[Single Application Server]
        APP1 --> UI1[UI Module]
        APP1 --> BUS1[Business Logic Module]
        APP1 --> DATA1[Data Access Module]
        UI1 --> DB1[(Single Shared Database)]
        BUS1 --> DB1
        DATA1 --> DB1
    end

    style MONO fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:2px
    style APP1 fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#7f1d1d
    style DB1 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MICRO["MICROSERVICE ARCHITECTURE"]
        direction TB
        C2[Client / Mobile App] --> GW[API Gateway]
        GW --> US[User Service]
        GW --> PS[Product Service]
        GW --> OS[Order Service]
        GW --> PYS[Payment Service]
        OS -. publishes events .-> KAF[(Kafka Event Bus)]
        PYS -. publishes events .-> KAF
        KAF --> IS[Inventory Service]
        KAF --> NS[Notification Service]
        US --> DBU[(User DB)]
        PS --> DBP[(Product DB)]
        OS --> DBO[(Order DB)]
        PYS --> DBY[(Payment DB)]
        IS --> DBI[(Inventory DB)]
        NS --> DBN[(Notification DB)]
    end

    style MICRO fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:2px
    style GW fill:#93c5fd,stroke:#1e3a8a
    style KAF fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#92400e
```

## 4.2 Service Decomposition Workflow (Step-by-Step)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Monolithic Application] --> B[Identify Bounded Contexts using DDD]
    B --> C[Define Service Boundaries]
    C --> D[Choose Communication Style REST or Messaging]
    D --> E[Decentralize Data per Service]
    E --> F[Add Cross Cutting Concerns Gateway, Discovery, Tracing]
    F --> G[Containerize with Docker]
    G --> H[Orchestrate with Kubernetes]
    H --> I[Operate with CI CD and Observability]

    style A fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d
    style I fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#14532d
```

## 4.3 Inter-Service Communication Patterns

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SYNC["SYNCHRONOUS"]
        direction LR
        A1[Order Service] -->|HTTP REST| B1[Payment Service]
        A2[Order Service] -->|gRPC| B2[Inventory Service]
    end

    subgraph ASYNC["ASYNCHRONOUS"]
        direction LR
        C1[Order Service] -->|publish OrderPlaced| MQ[(Kafka Topic)]
        MQ --> D1[Inventory Service consumes]
        MQ --> D2[Email Service consumes]
        MQ --> D3[Analytics Service consumes]
    end

    style SYNC fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3
    style ASYNC fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#78350f
    style MQ fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#78350f
```

## 4.4 Request Flow — How a User Order Travels

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Client
    participant GW as API Gateway
    participant OS as Order Service
    participant PYS as Payment Service
    participant IS as Inventory Service
    participant NS as Notification Service
    participant K as Kafka Event Bus

    U->>GW: POST /api/orders {productId, qty}
    GW->>OS: Forward request
    OS->>PYS: POST /payments/charge
    PYS-->>OS: 200 OK transactionId
    OS->>IS: PATCH /inventory/reserve
    IS-->>OS: 200 OK reserved
    OS->>K: publish OrderPlaced event
    K->>NS: consume OrderPlaced
    NS-->>U: Email confirmation
    OS-->>GW: 201 Created orderId
    GW-->>U: 201 Created orderId
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU:** In descriptive answers, always include a **labelled block diagram** comparing monolithic vs. microservices. Examiners reward visual clarity with 1–2 bonus marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PECMT632]**
**Define microservices. List any four characteristics of microservice architecture.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Microservices, or microservice architecture, is an architectural style that structures a single application as a suite of small, independently deployable services, each organized around a specific business capability and communicating over lightweight mechanisms like HTTP/REST or messaging.

**Four key characteristics:**

1. **Componentization via Services** — application is broken into independent replaceable service units.
2. **Organized Around Business Capability** — services align with bounded business domains, not technical layers.
3. **Decentralized Data Management** — each service owns its private database; no shared schema.
4. **Smart Endpoints and Dumb Pipes** — logic resides in services; communication channels are simple (no heavy ESB).

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for characteristics = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PECMT632]**
**Differentiate between monolithic and microservice architectures in terms of deployment, scalability, and fault tolerance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Monolithic | Microservice |
|--------|------------|--------------|
| **Deployment** | Single deployable unit; one failure affects all (1 mark) | Independent deployment per service; isolated releases (1 mark) |
| **Scalability** | Whole application scaled together — wasteful | Individual services scaled on demand — efficient (½ mark) |
| **Fault Tolerance** | One module's crash may take down the entire app | Failure isolated to a single service; system degrades gracefully (½ mark) |

---

## 📕 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — KTU Model Question**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **advantages of microservice architecture** over monolithic architecture. Support your answer with a labelled block diagram.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **disadvantages of microservices** and explain why they are not suitable for small applications.

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — Advantages

Microservice architecture offers significant benefits over the traditional monolithic model:

1. **Independent Deployability (1 mark):** Each service can be deployed independently without coordinating with other teams. This drastically reduces release-cycle time. For example, Netflix deploys thousands of times per day.

2. **Technology Heterogeneity / Polyglot Freedom (1 mark):** Different services can be written in different languages and use different databases. A machine-learning service may use Python + TensorFlow, while a transactional billing service may use Java + PostgreSQL.

3. **Granular Scalability (1 mark):** Only the heavily loaded service needs to be scaled. In a monolith, the entire application must be replicated. This translates directly into cloud cost savings.

4. **Fault Isolation (1 mark):** A memory leak in the *Reviews Service* will not crash the *Payment Service*. The blast radius is contained.

5. **Team Autonomy and Productivity (1 mark):** Small cross-functional teams (5–8 members) own a service end-to-end — from code to production. This aligns with Amazon's "two-pizza team" principle and boosts velocity.

6. **Reusability and Replaceability (1 mark):** Services like *Authentication* or *Notification* can be reused across multiple products. Obsolete services can be rewritten in isolation without disturbing the rest of the system.

7. **Evolutionary Design (1 mark):** The system can evolve incrementally; new services can be added, old ones retired, supporting long-term maintainability.

**Labelled Block Diagram (included in answer):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C[Client] --> G[API Gateway]
    G --> S1[User Service]
    G --> S2[Product Service]
    G --> S3[Order Service]
    G --> S4[Payment Service]
    S3 -->|Async Event| BUS[(Kafka Bus)]
    BUS --> S5[Inventory Service]
    BUS --> S6[Notification Service]
    S1 --> D1[(User DB)]
    S2 --> D2[(Product DB)]
    S3 --> D3[(Order DB)]
    S4 --> D4[(Payment DB)]

    style C fill:#bbf7d0
    style G fill:#93c5fd
    style BUS fill:#fbbf24
```

*(7 marks total: 6 × 1 mark for points + 1 mark for diagram)*

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — Disadvantages & Unsuitability for Small Apps

Despite its strengths, microservice architecture introduces serious challenges:

1. **Distributed System Complexity (1 mark):** Developers must reason about network failures, partial responses, retries, idempotency, and message ordering — concepts absent in a monolith.

2. **Data Consistency is Hard (1 mark):** Since each service owns its database, cross-service transactions (e.g., debit account + credit account) cannot use ACID. Developers must use the **Saga pattern** or accept **eventual consistency**, which complicates business logic.

3. **Network Latency and Bandwidth (1 mark):** Every inter-service call adds 1–10 ms of network latency. A 10-service deep call chain can become unacceptably slow.

4. **Operational Overhead (1 mark):** Hundreds of services mean hundreds of CI/CD pipelines, log streams, and dashboards. This demands mature DevOps, container orchestration (Kubernetes), and observability tooling.

5. **Testing Complexity (1 mark):** End-to-end testing becomes brittle as the number of service interactions grows. Requires **contract testing** (Pact), service virtualization, and test containers.

6. **Security Surface Expansion (1 mark):** More network endpoints mean more attack vectors. Requires OAuth2, JWT tokens, mutual TLS, and hardened API gateways.

7. **Not Suitable for Small Applications (1 mark):** For a team of 3–5 developers building a simple CRUD app, the operational cost of microservices (Kubernetes, service mesh, distributed tracing) far outweighs the benefits. A **modular monolith** is more appropriate — same internal structure, single deployment, simpler operations. Microservices are a solution to *organizational and scale* problems, not technical elegance for its own sake.

*(7 marks total: 6 × 1 mark for disadvantages + 1 mark for small-app justification)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare monolithic and microservice architectures. Highlight **at least six points of comparison** in a tabular format.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **nine characteristics of microservices** as identified by James Lewis and Martin Fowler. Why is "Smart Endpoints and Dumb Pipes" considered a defining trait?

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — Comparative Table

| # | Attribute | Monolithic | Microservice |
|---|-----------|------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Deployment Unit** | One single artifact (1) | Many independent units (1) |
| 2 | **Scalability** | Whole-app scaling (½) | Per-service fine-grained scaling (½) |
| 3 | **Technology Stack** | Single stack (½) | Polyglot (½) |
| 4 | **Data Storage** | Shared central database (½) | Database-per-service (½) |
| 5 | **Fault Tolerance** | Low — single point of failure (½) | High — failure isolated (½) |
| 6 | **Team Structure** | Large central team (½) | Small autonomous cross-functional teams (½) |
| 7 | **Communication** | In-process function calls (½) | Network-based (HTTP/gRPC/messaging) (½) |
| 8 | **Time to Market** | Slows as codebase grows (½) | Faster once infra is mature (½) |
| 9 | **Best Suited For** | Small teams, simple domains (½) | Large teams, complex domains (½) |

*(7 marks: 6 full comparisons × 1 mark + 1 bonus comparison = 7 marks)*

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — The Nine Characteristics

**James Lewis and Martin Fowler (2014)** identified the following characteristics:

1. **Componentization via Services (1 mark):** Software is decomposed into replaceable service units that run in independent processes — a stronger form of modularity than libraries.

2. **Organized Around Business Capability (1 mark):** Service boundaries follow the domain's bounded contexts, not technical layers like UI or DAO. Teams are cross-functional.

3. **Products, Not Projects (1 mark):** A team treats the service as a product with a lifecycle, not a project with an end date. "You build it, you run it" — DevOps culture.

4. **Smart Endpoints and Dumb Pipes (1 mark):** Business logic and intelligence live inside the services. Communication infrastructure (the "pipes") is kept simple — basic HTTP or a lightweight message bus. No heavy orchestration engines.

5. **Decentralized Governance (1 mark):** Each team decides its own technology stack, standards, and tooling. There is no central architecture police.

6. **Decentralized Data Storage (1 mark):** Each service manages its own data. Global transactions are replaced by eventual consistency.

7. **Infrastructure Automation (1 mark):** Heavy use of CI/CD, automated testing, and deployment pipelines. Cloud-native infrastructure.

8. **Design for Failure (½ mark):** The system is built assuming any service can fail. Patterns like circuit breakers, retries, timeouts, and bulkheads are mandatory.

9. **Evolutionary Design (½ mark):** Services can be replaced, retired, or rewritten independently. The architecture supports incremental change.

**Why "Smart Endpoints, Dumb Pipes" is a defining trait (additional ½ mark):**
It is a direct reaction against the **heavyweight ESB (Enterprise Service Bus)** of SOA, which embedded routing, transformation, and business rules in the middleware. In microservices, the bus does only one job — deliver messages — while all decisions stay in the service endpoints. This keeps services decoupled, testable, and language-agnostic.

*(7 marks: 6½ for characteristics + ½ for explanation of "smart endpoints")*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Read This!**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse SOA with Microservices.** SOA uses an **ESB** for orchestration; microservices use **dumb pipes** (HTTP/messaging). Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Avoid vague answers like "microservices are faster"** — KTU expects quantified reasoning: faster *deployment*, faster *scaling of a specific service*, faster *team velocity*.
> 3. **Always draw a labelled block diagram** in advantages/disadvantages questions. Examiners allocate 1–2 marks for diagrams. Even a hand-drawn one earns credit.
> 4. **In "Disadvantages" questions, mention distributed-system concepts** (CAP, eventual consistency, Saga) — KTU rewards conceptual depth.
> 5. **Spell "monolithic" correctly.** A common spelling slip ("monolythic") signals carelessness and may lead to mark deductions in strict valuation.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Microservices is an **architectural style**, not a technology or framework.
- ✅ Core mantra: **"One service, one bounded context, one deployable unit."**
- ✅ Inspired by **SOA, DDD, Cloud, DevOps, and Containers** — convergence of these forces.
- ✅ **Lewis & Fowler (2014)** formalized the nine characteristics; memorize at least six.
- ✅ Communication is either **synchronous (REST/gRPC)** or **asynchronous (Kafka/messaging)**.
- ✅ Data is **decentralized** — no shared database, no global ACID, use **Saga** for distributed transactions.
- ✅ **CAP theorem** forces eventual consistency in microservice data layers.
- ✅ **Smart Endpoints, Dumb Pipes** is the deliberate rejection of ESB-style orchestration.
- ✅ **Granular scaling** is the #1 cost advantage; **fault isolation** is the #1 resilience advantage.
- ✅ **Operational complexity, network latency, and testing difficulty** are the top 3 disadvantages.
- ✅ For **N services**, the number of communication links grows as $\frac{N(N-1)}{2}$ — quadratic complexity, hence the "right-sizing" rule.
- ✅ **Two-Pizza Team Rule** — 6–8 developers per service owner.
- ✅ Microservices are **NOT** for small teams or simple apps — start with a **modular monolith**, extract services when justified.
- ✅ Mandatory infrastructure: **API Gateway, Service Discovery, Circuit Breaker, Distributed Tracing, Centralized Logging, CI/CD, Containers (Docker), Orchestration (Kubernetes)**.
- ✅ **Conway's Law** applies — your team structure *will* shape your architecture.
- ✅ Real-world adopters: **Netflix, Amazon, Uber, Spotify, eBay** — use them as examples in KTU answers to demonstrate industry awareness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interprocess Communication - Types of Interactions, Protocol, Standard and Message Format, Discovery Service, API Gateway, Service Registry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interprocess Communication - Types of Interactions, Protocol, Standard and Message Format, Discovery Service, API Gateway, Service Registry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Interprocess Communication (IPC) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Interprocess Communication (IPC)** is a set of standardized mechanisms that allow independently executing software processes — potentially running on different physical or virtual machines — to exchange data, coordinate execution, and invoke each other's functionality through well-defined protocols, message formats, and service-discovery mechanisms.

In the context of **distributed systems, microservices, and service-oriented architectures (SOA)**, IPC extends beyond a single host's memory and includes network-level interactions governed by protocols such as **HTTP/HTTPS, gRPC, AMQP, MQTT, and WebSockets**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine two chefs in **separate kitchens** preparing a single dish:
- They cannot physically reach into the same pot (no shared memory).
- They communicate by passing **written tickets** through a **waiter** (the *network*).
- The ticket has a **fixed format** (item, table number, special instructions) — this is the **message format/protocol**.
- A **head maître d'** keeps a directory of which chef specializes in which cuisine — this is the **Service Registry**.
- When a new dish arrives, the waiter checks the maître d' to find the right chef — this is **Service Discovery**.
- The maître d' also handles problems (chef unavailable, wrong order) and routes the ticket to the **expeditor** — this is the **API Gateway**.

That is precisely what IPC achieves in modern computing systems.

## 1.2 Types of Interactions in IPC

There are four fundamental patterns of interprocess interaction. KTU examiners frequently test the **distinction between request-reply, one-way, and publish-subscribe** models.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Four Canonical IPC Interaction Types (per Tanenbaum & Distributed Systems Syllabus):**
> 1. **Request–Reply (Synchronous RPC-style)** — Client blocks until server responds.
> 2. **One-Way / Fire-and-Forget (Asynchronous)** — Sender continues without waiting.
> 3. **Publish–Subscribe (Pub/Sub)** — Decoupled many-to-many via a *broker*.
> 4. **Streaming / Pipe-based** — Continuous flow of data (used in pipelines and message queues).

### Quick Comparison Snapshot

| Interaction Type | Coupling | Latency | Example Protocol |
|---|---|---|---|
