| Request–Reply | Tight (sync) | Higher | HTTP/REST, gRPC |
| One-Way | Loose (async) | Low | UDP, AMQP fire-and-forget |
| Publish–Subscribe | Very Loose | Variable | MQTT, Kafka, AMQP Topics |
| Streaming | Sequential | Continuous | WebSockets, gRPC streams |

## 1.3 Protocol, Standard & Message Format

- **Protocol** = the *rules of conversation* (syntax, sequencing, error handling, retransmission).
- **Standard** = the *documented, agreed-upon specification* (e.g., RFC 7231 for HTTP/1.1).
- **Message Format** = the *structure of the actual bytes* exchanged (e.g., JSON, XML, Protocol Buffers, Avro).

> [!NOTE]
> A **protocol without a standard** is a proprietary invention. A **standard without a message format** is a hollow agreement. All three are required for interoperable IPC.

## 1.4 Discovery Service, API Gateway & Service Registry — At a Glance

- **Service Registry** — A *database* (e.g., Consul, Eureka, Zookeeper) that stores live locations (host, port, health) of service instances.
- **Discovery Service** — The *mechanism* by which a client locates a service, either by **querying the registry (client-side discovery)** or by **going through an intermediary (server-side discovery)**.
- **API Gateway** — A *single entry point* that handles routing, authentication, rate-limiting, protocol translation, and aggregation before forwarding the call to the appropriate microservice.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Taxonomy of IPC Mechanisms

### A. Same-Process / Same-Host IPC
- **Shared Memory** — Fastest; processes map a common memory segment. Requires synchronization (semaphores/mutexes).
- **Pipes (Anonymous & Named / FIFO)** — Unidirectional byte stream.
- **Message Queues** — Discrete messages; OS-managed buffers.
- **Sockets (Unix Domain)** — File-descriptor-based, low latency.
- **Signals** — Lightweight asynchronous notifications.
- **Semaphores & Mutexes** — Synchronization primitives, not pure data exchange.

### B. Cross-Host / Network IPC
- **Sockets (TCP/UDP)** — Foundation of the internet.
- **Remote Procedure Call (RPC)** — Abstraction that makes remote calls look local. **gRPC** (Google) is the modern high-performance successor using **HTTP/2 + Protocol Buffers**.
- **Representational State Transfer (REST)** — Resource-oriented over HTTP, stateless, cacheable.
- **Message-Oriented Middleware (MOM)** — RabbitMQ, ActiveMQ, Kafka.
- **Web Services (SOAP)** — XML-based, WS-* standards.
- **GraphQL** — Query language for flexible client-driven data fetching.

## 2.2 Protocol Stack for Modern IPC

KTU expects students to map a request to the correct layer.

| Layer | Example | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| Transport | TCP, UDP | Reliable byte delivery, port multiplexing |
| Application Protocol | HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, gRPC, MQTT | Request semantics, framing |
| Serialization | JSON, XML, Protobuf, Avro | Encoding structured data into bytes |
| Discovery | DNS, Consul, Eureka | Resolving service names to addresses |
| Routing/Gateway | NGINX, Kong, AWS API Gateway | Cross-cutting concerns |

## 2.3 Message Format Anatomy — A Structural Breakdown

A **well-formed message** in modern IPC has the following layered anatomy:

1. **Transport Header** — Source/Destination port, sequence number, checksums (TCP), or datagram header (UDP).
2. **Protocol Header** — HTTP method, path, version, headers, or gRPC frame headers.
3. **Message Envelope** — Metadata such as **Correlation ID, Message ID, Timestamp, Trace Token**.
4. **Payload (Body)** — The business data encoded in **JSON, XML, Protobuf, or Avro**.
5. **Trailer / Checksum** — Optional integrity verification.

### KTU Reference Table — Message Format Standards

| Standard | Encoding | Schema | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **JSON** | Text | Schema-less / JSON-Schema | REST APIs, web apps |
| **XML** | Text | XSD | SOAP, legacy enterprise |
| **Protocol Buffers** | Binary | .proto schema | gRPC, high-perf |
| **Avro** | Binary | JSON schema | Kafka, big data |
| **Thrift** | Binary | .thrift IDL | Cross-language RPC |
| **MessagePack** | Binary | Schema-less | Compact JSON alternative |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** *Binary formats (Protobuf, Avro) are 3–10× smaller and faster to parse than text formats (JSON, XML)*, but they are not human-readable. This trade-off is a frequent exam question.

## 2.4 Service Registry — In-Depth

A **Service Registry** is the **phone book of microservices**. It maintains:
- **Service Name → Network Location** mappings.
- **Health status** (UP / DOWN / STARTING / OUT_OF_SERVICE).
- **Metadata** (version, zone, weight, tags).

### Properties of a Robust Registry

| Property | Description |
|---|---|
| **Consistency** | All clients see the same view eventually (CAP theorem trade-off). |
| **Availability** | Registry itself must be highly available (Eureka favors AP; Consul favors CP). |
| **Watch / Subscribe** | Clients receive notifications of changes. |
| **Self-Registration vs. Third-Party** | Services register themselves OR a deployer pushes them. |

## 2.5 Discovery Service — Two Patterns

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern 1 — Client-Side Discovery:**
> The *client* queries the registry, selects an available instance, and makes a request directly. The client embeds load-balancing logic.
> *Examples: Netflix Eureka + Ribbon, Consul + client load balancer.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern 2 — Server-Side Discovery:**
> The *client* makes a request to a **router/load balancer** (e.g., AWS ELB, NGINX, Envoy), which queries the registry and forwards the request. Client is simpler.
> *Examples: Kubernetes Service, AWS ALB, Istio.*

### Comparison Table — Client-Side vs. Server-Side Discovery

| Criterion | Client-Side | Server-Side |
|---|---|---|
| Client Complexity | Higher (LB logic) | Lower |
| Language Dependency | Tied to client stack | Independent |
| Registry Load | Many direct queries | Fewer, mediated |
| Failure Handling | Client must retry | Handled by gateway |
| Example | Eureka + Ribbon | K8s Service + kube-proxy |

## 2.6 API Gateway — The Central Nervous System

The **API Gateway** sits between clients and microservices and consolidates **cross-cutting concerns** that would otherwise be duplicated in every service.

### Core Functions of an API Gateway

1. **Request Routing** — Maps URL path → backend service.
2. **Protocol Translation** — REST → gRPC, WebSocket → HTTP.
3. **Authentication & Authorization** — OAuth2, JWT validation.
4. **Rate Limiting & Throttling** — Protects backends from abuse.
5. **Load Balancing** — Round-robin, least-connections, weighted.
6. **Caching** — Reduces backend load.
7. **Request/Response Transformation** — Field filtering, aggregation.
8. **Circuit Breaking** — Fails fast when downstream is unhealthy.
9. **Logging, Monitoring, Tracing** — Distributed observability.
10. **SSL Termination** — Decrypts HTTPS at the edge.

### Popular API Gateway Implementations

| Gateway | Strength |
|---|---|
| **NGINX** | High-performance reverse proxy, Lua scripting |
| **Kong** | Plugin ecosystem, OpenResty-based |
| **AWS API Gateway** | Managed, serverless integration |
| **Spring Cloud Gateway** | Java/Spring-native |
| **Envoy + Istio** | Service mesh, xDS APIs |

## 2.7 Mathematical View — Latency of a Distributed Call

KTU expects familiarity with the cost model of a remote invocation.

Let:
- $T_{\text{net}}$ = network round-trip time (RTT)
- $T_{\text{proc}}$ = processing time at the service
- $N$ = number of service hops (gateway → registry → service → DB)
- $T_{\text{queue}}$ = queueing delay
- $T_{\text{ser}}$ = serialization/deserialization overhead

The end-to-end client-observed latency is given by:

$$T_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\Big(T_{\text{net}_i} + T_{\text{proc}_i} + T_{\text{queue}_i}\Big) + T_{\text{ser}}$$

For a **chained microservice call** (Service A → B → C), $N$ equals the number of internal RPCs. This is why **excessive chaining kills latency** — a frequent KTU short-answer point.

## 2.8 CAP Theorem in Registry Design

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** A Service Registry is a **distributed system itself**. It must choose between **Consistency (C)** and **Availability (A)** under network **Partition (P)** tolerance. Eureka sacrifices C for A; Consul/Zookeeper do the opposite. Examiners love this trade-off question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step: A Complete IPC Request Lifecycle (REST over HTTP via API Gateway with Service Discovery)

The following step-by-step trace is the **canonical KTU 14-mark answer** for "Explain the flow of an IPC request in a microservices architecture."

### Step 1 — Client Initiates Request

The client (mobile app, browser, or another service) constructs an HTTP request.

Example:

```
GET /api/v1/orders/12345
Host: api.shop.example.com
Authorization: Bearer <JWT>
X-Correlation-ID: 7f3a-9c2b
```

**Logic:** The client *does not know* which physical machine hosts the `orders` service. It only knows the gateway's stable URL.

### Step 2 — DNS Resolution

The client resolves `api.shop.example.com` to the gateway's IP address via DNS. This is the **first level of indirection**.

### Step 3 — TLS Termination at the API Gateway

The gateway decrypts HTTPS, validates the JWT, applies rate limits, and assigns a **trace ID** for distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry / Zipkin).

**Logic:** Cross-cutting concerns (auth, throttling) are centralized so each microservice does not re-implement them.

### Step 4 — Path-Based Routing to the Target Microservice

The gateway inspects the path prefix `/api/v1/orders` and consults its **routing table** (often dynamically fed by the service registry).

**Logic:** The gateway must answer: *Which running instance of the `orders` service should I forward this to?*

### Step 5 — Service Discovery Lookup

The gateway queries the **Service Registry** (e.g., Consul):

```
GET /v1/health/service/orders
```

The registry returns a list of healthy instances:

```json
[
  {"Address": "10.0.1.5", "Port": 8080, "Status": "passing"},
  {"Address": "10.0.1.6", "Port": 8080, "Status": "passing"},
  {"Address": "10.0.1.7", "Port": 8080, "Status": "warning"}
]
```

**Logic:** The registry enforces **health checks** (HTTP ping, TCP probe, TTL heartbeats) so only live instances are returned.

### Step 6 — Load-Balancing Decision

The gateway applies a **load-balancing algorithm** to pick one instance. Common algorithms:

- **Round Robin** — Distribute sequentially.
- **Least Connections** — Send to instance with fewest active connections.
- **Weighted Response Time** — Favor faster instances.
- **Consistent Hashing** — Same key → same instance (used for cache locality).

Let the load-balancer select instance `10.0.1.6:8080`.

### Step 7 — Protocol Translation (If Required)

If the internal `orders` service speaks **gRPC** but the client used **REST**, the gateway performs **REST → gRPC translation**, then encodes the JSON payload into **Protocol Buffers**.

**Logic:** This decouples external contract (REST/JSON) from internal contract (gRPC/Protobuf).

### Step 8 — Forwarding the Request

The gateway forwards the rewritten request to `10.0.1.6:8080`. Internally, the orders service may **fan-out** to other services (e.g., `inventory`, `payment`).

### Step 9 — Response Aggregation

If the client request was a **composite query** (e.g., "give me order + items + shipment status"), the gateway calls multiple backends in parallel and **aggregates** the response.

Aggregation latency is bounded by the **slowest** of the parallel calls:

$$T_{\text{agg}} = \max_{i=1}^{k} T_i + T_{\text{ser}}$$

where $k$ is the number of parallel backend calls.

### Step 10 — Response Returned to Client

The gateway translates back (e.g., Protobuf → JSON), applies response caching if applicable, signs/encrypts, and returns to the client.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step: Client-Side Service Discovery Flow

A direct contrast to §3.1 — useful for KTU comparison questions.

1. **Service Startup** — `orders` instance starts, sends `POST /registry/services/orders` with its IP, port, health-check URL.
2. **Heartbeats** — Instance sends heartbeats every 10 s. Registry marks DOWN after 3 missed heartbeats.
3. **Client Bootstraps** — Client loads the registry address (via config or DNS SRV record).
4. **Client Queries Registry** — `GET /registry/services/orders`.
5. **Client Applies Load Balancer Locally** — Selects an instance from the list.
6. **Client Calls Instance Directly** — Bypasses the gateway for service-to-service traffic.
7. **Client Caches Result** — Uses local cache with TTL; subscribes to registry watch for invalidation.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Service Discovery vs. Service Registry vs. API Gateway

| Dimension | Service Registry | Discovery Service | API Gateway |
|---|---|---|---|
| **What it is** | Database of service locations | Mechanism/process of finding services | Single entry point for clients |
| **Stores data?** | Yes (instances, metadata) | No (it queries) | Sometimes (routing rules) |
| **Initiation** | Services register with it | Triggered by client or gateway | Receives all client traffic |
| **Function** | Truth source for availability | Resolution of name → instance | Routing, auth, throttling, aggregation |
| **Couples client to registry?** | Indirectly (via discovery) | Yes (client or gateway) | No (gateway abstracts) |
| **Failure impact** | Stale routing data | Failed lookups | Whole API unavailable |
| **Implementation** | Consul, Eureka, Zookeeper | Eureka client, K8s DNS, Envoy EDS | Kong, NGINX, AWS API GW |
| **CAP behavior** | Varies (AP or CP) | Inherits from registry | Usually AP |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Message Format Construction in Protobuf

A KTU 14-mark question may ask to design a message format for a service. Below is a worked example for a `PlaceOrder` RPC.

**Step 1 — Define the `.proto` schema:**

```protobuf
syntax = "proto3";

message OrderRequest {
  string customer_id = 1;
  repeated LineItem items = 2;
  Address shipping = 3;
  PaymentMethod payment = 4;
}

message LineItem {
  string sku = 5;
  uint32 quantity = 6;
}

message Address { string street = 7; string city = 8; }

message OrderResponse {
  string order_id = 9;
  string status = 10;
  double total_amount = 11;
}

service OrderService {
  rpc PlaceOrder(OrderRequest) returns (OrderResponse);
}
```

**Step 2 — The `protoc` compiler generates** language-specific stubs in Java, Go, Python, etc.

**Step 3 — On the wire**, the message is encoded as a sequence of `<tag, type, value>` triples using **varint encoding** — drastically smaller than equivalent JSON.

**Step 4 — The receiving stub decodes** the bytes back into a typed object in microseconds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU loves this:** The exam tests whether you understand that *the schema is the contract*. Field numbers (`= 1`, `= 2`) must never be reused or reordered — that is the source of "backward-compatible evolution" in Protobuf.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — Latency Calculation

**Problem:** A client calls an API Gateway in 8 ms RTT. The gateway must call 3 backend microservices in parallel (each 5 ms RTT + 3 ms processing). Serialization overhead is 1 ms per call. What is the total client-observed latency?

**Step 1 — Client → Gateway RTT:**

$$T_{c \to g} = 8 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Three parallel backend calls (each 5 ms RTT + 3 ms processing):**

$$T_{\text{backend}} = \max(5 + 3,\ 5 + 3,\ 5 + 3) = 8 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Aggregation overhead:**

$$T_{\text{agg}} = T_{\text{backend}} + T_{\text{ser}} = 8 + 1 = 9 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Gateway → Client return path:**

$$T_{g \to c} = 8 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5 — Total observed latency:**

$$T_{\text{total}} = T_{c \to g} + T_{\text{agg}} + T_{g \to c} = 8 + 9 + 8 = 25 \text{ ms}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The *parallelism keeps the fan-out cost constant*, but the *per-hop RTT is additive* on the path. This is why **co-locating services geographically** (edge computing) is so important.

## 3.6 Decision Heuristics — Which IPC Style to Pick?

KTU frequently asks: *"When would you use gRPC over REST?"*

| Criterion | REST/JSON | gRPC/Protobuf | Message Queue (Kafka) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Browser-friendly | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (needs grpc-web) | ❌ No |
| Strict schema | ❌ Optional | ✅ Required (.proto) | ✅ Schema Registry |
| Streaming | ❌ Limited | ✅ Bi-directional | ✅ Native |
| Latency | Higher | Lower | Lowest (async) |
| Use case | Public APIs, CRUD | Internal microservices | Event-driven, log streaming |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level IPC Architecture (Client → Gateway → Registry → Services)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Client App] -->|HTTPS Request| B[API Gateway]
    B -->|Auth and Rate Limit Check| C[Cross Cutting Filters]
    C -->|Route Lookup| D[Service Registry Consul]
    D -->|Returns Healthy Instances| B
    B -->|Forwards to Selected Instance| E[Orders Service]
    B -->|Forwards Parallel| F[Payment Service]
    B -->|Forwards Parallel| G[Inventory Service]
    E --> H[(Orders DB)]
    F --> I[(Payments DB)]
    G --> J[(Inventory DB)]
    E -->|Response| B
    F -->|Response| B
    G -->|Response| B
    B -->|Aggregated JSON Response| A
```

## 4.2 Service Registration & Heartbeat Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SVC as Orders Service Instance
    participant REG as Service Registry
    participant CLI as Client Application
    participant GW as API Gateway

    SVC->>REG: POST /register {ip, port, healthUrl}
    REG-->>SVC: 200 OK
    loop Every 10 seconds
      SVC->>REG: Heartbeat
    end

    CLI->>GW: GET /api/orders/123
    GW->>REG: GET /services/orders
    REG-->>GW: [ip1, ip2, ip3 healthy]
    GW->>SVC: GET /orders/123
    SVC-->>GW: 200 OK with Order JSON
    GW-->>CLI: 200 OK with Response

    Note over SVC,REG: After 3 missed heartbeats
    REG-->>REG: Mark instance as DOWN
```

## 4.3 Client-Side vs Server-Side Discovery Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SCD["Server-Side Discovery"]
      C1[Client] --> LB1[Load Balancer]
      LB1 --> R1[Service Registry]
      LB1 --> SI1[Service Instance A]
      LB1 --> SI2[Service Instance B]
    end

    subgraph CCD["Client-Side Discovery"]
      C2[Client with LB Logic] --> R2[Service Registry]
      C2 --> SI3[Service Instance X]
      C2 --> SI4[Service Instance Y]
    end
```

## 4.4 Message Format Layered Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Application Layer - HTTP Method and Path] --> L2[Protocol Header - Headers and Cookies]
    L2 --> L3[Message Envelope - CorrelationID and TraceID]
    L3 --> L4[Payload - JSON or Protobuf Body]
    L4 --> L5[Transport Header - TCP or UDP]
    L5 --> L6[Physical Layer - Ethernet or WiFi Bits]
```

## 4.5 API Gateway Internal Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN[Inbound HTTPS Request] --> SSL[SSL Termination]
    SSL --> AUTH[AuthN and AuthZ Module]
    AUTH --> RL[Rate Limiter]
    RL --> ROUTE[Route Resolver]
    ROUTE --> LB[Load Balancer]
    LB --> TRANS[Protocol Translator]
    TRANS --> CACHE[Response Cache]
    CACHE --> OUT[Outbound Call to Microservice]
    OUT --> AGG[Response Aggregator]
    AGG --> LOG[Logging and Tracing]
    LOG --> RESP[Final Response to Client]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]`
**Differentiate between client-side and server-side service discovery. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Client-Side Discovery | Server-Side Discovery |
|---|---|---|
| Who queries registry? | The client itself | The load balancer / router |
| Client complexity | High (must embed LB logic) | Low (just hits a stable URL) |
| Advantage | Direct client-to-service path → fewer hops | Decouples clients from service topology; centralized LB |

*(1 mark for correct definition of each, 1 mark for the distinction, 1 mark for the advantage — examiner allocation.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]`
**What is the role of a Service Registry in a microservices architecture? Name any two popular service registry tools.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A Service Registry is a **centralized, dynamic database** that maintains the live network locations (IP, port) and health status of all running service instances. It enables **service discovery** so that clients and gateways can locate services without hard-coded addresses. (2 marks)
- Popular tools: **Netflix Eureka, Consul, Apache Zookeeper, etcd**. (1 mark for naming two)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **different types of interprocess communication mechanisms** used in distributed systems. Compare **synchronous (RPC)** and **asynchronous (message queue)** communication with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **anatomy of a well-formed IPC message**, including transport header, protocol header, envelope, and payload. Compare **JSON, XML, and Protocol Buffers** as message formats. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — IPC Mechanisms & Sync vs Async Comparison (7 marks):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** IPC mechanisms enable processes — possibly on different hosts — to communicate through shared memory, pipes, sockets, RPC, message queues, and REST/gRPC APIs.

2. **Classification (2 marks):**
   - **Same-host IPC:** Shared memory, pipes, FIFOs, message queues, signals, semaphores.
   - **Cross-host IPC:** TCP/UDP sockets, REST/HTTP, gRPC, SOAP, AMQP, MQTT, WebSockets.

3. **Synchronous RPC (2 marks):**
   - Client **blocks** until the server responds.
   - Tighter coupling; failure is immediately visible.
   - Examples: **gRPC, Java RMI, XML-RPC**.
   - *Use case:* When the client *needs the result* before continuing (e.g., payment authorization).

4. **Asynchronous Message Queue (2 marks):**
   - Sender posts the message to a **broker** and continues; consumer processes later.
   - Loose coupling, higher resilience, supports buffering.
   - Examples: **RabbitMQ, Apache Kafka, Amazon SQS**.
   - *Use case:* Event-driven workflows (e.g., order placed → email service, inventory service, analytics).

**(b) — Message Anatomy & Format Comparison (7 marks):**

1. **Message Layers (3 marks):**
   - **Transport Header** (TCP/UDP) — ports, sequence numbers, checksum.
   - **Protocol Header** (HTTP) — method, URI, version, headers.
   - **Message Envelope** — `Correlation-ID`, `Trace-ID`, `Timestamp`, `Message-Type`.
   - **Payload** — Business data, encoded in JSON/XML/Protobuf.
   - **Trailer** — Optional integrity check.

2. **Format Comparison (4 marks):**

| Property | JSON | XML | Protobuf |
|---|---|---|---|
| Encoding | Text | Text | Binary |
| Size | Medium | Large | Smallest (3-10× smaller) |
| Speed | Moderate | Slow | Fastest |
| Schema | Optional | XSD | Mandatory `.proto` |
| Human-readable | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| Use case | Web APIs | SOAP/Enterprise | gRPC, mobile |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Protobuf is preferred for high-performance internal RPC; JSON for public APIs; XML for legacy interoperability.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of a microservices system** using an **API Gateway, Service Registry, and Discovery Service**. Describe the request flow from client to backend service. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **functions of an API Gateway** in detail. Explain how the gateway implements **load balancing, circuit breaking, and rate limiting** to ensure reliability. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Architecture & Request Flow (7 marks):**

1. **Architecture Diagram (2 marks):**

   *(See SECTION 4.1 above — Client → API Gateway → Service Registry → Microservices.)*

2. **Component Roles (2 marks):**
   - **API Gateway** — Single entry point; routes requests.
   - **Service Registry** — Stores live service locations.
   - **Discovery Service** — Mechanism that resolves service names to instances.
   - **Microservices** — Independent, loosely coupled services.

3. **Request Flow (3 marks):**
   - Client → Gateway (HTTPS).
   - Gateway → Registry (lookup healthy instances).
   - Registry → Gateway (returns instance list).
   - Gateway applies load balancing, forwards to chosen microservice.
   - Microservice processes request, returns response via gateway to client.

**(b) — API Gateway Functions (7 marks):**

1. **Routing (1 mark):** Maps URL paths to backend services using dynamic routing rules.

2. **Authentication & Authorization (1 mark):** Validates JWTs, OAuth2 tokens; enforces RBAC.

3. **Load Balancing (2 marks):** Distributes traffic across healthy instances using:
   - **Round Robin** — equal distribution.
   - **Least Connections** — to the least-loaded instance.
   - **Weighted Response Time** — adaptive based on latency.
   - **Consistent Hashing** — sticky sessions.

4. **Circuit Breaking (2 marks):**
   - The gateway tracks failure rates of downstream calls.
   - When failures exceed a threshold (e.g., 50% in 10 s), the circuit **opens**, and subsequent calls **fail fast** for a cooldown period.
   - After the cooldown, the circuit enters **half-open** — a few test requests are allowed; if successful, it closes again.
   - **Pattern:** States = `CLOSED → OPEN → HALF_OPEN → CLOSED`.

5. **Rate Limiting (1 mark):** Algorithms such as **token bucket** or **leaky bucket** cap requests per second per client, protecting the backend from overload.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Do **NOT** confuse the *Service Registry* with the *Discovery Service*. The **registry is the data store**; the **discovery is the process** of querying it. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks.
> - Do **NOT** draw a diagram without arrows showing the *direction of lookup* (Gateway → Registry, not Registry → Gateway).
> - For **circuit breaker**, you must name all three states (`CLOSED, OPEN, HALF_OPEN`). Naming only two is incomplete.
> - For **message formats**, do **not** write "Protobuf is better than JSON" without justification. Examiners want a *trade-off* answer, not a one-sided opinion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — IPC, Discovery, Registry & Gateway**

- ✅ **IPC** = any mechanism for processes to exchange data — same-host (pipes, shared memory, signals) or cross-host (sockets, RPC, REST, gRPC, message queues).
- ✅ **Four interaction types:** Request–Reply, One-Way, Pub/Sub, Streaming.
- ✅ **Protocol** = rules of conversation; **Standard** = documented spec; **Message Format** = byte-level structure.
- ✅ **Service Registry** = dynamic database of service locations + health status. Examples: **Eureka, Consul, Zookeeper, etcd**.
- ✅ **Service Discovery** = the *process* of finding a service. Two styles:
  - *Client-side* — client queries registry directly (Eureka + Ribbon).
  - *Server-side* — gateway/load balancer queries on behalf of client (K8s Service, AWS ALB).
- ✅ **API Gateway** = single client entry point providing **routing, auth, rate limiting, load balancing, protocol translation, caching, circuit breaking, observability**.
- ✅ **Message Format Standards:** JSON, XML (text) vs Protobuf, Avro, Thrift (binary).
- ✅ **Circuit Breaker States:** `CLOSED → OPEN → HALF_OPEN → CLOSED`.
- ✅ **CAP Theorem in registries:** Eureka (AP), Consul/Zookeeper (CP).
- ✅ **Latency Model:** $T_{\text{total}} = \sum (T_{\text{net}} + T_{\text{proc}} + T_{\text{queue}}) + T_{\text{ser}}$.
- ✅ **Aggregation Latency** = $\max(T_1, T_2, \dots, T_k) + T_{\text{ser}}$.
- ✅ **gRPC** = HTTP/2 + Protobuf; supports streaming, strict schema, language-agnostic.
- ✅ **REST** = resource-oriented, stateless, HTTP verbs (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE).
- ✅ **Heartbeats** keep the registry fresh; 3 missed heartbeats → instance marked DOWN.
- ✅ **Correlation-ID / Trace-ID** propagates across hops for distributed tracing.
- ✅ **Backward compatibility** in Protobuf = never reuse or change a field number.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Containers - Comparison of Virtual Machines and Containers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Containers - Comparison of Virtual Machines and Containers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Virtual Machines (VMs)?

A **Virtual Machine (VM)** is a software-based emulation of a physical computer system that executes programs like a physical machine. Each VM runs its own complete operating system (Guest OS) on top of a hypervisor, which abstracts the underlying physical hardware.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Virtualization):**
> Virtualization is the process of creating multiple simulated environments or dedicated resources from a single physical hardware system, where the **hypervisor** (Virtual Machine Monitor - VMM) abstracts the hardware layer to enable multiple isolated OS instances to run concurrently.

### 1.2 What are Containers?

A **Container** is a lightweight, standalone, executable package that includes everything needed to run an application: code, runtime, system tools, system libraries, and settings. Containers share the host OS kernel and do not require a separate Guest OS, making them significantly more efficient than VMs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Containerization):**
> Containerization is an OS-level virtualization method used to deploy and run distributed applications without launching an entire VM. Containers package application code and dependencies into a single unit called a **container image**, which runs as an isolated user-space process on the host OS, sharing the kernel but maintaining process and filesystem isolation.

### 1.3 Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Housing Analogy for VMs vs. Containers:**
>
> **Virtual Machines = Independent Houses:**
> Imagine you want three separate living spaces. You must build three full houses from the ground up. Each house has its own foundation, plumbing, electrical wiring, walls, and roof — even if the soil and utilities are shared. Building is slow, costs more, and requires more land. This is exactly how VMs work — each VM has its own **full Guest OS**, kernel, and binaries.
>
> **Containers = Apartments in a Shared Building:**
> Now imagine the same three living spaces as apartments in a single building. The building (Host OS + Kernel) already has the foundation, plumbing, and electrical infrastructure. Each apartment is a self-contained unit with its own walls, furniture, and utilities, but they all share the building's core systems. Construction is faster, cheaper, and uses less land. This mirrors containers — they share the **Host OS kernel** but maintain isolated user-space environments.
>
> **Key Insight:** The building's plumbing = Linux Kernel; the apartments = Containers; the houses = VMs. Containers achieve isolation without duplicating the OS layer.

### 1.4 Core Components and Terminology

| Term | Full Form | Role |
|------|-----------|------|
| **Hypervisor** | Virtual Machine Monitor | Manages VMs and abstracts physical hardware (Type 1: Bare-metal, Type 2: Hosted) |
| **Host OS** | Host Operating System | The primary OS running directly on hardware |
| **Guest OS** | Guest Operating System | The OS running inside a VM (independent of Host OS) |
| **Container Engine** | Container Runtime | Software (e.g., Docker, containerd) that builds and runs containers |
| **Container Image** | Immutable Blueprint | Read-only template used to create container instances |
| **Kernel** | OS Core Engine | Lowest-level OS component managing system resources |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 PECMT632 syllabus explicitly requires students to understand the **architectural differences** between VMs and Containers, their **performance trade-offs**, and the **use-case scenarios** where one is preferred over the other.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Virtual Machine Architecture — Layered Breakdown

A VM system is composed of the following layers (from bottom to top):

1. **Physical Hardware Layer:** The actual CPU, RAM, disk, and network interfaces of the host machine.
2. **Hypervisor Layer (VMM):** The software that creates, runs, and manages VMs. Two types exist:
   - **Type 1 (Bare-Metal):** Runs directly on hardware (e.g., VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Xen). Used in enterprise data centers.
   - **Type 2 (Hosted):** Runs as an application on a Host OS (e.g., Oracle VirtualBox, VMware Workstation). Used for development and testing.
3. **Guest OS Layer:** Each VM has a complete, independent OS (e.g., Ubuntu, Windows Server). This consumes significant resources.
4. **Bins/Libraries Layer:** Application binaries and shared libraries specific to the Guest OS.
5. **Application Layer:** The actual user application running on the Guest OS.

**Why This Architecture Exists:** VMs were designed to solve **server consolidation** and **hardware abstraction** problems. By running multiple OS instances on one physical server, organizations could maximize hardware utilization. The trade-off is **high resource overhead** — each VM duplicates the entire OS stack.

### 2.2 Container Architecture — Layered Breakdown

A container system has a significantly thinner stack:

1. **Physical Hardware Layer:** Same physical infrastructure.
2. **Host OS Layer:** A single OS (typically Linux) with containerization features built into the kernel (namespaces, cgroups, union filesystems).
3. **Container Engine Layer:** Daemon process (e.g., Docker daemon, containerd) that manages container lifecycle.
4. **Container Layer:** Each container contains only the application code and its specific dependencies (binaries, libraries). **No Guest OS** — containers share the Host OS kernel.
5. **Application Layer:** The application process running inside the isolated container user-space.

**Why This Architecture Wins:** Containers eliminate the Guest OS layer, reducing the per-instance overhead from **gigabytes to megabytes** and boot time from **minutes to seconds**.

### 2.3 Key Theoretical Concepts

- **Namespaces (Linux Feature):** Provide process isolation. Each container gets its own view of process IDs (PID), network interfaces, mount points, and UTS (hostname). This is what makes containers *appear* to have their own OS environment.
- **Control Groups (cgroups):** Enforce resource limits (CPU shares, memory caps, I/O throttling) per container.
- **Union Filesystems (UnionFS / OverlayFS):** Enable layered image construction. Each Dockerfile instruction creates a read-only layer; the container adds a thin writable layer on top. This makes images compact and shareable.
- **Image Layering:** Multiple containers built from the same base image share the base layers in the host's storage driver, saving disk space.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Virtual Machines | Containers | Units |
|--------|-----------------|------------|-------|
| **Boot Time** | $30 - 120$ | $0.5 - 5$ | seconds |
| **Disk Footprint** | $10 - 50$ | $10 - 500$ | MB |
| **Memory Overhead** | $500 - 2000$ | $10 - 100$ | MB per instance |
| **Performance Loss vs. Native** | $5 - 15$ | $0 - 5$ | percent |
| **OS Instances on Host** | $1$ per VM | $1$ shared (Host) | count |
| **Max Density per Server** | $10 - 50$ | $100 - 1000$ | instances |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Performance Formulas:**

**Container Density Formula:**

$$\text{Max Containers per Host} = \left\lfloor \frac{\text{Total Host RAM (MB)} - \text{OS Reserved RAM (MB)}}{\text{Avg Container Memory (MB)}} \right\rfloor$$

**VM Density Formula:**

$$\text{Max VMs per Host} = \left\lfloor \frac{\text{Total Host RAM (MB)} - \text{Hypervisor Reserved RAM (MB)}}{\text{Guest OS RAM (MB) + App RAM (MB)}} \right\rfloor$$

**Storage Efficiency (Shared Layers):**

$$\text{Disk Savings (\%)} = \left(1 - \frac{\text{Unique Layers Size (MB)}}{\text{Total Image Size (MB) without sharing}}\right) \times 100$$

**Resource Overhead Ratio:**

$$\text{Overhead Ratio} = \frac{\text{VM Total Resources}}{\text{Container Total Resources (same workload)}}$$

Typical industry benchmark: **Overhead Ratio ≈ 5x to 20x** in favor of containers (i.e., containers use 5–20 times less overhead resources for equivalent workloads).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Microservices Architecture:** Containers are the de-facto deployment unit for microservices (Kubernetes orchestration).
- **CI/CD Pipelines:** Containers provide reproducible build and test environments.
- **Hybrid Cloud Portability:** Container images run identically on any Docker-compatible host — on-premise, AWS, Azure, or GCP.
- **Legacy Application Isolation:** VMs are preferred when running applications requiring different OS kernels (e.g., Linux app + Windows app on same hardware).
- **Security-Sensitive Workloads:** VMs provide stronger isolation (separate kernels), making them preferred for multi-tenant scenarios with strict security boundaries.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Industry Insight:** Major platforms like **Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE)**, **Amazon ECS**, and **Red Hat OpenShift** all leverage container technology. Google reportedly runs **billions of containers per week**, a transition that began with their internal **Borg** system and evolved into Kubernetes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Container Density vs. VM Density

**Problem Statement:** A physical server has **64 GB** of RAM. The Host OS / Hypervisor reserves **4 GB**. Each VM requires **4 GB** (Guest OS) + **2 GB** (Application) = **6 GB** total. Each container requires only **256 MB** of RAM. Calculate the maximum number of VMs and Containers that can run on this server.

**Given:**
- $R_{total} = 64 \text{ GB} = 65536 \text{ MB}$
- $R_{reserved} = 4 \text{ GB} = 4096 \text{ MB}$
- $R_{VM} = 6 \text{ GB} = 6144 \text{ MB}$ per VM
- $R_{container} = 256 \text{ MB}$ per container

**Step 1 — Calculate available RAM for workloads:**

$$R_{available} = R_{total} - R_{reserved}$$

$$R_{available} = 65536 - 4096 = 61440 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate maximum VM count:**

$$N_{VM} = \left\lfloor \frac{R_{available}}{R_{VM}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{61440}{6144} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 10 \right\rfloor = 10 \text{ VMs}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate maximum container count:**

$$N_{container} = \left\lfloor \frac{R_{available}}{R_{container}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{61440}{256} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 240 \right\rfloor = 240 \text{ containers}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the density ratio:**

$$\text{Density Ratio} = \frac{N_{container}}{N_{VM}} = \frac{240}{10} = 24$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** On the same 64 GB server, containers can host **24 times** more workload instances than VMs. This is why cloud-native architectures overwhelmingly favor containers for scalable microservices.

### 3.2 Derivation: Total Disk Footprint Analysis

**Problem Statement:** An organization deploys **50** application instances. Each instance needs a **2 GB** base image and **500 MB** of unique application code.

**Scenario A — VMs (No Image Sharing):**

Each VM has its own complete disk image.

$$\text{Total VM Storage} = 50 \times (2048 + 500) = 50 \times 2548 = 127400 \text{ MB} \approx 124.4 \text{ GB}$$

**Scenario B — Containers (With Layer Sharing):**

All 50 containers share the same 2 GB base image (stored once). Only the 500 MB application layer is duplicated per container.

$$\text{Total Container Storage} = 2048 + (50 \times 500) = 2048 + 25000 = 27048 \text{ MB} \approx 26.4 \text{ GB}$$

**Step — Calculate Storage Savings:**

$$\text{Savings (\%)} = \left(1 - \frac{27048}{127400}\right) \times 100$$

$$\text{Savings (\%)} = (1 - 0.2123) \times 100 = 78.77\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Containers achieve approximately **78.77%** storage savings in this scenario, demonstrating the power of union filesystem layering.

### 3.3 Derivation: Boot Time Aggregation at Scale

**Problem Statement:** During a scaling event, **100 new instances** must be ready. VM boot time = **45 seconds**. Container boot time = **2 seconds**.

**Total Provisioning Time — VMs:**

$$T_{VM} = 100 \times 45 = 4500 \text{ seconds} = 75 \text{ minutes}$$

**Total Provisioning Time — Containers:**

$$T_{container} = 100 \times 2 = 200 \text{ seconds} \approx 3.33 \text{ minutes}$$

**Speed-up Factor:**

$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{VM}}{T_{container}} = \frac{4500}{200} = 22.5\times$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Virtual Machines | Containers | KTU Exam Weightage |
|-----------|------------------|------------|---------------------|
| **Isolation Level** | Strong (separate kernel) | Process-level (shared kernel) | **High** — frequently asked |
| **OS Dependency** | Runs any OS (Windows on Linux host, etc.) | Must match Host OS kernel (Linux containers on Linux host) | **High** |
| **Startup Mechanism** | BIOS → Bootloader → Kernel → Init → App | Container engine creates namespaces + cgroups → App | **Medium** |
| **Security Boundary** | Hypervisor-enforced | Namespace + cgroup + capability-based | **High** |
| **Persistent Storage** | Dedicated virtual disks | Volume mounts from host or network storage | **Medium** |
| **Networking Model** | Virtual NIC + virtual switch | Virtual ethernet bridge (veth pair) | **Medium** |
| **Image Format** | VMDK, VHD, OVF, QCOW2 | OCI image spec, Docker layers (tar+gzip) | **Low** |
| **Orchestration** | vSphere, OpenStack | Kubernetes, Docker Swarm, Nomad | **Medium** |
| **Migration (Live)** | vMotion (VMware) | CRIU-based (complex, less mature) | **Low** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 VM Architecture — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[App1 - WordPress]:::vmapp
    B[App2 - MySQL]:::vmapp
    C[App3 - Nginx]:::vmapp
    D[Guest OS 1 - Ubuntu]:::guestos
    E[Guest OS 2 - Windows]:::guestos
    F[Guest OS 3 - CentOS]:::guestos
    G[Hypervisor Type 1 - ESXi]:::hypervisor
    H[Physical Hardware - CPU RAM Disk NIC]:::hardware
    BL1[Bins and Libs 1]:::binlib
    BL2[Bins and Libs 2]:::binlib
    BL3[Bins and Libs 3]:::binlib

    A --> D
    B --> E
    C --> F
    D --> BL1
    E --> BL2
    F --> BL3
    BL1 --> G
    BL2 --> G
    BL3 --> G
    G --> H

    classDef vmapp fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef guestos fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hypervisor fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hardware fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef binlib fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Notice that each application sits atop its **own full Guest OS**, which sits on its own **binaries/libraries** stack. All three Guest OS instances then share the single hypervisor, which directly controls the physical hardware. The thick layer of Guest OS duplication is the primary source of VM overhead.

### 4.2 Container Architecture — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[App1 - Python Service]:::contapp
    A2[App2 - Node JS API]:::contapp
    A3[App3 - Java Microservice]:::contapp
    A4[App4 - Go Worker]:::contapp
    BL1[App Libs 1]:::clib
    BL2[App Libs 2]:::clib
    BL3[App Libs 3]:::clib
    BL4[App Libs 4]:::clib
    CE[Container Engine - Docker]:::engine
    HO[Host OS Kernel - Linux]:::hostos
    HW[Physical Hardware - CPU RAM Disk NIC]:::hardware

    A1 --> BL1
    A2 --> BL2
    A3 --> BL3
    A4 --> BL4
    BL1 --> CE
    BL2 --> CE
    BL3 --> CE
    BL4 --> CE
    CE --> HO
    HO --> HW

    classDef contapp fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef clib fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef engine fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hostos fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hardware fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Compare this to the VM diagram. Notice that there is **NO Guest OS layer**. All four applications share the **single Host OS Kernel** (typically Linux). The **Container Engine** (Docker) is a lightweight daemon that uses kernel features (namespaces, cgroups) to create isolated environments. This is why containers are dramatically more efficient.

### 4.3 Side-by-Side Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VM_STACK[VM Stack - 5 Layers]
        VM1[Application]:::vmapp
        VM2[Guest Bins and Libs]:::binlib
        VM3[Guest Operating System]:::guestos
        VM4[Hypervisor]:::hypervisor
        VM5[Physical Hardware]:::hardware
        VM1 --> VM2 --> VM3 --> VM4 --> VM5
    end

    subgraph CONT_STACK[Container Stack - 3 Layers]
        C1[Application and Deps]:::contapp
        C2[Container Engine]:::engine
        C3[Host OS Kernel]:::hostos
        C4[Physical Hardware]:::hardware
        C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4
    end

    classDef vmapp fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef binlib fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef guestos fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef hypervisor fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef hardware fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef contapp fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef engine fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef hostos fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Visual Insight:** The container stack has **3 functional layers** (App+Deps, Engine, Kernel) versus the VM stack's **5 layers** (App, Bins, Guest OS, Hypervisor, Hardware). The elimination of the Guest OS and its associated binaries is the architectural innovation that makes containers lightweight.

### 4.4 Decision Flow — When to Use VM vs. Container

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Need to Deploy Application]:::start
    Q1{Run different OS kernels?}:::question
    USE_VM[Use Virtual Machine]:::vmchoice
    Q2{Need strong isolation and security boundary?}:::question
    Q3{Is it a microservice or stateless workload?}:::question
    USE_CONT[Use Container]:::contchoice
    HYBRID[Use Both - VM hosts containers]:::hybridchoice

    START --> Q1
    Q1 -->|Yes - Windows on Linux| USE_VM
    Q1 -->|No - Linux only| Q2
    Q2 -->|Yes - multi-tenant| USE_VM
    Q2 -->|No| Q3
    Q3 -->|Yes - scalable, cloud-native| USE_CONT
    Q3 -->|No - legacy monolith| USE_VM
    USE_VM --> HYBRID

    classDef start fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef question fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef vmchoice fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef contchoice fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hybridchoice fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]**

Define a **Virtual Machine** and a **Container**. List **two** key architectural differences between them.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Virtual Machine (VM):** A software emulation of a physical computer that runs a complete Guest Operating System on top of a hypervisor, which abstracts the physical hardware. *(1 mark)*
- **Container:** A lightweight, isolated user-space instance that packages an application with its dependencies and shares the Host OS kernel, using OS-level virtualization features (namespaces and cgroups). *(1 mark)*
- **Key Architectural Differences:** *(1 mark for any two)*
  1. VMs include a full Guest OS per instance; containers share the Host OS kernel and have no Guest OS.
  2. VMs are managed by a **hypervisor**; containers are managed by a **container engine** (e.g., Docker) running on the Host OS.
  3. VMs provide **hardware-level virtualization**; containers provide **OS-level virtualization**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, 3 marks]**

What is a **hypervisor**? Differentiate between **Type 1** and **Type 2** hypervisors with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Hypervisor Definition:** A hypervisor (also called Virtual Machine Monitor or VMM) is software that creates, runs, and manages virtual machines by abstracting the underlying physical hardware and allocating resources to each VM. *(1 mark)*
- **Type 1 (Bare-Metal) Hypervisor:** Runs directly on the physical hardware without a Host OS. Used in enterprise data centers for maximum performance and efficiency. **Example:** VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Xen. *(1 mark)*
- **Type 2 (Hosted) Hypervisor:** Runs as an application on top of a conventional Host OS. Used primarily for development, testing, and desktop virtualization. **Example:** Oracle VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, Parallels Desktop. *(1 mark)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam Pattern, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **layered architecture** of a Virtual Machine system. Describe the role of each layer and explain how a **Type 1 hypervisor** differs architecturally from a **Type 2 hypervisor**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **container architecture**. Discuss the role of **Linux namespaces** and **cgroups** in achieving container isolation and resource control. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Answer for Question A:**

**(a) Layered Architecture of a Virtual Machine System (7 marks):**

The VM system consists of the following layers, from bottom to top: *(1 mark for naming layers)*

| Layer | Role | Marks |
|-------|------|-------|
| **1. Physical Hardware Layer** | Actual CPU, RAM, disk, NIC — provides raw compute resources. | 0.5 |
| **2. Hypervisor Layer (VMM)** | Abstracts hardware; creates and manages VMs; schedules CPU, memory, I/O. | 1 |
| **3. Guest OS Layer** | Complete, independent OS inside each VM (e.g., Ubuntu, Windows). Each VM runs its own kernel. | 1 |
| **4. Binaries and Libraries Layer** | System libraries (glibc, .NET runtime) and utilities specific to the Guest OS. | 0.5 |
| **5. Application Layer** | The end-user application running in the Guest OS environment. | 0.5 |

**Architectural Difference between Type 1 and Type 2 Hypervisors:** *(2 marks)*

- **Type 1 (Bare-Metal):** Installed **directly on the physical hardware**, replacing the Host OS. The hypervisor itself becomes the OS. All hardware resources are managed by the hypervisor kernel. This provides **near-native performance** and is used in production data centers. The hardware → hypervisor → Guest OS path has only one software layer between the app and the metal.
- **Type 2 (Hosted):** Installed **as an application on a conventional Host OS** (Windows, Linux, macOS). The Host OS schedules hardware access, and the hypervisor runs as a process. This adds **extra overhead** (Host OS scheduling) but offers ease of installation and is suited for **development and testing**.

**Mark allocation breakdown:** 5 layers (4 marks) + Type 1 vs Type 2 explanation (3 marks) = **7 marks**.

---

**(b) Container Architecture and Linux Isolation Mechanisms (7 marks):**

**Container Architecture Diagram (Neat Block Diagram — 3 marks):**

```
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  App1  |  App2  |  App3  |  App4  |  (Userspace) |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  App-specific Libraries and Binaries (per cont)  |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|     Container Engine (Docker / containerd)       |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|   Host OS Kernel (Linux) — shared by all cont    |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|        Physical Hardware (CPU, RAM, Disk)        |
+--------------------------------------------------+
```

**Description:** Containers are isolated user-space processes that share the Host OS kernel. The **container engine** (Docker daemon) is responsible for creating, starting, and stopping containers. Each container bundles only the application and its specific dependencies — **no Guest OS** is present. *(1 mark for explanation)*

**Role of Linux Namespaces (2 marks):**

Namespaces provide **process isolation** by giving each container its own view of system resources:

- **PID Namespace:** Each container has its own process ID space. Processes inside a container see only container-internal PIDs. The host sees the container's main process as a single host PID.
- **Network Namespace:** Each container gets its own virtual network interfaces, IP address, and routing table. Containers are connected via virtual bridges (veth pairs).
- **Mount Namespace:** Each container has its own filesystem mount points, providing filesystem isolation.
- **UTS Namespace:** Each container can have its own hostname and domain name.
- **IPC Namespace:** Inter-process communication is isolated between containers.

**Role of Control Groups — cgroups (1 mark):**

cgroups enforce **resource limits and accounting** for containers:

- **CPU cgroup:** Limits CPU shares and pins specific cores to containers.
- **Memory cgroup:** Caps maximum RAM usage; triggers OOM killer if exceeded.
- **Block I/O cgroup:** Throttles disk read/write bandwidth.
- **Device cgroup:** Controls access to physical devices.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Many students incorrectly state that containers "have their own OS." This is **wrong** and costs 1 mark. The correct statement is: "Containers share the Host OS kernel but have isolated user-space." Also, do not confuse **namespaces** (isolation) with **cgroups** (resource limits) — they are distinct kernel features. Listing only one mechanism when the question asks for both will result in a 1-mark deduction.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Virtual Machines and Containers** under the following heads: **(i)** Architecture, **(ii)** Boot time, **(iii)** Resource overhead, **(iv)** Security isolation, **(v)** Use cases, **(vi)** OS dependency, **(vii)** Image size. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A cloud provider has a physical server with **128 GB RAM** and **32 CPU cores**. The Host OS and orchestration layer reserve **8 GB RAM** and **2 cores**. Each VM needs **8 GB RAM + 4 cores** (including Guest OS). Each container needs **512 MB RAM + 0.5 cores**. Calculate: **(i)** Maximum number of VMs that can run, **(ii)** Maximum number of containers that can run, **(iii)** The density ratio. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Answer for Question B:**

**(a) Comprehensive Comparison Table (7 marks — 1 mark per head):**

| Head | Virtual Machines | Containers |
|------|------------------|------------|
| **(i) Architecture** | Hardware → Hypervisor → Guest OS → Bins/Libs → App | Hardware → Host OS Kernel → Container Engine → App+Deps |
| **(ii) Boot Time** | 30–120 seconds (full OS boot) | 0.5–5 seconds (process spawn) |
| **(iii) Resource Overhead** | High: 500 MB–2 GB RAM per VM for Guest OS alone | Low: 10–100 MB RAM per container |
| **(iv) Security Isolation** | Very strong: separate kernels, hardware-enforced boundaries | Weaker: shared kernel, namespace-based; potential kernel exploits affect all containers |
| **(v) Use Cases** | Running multiple OS, legacy apps, strong multi-tenancy, security-critical workloads | Microservices, CI/CD, cloud-native apps, rapid scaling, dev/test environments |
| **(vi) OS Dependency** | VM can run any OS (Windows VM on Linux host) | Container must match Host OS kernel family (Linux containers on Linux host) |
| **(vii) Image Size** | 10–50 GB (full OS image) | 10–500 MB (app + minimal deps) |

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $R_{total} = 128 \text{ GB} = 131072 \text{ MB}$
- $C_{total} = 32 \text{ cores}$
- $R_{reserved} = 8 \text{ GB} = 8192 \text{ MB}$
- $C_{reserved} = 2 \text{ cores}$
- $R_{VM} = 8 \text{ GB} = 8192 \text{ MB}$ per VM
- $C_{VM} = 4 \text{ cores}$ per VM
- $R_{container} = 512 \text{ MB}$ per container
- $C_{container} = 0.5 \text{ cores}$ per container

**Step 1 — Calculate available resources:**

$$R_{avail} = 131072 - 8192 = 122880 \text{ MB}$$

$$C_{avail} = 32 - 2 = 30 \text{ cores}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate maximum VMs (RAM-limited and CPU-limited):**

$$\text{By RAM: } N_{VM}^{RAM} = \left\lfloor \frac{122880}{8192} \right\rfloor = 15 \text{ VMs}$$

$$\text{By CPU: } N_{VM}^{CPU} = \left\lfloor \frac{30}{4} \right\rfloor = 7.5 \rightarrow 7 \text{ VMs}$$

**The binding constraint is the smaller value:**

$$N_{VM} = \min(15, 7) = 7 \text{ VMs}$$

**(i) Maximum VMs = 7** *(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Calculate maximum containers:**

$$\text{By RAM: } N_{c}^{RAM} = \left\lfloor \frac{122880}{512} \right\rfloor = 240 \text{ containers}$$

$$\text{By CPU: } N_{c}^{CPU} = \left\lfloor \frac{30}{0.5} \right\rfloor = 60 \text{ containers}$$

**Binding constraint:**

$$N_{c} = \min(240, 60) = 60 \text{ containers}$$

**(ii) Maximum Containers = 60** *(2 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Students often divide only by RAM and ignore the CPU constraint, or vice versa. In a real cloud server, **both constraints must be checked**, and the **minimum** is the true maximum. This mistake costs 1 mark.

**Step 4 — Calculate density ratio:**

$$\text{Density Ratio} = \frac{N_{c}}{N_{VM}} = \frac{60}{7} \approx 8.57$$

**(iii) Density Ratio ≈ 8.57×** *(1 mark)*

**Bonus Interpretation (1 mark):** In this CPU-bound scenario, containers achieve approximately **8.57 times** the workload density of VMs, although the ratio would be far higher in memory-bound scenarios (up to 24× as shown in Section 3.1).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Virtual Machines** virtualize the **hardware layer**; **Containers** virtualize the **OS layer**. This is the single most important distinction.
- VMs include a **full Guest OS per instance**; Containers share the **Host OS kernel** and have no Guest OS.
- **Hypervisors** manage VMs; **Container Engines** (Docker, containerd) manage containers.
- **Linux Namespaces** provide isolation (process, network, mount, UTS, IPC, user).
- **Control Groups (cgroups)** provide resource limits (CPU, memory, I/O, devices).
- **Union Filesystems** (OverlayFS) enable image layering and shared base layers, saving disk space.
- Containers achieve **10×–24× higher density** than VMs for typical workloads.
- Container **boot time** is in seconds; VM boot time is in tens of seconds to minutes.
- VMs provide **stronger security** due to kernel separation; containers have a **larger shared attack surface**.
- VMs are preferred for **multi-OS, legacy, and high-security** scenarios; Containers are preferred for **microservices, cloud-native, and rapid scaling**.
- **Density calculations must consider BOTH RAM and CPU constraints** — take the minimum.
- **Image sharing** (container layering) provides significant **storage savings** (often 70–80%).
- **Kubernetes** is the de-facto orchestrator for containers at scale; **vSphere/OpenStack** for VMs.
- The KTU 2024 PECMT632 Module 4 focuses on the **architectural comparison** and **use-case trade-offs** — memorize the comparison table for the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Docker
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Docker

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Docker

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Docker** is an open-source **containerization** platform that enables developers to package applications along with their entire runtime dependencies—libraries, system tools, code, and configuration—into a single, lightweight, portable, and self-sufficient unit called a **container**. This container can then be executed consistently across any environment that supports Docker's container runtime, irrespective of the underlying host operating system, hardware, or cloud infrastructure.

In the formal KTU 2024 scheme lexicon, Docker is positioned as a **Operating-System-Level Virtualization** tool (as opposed to Hardware-Level Virtualization used by traditional Hypervisors). It utilizes the host kernel's features—such as **cgroups** (control groups) for resource limiting, **namespaces** for process isolation, and **UnionFS** (Union File Systems) for layered image construction—to deliver isolated execution environments without the overhead of booting a full guest operating system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECMT632 Module 4):** Docker is studied as a foundational technology in modern **Cloud-Native Computing**, **DevOps**, and **Microservices Architecture**. KTU examiners frequently expect students to distinguish between **Containers vs Virtual Machines (VMs)**, describe the **Docker Engine architecture**, and explain the **Image–Container lifecycle**.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Docker Terminology

| Pillar | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Image** | A read-only, immutable template/blueprint used to create containers. Built from a `Dockerfile`. |
| **Container** | A runnable instance of an image. It is a lightweight, isolated executable package. |
| **Registry** | A repository service (e.g., Docker Hub) for storing and distributing Docker images. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Shipping Container" Paradigm

Imagine the global shipping industry **before the 1950s**. Goods were transported using crates of wildly different shapes and sizes—bags, barrels, wooden boxes. Loading them onto ships, trains, and trucks was a nightmare because each form of transport handled cargo differently. A crate designed for a train often couldn't fit on a ship.

Then the standardized **intermodal shipping container** was invented. Every container is the **same size**, has the **same corner fittings**, and can be lifted by **any crane** in any port, regardless of what is inside. The contents (TVs, bananas, cars, textiles) are irrelevant to the transport infrastructure.

**Docker applies the exact same principle to software:**

| Real-World Analogy | Docker Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Standardized Shipping Container | Docker Container |
| Cargo inside the container | Application + Dependencies |
| Bill of Lading / Manufacturing spec | Dockerfile |
| Container Yard / Warehouse | Docker Registry (Docker Hub) |
| Crane that lifts the container | Docker Engine / Daemon |
| Ship/Train/Truck (any transport) | Linux Server / Cloud VM / Laptop |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The famous Docker slogan *"Build once, Run anywhere"* is the software equivalent of *"Load once, Ship anywhere."* Before Docker, developers suffered from the classic *"It works on my machine"* syndrome because applications had implicit dependencies on the host OS. Docker eliminates this by packaging everything explicitly.

## 1.4 Key High-Yield Vocabulary for KTU Examinations

- **Daemon (`dockerd`)**: The long-running background service that manages Docker objects (images, containers, networks, volumes).
- **Client (`docker`)**: The command-line interface (CLI) used by users to interact with the daemon via REST API.
- **Dockerfile**: A plain-text script containing sequential instructions to automate the image-building process.
- **Union File System (UnionFS)**: A layered file system that allows multiple directories to be mounted simultaneously, enabling Docker images to be built as a stack of read-only layers.
- **cgroups (Control Groups)**: Linux kernel feature for limiting, accounting, and isolating resource usage (CPU, memory, disk I/O) of process groups.
- **Namespaces**: Linux kernel feature that partitions kernel resources so that one set of processes sees one set of resources while another set sees a different set—provides process isolation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Docker Engine Architecture — Component Breakdown

The Docker Engine is a **client-server application** comprising three primary components. Understanding this triad is **mandatory** for KTU Module 4 questions.

### 2.1.1 The Docker Daemon (`dockerd`)
- A persistent **background process** (server).
- Listens for Docker API requests on a Unix socket `/var/run/docker.sock` (Linux) or named pipe `\\.\pipe\docker_engine` (Windows).
- Responsible for building, running, and distributing Docker containers.
- Can communicate with other daemons for Docker Swarm (cluster mode).

### 2.1.2 The REST API
- A standardized **interface** used by applications to communicate with the daemon.
- Acts as the bridge between the Docker Client and the Daemon.
- Allows remote control—i.e., a Docker client on Machine A can control a daemon running on Machine B over a network.

### 2.1.3 The Docker CLI Client
- The primary user-facing tool invoked from the terminal as `docker <command>`.
- Translates user commands into API calls sent to the daemon.
- Multiple CLI tools (e.g., `docker`, `docker-compose`, `docker-machine`) can communicate with the same daemon.

## 2.2 Docker Image — The Layered Construction Model

A Docker image is constructed as a stack of **read-only layers**. Each instruction in a `Dockerfile` creates a corresponding layer. Layers enable:
- **Reusability**: Identical layers across images are shared, saving disk space.
- **Speed**: Only changed layers need to be re-downloaded or rebuilt.
- **Immutability**: Once built, an image never changes; new changes create new layers.

When a container is launched from an image, Docker adds a thin **writable container layer** (the *container layer*) on top of the image's read-only layers. All runtime changes (file modifications, new files) are written to this top layer using **Copy-on-Write (CoW)** semantics.

## 2.3 The Container Lifecycle — From Blueprint to Running Process

The lifecycle passes through distinct stages:

1. **Write `Dockerfile`** → Author the build recipe.
2. **Build Image** → `docker build` produces an immutable image with a SHA-256 digest.
3. **Push to Registry** → `docker push` uploads the image to Docker Hub / private registry.
4. **Pull from Registry** → `docker pull` downloads the image to the target host.
5. **Run Container** → `docker run` instantiates the image as a running container.
6. **Stop / Start / Pause / Remove** → Operational control commands.
7. **Commit (optional)** → `docker commit` snapshots the container's writable layer as a new image.

## 2.4 Docker vs. Virtual Machines — The Critical Comparison

This is the **single most-asked comparison** in KTU Module 4 examinations.

| Parameter | Virtual Machine (VM) | Docker Container |
|---|---|---|
| **Virtualization Level** | Hardware-level | OS-level |
| **Hypervisor** | Required (Type-1 / Type-2) | Not required (uses host kernel) |
| **Guest OS** | Includes full OS per VM (GBs) | Shares host OS kernel (MBs) |
| **Boot Time** | Minutes (BIOS → Boot → OS) | Milliseconds to seconds |
| **Resource Overhead** | High (dedicated RAM, CPU, disk) | Minimal (process-level isolation) |
| **Isolation Strength** | Strong (separate kernel) | Weaker (shared kernel) |
| **Image/Artifact Size** | Gigabytes (entire OS) | Megabytes (app + deps only) |
| **Performance** | Near-native but degraded by hypervisor | Near-native, almost no overhead |
| **Portability** | Limited by hypervisor compatibility | Highly portable across any Docker host |
| **Density per Host** | 10–20 VMs typical | Hundreds to thousands of containers |
| **Security Model** | Hardware-enforced isolation | Kernel namespaces + capabilities |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Key Insight:** A VM virtualizes the **hardware**; a container virtualizes the **operating system**. Containers are NOT mini-VMs—they are processes on the host kernel that *think* they have their own system.

## 2.5 Resource Limit Formulas (Quantitative Aspect)

When Docker imposes resource constraints, the following quantitative relationships govern behavior:

### CPU Shares (Relative Weighting)

$$\text{Effective CPU}_{\text{container}} = \frac{\text{CPU Shares}_{\text{container}}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{CPU Shares}_{\text{all containers}}} \times \text{Total Host CPU}$$

Default share value = **1024**. Therefore, a container assigned 512 shares on a host with two such containers receives approximately **25%** of one CPU core under contention.

### Memory Limit Enforcement

Docker enforces a **hard memory limit** (no swap by default). When the limit is hit, the kernel invokes the **OOM (Out-Of-Memory) Killer**, terminating the offending container with exit code **137**.

$$\text{Exit Code}_{\text{OOM}} = 137 \quad \text{(where } 128 + 9 \text{ = SIGKILL)}$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula / Reference Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Command | Definition / Use |
|---|---|---|
| Container ID | `$CID` | 64-character SHA-256 hash, abbreviated to 12 chars |
| Image ID | `$IID` | SHA-256 digest uniquely identifying an image |
| Layer Count | $L$ | Number of read-only filesystem layers in an image |
| Container Disk Footprint | $D_c = D_i + W_c$ | $D_i$ = image size, $W_c$ = writable layer size |
| Image Pull Command | `docker pull <image>:<tag>` | Fetches image from registry |
| Container Run Command | `docker run -d -p 80:80 nginx` | `-d` daemon mode, `-p` port mapping |
| List Running Containers | `docker ps` | Shows active containers |
| Resource Limit Flag | `--memory="512m" --cpus="1.5"` | Caps memory & CPU shares |
| Build Command | `docker build -t app:v1 .` | Builds image from Dockerfile in CWD |
| Registry Push | `docker push username/app:v1` | Publishes to Docker Hub |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Docker underpins modern Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) pipelines, Kubernetes orchestration, serverless edge computing, and reproducible research environments. Every major cloud provider (AWS, Azure, GCP) offers managed container services (ECS, AKS, GKE) rooted in Docker's image format standard—the **OCI (Open Container Initiative)** specification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Workflow & Container Lifecycle Derivation

This section walks through the **complete operational lifecycle** of a Docker container, from the empty host system to a fully deployed and managed application instance. Each transition is annotated with its underlying system call or engine action.

## 3.1 Phase 1 — Authoring the Dockerfile

The `Dockerfile` is a declarative recipe. Consider the canonical *Node.js web server* example:

```dockerfile
# Step 1: Specify the base image layer
FROM node:18-alpine

# Step 2: Set the working directory inside the image
WORKDIR /app

# Step 3: Copy package manifests (layer cached separately)
COPY package*.json ./

# Step 4: Install dependencies (creates a new layer)
RUN npm install

# Step 5: Copy application source code
COPY . .

# Step 6: Expose the application port (metadata only)
EXPOSE 3000

# Step 7: Define the default runtime command
CMD ["node", "server.js"]
```

**Engineering rationale for layer ordering:**
Dependencies (`npm install`) are placed *before* source-code copy. Because Docker caches layers and rebuilds only changed layers, editing source code does not invalidate the expensive `npm install` layer—drastically accelerating rebuild times.

## 3.2 Phase 2 — Image Build Process (Layer-by-Layer Construction)

When the engineer executes `docker build -t myapp:1.0 .`, the Docker daemon performs the following sequence:

1. **Context Transfer** — The CLI sends the build context (CWD contents) to the daemon over the REST API.
2. **Dockerfile Parse** — The daemon reads the Dockerfile line by line.
3. **Sequential Layer Construction** — Each instruction triggers an intermediate container:
   - `FROM` → Pulls base image; if absent locally, pulls from registry.
   - `WORKDIR` → Creates and sets the working directory.
   - `COPY` → Adds files from build context into a new layer.
   - `RUN` → Executes the command inside an intermediate container, committing the filesystem change as a new layer.
   - `EXPOSE` → Records metadata; no filesystem change.
   - `CMD` → Records default execution arguments; no filesystem change.
4. **Image Finalization** — A unified image manifest is produced with an SHA-256 digest.

The resulting image can be represented mathematically as:

$$I_{\text{myapp:1.0}} = \left[ L_{\text{base}} \oplus L_{\text{workdir}} \oplus L_{\text{copy-pkg}} \oplus L_{\text{npm}} \oplus L_{\text{copy-src}} \oplus L_{\text{cmd}} \right]$$

where $\oplus$ denotes the layer-stacking (union mount) operator.

## 3.3 Phase 3 — Registry Push / Pull

### Push (Publishing)
$$ \text{Engineer} \xrightarrow{\texttt{docker push}} \text{Docker Hub} $$

The image manifest + compressed layers are uploaded. Each layer's SHA-256 is verified by the registry for integrity.

### Pull (Distribution)
$$ \text{Host} \xrightarrow{\texttt{docker pull myapp:1.0}} \text{Registry} $$

The daemon performs a **content-addressable lookup**: it requests the manifest, then downloads only those layers whose SHA-256 digests are not already present in the local image cache.

## 3.4 Phase 4 — Container Instantiation (Runtime Boot)

When the user executes `docker run -d -p 8080:3000 --name webapp myapp:1.0`, the daemon performs these kernel-level operations in rapid succession:

| Step | Daemon Action | Kernel Feature Invoked |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Verifies image `myapp:1.0` exists locally | Local store lookup |
| 2 | Creates a new writable container layer atop the image | UnionFS mount |
| 3 | Allocates a PID namespace | `clone(CLONE_NEWPID)` |
| 4 | Allocates Network namespace | `clone(CLONE_NEWNET)` |
| 5 | Allocates Mount, UTS, IPC namespaces | `clone(CLONE_NEWNS/NEWUTS/NEWIPC)` |
| 6 | Sets resource limits (cgroups) | `cgcreate`, `cgroup.procs` writes |
| 7 | Sets up port forwarding `-p 8080:3000` | `iptables` NAT rule, veth pair |
| 8 | Executes `node server.js` (from CMD) | `execve()` system call |
| 9 | Returns container ID `0xCAFEBABE...` | Engine response |

The total cold-start time is typically **sub-second** because no BIOS, bootloader, or kernel boot sequence is invoked—`execve()` directly loads the user-space process into memory.

## 3.5 Phase 5 — Operational Lifecycle Commands

Once running, the container can be manipulated through state transitions:

$$
\text{Created} \xrightarrow{\texttt{docker start}} \text{Running} \xrightarrow{\texttt{docker pause}} \text{Paused}
$$

$$
\text{Running} \xrightarrow{\texttt{docker stop}} \text{Stopped} \xrightarrow{\texttt{docker rm}} \text{Removed}
$$

$$
\text{Paused} \xrightarrow{\texttt{docker unpause}} \text{Running}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for Examinations:** A *Stopped* container still occupies disk space (its writable layer persists) and can be restarted. A *Removed* container is permanently deleted via `docker rm`, releasing all storage. This differs from images, which are deleted via `docker rmi`.

## 3.6 Phase 6 — Resource Accounting

The total disk footprint of a running container can be expressed as:

$$
D_{\text{total}} = \sum_{j=1}^{L} S(L_j) + S(W_c)
$$

where $S(L_j)$ is the on-disk size of read-only layer $j$, and $S(W_c)$ is the size of the container's writable layer.

The memory footprint (Resident Set Size, RSS) of all containers running on a host must satisfy:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{Mem}_{\text{RSS}}(C_i) \leq \text{Mem}_{\text{host}} - \text{Mem}_{\text{kernel}} - \text{Mem}_{\text{daemon}}
$$

Failure of this inequality triggers the OOM killer (exit code 137).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Docker Engine Architecture (Client–Daemon Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    user["User Terminal"] --> cli["Docker CLI Client"]
    cli -->|"REST API over Unix Socket"| daemon["Docker Daemon dockerd"]
    daemon -->|"manages"| images["Local Image Cache"]
    daemon -->|"manages"| containers["Running Containers"]
    daemon -->|"manages"| networks["Docker Networks"]
    daemon -->|"manages"| volumes["Docker Volumes"]
    daemon <-->|"pull or push"| registry["Docker Registry Hub"]
    subgraph HostKernel["Linux Host Kernel"]
        ns["Namespaces PID NET MNT UTS IPC USER"]
        cg["cgroups CPU Memory BlkIO"]
        ufs["UnionFS OverlayFS"]
    end
    daemon -.->|"system calls"| HostKernel
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Docker Container vs Virtual Machine Stack Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VMStack["Virtual Machine Stack"]
        vApp1["App A"] --> vGuest1["Guest OS Ubuntu"]
        vApp2["App B"] --> vGuest2["Guest OS Windows"]
        vGuest1 --> vHyper["Hypervisor ESXi or KVM"]
        vGuest2 --> vHyper
        vHyper --> vHost["Host Operating System"]
        vHost --> vHW["Physical Hardware"]
    end

    subgraph ContainerStack["Docker Container Stack"]
        cApp1["App A"] --> cEngine1["Docker Engine Shared"]
        cApp2["App B"] --> cEngine1
        cApp3["App C"] --> cEngine1
        cEngine1 --> cHost2["Host Operating System Single Kernel"]
        cHost2 --> cHW2["Physical Hardware"]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Image Build Pipeline (Layered Construction)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    df["Dockerfile"] --> b1["Instruction 1 FROM"]
    b1 --> b2["Instruction 2 WORKDIR"]
    b2 --> b3["Instruction 3 COPY package.json"]
    b3 --> b4["Instruction 4 RUN npm install"]
    b4 --> b5["Instruction 5 COPY source code"]
    b5 --> b6["Instruction 6 EXPOSE 3000"]
    b6 --> b7["Instruction 7 CMD"]
    b7 --> img["Final Image myapp 1.0 with SHA256"]
    img -->|"docker push"| hub["Docker Hub Registry"]
    hub -->|"docker pull"| remote["Remote Host"]
    remote -->|"docker run"| cont["Running Container Layer Writable"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Aid for the Architecture Diagram:** Note how the **Docker Client** never touches images, containers, or the kernel directly—it always negotiates through the **Daemon** via the **REST API**. This decoupling is what enables remote daemon control (e.g., a developer on a laptop managing containers on a cloud VM).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define Docker and explain the concept of containerization. How does it differ from traditional virtualization?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Docker is an open-source containerization platform that packages applications and their dependencies into standardized, portable units called containers.* *(1 mark)*
