*Containerization uses OS-level virtualization through Linux kernel features—namespaces for isolation and cgroups for resource limiting—allowing multiple containers to share the host kernel while appearing as isolated systems.* *(1 mark)*

*Unlike traditional virtualization that requires a hypervisor and runs full guest operating systems per VM, containerization shares the host OS kernel, resulting in smaller image sizes (MBs vs GBs), faster startup (milliseconds vs minutes), and higher density.* *(1 mark)*

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List and briefly explain any three components of the Docker Engine architecture.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Docker Daemon (`dockerd`)** — The background server process that builds images, runs containers, and manages Docker objects. *(1 mark)*
2. **REST API** — The interface through which the daemon exposes its services; enables both local and remote communication. *(1 mark)*
3. **Docker CLI Client** — The command-line tool invoked as `docker <command>`; translates user commands into API calls to the daemon. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### ✦ Question A **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the layered architecture of Docker images. How does the layer caching mechanism improve build efficiency? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare and contrast Docker containers with Virtual Machines across **at least eight** parameters. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

A Docker image is built as a **stack of read-only layers**, where each instruction in the Dockerfile produces a corresponding layer using UnionFS (commonly OverlayFS2 on modern Linux). *(1 mark)*

**Layer Construction Process:**
- `FROM node:18-alpine` → pulls the base image, which itself contains multiple layers (OS, Node.js runtime, package manager). *(1 mark)*
- `WORKDIR /app` → creates a directory; this is a metadata-only change but recorded as a new layer.
- `COPY package*.json ./` → adds JSON files; cached as layer $L_3$.
- `RUN npm install` → installs dependencies into layer $L_4$.
- `COPY . .` → copies application source into layer $L_5$.

The final image manifest is the ordered union: $I = [L_1 \oplus L_2 \oplus L_3 \oplus L_4 \oplus L_5]$. *(1 mark)*

**Layer Caching Mechanism:**
Docker caches each layer indexed by its SHA-256 hash. During rebuilds, if the Dockerfile instruction and its inputs are unchanged, Docker reuses the cached layer instead of re-executing the instruction. *(1 mark)*

This yields three efficiency benefits:
1. **Build Speed** — Unchanged layers are skipped, reducing rebuild time from minutes to seconds. *(1 mark)*
2. **Storage Efficiency** — Identical layers across multiple images (e.g., shared base images) are stored only once on disk and shared via hard links. *(1 mark)*
3. **Network Efficiency** — When pulling images, only missing layers are downloaded. *(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently answer this question by merely describing Dockerfile instructions without explaining **how** layer caching uses content hashes and **why** layer ordering (e.g., dependencies before source code) affects cache validity. Marks are lost for omitting the SHA-256 indexing mechanism.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

| Parameter | Docker Container | Virtual Machine |
|---|---|---|
| Virtualization Level *(1 mark)* | OS-level virtualization | Hardware-level virtualization |
| Hypervisor Required *(1 mark)* | No; uses host kernel directly | Yes (Type-1 bare-metal or Type-2 hosted) |
| Guest OS *(1 mark)* | None—shares host kernel | Full guest OS per VM |
| Image/Artifact Size *(1 mark)* | Typically **10–500 MB** | Typically **5–50 GB** |
| Boot Time *(1 mark)* | **Milliseconds to seconds** | **30 seconds to several minutes** |
| Isolation *(1 mark)* | Process-level via namespaces; weaker | Hardware-enforced; stronger |
| Density per Host *(1 mark)* | Hundreds to thousands | 10–20 typically |
| Performance Overhead *(1 mark)* | Near-native (~1–3%) | 5–15% hypervisor overhead |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write generic "containers are lightweight, VMs are heavy" answers without concrete numbers, mechanisms, or naming the specific kernel features (namespaces, cgroups, UnionFS) that enable containerization. KTU examiners award partial credit for *quantitative* comparison and *technical depth*.

---

### ✦ Question B **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **Docker Engine architecture** and the role of each component. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **complete lifecycle of a Docker container** from writing a Dockerfile to removing a running container. Include all major Docker commands. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Architecture Diagram (Student Reproduces):** *(3 marks)*

```
┌──────────────────┐
│  Docker Client   │
│      (CLI)       │
└────────┬─────────┘
         │ REST API calls
         │ (HTTP over Unix socket / TCP)
         ▼
┌──────────────────┐
│  Docker Daemon   │
│     (dockerd)    │
└────────┬─────────┘
         │ Manages
         ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Images │ Containers │ Networks │ Volumes │
└──────────────────────────────────────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│       Linux Host Kernel                   │
│  Namespaces │ cgroups │ UnionFS (Overlay) │
└──────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Role of Each Component:** *(4 marks — 1 mark each for explanation*

1. **Docker CLI Client** — User-facing command-line interface; issues commands like `docker build`, `docker run`; communicates with daemon via REST API. *(1 mark)*
2. **REST API** — Standardized interface (HTTP-based) for daemon interaction; allows remote control over TCP and local control via Unix socket. *(1 mark)*
3. **Docker Daemon (`dockerd`)** — Background service managing all Docker objects—building images, starting containers, configuring networks, mounting volumes, communicating with registries. *(1 mark)*
4. **Host Kernel (Namespaces + cgroups + UnionFS)** — Linux kernel primitives providing process isolation, resource limits, and layered filesystem support—these are the **actual mechanism** of containerization, not Docker itself. *(1 mark)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Authoring the Dockerfile** *(1 mark)*
The engineer writes a declarative `Dockerfile` containing `FROM`, `RUN`, `COPY`, `CMD`, etc. instructions describing how to construct the image.

**Step 2 — Building the Image** *(1 mark)*
Command: `docker build -t myapp:1.0 .`
The daemon reads the Dockerfile, executes each instruction sequentially, and produces an immutable image with a SHA-256 digest stored in the local image cache.

**Step 3 — Publishing to a Registry** *(1 mark)*
Command: `docker push username/myapp:1.0`
The image is uploaded to Docker Hub or a private registry for distribution.

**Step 4 — Pulling the Image on a Target Host** *(1 mark)*
Command: `docker pull username/myapp:1.0`
Only layers not already present locally are downloaded.

**Step 5 — Running the Container** *(1 mark)*
Command: `docker run -d -p 8080:3000 --name webapp myapp:1.0`
The daemon creates namespaces, sets cgroup limits, mounts the UnionFS writable layer, and executes `CMD` via `execve()`.

**Step 6 — Operational Management** *(1 mark)*
Commands:
- `docker ps` — list running containers
- `docker logs <id>` — view output
- `docker exec -it <id> sh` — interactive shell
- `docker stop <id>` — graceful termination (SIGTERM, then SIGKILL after 10s)
- `docker start <id>` — restart a stopped container

**Step 7 — Cleanup** *(1 mark)*
Commands: `docker rm <id>` (removes stopped container) and `docker rmi <image>` (removes image from cache).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse `docker rm` (removes containers) with `docker rmi` (removes images). They also often forget that `docker stop` sends **SIGTERM first** and only escalates to SIGKILL after a grace period—this signaling behavior is a frequent viva question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Docker** is an OS-level virtualization platform leveraging **Linux namespaces**, **cgroups**, and **UnionFS**.
- The **Docker Engine** consists of three components: **CLI Client**, **REST API**, and **Daemon (`dockerd`)**.
- **Images** are read-only, layered, content-addressable templates built from a **Dockerfile**.
- **Containers** are runnable instances of images; they add a thin **writable layer** atop image layers using Copy-on-Write semantics.
- **Registries** (e.g., Docker Hub) store and distribute images; content is addressed via **SHA-256 digests**.
- The core slogan **"Build once, Run anywhere"** encapsulates Docker's portability promise.
- **Containers vs VMs**: Containers share the host kernel (MB-sized, ms-boot, high density); VMs emulate full hardware with dedicated guest OSes (GB-sized, minute-boot, low density).
- **Layer caching** uses SHA-256 hashes; identical layers are shared across images; ordering matters for cache validity.
- **Resource limits** are enforced via cgroups; CPU shares default to **1024**; memory overruns trigger **OOM Killer** (exit code **137**).
- **Key commands** to memorize: `docker build`, `docker pull`, `docker run`, `docker ps`, `docker stop`, `docker rm`, `docker rmi`, `docker exec`, `docker logs`, `docker push`.
- **Port mapping** syntax `-p HOST_PORT:CONTAINER_PORT` uses iptables NAT under the hood.
- The container lifecycle moves through: **Created → Running → Paused/Stopped → Removed**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study - Docker Containers - Architecture, Components, Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT632 - TOPICS IN COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Study - Docker Containers - Architecture, Components, Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Docker** is an open-source **containerization platform** that enables developers to package applications and their entire dependency stack (libraries, binaries, configuration files, runtime) into a standardized, portable, and isolated unit called a **container**. Containers are executed by a lightweight, OS-level virtualization engine that shares the host operating system kernel while maintaining strict process, filesystem, and network isolation through Linux kernel primitives such as **namespaces** and **cgroups** (control groups).

A **Docker Container** is a runtime instance of a **Docker Image**. The image is a read-only, layered, immutable blueprint; the container is the running, writable, ephemeral execution environment spawned from that image.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Standard (PECMT632 Module 4):**
> "A Docker container is a lightweight, standalone, executable package of software that includes everything needed to run an application: code, runtime, system tools, system libraries and settings."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of Docker Containers like **standardized international shipping containers**.

| Shipping World (Analogy) | Docker World (Technical) |
|---|---|
| Standard ISO shipping container | Docker Container |
| Manufacturing blueprint of the container | Docker Image |
| Container ship (engine + crane + storage) | Docker Engine (Daemon) |
| Port authority / container registry | Docker Hub / Private Registry |
| Loading dock & manifest system | Docker CLI (`docker` commands) |
| Different goods (TV, clothes, food) inside | Application code + dependencies |

Just as a shipping container can be loaded on **any ship, train, or truck** anywhere in the world and still hold the same goods safely, a Docker container can run on **any host (laptop, server, cloud VM, Kubernetes node)** with the same predictable behaviour — **"Build once, run anywhere."**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Docker Matters in Modern Computing:**
> - Eliminates the classic **"It works on my machine"** problem.
> - Reduces deployment time from **hours (VMs) to seconds (containers)**.
> - Improves **CI/CD pipeline** efficiency by 3x–10x in industry benchmarks.
> - Foundation of **microservices, DevOps, and cloud-native architectures**.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Industry-Standard)

- **Container Startup Time:** **~50–500 ms** (compared to 30–60 s for VMs)
- **Image Size:** Typically **5 MB – 1 GB** (Alpine Linux base ≈ **5 MB**)
- **Density:** Up to **10x more containers per host** vs. VMs
- **Performance Overhead:** **< 2%** native CPU/RAM (vs. 5–15% for hypervisors)

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** A student must clearly distinguish between **Images vs Containers**, **Docker Engine vs Docker Desktop**, and **Containers vs Virtual Machines** — these are favourite examiner traps.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: How Docker Actually Works

Docker leverages three pillars of the Linux kernel:

1. **Namespaces** → Provide **process isolation** (PID, NET, MNT, UTS, IPC, USER namespaces). Each container believes it owns the system.
2. **Control Groups (cgroups)** → Provide **resource limiting & accounting** (CPU shares, memory caps, block I/O).
3. **Union File Systems (UnionFS / OverlayFS)** → Enable **layered, copy-on-write images** that share base layers efficiently.

The execution chain:
1. Developer writes a `Dockerfile` (recipe).
2. `docker build` produces an **Image** (layered tarballs).
3. `docker run` instantiates a **Container** (writable layer + image layers).
4. `docker push` uploads image to a **Registry** (Docker Hub / private).

## 2.2 Docker Architecture — Component Breakdown

Docker follows a **Client–Server (C/S) architecture**:

### 2.2.1 Docker Client (`docker` CLI)
- The primary user interface; sends REST API requests to the daemon.
- Can communicate with **local or remote** daemons via `DOCKER_HOST` environment variable.

### 2.2.2 Docker Daemon (`dockerd`)
- Long-running background service (server).
- Manages **images, containers, networks, and volumes** via `libcontainer`.
- Exposes a **REST API on `/var/run/docker.sock` (Unix socket)** or **TCP port 2375/2376**.

### 2.2.3 Docker Registry
- Stateless, scalable HTTP server storing Docker images.
- **Docker Hub** (public, default) and **Docker Trusted Registry / Harbor** (private enterprise).

### 2.2.4 Docker Objects
| Object | Role |
|---|---|
| **Image** | Read-only template with instructions for building a container |
| **Container** | Runnable instance of an image |
| **Network** | Virtual network for container communication (bridge, host, overlay) |
| **Volume** | Persistent storage bypassing the UnionFS |
| **Dockerfile** | Text file with build instructions |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Docker Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Per the engine protocol, the absolute-value symbol `\|` is replaced with `\vert` to prevent markdown table breakage.

| Symbol / Concept | Definition | Typical Value / Location |
|---|---|---|
| **Image** | Immutable blueprint (layers) | Stored in `/var/lib/docker/overlay2/` |
| **Container** | Runtime instance | Ephemeral by default |
| **Dockerfile** | Build recipe | Naming convention: `Dockerfile` |
| **Docker Compose** | Multi-container orchestrator | File: `docker-compose.yml` |
| **Layer** | Each instruction in Dockerfile | Cached for reuse |
| **Volume** | Persistent data store | `/var/lib/docker/volumes/` |
| **Network: bridge** | Default isolated network | IP range `172.17.0.0/16` |
| **Network: host** | No namespace isolation | Shares host's network stack |
| **cgroup v1 vs v2** | Resource control mechanism | Kernel `\geq 4.5` supports v2 |
| **OCI Spec** | Open Container Initiative standard | Runtime = `runc` (default) |
| **Context Size Limit** | Max `docker build` context | Default `∞` (use `.dockerignore`) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Microservices** | Each service packaged as a container, scaled independently via Kubernetes |
| **DevOps / CI-CD** | Jenkins, GitLab CI run pipelines inside ephemeral Docker containers |
| **Edge & IoT** | Lightweight footprint suits ARM devices (Raspberry Pi, edge gateways) |
| **MLOps** | Reproducible training environments with GPU-passthrough |
| **Legacy Migration** | Lift-and-shift of monolithic apps into cloud-native pods |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In production, Docker containers are typically managed by **Kubernetes (K8s)**, **Docker Swarm**, or **Amazon ECS** — pure Docker daemon usage is rare at scale. The KTU 2024 syllabus frames Docker as the **foundational layer** under these orchestrators.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Build & Containerization Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: From Source Code to Running Container

Let us trace a complete Docker lifecycle using a canonical **Node.js "Hello World"** example, since this is a CS-focused KTU elective.

### Step 1 — Author the Application

Create `app.js`:

```javascript
const http = require('http');
const server = http.createServer((req, res) => {
  res.end('Hello from Docker Container!');
});
server.listen(8080, () => console.log('Server running on port 8080'));
```

### Step 2 — Write the Dockerfile (Build Recipe)

```dockerfile
# Step A: Choose base image (the foundation layer)
FROM node:18-alpine

# Step B: Set working directory inside the image
WORKDIR /app

# Step C: Copy dependency manifest first (cache optimization)
COPY package*.json ./

# Step D: Install dependencies
RUN npm install --production

# Step E: Copy application source
COPY . .

# Step F: Expose the application port
EXPOSE 8080

# Step G: Define the runtime command
CMD ["node", "app.js"]
```

### Step 3 — Build the Image

The following command is executed:

```bash
docker build -t hello-docker:v1.0 .
```

### Step 4 — Layer-by-Layer Image Construction (Analytical Trace)

Each `Dockerfile` instruction produces an **independent, cacheable layer**. The build engine processes them sequentially:

| Build Step | Dockerfile Instruction | Resulting Layer | Size Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `FROM node:18-alpine` | Base OS + Node.js runtime | ~180 MB |
| 2 | `WORKDIR /app` | Metadata layer | ~0 B |
| 3 | `COPY package*.json ./` | Source manifest layer | ~2 KB |
| 4 | `RUN npm install` | `node_modules` layer | ~50 MB |
| 5 | `COPY . .` | Application code layer | ~5 KB |
| 6 | `EXPOSE 8080` | Metadata layer | ~0 B |
| 7 | `CMD ["node", "app.js"]` | Runtime metadata | ~0 B |

> [!TIP]
> **Cache Optimization Insight:** Notice how Step C copies `package*.json` **before** the source code. If source code changes, the `npm install` layer is **reused from cache** — saving rebuild time. This is a high-yield KTU exam point.

### Step 5 — Mathematical Representation of Image Storage

The total on-disk size of a Docker image can be expressed as:

$$
S_{\text{image}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i
$$

where $s_i$ is the size of the $i$-th layer, and shared layers across images are stored **only once** on the host. Effective host storage consumption is:

$$
S_{\text{host}} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} S_{\text{image},j} - \sum_{k \in \text{shared}} s_k
$$

This **deduplication** is the primary reason Docker storage is so efficient compared to VM disk images.

### Step 6 — Run the Container

```bash
docker run -d -p 8080:8080 --name my-hello hello-docker:v1.0
```

Port mapping logic:

$$
\text{Host Port} \rightarrow \text{Container Port} = 8080 \rightarrow 8080
$$

The `-d` flag runs in detached mode; `--name` assigns a human-readable identifier.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Docker Containers vs Virtual Machines

This is the **single most important comparison** for KTU 2024 board exams.

| Parameter | Virtual Machine (VM) | Docker Container |
|---|---|---|
| **Virtualization Level** | Hardware-level | OS-level |
| **Hypervisor Required** | Yes (Type 1 / Type 2) | No (uses host kernel) |
| **Guest OS** | Full OS per VM (GBs) | None (shares host kernel) |
| **Boot Time** | 30 – 60 seconds | 50 – 500 ms |
| **Image Size** | 5 – 50 GB | 5 MB – 1 GB |
| **Performance Overhead** | 5 – 15% | $\lt 2\%$ |
| **Isolation Strength** | Very strong (separate kernel) | Process-level (weaker) |
| **Density (per host)** | 10 – 30 VMs | 100 – 1000+ containers |
| **Portability** | Hypervisor-dependent | Kernel-compatible hosts only |
| **Security Surface** | Larger attack surface | Smaller, but shared kernel risk |
| **Use Case** | Multi-OS, strong isolation | Microservices, cloud-native |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern (2024):** A frequent 14-mark question asks to "Compare and contrast containers and VMs with a neat diagram." The above matrix + the architecture diagram in Section 4 is a complete answer.

## 3.3 Analytical Trace: Container Lifecycle State Machine

A container transitions through these states:

$$
\text{created} \rightarrow \text{running} \rightarrow \text{paused} \mid \text{stopped} \rightarrow \text{removed}
$$

Corresponding Docker commands:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Create} &: \texttt{docker create [image]} \\
\text{Run} &: \texttt{docker start [id]} \\
\text{Pause} &: \texttt{docker pause [id]} \\
\text{Stop} &: \texttt{docker stop [id]} \\
\text{Remove} &: \texttt{docker rm [id]}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exams — "C-R-U-P-S-R":** **C**reate, **R**un, **U**se, **P**ause, **S**top, **R**emove.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Docker Client–Server–Registry Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    user1["DEVELOPER / USER"] --> cli1["DOCKER CLI CLIENT"]
    cli1 -->|"REST API OVER UNIX SOCKET OR TCP"| daemon1["DOCKER DAEMOND"]
    daemon1 -->|"MANAGES"| images1["DOCKER IMAGES"]
    daemon1 -->|"RUNS"| containers1["DOCKER CONTAINERS"]
    daemon1 -->|"CREATES"| networks1["DOCKER NETWORKS"]
    daemon1 -->|"MOUNTS"| volumes1["DOCKER VOLUMES"]
    daemon1 -->|"PULL OR PUSH"| registry1["DOCKER REGISTRY HUB"]
    registry1 -.->|"FETCHES BASE IMAGES"| daemon1
    images1 -->|"INSTANTIATED INTO"| containers1
```

## 4.2 Layered Image & Container Construction (OverlayFS)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph writeLayer["WRITABLE CONTAINER LAYER TOP"]
        wl["RUNTIME CHANGES LOGS TMP FILES"]
    end
    subgraph imgLayers["DOCKER IMAGE LAYERS READ ONLY BOTTOM TO TOP"]
        l5["LAYER 5 APP JS CODE"]
        l4["LAYER 4 NPM INSTALL"]
        l3["LAYER 3 PACKAGE JSON"]
        l2["LAYER 2 WORKDIR METADATA"]
        l1["LAYER 1 BASE IMAGE NODE 18 ALPINE"]
    end
    writeLayer --> l5
    l5 --> l4
    l4 --> l3
    l3 --> l2
    l2 --> l1
```

## 4.3 Container vs Virtual Machine Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph vmStack["VIRTUAL MACHINE STACK"]
        appA1["APP A"] --> guestOS1["GUEST OS UBUNTU 2 GB"]
        appA2["APP B"] --> guestOS2["GUEST OS WINDOWS 10 GB"]
        appA3["APP C"] --> guestOS3["GUEST OS DEBIAN 2 GB"]
        guestOS1 --> hyper1["HYPERVISOR VMWARE KVM"]
        guestOS2 --> hyper1
        guestOS3 --> hyper1
        hyper1 --> hostOS1["HOST OPERATING SYSTEM"]
        hostOS1 --> infra1["INFRASTRUCTURE PHYSICAL HARDWARE"]
    end
    subgraph contStack["CONTAINER STACK"]
        appB1["APP A CONTAINER"] --> dockerEng["DOCKER ENGINE USER SPACE"]
        appB2["APP B CONTAINER"] --> dockerEng
        appB3["APP C CONTAINER"] --> dockerEng
        dockerEng --> hostOS2["HOST OS LINUX KERNEL"]
        hostOS2 --> infra2["INFRASTRUCTURE PHYSICAL HARDWARE"]
    end
```

## 4.4 Complete Container Lifecycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> created: docker create
    created --> running: docker start
    running --> paused: docker pause
    paused --> running: docker unpause
    running --> stopped: docker stop
    running --> exited: process ends
    stopped --> [*]: docker rm
    exited --> [*]: docker rm
    paused --> stopped: docker stop
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** Always label the **direction of REST API calls** (solid arrow) versus **passive relationships** (dashed arrow) in your hand-drawn diagrams. Examiners award marks for clarity of arrows.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Q: Define a Docker container. How is it different from a Docker image?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Docker container** is a lightweight, runnable, isolated instance of a Docker image, encapsulating an application along with all its dependencies, libraries, and configuration. **(1 mark)**

A **Docker image** is an immutable, read-only template consisting of a stack of layers; the container is the live, writable runtime instance spawned from the image. **(1 mark)**

Key differences:
- Image = static blueprint; Container = running instance. **(1 mark)**
- Multiple containers can be instantiated from a single image; an image cannot be created from a container (only committed into a new image via `docker commit`).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: List and briefly explain any three main components of the Docker architecture.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Docker Client (`docker` CLI):** The user-facing command-line tool that sends REST API requests to the Docker daemon. **(1 mark)**
2. **Docker Daemon (`dockerd`):** The background server process that builds, runs, and distributes Docker containers by managing images, networks, and volumes. **(1 mark)**
3. **Docker Registry:** A storage and distribution system for Docker images, e.g., **Docker Hub** (public) or **Harbor** (private). **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing "Docker Daemon and Docker Engine are different components." They are **synonymous** in the KTU 2024 syllabus — the daemon is the server-side component of the Docker Engine. Avoid this trap.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B offers an **internal choice** between two full 14-mark questions. Both sub-parts carry **7 marks each**. The model solutions below mirror the exact KTU valuation key style.

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Q (A):**
**(a)** Explain the complete **Docker architecture** with a neat block diagram. Describe the roles of the **Docker Client, Docker Daemon, Docker Images, Docker Containers, and Docker Registry**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Docker Containers with Virtual Machines** in detail. Discuss the role of **namespaces and cgroups** in container isolation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Docker Architecture (7 marks):**

Docker follows a **Client–Server architecture** communicating via REST API over a UNIX socket (`/var/run/docker.sock`) or TCP port `2375/2376`. **(1 mark)**

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **Docker Client** | CLI tool (`docker`); user-facing interface. **(1 mark)** |
| **Docker Daemon (`dockerd`)** | Background service managing images, containers, networks, volumes. **(1 mark)** |
| **Docker Images** | Read-only, layered blueprints stored in `/var/lib/docker/overlay2/`. **(1 mark)** |
| **Docker Containers** | Runnable, isolated instances of images with a thin writable layer. **(1 mark)** |
| **Docker Registry** | HTTP-based image repository (Docker Hub public / Harbor private). **(1 mark)** |
| **Block Diagram** | Refer to Section 4.1 mermaid diagram. **(1 mark)** |

---

**(b) Containers vs Virtual Machines (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Virtual Machine | Docker Container |
|---|---|---|
| Virtualization level | Hardware | OS / Process |
| Guest OS | Full OS per VM | None (shares host kernel) |
| Boot time | 30 – 60 s | 50 – 500 ms |
| Size | GBs | MBs |
| Density | 10 – 30 per host | 100 – 1000+ per host |
| Overhead | 5 – 15% | $\lt 2\%$ |
| Isolation | Strong (separate kernel) | Weaker (shared kernel) |

**(4 marks for the table above with explanation)**

**Role of Namespaces and cgroups: (3 marks)**

- **Namespaces** wrap OS resources (PIDs, network interfaces, mount points, hostnames, IPC, UIDs) so that each container believes it has its own dedicated system. Six types: `PID`, `NET`, `MNT`, `UTS`, `IPC`, `USER`. **(1.5 marks)**
- **cgroups (Control Groups)** limit, account for, and isolate resource usage (CPU, memory, block I/O, network) of a collection of processes. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Q (B):**
**(a)** Explain the concept of **Docker image layering** and the **Union File System (UnionFS / OverlayFS)**. Why is the ordering of Dockerfile instructions critical for build performance? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With suitable **Dockerfile and CLI examples**, describe the steps to **containerize, run, expose, and manage the lifecycle** of a simple web application. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Image Layering & UnionFS (7 marks):**

A Docker image is a **stack of read-only layers**, each corresponding to an instruction in the `Dockerfile`. **(1 mark)**

The **Union File System (UnionFS / OverlayFS)** merges these layers into a single unified view at runtime, with a thin **writable layer** added on top for the container. **(1 mark)**

Layered structure (bottom → top):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Layer 1} &: \text{ Base image (`node:18-alpine`)} \\
\text{Layer 2} &: \text{ `WORKDIR` metadata} \\
\text{Layer 3} &: \text{ `COPY package*.json`} \\
\text{Layer 4} &: \text{ `RUN npm install`} \\
\text{Layer 5} &: \text{ `COPY . .`} \\
\text{Layer 6} &: \text{ `CMD [ ... ]`}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks for the layered representation above)**

**Why instruction ordering matters (3 marks):**

- Docker caches each layer. If a layer's inputs (file content) are unchanged, the cached layer is **reused**. **(1 mark)**
- Therefore, frequently changing files (e.g., source code) should be copied **AFTER** rarely changing ones (e.g., `package.json`). **(1 mark)**
- This is the **"Cache Optimization"** principle: place volatile instructions at the **bottom** of the Dockerfile and stable ones at the **top** to maximize cache hits and minimize rebuild time. **(1 mark)**

---

**(b) End-to-End Containerization Example (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Sample `app.py` (Python Flask):**

```python
from flask import Flask
app = Flask(__name__)
@app.route('/')
def hello():
    return "Hello from Docker!"
```

**Step 2 — `requirements.txt`:**

```
flask==3.0.0
```

**Step 3 — `Dockerfile`:**

```dockerfile
FROM python:3.11-slim
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt
COPY . .
EXPOSE 5000
CMD ["python", "app.py"]
```

**(2 marks for correct Dockerfile structure)**

**Step 4 — Lifecycle commands (5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Build image} &: \texttt{docker build -t flask-app:v1 .} \quad \text{(1 mark)} \\
\text{Run container} &: \texttt{docker run -d -p 5000:5000 --name webapp flask-app:v1} \quad \text{(1 mark)} \\
\text{List containers} &: \texttt{docker ps -a} \quad \text{(0.5 marks)} \\
\text{View logs} &: \texttt{docker logs webapp} \quad \text{(0.5 marks)} \\
\text{Stop container} &: \texttt{docker stop webapp} \quad \text{(0.5 marks)} \\
\text{Remove container} &: \texttt{docker rm webapp} \quad \text{(0.5 marks)} \\
\text{Remove image} &: \texttt{docker rmi flask-app:v1} \quad \text{(0.5 marks)} \\
\text{Push to registry} &: \texttt{docker push username/flask-app:v1} \quad \text{(0.5 marks)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Image vs Container** — saying "container is a file" loses 2 marks instantly. A container is a **process + namespace**, not a file.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the writable layer** when describing UnionFS — the top writable layer is mandatory in the explanation.
> 3. **Not labelling arrows** in the architecture diagram — examiners deduct 1–2 marks for unclear directionality.
> 4. **Missing `EXPOSE` vs `-p` distinction** — `EXPOSE` is documentation-only; actual port publishing requires `-p host:container` at run time.
> 5. **Ignoring cache optimization** in layering questions — always explain *why* `package.json` is copied before source code.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section before your exam. It contains 95% of expected questions.**

- **Docker** is a **containerization platform** (OS-level virtualization), not a hypervisor.
- **Image** = read-only, layered blueprint. **Container** = runnable, writable instance.
- **Architecture:** Client (`docker CLI`) → REST API → Daemon (`dockerd`) → Registry (Docker Hub / private).
- **Three Linux kernel pillars:** **Namespaces** (isolation), **cgroups** (resource limits), **UnionFS/OverlayFS** (layered storage).
- **Container startup:** 50–500 ms. **VM startup:** 30–60 s.
- **Container footprint:** 5 MB – 1 GB. **VM footprint:** 5–50 GB.
- **Image storage location on host:** `/var/lib/docker/overlay2/`.
- **Daemon socket:** `/var/run/docker.sock` (default UNIX socket).
- **Docker Compose** (`docker-compose.yml`) orchestrates **multi-container** applications.
- **Production orchestrators** running Docker: **Kubernetes, Docker Swarm, Amazon ECS**.
- **Cache optimization principle:** place **frequently changing** instructions **last** in a Dockerfile.
- **Top writable layer** is unique per container; lower layers are shared across all containers spawned from the same image.
- **Default network driver:** `bridge` (IP range `172.17.0.0/16`).
- **Key Dockerfile instructions:** `FROM`, `WORKDIR`, `COPY`, `RUN`, `EXPOSE`, `CMD`, `ENTRYPOINT`, `ENV`, `ARG`, `VOLUME`.
- **Distinguish:** `CMD` (overridable default) vs `ENTRYPOINT` (fixed executable prefix).
- **OCI standard:** `runc` is the default low-level runtime; `containerd` is the higher-level daemon.
- **Mnemonic for lifecycle states:** **C → R → (P | S) → R(emove)** : **Create, Run, Pause/Stop, Remove**.
- **Security note:** Containers share the host kernel → a kernel exploit compromises all containers. Use **rootless Docker** or **gVisor** for hardened isolation.
- **Real-world impact:** Docker reduced average deployment time by **~80%** in published industry case studies (e.g., PayPal, Spotify, eBay migrations).
